commonMistakes: Rushing through the partial rep portion instead of controlling it
Inconsistent depth on either the partial or full range portions
Losing tightness during the extended time under tension
exerciseTips: Maintain tension throughout the entire extended repetition sequence
Control both the full and partial range movements equally
Keep consistent squat form during all phases of the 1.5 repetition
Focus on the stretch reflex benefits from the additional bottom position work
instructions: Set up under barbell with standard squat positioning and bar placement on upper traps.
Unrack and position feet shoulder-width apart with toes slightly turned out.
Descend into full depth squat position with control and proper mechanics.
Rise halfway up to approximately parallel position, then descend back to full depth.
Complete the repetition by driving all the way up to full standing position.
exercises.name: 1.5 Rep Squat
exercises.overview: The 1.5 rep squat combines a full rep with a partial rep, increasing time under tension and volume per repetition. This intermediate variation builds strength endurance and emphasizes the bottom portion of the squat.
exercises.safetyInfo: The extended time under tension and additional volume per rep increases fatigue significantly. Reduce working weight by 15-20% from normal squat loads to accommodate the increased demands.

commonMistakes: Going all the way down and losing tension
Using momentum instead of core strength
Pulling on the neck with hands
exerciseTips: Keep your core engaged throughout the entire movement
Focus on controlled movement rather than speed
Pause briefly at the top position
Lower slowly to maintain tension
instructions: Lie down on the floor and secure your feet. Your legs should be bent at the knees.
Place your hands behind or to the side of your head. You will begin with your back on the ground. This will be your starting position.
Flex your hips and spine to raise your torso toward your knees.
At the top of the contraction your torso should be perpendicular to the ground. Reverse the motion, going only ¾ of the way down.
Repeat for the recommended amount of repetitions.
exercises.name: 3/4 Sit-Up
exercises.overview: The 3/4 sit-up maintains constant tension on the abdominals by stopping short of the full range of motion. This partial movement keeps the core engaged throughout the entire set.
exercises.safetyInfo: Avoid if you have lower back issues. Keep hands lightly behind head without pulling on neck to prevent cervical strain.

commonMistakes: Using too much weight and relying on momentum
Pulling with arms instead of crunching with abs
Moving too fast through the range of motion
exerciseTips: Use slow and controlled movements
Focus on contracting your abs rather than moving weight
Keep your triceps relaxed on the pads
Breathe out during the crunch phase
instructions: Select a light resistance and sit down on the ab machine placing your feet under the pads provided and grabbing the top handles. Your arms should be bent at a 90 degree angle as you rest the triceps on the pads provided. This will be your starting position.

At the same time, begin to lift the legs up as you crunch your upper torso. Breathe out as you perform this movement. Tip: Be sure to use a slow and controlled motion. Concentrate on using your abs to move the weight while relaxing your legs and feet.
After a second pause, slowly return to the starting position as you breathe in.
Repeat the movement for the prescribed amount of repetitions.
exercises.name: Ab Crunch Machine
exercises.overview: The ab crunch machine provides a controlled environment to target the rectus abdominis with adjustable resistance. It's ideal for beginners learning proper crunch mechanics.
exercises.safetyInfo: Start with light weight to master the movement pattern. Avoid this exercise if you have lower back problems.

commonMistakes: Rolling out too far and losing core stability
Allowing lower back to sag during the movement
Using hip flexors instead of core to return to start
exerciseTips: Start with small range of motion and progress gradually
Keep your core braced throughout the movement
Roll out slowly and pull back with control
Maintain neutral spine position
instructions: Hold the Ab Roller with both hands and kneel on the floor.
Now place the ab roller on the floor in front of you so that you are on all your hands and knees (as in a kneeling push up position). This will be your starting position.

Slowly roll the ab roller straight forward, stretching your body into a straight position. Tip: Go down as far as you can without touching the floor with your body. Breathe in during this portion of the movement.
After a pause at the stretched position, start pulling yourself back to the starting position as you breathe out. Tip: Go slowly and keep your abs tight at all times.
exercises.name: Ab Roller
exercises.overview: The ab roller is one of the most challenging core exercises, requiring significant strength and stability. It effectively targets the entire core while also engaging the shoulders.
exercises.safetyInfo: This is an advanced core exercise. Avoid if you have lower back issues. Start with wall rollouts before progressing to floor rollouts.

commonMistakes: Decelerating at the top instead of maintaining acceleration against increasing resistance
Allowing bar path to drift forward as resistance increases
Using too much accommodating resistance relative to base weight
exerciseTips: Accelerate maximally through the entire range of motion to overcome variable resistance
Maintain bar path close to body as resistance increases toward lockout
Focus on speed and explosiveness rather than grinding through the lift
Keep core braced throughout to handle changing resistance curve
instructions: Set up a barbell with bands or chains attached to provide variable resistance throughout the lift.
Position feet hip-width apart with the bar over mid-foot, hands gripping the bar outside legs.
Maintain neutral spine with chest up and shoulders back, engaging lats to keep bar close.
Drive through heels and extend hips and knees simultaneously, accelerating against increasing resistance.
Complete the lift by driving hips forward and standing tall, then reverse the movement with control.
exercises.name: Accommodating Resistance Deadlift
exercises.overview: Accommodating resistance deadlifts use bands or chains to provide variable resistance that increases through the range of motion. This advanced technique develops explosive power and strength at different joint angles.
exercises.safetyInfo: This advanced technique requires solid deadlift form and should only be attempted by experienced lifters. The changing resistance can stress joints differently than traditional deadlifts.

commonMistakes: Bending the overhead arm during the movement
Rotating the torso instead of hinging at the hip
Placing too much weight on the bottom hand
exerciseTips: Keep the overhead arm locked and eyes focused on the kettlebell throughout
Push hips back toward the kettlebell side to initiate movement
Maintain a straight line from overhead hand to planted foot
Control the descent and avoid rushing the bottom position
instructions: Clean and press a kettlebell overhead with one arm.

Keeping the kettlebell locked out at all times, push your butt out in the direction of the locked out kettlebell. Keep the non-working arm behind your back and turn your feet out at a forty-five degree angle from the arm with the kettlebell.
Lower yourself as far as possible.
Pause for a second and reverse the motion back to the starting position.
exercises.name: Advanced Kettlebell Windmill
exercises.overview: The advanced kettlebell windmill is a unilateral movement that combines overhead stability with hip hinge mechanics. It develops core strength, shoulder stability, and hip mobility while challenging coordination.
exercises.safetyInfo: Avoid if you have shoulder impingement or lower back issues. Start with bodyweight windmills before adding load. Stop if you experience any shoulder or back pain.

commonMistakes: Pulling on the neck with hands
Moving too fast and losing control
Not fully extending the working leg
exerciseTips: Focus on bringing elbow toward opposite knee rather than knee to elbow
Keep constant tension in the core throughout the movement
Maintain controlled tempo rather than rushing through reps
Keep lower back pressed against the floor
instructions: Lie flat on the floor with your lower back pressed to the ground. For this exercise, you will need to put your hands beside your head. Be careful however to not strain with the neck as you perform it. Now lift your shoulders into the crunch position.
Bring knees up to where they are perpendicular to the floor, with your lower legs parallel to the floor. This will be your starting position.

Now simultaneously, slowly go through a cycle pedal motion kicking forward with the right leg and bringing in the knee of the left leg. Bring your right elbow close to your left knee by crunching to the side, as you breathe out.
Go back to the initial position as you breathe in.
Crunch to the opposite side as you cycle your legs and bring closer your left elbow to your right knee and exhale.
Continue alternating in this manner until all of the recommended repetitions for each side have been completed.
exercises.name: Air Bike
exercises.overview: The air bike is a dynamic core exercise that targets the abdominals and obliques through rotational movement. It's effective for building core strength and endurance with no equipment needed.
exercises.safetyInfo: Avoid if you have neck problems. Keep hands lightly touching behind head without pulling. Stop if you feel neck strain.

commonMistakes: Swinging the weights using momentum
Allowing elbows to drift forward during the curl
Rotating wrists during the movement
exerciseTips: Keep elbows pinned to your sides throughout the movement
Maintain neutral wrist position with thumbs up
Control both the lifting and lowering phases
Squeeze at the top before lowering
instructions: Stand up with your torso upright and a dumbbell in each hand being held at arms length. The elbows should be close to the torso.
The palms of the hands should be facing your torso. This will be your starting position.

While holding the upper arm stationary, curl the right weight forward while contracting the biceps as you breathe out. Continue the movement until your biceps is fully contracted and the dumbbells are at shoulder level. Hold the contracted position for a second as you squeeze the biceps. Tip: Only the forearms should move.
Slowly begin to bring the dumbbells back to starting position as your breathe in.
Repeat the movement with the left hand. This equals one repetition.
Continue alternating in this manner for the recommended amount of repetitions.
exercises.name: Alternate Hammer Curl
exercises.overview: Alternate hammer curls target the biceps and brachialis with a neutral grip, building arm strength while reducing wrist stress compared to traditional curls.
exercises.safetyInfo: Start with lighter weights to master form. Avoid if you have elbow tendinitis. Stop if you feel sharp pain in the elbow or wrist.

commonMistakes: Lifting the head too high off the ground
Rushing through the movement without control
Moving feet closer to make the exercise easier
exerciseTips: Focus on lateral flexion of the spine rather than forward crunching
Keep knees bent and feet planted throughout
Reach directly to the side without lifting shoulders high
Maintain constant tension in the core
instructions: Lie on the floor with the knees bent and the feet on the floor around 18-24 inches apart. Your arms should be extended by your side. This will be your starting position.
Crunch over your torso forward and up about 3-4 inches to the right side and touch your right heel as you hold the contraction for a second. Exhale while performing this movement.
Now go back slowly to the starting position as you inhale.

Now crunch over your torso forward and up around 3-4 inches to the left side and touch your left heel as you hold the contraction for a second. Exhale while performing this movement and then go back to the starting position as you inhale. Now that both heels have been touched, that is considered 1 repetition.
Continue alternating sides in this manner until all prescribed repetitions are done.
exercises.name: Alternate Heel Touchers
exercises.overview: Alternate heel touchers target the obliques through lateral spinal flexion, helping build side core strength and definition with bodyweight resistance.
exercises.safetyInfo: Keep movements controlled and avoid jerky motions. Stop if you experience neck strain or lower back discomfort.

commonMistakes: Using too steep of an incline angle
Not allowing full arm extension at the bottom
Bringing elbows forward during the curl
exerciseTips: Set bench to 45-60 degree incline for optimal angle
Allow full stretch at the bottom position
Keep shoulders back against the pad throughout
Supinate wrists as you curl up
instructions: Sit down on an incline bench with a dumbbell in each hand being held at arms length. Tip: Keep the elbows close to the torso.This will be your starting position.

While holding the upper arm stationary, curl the right weight forward while contracting the biceps as you breathe out. As you do so, rotate the hand so that the palm is facing up. Continue the movement until your biceps is fully contracted and the dumbbells are at shoulder level. Hold the contracted position for a second as you squeeze the biceps. Tip: Only the forearms should move.
Slowly begin to bring the dumbbell back to starting position as your breathe in.
Repeat the movement with the left hand. This equals one repetition.
Continue alternating in this manner for the recommended amount of repetitions.
exercises.name: Alternate Incline Dumbbell Curl
exercises.overview: The incline position pre-stretches the biceps, allowing for greater range of motion and muscle activation compared to standing curls.
exercises.safetyInfo: The incline position increases stretch on the biceps, so use lighter weight initially. Avoid if you have shoulder impingement or bicep tendon issues.

commonMistakes: Pressing at an angle rather than straight up
Using momentum to initiate the press
Allowing the torso to rotate excessively
exerciseTips: Keep core engaged to prevent excessive back arch
Press in a straight line directly overhead
Maintain tension in the non-pressing arm
Control the descent to maximize time under tension
instructions: Move the cables to the bottom of the tower and select an appropriate weight.
Grasp the cables and hold them at shoulder height, palms facing forward. This will be your starting position.
Keeping your head and chest up, extend through the elbow to press one side directly over head.
After pausing at the top, return to the starting position and repeat on the opposite side.
exercises.name: Alternating Cable Shoulder Press
exercises.overview: A unilateral pressing movement that challenges shoulder stability and core strength by pressing one arm at a time. Develops balanced strength and identifies strength imbalances between sides.
exercises.safetyInfo: Start with lighter weight to master the alternating pattern. Avoid if experiencing shoulder impingement or rotator cuff injuries.

commonMistakes: Raising weights too high above shoulder level
Using momentum instead of controlled movement
Allowing shoulders to shrug during the lift
exerciseTips: Keep a slight bend in the elbows throughout
Raise weights to shoulder height, not above
Control the descent to maximize muscle tension
Avoid swinging or using momentum
instructions: In a standing position, hold a pair of dumbbells at your side.
Keeping your elbows slightly bent, raise the weights directly in front of you to shoulder height, avoiding any swinging or cheating.
Return the weights to your side.
On the next repetition, raise the weights laterally, raising them out to your side to about shoulder height.
Return the weights to the starting position and continue alternating to the front and side.
exercises.name: Alternating Deltoid Raise
exercises.overview: A combination shoulder exercise alternating between front and lateral raises to target different heads of the deltoid. Provides comprehensive shoulder development in a single movement pattern.
exercises.safetyInfo: Use lighter weights when combining movement patterns. Stop if experiencing shoulder impingement pain during overhead movements.

commonMistakes: Pressing at an angle rather than straight up
Not maintaining tension in the non-working arm
Bouncing the kettlebell off the chest
exerciseTips: Keep the non-pressing arm locked out for stability
Allow your upper back to touch the floor at bottom
Press straight up, not angled toward midline
Rotate wrist toward the locked-out kettlebell at bottom
instructions: Lie on the floor with two kettlebells next to your shoulders.
Position one in place on your chest and then the other, gripping the kettlebells on the handle with the palms facing forward.
Extend both arms, so that the kettlebells are being held above your chest. Lower one kettlebell, bringing it to your chest and turn the wrist in the direction of the locked out kettlebell.
Raise the kettlebell and repeat on the opposite side.
exercises.name: Alternating Floor Press
exercises.overview: A unilateral pressing movement performed on the floor that eliminates excessive shoulder extension while building chest strength. Challenges core stability and identifies strength imbalances.
exercises.safetyInfo: The floor provides natural range of motion limitation, making this safer than full-range pressing movements. Ensure proper kettlebell grip to prevent wrist injury.

commonMistakes: Pulling with arms instead of driving with hips
Allowing the kettlebell to swing away from the body
Not rotating the wrist properly at the catch
exerciseTips: Drive through legs and hips explosively
Keep the kettlebell close to your body during the pull
Rotate wrist at the top to catch cleanly
Maintain straight back throughout the movement
instructions: Place two kettlebells between your feet. To get in the starting position, push your butt back and look straight ahead.

Clean one kettlebell to your shoulder and hold on to the other kettlebell in a hanging position. Clean the kettlebell to your shoulder by extending through the legs and hips as you pull the kettlebell towards your shoulders. Rotate your wrist as you do so.
Lower the cleaned kettlebell to a hanging position and clean the alternate kettlebell. Repeat.
exercises.name: Alternating Hang Clean
exercises.name_pt: Clean Alternado a Partir do Hang
exercises.overview: An explosive pulling movement that develops posterior chain power while improving coordination and timing. Builds functional strength for athletic movements requiring hip drive and coordination.
exercises.safetyInfo: Master the hip hinge pattern before attempting. Use appropriate weight to maintain proper form throughout all repetitions. Avoid if experiencing lower back issues.

commonMistakes: Allowing the non-pressing kettlebell to drop
Pressing forward instead of overhead
Using legs to assist the press
exerciseTips: Clean both kettlebells to shoulders before beginning
Keep non-pressing kettlebell stable at shoulder
Press straight up and slightly back
Engage core to prevent excessive back arch
instructions: Clean two kettlebells to your shoulders. Clean the kettlebells to your shoulders by extending through the legs and hips as you pull the kettlebells towards your shoulders. Rotate your wrists as you do so.
Press one directly overhead by extending through the elbow, turning it so the palm faces forward while holding the other kettlebell stationary .
Lower the pressed kettlebell to the starting position and immediately press with your other arm.
exercises.name: Alternating Kettlebell Press
exercises.overview: A unilateral overhead pressing movement that challenges shoulder stability and core strength. Develops balanced pressing power and identifies strength differences between arms.
exercises.safetyInfo: Ensure proper clean technique before attempting the press. Start with lighter weights to master the alternating pattern. Avoid if experiencing shoulder impingement.

commonMistakes: Rotating hips with each row
Using momentum instead of controlled movement
Pulling kettlebell straight up instead of back toward hip
exerciseTips: Retract shoulder blade fully before pulling
Keep hips square and avoid rotating
Drive elbow back toward hip
Maintain neutral spine throughout movement
instructions: Place two kettlebells in front of your feet. Bend your knees slightly and push your butt out as much as possible. As you bend over to get into the starting position grab both kettlebells by the handles.

Pull one kettlebell off of the floor while holding on to the other kettlebell. Retract the shoulder blade of the working side, as you flex the elbow, drawing the kettlebell towards your stomach or rib cage.
Lower the kettlebell in the working arm and repeat with your other arm.
exercises.name: Alternating Kettlebell Row
exercises.overview: A unilateral rowing exercise that targets the middle back while challenging core stability. Excellent for addressing muscle imbalances and building functional pulling strength.
exercises.safetyInfo: Avoid if you have lower back issues. Keep core engaged to protect spine and maintain proper hip hinge position throughout the movement.
variations: Bent Over Two-Dumbbell Row With Palms In

commonMistakes: Allowing hips to rotate with each row
Rowing too high toward shoulder
Losing plank position and sagging hips
exerciseTips: Press inactive hand firmly into floor
Keep hips level and avoid rotation
Row to ribs not straight up
Maintain plank position throughout
instructions: Place two kettlebells on the floor about shoulder width apart. Position yourself on your toes and your hands as though you were doing a pushup, with the body straight and extended. Use the handles of the kettlebells to support your upper body. You may need to position your feet wide for support.
Push one kettlebell into the floor and row the other kettlebell, retracting the shoulder blade of the working side as you flex the elbow, pulling it to your side.
Then lower the kettlebell to the floor and begin the kettlebell in the opposite hand. Repeat for several reps.
exercises.name: Alternating Renegade Row
exercises.overview: An advanced compound movement combining rowing with anti-rotation core stability. Builds functional strength while challenging the entire kinetic chain.
exercises.safetyInfo: Advanced exercise requiring good core strength and wrist stability. Avoid if you have wrist or shoulder impingement issues.

commonMistakes: Relaxing completely on the pins and losing muscle tension
Setting pins too high or low for individual anthropometry
Rushing the transition from pins instead of focusing on explosiveness
exerciseTips: Maintain full-body tension even while resting briefly on the pins
Focus on explosive concentric power since there's no stretch reflex assistance
Keep chest up and knees tracking over toes throughout the movement
Pause long enough to eliminate bounce but not so long that you lose tightness
instructions: Set safety pins in a squat rack at the bottom of your squat depth with barbell resting on pins.
Position yourself under the bar in the bottom squat position with proper form and tension.
Grip the bar with hands evenly spaced and create upper back tightness before lifting.
Pause briefly at the bottom to eliminate stretch reflex, then drive explosively upward.
Complete the concentric portion by extending hips and knees, then lower back to pins with control.
exercises.name: Anderson Squat
exercises.overview: The Anderson squat starts from a dead stop at the bottom position, eliminating the stretch reflex and forcing pure concentric strength development. This advanced variation builds explosive power and addresses sticking points.
exercises.safetyInfo: Requires advanced squatting technique and proper pin height setup. The lack of eccentric loading can be jarring on joints, so start with lighter weights than normal squats.

commonMistakes: Pressing upward instead of forward
Losing contact with the incline bench
exerciseTips: Keep arms parallel to ground
Press forward not upward
Maintain contact with bench
Control the eccentric portion
instructions: Place a bar on the ground behind the head of an incline bench.
Lay on the bench face down. With a pronated grip, pick the barbell up from the floor. Flex the elbows, performing a reverse curl to bring the bar near your chest. This will be your starting position.
To begin, press the barbell out in front of your head by extending your elbows. Keep your arms parallel to the ground throughout the movement.
Return to the starting position and repeat to complete the set.
exercises.name: Anti-Gravity Press
exercises.overview: A unique shoulder pressing variation that eliminates leg drive and core compensation while targeting the front and side deltoids. Excellent for building pure shoulder strength.
exercises.safetyInfo: Ensure proper bench angle to avoid shoulder impingement. Use lighter weight initially to master the movement pattern.

commonMistakes: Distributing weight equally between both arms instead of focusing on the working side
Allowing the hips to sag or twist during the movement
Not achieving sufficient depth on the working side
exerciseTips: Focus most of your weight on the working arm while using the extended arm for balance
Keep your body in a straight line and avoid twisting
Control the movement slowly to maximize strength gains
Start with partial range of motion and gradually increase depth
instructions: Start in a wide-grip push-up position with hands significantly wider than shoulder-width apart.
Shift your weight to one side as you lower down, bending one arm while keeping the other arm relatively straight.
Descend until your chest is near the bent arm while the straight arm extends out to the side.
Push through the bent arm to return to the starting position.
Alternate sides with each repetition or complete all reps on one side before switching.
Maintain a straight body line and engage your core throughout the movement.
exercises.name: Archer Push Up
exercises.overview: The archer push-up is an advanced unilateral pushing exercise that serves as a progression toward one-arm push-ups. It develops significant upper body strength while challenging stability and coordination.
exercises.safetyInfo: This is an advanced exercise that places significant stress on one arm at a time. Ensure you have mastered regular push-ups and have adequate shoulder stability before attempting.

commonMistakes: Rotating too quickly without control
Pressing straight up instead of slightly forward
Using too much weight compromising form
exerciseTips: Rotate palms smoothly from facing inward to forward
Keep core engaged throughout the movement
Control the weight during the lowering phase
Press dumbbells slightly forward at the top
instructions: Sit on an exercise bench with back support and hold two dumbbells in front of you at about upper chest level with your palms facing your body and your elbows bent. Tip: Your arms should be next to your torso. The starting position should look like the contracted portion of a dumbbell curl.
Now to perform the movement, raise the dumbbells as you rotate the palms of your hands until they are facing forward.
Continue lifting the dumbbells until your arms are extended above you in straight arm position. Breathe out as you perform this portion of the movement.

After a second pause at the top, begin to lower the dumbbells to the original position by rotating the palms of your hands towards you. Tip: The left arm will be rotated in a counter clockwise manner while the right one will be rotated clockwise. Breathe in as you perform this portion of the movement.
Repeat for the recommended amount of repetitions.
exercises.name: Arnold Dumbbell Press
exercises.overview: A compound shoulder exercise that combines rotation with pressing motion to target all deltoid heads. Popularized by Arnold Schwarzenegger for complete shoulder development.
exercises.safetyInfo: The rotation places additional stress on shoulder joints. Use lighter weight than standard shoulder press and avoid if you have shoulder impingement.

commonMistakes: Allowing arms to rise above parallel to floor
Using too heavy weight leading to poor control
Straightening elbows completely during movement
exerciseTips: Keep elbows slightly bent throughout entire movement
Maintain arms parallel to floor at all times
Control the weight during the full range of motion
Focus on squeezing chest at the starting position
instructions: Lay down on a flat bench holding a dumbbell in each hand with the palms of the hands facing towards the ceiling. Tip: Your arms should be parallel to the floor and next to your thighs. To avoid injury, make sure that you keep your elbows slightly bent. This will be your starting position.

Now move the dumbbells by creating a semi-circle as you displace them from the initial position to over the head. All of the movement should happen with the arms parallel to the floor at all times. Breathe in as you perform this portion of the movement.
Reverse the movement to return the weight to the starting position as you exhale.
exercises.name: Around The Worlds
exercises.overview: An isolation exercise that targets the chest through a unique semi-circular motion. Provides constant tension on the pectorals throughout the full range of motion.
exercises.safetyInfo: High risk exercise for shoulder injury if performed incorrectly. Use light weight and avoid if you have shoulder impingement or chest muscle strains.

commonMistakes: Allowing the weight to drift away from the body during the lift
Rounding the back excessively instead of using the legs
Attempting to lift with the arms rather than driving with the lower body
exerciseTips: Keep the weight as close to your body as possible throughout the lift
Drive with your legs and hips rather than pulling with your back
Maintain a neutral spine and avoid excessive rounding
Use your entire body to hug and secure the weight plate
instructions: Position yourself behind the weight plate with feet shoulder-width apart.
Squat down and wrap your arms around the plate, getting your chest as close as possible.
Drive through your heels and extend your hips and knees to lift the plate off the ground.
As the plate rises, pull it tight to your chest and continue standing up.
Once fully upright, carefully lower the plate back to the ground by reversing the movement.
exercises.name: Atlas Stone Lift
exercises.overview: The Atlas Stone Lift simulates the strongman atlas stone event using a weight plate. It develops tremendous posterior chain strength and teaches proper lifting mechanics for awkward, heavy objects.
exercises.safetyInfo: This is an advanced exercise that places significant stress on the lower back and should be avoided by those with back problems. Proper warm-up and progressive loading are essential.

commonMistakes: Underestimating the grip strength demands of the thick bar
Allowing the bar to drift away from the body due to grip fatigue
exerciseTips: Squeeze the thick bar as hard as possible throughout the lift
Keep the bar path straight and close to your body
Engage your lats to prevent the bar from drifting forward
Build up your grip strength progressively with this implement
instructions: Approach the loaded axle bar and position your feet hip-width apart.
Bend down and grip the thick bar with both hands using an overhand grip.
Set your back in neutral position and engage your lats.
Drive through your heels while simultaneously extending hips and knees.
Pull the bar close to your body until you reach full hip extension.
Lower the bar under control back to the starting position.
exercises.name: Axle Bar Deadlift
exercises.overview: The axle bar deadlift uses a thick diameter barbell (usually 2 inches) that dramatically increases grip strength demands. It's commonly used in strongman training to develop crushing grip strength alongside posterior chain power.
exercises.safetyInfo: The axle bar's thick diameter significantly increases grip demands and can cause premature grip failure. Ensure your grip strength is adequate for the load being attempted to maintain safe bar control.

commonMistakes: Allowing arms to drift below parallel
Using momentum instead of controlled movement
Letting shoulders roll forward at start position
exerciseTips: Keep your arms parallel to the floor throughout the movement
Squeeze your shoulder blades together at the end of the movement
Maintain a slight bend in your elbows
Control the return phase slowly
instructions: Run a band around a stationary post like that of a squat rack.
Grab the band by the handles and stand back so that the tension in the band rises.

Extend and lift the arms straight in front of you. Tip: Your arms should be straight and parallel to the floor while perpendicular to your torso. Your feet should be firmly planted on the floor spread at shoulder width. This will be your starting position.
As you exhale, move your arms to the sides and back. Keep your arms extended and parallel to the floor. Continue the movement until the arms are extended to your sides.
After a pause, go back to the original position as you inhale.
Repeat for the recommended amount of repetitions.
exercises.name: Back Flyes - With Bands
exercises.overview: Back flyes with bands target the rear deltoids and upper back muscles to improve posture and shoulder stability. This exercise helps counteract forward shoulder posture from daily activities.
exercises.safetyInfo: Avoid if you have recent shoulder impingement or rotator cuff injuries. Start with lighter resistance bands to prevent overloading the posterior deltoids.
variations: Bent Over Two-Dumbbell Row With Palms In

commonMistakes: Looking down at feet instead of ahead
Making large, jerky corrections
Holding breath during balance attempts
exerciseTips: Focus on a fixed point in front of you
Make small corrections rather than large movements
Engage your core muscles
Start with shorter holds and progress gradually
instructions: Place a balance board in front of you.
Stand up on it and try to balance yourself.
Hold the balance for as long as desired.
exercises.name: Balance Board
exercises.overview: Balance board training improves proprioception, ankle stability, and lower leg strength. It's essential for injury prevention and athletic performance enhancement.
exercises.safetyInfo: Use near a wall or have someone spot you initially. Start with both feet before progressing to single leg variations.

commonMistakes: Dropping hips during the curl
Using calves instead of hamstrings to pull ball
Moving too quickly through the range of motion
exerciseTips: Keep hips elevated throughout the movement
Squeeze glutes to maintain bridge position
Pull ball toward glutes with heels
Control the eccentric phase slowly
instructions: Begin on the floor laying on your back with your feet on top of the ball.
Position the ball so that when your legs are extended your ankles are on top of the ball. This will be your starting position.
Raise your hips off of the ground, keeping your weight on the shoulder blades and your feet.
Flex the knees, pulling the ball as close to you as you can, contracting the hamstrings.
After a brief pause, return to the starting position.
exercises.name: Ball Leg Curl
exercises.name_pt: Flexão de Pernas na Bola Suíça
exercises.overview: Ball leg curl is an effective hamstring isolation exercise that also challenges core stability. It provides a functional movement pattern while targeting the posterior chain.
exercises.safetyInfo: Ensure ball is properly inflated and rated for your body weight. Avoid if you have recent hamstring strains or lower back issues.

commonMistakes: Swinging or using momentum
Not pulling to full range of motion
Letting band assistance do all the work
exerciseTips: Pull elbows down toward your ribs
Keep chest up and shoulders back
Focus on initiating movement with lats
Control descent slowly
instructions: Choke the band around the center of the pullup bar. You can use different bands to provide varying levels of assistance.
Pull the end of the band down, and place one bent knee into the loop, ensuring it won't slip out. Take a medium to wide grip on the bar. This will be your starting position.

Pull yourself upward by contracting the lats as you flex the elbow. The elbow should be driven to your side. Pull to the front, attempting to get your chin over the bar. Avoid swinging or jerking movements.
After a brief pause, return to the starting position.
exercises.name: Band Assisted Pull-Up
exercises.name_pt: Barra Fixa Assistida com Faixa Elástica
exercises.overview: Band assisted pull-ups help develop the strength needed for unassisted pull-ups by providing variable assistance. They're essential for building lat strength and upper body pulling power.
exercises.safetyInfo: Ensure band is securely attached and in good condition. Check for wear or tears before use. Progress to thinner bands as strength improves.

commonMistakes: Relying too heavily on the band instead of using leg muscles
Allowing knees to cave inward during the movement
Leaning too far forward and losing upright torso position
exerciseTips: Keep chest up and core engaged throughout the movement
Allow the band to provide just enough assistance to maintain proper form
Focus on controlling the descent rather than relying entirely on the band
Push knees out in line with toes during the squat
instructions: Attach a resistance band to a stable anchor point at chest height and hold the handles in both hands.
Stand with feet shoulder-width apart, toes slightly turned out, holding the band with arms extended forward.
Lower into a squat by pushing hips back and bending knees while allowing the band to assist your balance and provide upward support.
Descend until thighs are parallel to the floor or as low as mobility allows.
Drive through heels and use the band's assistance to return to starting position.
exercises.name: Band Assisted Squat
exercises.overview: The band assisted squat uses resistance band support to help beginners learn proper squat form while building lower body strength. The band provides upward assistance, making the movement more accessible while still engaging the target muscles.
exercises.safetyInfo: This exercise is ideal for beginners learning squat mechanics or those recovering from lower body injuries. Ensure the band is securely anchored to prevent snapping back.

commonMistakes: Using momentum instead of controlled movement
Leaning away from the working leg
Not maintaining tension at the bottom position
exerciseTips: Keep standing leg slightly bent for stability
Control both the lifting and lowering phases
Maintain upright posture throughout movement
Focus on squeezing inner thigh muscles
instructions: Anchor a band around a solid post or other object.
Stand with your left side to the post, and put your right foot through the band, getting it around the ankle.
Stand up straight and hold onto the post if needed. This will be your starting position.
Keeping the knee straight, raise your right legs out to the side as far as you can.
Return to the starting position and repeat for the desired rep count.
Switch sides.
exercises.name: Band Hip Adductions
exercises.name_pt: Adução de Quadril com Faixa Elástica
exercises.overview: Band Hip Adductions target the inner thigh muscles using lateral resistance. This exercise is essential for hip stability and can help prevent groin injuries in athletes.
exercises.safetyInfo: Start with light resistance to avoid groin strains. Stop immediately if you feel sharp pain in the inner thigh or groin area.

commonMistakes: Allowing shoulders to roll forward
Using arms instead of squeezing shoulder blades
Not maintaining constant tension in the band
exerciseTips: Squeeze shoulder blades together at the end
Keep arms parallel to the floor
Maintain tension in the band throughout
Focus on initiating movement from the rear delts
instructions: Begin with your arms extended straight out in front of you, holding the band with both hands.
Initiate the movement by performing a reverse fly motion, moving your hands out laterally to your sides.
Keep your elbows extended as you perform the movement, bringing the band to your chest. Ensure that you keep your shoulders back during the exercise.
Pause as you complete the movement, returning to the starting position under control.
exercises.name: Band Pull Apart
exercises.overview: The Band Pull Apart is a simple yet effective exercise for strengthening the rear deltoids and upper back. It's perfect for improving posture and shoulder health.
exercises.safetyInfo: This is a low-risk exercise suitable for most fitness levels. Excellent for counteracting poor posture from desk work.

commonMistakes: Using a band with too much resistance that compromises form
Allowing the band to slip or shift during the movement
exerciseTips: Choose band resistance that challenges the top portion most
Focus on explosive concentric movement against increasing resistance
Keep the band positioned properly across your upper back
Maintain rigid core throughout to handle the variable resistance
instructions: Place a resistance band across your upper back and hold the ends in each hand.
Assume push up position with the band creating tension across your back.
Keep your body in straight line from head to heels.
Lower your chest to the ground against the band tension.
Press back up explosively as the band resistance increases.
Complete repetitions maintaining constant tension from the band.
exercises.name: Band Resisted Push Up
exercises.overview: The band resisted push up adds accommodating resistance that increases as you press up, making the top portion of the movement more challenging. It develops explosive pushing power and challenges the muscles differently than regular push ups.
exercises.safetyInfo: Ensure the resistance band is in good condition without nicks or wear that could cause failure. The variable resistance requires good body control throughout the range of motion.

commonMistakes: Allowing the band to pull the torso forward during the descent
Not maintaining proper foot position on the band causing slippage
Using too light of a band that provides insufficient resistance at the top
exerciseTips: Maintain constant tension in the band throughout the entire range of motion
Keep elbows up and chest proud to prevent the band from pulling you forward
Focus on explosive upward movement against increasing band resistance
Control the descent to maximize time under tension
instructions: Step on the center of a resistance band with both feet shoulder-width apart, holding handles at shoulder height.
Position the band so it runs behind your arms and across your upper back like a barbell.
Initiate the squat by pushing hips back and bending at knees while maintaining upright torso.
Descend until thighs are parallel to floor, feeling increased band tension.
Drive through heels and extend hips and knees to return to starting position against band resistance.
exercises.name: Band Resisted Squat
exercises.name_pt: Agachamento com Resistência de Faixa Elástica
exercises.overview: The band resisted squat adds variable resistance that increases as you stand up, providing accommodating resistance training. This method challenges muscles differently than traditional weights by providing maximum resistance at full extension.
exercises.safetyInfo: Ensure the band is properly secured under feet to prevent snapping. Those with knee issues should start with lighter resistance and monitor for any discomfort.

commonMistakes: Moving the upper arms during extension
Not achieving full range of motion
Rushing through the lowering phase
exerciseTips: Keep upper arms stationary throughout movement
Control the eccentric portion slowly
Fully extend arms at the top
Maintain wrist alignment with forearms
instructions: Secure a band to the base of a rack or the bench. Lay on the bench so that the band is lined up with your head.
Take hold of the band, raising your elbows so that the upper arm is perpendicular to the floor. With the elbow flexed, the band should be above your head. This will be your starting position.
Extend through the elbow to straighten your arm, keeping your upper arm in place. Pause at the top of the motion, and return to the starting position.
exercises.name: Band Skull Crusher
exercises.overview: The Band Skull Crusher isolates the triceps using resistance band tension from a lying position. It provides constant tension throughout the range of motion for effective triceps development.
exercises.safetyInfo: Use controlled movements to avoid elbow strain. If you experience elbow pain, reduce the resistance or range of motion.

commonMistakes: Using too much band tension relative to bar weight
Decelerating at lockout instead of accelerating against band resistance
Allowing bands to pull bar out of proper pressing path
exerciseTips: Accelerate maximally through the entire pressing range to overcome variable resistance
Maintain tight setup throughout as bands will try to destabilize the bar
Focus on speed and explosiveness rather than grinding through heavy resistance
Keep bar path consistent despite changing resistance patterns
instructions: Attach resistance bands to barbell and anchor points, creating variable resistance throughout the movement.
Lie on bench with proper setup including retracted shoulder blades and firm foot position.
Unrack the bar feeling initial band tension and position over chest with full arm extension.
Lower bar to chest with control, noting decreasing band tension during eccentric phase.
Explosively press bar back up, accelerating against increasing band resistance toward lockout.
exercises.name: Banded Bench Press
exercises.name_pt: Supino com Faixa Elástica na Barra
exercises.overview: Banded bench press adds variable resistance through bands, creating accommodating resistance that increases toward lockout. This advanced method develops explosive pressing power and trains the strength curve more completely.
exercises.safetyInfo: Advanced technique requiring solid bench press fundamentals. The variable resistance can create unusual stress patterns, so progress gradually and ensure bands are properly secured.

commonMistakes: Allowing hips to sag during the rollout
Rolling out too far and losing control
Using back muscles instead of abs to return
exerciseTips: Brace core before rolling forward
Keep hips elevated throughout movement
Roll out only as far as you can control
Maintain neutral spine position
instructions: For this exercise you will need to get into a pushup position, but instead of having your hands of the floor, you will be grabbing on to an Olympic barbell (loaded with 5-10 lbs on each side) instead. This will be your starting position.

While keeping a slight arch on your back, lift your hips and roll the barbell towards your feet as you exhale. Tip: As you perform the movement, your glutes should be coming up, you should be keeping the abs tight and should maintain your back posture at all times. Also your arms should be staying perpendicular to the floor throughout the movement. If you don't, you will work out your shoulders and back more than the abs.
After a second contraction at the top, start to roll the barbell back forward to the starting position slowly as you inhale.
Repeat for the recommended amount of repetitions.
exercises.name: Barbell Ab Rollout
exercises.overview: The Barbell Ab Rollout is an advanced anti-extension core exercise that challenges the entire anterior chain. It builds exceptional core strength and stability when performed correctly.
exercises.safetyInfo: This is an advanced core exercise that can strain the lower back if performed incorrectly. Start with shorter ranges of motion and progress gradually.

commonMistakes: Allowing hips to sag or pike during the rollout
Rolling out too far too quickly before building strength
Using momentum to pull back instead of controlled muscle contraction
exerciseTips: Keep your core braced throughout the entire movement
Control the descent slowly to maximize muscle tension
Pull yourself back using your abs, not your hips
Maintain neutral spine position throughout the movement
instructions: Hold an Olympic barbell loaded with 5-10lbs on each side and kneel on the floor.
Now place the barbell on the floor in front of you so that you are on all your hands and knees (as in a kneeling push up position). This will be your starting position.

Slowly roll the barbell straight forward, stretching your body into a straight position. Tip: Go down as far as you can without touching the floor with your body. Breathe in during this portion of the movement.
After a second pause at the stretched position, start pulling yourself back to the starting position as you breathe out. Tip: Go slowly and keep your abs tight at all times.
exercises.name: Barbell Ab Rollout - On Knees
exercises.overview: The barbell ab rollout is an advanced core exercise that challenges the entire anterior chain. It builds exceptional core stability and strength through an extended range of motion.
exercises.safetyInfo: Avoid this exercise if you have lower back issues. Start with shorter ranges of motion and gradually increase distance as core strength improves.

commonMistakes: Bouncing the bar off the pins instead of dead stopping
Starting with rounded shoulders and loose lats
Allowing the bar to drift away from the body
exerciseTips: Pull the slack out of the bar before initiating the lift
Keep the bar path vertical and close to your body
Reset completely on the pins between each rep
Maintain tension in your lats throughout the movement
instructions: Set up a barbell on safety pins in a squat rack at mid-shin height.
Position yourself with feet hip-width apart and the bar over mid-foot.
Hinge at hips and bend knees to grip the bar with hands just outside your legs.
Create tension by pulling slack out of the bar while maintaining a neutral spine.
Drive through heels and extend hips and knees simultaneously to lift the bar.
Lower the bar back to the pins with control and reset for the next rep.
exercises.name: Barbell Anderson Deadlift
exercises.overview: The Anderson deadlift starts from a dead stop position on pins, eliminating the stretch reflex and requiring pure concentric strength. This variation helps build lockout strength and teaches proper deadlift mechanics from the bottom position.
exercises.safetyInfo: This exercise places significant stress on the lower back and requires proper hip hinge mechanics. Those with lower back injuries should avoid this movement or use lighter loads.

commonMistakes: Allowing knees to cave inward during the squat
Not reaching adequate depth with hips below knees
Shifting weight to the toes instead of keeping it balanced
exerciseTips: Keep your knees tracking in line with your toes
Maintain three points of contact with your feet throughout
Drive your knees out during both the descent and ascent
Keep your core braced throughout the entire movement
instructions: Position the barbell on your upper trapezius muscles and step back from the rack with feet shoulder-width apart.
Keep your chest up, shoulders back, and maintain a neutral spine throughout the movement.
Initiate the squat by pushing your hips back and bending at the knees simultaneously.
Descend until your hip crease is just below your knee caps while keeping your knees tracking over your toes.
Drive through your heels and push the floor away to return to the starting position.
Fully extend your hips and knees at the top before beginning the next repetition.
exercises.name: Barbell Back Squat
exercises.overview: The Barbell Back Squat is a fundamental compound exercise that targets the quadriceps, glutes, and entire lower body while engaging the core for stability. It's considered one of the most effective exercises for building lower body strength and muscle mass.
exercises.safetyInfo: Always use a squat rack with safety bars set just below your lowest squat position. Start with bodyweight or empty barbell to master form. Avoid if you have knee or lower back injuries without proper guidance.

commonMistakes: Bouncing the bar off the chest instead of controlled touch-and-go
Flaring elbows too wide, creating shoulder impingement risk
Lifting feet off the ground, reducing stability and power
exerciseTips: Retract your shoulder blades and maintain arch in your back
Drive through your heels and engage your glutes for stability
Lower the bar to your chest with control over 2-3 seconds
Press the bar in a slight arc toward your face at the top
instructions: Lie back on a flat bench. Using a medium width grip (a grip that creates a 90-degree angle in the middle of the movement between the forearms and the upper arms), lift the bar from the rack and hold it straight over you with your arms locked. This will be your starting position.
From the starting position, breathe in and begin coming down slowly until the bar touches your middle chest.

After a brief pause, push the bar back to the starting position as you breathe out. Focus on pushing the bar using your chest muscles. Lock your arms and squeeze your chest in the contracted position at the top of the motion, hold for a second and then start coming down slowly again. Tip: Ideally, lowering the weight should take about twice as long as raising it.
Repeat the movement for the prescribed amount of repetitions.
When you are done, place the bar back in the rack.
exercises.name: Barbell Bench Press - Medium Grip
exercises.name_pt: Supino Reto com Barra - Pegada Média
exercises.overview: The barbell bench press is a fundamental compound movement for building chest, shoulder, and tricep strength. It's considered the gold standard for upper body pressing power.
exercises.safetyInfo: Always use a spotter or safety bars when lifting heavy weights. Avoid this exercise if you have shoulder impingement issues.

commonMistakes: Using too much weight and compromising form
Raising the torso up during the lift instead of keeping it stationary
Allowing the elbows to bend excessively during the movement
exerciseTips: Keep your core engaged to protect your lower back
Focus on squeezing your shoulder blades together
Maintain a slight bend in your elbows throughout
Control the weight on both the lifting and lowering phases
instructions: Hold a barbell with an overhand grip, hands positioned wider than shoulder-width apart.
Hinge at the hips to bend forward until your torso is nearly parallel to the floor, keeping a slight bend in your knees.
Let the barbell hang at arm's length with a slight bend in your elbows.
Raise the barbell out to your sides in a wide arc until your arms are parallel to the floor.
Squeeze your shoulder blades together at the top, then slowly lower the barbell back to the starting position.
exercises.name: Barbell Bent Over Reverse Fly
exercises.overview: The Barbell Bent Over Reverse Fly targets the often-neglected posterior deltoids and upper back muscles. This exercise helps improve posture and shoulder stability while balancing the effects of pressing movements.
exercises.safetyInfo: This exercise places stress on the lower back due to the bent-over position. Those with lower back issues should consider seated or chest-supported variations. Start with light weight to master the movement pattern.

commonMistakes: Rounding the back during the rowing motion
Using too much momentum and swinging the weight
Pulling the bar too high toward the neck instead of the lower chest
exerciseTips: Keep your core tight throughout the entire movement to protect your lower back
Squeeze your shoulder blades together at the top of each rep
Focus on pulling with your back muscles rather than your arms
Maintain a slight bend in your knees throughout the exercise
instructions: Stand with feet hip-width apart, holding a barbell with an underhand grip slightly wider than shoulder-width apart.
Hinge at the hips and bend forward to about 45 degrees, keeping your back straight and knees slightly bent.
Let the barbell hang with arms fully extended, maintaining a neutral spine and engaged core.
Pull the barbell toward your lower chest by driving your elbows back and squeezing your shoulder blades together.
Pause briefly at the top of the movement, then slowly lower the barbell back to the starting position.
Return to the starting position with control and repeat for desired repetitions.
exercises.name: Barbell Bent Over Row Underhand Grip
exercises.name_pt: Remada Curvada com Barra - Pegada Supinada
exercises.overview: The underhand grip barbell bent over row is a compound pulling exercise that targets the lats and mid-back muscles. The supinated grip allows for greater bicep involvement and can feel more natural for many lifters.
exercises.safetyInfo: Avoid this exercise if you have lower back issues. Those with shoulder impingement should use caution and consider a lighter weight or different grip width.
variations: Barbell Bent Over Row Overhand Grip

commonMistakes: Allowing the bar to drift away from the body
Hyperextending the back at the top position
Using the lower back instead of hips to lift
exerciseTips: Keep the bar path vertical and close to your body
Engage your lats to prevent the bar from drifting away
Drive your hips forward explosively at the top
Maintain tension in your core throughout the movement
instructions: Set up blocks or platforms to elevate the barbell 2-6 inches off the ground.
Stand with feet hip-width apart, bar over mid-foot, and grip the bar with hands just outside your legs.
Keep your chest up, shoulders back, and maintain a neutral spine with knees slightly bent.
Drive through your heels and extend your hips to lift the bar, keeping it close to your body.
Stand tall with shoulders back and hips fully extended at the top.
Lower the bar back to the blocks with control, maintaining the same movement pattern.
exercises.name: Barbell Block Pull
exercises.name_pt: Levantamento Terra com Barra a Partir de Blocos
exercises.overview: The barbell block pull is a partial deadlift performed from an elevated position. It allows you to work the top portion of the deadlift with heavier loads while reducing stress on the lower back.
exercises.safetyInfo: Those with lower back injuries should avoid this exercise or use very light weight. Ensure proper hip hinge mechanics before loading heavily.

commonMistakes: Using the trailing leg to push off the ground for assistance
Leaning forward excessively while stepping up
Allowing the knee to cave inward during the step up
exerciseTips: Keep your torso upright throughout the movement to maintain balance
Drive through your heel rather than pushing off your toe
Control the descent to maximize muscle engagement
Choose a box height that allows your thigh to be parallel to the ground
instructions: Position a barbell across your upper back and stand facing a sturdy box or platform at knee height.
Place your entire right foot firmly on the box, ensuring your heel is not hanging off the edge.
Drive through your right heel to step up onto the box, bringing your left foot to meet your right.
Step down with control, leading with your left foot first, then your right foot.
Complete all reps on one leg before switching to the other leg.
exercises.name: Barbell Box Step Up
exercises.overview: The barbell box step up is a unilateral leg exercise that builds quadriceps strength while improving balance and addressing muscle imbalances. It's an excellent functional movement that translates to daily activities.
exercises.safetyInfo: Ensure the box is stable and non-slip. Avoid this exercise if you have knee or ankle injuries. Start with bodyweight before adding the barbell load.

commonMistakes: Allowing the bar to drift away from the body
Pulling with arms instead of driving with legs and hips
Rounding the back during the setup or pull
exerciseTips: Keep the bar close to your body throughout the pull
Drive through your heels during the initial pull
Explode through your hips at the top of the movement
Maintain a neutral spine throughout the lift
instructions: Set up with barbell over mid-foot, feet hip-width apart, hands slightly wider than shoulders.
Hinge at hips and bend knees to grip bar with overhand grip.
Keep chest up, shoulders over bar, and maintain neutral spine.
Drive through heels extending hips and knees simultaneously.
As bar passes knees, explosively extend hips and shrug shoulders.
Return bar to starting position with control.
exercises.name: Barbell Clean Pull
exercises.overview: The barbell clean pull is a partial Olympic lifting movement that develops explosive hip extension and posterior chain power. It's used to build strength for the full clean and improve athletic performance.
exercises.safetyInfo: This is an advanced Olympic lifting movement requiring proper coaching and technique. Avoid if you have lower back issues or lack mobility in hips and ankles.

commonMistakes: Gripping the bar too narrow, causing wrist and elbow stress
Allowing the elbows to flare out wide like a regular bench press
Lowering the bar too high on the chest, reducing tricep activation
exerciseTips: Keep your elbows tucked close to your sides throughout the movement
Focus on pressing with your triceps rather than your chest
Maintain a firm grip and stable shoulder position
Lower the bar to your lower chest or upper abdomen, not your neck
instructions: Lie on a bench and grip the barbell with hands positioned closer than shoulder-width apart.
Unrack the bar and position it over your chest with arms fully extended.
Keep your elbows close to your body and lower the bar to your lower chest or upper abdomen.
Press the bar back up by extending your arms, focusing on tricep contraction.
Return to the starting position with arms fully extended and repeat.
exercises.name: Barbell Close Grip Bench Press
exercises.overview: The close grip bench press is a compound movement that primarily targets the triceps while also working the chest and shoulders. It's an excellent exercise for building tricep mass and pressing strength.
exercises.safetyInfo: Use a spotter when lifting heavy weights. Avoid gripping too narrow as this can stress the wrists and elbows. Those with elbow issues should start with lighter weights.

commonMistakes: Gripping too narrow causing wrist discomfort
Allowing knees to cave inward
Starting with the bar too far from the shins
exerciseTips: Keep your arms straight and close to your body
Focus on hip hinge movement pattern
Maintain tight upper back throughout the lift
Drive your hips forward to complete the movement
instructions: Stand with feet hip-width apart, barbell over mid-foot on the floor.
Bend down and grip the bar with hands closer than shoulder-width apart.
Keep your chest up, shoulders back, and maintain a neutral spine.
Drive through your heels and extend your hips and knees simultaneously.
Pull the bar straight up, keeping it close to your body throughout.
Stand tall with hips and knees fully extended, then lower with control.
exercises.name: Barbell Close Grip Deadlift
exercises.name_pt: Levantamento Terra com Barra - Pegada Fechada
exercises.overview: The barbell close grip deadlift is a variation that emphasizes the lats and upper back more than conventional deadlifts. The narrower grip creates a different pulling angle and can help break through plateaus.
exercises.safetyInfo: The close grip may feel awkward initially and can stress the wrists. Those with wrist issues should use a conventional grip. Ensure proper warm-up before heavy lifting.

commonMistakes: Using the bottom leg to push off instead of stepping up
Leaning the torso excessively toward the box
Allowing the knee to cave inward during the step up
exerciseTips: Keep your torso upright throughout the movement
Drive through the heel of the elevated leg
Control the descent to maximize muscle engagement
Focus on the working leg doing most of the work
instructions: Position a barbell across your upper back and stand beside a box or bench.
Cross the far leg over to step onto the box with your full foot planted.
Keep most of your weight on the leg that's on the box.
Drive through the heel of the elevated leg to step up to full extension.
Step down with control, leading with the same leg that stepped up.
Complete all reps crossing over to the same side before switching.
exercises.name: Barbell Crossover Step Up
exercises.name_pt: Subida Cruzada no Banco com Barra
exercises.overview: This crossover step up variation challenges the legs in a different plane of movement while improving balance and coordination. The crossover pattern better mimics many athletic and daily movement patterns.
exercises.safetyInfo: Ensure the box or bench is stable and appropriate height. Avoid if you have knee or ankle injuries. Start with bodyweight before adding load.

commonMistakes: Swinging the body or using momentum to lift the weight
Allowing elbows to drift forward during the curl
Not fully extending arms at the bottom of the movement
exerciseTips: Keep your elbows stationary at your sides throughout the movement
Squeeze your biceps hard at the top of each repetition
Control the weight on the way down for maximum muscle tension
Stand with feet shoulder-width apart for optimal stability
instructions: Stand up with your torso upright while holding a barbell at a shoulder-width grip. The palm of your hands should be facing forward and the elbows should be close to the torso. This will be your starting position.
While holding the upper arms stationary, curl the weights forward while contracting the biceps as you breathe out. Tip: Only the forearms should move.
Continue the movement until your biceps are fully contracted and the bar is at shoulder level. Hold the contracted position for a second and squeeze the biceps hard.
Slowly begin to bring the bar back to starting position as your breathe in.
Repeat for the recommended amount of repetitions.
exercises.name: Barbell Curl
exercises.overview: The barbell curl is the classic bicep-building exercise that allows for progressive overload. It effectively targets both heads of the biceps for arm development.
exercises.safetyInfo: Use appropriate weight to maintain proper form. Excessive weight can lead to elbow or wrist strain.

commonMistakes: Lifting chest off the bench to gain momentum
Not achieving full range of motion at the bottom
Using too much weight and sacrificing form
exerciseTips: Keep your chest pressed firmly against the incline bench
Focus on squeezing the biceps at the peak contraction
Lower the weight slowly to fully stretch the biceps
Keep wrists straight and aligned with forearms
instructions: Lie against an incline bench, with your arms holding a barbell and hanging down in a horizontal line. This will be your starting position.

While keeping the upper arms stationary, curl the weight up as high as you can while squeezing the biceps. Breathe out as you perform this portion of the movement. Tip: Only the forearms should move. Do not swing the arms.
After a second contraction, slowly go back to the starting position as you inhale. Tip: Make sure that you go all of the way down.
Repeat for the recommended amount of repetitions.
exercises.name: Barbell Curls Lying Against An Incline
exercises.name_pt: Rosca com Barra Deitado no Banco Inclinado
exercises.overview: This incline variation eliminates momentum and body swing, providing strict isolation of the biceps. The chest support ensures pure bicep contraction throughout the movement.
exercises.safetyInfo: This exercise reduces cheating potential but can place stress on the biceps in the stretched position. Start with lighter weights than standing curls.

commonMistakes: Allowing the bar to drift away from the body during the lift
Rounding the lower back under heavy loads
Hyperextending at the top instead of standing tall with neutral spine
exerciseTips: Keep the bar close to your body throughout the entire movement
Drive through your heels and engage your lats to maintain bar path
Hinge at the hips first, then bend the knees when lowering
Keep your chest up and maintain neutral spine alignment
instructions: Stand in front of a loaded barbell.

While keeping the back as straight as possible, bend your knees, bend forward and grasp the bar using a medium (shoulder width) overhand grip. This will be the starting position of the exercise. Tip: If it is difficult to hold on to the bar with this grip, alternate your grip or use wrist straps.

While holding the bar, start the lift by pushing with your legs while simultaneously getting your torso to the upright position as you breathe out. In the upright position, stick your chest out and contract the back by bringing the shoulder blades back. Think of how the soldiers in the military look when they are in standing in attention.

Go back to the starting position by bending at the knees while simultaneously leaning the torso forward at the waist while keeping the back straight. When the weights on the bar touch the floor you are back at the starting position and ready to perform another repetition.
Perform the amount of repetitions prescribed in the program.
exercises.name: Barbell Deadlift
exercises.overview: The deadlift is considered the king of exercises, working more muscles than any other single movement. It builds total-body strength and power through the posterior chain.
exercises.safetyInfo: Master proper hip hinge mechanics before adding heavy weight. Avoid if you have acute lower back injuries. Consider using lifting straps for grip assistance with heavy loads.

commonMistakes: Using too large of a deficit before mastering the movement
Rounding the back in the extended bottom position
Allowing the bar to drift away from the body
exerciseTips: Start with a smaller deficit until you master the extended range
Keep your chest up throughout the extended bottom position
Drive your hips back aggressively to initiate the movement
Maintain tension in your lats to keep the bar close
instructions: Stand on a 1-3 inch platform or plates with the barbell on the floor in front of you.
Position your feet hip-width apart with the bar over your mid-foot.
Hinge at the hips and bend your knees to grip the bar just outside your legs.
Keep your chest up, shoulders over the bar, and maintain a neutral spine.
Drive through your heels and extend your hips to lift the bar, keeping it close to your body.
Stand tall with full hip extension, then lower the bar back to the floor with control.
exercises.name: Barbell Deficit Deadlift
exercises.name_pt: Levantamento Terra com Déficit com Barra
exercises.overview: The deficit deadlift increases the range of motion by elevating your starting position. This variation strengthens the bottom portion of the deadlift and improves overall pulling power.
exercises.safetyInfo: This advanced variation requires excellent mobility and strength. Only attempt after mastering regular deadlifts and ensure adequate hamstring and ankle flexibility.

commonMistakes: Allowing the bar to drift away from the body during the movement
Using too much weight and compensating with body swing
Bringing the elbows forward instead of keeping them back
exerciseTips: Keep the bar in constant contact with your body throughout the movement
Focus on pulling your elbows back rather than forward during the curl
Squeeze your shoulder blades together to maintain proper posture
Use a controlled tempo to maximize muscle tension and activation
instructions: Stand with feet shoulder-width apart, holding a barbell with an underhand grip at shoulder width.
Start with the bar against your thighs and pull your elbows back slightly behind your torso.
Curl the bar up by dragging it along your torso, keeping the bar in contact with your body throughout.
Continue curling until the bar reaches your lower chest, squeezing your biceps at the top.
Slowly lower the bar back down along your torso to the starting position at your thighs.
exercises.name: Barbell Drag Curl
exercises.overview: The barbell drag curl is an advanced bicep exercise that eliminates shoulder involvement by keeping the elbows pulled back behind the torso. This unique movement pattern provides intense bicep activation and targets the long head of the biceps effectively.
exercises.safetyInfo: This advanced exercise requires good shoulder mobility and may not be suitable for those with shoulder impingement. Start with lighter weights to master the technique before progressing.

commonMistakes: Allowing the torso to lean forward excessively
Letting the front knee cave inward during the lunge
Dropping the elbows and losing front rack position
exerciseTips: Keep your torso upright throughout the movement
Drive through your front heel when returning to start
Maintain high elbows to secure the barbell
Control the descent to maximize muscle activation
instructions: Set up a barbell in the front rack position across your collar bones and front deltoids with elbows high.
Stand with feet hip-width apart and core engaged.
Step one foot back into a reverse lunge, lowering until both knees reach 90 degrees.
Push through your front heel to return to the starting position.
Complete all reps on one leg before switching sides.
exercises.name: Barbell Front Loaded Reverse Lunge
exercises.name_pt: Afundo Reverso com Barra à Frente
exercises.overview: The barbell front loaded reverse lunge is a unilateral leg exercise that challenges your quadriceps while improving balance and core stability. The front rack position increases core activation and teaches proper upright posture.
exercises.safetyInfo: Avoid this exercise if you have knee injuries or limited shoulder mobility. The front rack position requires adequate wrist and shoulder flexibility.

commonMistakes: Leaning forward due to front-loaded weight
Putting too much weight on the back foot
Allowing the front knee to cave inward
exerciseTips: Maintain upright torso throughout the movement
Keep most weight on your front leg
Drive through your front heel when standing up
Control the descent into the split squat position
instructions: Set barbell in front rack position across front delts and collar bones.
Step into split stance with one foot forward, one foot back.
Keep torso upright with weight evenly distributed.
Lower by bending both knees until front thigh is parallel to floor.
Drive through front heel to return to starting position.
Complete all reps before switching leg positions.
exercises.name: Barbell Front Loaded Split Squat
exercises.name_pt: Agachamento Búlgaro com Barra à Frente
exercises.overview: The barbell front loaded split squat challenges the quadriceps while requiring significant core stability due to the front-loaded position. It builds unilateral leg strength while improving posture and anterior core strength.
exercises.safetyInfo: Requires good thoracic spine mobility and front rack flexibility. Start with lighter weight to master the position before progressing. Avoid if you have wrist or shoulder mobility limitations.

commonMistakes: Allowing elbows to drop, causing the bar to slide forward
Leaning forward excessively during the descent
Gripping the bar too tightly instead of resting it on the shoulders
exerciseTips: Keep your elbows high throughout the entire movement
Maintain an upright torso to prevent the bar from rolling forward
Focus on driving your knees out over your toes
Breathe at the top of each rep, not at the bottom
instructions: Set the barbell in a squat rack at shoulder height and position it across your front deltoids and clavicles.
Grip the bar with hands slightly wider than shoulders, lifting your elbows high to create a shelf.
Unrack the bar and step back with feet shoulder-width apart, toes slightly turned out.
Descend by pushing your hips back and bending your knees while keeping your torso upright.
Squat until your thighs are parallel to the floor, then drive up through your heels to return to start.
exercises.name: Barbell Front Squat
exercises.overview: The barbell front squat places the load in front of the body, forcing an upright torso position that emphasizes quadriceps development. It's a fundamental movement for Olympic lifting and functional strength development.
exercises.safetyInfo: This exercise requires good thoracic spine and ankle mobility. Avoid if you have wrist, elbow, or shoulder injuries. The front-loaded position is generally safer for the lower back than back squats.

commonMistakes: Allowing knees to cave inward during the ascent
Rising hips faster than shoulders, causing forward lean
Not achieving full depth due to mobility limitations
exerciseTips: Keep your knees tracking in line with your toes throughout the movement
Maintain three points of contact with your feet on the ground
Drive your hips back first, then bend your knees to descend
Keep your core braced and chest up throughout the entire range
instructions: This exercise is best performed inside a squat rack for safety purposes. To begin, first set the bar on a rack just above shoulder level. Once the correct height is chosen and the bar is loaded, step under the bar and place the back of your shoulders (slightly below the neck) across it.
Hold on to the bar using both arms at each side and lift it off the rack by first pushing with your legs and at the same time straightening your torso.

Step away from the rack and position your legs using a shoulder-width medium stance with the toes slightly pointed out. Keep your head up at all times and maintain a straight back. This will be your starting position.

Begin to slowly lower the bar by bending the knees and sitting back with your hips as you maintain a straight posture with the head up. Continue down until your hamstrings are on your calves. Inhale as you perform this portion of the movement.
Begin to raise the bar as you exhale by pushing the floor with the heel or middle of your foot as you straighten the legs and extend the hips to go back to the starting position.
Repeat for the recommended amount of repetitions.
exercises.name: Barbell Full Squat
exercises.overview: The barbell full squat is the ultimate lower body compound exercise, building strength and mass through the legs and core. Full depth squats maximize muscle activation and functional strength.
exercises.safetyInfo: Use safety bars set just below your lowest squat position. Ensure adequate ankle and hip mobility before attempting full depth. Consider goblet squats to learn movement pattern first.

commonMistakes: Lowering the bar too quickly, risking neck injury
Using too heavy of a weight before mastering the technique
Allowing the bar to drift too far over the face
exerciseTips: Lower the bar slowly to your neck/upper chest area
Use a wider grip than traditional bench press
Focus on stretching the pecs at the bottom position
Keep your shoulders pulled back and down throughout
instructions: Using a medium width grip (a grip that creates a 90-degree angle in the middle of the movement between the forearms and the upper arms), lift the bar from the rack and hold it straight over your neck with your arms locked. This will be your starting position.
As you breathe in, bring the bar down slowly until it is about 1 inch from your neck.
