After a second pause, bring the bar back to the starting position as you breathe out and push the bar using your chest muscles. Lock your arms and squeeze your chest in the contracted position, hold for a second and then start coming down slowly again. It should take at least twice as long to go down than to come up.
Repeat the movement for the prescribed amount of repetitions.
When you are done, place the bar back in the rack.
exercises.name: Barbell Guillotine Bench Press
exercises.overview: The guillotine bench press is an advanced chest exercise that provides a deep stretch and targets the pectorals from a unique angle. It's favored by bodybuilders for chest development but requires careful execution.
exercises.safetyInfo: This exercise carries significant risk to the neck and throat area. Always use a spotter, start with light weight, and avoid if you have shoulder or neck issues. Consider using dumbbells as a safer alternative.

commonMistakes: Leaning too far forward, shifting weight to toes
Not achieving parallel depth due to balance issues
Allowing the bar to drift away from the legs
exerciseTips: Keep your torso upright throughout the entire movement
Drive through your heels while maintaining balance
Focus on sitting back into the squat position
Use wrist wraps if grip becomes limiting factor
instructions: Stand up straight while holding a barbell behind you at arms length and your feet at shoulder width. Tip: A shoulder width grip is best with the palms of your hands facing back. You can use wrist wraps for this exercise for a better grip. This will be your starting position.
While keeping your head and eyes up and back straight, squat until your upper thighs are parallel to the floor. Breathe in as you slowly go down.
Pressing mainly with the heel of the foot and squeezing the thighs, go back up as you breathe out.
Repeat for the recommended amount of repetitions.
exercises.name: Barbell Hack Squat
exercises.overview: The barbell hack squat is a unique squat variation performed with the barbell behind the legs. It emphasizes the quadriceps while challenging balance and coordination.
exercises.safetyInfo: This exercise can be challenging for balance and may stress the wrists and shoulders. Start with light weight to master the movement pattern. Avoid if you have wrist or shoulder mobility issues.

commonMistakes: Relying too heavily on hand support
Allowing the torso to lean forward excessively
Placing too much weight on the rear foot
exerciseTips: Use your hands for balance only, not to assist the lift
Keep your torso upright throughout the movement
Focus the work on your front leg
Control the descent to maximize muscle tension
instructions: Set up a barbell in a squat rack and position yourself in a split stance with rear foot elevated on a bench.
Place the barbell across your upper traps and grip the safety bars or rack for light support.
Keep most of your weight on your front leg with your torso upright.
Lower your body by bending your front knee until your thigh is parallel to the floor.
Use minimal assistance from your hands while maintaining balance and control.
Drive through your front heel to return to the starting position, then repeat for all reps before switching legs.
exercises.name: Barbell Hatfield Split Squat
exercises.overview: The Hatfield split squat uses light hand support to allow for heavier loading of single-leg movements. It bridges the gap between bodyweight and heavily loaded unilateral exercises.
exercises.safetyInfo: This advanced variation requires excellent balance and single-leg strength. Ensure you can perform regular split squats proficiently before attempting this version.

commonMistakes: Placing the bar too high on the neck instead of the traps
Allowing knees to cave inward during the movement
Leaning too far forward during the descent
exerciseTips: Keep your chest up and maintain an upright torso throughout the movement
Drive your knees out in line with your toes during the squat
Maintain tension in your upper back to keep the bar stable
Control the descent and avoid bouncing at the bottom
instructions: Set the barbell in a squat rack at shoulder height and position it on your upper trapezius muscles.
Grip the bar with hands evenly spaced and wider than shoulder-width apart.
Unrack the bar and step back, positioning your feet shoulder-width apart with toes slightly turned out.
Descend by simultaneously bending at the hips and knees, keeping your chest up and knees tracking over toes.
Squat until your thighs are parallel to the floor or below, then drive through your heels to return to start.
exercises.name: Barbell High Bar Back Squat
exercises.overview: The high bar back squat places the barbell on the upper traps, promoting an upright torso position that emphasizes quadriceps development. It's a fundamental movement for building lower body strength and power.
exercises.safetyInfo: Use safety bars or pins set just below your lowest squat position. This exercise requires good ankle and hip mobility. Avoid if you have acute knee or back injuries.

commonMistakes: Allowing the extended leg to bend or touch the ground for assistance
Hyperextending the lower back instead of focusing on hip extension
Not achieving full hip extension at the top of the movement
exerciseTips: Keep your extended leg straight and engaged throughout the movement
Focus on driving through your heel of the planted foot
Squeeze your glutes hard at the top of each repetition
Maintain a neutral spine by keeping your core tight
instructions: Position your upper back against a bench with a barbell across your hips, using a pad for comfort.
Place one foot flat on the ground while extending the other leg straight out in front of you.
Hold the barbell securely with both hands and brace your core.
Drive through your planted heel to thrust your hips up while keeping the extended leg straight.
Squeeze your glutes at the top position, creating a straight line from your knee to your shoulders.
Lower your hips back down with control and repeat for all repetitions before switching legs.
exercises.name: Barbell Hip Thrust Single Leg
exercises.overview: The barbell hip thrust single leg is an advanced unilateral exercise that targets each glute individually while challenging core stability. This movement helps address muscle imbalances and builds serious glute strength and power.
exercises.safetyInfo: This advanced exercise requires good hip mobility and core stability. Those with lower back issues should master bilateral hip thrusts first and use lighter weight when progressing to single leg variations.
variations: Resistance Band Single Leg Hip Thrust

commonMistakes: Setting the bench angle too steep (over 45 degrees)
Lowering the bar to the wrong position on the chest
Bouncing the bar off the chest instead of pausing
exerciseTips: Set the bench angle between 30-45 degrees for optimal chest targeting
Lower the bar to your upper chest, not your neck
Keep your feet firmly planted on the ground
Maintain a slight arch in your lower back
instructions: Lie back on an incline bench. Using a medium-width grip (a grip that creates a 90-degree angle in the middle of the movement between the forearms and the upper arms), lift the bar from the rack and hold it straight over you with your arms locked. This will be your starting position.
As you breathe in, come down slowly until you feel the bar on you upper chest.

After a second pause, bring the bar back to the starting position as you breathe out and push the bar using your chest muscles. Lock your arms in the contracted position, squeeze your chest, hold for a second and then start coming down slowly again. Tip: it should take at least twice as long to go down than to come up.
Repeat the movement for the prescribed amount of repetitions.
When you are done, place the bar back in the rack.
exercises.name: Barbell Incline Bench Press - Medium Grip
exercises.name_pt: Supino Inclinado com Barra - Pegada Média
exercises.overview: The incline barbell bench press targets the upper portion of the pectorals more than the flat bench variation. It's essential for balanced chest development and building pressing strength.
exercises.safetyInfo: Always use a spotter when possible, especially with heavy weights. Ensure the bench is properly adjusted and locked in position. Warm up thoroughly before heavy lifting.

commonMistakes: Lifting the chest off the bench and using momentum
Pulling the weight too high toward the neck instead of lower chest
exerciseTips: Keep your chest pressed firmly against the incline bench throughout
Focus on pulling with your back muscles rather than your arms
Squeeze your shoulder blades together at the top of each rep
Control the weight during both the pulling and lowering phases
instructions: Set an incline bench to about 45 degrees and position a barbell in front of it.
Lie face down on the incline bench with your chest supported and feet firmly on the ground.
Reach down and grab the barbell with an overhand grip slightly wider than shoulder-width apart.
Pull the barbell up toward your lower chest by driving your elbows back and squeezing your shoulder blades.
Hold the contraction briefly at the top, then slowly lower the barbell back to the starting position.
Maintain the chest-supported position throughout and repeat for desired repetitions.
exercises.name: Barbell Incline Row
exercises.overview: The barbell incline row is a chest-supported rowing exercise that targets the lats and mid-back muscles while eliminating lower back stress. The inclined position allows for focused back development with proper form.
exercises.safetyInfo: This exercise is generally safer for the lower back compared to bent-over rows due to the chest support. Those with shoulder issues should adjust grip width as needed.

commonMistakes: Bending the arms and turning it into a pressing movement
Using too much weight and compensating with other muscles
Moving too quickly without focusing on scapular movement
exerciseTips: Keep your arms completely straight throughout the movement
Focus on pushing your shoulder blades forward, not pressing up
Use a controlled tempo on both the lifting and lowering phases
Maintain contact between your back and the bench
instructions: Lie back on an Incline Bench. Using a medium width grip (a grip that is slightly wider than shoulder width), lift the bar from the rack and hold it straight over you with your arms straight. This will be your starting position.
While keeping the arms straight, lift the bar by protracting your shoulder blades, raising the shoulders from the bench as you breathe out.
Bring back the bar to the starting position as you breathe in.
Repeat for the recommended amount of repetitions.
exercises.name: Barbell Incline Shoulder Raise
exercises.name_pt: Elevação de Ombro Inclinada com Barra
exercises.overview: The barbell incline shoulder raise is a specialized exercise that targets the serratus anterior muscle responsible for scapular protraction. It's excellent for shoulder health and improving pressing mechanics.
exercises.safetyInfo: This is typically performed with light weight. Focus on movement quality over load. Avoid if you have shoulder impingement or instability issues.

commonMistakes: Allowing the bar to drift away from the body
Favoring one side due to the asymmetrical grip
Not achieving full hip extension at the top
exerciseTips: Keep the bar close to your body throughout the lift
Maintain an upright torso position
Focus on driving through both heels equally
Switch your grip orientation between sets to balance development
instructions: Straddle a barbell with one foot on each side of the bar.
Squat down and grasp the bar with a mixed grip, one hand in front and one behind your body.
Keep your chest up and your back in a neutral position.
Drive through your heels and extend your hips and knees to lift the bar.
Stand tall with the bar between your legs, squeezing your glutes at the top.
Lower the bar with control by reversing the movement pattern.
exercises.name: Barbell Jefferson Deadlift
exercises.overview: The barbell Jefferson deadlift is an unconventional deadlift variation where you straddle the barbell. This unique position emphasizes the quadriceps more than traditional deadlifts while providing a significant core challenge due to the asymmetrical loading.
exercises.safetyInfo: This is an advanced lift requiring excellent mobility and coordination. The asymmetrical nature can create imbalances, so alternate grip positions regularly. Those with hip or back issues should avoid this exercise.

commonMistakes: Standing too close to the anchor point limiting range of motion
Pressing straight out instead of following the natural arc
Allowing the lower back to excessively arch
exerciseTips: Keep your core engaged to prevent excessive back extension
Press in a slight upward arc following the natural path
Maintain tension in your chest throughout the full range
Step into the movement with your whole body
instructions: Secure one end of the barbell in a landmine attachment or corner, and load the opposite end with weight plates.
Stand facing away from the anchor point and grasp the weighted end with both hands at chest level.
Step into a split stance for stability with the barbell positioned at your sternum.
Press the barbell forward and slightly upward in an arc motion until your arms are fully extended.
Slowly return the weight to the starting position at your chest.
Maintain core tension throughout the entire movement.
exercises.name: Barbell Landmine Chest Press
exercises.overview: The Barbell Landmine Chest Press is a compound upper body exercise that targets the chest muscles through a unique angled pressing motion. It provides a shoulder-friendly alternative to traditional bench pressing while engaging the core for stability.
exercises.safetyInfo: Ensure the barbell is properly secured in the landmine attachment. Avoid if you have shoulder impingement or lower back issues. Start with lighter weight to master the movement pattern.

commonMistakes: Allowing the torso to rotate during the press
Using too much weight and compromising form
Pressing straight up instead of following the natural arc
exerciseTips: Keep your core tight throughout the movement to prevent rotation
Press the barbell in a slight arc rather than straight up
Maintain a neutral spine and avoid leaning backward
Control the descent to maximize muscle engagement
instructions: Set up a barbell in a landmine attachment or secure one end in a corner.
Stand facing the barbell with feet shoulder-width apart and grab the free end with one hand.
Position the barbell at shoulder height with your elbow bent and core engaged.
Press the barbell up and forward in an arc motion until your arm is fully extended.
Slowly lower the barbell back to the starting position at shoulder height.
Complete all reps on one side before switching arms.
exercises.name: Barbell Landmine Press Single Arm
exercises.overview: The barbell landmine press is a unilateral shoulder exercise that challenges core stability while building pressing strength. The angled bar path reduces shoulder stress compared to traditional overhead presses.
exercises.safetyInfo: Those with shoulder impingement or rotator cuff injuries should avoid this exercise. Start with lighter weight to master the movement pattern before progressing.

commonMistakes: Using arm strength instead of core rotation to move the weight
Rotating too far and losing control of the movement
Allowing the hips to rotate excessively instead of isolating the core
exerciseTips: Keep your arms straight and move from your core, not your arms
Maintain a stable base with your feet planted throughout the movement
Engage your core to control the rotation and prevent over-twisting
Keep your hips facing forward while allowing your torso to rotate
instructions: Set up a barbell in a landmine attachment or secure one end in a corner.
Hold the free end of the barbell with both hands at chest level, arms extended.
Stand with feet shoulder-width apart and maintain a slight bend in your knees.
Rotate the barbell from side to side in an arc motion, engaging your core throughout.
Control the movement and return to center before rotating to the opposite side.
exercises.name: Barbell Landmine Rotation
exercises.overview: Barbell landmine rotations train rotational core strength and stability through a natural arc of motion. This exercise improves functional core strength for sports and daily activities.
exercises.safetyInfo: Start with lighter weight to master the movement pattern. Those with lower back issues should perform this exercise with caution and reduced range of motion.

commonMistakes: Not using leg momentum to assist the press
Allowing the barbell to drift away from the body
Pausing between the squat and press instead of flowing
exerciseTips: Keep the barbell close to your body throughout
Use the leg drive to help initiate the press
Maintain a neutral spine during both movements
Control the descent of both the squat and the press
instructions: Set up a barbell in a landmine attachment or corner and hold the free end with both hands at chest level.
Stand with feet shoulder-width apart and the barbell positioned close to your chest.
Perform a squat by pushing your hips back and bending your knees.
As you drive up from the squat, use the momentum to press the barbell overhead.
Lower the barbell back to chest level as you descend into the next squat.
Repeat the fluid squat-to-press movement for the desired repetitions.
exercises.name: Barbell Landmine Squat To Press
exercises.overview: The barbell landmine squat to press is a compound movement that combines a squat with an overhead press. This full-body exercise builds functional strength and power while improving coordination between upper and lower body movements.
exercises.safetyInfo: This exercise combines two movements and requires good coordination and mobility. Those with shoulder or back issues should master each component separately first. Start with lighter weights than you would use for individual exercises.

commonMistakes: Allowing the knee to cave inward on the working leg
Leaning the torso too far forward
Lifting the foot of the straight leg off the ground
exerciseTips: Keep your chest up and spine neutral throughout the movement
Push your hips back as you descend laterally
Keep the straight leg's foot planted and pointed forward
Drive through your heel to return to center
instructions: Position a barbell across your upper back in the high bar position.
Stand with feet wider than shoulder-width apart, toes pointing slightly outward.
Shift your weight to one side and push your hips back as you lower into a lateral lunge.
Keep the opposite leg straight as you descend until your thigh is parallel to the floor.
Push through the heel of the bent leg to return to the starting position.
Alternate sides or complete all reps on one side before switching.
exercises.name: Barbell Lateral Lunge
exercises.overview: The barbell lateral lunge targets the quadriceps and glutes while emphasizing the frontal plane of movement. This exercise improves hip mobility and strengthens muscles often neglected in traditional forward-backward movements.
exercises.safetyInfo: Avoid if you have hip, knee, or ankle mobility restrictions. Start with bodyweight to assess range of motion before adding load.

commonMistakes: Placing the bar too high, closer to the neck
Not creating enough upper back tightness to support the bar
Trying to stay too upright, causing forward bar drift
exerciseTips: Create a tight shelf with your rear delts and traps for bar placement
Maintain forward lean of the torso to keep the bar over mid-foot
Drive your knees out over your toes throughout the movement
Focus on hip drive when ascending from the bottom position
instructions: Set the barbell lower on your back, resting on the rear deltoids and upper traps shelf.
Grip the bar with hands wider than high bar position and create a tight upper back shelf.
Unrack the bar and step back, positioning feet slightly wider than shoulder-width apart.
Initiate the squat by pushing your hips back first, then bending at the knees.
Descend until your hip crease is just below your knee cap, then drive up through your heels.
exercises.name: Barbell Low Bar Back Squat
exercises.overview: The low bar back squat places the barbell lower on the back, allowing for greater hip involvement and typically heavier loads. It's the preferred squat variation in powerlifting competition due to its mechanical advantages.
exercises.safetyInfo: This advanced variation requires excellent shoulder flexibility and upper back strength. Not recommended for beginners. Use safety bars and consider working with an experienced coach to learn proper bar placement.

commonMistakes: Allowing the front knee to travel past the toes
Leaning the torso too far forward
Taking too short of a step forward
exerciseTips: Keep your torso upright throughout the movement
Step forward with a stride length that allows your front thigh to reach parallel
Drive through your front heel to return to starting position
Maintain equal weight distribution between both legs
instructions: This exercise is best performed inside a squat rack for safety purposes. To begin, first set the bar on a rack just below shoulder level. Once the correct height is chosen and the bar is loaded, step under the bar and place the back of your shoulders (slightly below the neck) across it.
Hold on to the bar using both arms at each side and lift it off the rack by first pushing with your legs and at the same time straightening your torso.

Step away from the rack and step forward with your right leg and squat down through your hips, while keeping the torso upright and maintaining balance. Inhale as you go down. Note: Do not allow your knee to go forward beyond your toes as you come down, as this will put undue stress on the knee joint. li>
Using mainly the heel of your foot, push up and go back to the starting position as you exhale.
Repeat the movement for the recommended amount of repetitions and then perform with the left leg.
exercises.name: Barbell Lunge
exercises.overview: The barbell lunge is a unilateral lower body exercise that builds leg strength, improves balance, and addresses muscle imbalances between legs.
exercises.safetyInfo: Avoid this exercise if you have knee injuries or balance issues. Use a squat rack for safety and consider starting with bodyweight lunges before adding load.

commonMistakes: Allowing the barbell to drift forward or backward overhead
Taking steps that are too large, causing instability
Letting the arms bend or shoulders shrug up toward the ears
exerciseTips: Keep your arms locked out and actively push up into the barbell
Engage your core throughout to maintain spinal stability
Take small steps to maintain balance and control
Look straight ahead, not up at the barbell
instructions: Clean or press a barbell overhead with arms fully extended and hands positioned wider than shoulder-width.
Lock your arms out completely and engage your core to create a stable base.
Begin walking forward with small, controlled steps while keeping the barbell directly overhead.
Maintain an upright torso and avoid leaning forward or backward during the carry.
Complete the desired distance or time, then safely lower the barbell back to the starting position.
exercises.name: Barbell Overhead Carry
exercises.name_pt: Caminhada com Barra Acima da Cabeça
exercises.overview: The Barbell Overhead Carry is an advanced loaded carry exercise that builds shoulder stability, core strength, and total-body coordination. It challenges your ability to maintain position while moving, making it excellent for functional strength development.
exercises.safetyInfo: This exercise requires excellent shoulder mobility and stability. Those with shoulder impingement or overhead movement restrictions should avoid this exercise. Start with lighter weights and shorter distances to build capacity gradually.

commonMistakes: Arching the back excessively to assist with the press
Pressing the bar forward instead of straight up
Lowering the bar too far below shoulder level
exerciseTips: Keep your back firmly pressed against the bench throughout the movement
Press the bar in a straight line directly overhead
Maintain a tight grip and stable shoulder position
Control the lowering phase to maximize muscle engagement
instructions: Set up a bench with back support at about 85-90 degrees and position a barbell at shoulder height in a rack.
Sit on the bench with your back firmly against the pad and feet flat on the floor.
Grip the barbell with hands slightly wider than shoulder-width apart and lift it to shoulder level.
Keep your core tight and press the barbell straight up until your arms are fully extended overhead.
Lower the barbell back to shoulder level with control, maintaining tension in your shoulders.
Repeat the pressing motion for the desired number of repetitions.
exercises.name: Barbell Overhead Press Seated
exercises.overview: The seated barbell overhead press is an intermediate compound exercise that targets the shoulder muscles while providing back support for stability. This variation allows for heavier loading than standing versions while reducing core fatigue.
exercises.safetyInfo: Those with shoulder impingement or lower back issues should use lighter weights and ensure proper bench setup. The seated position reduces core involvement but requires good shoulder mobility for safe execution.

commonMistakes: Allowing the barbell to drift forward or backward overhead
Taking too small of a step backward
Leaning the torso forward during the lunge
exerciseTips: Keep the barbell directly overhead throughout the movement
Maintain an upright torso and avoid leaning forward
Step back far enough to achieve proper lunge depth
Keep your core engaged to stabilize the overhead load
instructions: Hold a barbell with both hands pressed overhead, arms fully extended.
Stand with feet hip-width apart and maintain an upright torso.
Step backward with your right leg into a reverse lunge position.
Lower until both knees reach approximately 90 degrees.
Push through your front heel to return to the starting position.
Alternate legs or complete all reps on one side before switching.
exercises.name: Barbell Overhead Reverse Lunge
exercises.name_pt: Afundo Reverso com Barra Acima da Cabeça
exercises.overview: The barbell overhead reverse lunge is an advanced exercise combining lower body strength with overhead stability. It challenges mobility, stability, and coordination while building functional strength patterns.
exercises.safetyInfo: Requires excellent shoulder mobility and core stability. Master overhead squats and regular reverse lunges before combining movements. Avoid if you have shoulder, lower back, or knee issues.

commonMistakes: Allowing the barbell to drift forward or backward
Taking steps that are too small
Rushing through the movement without control
exerciseTips: Keep the barbell directly overhead throughout the entire walk
Maintain an upright torso and avoid leaning forward
Take large enough steps to achieve proper lunge depth
Keep your core tight to stabilize the overhead load
instructions: Hold a barbell overhead with arms fully extended and hands wider than shoulder-width.
Stand tall with feet hip-width apart and core engaged.
Step forward with your right leg into a lunge position.
Lower until both knees reach approximately 90 degrees while keeping the barbell overhead.
Push through your front heel and bring your back leg forward into the next lunge.
Continue walking forward, alternating legs for the prescribed distance or repetitions.
exercises.name: Barbell Overhead Walking Lunge
exercises.name_pt: Afundo Caminhando com Barra Acima da Cabeça
exercises.overview: The barbell overhead walking lunge is an advanced full-body exercise that combines lower body strength with overhead stability and dynamic balance. It builds functional strength and challenges multiple systems simultaneously.
exercises.safetyInfo: Requires exceptional shoulder mobility, core stability, and coordination. Master stationary overhead lunges before attempting the walking variation. Avoid with shoulder, back, or knee issues.

commonMistakes: Losing back tightness during the pause
Allowing the bar to drift away from the body
Relaxing grip strength during the pause
exerciseTips: Maintain tension during the pause phase
Keep the bar close to your body throughout
Engage your core before initiating the pull
Focus on driving through your heels
instructions: Set up in conventional deadlift position with barbell over mid-foot.
Grip the bar with hands just outside your legs and engage your lats.
Pull the bar off the floor and pause when it reaches mid-shin or knee level.
Hold the pause for 2-3 seconds while maintaining proper form.
Complete the lift by driving hips forward to full extension.
Lower the bar with control back to the starting position.
exercises.name: Barbell Pause Deadlift
exercises.name_pt: Levantamento Terra com Pausa com Barra
exercises.overview: The barbell pause deadlift is an advanced deadlift variation that builds strength at specific sticking points and improves overall deadlift technique. The pause eliminates momentum and forces greater muscle recruitment.
exercises.safetyInfo: This advanced variation requires excellent deadlift technique. Those with lower back issues should master conventional deadlifts first. Use significantly less weight than your regular deadlift max.

commonMistakes: Starting with the bar too far forward away from the body
Hyperextending the back at the top of the movement
Not fully resetting between reps and bouncing off the pins
exerciseTips: Squeeze your shoulder blades together before lifting
Drive your hips forward aggressively at the top of the movement
Keep the bar close to your body throughout the entire range
Reset your position completely between each repetition
instructions: Set up a barbell on safety pins in a squat rack at knee height or just below.
Position yourself with the bar over mid-foot and feet hip-width apart.
Hinge at hips and grip the bar with hands just outside your legs.
Engage your lats and create tension before initiating the pull.
Drive through your heels and extend hips to lift the bar to full lockout.
Lower the bar back to the pins with control and reset for the next repetition.
exercises.name: Barbell Pin Deadlift
exercises.name_pt: Levantamento Terra nos Pinos com Barra
exercises.overview: The pin deadlift starts from an elevated position, focusing on the top portion of the deadlift movement. This variation helps build lockout strength and allows for heavier loading while reducing range of motion stress.
exercises.safetyInfo: This exercise allows for heavier loading than full deadlifts, so ensure proper form is maintained. Those with lower back issues should start with conservative weights.

commonMistakes: Rolling the shoulders instead of shrugging straight up
Bending the arms and turning it into an upright row
Using too much weight and compromising the explosive nature
exerciseTips: Keep your arms completely straight throughout the movement
Focus on explosive upward movement followed by controlled descent
Shrug straight up rather than rolling shoulders forward or backward
Maintain a tight core to support your spine under load
instructions: Stand with feet hip-width apart holding a barbell with an overhand grip at arm's length.
Keep your arms straight and maintain a neutral spine with shoulders back.
Explosively shrug your shoulders straight up toward your ears as high as possible.
Focus on speed and power during the upward movement.
Control the descent back to the starting position.
Reset briefly before performing the next explosive repetition.
exercises.name: Barbell Power Shrug
exercises.overview: The barbell power shrug is an explosive variation of the traditional shrug that develops trap power and strength. This exercise is commonly used by athletes to improve pulling power and upper back development.
exercises.safetyInfo: Those with neck or upper back injuries should use lighter weight and focus on proper form. Avoid excessive forward head posture during the movement.

commonMistakes: Fully extending the arms at the bottom, which can stress the elbow joint
Lifting the upper arms off the preacher pad during the movement
Using too much weight and compromising form or range of motion
exerciseTips: Keep your wrists straight and avoid letting them bend backward
Maintain contact between your upper arms and the preacher pad
Control the negative portion to maximize muscle activation
Stop just short of full elbow extension to maintain tension
instructions: Sit at a preacher bench and adjust the height so your armpits rest at the top of the pad.
Grasp the barbell with an underhand grip, hands shoulder-width apart.
Place your upper arms flat against the preacher pad with a slight bend in your elbows at the bottom.
Curl the barbell up by contracting your biceps until your forearms are vertical.
Slowly lower the weight back to the starting position, maintaining control throughout.
exercises.name: Barbell Preacher Curl Seated
exercises.overview: The seated barbell preacher curl isolates the biceps by eliminating momentum and stabilizing the upper arms. It's excellent for building bicep peak and strength.
exercises.safetyInfo: Avoid locking out your elbows completely at the bottom to prevent hyperextension injury. Those with elbow issues should use lighter weight and focus on form.

commonMistakes: Setting the pins too high and reducing the effective range of motion
Rounding the shoulders and losing lat engagement
Hyperextending at the top instead of achieving neutral lockout
exerciseTips: Set the pins at a height that challenges your weak point
Keep your chest up and shoulders back throughout the movement
Focus on driving your hips forward at the top of the lift
Maintain a tight grip and engaged lats during the entire pull
instructions: Set up a barbell on safety pins in a squat rack at knee height.
Stand with feet hip-width apart and the bar positioned over mid-foot.
Hinge at your hips and grasp the bar with hands just outside your legs.
Engage your lats and create tension throughout your posterior chain.
Drive through your heels and extend your hips to pull the bar to lockout.
Lower the bar back to the pins with control and reset for the next rep.
exercises.name: Barbell Rack Pull
exercises.overview: The rack pull focuses on the top portion of the deadlift movement, allowing for heavier loading and specific strength development. This exercise helps build lockout strength and targets the posterior chain with reduced range of motion.
exercises.safetyInfo: This variation allows for heavier loads than full deadlifts, so maintain proper form despite the increased weight capacity. Those with lower back issues should start conservatively.

commonMistakes: Allowing the bar to drift away from the body
Hyperextending the lower back at the top of the movement
Using primarily arm strength instead of hip drive
exerciseTips: Keep the bar close to your body throughout the entire movement
Drive through your heels and focus on hip extension
Squeeze your lats to keep the bar path straight
Maintain tension in your upper back throughout the lift
instructions: Set up a barbell in a power rack with the bar positioned just below knee height.
Stand with feet hip-width apart and grip the bar with hands slightly wider than shoulder-width.
Maintain a neutral spine with chest up and shoulder blades retracted.
Pull the bar up by driving your hips forward and extending your knees until you reach full hip extension.
Squeeze your glutes and pull your shoulder blades together at the top of the movement.
Lower the bar back to the starting position with control until it touches the rack pins.
exercises.name: Barbell Rack Pull Below Knee
exercises.name_pt: Rack Pull com Barra Abaixo do Joelho
exercises.overview: The barbell rack pull below knee is a partial range deadlift that allows for heavier loading while focusing on the lockout portion of the movement. This exercise builds tremendous posterior chain strength and improves deadlift performance.
exercises.safetyInfo: This exercise involves heavy loading and requires proper deadlift technique. Those with lower back injuries should avoid this movement. Ensure proper warm-up and start with lighter weights to establish form.

commonMistakes: Using biceps instead of rear delts to pull
Allowing elbows to drift toward the body
Rounding the back during the movement
exerciseTips: Keep your elbows flared perpendicular to your torso
Focus on squeezing your shoulder blades together
Maintain a neutral spine throughout the movement
Pull the bar to your upper chest level
instructions: Stand up straight while holding a barbell using a wide (higher than shoulder width) and overhand (palms facing your body) grip.

Bend knees slightly and bend over as you keep the natural arch of your back. Let the arms hang in front of you as they hold the bar. Once your torso is parallel to the floor, flare the elbows out and away from your body. Tip: Your torso and your arms should resemble the letter "T". Now you are ready to begin the exercise.

While keeping the upper arms perpendicular to the torso, pull the barbell up towards your upper chest as you squeeze the rear delts and you breathe out. Tip: When performed correctly, this exercise should resemble a bench press in reverse. Also, refrain from using your biceps to do the work. Focus on targeting the rear delts; the arms should only act as hooks.
Slowly go back to the initial position as you breathe in.
Repeat for the recommended amount of repetitions.
exercises.name: Barbell Rear Delt Row
exercises.name_pt: Remada para Deltoide Posterior com Barra
exercises.overview: The barbell rear delt row targets the often-neglected posterior deltoids while improving posture and shoulder stability.
exercises.safetyInfo: Maintain proper hip hinge position to protect your lower back. Start with lighter weight to master the movement pattern.
variations: Bent Over Two-Dumbbell Row With Palms In

commonMistakes: Using too much weight and allowing the wrists to bend backward
Swinging the body or using momentum to lift the weight
Letting the elbows drift forward or away from the body
exerciseTips: Keep your wrists in a strong, neutral position throughout the movement
Focus on slow, controlled movements to maximize muscle activation
Maintain tension in your forearms and avoid letting the bar roll in your hands
Keep your elbows stationary and close to your torso
instructions: Stand with feet hip-width apart, holding a barbell with an overhand grip, hands shoulder-width apart.
Keep your elbows close to your sides and maintain an upright posture.
Curl the barbell up by flexing at the elbows while keeping your wrists straight and strong.
Lower the barbell back to the starting position with control, maintaining the overhand grip.
exercises.name: Barbell Reverse Curl
exercises.overview: The barbell reverse curl is a variation of the traditional bicep curl using an overhand grip that primarily targets the forearm extensors and brachioradialis. It's excellent for developing balanced arm strength and improving grip strength.
exercises.safetyInfo: Start with significantly lighter weight than regular bicep curls as this exercise places more stress on the smaller forearm muscles. Those with wrist or elbow issues should proceed cautiously.

commonMistakes: Letting the front knee drift forward past the toes
Leaning the torso too far forward under the bar
Taking too short a step back and cutting the range of motion short
Pushing off the back foot instead of driving through the front heel
exerciseTips: Keep your torso upright and let the bar sit stable across your upper back
Step back far enough that both knees reach roughly 90 degrees
Keep your weight on the front leg and drive through the front heel to stand
Control the descent instead of dropping into the bottom position
instructions: Set up a barbell in a squat rack just below shoulder height, step under it, and rest the bar across your upper back (below the neck), holding it with both hands.
Stand tall with feet hip-width apart, brace your core, and step back from the rack into an open space.
Step one foot straight back, landing on the ball of your back foot, and lower until both knees are bent to about 90 degrees.
Keep your front knee stacked over your ankle and your torso upright as you descend.
Drive through your front heel to return to a tall standing position. Complete all reps on one leg before switching, or alternate legs each rep.
exercises.name: Barbell Reverse Lunge
exercises.overview: The barbell reverse lunge is a unilateral lower-body strength exercise in which you step backward into a lunge with a barbell loaded across your upper back. Stepping back rather than forward keeps the front shin more vertical and tends to be easier on the knee, making it a joint-friendly way to build single-leg strength, balance, and quad and glute size.
exercises.safetyInfo: Set up inside a squat rack and start light to groove the pattern, since the back-loaded bar raises the balance demand. The reverse step is generally easier on the front knee than a forward lunge, but anyone with knee or balance issues should master bodyweight reverse lunges first. Make sure the space behind you is clear before stepping back.

commonMistakes: Allowing the lower back to arch excessively
Using momentum instead of controlled movement
Rolling out too far beyond your strength capacity
exerciseTips: Keep your arms straight throughout the entire movement
Control the eccentric portion slowly
Maintain a neutral spine and avoid arching your back
Only roll out as far as you can control the return
instructions: Place a loaded barbell on the ground, near the end of a bench. Kneel with both legs on the bench, and take a medium to narrow grip on the barbell. This will be your starting position.
To begin, extend through the hips to slowly roll the bar forward. As you roll out, flex the shoulder to roll the bar above your head. Ensure that your arms remain extended throughout the movement.
When the bar has been moved as far forward as possible, return to the starting position.
exercises.name: Barbell Rollout from Bench
exercises.name_pt: Rollout com Barra a Partir do Banco
exercises.overview: The barbell rollout from bench is an advanced core exercise that challenges anti-extension strength and shoulder stability simultaneously.
exercises.safetyInfo: This exercise places significant stress on the core and shoulders. Avoid if you have lower back issues and progress gradually from shorter ranges of motion.

commonMistakes: Bending the knees too much and turning it into a squat
Allowing the bar to drift away from the body
Rounding the back instead of hinging at the hips
exerciseTips: Focus on pushing your hips back rather than bending your knees
Keep the bar in contact with your legs throughout
Feel the stretch in your hamstrings at the bottom
Drive through your heels to return to standing
instructions: Hold a barbell with an overhand grip, hands shoulder-width apart, standing with feet hip-width apart.
Start with the bar at hip level with your knees slightly bent and shoulders back.
Push your hips back and lower the bar by hinging at the hips, keeping it close to your legs.
Lower until you feel a stretch in your hamstrings, typically to mid-shin level.
Drive your hips forward to return to the starting position, squeezing your glutes at the top.
Maintain a neutral spine and keep the bar close to your body throughout the movement.
exercises.name: Barbell Romanian Deadlift
exercises.overview: The Romanian deadlift is a hip-hinge movement that primarily targets the hamstrings and glutes. It teaches proper posterior chain activation and is fundamental for athletic performance and injury prevention.
exercises.safetyInfo: Those with lower back injuries should avoid heavy loading and focus on perfect hip hinge mechanics. Ensure adequate hamstring flexibility before attempting full range of motion.

commonMistakes: Bouncing at the bottom of the movement
Not achieving full range of motion
Placing the barbell too close to the knees
exerciseTips: Place the weight on your thighs just above your knees
Rise up as high as possible on your toes
Hold the peak contraction for one second
Control the descent to get a full stretch
instructions: Place a block about 12 inches in front of a flat bench.
Sit on the bench and place the ball of your feet on the block.
Have someone place a barbell over your upper thighs about 3 inches above your knees and hold it there. This will be your starting position.
Raise up on your toes as high as possible as you squeeze the calves and as you breathe out.
After a second contraction, slowly go back to the starting position. Tip: To get maximum benefit stretch your calves as far as you can.
Repeat for the recommended amount of repetitions.
exercises.name: Barbell Seated Calf Raise
exercises.overview: The barbell seated calf raise specifically targets the soleus muscle, which is better activated in a seated position compared to standing calf exercises.
exercises.safetyInfo: Ensure the barbell is securely positioned and have a spotter help place and remove the weight. Start with lighter loads to assess comfort.
variations: Smith Machine Calf Raise Single Leg

commonMistakes: Pressing the bar too far forward
Arching the back excessively
Not lowering the bar to a consistent depth
exerciseTips: Keep your core tight and back pressed against the pad
Press the bar in a straight line overhead
Lower the bar to shoulder level with control
Drive through your heels while pressing
instructions: Sit on a bench with back support in a squat rack. Position a barbell at a height that is just above your head. Grab the barbell with a pronated grip (palms facing forward).

Once you pick up the barbell with the correct grip width, lift the bar up over your head by locking your arms. Hold at about shoulder level and slightly in front of your head. This is your starting position.
Lower the bar down to the shoulders slowly as you inhale.
Lift the bar back up to the starting position as you exhale.
Repeat for the recommended amount of repetitions.
exercises.name: Barbell Shoulder Press
exercises.overview: The barbell shoulder press is a fundamental upper body pressing movement that builds shoulder strength, stability, and pressing power.
exercises.safetyInfo: Use a squat rack with safety bars set appropriately. Avoid this exercise if you have shoulder impingement or neck issues.

commonMistakes: Rolling the shoulders instead of lifting straight up
Using the arms to lift the weight
Not achieving full range of motion
exerciseTips: Lift your shoulders straight up toward your ears
Hold the peak contraction for one second
Keep your arms straight throughout the movement
Focus on moving only your shoulder blades
instructions: Stand up straight with your feet at shoulder width as you hold a barbell with both hands in front of you using a pronated grip (palms facing the thighs). Tip: Your hands should be a little wider than shoulder width apart. You can use wrist wraps for this exercise for a better grip. This will be your starting position.
Raise your shoulders up as far as you can go as you breathe out and hold the contraction for a second. Tip: Refrain from trying to lift the barbell by using your biceps.
Slowly return to the starting position as you breathe in.
Repeat for the recommended amount of repetitions.
exercises.name: Barbell Shrug
exercises.overview: The barbell shrug is an isolation exercise that specifically targets the upper trapezius muscles to build neck and upper back strength.
exercises.safetyInfo: Use wrist straps if grip strength is limiting. Avoid excessive weight that causes forward head posture or neck strain.

commonMistakes: Using biceps to lift the weight instead of traps
Rolling shoulders forward or backward during the movement
Taking too wide of a grip causing shoulder impingement
exerciseTips: Keep arms straight throughout the movement
Focus on squeezing shoulder blades together at the top
Hold the peak contraction for 1-2 seconds
Use a controlled tempo on both up and down phases
instructions: Stand up straight with your feet at shoulder width as you hold a barbell with both hands behind your back using a pronated grip (palms facing back). Tip: Your hands should be a little wider than shoulder width apart. You can use wrist wraps for this exercise for better grip. This will be your starting position.

Raise your shoulders up as far as you can go as you breathe out and hold the contraction for a second. Tip: Refrain from trying to lift the barbell by using your biceps. The arms should remain stretched out at all times.
Slowly return to the starting position as you breathe in.
Repeat for the recommended amount of repetitions.
exercises.name: Barbell Shrug Behind The Back
exercises.name_pt: Encolhimento com Barra por Trás do Corpo
exercises.overview: The behind-the-back barbell shrug targets the upper trapezius from a unique angle while minimizing upper trap dominance. This variation emphasizes the middle traps and rhomboids more than traditional front shrugs.
exercises.safetyInfo: Avoid if you have shoulder impingement or rotator cuff injuries. The behind-the-back position can stress the shoulder joint in individuals with limited shoulder mobility.

commonMistakes: Bending forward or backward instead of purely to the side
Using momentum or bouncing at the bottom of the movement
exerciseTips: Keep your back straight and avoid leaning forward or backward
Bend only at the waist in a pure lateral plane
Control the movement and avoid bouncing at the bottom
Keep your head in neutral position throughout
instructions: Stand up straight while holding a barbell placed on the back of your shoulders (slightly below the neck). Your feet should be shoulder width apart. This will be your starting position.

While keeping your back straight and your head up, bend only at the waist to the right as far as possible. Breathe in as you bend to the side. Then hold for a second and come back up to the starting position as you exhale. Tip: Keep the rest of the body stationary.
Now repeat the movement but bending to the left instead. Hold for a second and come back to the starting position.
Repeat for the recommended amount of repetitions.
exercises.name: Barbell Side Bend
exercises.overview: The barbell side bend is an isolation exercise that targets the obliques through lateral spinal flexion. It helps develop core strength and definition in the side abdominal muscles.
exercises.safetyInfo: This exercise can place stress on the spine, particularly for those with lower back issues. The loaded spine in lateral flexion may aggravate disc problems.

commonMistakes: Allowing the knee to cave inward on the working leg
Leaning too far forward during the descent
Not achieving adequate depth on the working side
exerciseTips: Keep most weight on the heel of the working leg
Maintain an upright torso throughout the movement
Push through the heel to return to starting position
Keep the non-working leg relatively straight
instructions: Stand up straight while holding a barbell placed on the back of your shoulders (slightly below the neck). Your feet should be placed wide apart with the foot of the lead leg angled out to the side. This will be your starting position.
Lower your body towards the side of your angled foot by bending the knee and hip of your lead leg and while keeping the opposite leg only slightly bent. Breathe in as you lower your body.
Return to the starting position by extending the hip and knee of the lead leg. Breathe out as you perform this movement.
After performing the recommended amount of reps, repeat the movement with the opposite leg.
exercises.name: Barbell Side Split Squat
exercises.overview: The barbell side split squat is a unilateral exercise that emphasizes the quadriceps while working in the frontal plane. It helps develop single-leg strength and hip stability.
exercises.safetyInfo: Requires good hip and ankle mobility. Those with knee problems should be cautious with the lateral loading pattern, which can stress the medial knee structures.

commonMistakes: Allowing the barbell to drift away from the body
Letting the knees cave inward during the squat
Not descending to adequate depth
exerciseTips: Keep your chest up and core braced throughout
Push your hips back to initiate the squat
Maintain the barbell position at chest level
Drive through your heels to stand up
instructions: Set up a barbell in a landmine attachment or corner with weight on one end.
Grasp the end of the barbell with both hands at chest level.
Stand with feet shoulder-width apart and toes slightly turned out.
Lower into a squat by pushing your hips back and bending your knees.
Descend until your thighs are parallel to the floor while keeping the bar at chest level.
Drive through your heels to return to the starting position.
exercises.name: Barbell Single Arm Landmine Squat
exercises.overview: The landmine squat provides a unique angle of resistance that's easier on the shoulders than traditional front squats. It challenges the core while allowing for a natural squatting pattern.
exercises.safetyInfo: Ensure the landmine setup is secure before beginning. Avoid if you have shoulder mobility restrictions or knee injuries.

commonMistakes: Arching the lower back excessively at the top position
Using the extended leg to assist with the movement
Rising too high and hyperextending the spine
exerciseTips: Keep your chin tucked and avoid hyperextending your neck
Focus on squeezing the glute of the working leg at the top
Maintain a straight line with your extended leg throughout
Drive through your heel rather than your toes
instructions: Sit with your upper back against a bench and a barbell positioned over your hips.
Plant your right foot firmly on the ground and extend your left leg straight out.
Drive through your right heel to lift your hips up, creating a straight line from knee to shoulder.
Squeeze your glutes at the top position and hold briefly.
Lower your hips with control back to the starting position.
Complete all reps on one leg before switching to the other leg.
exercises.name: Barbell Single Leg Hip Thrust
exercises.overview: The barbell single leg hip thrust is an advanced glute exercise that maximally activates the gluteus maximus while challenging stability. It's highly effective for building unilateral hip extension strength and power.
exercises.safetyInfo: Master the bilateral hip thrust before attempting the single leg variation. Use a pad to protect your hip bones from the barbell. Avoid if you have hip flexor tightness or lower back pain.

commonMistakes: Gripping too wide beyond flexibility limits
Allowing the bar to drift forward
Inadequate warm-up for the extreme grip width
exerciseTips: Use the widest comfortable grip possible
Keep your lats engaged to control the bar
Focus on hip drive to complete the movement
Maintain upper back tightness throughout
instructions: Stand with feet hip-width apart, barbell over mid-foot on the floor.
Grip the bar with a very wide overhand grip, arms nearly straight out to the sides.
Lower into position with chest up, shoulders back, and neutral spine.
Drive through your heels while extending hips and knees simultaneously.
Keep the bar path straight and close to your body throughout the lift.
Stand tall with full hip and knee extension, then lower with control.
exercises.name: Barbell Snatch Grip Deadlift
exercises.name_pt: Levantamento Terra com Pegada de Snatch
exercises.overview: The barbell snatch grip deadlift uses an extremely wide grip that increases range of motion and heavily emphasizes the upper back and posterior chain. It's commonly used by Olympic lifters to build pulling strength for the snatch.
exercises.safetyInfo: This variation requires excellent shoulder and thoracic spine mobility. Those with shoulder injuries or limited mobility should avoid this exercise. The wide grip significantly increases range of motion and difficulty.

commonMistakes: Leaning too far forward
Pushing off the back foot instead of the front
Taking too narrow or too wide of a stance
exerciseTips: Keep your torso upright throughout the movement
Focus your weight on your front leg
Drive through your front heel when ascending
Maintain a consistent foot position between reps
instructions: Position a barbell on your upper back as you would for a back squat.
Step one foot forward into a lunge position with your back foot remaining stationary.
Keep most of your weight on your front leg with your torso upright.
Lower your body by bending your front knee until your thigh is parallel to the floor.
Push through your front heel to return to the starting position.
Complete all reps on one leg before switching to the other side.
keywords: barbell rear foot elevated split squat
exercises.name: Barbell Split Squat
exercises.overview: The barbell split squat is a unilateral leg exercise that builds single-leg strength and addresses muscle imbalances. The barbell loading allows for progressive overload while challenging balance and stability.
exercises.safetyInfo: This exercise places significant load on the spine and should be avoided by those with back injuries. Ensure proper barbell positioning and start with lighter weights than your regular squat.

commonMistakes: Allowing knees to cave inward during the ascent
Not reaching proper depth with hips below knees
Shifting weight to toes instead of keeping it on heels
exerciseTips: Drive through your heels during the ascent
Keep your chest up and maintain a neutral spine
Descend until your hip crease is below your kneecap
Engage your core throughout the entire movement
instructions: This exercise is best performed inside a squat rack for safety purposes. To begin, first set the bar on a rack to just below shoulder level. Once the correct height is chosen and the bar is loaded, step under the bar and place the back of your shoulders (slightly below the neck) across it.
Hold on to the bar using both arms at each side and lift it off the rack by first pushing with your legs and at the same time straightening your torso.

Step away from the rack and position your legs using a shoulder width medium stance with the toes slightly pointed out. Keep your head up at all times and also maintain a straight back. This will be your starting position. (Note: For the purposes of this discussion we will use the medium stance described above which targets overall development; however you can choose any of the three stances discussed in the foot stances section).

Begin to slowly lower the bar by bending the knees and hips as you maintain a straight posture with the head up. Continue down until the angle between the upper leg and the calves becomes slightly less than 90-degrees. Inhale as you perform this portion of the movement. Tip: If you performed the exercise correctly, the front of the knees should make an imaginary straight line with the toes that is perpendicular to the front. If your knees are past that imaginary line (if they are past your toes) then you are placing undue stress on the knee and the exercise has been performed incorrectly.
Begin to raise the bar as you exhale by pushing the floor with the heel of your foot as you straighten the legs again and go back to the starting position.
Repeat for the recommended amount of repetitions.
exercises.name: Barbell Squat
exercises.overview: The barbell squat is a fundamental compound movement that targets the entire lower body while strengthening the core. It's considered one of the most effective exercises for building overall strength and muscle mass.
exercises.safetyInfo: Use a squat rack with safety bars set just below your lowest squat position. Avoid if you have knee or lower back injuries without proper rehabilitation and technique assessment.

commonMistakes: Fully relaxing and sitting down on the bench instead of lightly touching
Using the bench to bounce back up rather than driving with legs
exerciseTips: Sit back onto the bench rather than straight down
Pause briefly on the bench without fully relaxing
Drive explosively off the bench using your heels
Keep tension in your muscles even when touching the bench
instructions: This exercise is best performed inside a squat rack for safety purposes. To begin, first place a flat bench or a box behind you. The flat bench is used to teach you to set your hips back and to hit depth.

Then, set the bar on a rack that best matches your height. Once the correct height is chosen and the bar is loaded, step under the bar and place the back of your shoulders (slightly below the neck) across it.
Hold on to the bar using both arms at each side and lift it off the rack by first pushing with your legs and at the same time straightening your torso.

Step away from the rack and position your legs using a shoulder width medium stance with the toes slightly pointed out. Keep your head up at all times as looking down will get you off balance and also maintain a straight back. This will be your starting position. (Note: For the purposes of this discussion we will use the medium stance described above which targets overall development; however you can choose any of the three stances discussed in the foot stances section).

Begin to slowly lower the bar by bending the knees and sitting your hips back as you maintain a straight posture with the head up. Continue down until you slightly touch the bench behind you. Inhale as you perform this portion of the movement. Tip: If you performed the exercise correctly, the front of the knees should make an imaginary straight line with the toes that is perpendicular to the front. If your knees are past that imaginary line (if they are past your toes) then you are placing undue stress on the knee and the exercise has been performed incorrectly.
Begin to raise the bar as you exhale by pushing the floor with the heel of your foot as you straighten the legs and extend the hips to go back to the starting position.
Repeat for the recommended amount of repetitions.
exercises.name: Barbell Squat To A Bench
exercises.overview: The barbell squat to bench teaches proper squat depth and hip hinge mechanics while providing a depth reference. It's excellent for beginners learning squat technique and for experienced lifters working on consistency.
exercises.safetyInfo: Ensure the bench height allows for proper squat depth. The bench should be positioned so your hips reach parallel or slightly below when seated. Use appropriate safety measures as with regular squats.

commonMistakes: Using the bottom leg to push off and assist the movement
Leaning forward excessively during the step up
Stepping down too quickly without control
exerciseTips: Drive through the heel of the foot on the platform
Step down with control rather than jumping down
Keep your torso upright throughout the movement
Focus on the working leg doing all the work
instructions: Stand up straight while holding a barbell placed on the back of your shoulders (slightly below the neck) and stand upright behind an elevated platform (such as the one used for spotting behind a flat bench). This is your starting position.

Place the right foot on the elevated platform. Step on the platform by extending the hip and the knee of your right leg. Use the heel mainly to lift the rest of your body up and place the foot of the left leg on the platform as well. Breathe out as you execute the force required to come up.

Step down with the left leg by flexing the hip and knee of the right leg as you inhale. Return to the original standing position by placing the right foot of to next to the left foot on the initial position.
Repeat with the right leg for the recommended amount of repetitions and then perform with the left leg.
exercises.name: Barbell Step Ups
exercises.overview: Barbell step ups are a unilateral exercise that builds single-leg strength, balance, and coordination. They effectively target the quadriceps while improving functional movement patterns used in daily activities.
exercises.safetyInfo: Ensure the platform height allows your thigh to be parallel to the ground when your foot is placed on it. Use a stable, non-slip surface and consider starting with bodyweight before adding load.

commonMistakes: Rounding the back instead of maintaining a neutral spine
Bending the knees too much and turning it into a squat
Allowing the bar to drift away from the body
exerciseTips: Keep the barbell close to your body throughout the movement
Focus on pushing your hips back rather than bending forward
Maintain a neutral spine and avoid rounding your back
Feel the stretch in your hamstrings at the bottom position
instructions: Stand with feet hip-width apart holding a barbell with an overhand grip at hip level.
Keep a slight bend in your knees and maintain this angle throughout.
Hinge at the hips by pushing your hips back and lowering the barbell toward the floor.
Keep the bar close to your body and maintain a neutral spine.
Lower until you feel a stretch in your hamstrings or the bar reaches mid-shin level.
Drive your hips forward to return to the starting position.
exercises.name: Barbell Stiff Leg Deadlift
exercises.overview: The barbell stiff leg deadlift is a hip-hinge movement that primarily targets the hamstrings and glutes while strengthening the posterior chain. This exercise emphasizes the eccentric lengthening of the hamstrings.
exercises.safetyInfo: Avoid this exercise if you have lower back injuries. Start with lighter weight to master the hip hinge movement pattern before progressing to heavier loads.

commonMistakes: Allowing the knees to cave inward during the movement
Taking too wide a stance which limits depth
Leaning forward excessively instead of maintaining upright posture
exerciseTips: Keep your knees tracking in line with your toes throughout
Maintain an upright torso and avoid excessive forward lean
Push your knees out to engage your glutes and adductors
Focus on sitting back with your hips while descending
instructions: Set up a barbell on your upper back in a squat rack with feet wider than shoulder-width.
Point your toes out at approximately 45-degree angles.
Unrack the barbell and step back to clear the rack posts.
Lower your body by bending at the hips and knees, keeping knees tracking over toes.
Descend until your thighs are parallel to the floor or as deep as mobility allows.
Drive through your heels and extend hips and knees to return to the starting position.
exercises.name: Barbell Sumo Squat
exercises.overview: The barbell sumo squat is a wide-stance variation of the traditional squat that emphasizes the inner thighs and glutes while still targeting the quadriceps. The wider stance allows for a more upright torso position.
exercises.safetyInfo: The wide stance requires good hip and ankle mobility. Those with hip or knee issues should start with bodyweight to assess comfort and range of motion before adding load.

commonMistakes: Rounding the back during the pull
Using the legs to help lift the weight
Not achieving full range of motion
exerciseTips: Keep your core engaged to protect your lower back
Drive your elbows back rather than out wide
Squeeze your shoulder blades together at the top
Maintain a neutral spine throughout the movement
instructions: Position one end of a barbell in a corner or landmine attachment and load the other end with weight plates.
Straddle the bar and bend forward at the hips, keeping your back straight and knees slightly bent.
Grasp the bar close to the weight plates with both hands or use a T-bar handle attachment.
Pull the bar up toward your lower chest, driving your elbows back and squeezing your shoulder blades.
Lower the weight with control back to the starting position, feeling a stretch in your lats.
exercises.name: Barbell T-Bar Row
exercises.overview: The barbell T-bar row is a compound pulling exercise that effectively targets the lats and middle back. The neutral grip position often feels more natural than other rowing variations and allows for heavy loading.
exercises.safetyInfo: Those with lower back problems should use lighter weight or consider chest-supported alternatives. Proper hip hinge technique is crucial for safety.

commonMistakes: Rushing through the tempo portions
Allowing the bar to drift away from the body
Losing tightness during the pause at the top
exerciseTips: Control the eccentric lowering phase for full 3 seconds
Maintain tension throughout the entire movement
Keep the bar close to your body during both phases
Focus on perfect form rather than heavy weight
instructions: Set up in conventional deadlift position with barbell over mid-foot.
Grip bar with hands just outside legs, maintain neutral spine.
Drive through heels to lift bar, extending hips and knees simultaneously.
At top, pause for 1 second maintaining full hip extension.
Lower bar slowly over 3 seconds, controlling the eccentric phase.
Reset at bottom and repeat for prescribed repetitions.
exercises.name: Barbell Tempo Deadlift
exercises.overview: The barbell tempo deadlift emphasizes time under tension to build strength through controlled movement. The tempo component increases muscle activation and improves movement quality while building posterior chain strength.
exercises.safetyInfo: Use significantly lighter weight than normal deadlift due to increased time under tension. Ensure proper deadlift technique before adding tempo components.

commonMistakes: Losing tempo consistency and speeding up the descent
Relaxing at the bottom instead of maintaining muscle tension
Using too much weight and compromising the tempo
exerciseTips: Count the tempo in your head to maintain consistency
Keep your core braced throughout the extended descent
Maintain control and tension even during the slow lowering phase
Focus on proper knee tracking over your toes throughout
instructions: Set up with barbell on your upper back and feet shoulder-width apart.
Begin the descent slowly, taking 3-4 seconds to reach the bottom position.
Descend until your thighs are parallel to the floor or as deep as mobility allows.
Pause briefly at the bottom position maintaining tension.
Drive through your heels to return to standing position at normal speed.
Reset your breathing and repeat for the prescribed number of repetitions.
exercises.name: Barbell Tempo Squat
exercises.overview: The tempo squat emphasizes the eccentric portion of the squat, increasing time under tension and building strength through the full range of motion. This variation improves control, stability, and strength in the stretched position.
exercises.safetyInfo: Use significantly less weight than your normal squat due to the increased time under tension. Those with knee issues should be cautious with extended time in the bottom position.

commonMistakes: Using too narrow of a grip which can cause shoulder impingement
Pulling the bar too high above chest level
exerciseTips: Keep the barbell close to your body throughout the entire movement
Lead with your elbows and keep them higher than your wrists
Maintain an upright posture without leaning backward
Focus on lifting the elbows rather than the weight
instructions: Stand upright holding a barbell with an overhand grip, hands positioned wider than shoulder-width apart.
Let the barbell hang in front of your body with arms fully extended and feet hip-width apart.
Pull the barbell straight up along your body by lifting your elbows high and wide.
Continue pulling until the bar reaches chest level with your elbows higher than your wrists.
Pause briefly at the top, then slowly lower the barbell back to the starting position.
Maintain control throughout the descent and repeat for desired repetitions.
exercises.name: Barbell Upright Row Wide Grip
exercises.name_pt: Remada Alta com Barra - Pegada Aberta
exercises.overview: The wide grip barbell upright row targets the side delts and upper traps while building shoulder width and upper body strength. The wider grip reduces shoulder stress compared to narrow grip variations.
exercises.safetyInfo: Those with shoulder impingement or rotator cuff issues should avoid this exercise or use a wider grip. Stop immediately if you feel any shoulder pain during the movement.

commonMistakes: Allowing the front knee to cave inward
Taking steps that are too short, causing the knee to track over the toes
Leaning forward excessively during the descent
exerciseTips: Drive through your front heel to maximize quad activation
Keep your torso upright throughout the entire movement
Control the descent for 2-3 seconds to increase time under tension
Step far enough forward so your front shin remains vertical
instructions: Begin standing with your feet shoulder width apart and a barbell across your upper back.

Step forward with one leg, flexing the knees to drop your hips. Descend until your rear knee nearly touches the ground. Your posture should remain upright, and your front knee should stay above the front foot.
Drive through the heel of your lead foot and extend both knees to raise yourself back up.
Step forward with your rear foot, repeating the lunge on the opposite leg.
exercises.name: Barbell Walking Lunge
exercises.overview: The barbell walking lunge is a dynamic compound movement that builds unilateral leg strength while challenging balance and coordination. It's excellent for developing functional strength and addressing muscle imbalances between legs.
exercises.safetyInfo: Avoid this exercise if you have knee instability or ankle mobility issues. Start with bodyweight before adding a barbell to master the movement pattern.

commonMistakes: Gripping too wide causing shoulder strain
Allowing the bar to drift away from the body
Rounding the upper back excessively
exerciseTips: Keep the bar path straight and close to your body
Engage your lats to keep the bar tight
Drive your hips forward at the top
Maintain tension in your upper back throughout
instructions: Stand with feet hip-width apart, barbell over mid-foot on the floor.
Bend down and grip the bar with hands much wider than shoulder-width apart.
Keep your chest up, shoulders back, and maintain a neutral spine.
Drive through your heels and extend your hips and knees simultaneously.
Pull the bar straight up, keeping it close to your body throughout.
Stand tall with hips and knees fully extended, then lower with control.
exercises.name: Barbell Wide Grip Deadlift
exercises.name_pt: Levantamento Terra com Barra - Pegada Aberta
exercises.overview: The barbell wide grip deadlift increases the range of motion and emphasizes the upper back and rear delts more than conventional deadlifts. It's excellent for building pulling strength and improving posture.
exercises.safetyInfo: The wide grip increases range of motion and shoulder stress. Those with shoulder mobility issues or injuries should avoid this variation. Start with lighter weight than conventional deadlifts.

commonMistakes: Lifting the forearms off the thighs and using the biceps to assist
Using too much weight and sacrificing range of motion
Performing the movement too quickly without proper control
exerciseTips: Keep your forearms pressed firmly against your thighs throughout the movement
Use a full range of motion by allowing the bar to roll to your fingertips
Focus on slow, controlled movements rather than using momentum
Squeeze your forearms at the top of each repetition
instructions: Sit on a bench holding a barbell with an underhand grip, hands about shoulder-width apart.
Rest your forearms on your thighs with your wrists hanging over your knees.
Allow the barbell to roll down to your fingertips by extending your wrists.
Curl your wrists upward by flexing your wrist flexors, lifting the barbell as high as possible.
exercises.name: Barbell Wrist Curl
exercises.overview: The barbell wrist curl is an isolation exercise that specifically targets the wrist flexor muscles in the forearms. It's essential for developing grip strength and forearm muscular development.
exercises.safetyInfo: Start with very light weight as the wrist flexors are small muscles that fatigue quickly. Those with wrist or forearm injuries should avoid this exercise or use extremely light resistance.

commonMistakes: Allowing the torso to fold forward excessively
Letting the elbows spread apart and losing bar position
Descending too quickly without control
exerciseTips: Keep your elbows close together and chest up throughout
Maintain an upright torso position more than in conventional deadlifts
Focus on sitting back with your hips while keeping knees tracking over toes
Use elbow sleeves or padding to protect your arms from the barbell
instructions: Set a barbell on a rack at about elbow height or deadlift it to your elbows.
Position the bar in the crook of your elbows with arms crossed over your chest.
Stand with feet shoulder-width apart and maintain an upright torso.
Lower the weight by sitting back at the hips and bending the knees.
Descend until the bar reaches mid-shin level or as low as mobility allows.
Drive through your heels and extend hips and knees to return to standing.
exercises.name: Barbell Zercher Deadlift
exercises.overview: The barbell Zercher deadlift is an advanced compound movement where the barbell is held in the crook of the elbows. This front-loaded position emphasizes the quadriceps and core while teaching an upright torso position.
exercises.safetyInfo: This is an advanced movement requiring significant core strength and mobility. The bar position can be uncomfortable on the arms, so consider using padding. Avoid if you have elbow or wrist injuries.

commonMistakes: Using only arm muscles instead of engaging the entire body
Standing too upright without athletic positioning
Creating uneven waves between arms
exerciseTips: Maintain a slight bend in your knees throughout the movement
Generate power from your core, not just your arms
Keep your shoulders pulled back and chest up
Focus on creating consistent wave patterns
instructions: For this exercise you will need a heavy rope anchored at its center 15-20 feet away. Standing in front of the rope, take an end in each hand with your arms extended at your side. This will be your starting position.
Initiate the movement by rapidly raising one arm to shoulder level as quickly as you can.
As you let that arm drop to the starting position, raise the opposite side.
Continue alternating your left and right arms, whipping the ropes up and down as fast as you can.
exercises.name: Battling Ropes
exercises.overview: Battling ropes provide an intense cardiovascular and strength training workout that targets the shoulders while engaging the entire body. This exercise improves power, endurance, and coordination simultaneously.
exercises.safetyInfo: Ensure adequate shoulder mobility before performing. Stop if you experience sharp shoulder pain or excessive fatigue that compromises form.

commonMistakes: Allowing the hips to pike up too high
Moving the same side hand and foot together
Letting the knees drop to touch the ground
exerciseTips: Keep your knees just barely off the ground throughout
Maintain a flat back and avoid letting your hips pike up
Move in opposite patterns (right hand with left foot)
Keep your core tight to stabilize your spine
instructions: Start in a tabletop position with hands directly under shoulders and knees under hips.
Lift your knees 2-3 inches off the ground while keeping your back flat and core engaged.
