Move forward by stepping your right hand and left foot forward simultaneously.
Follow with your left hand and right foot, maintaining the low crawling position.
Continue crawling forward for the desired distance while keeping your knees hovering off the ground.
Reverse the pattern to crawl backward if desired.
exercises.name: Bear Crawl
exercises.overview: The Bear Crawl is a full-body bodyweight exercise that primarily targets the shoulders while engaging the core, arms, and legs for stability. It's excellent for building functional strength, coordination, and endurance while improving core stability.
exercises.safetyInfo: Start with short distances and build up gradually. Avoid if you have wrist, shoulder, or lower back problems. Perform on a soft surface if you have sensitive knees or wrists.

commonMistakes: Allowing elbows to flare out wide
Descending too low and stressing the shoulder joint
Using legs to assist the movement excessively
exerciseTips: Keep your elbows close to your body throughout the movement
Lower yourself until your upper arms are parallel to the floor
Push through your palms to engage triceps maximally
Keep your legs extended to increase difficulty
instructions: For this exercise you will need to place a bench behind your back. With the bench perpendicular to your body, and while looking away from it, hold on to the bench on its edge with the hands fully extended, separated at shoulder width. The legs will be extended forward, bent at the waist and perpendicular to your torso. This will be your starting position.

Slowly lower your body as you inhale by bending at the elbows until you lower yourself far enough to where there is an angle slightly smaller than 90 degrees between the upper arm and the forearm. Tip: Keep the elbows as close as possible throughout the movement. Forearms should always be pointing down.
Using your triceps to bring your torso up again, lift yourself back to the starting position.
Repeat for the recommended amount of repetitions.
exercises.name: Bench Dips
exercises.overview: Bench dips are an effective bodyweight exercise for building triceps strength using minimal equipment. They provide a convenient way to target the triceps while also engaging the chest and front deltoids.
exercises.safetyInfo: Avoid this exercise if you have shoulder impingement or wrist pain. The position can place significant stress on the anterior shoulder capsule.

commonMistakes: Moving too quickly and losing control of the bands
Allowing the bands to snap back uncontrolled
Not maintaining consistent tension at the bottom position
exerciseTips: Control the band tension throughout the full range of motion
Keep your core engaged to maintain stability
Focus on squeezing your chest at the top of the movement
Lower the handles slowly to maximize muscle activation
instructions: Using a flat bench secure a band under the leg of the bench that is nearest to your head.
Once the band is secure, grab it by both handles and lie down on the bench.
Extend your arms so that you are holding the band handles in front of you at shoulder width.
Once at shoulder width, rotate your wrists forward so that the palms of your hands are facing away from you. This will be your starting position.
Bring down the handles slowly until your elbow forms a 90 degree angle. Keep full control at all times.

As you breathe out, bring the handles up using your pectoral muscles. Lock your arms in the contracted position, squeeze your chest, hold for a second and then start coming down slowly. Tip: It should take at least twice as long to go down than to come up.
Repeat the movement for the prescribed amount of repetitions of your training program.
exercises.name: Bench Press - With Bands
exercises.name_pt: Supino com Faixa Elástica sobre a Barra
exercises.overview: This variation uses resistance bands to provide variable resistance that increases throughout the range of motion. It's excellent for home workouts and developing explosive pressing power.
exercises.safetyInfo: Inspect bands for wear or damage before use. Ensure bands are securely anchored to prevent snapping during the exercise.

commonMistakes: Using too much momentum and swinging the torso
Pulling the bar too high toward the neck
Allowing the lower back to round excessively
exerciseTips: Keep your chest up and shoulders back throughout the movement
Pull the bar to your lower chest or upper abdomen
Squeeze your shoulder blades together at the top
Control the weight on both the lifting and lowering phases
instructions: Holding a barbell with a pronated grip (palms facing down), bend your knees slightly and bring your torso forward, by bending at the waist, while keeping the back straight until it is almost parallel to the floor. Tip: Make sure that you keep the head up. The barbell should hang directly in front of you as your arms hang perpendicular to the floor and your torso. This is your starting position.

Now, while keeping the torso stationary, breathe out and lift the barbell to you. Keep the elbows close to the body and only use the forearms to hold the weight. At the top contracted position, squeeze the back muscles and hold for a brief pause.
Then inhale and slowly lower the barbell back to the starting position.
Repeat for the recommended amount of repetitions.
exercises.name: Bent Over Barbell Row
exercises.overview: This fundamental pulling exercise targets the middle back muscles and improves posture. It's essential for balanced upper body development and pulling strength.
exercises.safetyInfo: Maintain neutral spine alignment and avoid excessive forward lean. Those with lower back issues should consider supported variations or reduce the weight.
variations: Bent Over Two-Dumbbell Row With Palms In

commonMistakes: Using too much weight and swinging the dumbbells
Lifting the arms behind the body instead of to the sides
Not maintaining the head position on the bench
exerciseTips: Keep a slight bend in your elbows throughout the movement
Focus on initiating the movement with your rear delts
Squeeze your shoulder blades together at the top
Control the weight on the way down to maximize muscle activation
instructions: Stand up straight while holding a dumbbell in each hand and with an incline bench in front of you.

While keeping your back straight and maintaining the natural arch of your back, lean forward until your forehead touches the bench in front of you. Let the arms hang in front of you perpendicular to the ground. The palms of your hands should be facing each other and your torso should be parallel to the floor. This will be your starting position.

Keeping your torso forward and stationary, and the arms straight with a slight bend at the elbows, lift the dumbbells straight to the side until both arms are parallel to the floor. Exhale as you lift the weights. Caution: avoid swinging the torso or bringing the arms back as opposed to the side.
After a one second contraction at the top, slowly lower the dumbbells back to the starting position.
Repeat the recommended amount of repetitions.
exercises.name: Bent Over Dumbbell Rear Delt Raise With Head On Bench
exercises.name_pt: Crucifixo Invertido com Halteres e Cabeça Apoiada no Banco
exercises.overview: This isolation exercise specifically targets the posterior deltoids with the added stability of head support. It's crucial for shoulder health and balanced shoulder development.
exercises.safetyInfo: Use lighter weights as the rear delts are smaller muscles. Avoid this exercise if you have shoulder impingement or acute shoulder pain.

commonMistakes: Standing too upright reducing rear delt emphasis
Using momentum instead of controlled movement
Allowing torso to rotate during the lift
exerciseTips: Keep torso parallel to floor throughout movement
Lead with pinky finger to maximize rear delt activation
Squeeze shoulder blades together at top of movement
Control the eccentric portion slowly
instructions: Select a weight and hold the handle of the low pulley with your right hand.

Bend at the waist until your torso is nearly parallel to the floor. Your legs should be slightly bent with your left hand placed on your lower left thigh. Your right arm should be hanging from your shoulder in front of you and with a slight bend at the elbow. This will be your starting position.
Raise your right arm, elbow slightly bent, to the side until the arm is parallel to the floor and in line with your right ear. Breathe out as you perform this step.
Slowly lower the weight back to the starting position as you breathe in.
Repeat for the recommended amount of repetitions and repeat the movement with the other arm.
exercises.name: Bent Over Low-Pulley Side Lateral
exercises.name_pt: Elevação Lateral Curvada na Polia Baixa
exercises.overview: This cable exercise targets the posterior deltoids through a bent-over lateral raise motion. It's excellent for improving shoulder balance and posture by strengthening often-neglected rear deltoid muscles.
exercises.safetyInfo: Avoid this exercise if you have lower back issues. Keep knees slightly bent and maintain neutral spine to prevent injury.

commonMistakes: Allowing torso to twist during the movement
Pulling with arms instead of initiating with back
Using plates that are too large limiting range of motion
exerciseTips: Pull elbow close to body to maximize back engagement
Squeeze shoulder blades together at top
Keep core tight to prevent torso rotation
Focus on pulling with back muscles not arms
instructions: Put weight on one of the ends of an Olympic barbell. Make sure that you either place the other end of the barbell in the corner of two walls; or put a heavy object on the ground so the barbell cannot slide backward.
Bend forward until your torso is as close to parallel with the floor as you can and keep your knees slightly bent.
Now grab the bar with one arm just behind the plates on the side where the weight was placed and put your other hand on your knee. This will be your starting position.

Pull the bar straight up with your elbow in (to maximize back stimulation) until the plates touch your lower chest. Squeeze the back muscles as you lift the weight up and hold for a second at the top of the movement. Breathe out as you lift the weight. Tip: Do not allow for any swinging of the torso. Only the arm should move.

Slowly lower the bar to the starting position getting a nice stretch on the lats. Tip: Do not let the plates touch the floor. To ensure the best range of motion, I recommend using small plates (25-lb ones) as opposed to larger plates (like 35-45lb ones).
Repeat for the recommended amount of repetitions and switch arms.
exercises.name: Bent Over One-Arm Long Bar Row
exercises.overview: This landmine-style row allows for a natural pulling pattern while providing unilateral training benefits. The single-arm approach helps address muscle imbalances and provides core stability challenges.
exercises.safetyInfo: Ensure barbell is properly secured in corner or with heavy weight. Maintain neutral spine throughout to avoid lower back strain.
variations: Bent Over Two-Dumbbell Row With Palms In

commonMistakes: Rounding the back during the movement
Pulling too high toward chest instead of lower ribcage
Using excessive body english or momentum
exerciseTips: Use a stirrup attachment for better grip
Drive elbows back behind your torso
Keep chest up and shoulders back
Initiate pull with shoulder blade retraction
instructions: Put weight on one of the ends of an Olympic barbell. Make sure that you either place the other end of the barbell in the corner of two walls; or put a heavy object on the ground so the barbell cannot slide backward.
Bend forward until your torso is as close to parallel with the floor as you can and keep your knees slightly bent.
Now grab the bar with both arms just behind the plates on the side where the weight was placed and put your other hand on your knee. This will be your starting position.

Pull the bar straight up with your elbows in (to maximize back stimulation) until the plates touch your lower chest. Squeeze the back muscles as you lift the weight up and hold for a second at the top of the movement. Breathe out as you lift the weight. Tip: Use a stirrup or double handle cable attachment by hooking it under the end of the bar.

Slowly lower the bar to the starting position getting a nice stretch on the lats. Tip: Do not let the plates touch the floor. To ensure the best range of motion, I recommend using small plates (25-lb ones) as opposed to larger plates (like 35-45lb ones).
Repeat for the recommended amount of repetitions.
exercises.name: Bent Over Two-Arm Long Bar Row
exercises.overview: This bilateral landmine row provides a stable pulling pattern that's easier on the lower back than traditional bent-over rows. The angle allows for heavy loading while maintaining proper form.
exercises.safetyInfo: Secure the barbell properly before beginning. Use smaller plates to achieve full range of motion and avoid lower back strain.
variations: Bent Over Two-Dumbbell Row With Palms In

commonMistakes: Rounding the back excessively
Standing up during the pulling motion
Flaring elbows too wide from body
exerciseTips: Hinge at hips while keeping back straight
Pull dumbbells to lower ribcage not chest
Keep head in neutral position
Squeeze shoulder blades at top of movement
instructions: With a dumbbell in each hand (palms facing your torso), bend your knees slightly and bring your torso forward by bending at the waist; as you bend make sure to keep your back straight until it is almost parallel to the floor. Tip: Make sure that you keep the head up. The weights should hang directly in front of you as your arms hang perpendicular to the floor and your torso. This is your starting position.

While keeping the torso stationary, lift the dumbbells to your side (as you breathe out), keeping the elbows close to the body (do not exert any force with the forearm other than holding the weights). On the top contracted position, squeeze the back muscles and hold for a second.
Slowly lower the weight again to the starting position as you inhale.
Repeat for the recommended amount of repetitions.
exercises.name: Bent Over Two-Dumbbell Row
exercises.overview: This fundamental pulling exercise develops the middle back muscles while allowing independent arm movement. It's excellent for building balanced back strength and improving posture.
exercises.safetyInfo: Start with lighter weights to master the hip hinge pattern. Avoid if you have acute lower back pain.
variations: Bent Over Two-Dumbbell Row With Palms In

commonMistakes: Allowing wrists to bend or rotate
Pulling dumbbells too wide from body
Using legs to assist the pulling motion
exerciseTips: Maintain neutral wrist position throughout
Focus on pulling elbows straight back
Keep dumbbells close to body during pull
Pause briefly at top contraction
instructions: With a dumbbell in each hand (palms facing each other), bend your knees slightly and bring your torso forward, by bending at the waist, while keeping the back straight until it is almost parallel to the floor. Tip: Make sure that you keep the head up. The weights should hang directly in front of you as your arms hang perpendicular to the floor and your torso. This is your starting position.

While keeping the torso stationary, lift the dumbbells to your side as you breathe out, squeezing your shoulder blades together. On the top contracted position, squeeze the back muscles and hold for a second.
Slowly lower the weight again to the starting position as you inhale.
Repeat for the recommended amount of repetitions.
exercises.name: Bent Over Two-Dumbbell Row With Palms In
exercises.name_pt: Remada Curvada com Halteres - Pegada Neutra
exercises.overview: The neutral grip variation reduces wrist stress while emphasizing the middle back muscles. This grip position often allows for better muscle activation and is joint-friendly.
exercises.safetyInfo: The neutral grip is often more comfortable for those with wrist issues. Maintain proper hip hinge position to protect lower back.

commonMistakes: Pressing at an angle instead of vertically
Moving too quickly without control
Losing sight of the weight overhead
exerciseTips: Keep eyes on kettlebell throughout entire movement
Press weight straight up as you bend sideways
Move slowly and under control
Practice with light weight first
instructions: Clean a kettlebell to your shoulder. Clean the kettlebell to your shoulders by extending through the legs and hips as you raise the kettlebell towards your shoulder. The wrist should rotate as you do so. This will be your starting position.

Begin my leaning to the side opposite the kettlebell, continuing until you are able to touch the ground with your free hand, keeping your eyes on the kettlebell. As you do so, press the weight vertically be extending through the elbow, keeping your arm perpendicular to the ground.
Return to an upright position, with the kettlebell above your head. Return the kettlebell to the shoulder and repeat for the desired number of repetitions.
exercises.name: Bent Press
exercises.overview: This old-school strongman exercise combines a side bend with an overhead press, creating a unique full-body challenge. It develops core strength, shoulder stability, and total-body coordination.
exercises.safetyInfo: This is an advanced movement requiring significant mobility and strength. Master basic overhead pressing first. Avoid with shoulder or lower back injuries.

commonMistakes: Straightening arms completely during the movement
Lowering the weight too far and losing shoulder stability
Using momentum instead of controlled movement
exerciseTips: Keep a consistent bend in your arms throughout the movement
Lower the weight slowly in a controlled arc
Focus on feeling the stretch in your lats and chest at the bottom
Drive your elbows forward on the return movement
instructions: Lie on a flat bench with a barbell using a shoulder grip width.
Hold the bar straight over your chest with a bend in your arms. This will be your starting position.
While keeping your arms in the bent arm position, lower the weight slowly in an arc behind your head while breathing in until you feel a stretch on the chest.
At that point, bring the barbell back to the starting position using the arc through which the weight was lowered and exhale as you perform this movement.
Hold the weight on the initial position for a second and repeat the motion for the prescribed number of repetitions.
exercises.name: Bent-Arm Barbell Pullover
exercises.name_pt: Pullover com Barra e Braços Flexionados
exercises.overview: The bent-arm barbell pullover is a classic upper body exercise that targets the lats while also engaging the chest and triceps. It's excellent for improving ribcage expansion and developing the connection between chest and back muscles.
exercises.safetyInfo: Avoid this exercise if you have shoulder impingement or rotator cuff injuries. The extreme range of motion can stress the shoulder joint, so start with lighter weights to assess your mobility.

commonMistakes: Lying along the bench instead of across it
Allowing hips to rise too high
Using too heavy of a weight compromising form
exerciseTips: Lie perpendicular across the bench for maximum chest stretch
Keep your hips low to increase the stretch
Maintain the arm bend throughout the entire range of motion
Focus on pulling with your chest muscles on the return
instructions: Place a dumbbell standing up on a flat bench.

Ensuring that the dumbbell stays securely placed at the top of the bench, lie perpendicular to the bench (torso across it as in forming a cross) with only your shoulders lying on the surface. Hips should be below the bench and legs bent with feet firmly on the floor. The head will be off the bench as well.

Grasp the dumbbell with both hands and hold it straight over your chest with a bend in your arms. Both palms should be pressing against the underside one of the sides of the dumbbell. This will be your starting position. Caution: Always ensure that the dumbbell used for this exercise is secure. Using a dumbbell with loose plates can result in the dumbbell falling apart and falling on your face.
While keeping your arms locked in the bent arm position, lower the weight slowly in an arc behind your head while breathing in until you feel a stretch on the chest.
At that point, bring the dumbbell back to the starting position using the arc through which the weight was lowered and exhale as you perform this movement.
Hold the weight on the initial position for a second and repeat the motion for the prescribed number of repetitions.
exercises.name: Bent-Arm Dumbbell Pullover
exercises.name_pt: Pullover com Halter e Braços Flexionados
exercises.overview: The bent-arm dumbbell pullover is primarily a chest exercise when performed across a bench. The cross-bench position allows for greater chest expansion and targets the pectorals more effectively than the barbell version.
exercises.safetyInfo: Ensure the dumbbell is secure before beginning - loose plates can be dangerous. This exercise requires good shoulder mobility, so warm up thoroughly and start with lighter weights.

commonMistakes: Using momentum to swing legs up
Not achieving full pelvic tilt at the top
Allowing feet to touch the ground between reps
exerciseTips: Focus on rolling your pelvis backward rather than just lifting knees
Keep constant tension on your abs throughout the movement
Control the descent to maximize time under tension
Exhale forcefully as you bring knees to chest
instructions: Lay flat on the floor with your arms next to your sides.
Now bend your knees at around a 75 degree angle and lift your feet off the floor by around 2 inches.

Using your lower abs, bring your knees in towards you as you maintain the 75 degree angle bend in your legs. Continue this movement until you raise your hips off of the floor by rolling your pelvis backward. Breathe out as you perform this portion of the movement. Tip: At the end of the movement your knees will be over your chest.
Squeeze your abs at the top of the movement for a second and then return to the starting position slowly as you breathe in. Tip: Maintain a controlled motion at all times.
Repeat for the recommended amount of repetitions.
exercises.name: Bent-Knee Hip Raise
exercises.name_pt: Elevação de Quadril com Joelhos Flexionados
exercises.overview: The bent-knee hip raise is an effective bodyweight exercise for targeting the lower portion of the rectus abdominis. It emphasizes the often-neglected lower abs through hip flexion and pelvic tilting.
exercises.safetyInfo: Keep your lower back pressed into the floor to avoid strain. If you experience lower back discomfort, reduce the range of motion or try the exercise with bent knees throughout.

commonMistakes: Allowing the body to sag or arch excessively
Using momentum instead of controlled movement
Placing hands too wide on the bar
exerciseTips: Keep your body straight throughout the movement
Focus on controlling the descent for maximum muscle activation
Drive through your palms to extend your elbows fully
Maintain tension in your core to prevent sagging
instructions: Position a bar in a rack at chest height.
Standing, take a shoulder width grip on the bar and step a yard or two back, feet together and arms extended so that you are leaning on the bar. This will be your starting position.
Begin by flexing the elbow, lowering yourself towards the bar.
Pause, and then reverse the motion by extending the elbows.
Progress from bodyweight by adding chains over your shoulders.
exercises.name: Body Tricep Press
exercises.overview: The Body Tricep Press is a bodyweight exercise that targets the triceps through a pushing motion while leaning against a bar. It's an excellent alternative to tricep dips for building upper arm strength.
exercises.safetyInfo: Avoid this exercise if you have elbow or wrist injuries. Start with a higher bar position to reduce difficulty and progress gradually.

commonMistakes: Allowing hips to pike up during the movement
Rushing through the motion without control
Placing hands too far forward when pressing up
exerciseTips: Press firmly through your palms when extending up
Keep your core tight throughout the entire movement
Maintain a straight line from head to heels
Control the descent back to forearms
instructions: Assume a plank position on the ground. You should be supporting your bodyweight on your toes and forearms, keeping your torso straight. Your forearms should be shoulder-width apart. This will be your starting position.
Pressing your palms firmly into the ground, extend through the elbows to raise your body from the ground. Keep your torso rigid as you perform the movement.
Slowly lower your forearms back to the ground by allowing the elbows to flex.
Repeat.
exercises.name: Body-Up
exercises.overview: The Body-Up is an advanced bodyweight exercise that combines plank stability with tricep strength. It involves transitioning from a forearm plank to an extended arm position.
exercises.safetyInfo: This exercise places significant stress on the wrists and elbows. Avoid if you have wrist or elbow injuries, and warm up thoroughly before performing.

commonMistakes: Moving too quickly and losing control of the bars
Allowing the hips to sag during the movement
Not achieving full range of motion at the stretched position
exerciseTips: Move the bars slowly and under complete control
Focus on feeling the stretch across your chest
Keep your core engaged to maintain body position
Squeeze your chest muscles as you bring the bars back together
instructions: Position two equally loaded EZ bars on the ground next to each other. Ensure they are able to roll.
Assume a push-up position over the bars, supporting your weight on your toes and hands with your arms extended and body straight.
Place your hands on the bars. This will be your starting position.
Using a slow and controlled motion, move your hands away from the midline of your body, rolling the bars apart. Inhale during this portion of the motion.
After moving the bars as far apart as you can, return to the starting position by pulling them back together. Exhale as you perform this movement.
exercises.name: Bodyweight Flyes
exercises.overview: Bodyweight Flyes provide chest isolation using rolling barbells to create a fly motion while in a push-up position. This exercise uniquely combines stability challenges with targeted chest development.
exercises.safetyInfo: Ensure the bars are securely weighted and cannot roll unpredictably. This exercise requires significant shoulder stability and should be avoided with shoulder injuries.

commonMistakes: Using too much arm and not enough back muscle activation
Allowing legs to drop during the movement
Not achieving full range of motion at the top position
exerciseTips: Pull your shoulder blades together before pulling with your arms
Keep your legs tucked throughout the movement
Focus on squeezing your mid-back muscles at the top
Control the lowering portion of the movement
instructions: Begin by taking a medium to wide grip on a pull-up apparatus with your palms facing away from you. From a hanging position, tuck your knees to your chest, leaning back and getting your legs over your side of the pull-up apparatus. This will be your starting position.
Beginning with your arms straight, flex the elbows and retract the shoulder blades to raise your body up until your legs contact the pull-up apparatus.
After a brief pause, return to the starting position.
exercises.name: Bodyweight Mid Row
exercises.overview: The Bodyweight Mid Row targets the middle back muscles through a horizontal pulling motion while hanging from a pull-up bar. It's excellent for developing mid-back strength and improving posture.
exercises.safetyInfo: Requires significant grip strength and shoulder stability. Ensure the pull-up bar can support your full body weight before attempting.
variations: Bent Over Two-Dumbbell Row With Palms In

commonMistakes: Allowing the non-working leg to touch the ground for support
Leaning too far forward and losing balance during the descent
Not achieving adequate depth due to lack of strength or mobility
exerciseTips: Keep your extended leg straight and off the ground throughout the movement
Focus on sitting back into the squat rather than just bending your knee
Maintain an upright torso and avoid excessive forward lean
Use your arms for counterbalance and stability throughout the movement
instructions: Stand on one leg with the other leg extended straight out in front of you.
Extend your arms forward for balance and engage your core for stability.
Lower yourself down by bending your standing leg until your thigh is parallel to the ground.
Drive through your heel to return to the starting position while maintaining balance on one leg.
exercises.name: Bodyweight Single Leg Squat
exercises.overview: The bodyweight single leg squat, also known as a pistol squat, is an advanced unilateral movement that challenges leg strength, balance, and mobility simultaneously. It requires mastery of basic squatting patterns and significant single-leg strength.
exercises.safetyInfo: This is an extremely challenging exercise requiring significant strength, balance, and mobility. Master regular squats, lunges, and assisted single-leg squats before attempting. Those with knee issues should avoid or modify this exercise.

commonMistakes: Knees caving inward during the movement
Not descending to adequate depth
Rising up on toes instead of staying flat-footed
exerciseTips: Keep your chest up and eyes looking forward
Push your knees out in line with your toes
Sit back into your hips rather than just bending your knees
Drive through your heels to return to standing
instructions: Stand with your feet shoulder width apart. You can place your hands behind your head. This will be your starting position.
Begin the movement by flexing your knees and hips, sitting back with your hips.
Continue down to full depth if you are able,and quickly reverse the motion until you return to the starting position. As you squat, keep your head and chest up and push your knees out.
exercises.name: Bodyweight Squat
exercises.overview: The Bodyweight Squat is a fundamental lower body exercise that builds leg strength, mobility, and movement patterns. It's the foundation for all squatting movements and requires no equipment.
exercises.safetyInfo: Generally safe for most individuals. Those with knee or hip injuries should consult a healthcare provider and may need to limit range of motion.

commonMistakes: Descending too deep and straining the shoulder joint
Leaning too far forward and shifting emphasis away from triceps
exerciseTips: Keep your elbows close to your body rather than flaring them out wide
Maintain an upright torso position throughout the movement
Control the descent to maximize muscle engagement
Focus on pushing through your palms rather than your fingers
instructions: Position yourself between parallel bars or on the edge of a bench with hands gripping the surface, arms extended.
Support your body weight with your arms extended and legs either bent or straight depending on difficulty desired.
Lower your body by bending your elbows until your shoulders are below your elbows or as deep as comfortable.
Press through your palms to push your body back up to the starting position with arms fully extended.
Keep your torso upright and avoid swinging or using momentum during the movement.
Repeat for the desired number of repetitions maintaining control throughout.
exercises.name: Bodyweight Tricep Dip
exercises.name_pt: Mergulho para Tríceps com Peso Corporal
exercises.overview: Bodyweight tricep dips are a compound exercise that primarily targets the triceps while also engaging the chest and front delts. This exercise builds functional upper body pushing strength using only your body weight.
exercises.safetyInfo: Avoid this exercise if you have shoulder impingement or wrist issues. Those with limited shoulder mobility should not descend too deeply to prevent injury.

commonMistakes: Taking steps that are too small, causing knee to track over toes
Leaning forward excessively during the descent
Pushing off the back toe instead of driving through the front heel
exerciseTips: Take a large enough step to allow proper knee alignment
Keep most of your weight on your front heel
Maintain an upright torso throughout the movement
Drive powerfully through your front heel to step forward
instructions: Begin standing with your feet shoulder width apart and your hands on your hips.

Step forward with one leg, flexing the knees to drop your hips. Descend until your rear knee nearly touches the ground. Your posture should remain upright, and your front knee should stay above the front foot.
Drive through the heel of your lead foot and extend both knees to raise yourself back up.
Step forward with your rear foot, repeating the lunge on the opposite leg.
exercises.name: Bodyweight Walking Lunge
exercises.overview: The Bodyweight Walking Lunge is a dynamic lower body exercise that develops unilateral leg strength, balance, and coordination. It mimics functional movement patterns used in daily activities.
exercises.safetyInfo: Be cautious if you have knee injuries, particularly to the ACL or meniscus. Start with shorter steps and limited range of motion if experiencing discomfort.

commonMistakes: Pulling with arms instead of crunching with abs
Allowing back to flatten against bosu ball
Moving too quickly through the range of motion
exerciseTips: Maintain constant tension on cables throughout movement
Keep arms straight and aligned with cable angle
Arch lower back around bosu ball in starting position
Drive through core without using arm momentum
instructions: Connect a standard handle to each arm of a cable machine, and position them in the most downward position.
Grab a Bosu Ball and position it in front and center of the cable machine.
Lie down on the Bosu Ball with the small of your back arched around the ball. Your rear end should be close to the floor without touching it.
With both hands, reach back and grab the handle of each cable.
With your feet positioned in a wide stance, extend your arms straight out in front of you and in between your knees. Your hands should be at knee level.
Keep your arms straight and in-line with the upward angle of the cable. Elevate your torso in a crunching motion without dropping or bending your arms.
Maintain the rigid position with your arms. Slowly descend back to the starting position with your back arched around the Bosu Ball and your abdominals elongated.
Repeat the same series of movements to failure.
Once you reach failure, keep your abs tight and raise your torso into plank position so your back is elevated off the Bosu Ball.
Lower your arms down to your side; keep them straight. Start doing alternating side bends; reach for your heels! This finishing movement will focus on your obliques.
exercises.name: Bosu Ball Cable Crunch With Side Bends
exercises.name_pt: Abdominal na Polia com Bosu e Flexão Lateral
exercises.overview: A combination movement using bosu ball instability and cable resistance to target the entire core. The instability challenges stabilizing muscles while cables provide variable resistance.
exercises.safetyInfo: Avoid if you have lower back injuries. The unstable surface increases spinal stress, so maintain proper form throughout.

commonMistakes: Using momentum to swing legs up
Lifting legs without engaging pelvis
Allowing lower back to arch off ground
exerciseTips: Focus on lifting pelvis rather than just legs
Control the descent slowly
Keep lower back pressed against floor
Exhale as you lift hips up
instructions: Begin by lying on your back on the ground. Your legs should be straight and your arms at your side. This will be your starting position.

To perform the movement, tuck the knees toward your chest by flexing the hips and knees. Following this, extend your legs directly above you so that they are perpendicular to the ground. Rotate and elevate your pelvis to raise your glutes from the floor.
After a brief pause, return to the starting position.
exercises.name: Bottoms Up
exercises.overview: A reverse crunch variation that targets the lower portion of the rectus abdominis by lifting the pelvis off the ground. Effective for developing lower core strength.
exercises.safetyInfo: Keep movements controlled to avoid strain on hip flexors and lower back. Stop if you feel lower back discomfort.

commonMistakes: Gripping the handle too lightly, causing loss of control
Moving too quickly and losing balance of the inverted kettlebell
exerciseTips: Squeeze the handle extremely hard to maintain control of the inverted kettlebell
Move slowly and deliberately to maintain balance of the weight
Keep your shoulder packed down and engaged throughout the carry
Focus on one step at a time and avoid rushing the movement
instructions: Clean a kettlebell to the rack position, then flip it so the bell is pointing upward.
Grip the handle firmly and press the inverted kettlebell overhead with the bell on top.
Lock out your arm completely and engage your grip strength to keep the kettlebell stable.
Begin walking forward with controlled steps, maintaining the bottoms-up position throughout.
Focus on keeping the kettlebell perfectly balanced and preventing it from tilting.
Complete the prescribed distance, then carefully lower the kettlebell to rack position.
exercises.name: Bottoms Up Carry
exercises.overview: The bottoms up carry involves walking with an inverted kettlebell held overhead, creating an extreme challenge for grip strength, shoulder stability, and proprioception. It's one of the most demanding carry variations and requires exceptional control and focus.
exercises.safetyInfo: This is an advanced exercise requiring exceptional grip strength and shoulder stability. Not suitable for beginners or those with wrist, shoulder, or grip strength issues. High risk of dropping the weight if grip fails.

commonMistakes: Insufficient grip strength causing kettlebell to fall
Using only arms instead of hip drive
Allowing wrist to bend under load
exerciseTips: Grip kettlebell handle as hard as possible
Generate power from hips during swing
Keep wrist straight and strong throughout
Control the flip to inverted position
instructions: Initiate the exercise by standing upright with a kettlebell in one hand.
Swing the kettlebell back forcefully and then reverse the motion forcefully. Crush the kettlebell handle as hard as possible and raise the kettlebell to your shoulder.
exercises.name: Bottoms-Up Clean From The Hang Position
exercises.name_pt: Clean Invertido a Partir do Hang
exercises.overview: An advanced kettlebell exercise that challenges grip strength and coordination by cleaning the kettlebell to an inverted position. Develops functional strength and stability.
exercises.safetyInfo: Requires significant grip strength. Start with lighter weights and progress gradually. Ensure clear space around you in case of drops.

commonMistakes: Chains fully lifting off ground at bottom position
Bouncing or rocking on the box
Losing upper back tightness during the pause
exerciseTips: Adjust chain length so some links remain on floor at top
Pause completely on the box without relaxing
Drive explosively off the box leading with your head
Maintain tight core throughout the entire movement
instructions: Begin in a power rack with a box at the appropriate height behind you. Typically, you would aim for a box height that brings you to a parallel squat, but you can train higher or lower if desired.

To set up the chains, begin by looping the leader chain over the sleeves of the bar. The heavy chain should be attached using a snap hook. Adjust the length of the lead chain so that a few links are still on the floor at the top of the movement.

Begin by stepping under the bar and placing it across the back of the shoulders. Squeeze your shoulder blades together and rotate your elbows forward, attempting to bend the bar across your shoulders. Remove the bar from the rack, creating a tight arch in your lower back, and step back into position. Place your feet wider for more emphasis on the back, glutes, adductors, and hamstrings, or closer together for more quad development. Keep your head facing forward.

With your back, shoulders, and core tight, push your knees and butt out and you begin your descent. Sit back with your hips until you are seated on the box. Ideally, your shins should be perpendicular to the ground. Pause when you reach the box, and relax the hip flexors. Never bounce off of a box.
Keeping the weight on your heels and pushing your feet and knees out, drive upward off of the box as you lead the movement with your head. Continue upward, maintaining tightness head to toe.
exercises.name: Box Squat with Chains
exercises.overview: A powerlifting exercise that combines box squats with chain resistance to develop explosive strength and power. The chains provide variable resistance that increases as you stand up from the box.
exercises.safetyInfo: Properly secure chains to prevent shifting during movement. Ensure box is stable and appropriate height. Start with lighter chain weight to learn the movement pattern.

commonMistakes: Using too much weight for the range of motion
Lowering bar too far behind the neck
Arching back excessively during the movement
exerciseTips: Maintain strict form throughout the alternating movement
Keep core engaged to protect lower back
Use lighter weight than standard overhead press
Control the bar path behind the neck carefully
instructions: Sit on a Military Press Bench with a bar at shoulder level with a pronated grip (palms facing forward). Tip: Your grip should be wider than shoulder width and it should create a 90-degree angle between the forearm and the upper arm as the barbell goes down. This is your starting position.
Once you pick up the barbell with the correct grip, lift the bar up over your head by locking your arms.
Now lower the bar down to the back of the head slowly as you inhale.
Lift the bar back up to the starting position as you exhale.
Lower the bar down to the starting position slowly as you inhale. This is one repetition.
Alternate in this manner until you complete the recommended amount of repetitions.
exercises.name: Bradford/Rocky Presses
exercises.overview: An advanced shoulder exercise that alternates pressing in front and behind the neck. This develops shoulder strength and mobility while challenging stability throughout the full range of motion.
exercises.safetyInfo: This exercise requires good shoulder mobility and should be avoided by those with shoulder impingement or neck issues. Use significantly lighter weight than standard pressing movements.

commonMistakes: Allowing the bar to shift forward due to inadequate upper back tightness
Gripping too wide on the curved portions causing instability
exerciseTips: Keep your upper back extremely tight to control the bar
Find your grip position on the angled portions for comfort
Maintain vertical bar path despite the bar's unique shape
Focus on keeping your elbows pulled down and back
instructions: Position the buffalo bar on your upper traps, noting the curved ends that angle downward.
Grip the bar at a comfortable width along the angled portions.
Unrack and step back into your preferred squat stance.
Descend into the squat while maintaining tight upper back tension.
Drive through your heels to return to the starting position.
Re-rack the bar carefully maintaining control throughout.
exercises.name: Buffalo Bar Squat
exercises.overview: The buffalo bar squat uses a specialized barbell with curved, angled ends that sits differently on the back than a straight bar. It can be easier on the shoulders while providing a unique training stimulus.
exercises.safetyInfo: The buffalo bar's design can be challenging for those with shoulder mobility issues. The curved ends require strong upper back engagement to maintain proper bar position.

commonMistakes: Placing too much weight on the rear foot instead of using it for balance only
Allowing the front knee to cave inward during the movement
Leaning too far forward and losing balance
exerciseTips: Focus 90% of your weight on your front leg, using the rear leg only for balance
Keep your front knee tracking over your toes, not caving inward
Maintain an upright torso throughout the entire movement
Control the descent to maximize muscle activation
instructions: Stand 2-3 feet in front of a bench holding dumbbells at your sides.
Place the top of your rear foot on the bench behind you with most weight on your front leg.
Lower your body by bending your front knee until your thigh is parallel to the floor.
Keep your torso upright and avoid leaning forward during the descent.
Push through your front heel to return to the starting position.
Complete all reps on one leg before switching to the other side.
exercises.name: Bulgarian Split Squat
exercises.overview: The Bulgarian split squat is a unilateral leg exercise that targets the quadriceps while improving balance and addressing muscle imbalances. It's excellent for building single-leg strength and stability.
exercises.safetyInfo: Be cautious if you have knee or ankle injuries. Start with bodyweight only before adding external resistance, and ensure proper balance and control before increasing load.

commonMistakes: Allowing the hips to sag during the plank position
Landing too heavily on the feet when jumping back and forth
exerciseTips: Keep your core tight throughout the entire movement sequence
Land softly when jumping back and forward to protect your joints
Maintain a straight plank position during the push-up phase
Breathe rhythmically to maintain energy throughout the exercise
instructions: Start standing with feet shoulder-width apart and arms at your sides.
Drop into a squat position and place your hands on the floor in front of you.
Jump both feet back into a plank position, keeping your body in a straight line.
Perform a push-up, lowering your chest to the floor and pressing back up.
Jump your feet back toward your hands, returning to the squat position.
Explosively jump up with arms overhead, then return to starting position.
exercises.name: Burpee
exercises.overview: The burpee is a full-body, high-intensity exercise that combines a squat, plank, push-up, and jump into one fluid movement. It's highly effective for building cardiovascular endurance, strength, and explosive power.
exercises.safetyInfo: High-impact exercise that may not be suitable for those with knee, ankle, or lower back problems. Pregnant women should avoid this exercise, and beginners should start with modified versions.

commonMistakes: Pushing through toes instead of heels
Arching lower back excessively at the top
Allowing knees to fall inward during the movement
exerciseTips: Push through your heels rather than toes
Squeeze glutes at the top of the movement
Keep knees aligned with hips and ankles
Maintain neutral spine throughout the movement
instructions: Lie flat on the floor on your back with the hands by your side and your knees bent. Your feet should be placed around shoulder width. This will be your starting position.
Pushing mainly with your heels, lift your hips off the floor while keeping your back straight. Breathe out as you perform this part of the motion and hold at the top for a second.
Slowly go back to the starting position as you breathe in.
exercises.name: Butt Lift (Bridge)
exercises.overview: A fundamental glute strengthening exercise performed lying on your back. This exercise activates the glutes and hamstrings while improving hip mobility and core stability.
exercises.safetyInfo: Keep movements controlled and avoid hyperextending the lower back. Those with lower back issues should start with smaller range of motion.

commonMistakes: Allowing hips to sag below neutral plank position
Using momentum instead of controlled movement
Lifting head up during the pike motion
exerciseTips: Start from a forearm plank position
Drive hips toward ceiling while contracting abs
Control the descent back to plank position
Keep forearms planted firmly on the ground
instructions: Begin a pushup position but with your elbows on the ground and resting on your forearms. Your arms should be bent at a 90 degree angle.
Arch your back slightly out rather than keeping your back completely straight.

Raise your glutes toward the ceiling, squeezing your abs tightly to close the distance between your ribcage and hips. The end result will be that you'll end up in a high bridge position. Exhale as you perform this portion of the movement.
Lower back down slowly to your starting position as you breathe in. Tip: Don't let your back sag downwards.
Repeat for the recommended amount of repetitions.
exercises.name: Butt-Ups
exercises.overview: A dynamic core exercise that combines plank stability with hip flexion. This movement targets the entire core while challenging shoulder stability and control.
exercises.safetyInfo: Maintain proper plank form throughout the movement. Those with lower back issues should start with standard planks before progressing to this exercise.

commonMistakes: Using too much weight and relying on momentum
Allowing shoulders to roll forward during the movement
Not maintaining constant tension on the chest muscles
exerciseTips: Keep your back flat against the pad throughout the movement
Focus on squeezing your chest muscles at the peak contraction
Control the negative portion slowly to maximize muscle activation
Maintain a slight bend in your elbows to protect the joints
instructions: Sit on the machine with your back flat on the pad.
Take hold of the handles. Tip: Your upper arms should be positioned parallel to the floor; adjust the machine accordingly. This will be your starting position.
Push the handles together slowly as you squeeze your chest in the middle. Breathe out during this part of the motion and hold the contraction for a second.
Return back to the starting position slowly as you inhale until your chest muscles are fully stretched.
Repeat for the recommended amount of repetitions.
exercises.name: Butterfly
exercises.overview: The butterfly is an isolation exercise that targets the chest muscles using a controlled arc motion. It's excellent for developing chest muscle definition and improving the mind-muscle connection.
exercises.safetyInfo: Avoid this exercise if you have shoulder impingement or recent chest/shoulder injuries. Start with lighter weight to assess shoulder comfort and range of motion.

commonMistakes: Allowing the torso to rotate toward the cable machine
Holding the breath instead of breathing normally
Standing too close to the machine and not creating enough tension
exerciseTips: Keep your core braced and avoid any rotation of your torso
Breathe normally while maintaining the isometric hold
Keep your shoulders square and resist the pull from the side
Maintain a slight bend in your knees for better stability
instructions: Set cable at chest height and attach a D-handle or rope.
Stand perpendicular to the cable machine with feet shoulder-width apart.
Grasp the handle with both hands and step away to create tension.
Hold the handle at your chest, then press it straight out in front of you.
Hold this position while resisting the rotational force from the cable.
Maintain the hold for the prescribed time before switching sides.
exercises.name: Cable Anti-Rotation Hold
exercises.overview: The cable anti-rotation hold trains the core to resist rotational forces, building stability and strength in the transverse plane. This isometric exercise improves core endurance and helps prevent injury during rotational movements.
exercises.safetyInfo: This is a low-risk exercise suitable for most fitness levels. Start with shorter hold times and lighter resistance before progressing to longer holds or heavier weight.

commonMistakes: Letting the elbows drift forward during the curl
Using the shoulders and back to assist
Not controlling the eccentric portion of the movement
exerciseTips: Keep your elbows stationary at your sides
Focus on the muscle contraction rather than the weight
Control both the lifting and lowering phases
Avoid swinging or using momentum
instructions: Set the cable pulley to the lowest position and attach a straight bar or EZ-curl bar.
Stand facing the machine with feet shoulder-width apart, grasping the bar with an underhand grip.
Keep your elbows tucked at your sides and curl the bar up by contracting your biceps.
Squeeze your biceps at the top when the bar reaches chest level.
Slowly lower the bar back to the starting position, maintaining tension throughout.
exercises.name: Cable Bicep Curl
exercises.overview: The cable bicep curl is a fundamental isolation exercise that targets the biceps with constant tension throughout the full range of motion. The cable provides smooth resistance and is excellent for building bicep strength and size.
exercises.safetyInfo: This is generally a safe exercise for most people. Those with elbow tendinitis should use lighter weight and focus on proper form.

commonMistakes: Using too much weight and compromising form
Allowing the cables to pull the arms too far back
Bending the elbows too much, turning it into a press
exerciseTips: Keep a slight bend in your elbows throughout the movement
Focus on squeezing your chest muscles at the peak contraction
Control the negative portion of the movement
Maintain a stable stance with slight forward lean
instructions: Set cable pulleys to chest height and attach D-handles to each side.
Stand in the center with feet shoulder-width apart, grasping a handle in each hand.
Step forward slightly with arms extended out to your sides, feeling a stretch in your chest.
Bring your hands together in front of your chest in a wide arc motion, squeezing your pecs.
Slowly return to the starting position with control, maintaining the arc motion.
exercises.name: Cable Chest Fly
exercises.overview: The cable chest fly is an isolation exercise that specifically targets the pectoral muscles through a wide arc of motion. It provides constant tension throughout the range of motion and helps develop chest width and definition.
exercises.safetyInfo: Those with shoulder injuries should use caution and avoid excessive range of motion. Start with lighter weight to master the movement pattern.

commonMistakes: Allowing shoulder blades to protract during the press
Using excessive weight that compromises form
Not maintaining proper elbow positioning at 90 degrees
exerciseTips: Retract your shoulder blades and maintain throughout the movement
Press the handles together in a straight line forward
Keep your core engaged to maintain stability
Control the return phase to maintain constant muscle tension
instructions: Adjust the weight to an appropriate amount and be seated, grasping the handles. Your upper arms should be about 45 degrees to the body, with your head and chest up. The elbows should be bent to about 90 degrees. This will be your starting position.
Begin by extending through the elbow, pressing the handles together straight in front of you. Keep your shoulder blades retracted as you execute the movement.
After pausing at full extension, return to th starting position, keeping tension on the cables.
You can also execute this movement with your back off the pad, at an incline or decline, or alternate hands.
exercises.name: Cable Chest Press
exercises.overview: The cable chest press is a compound movement that targets the chest while engaging supporting muscles. It provides constant tension throughout the range of motion and allows for various angle adjustments.
exercises.safetyInfo: This exercise is generally safe for most individuals. Those with shoulder impingement should start with lighter weights and ensure proper scapular positioning.

commonMistakes: Using too much weight and bending arms excessively
Allowing torso to move during the exercise
Not maintaining constant tension at the stretched position
exerciseTips: Maintain a slight forward lean throughout the movement
Keep a consistent arc pattern with arms slightly bent
Focus on bringing your hands together in front of your chest
Control the eccentric phase to maximize muscle activation
instructions: To get yourself into the starting position, place the pulleys on a high position (above your head), select the resistance to be used and hold the pulleys in each hand.

Step forward in front of an imaginary straight line between both pulleys while pulling your arms together in front of you. Your torso should have a small forward bend from the waist. This will be your starting position.

With a slight bend on your elbows in order to prevent stress at the biceps tendon, extend your arms to the side (straight out at both sides) in a wide arc until you feel a stretch on your chest. Breathe in as you perform this portion of the movement. Tip: Keep in mind that throughout the movement, the arms and torso should remain stationary; the movement should only occur at the shoulder joint.
Return your arms back to the starting position as you breathe out. Make sure to use the same arc of motion used to lower the weights.
Hold for a second at the starting position and repeat the movement for the prescribed amount of repetitions.
exercises.name: Cable Crossover
exercises.overview: The cable crossover is an isolation exercise that targets the chest through a wide arc of motion. It provides excellent muscle stretch and contraction while allowing for precise muscle targeting.
exercises.safetyInfo: Start with lighter weights to master the movement pattern. Avoid if you have shoulder impingement or bicep tendon issues due to the stretched position.

commonMistakes: Using hip flexors instead of abdominal muscles
Choosing too heavy weight that compromises spinal flexion
Not controlling the eccentric portion of the movement
exerciseTips: Keep your hips stationary throughout the movement
Focus on flexing your spine rather than pulling with your arms
Exhale forcefully during the crunch phase
Maintain constant tension on your abs during the entire set
instructions: Kneel below a high pulley that contains a rope attachment.
Grasp cable rope attachment and lower the rope until your hands are placed next to your face.
Flex your hips slightly and allow the weight to hyperextend the lower back. This will be your starting position.

With the hips stationary, flex the waist as you contract the abs so that the elbows travel towards the middle of the thighs. Exhale as you perform this portion of the movement and hold the contraction for a second.

Slowly return to the starting position as you inhale. Tip: Make sure that you keep constant tension on the abs throughout the movement. Also, do not choose a weight so heavy that the lower back handles the brunt of the work.
Repeat for the recommended amount of repetitions.
exercises.name: Cable Crunch
exercises.overview: The cable crunch is an effective abdominal exercise that allows for progressive overload. It targets the rectus abdominis through spinal flexion while providing constant resistance.
exercises.safetyInfo: Avoid excessive weight that forces the lower back to compensate. Those with lower back issues should start with bodyweight crunches first.

commonMistakes: Allowing knees to cave inward during the movement
Rounding the back during the descent
Not fully extending hips at the top position
exerciseTips: Drive through your heels during the ascent
Keep your chest up and shoulders back throughout
Maintain a neutral spine position
Engage your core to protect your lower back
instructions: Move the cables to the bottom of the towers and select an appropriate weight. Stand directly in between the uprights.
To begin, squat down be flexing your hips and knees until you can reach the handles.
After grasping them, begin your ascent. Driving through your heels extend your hips and knees keeping your hands hanging at your side. Keep your head and chest up throughout the movement.
After reaching a full standing position, Return to the starting position and repeat.
exercises.name: Cable Deadlifts
exercises.overview: Cable deadlifts provide a unique resistance profile that challenges the posterior chain and legs. This compound movement builds functional strength while improving hip mobility.
exercises.safetyInfo: Start with lighter weight to master the hip hinge pattern. Those with lower back issues should focus on proper form and consider alternatives if pain occurs.

commonMistakes: Pulling the rope to the chest or chin instead of face level
Using too much weight and allowing the shoulders to roll forward
Failing to separate the rope ends and missing the external rotation component
exerciseTips: Focus on pulling your elbows back rather than just moving your hands
Separate the rope ends as you pull to maximize external rotation
Keep your shoulders down and avoid shrugging during the movement
Squeeze your shoulder blades together at the end of each repetition
instructions: Set the cable machine to upper chest height and attach a rope handle.
Grab the rope with both hands using an overhand grip, thumbs pointing toward you.
Step back and assume a staggered stance with arms extended in front of you.
Pull the rope toward your face by driving your elbows back and separating the rope ends near your ears.
exercises.name: Cable Face Pull
exercises.overview: The cable face pull is a pulling exercise that targets the posterior deltoids and improves shoulder health through external rotation. It's excellent for counteracting forward head posture and rounded shoulders from excessive pressing movements.
exercises.safetyInfo: This exercise is generally safe for most individuals and is often used in rehabilitation settings. Start with lighter weight to master the movement pattern and external rotation component.

commonMistakes: Lifting the weight too high above shoulder level
Using momentum or swinging the body to assist the movement
Allowing the shoulders to roll forward during the exercise
exerciseTips: Keep your core engaged to prevent arching your back
Maintain a slight bend in your elbows throughout the movement
Focus on lifting with your front deltoids rather than using momentum
Keep your shoulders down and back, avoiding shoulder shrugging
instructions: Stand facing away from a low cable pulley with a straight bar or rope attachment.
Hold the attachment with both hands using an overhand grip, arms extended down in front of you.
Keep a slight bend in your elbows and maintain this throughout the movement.
Raise the attachment directly in front of you until your arms are parallel to the floor.
Slowly lower the weight back to the starting position with control.
exercises.name: Cable Front Raise
exercises.overview: Cable front raises isolate the front deltoids with constant tension throughout the range of motion. The cable provides smooth resistance for controlled shoulder development.
exercises.safetyInfo: Use a weight that allows for controlled movement throughout the full range of motion. Those with shoulder impingement should avoid lifting above shoulder height.

commonMistakes: Using arms instead of core to pull the weight
Allowing the torso to lean or collapse
Moving too quickly without control
exerciseTips: Keep your core braced throughout the entire movement
Maintain tall posture in your torso during the chop
Control the eccentric portion of the movement
Focus on rotation through your trunk, not your arms
instructions: Set cable machine to high position and attach a D-handle.
Kneel on inside knee (closest to cable) in half-kneeling position with outside leg planted firmly.
Grasp handle with both hands at shoulder height, arms extended.
Pull cable down and across your body toward outside hip in chopping motion.
Control the weight back to starting position.
Complete all reps on one side before switching positions.
exercises.name: Cable Half Kneeling Chop
exercises.overview: The cable half kneeling chop is an anti-rotation core exercise that trains rotational power and stability. It's excellent for developing functional core strength and improving athletic performance.
exercises.safetyInfo: Avoid this exercise if you have lower back injuries or shoulder impingement. Start with lighter weight to master the movement pattern before progressing.

commonMistakes: Using arms to pull instead of rotating through core
Losing tall posture during the lift
Moving too fast without proper control
exerciseTips: Brace your core to prevent unwanted rotation
Keep your torso tall throughout the movement
Focus on smooth, controlled lifting motion
Initiate movement from your core, not your arms
instructions: Set cable machine to low position and attach D-handle.
Kneel on inside knee (closest to cable) with outside leg planted.
Grasp handle with both hands at hip level, arms extended.
Pull cable up and across body toward opposite shoulder.
Control the weight back to starting position at hip.
Complete all reps before switching to opposite side.
exercises.name: Cable Half Kneeling Lift
exercises.overview: The cable half kneeling lift is an anti-rotation exercise that trains the core to resist unwanted movement while generating rotational power. It complements chopping movements and builds functional core strength.
exercises.safetyInfo: Safe for most individuals when performed with proper form. Those with shoulder impingement or lower back issues should start with lighter resistance.

commonMistakes: Swinging the body to generate momentum
Allowing elbows to drift forward during the curl
Not fully controlling the eccentric portion
exerciseTips: Keep your elbows pinned to your sides throughout the movement
Maintain a neutral wrist position during the curl
Squeeze your biceps at the top of the contraction
Control the lowering phase to maximize muscle activation
instructions: Attach a rope attachment to a low pulley and stand facing the machine about 12 inches away from it.
Grasp the rope with a neutral (palms-in) grip and stand straight up keeping the natural arch of the back and your torso stationary.
Put your elbows in by your side and keep them there stationary during the entire movement. Tip: Only the forearms should move; not your upper arms. This will be your starting position.
Using your biceps, pull your arms up as you exhale until your biceps touch your forearms. Tip: Remember to keep the elbows in and your upper arms stationary.
After a 1 second contraction where you squeeze your biceps, slowly start to bring the weight back to the original position.
Repeat for the recommended amount of repetitions.
exercises.name: Cable Hammer Curls - Rope Attachment
exercises.name_pt: Rosca Martelo na Polia com Corda
exercises.overview: Cable hammer curls with rope attachment target the biceps and forearms through a neutral grip position. The constant cable tension provides continuous muscle activation throughout the range of motion.
exercises.safetyInfo: This exercise is generally safe for most individuals. Those with elbow tendinitis should start with lighter weights and focus on controlled movements.

commonMistakes: Using momentum to swing the leg across
Allowing the torso to lean or twist
Moving too fast through the range of motion
exerciseTips: Keep your core engaged throughout the movement
Control the eccentric portion of the lift
Focus on squeezing your inner thigh muscles
Maintain a stable stance on your supporting leg
instructions: Stand in front of a low pulley facing forward with one leg next to the pulley and the other one away.
Attach the ankle cuff to the cable and also to the ankle of the leg that is next to the pulley.
Now step out and away from the stack with a wide stance and grasp the bar of the pulley system.
Stand on the foot that does not have the ankle cuff (the far foot) and allow the leg with the cuff to be pulled towards the low pulley. This will be your starting position.
Now perform the movement by moving the leg with the ankle cuff in front of the far leg by using the inner thighs to abduct the hip. Breathe out during this portion of the movement.
Slowly return to the starting position as you breathe in.
Repeat for the recommended amount of repetitions and then repeat the same movement with the opposite leg.
exercises.name: Cable Hip Adduction
exercises.overview: Cable hip adduction targets the inner thigh muscles using constant tension from a cable pulley system. This isolation exercise helps develop adductor strength and can improve hip stability.
exercises.safetyInfo: Avoid this exercise if you have groin strains or hip impingement. Start with lighter weight to assess comfort and range of motion.
variations: Side-Lying Hip Abduction With Mini Band

commonMistakes: Using arm muscles instead of lats
Moving too quickly through the eccentric phase
Allowing the shoulders to roll forward
exerciseTips: Keep your core tight against the bench
Focus on pulling with your lats rather than arms
Control the weight through the full range of motion
Squeeze your shoulder blades together at the bottom
instructions: Lie on incline an bench facing away from a high pulley machine that has a straight bar attachment on it.

Grasp the straight bar attachment overhead with a pronated (overhand; palms down) shoulder width grip and extend your arms in front of you. The bar should be around 2 inches away from your upper thighs. This will be your starting position.
Keeping the upper arms stationary, lift your arms back in a semi circle until the bar is straight over your head. Breathe in during this portion of the movement.
Slowly go back to the starting position using your lats and hold the contraction once you reach the starting position. Breathe out during the execution of this movement.
Repeat for the recommended amount of repetitions.
exercises.name: Cable Incline Pushdown
exercises.overview: The cable incline pushdown is a unique lat isolation exercise performed on an incline bench. It targets the latissimus dorsi through a full stretch and contraction pattern.
exercises.safetyInfo: Avoid if you have shoulder impingement or rotator cuff injuries. The incline position may place stress on the shoulder joint at end range.
variations: Bent Over Two-Dumbbell Row With Palms In

commonMistakes: Allowing elbows to flare out to the sides
Using shoulder movement to assist the lift
Bouncing at the bottom of the movement
exerciseTips: Keep your elbows stationary throughout the movement
Focus on extending only at the elbow joint
Control the negative portion slowly
Maintain tension in your triceps at the top
instructions: Lie on incline an bench facing away from a high pulley machine that has a straight bar attachment on it.

Grasp the straight bar attachment overhead with a pronated (overhand; palms down) narrow grip (less than shoulder width) and keep your elbows tucked in to your sides. Your upper arms should create around a 25 degree angle when measured from the floor.
Keeping the upper arms stationary, extend the arms as you flex the triceps. Breathe out during this portion of the movement and hold the contraction for a second.
Slowly go back to the starting position.
Repeat for the recommended amount of repetitions.
exercises.name: Cable Incline Triceps Extension
exercises.name_pt: Extensão de Tríceps na Polia Inclinado
exercises.overview: Cable incline triceps extension isolates the triceps muscles using an incline bench setup. The angle provides a unique stretch and allows for constant tension throughout the range of motion.
exercises.safetyInfo: Stop if you feel elbow pain or discomfort. The incline position can place stress on the shoulder joint, so warm up thoroughly before performing.

commonMistakes: Allowing the elbow to move away from the body
Using the wrist or forearm to generate movement
Moving too quickly through the range
exerciseTips: Keep your elbow pinned to your side
Move only at the shoulder joint
Control the movement in both directions
Start with light resistance to assess comfort
instructions: Sit next to a low pulley sideways (with legs stretched in front of you or crossed) and grasp the single hand cable attachment with the arm nearest to the cable. Tip: If you can adjust the pulley's height, you can use a flat bench to sit on instead.
Position the elbow against your side with the elbow bent at 90° and the arm pointing towards the pulley. This will be your starting position.

Pull the single hand cable attachment toward your body by internally rotating your shoulder until your forearm is across your abs. You will be creating an imaginary semi-circle. Tip: The forearm should be perpendicular to your torso at all times.
Slowly go back to the initial position.
Repeat for the recommended amount of repetitions and then repeat the movement with the next arm.
exercises.name: Cable Internal Rotation
exercises.overview: Cable internal rotation is a targeted exercise for strengthening the internal rotator muscles of the shoulder. It's commonly used in rehabilitation and injury prevention programs.
exercises.safetyInfo: This is commonly used for rehabilitation. Stop immediately if you feel sharp pain. Consult a healthcare provider if you have existing shoulder injuries.

commonMistakes: Bringing arms too far behind the body at the start
Using excessive arm bend that turns it into a press
Allowing the torso to lean forward excessively
exerciseTips: Maintain a slight bend in your elbows throughout
Focus on squeezing your chest muscles together
Keep your core engaged for stability
Control the weight through the full range of motion
instructions: Begin by moving the pulleys to the high position, select the resistance to be used, and take a handle in each hand.
Stand directly between both pulleys with your arms extended out to your sides. Your head and chest should be up while your arms form a "T". This will be your starting position.
Keeping the elbows extended, pull your arms straight to your sides.
Return your arms back to the starting position after a pause at the peak contraction.
Continue the movement for the prescribed number of repetitions.
exercises.name: Cable Iron Cross
exercises.overview: The cable iron cross is a chest isolation exercise that mimics a fly movement pattern. It provides constant tension throughout the range of motion and targets the pectoral muscles effectively.
exercises.safetyInfo: Be cautious with the stretch position as it can stress the shoulder joint. Start with lighter weight and gradually increase as flexibility improves.

commonMistakes: Using only arm strength instead of core rotation
Moving too quickly without control
Not shifting weight properly between feet
exerciseTips: Initiate the movement from your core
Keep your arms relatively straight throughout
Shift weight from foot to foot as you rotate
Control the return to starting position
instructions: Connect a rope attachment to a tower, and move the cable to the lowest pulley position. Stand with your side to the cable with a wide stance, and grab the rope with both hands.
Twist your body away from the pulley as you bring the rope over your shoulder like you're performing a judo flip.
Shift your weight between your feet as you twist and crunch forward, pulling the cable downward.
Return to the starting position and repeat until failure.
Then, reposition and repeat the same series of movements on the opposite side.
exercises.name: Cable Judo Flip
exercises.overview: The cable judo flip is a dynamic core exercise that combines rotation and anti-extension patterns. It mimics the throwing motion in judo while building functional core strength.
exercises.safetyInfo: Warm up your spine thoroughly before performing rotational exercises. Avoid if you have lower back issues or disc problems.

commonMistakes: Leaning back too far and turning the exercise into a row
Pulling the bar behind the neck which can strain the shoulders
exerciseTips: Focus on pulling with your lats rather than your arms
Keep your chest up and shoulders back throughout the movement
Squeeze your shoulder blades together at the bottom of each rep
Control the weight on both the pulling and returning phases
instructions: Sit at a lat pulldown machine and grab the wide bar with an overhand grip, hands positioned wider than shoulder-width.
Secure your thighs under the leg pads and sit with your torso upright and chest out.
Pull the bar down toward your upper chest by driving your elbows down and back.
Squeeze your shoulder blades together at the bottom of the movement while keeping your torso upright.
Slowly control the weight as you return the bar to the starting position with arms fully extended.
Repeat for the desired number of repetitions maintaining control throughout.
exercises.name: Cable Lat Pulldown Wide Grip
exercises.name_pt: Puxada Frontal na Polia - Pegada Aberta
exercises.overview: The wide grip lat pulldown is a fundamental pulling exercise that targets the latissimus dorsi and builds upper body pulling strength. The wide grip emphasizes the outer portion of the lats and helps develop the V-taper appearance.
exercises.safetyInfo: Avoid pulling the bar behind your neck as this can cause shoulder impingement. Those with shoulder issues should use a narrower grip or consider alternative exercises.

commonMistakes: Raising the arm too high above shoulder level
Using the traps to shrug the weight up instead of using the deltoids
Allowing the torso to lean away from the cable
exerciseTips: Keep a slight bend in your elbow throughout the movement
Lead with your pinky finger to maximize lateral deltoid activation
Control both the lifting and lowering phases of the movement
Avoid using momentum or swinging the weight
instructions: Set a cable machine to the lowest position and attach a D-handle.
Stand with the cable machine to one side and grab the handle with the far hand.
Position your feet shoulder-width apart with a slight bend in your knees.
Raise your arm out to the side until it reaches shoulder height, leading with your pinky finger.
Slowly lower the weight back to the starting position with control.
exercises.name: Cable Lateral Raise
exercises.overview: The cable lateral raise is an isolation exercise that specifically targets the lateral deltoid muscle. The cable provides constant tension throughout the range of motion, making it effective for building shoulder width and definition.
exercises.safetyInfo: This exercise is generally safe for most individuals. Start with light weight to avoid shoulder impingement. Stop if you experience any shoulder pain or discomfort.

commonMistakes: Using too much weight and compensating with body movement
Raising the arm too high above shoulder level
Allowing the cable to pull the arm across the body at the bottom
exerciseTips: Keep constant tension on the cable throughout the entire range of motion
Focus on leading with your pinky finger to optimize deltoid activation
Maintain a slight forward lean to align with the muscle fiber direction
Control both the lifting and lowering phases of the movement
instructions: Stand next to a cable machine with the cable set to the lowest position and attach a D-handle.
Grab the handle with the hand furthest from the machine and step away to create tension.
Keep your working arm slightly bent and maintain good posture with shoulders back.
Raise your arm out to the side until it reaches shoulder height, leading with your pinky.
Slowly lower the weight back to the starting position, maintaining tension throughout.
exercises.name: Cable Lateral Raise Single Arm
