exercises.overview: The Chain Squat is an advanced variation of the back squat that uses chains to create accommodating resistance, making the weight lighter at the bottom and heavier at the top. This technique helps develop explosive power and strength through the full range of motion.
exercises.safetyInfo: This is an advanced technique requiring mastery of regular back squats first. Ensure chains are properly secured and the correct length. Start with lighter chain weight to adapt to variable resistance. Use safety bars in a squat rack.

commonMistakes: Moving too quickly without controlling the chain instability
Allowing the hips to sag or pike during the movement
exerciseTips: Start with shorter chains for less instability initially
Keep your core extremely tight to control body position
Move slowly and deliberately to master the instability
Focus on maintaining straight body alignment throughout
instructions: Set up chains or suspension system at appropriate height for push up position.
Place your hands on the chain handles and assume plank position.
Maintain rigid body alignment from head to heels.
Lower your chest toward the chains while controlling the instability.
Press back up to starting position while stabilizing throughout.
Complete repetitions maintaining strict form despite the unstable surface.
exercises.name: Chain Suspended Push Up
exercises.overview: The chain suspended push up is performed with hands gripping chains or suspended handles, creating an unstable surface that dramatically increases the stability demands. It challenges proprioception and core control while building pressing strength.
exercises.safetyInfo: Chain suspended push ups require significant core stability and shoulder control. Master regular push ups and basic suspension training before attempting this advanced variation.

commonMistakes: Allowing knees to cave inward during the movement
Leaning too far forward and losing spinal alignment
Using momentum to stand up instead of controlled muscle engagement
exerciseTips: Keep your chest up and spine neutral throughout the movement
Drive through your heels when standing up
Control the descent slowly to build strength
Keep your knees aligned over your toes
instructions: To begin, first set the bar to a position that best matches your height. Once the bar is loaded, step under it and position it across the back of your shoulders.
Take the bar with your hands facing forward, unlock it and lift it off the rack by extending your legs.

Move your feet forward about 18 inches in front of the bar. Position your legs using a shoulder width stance with the toes slightly pointed out. Look forward at all times and maintain a neutral or slightly arched spine. This will be your starting position.
Slowly lower the bar by bending the knees as you maintain a straight posture with the head up. Continue down until the angle between the upper and lower leg breaks 90 degrees.
Begin to raise the bar as you exhale by pushing the floor with the heels of your feet, extending the knees and returning to the starting position.
Repeat for the recommended amount of repetitions.
exercises.name: Chair Squat
exercises.overview: Chair squats are an excellent beginner exercise that builds lower body strength while providing support and confidence for the squatting movement pattern.
exercises.safetyInfo: Ensure the chair is stable and positioned correctly. Avoid this exercise if you have acute knee or hip pain.

commonMistakes: Lifting the chest off the bench to use momentum
Allowing the shoulders to roll forward at the bottom position
Rowing the weights too high toward the shoulders instead of the ribcage
exerciseTips: Keep your chest firmly pressed against the bench throughout the movement
Focus on pulling your elbows back rather than just lifting the weight
Squeeze your shoulder blades together at the top for maximum activation
Maintain a neutral wrist position throughout the exercise
instructions: Set an incline bench to approximately 45 degrees and lie face down with your chest supported against the pad.
Grasp a dumbbell in each hand with a neutral grip, allowing your arms to hang straight down.
Pull the dumbbells up toward your lower ribcage, leading with your elbows and squeezing your shoulder blades.
Pause briefly at the top of the movement with your shoulder blades fully retracted.
Lower the dumbbells slowly back to the starting position with control.
exercises.name: Chest Supported Row
exercises.overview: The Chest Supported Row is an excellent beginner-friendly exercise that targets the lats and rhomboids while eliminating lower back involvement. The supported position allows for focused muscle development with reduced risk of injury.
exercises.safetyInfo: This exercise is beginner-friendly as the chest support eliminates lower back stress and prevents excessive momentum. Ensure the bench is stable and properly adjusted before beginning.

commonMistakes: Using momentum or swinging
Not achieving full range of motion
Flaring elbows out wide instead of keeping them close
exerciseTips: Keep elbows close to your sides
Focus on pulling your chest to the bar
Squeeze your shoulder blades together
Control the negative portion slowly
instructions: Grab the pull-up bar with the palms facing your torso and a grip closer than the shoulder width.

As you have both arms extended in front of you holding the bar at the chosen grip width, keep your torso as straight as possible while creating a curvature on your lower back and sticking your chest out. This is your starting position. Tip: Keeping the torso as straight as possible maximizes biceps stimulation while minimizing back involvement.

As you breathe out, pull your torso up until your head is around the level of the pull-up bar. Concentrate on using the biceps muscles in order to perform the movement. Keep the elbows close to your body. Tip: The upper torso should remain stationary as it moves through space and only the arms should move. The forearms should do no other work other than hold the bar.

After a second of squeezing the biceps in the contracted position, slowly lower your torso back to the starting position; when your arms are fully extended. Breathe in as you perform this portion of the movement.
Repeat this motion for the prescribed amount of repetitions.
exercises.name: Chin-Up
exercises.overview: A bodyweight pulling exercise that primarily targets the lats and biceps using an underhand grip for maximum bicep activation.
exercises.safetyInfo: Progress gradually if new to pull-ups. Those with shoulder impingement or elbow issues should be cautious with the supinated grip.

commonMistakes: Not generating enough power to achieve sufficient airtime
Landing too hard and losing form
Attempting the exercise before mastering explosive regular push-ups
exerciseTips: Generate maximum explosive power from the bottom position
Keep your core tight to maintain body alignment during the explosive phase
Land softly to absorb the impact
Start with single claps before attempting multiple claps
instructions: Start in a standard push-up position with hands shoulder-width apart.
Lower your body into a push-up with controlled form.
Explosively press up with maximum force to launch your hands off the ground.
Quickly clap your hands together while airborne.
Land with hands back in starting position and immediately descend into the next repetition.
exercises.name: Clap Push Up
exercises.overview: Clap push-ups are an advanced plyometric exercise that develops explosive upper body power. The exercise requires generating enough force to become airborne and clap before landing, making it excellent for athletic power development.
exercises.safetyInfo: This advanced plyometric exercise places high stress on wrists, elbows, and shoulders. Only attempt after mastering standard push-ups and building significant upper body power. Use proper landing technique to prevent injury.

commonMistakes: Using leg drive during the press portion
Arching the back excessively during the press
Not fully extending the arms at the top
exerciseTips: Pause briefly in the clean position before pressing
Press the bar straight up without using leg drive
Keep your core tight throughout the press phase
Lower the bar under control to the front rack position
instructions: Assume a shoulder-width stance, with knees inside the arms. Now while keeping the back flat, bend at the knees and hips so that you can grab the bar with the arms fully extended and a pronated grip that is slightly wider than shoulder width. Point the elbows out to sides. The bar should be close to the shins. Position the shoulders over or slightly ahead of the bar. Establish a flat back posture. This will be your starting position.

Begin to pull the bar by extending the knees. Move your hips forward and raise the shoulders at the same rate while keeping the angle of the back constant; continue to lift the bar straight up while keeping it close to your body.

As the bar passes the knee, extend at the ankles, knees, and hips forcefully, similar to a jumping motion. As you do so, continue to guide the bar with your hands, shrugging your shoulders and using the momentum from your movement to pull the bar as high as possible. The bar should travel close to your body, and you should keep your elbows out.

At maximum elevation, your feet should clear the floor and you should start to pull yourself under the bar. The mechanics of this could change slightly, depending on the weight used. You should descend into a squatting position as you pull yourself under the bar.

As the bar hits terminal height, rotate your elbows around and under the bar. Rack the bar across the front of the shoulders while keeping the torso erect and flexing the hips and knees to absorb the weight of the bar.
Stand to full height, holding the bar in the clean position.
Without moving your feet, press the bar overhead as you exhale. Lower the bar under control .
exercises.name: Clean and Press
exercises.overview: The clean and press combines Olympic lifting technique with strict overhead pressing strength. It develops explosive power in the clean phase followed by pure pressing strength.
exercises.safetyInfo: Requires good shoulder mobility and stability. Avoid if you have shoulder impingement or lower back issues. Use lighter weights than clean and jerk.

commonMistakes: Landing with locked elbows
Insufficient explosive force to become airborne
Poor body alignment during flight
exerciseTips: Generate maximum explosive force through your hands
Land softly to absorb impact
Keep core tight throughout the movement
Focus on controlled landing before next rep
instructions: Move into a prone position on the floor, supporting your weight on your hands and toes.
Your arms should be fully extended with the hands around shoulder width. Keep your body straight throughout the movement. This will be your starting position.
Descend by flexing at the elbow, lowering your chest toward the ground.
At the bottom, reverse the motion by pushing yourself up through elbow extension as quickly as possible until you are air borne. Aim to "jump" 12-18 inches to one side.
As you accelerate up, move your outside foot away from your direction of travel. Leaving the ground, shift your body about 30 degrees for the next repetition.
Return to the starting position and repeat the exercise, working all the way around until you are back where you started.
exercises.name: Clock Push-Up
exercises.overview: An advanced plyometric push-up variation that combines explosive power with rotational movement. Develops upper body power, coordination, and challenges stability in multiple planes.
exercises.safetyInfo: High impact exercise that stresses wrists, elbows, and shoulders. Not recommended for those with joint issues or insufficient push-up strength. Ensure adequate space around workout area.

commonMistakes: Grip too narrow causing wrist strain
Elbows flaring out wide like regular bench
Touching bar too high on chest
exerciseTips: Keep elbows tucked close to your sides
Lower the bar to mid-chest level
Press straight up following natural bar path
Squeeze triceps at the top
instructions: Lie back on a flat bench. Using a close grip (around shoulder width), lift the bar from the rack and hold it straight over you with your arms locked. This will be your starting position.

As you breathe in, come down slowly until you feel the bar on your middle chest. Tip: Make sure that - as opposed to a regular bench press - you keep the elbows close to the torso at all times in order to maximize triceps involvement.

After a second pause, bring the bar back to the starting position as you breathe out and push the bar using your triceps muscles. Lock your arms in the contracted position, hold for a second and then start coming down slowly again. Tip: It should take at least twice as long to go down than to come up.
Repeat the movement for the prescribed amount of repetitions.
When you are done, place the bar back in the rack.
exercises.name: Close-Grip Barbell Bench Press
exercises.overview: A compound pressing movement that primarily targets the triceps while still engaging chest and shoulders. Excellent for building tricep mass and pressing strength.
exercises.safetyInfo: Use a spotter when working with heavy weights. Grip should be shoulder-width, not extremely narrow to avoid wrist stress.

commonMistakes: Allowing dumbbells to separate during movement
Using too heavy weight compromising form
Pressing dumbbells at an angle instead of straight up
exerciseTips: Squeeze dumbbells together throughout the movement
Keep elbows close to your body
Lower with control to maximize tricep stretch
Press dumbbells straight up
instructions: Place a dumbbell standing up on a flat bench.

Ensuring that the dumbbell stays securely placed at the top of the bench, lie perpendicular to the bench with only your shoulders lying on the surface. Hips should be below the bench and your legs bent with your feet firmly on the floor.

Grasp the dumbbell with both hands and hold it straight over your chest at arm's length. Both palms should be pressing against the underside of the sides of the dumbbell. This will be your starting position.
Initiate the movement by lowering the dumbbell to your chest.
Return to the starting position by extending the elbows.
exercises.name: Close-Grip Dumbbell Press
exercises.overview: A dumbbell pressing variation that emphasizes tricep development through a neutral grip and close hand position. Provides constant tension through the squeeze component.
exercises.safetyInfo: Ensure dumbbells are secure in grip to prevent dropping. Start with lighter weight to master the coordination required for this movement.

commonMistakes: Swinging body to lift weight
Moving elbows forward during curl
Using momentum instead of muscle control
exerciseTips: Keep elbows pinned to your sides
Squeeze biceps hard at the top
Lower slowly with control
Focus on the inner bicep contraction
instructions: Stand up with your torso upright while holding an E-Z Curl Bar at the closer inner handle. The palm of your hands should be facing forward and they should be slightly tilted inwards due to the shape of the bar. The elbows should be close to the torso. This will be your starting position.
While holding the upper arms stationary, curl the weights forward while contracting the biceps as you breathe out. Tip: Only the forearms should move.
Continue the movement until your biceps are fully contracted and the bar is at shoulder level. Hold the contracted position for a second and squeeze the biceps hard.
Slowly begin to bring the bar back to starting position as your breathe in.
Repeat for the recommended amount of repetitions.
exercises.name: Close-Grip EZ Bar Curl
exercises.name_pt: Rosca Direta com Barra W - Pegada Fechada
exercises.overview: An isolation exercise targeting the biceps with emphasis on the inner/short head due to the close grip. The EZ bar provides a more comfortable wrist position than a straight bar.
exercises.safetyInfo: The EZ bar reduces wrist strain compared to straight bar. Avoid lifting too heavy which can cause elbow or shoulder stress.
variations: Seated One-Arm Dumbbell Palms-Down Wrist Curl

commonMistakes: Stepping too far forward reducing band tension
Rushing through the negative portion
Allowing band to snap back uncontrolled
exerciseTips: Step firmly on band center for stable base
Control the increased resistance at the top
Fight the band tension on the way down
Keep constant tension throughout range
instructions: Attach a band to each end of the bar. Take the bar, placing a foot on the middle of the band. Stand upright with a narrow, supinated grip on the EZ bar. The elbows should be close to the torso. This will be your starting position.
While keeping the upper arms in place, flex the elbows to execute the curl. Exhale as the weight is lifted.
Continue the movement until your biceps are fully contracted and the bar is at shoulder level. Hold the contracted position for a second and squeeze the biceps hard.
Slowly begin to bring the bar back to starting position as your breathe in.
Repeat for the recommended amount of repetitions.
exercises.name: Close-Grip EZ-Bar Curl with Band
exercises.name_pt: Rosca Direta com Barra W e Faixa Elástica - Pegada Fechada
exercises.overview: A bicep isolation exercise using accommodating resistance where the band increases difficulty at peak contraction. Provides variable resistance throughout the range of motion for enhanced muscle development.
exercises.safetyInfo: Inspect band for wear or damage before use. Ensure proper foot placement to prevent band from slipping during exercise.

commonMistakes: Allowing elbows to flare out wide
Lowering the bar too high toward the neck
Using too much weight and compromising form
exerciseTips: Keep elbows tucked close to your sides throughout the movement
Lower the bar to your lower chest rather than your neck
Focus on squeezing triceps at the top of each rep
Maintain control during the eccentric (lowering) phase
instructions: Lie on a flat bench with an EZ bar loaded to an appropriate weight.
Using a narrow grip lift the bar and hold it straight over your torso with your elbows in. The arms should be perpendicular to the floor. This will be your starting position.
Now lower the bar down to your lower chest as you breathe in. Keep the elbows in as you perform this movement.
Using the triceps to push the bar back up, press it back to the starting position by extending the elbows as you exhale.
Repeat.
keywords: ez bar close grip bench press
close grip ez curl bar press
exercises.name: Close-Grip EZ-Bar Press
exercises.overview: The close-grip EZ-bar press is a compound triceps exercise that targets all three heads of the triceps while minimizing wrist strain. It's an excellent alternative to close-grip barbell pressing for building triceps strength and mass.
exercises.safetyInfo: Avoid lowering the bar to your neck or throat area as this can cause shoulder impingement. Use a spotter when lifting heavy weights.

commonMistakes: Pulling the bar behind the neck
Using momentum and swinging the torso
Not achieving full range of motion at the top
exerciseTips: Pull the bar to your upper chest, not behind your neck
Lean back slightly and stick your chest out
Focus on pulling with your lats rather than your arms
Squeeze shoulder blades together at the bottom
instructions: Sit down on a pull-down machine with a wide bar attached to the top pulley. Make sure that you adjust the knee pad of the machine to fit your height. These pads will prevent your body from being raised by the resistance attached to the bar.

Grab the bar with the palms facing forward using the prescribed grip. Note on grips: For a wide grip, your hands need to be spaced out at a distance wider than your shoulder width. For a medium grip, your hands need to be spaced out at a distance equal to your shoulder width and for a close grip at a distance smaller than your shoulder width.

As you have both arms extended in front of you - while holding the bar at the chosen grip width - bring your torso back around 30 degrees or so while creating a curvature on your lower back and sticking your chest out. This is your starting position.

As you breathe out, bring the bar down until it touches your upper chest by drawing the shoulders and the upper arms down and back. Tip: Concentrate on squeezing the back muscles once you reach the full contracted position. The upper torso should remain stationary (only the arms should move). The forearms should do no other work except for holding the bar; therefore do not try to pull the bar down using the forearms.

After a second in the contracted position, while squeezing your shoulder blades together, slowly raise the bar back to the starting position when your arms are fully extended and the lats are fully stretched. Inhale during this portion of the movement.
6. Repeat this motion for the prescribed amount of repetitions.
exercises.name: Close-Grip Front Lat Pulldown
exercises.overview: The close-grip front lat pulldown targets the latissimus dorsi with emphasis on the lower portion while also engaging the biceps and middle back. The close grip allows for greater range of motion and lat activation.
exercises.safetyInfo: Never pull the bar behind your neck as this can cause shoulder impingement and cervical spine injury. Maintain control throughout the full range of motion.
variations: Bent Over Two-Dumbbell Row With Palms In

commonMistakes: Allowing hips to sag or pike up
Placing hands too wide on the dumbbell
Not maintaining core engagement
exerciseTips: Keep your body in a straight line from head to heels
Maintain a tight grip on the dumbbell handle
Keep elbows close to your body throughout the movement
Control the descent and avoid bouncing at the bottom
instructions: Lie on the floor and place your hands on an upright dumbbell. Supporting your weight on your toes and hands, keep your torso rigid and your elbows in with your arms straight. This will be your starting position.
Lower your body, allowing the elbows to flex while you inhale. Keep your body straight, not allowing your hips to rise or sag.
Press yourself back up to the starting position by extending the elbows. Breathe out as you perform this step.
After a pause at the contracted position, repeat the movement for the prescribed amount of repetitions.
exercises.name: Close-Grip Push-Up off of a Dumbbell
exercises.overview: This advanced push-up variation uses a dumbbell to create an unstable surface while targeting the triceps with a close grip position. It challenges core stability while building upper body strength.
exercises.safetyInfo: Ensure the dumbbell is stable and won't roll. Start with a lighter weight dumbbell to test stability before progressing to heavier weights.

commonMistakes: Using too wide of a grip
Swinging the body to generate momentum
Not controlling the eccentric portion
exerciseTips: Keep elbows pinned to your sides throughout the movement
Maintain a straight torso and avoid swinging
Squeeze biceps hard at the top of each rep
Control the weight on the way down
instructions: Hold a barbell with both hands, palms up and a few inches apart.

Stand with your torso straight and your head up. Your feet should be about shoulder width and your elbows close to your torso. This will be your starting position. Tip: You will keep your upper arms and elbows stationary throughout the movement.

Curl the bar up in a semicircular motion until the forearms touch your biceps. Exhale as you perform this portion of the movement and contract your biceps hard for a second at the top. Tip: Avoid bending the back or using swinging motions as you lift the weight. Only the forearms should move.
Slowly go back down to the starting position as you inhale.
Repeat for the recommended amount of repetitions.
exercises.name: Close-Grip Standing Barbell Curl
exercises.name_pt: Rosca Direta com Barra - Pegada Fechada
exercises.overview: The close-grip standing barbell curl emphasizes the inner portion of the biceps and provides a different stimulus than wide-grip variations. It's excellent for building bicep peak and overall arm mass.
exercises.safetyInfo: Avoid using excessive weight that forces you to swing or use momentum. Keep wrists in a neutral position to prevent strain.

commonMistakes: Moving arms and legs without engaging the core
Rushing through the movement without control
Not achieving full range of motion in either direction
exerciseTips: Coordinate the upper and lower body movement simultaneously
Focus on flexing your spine rather than just moving your limbs
Exhale forcefully as you crunch up
Control the movement back to starting position
instructions: Begin by lying on your back on the ground. Your legs should be straight and your arms extended behind your head. This will be your starting position.

To perform the movement, tuck the knees toward your chest, rotating your pelvis to lift your glutes from the floor. As you do so, flex the spine, bringing your arms back over your head to perform a simultaneous crunch motion.
After a brief pause, return to the starting position.
exercises.name: Cocoons
exercises.overview: Cocoons are a dynamic abdominal exercise that simultaneously works both the upper and lower portions of the rectus abdominis. This full-body crunch variation provides comprehensive core activation.
exercises.safetyInfo: Perform on a padded surface to protect your lower back. Avoid this exercise if you have lower back issues or herniated discs.

commonMistakes: Rushing through the lateral movement without control
Favoring one side over the other
Using excessive momentum to complete the movement
exerciseTips: Control the lateral movement to maximize muscle activation
Keep your core tight to prevent excessive swaying
Focus on pulling to each side equally
Maintain proper pulling mechanics despite the lateral movement
instructions: Hang from a pull-up bar with an overhand grip, hands shoulder-width apart.
Pull yourself up and to one side, bringing your head to one side of the bar.
Lower yourself with control back to the dead hang starting position.
Pull yourself up to the opposite side, alternating the direction with each rep.
Continue alternating sides for the desired number of repetitions.
exercises.name: Commando Pull Up
exercises.overview: Commando pull-ups add a lateral component to traditional pull-ups by alternating which side of the bar you pull to. This advanced variation challenges the lats, core stability, and coordination while building functional upper body strength.
exercises.safetyInfo: This advanced variation places additional stress on the shoulders and core. Ensure you can perform regular pull-ups proficiently before attempting. Avoid if you have shoulder instability or neck issues.

commonMistakes: Allowing the upper arm to lift off the thigh during the curl
Using momentum or swinging the weight up
Not achieving full range of motion at the bottom
exerciseTips: Keep your upper arm pressed firmly against your inner thigh throughout the movement
Focus on squeezing the bicep at the top of the movement
Control the eccentric portion by lowering the weight slowly
Ensure your little finger is higher than your thumb at peak contraction
instructions: Sit down on a flat bench with one dumbbell in front of you between your legs. Your legs should be spread with your knees bent and feet on the floor.

Use your right arm to pick the dumbbell up. Place the back of your right upper arm on the top of your inner right thigh. Rotate the palm of your hand until it is facing forward away from your thigh. Tip: Your arm should be extended and the dumbbell should be above the floor. This will be your starting position.

While holding the upper arm stationary, curl the weights forward while contracting the biceps as you breathe out. Only the forearms should move. Continue the movement until your biceps are fully contracted and the dumbbells are at shoulder level. Tip: At the top of the movement make sure that the little finger of your arm is higher than your thumb. This guarantees a good contraction. Hold the contracted position for a second as you squeeze the biceps.
Slowly begin to bring the dumbbells back to starting position as your breathe in. Caution: Avoid swinging motions at any time.
Repeat for the recommended amount of repetitions. Then repeat the movement with the left arm.
exercises.name: Concentration Curls
exercises.overview: Concentration curls are an isolation exercise that targets the biceps by eliminating momentum and body assistance. The seated position and arm bracing allows for maximum muscle isolation and peak contraction.
exercises.safetyInfo: Avoid using excessive weight that forces you to use momentum. Stop if you experience any elbow or wrist pain.

commonMistakes: Allowing the bar to drift away from the body during the lift
Hyperextending the back at the top of the movement
Starting with hips too low, creating an inefficient pulling position
exerciseTips: Keep the bar path vertical and close to your body throughout the movement
Engage your lats to prevent the bar from drifting forward
Drive through your whole foot, not just your heels
Maintain a neutral neck position by looking straight ahead
instructions: Stand with feet hip-width apart, toes under the barbell with the bar over mid-foot.
Bend at hips and knees to grip the bar with hands just outside your legs using an overhand or mixed grip.
Keep your chest up, shoulders back, and maintain a neutral spine with the bar close to your shins.
Drive through your heels and extend your hips and knees simultaneously to lift the bar.
Stand tall with shoulders back and hips fully extended at the top.
Lower the bar by pushing your hips back first, then bending your knees to return the bar to the floor.
exercises.name: Conventional Deadlift
exercises.overview: The conventional deadlift is a fundamental hip-hinge movement that develops posterior chain strength and power. It's considered one of the most effective exercises for building overall strength and muscle mass.
exercises.safetyInfo: Avoid this exercise if you have lower back injuries or disc problems. Always warm up thoroughly and start with lighter weights to master the movement pattern before progressing to heavier loads.

commonMistakes: Allowing the knee of the working leg to cave inward
Rounding the back instead of maintaining an upright torso
Not keeping the non-working leg completely straight
exerciseTips: Keep your chest up and avoid rounding your back during the movement
Maintain weight on the heel of your working leg throughout the squat
Point the toe of your straight leg upward to increase the stretch
Start with partial range of motion and gradually work toward full depth
instructions: Stand with feet wider than shoulder-width apart and toes pointing forward.
Shift your weight to one side and sit back into that hip while keeping the other leg straight.
Lower down until your working thigh is parallel to the ground with your straight leg's toe pointed up.
Keep your chest up and the heel of your working leg planted on the ground.
Push through your working heel to return to the center starting position and repeat on the other side.
exercises.name: Cossack Squat
exercises.overview: The Cossack squat is a lateral squatting movement that targets one leg at a time while the other remains straight. It improves hip mobility, strengthens legs in multiple planes, and enhances overall functional movement.
exercises.safetyInfo: This exercise requires good hip and ankle mobility. Those with hip, knee, or groin injuries should approach cautiously and may need to modify the range of motion. Build up flexibility gradually.

commonMistakes: Allowing hips to drop toward the ground during movement
Moving too quickly and losing control of the position
exerciseTips: Keep your hips elevated throughout the entire movement
Engage your core to prevent sagging in the middle
Move slowly and controlled to maintain proper form
Look straight ahead rather than down at your feet
instructions: Sit on the floor with knees bent and feet flat, hands planted behind you with fingers pointing toward your body.
Lift your hips off the ground, supporting your weight on your hands and feet in a reverse tabletop position.
Keep your core engaged and maintain a straight line from knees to shoulders.
Walk forward by moving opposite hand and foot simultaneously, maintaining the crab position throughout.
Continue for desired distance or time, then return to starting position.
exercises.name: Crab Walk
exercises.overview: The crab walk is a bodyweight exercise performed in a reverse tabletop position that strengthens the triceps, shoulders, and core while improving coordination. It's an excellent functional movement that builds posterior chain strength and stability.
exercises.safetyInfo: Avoid this exercise if you have wrist, shoulder, or lower back injuries. Those with carpal tunnel syndrome should be cautious due to the weight-bearing position on the hands.

commonMistakes: Rotating the wrist during the curl motion
Using the torso to help lift the weight
Not bringing the weight fully across the body to the opposite shoulder
exerciseTips: Maintain neutral wrist position throughout the movement
Touch the dumbbell to your opposite shoulder at the peak
Keep your core engaged to prevent body sway
Alternate arms in a controlled rhythm
instructions: Stand up straight with a dumbbell in each hand. Your hands should be down at your side with your palms facing in.

While keeping your palms facing in and without twisting your arm, curl the dumbbell of the right arm up towards your left shoulder as you exhale. Touch the top of the dumbbell to your shoulder and hold the contraction for a second.
Slowly lower the dumbbell along the same path as you inhale and then repeat the same movement for the left arm.
Continue alternating in this fashion until the recommended amount of repetitions is performed for each arm.
exercises.name: Cross Body Hammer Curl
exercises.overview: Cross body hammer curls target the biceps and brachialis while incorporating a crossover movement pattern. This variation enhances muscle activation through the diagonal plane of motion.
exercises.safetyInfo: Keep movements controlled to avoid shoulder impingement. Use appropriate weight to maintain proper form throughout all repetitions.

commonMistakes: Allowing the torso to lean toward the heavier side
Hiking the shoulder up on the rack side
exerciseTips: Focus on keeping your shoulders level throughout the walk
Engage your core to resist the uneven loading pattern
Take deliberate steps and maintain good posture
Keep the racked kettlebell close to your body
instructions: Hold one kettlebell in the rack position on one side and another kettlebell at your side in the opposite hand.
Maintain an upright posture with shoulders level and core engaged.
Walk forward with controlled steps while resisting lateral flexion.
Keep both shoulders square and avoid tilting to either side.
Complete the prescribed distance then switch positions and repeat.
exercises.name: Cross Carry
exercises.overview: The cross carry combines two different carry positions simultaneously, creating an asymmetrical load that challenges core stability and postural control. It trains the body to resist unwanted movement patterns.
exercises.safetyInfo: This exercise creates significant anti-lateral flexion demands. Those with lower back issues should start with lighter weights and shorter distances.

commonMistakes: Allowing arms to drift too far behind the torso at the start
Using jerky or uncontrolled movements
Not maintaining consistent elbow angle throughout the exercise
exerciseTips: Keep a slight bend in your elbows throughout the movement
Squeeze your chest muscles at the peak contraction
Control the resistance on the return to starting position
Maintain tension in the band at all times
instructions: Secure an exercise band around a stationary post.
While facing away from the post, grab the handles on both ends of the band and step forward enough to create tension on the band.

Raise your arms to the sides, parallel to the floor, perpendicular to your torso (your torso and the arms should resemble the letter "T") and with the palms facing forward. Have them extended with a slight bend at the elbows. This will be your starting position.
While keeping your arms straight, bring them across your chest in a semicircular motion to the front as you exhale and flex your pecs. Hold the contraction for a second.
Slowly return to the starting position as you inhale.
Perform for the recommended amount of repetitions.
exercises.name: Cross Over - With Bands
exercises.overview: Band crossovers provide constant tension chest training that mimics cable crossover movements. This exercise effectively targets the pectorals through a full range of motion with variable resistance.
exercises.safetyInfo: Ensure the resistance band is securely anchored to prevent snapping back. Avoid overstretching the chest muscles in the starting position.

commonMistakes: Pulling on the neck with the hands
Only moving the elbow instead of the entire shoulder
Rushing through the movement without proper muscle contraction
exerciseTips: Focus on bringing your shoulder toward your knee, not just your elbow
Contract your abs before initiating the movement
Keep your lower back pressed against the floor
Breathe out during the crunch phase
instructions: Lie flat on your back and bend your knees about 60 degrees.
Keep your feet flat on the floor and place your hands loosely behind your head. This will be your starting position.

Now curl up and bring your right elbow and shoulder across your body while bring your left knee in toward your left shoulder at the same time. Reach with your elbow and try to touch your knee. Exhale as you perform this movement. Tip: Try to bring your shoulder up towards your knee rather than just your elbow and remember that the key is to contract the abs as you perform the movement; not just to move the elbow.
Now go back down to the starting position as you inhale and repeat with the left elbow and the right knee.
Continue alternating in this manner until all prescribed repetitions are done.
exercises.name: Cross-Body Crunch
exercises.overview: Cross-body crunches target the rectus abdominis and obliques through a diagonal movement pattern. This exercise enhances core stability and rotational strength.
exercises.safetyInfo: Keep hands loosely behind the head to avoid neck strain. Stop if you experience any lower back discomfort.

commonMistakes: Moving arms forward during the crunch
Lifting too high and engaging hip flexors
Using momentum instead of controlled movement
exerciseTips: Keep arms aligned with head and shoulders throughout
Focus on lifting shoulder blades just off the floor
Exhale forcefully during the crunch
Hold contraction for one second at the top
instructions: Lie on the floor with your back flat and knees bent with around a 60-degree angle between the hamstrings and the calves.
Keep your feet flat on the floor and stretch your arms overhead with your palms crossed. This will be your starting position.

Curl your upper body forward and bring your shoulder blades just off the floor. At all times, keep your arms aligned with your head, neck and shoulder. Don't move them forward from that position. Exhale as you perform this portion of the movement and hold the contraction for a second.
Slowly lower down to the starting position as you inhale.
Repeat for the recommended amount of repetitions.
exercises.name: Crunch - Hands Overhead
exercises.name_pt: Abdominal com Mãos Acima da Cabeça
exercises.overview: This crunch variation increases difficulty by extending the arms overhead, creating a longer lever arm that challenges the core more intensely than standard crunches.
exercises.safetyInfo: Avoid if you have neck issues. The overhead arm position increases difficulty significantly, so start with fewer reps.

commonMistakes: Allowing lower back to lift off floor
Pulling on neck with hands
Using the ball to push off with legs
exerciseTips: Keep feet touching and toes pointed inward
Press lower back into floor throughout movement
Lift shoulders only 4 inches off ground
Control the movement - avoid using momentum
instructions: Lie flat on your back with your feet resting on an exercise ball and your knees bent at a 90 degree angle.
Place your feet three to four inches apart and point your toes inward so they touch.
Place your hands lightly on either side of your head keeping your elbows in. Tip: Don't lock your fingers behind your head.
Push the small of your back down in the floor in order to better isolate your abdominal muscles. This will be your starting position.

Begin to roll your shoulders off the floor and continue to push down as hard as you can with your lower back. Your shoulders should come up off the floor only about four inches, and your lower back should remain on the floor. Breathe out as you execute this portion of the movement. Squeeze your abdominals hard at the top of the contraction and hold for a second. Tip: Focus on a slow, controlled movement. Refrain from using momentum at any time.
Slowly go back down to the starting position as you inhale.
Repeat for the recommended amount of repetitions.
exercises.name: Crunch - Legs On Exercise Ball
exercises.name_pt: Abdominal com Pernas na Bola Suíça
exercises.overview: This crunch variation uses an exercise ball to elevate the legs, which helps maintain proper hip angle and reduces hip flexor involvement while targeting the rectus abdominis.
exercises.safetyInfo: Ensure the exercise ball is properly inflated and stable. Avoid if you have lower back pain or instability issues.

commonMistakes: Pulling on neck with interlocked fingers
Lifting too high and engaging hip flexors
Using momentum to swing up
exerciseTips: Keep lower back pressed against floor
Lift shoulders only 4 inches off ground
Breathe out during the crunch phase
Focus on slow, controlled movement
instructions: Lie flat on your back with your feet flat on the ground, or resting on a bench with your knees bent at a 90 degree angle. If you are resting your feet on a bench, place them three to four inches apart and point your toes inward so they touch.
Now place your hands lightly on either side of your head keeping your elbows in. Tip: Don't lock your fingers behind your head.
While pushing the small of your back down in the floor to better isolate your abdominal muscles, begin to roll your shoulders off the floor.

Continue to push down as hard as you can with your lower back as you contract your abdominals and exhale. Your shoulders should come up off the floor only about four inches, and your lower back should remain on the floor. At the top of the movement, contract your abdominals hard and keep the contraction for a second. Tip: Focus on slow, controlled movement - don't cheat yourself by using momentum.
After the one second contraction, begin to come down slowly again to the starting position as you inhale.
Repeat for the recommended amount of repetitions.
exercises.name: Crunches
exercises.overview: The basic crunch is a fundamental abdominal exercise that targets the rectus abdominis by flexing the spine while keeping the lower back stable on the floor.
exercises.safetyInfo: Place hands lightly beside head rather than behind to avoid neck strain. Stop if you experience lower back pain.

commonMistakes: Allowing upper arms to drop below parallel
Rushing through the external rotation phase
Using too much weight and compromising form
exerciseTips: Keep upper arms parallel to floor throughout
Externally rotate shoulders before pressing
Control the movement in both directions
Start with light weight to master the pattern
instructions: Take a dumbbell in each hand with a pronated grip in a standing position. Raise your upper arms so that they are parallel to the floor, allowing your lower arms to hang in the "scarecrow" position. This will be your starting position.

To initiate the movement, externally rotate the shoulders to move the upper arm 180 degrees. Keep the upper arms in place, rotating the upper arms until the wrists are directly above the elbows, the forearms perpendicular to the floor.
Now press the dumbbells by extending at the elbows, straightening your arms overhead.
Return to the starting position as you breathe in by reversing the steps.
Repeat for the recommended amount of repetitions.
exercises.name: Cuban Press
exercises.overview: The Cuban Press combines external shoulder rotation with an overhead press, making it excellent for shoulder stability and rotator cuff strengthening while building deltoid strength.
exercises.safetyInfo: Start with very light weights as this exercise places significant stress on the rotator cuff. Avoid if you have shoulder impingement or rotator cuff injuries.

commonMistakes: Placing too much weight on the back leg instead of the front leg
Allowing the front knee to drift past the toes
Stepping too far behind, which limits range of motion and stability
exerciseTips: Keep most of your weight on your front leg throughout the movement
Maintain an upright torso and avoid leaning forward
Focus on dropping your hips straight down rather than forward
Engage your core to maintain stability during the crossing motion
instructions: Stand with feet hip-width apart and hands on your hips or in front of your chest.
Step your right foot diagonally behind your left leg, crossing behind your body.
Lower your body by bending both knees until your front thigh is parallel to the ground.
Keep your weight on your front heel and maintain an upright torso throughout the movement.
Push through your front heel to return to the starting position and repeat on the other side.
exercises.name: Curtsy Lunge
exercises.overview: The curtsy lunge is a multi-planar exercise where you step one leg diagonally behind the other in a curtsy-like motion. It effectively targets the glutes while improving balance and hip stability.
exercises.safetyInfo: This exercise involves crossing movement patterns that may challenge balance. Those with knee injuries should use caution with the twisting motion. Start with bodyweight only before adding resistance.

commonMistakes: Using too wide of a stance, reducing quad emphasis
Leaning forward excessively during the squat
Allowing knees to cave inward during the movement
exerciseTips: Keep your torso as upright as possible throughout the movement
Focus on driving your knees forward over your toes
Maintain tension in your core to support your spine
Control the tempo on both the descent and ascent
instructions: Set up a barbell in a squat rack at shoulder height and position it on your upper traps.
Place your feet in a narrow stance with heels elevated on weight plates, toes pointing forward.
Unrack the bar and take a step back, maintaining an upright torso position.
Descend by bending at the knees and hips, keeping your torso as vertical as possible.
Squat down until your thighs are parallel or below, then drive up through your heels to return to start.
exercises.name: Cyclist Squat
exercises.overview: The cyclist squat uses a narrow stance and elevated heels to maximize quadriceps activation while minimizing hip involvement. It's particularly effective for building the quad strength needed for cycling and knee-dominant activities.
exercises.safetyInfo: This exercise places high demands on the knees and ankles. Avoid if you have knee injuries or limited ankle mobility. Start with lighter weights to assess comfort and range of motion.

commonMistakes: Allowing back to arch when extending leg
Moving too quickly through the range of motion
Losing 90-degree position with stationary leg
exerciseTips: Maintain flat back position throughout
Exhale hard to engage core before moving
Lower leg until just above ground
Keep non-moving leg stable at 90 degrees
instructions: Begin lying on your back with your hands extended above you toward the ceiling.
Bring your feet, knees, and hips up to 90 degrees.

Exhale hard to bring your ribcage down and flatten your back onto the floor, rotating your pelvis up and squeezing your glutes. Hold this position throughout the movement. This will be your starting position.
Initiate the exercise by extending one leg, straightening the knee and hip to bring the leg just above the ground.
Maintain the position of your lumbar and pelvis as you perform the movement, as your back is going to want to arch.
Stay tight and return the working leg to the starting position.
Repeat on the opposite side, alternating until the set is complete.
exercises.name: Dead Bug
exercises.overview: The Dead Bug is a core stability exercise that teaches anti-extension while challenging coordination, making it excellent for building functional core strength and spinal stability.
exercises.safetyInfo: This is a low-impact core exercise suitable for most fitness levels. Focus on quality over quantity to prevent lower back strain.

commonMistakes: Bouncing the bar off the chest instead of pausing briefly
Pressing the bar straight up instead of following the natural arc
Allowing shoulder blades to protract during the press
exerciseTips: Retract your shoulder blades and maintain the position throughout the movement
Lower the bar to your lower chest in a controlled manner
Drive your feet into the footrests to maintain stability
Press the bar in a slight arc toward your face at the top
instructions: Secure your legs at the end of the decline bench and slowly lay down on the bench.

Using a medium width grip (a grip that creates a 90-degree angle in the middle of the movement between the forearms and the upper arms), lift the bar from the rack and hold it straight over you with your arms locked. The arms should be perpendicular to the floor. This will be your starting position. Tip: In order to protect your rotator cuff, it is best if you have a spotter help you lift the barbell off the rack.
As you breathe in, come down slowly until you feel the bar on your lower chest.

After a second pause, bring the bar back to the starting position as you breathe out and push the bar using your chest muscles. Lock your arms and squeeze your chest in the contracted position, hold for a second and then start coming down slowly again. Tip: It should take at least twice as long to go down than to come up).
Repeat the movement for the prescribed amount of repetitions.
When you are done, place the bar back in the rack.
exercises.name: Decline Barbell Bench Press
exercises.overview: A bench press variation performed on a decline bench to emphasize the lower portion of the pectoral muscles. The declined angle allows for heavier loading while reducing shoulder stress compared to flat bench pressing.
exercises.safetyInfo: Always use a spotter when possible due to the inverted position. Ensure proper foot placement in the ankle supports to prevent sliding. The decline position increases blood flow to the head, so sit up slowly after completing the set.

commonMistakes: Allowing elbows to flare out during the close-grip press portion
Moving the upper arms during the skull crusher phase
Using too much weight and compromising form during the transition
exerciseTips: Keep your elbows tucked close to your body during the pressing portion
Maintain upper arm position during the skull crusher phase
Control the transition between the two movement patterns
Focus on tricep contraction throughout both phases of the exercise
instructions: Secure your legs at the end of the decline bench and slowly lay down on the bench.

Using a close grip (a grip that is slightly less than shoulder width), lift the bar from the rack and hold it straight over you with your arms locked and elbows in. The arms should be perpendicular to the floor. This will be your starting position. Tip: In order to protect your rotator cuff, it is best if you have a spotter help you lift the barbell off the rack.
Now lower the bar down to your lower chest as you breathe in. Keep the elbows in as you perform this movement.
Using the triceps to push the bar back up, press it back to the starting position as you exhale.

As you breathe in and you keep the upper arms stationary, bring the bar down slowly by moving your forearms in a semicircular motion towards you until you feel the bar slightly touch your forehead. Breathe in as you perform this portion of the movement.
Lift the bar back to the starting position by contracting the triceps and exhaling.
Repeat steps 3-6 until the recommended amount of repetitions is performed.
exercises.name: Decline Close-Grip Bench To Skull Crusher
exercises.name_pt: Supino Declinado Fechado com Tríceps Testa
exercises.overview: A combination exercise that merges a decline close-grip bench press with a skull crusher extension. This compound movement maximally targets the triceps through two different movement patterns in one fluid motion.
exercises.safetyInfo: This advanced combination exercise requires good shoulder stability and tricep strength. Start with lighter weights to master the movement pattern. The decline position increases difficulty in controlling the weight.

commonMistakes: Pulling on the neck with interlaced fingers behind the head
Using momentum to swing up rather than controlled abdominal contraction
Coming up too high and engaging hip flexors instead of abs
exerciseTips: Focus on pulling your ribcage toward your pelvis rather than lifting your torso
Keep your lower back pressed into the bench throughout the movement
Exhale forcefully at the top of the contraction
Control the negative portion of the movement
instructions: Secure your legs at the end of the decline bench and lie down.
Now place your hands lightly on either side of your head keeping your elbows in. Tip: Don't lock your fingers behind your head.
While pushing the small of your back down in the bench to better isolate your abdominal muscles, begin to roll your shoulders off it.

Continue to push down as hard as you can with your lower back as you contract your abdominals and exhale. Your shoulders should come up off the bench only about four inches, and your lower back should remain on the bench. At the top of the movement, contract your abdominals hard and keep the contraction for a second. Tip: Focus on slow, controlled movement - don't cheat yourself by using momentum.
After the one second contraction, begin to come down slowly again to the starting position as you inhale.
Repeat for the recommended amount of repetitions.
exercises.name: Decline Crunch
exercises.overview: An advanced abdominal exercise performed on a decline bench to increase resistance and range of motion. The declined angle provides greater challenge to the rectus abdominis compared to floor crunches.
exercises.safetyInfo: Avoid interlacing fingers behind the head to prevent neck strain. The decline position increases difficulty significantly, so progress gradually. Those with lower back issues should start with a slight decline before progressing to steeper angles.

commonMistakes: Lowering the dumbbells too far and risking shoulder impingement
Pressing straight up instead of following the natural arc of motion
Using momentum to bounce out of the bottom position
exerciseTips: Maintain a slight arch in your lower back throughout the movement
Keep your shoulder blades retracted and pulled down
Lower the dumbbells with control until you feel a stretch in your chest
Press the weights in a slight arc, bringing them together at the top
instructions: Secure your legs at the end of the decline bench and lie down with a dumbbell on each hand on top of your thighs. The palms of your hand will be facing each other.
Once you are laying down, move the dumbbells in front of you at shoulder width.
Once at shoulder width, rotate your wrists forward so that the palms of your hands are facing away from you. This will be your starting position.
Bring down the weights slowly to your side as you breathe out. Keep full control of the dumbbells at all times. Tip: Throughout the motion, the forearms should always be perpendicular to the floor.

As you breathe out, push the dumbbells up using your pectoral muscles. Lock your arms in the contracted position, squeeze your chest, hold for a second and then start coming down slowly. Tip: It should take at least twice as long to go down than to come up..
Repeat the movement for the prescribed amount of repetitions of your training program.
exercises.name: Decline Dumbbell Bench Press
exercises.overview: A dumbbell chest exercise performed on a decline bench targeting the lower pectoral fibers. The unilateral loading allows for greater range of motion and helps address strength imbalances between sides.
exercises.safetyInfo: Getting into position safely is crucial - have dumbbells placed on your thighs before lying back. Consider having a spotter assist with heavier weights. The decline position can cause dizziness, so sit up slowly after completing the set.

commonMistakes: Using too heavy of weight causing the arms to bend excessively at the elbows
Allowing the shoulders to roll forward and lift off the bench
Bringing the weights too low causing excessive shoulder stress
exerciseTips: Maintain a slight bend in your elbows throughout the movement to protect the biceps tendon
Focus on feeling the stretch at the bottom and squeeze your chest at the top
Keep your shoulders pulled back and down against the bench
Control the weight during the eccentric (lowering) portion for maximum muscle activation
instructions: Secure your legs at the end of the decline bench and lie down with a dumbbell on each hand on top of your thighs. The palms of your hand will be facing each other.

Once you are laying down, move the dumbbells in front of you at shoulder width. The palms of the hands should be facing each other and the arms should be perpendicular to the floor and fully extended. This will be your starting position.

With a slight bend on your elbows in order to prevent stress at the biceps tendon, lower your arms out at both sides in a wide arc until you feel a stretch on your chest. Breathe in as you perform this portion of the movement. Tip: Keep in mind that throughout the movement, the arms should remain stationary; the movement should only occur at the shoulder joint.
Return your arms back to the starting position as you squeeze your chest muscles and breathe out. Tip: Make sure to use the same arc of motion used to lower the weights.
Hold for a second at the contracted position and repeat the movement for the prescribed amount of repetitions.
exercises.name: Decline Dumbbell Flyes
exercises.overview: The decline dumbbell fly is an isolation exercise that primarily targets the lower portion of the pectoral muscles. This exercise helps develop chest width and definition while improving the mind-muscle connection with the pectorals.
exercises.safetyInfo: Avoid this exercise if you have shoulder impingement or previous shoulder injuries. The decline position can place significant stress on the shoulder joint, especially at the bottom of the movement.

commonMistakes: Allowing the elbows to flare out excessively during the movement
Moving the upper arms instead of isolating the triceps
Using momentum to lift the weights instead of controlled muscle contraction
exerciseTips: Keep your upper arms stationary and perpendicular to the floor throughout the movement
Control the descent to prevent the weights from dropping too quickly
Squeeze your triceps at the top of the movement for maximum contraction
Keep your elbows tucked in to maintain proper form and target the triceps effectively
instructions: Secure your legs at the end of the decline bench and lie down with a dumbbell on each hand on top of your thighs. The palms of your hand will be facing each other.

Once you are laying down, move the dumbbells in front of you at shoulder width. The palms of the hands should be facing each other and the arms should be perpendicular to the floor and fully extended. This will be your starting position.

As you breathe in and you keep the upper arms stationary (and elbows in), bring the dumbbells down slowly by moving your forearms in a semicircular motion towards you until your thumbs are next to your ears. Breathe in as you perform this portion of the movement.
Lift the dumbbells back to the starting position by contracting the triceps and exhaling.
Repeat for the recommended amount of repetitions.
exercises.name: Decline Dumbbell Triceps Extension
exercises.name_pt: Extensão de Tríceps Declinado com Halteres
exercises.overview: The decline dumbbell triceps extension is an isolation exercise that targets all three heads of the triceps muscle. The decline angle provides a unique stimulus and allows for a greater range of motion compared to flat variations.
exercises.safetyInfo: Use controlled movements to prevent dropping weights on your face or chest. Start with lighter weights to master the form before progressing to heavier loads.

commonMistakes: Lowering the bar too quickly without control, risking injury to the face
Using too wide of a grip which reduces triceps activation
Allowing the elbows to move backward during the lowering phase
exerciseTips: Use a spotter when lifting the bar off the rack for safety
Keep your elbows pointed forward and avoid letting them flare outward
Lower the bar in a controlled arc toward your forehead, not straight down
Focus on squeezing the triceps at the top of each repetition
instructions: Secure your legs at the end of the decline bench and slowly lay down on the bench.

Using a close grip (a grip that is slightly less than shoulder width), lift the EZ bar from the rack and hold it straight over you with your arms locked and elbows in. The arms should be perpendicular to the floor. This will be your starting position. Tip: In order to protect your rotator cuff, it is best if you have a spotter help you lift the barbell off the rack.

As you breathe in and you keep the upper arms stationary, bring the bar down slowly by moving your forearms in a semicircular motion towards you until you feel the bar slightly touch your forehead. Breathe in as you perform this portion of the movement.
Lift the bar back to the starting position by contracting the triceps and exhaling.
Repeat until the recommended amount of repetitions is performed.
exercises.name: Decline EZ Bar Triceps Extension
exercises.name_pt: Extensão de Tríceps Declinado com Barra W
exercises.overview: The decline EZ bar triceps extension is a compound isolation exercise that effectively targets all three heads of the triceps. The EZ bar provides a more comfortable grip angle while the decline position allows for an extended range of motion.
exercises.safetyInfo: Always use a spotter when possible and ensure the bar is secured properly. The EZ bar shape reduces wrist strain compared to a straight barbell. Avoid this exercise if you have elbow pain or injuries.

commonMistakes: Pulling on the neck with the hand behind the head causing cervical strain
Moving too quickly and using momentum instead of muscle control
Not rotating the torso enough, limiting oblique engagement
exerciseTips: Focus on rotating your torso rather than just moving your elbow toward your knee
Keep the movement slow and controlled to maximize muscle activation
Exhale forcefully as you crunch up and twist to engage the core fully
Maintain tension in your abs throughout the entire range of motion
instructions: Secure your legs at the end of the decline bench and slowly lay down on the bench.
Raise your upper body off the bench until your torso is about 35-45 degrees if measured from the floor.
Put one hand beside your head and the other on your thigh. This will be your starting position.

Raise your upper body slowly from the starting position while turning your torso to the left. Continue crunching up as you exhale until your right elbow touches your left knee. Hold this contracted position for a second. Tip: Focus on keeping your abs tight and keeping the movement slow and controlled.
Lower your body back down slowly to the starting position as you inhale.

After completing one set on the right for the recommended amount of repetitions, switch to your left side. Tip: Focus on really twisting your torso and feeling the contraction when you are in the up position.
exercises.name: Decline Oblique Crunch
exercises.overview: The decline oblique crunch is a challenging core exercise that targets the external and internal obliques while also engaging the rectus abdominis. The decline angle increases the difficulty and range of motion compared to flat surface variations.
exercises.safetyInfo: Avoid pulling on your neck during the movement. If you experience lower back pain, reduce the range of motion or discontinue the exercise. The decline position increases difficulty significantly.

commonMistakes: Allowing the hips to sag or pike up during the movement
Not lowering deep enough to achieve full range of motion
Placing hands too wide or too narrow for optimal chest activation
exerciseTips: Maintain a straight line from your head to your heels throughout the movement
Lower yourself until your chest nearly touches the ground for full range of motion
Keep your core engaged to prevent sagging at the hips
Push through your palms and squeeze your chest at the top of the movement
instructions: Lie on the floor face down and place your hands about 36 inches apart while holding your torso up at arms length. Move your feet up to a box or bench. This will be your starting position.
Next, lower yourself downward until your chest almost touches the floor as you inhale.
Now breathe out and press your upper body back up to the starting position while squeezing your chest.
After a brief pause at the top contracted position, you can begin to lower yourself downward again for as many repetitions as needed.
exercises.name: Decline Push-Up
exercises.overview: The decline push-up is an advanced bodyweight exercise that increases the difficulty of the standard push-up by elevating the feet. This variation places greater emphasis on the upper chest and shoulders while building functional pushing strength.
exercises.safetyInfo: This is an advanced push-up variation that places more stress on the upper body. Ensure you can perform regular push-ups with perfect form before progressing to decline variations.

commonMistakes: Using momentum to swing the legs up instead of controlled abdominal contraction
Not rolling the pelvis backward, limiting the range of motion and muscle activation
Allowing the legs to separate or the knees to drift apart during the movement
exerciseTips: Grip the top of the bench firmly to maintain your position and prevent sliding
Focus on rolling your pelvis backward rather than just lifting your legs
Keep your knees together throughout the movement for proper form
Control the lowering phase to maximize muscle engagement and prevent momentum
instructions: Lie on your back on a decline bench and hold on to the top of the bench with both hands. Don't let your body slip down from this position.

Hold your legs parallel to the floor using your abs to hold them there while keeping your knees and feet together. Tip: Your legs should be fully extended with a slight bend on the knee. This will be your starting position.
While exhaling, move your legs towards the torso as you roll your pelvis backwards and you raise your hips off the bench. At the end of this movement your knees will be touching your chest.
Hold the contraction for a second and move your legs back to the starting position while inhaling.
Repeat for the recommended amount of repetitions.
exercises.name: Decline Reverse Crunch
exercises.overview: The decline reverse crunch is an advanced abdominal exercise that targets the rectus abdominis with emphasis on the lower portion. The decline angle increases the resistance and range of motion, making it more challenging than flat variations.
exercises.safetyInfo: Ensure you have a secure grip on the bench throughout the exercise. The decline position significantly increases difficulty, so master flat reverse crunches first. Avoid if you have lower back issues.

commonMistakes: Lowering the bar too high on the chest instead of the lower pecs
Pressing the bar straight up instead of following the natural arc
Allowing shoulders to roll forward at the top
exerciseTips: Keep your shoulder blades retracted and pressed into the bench
Lower the bar to lightly touch your lower chest
Drive through your heels and press the bar in a slight arc toward your face
Maintain tension in your core throughout the movement
instructions: Place a decline bench underneath the Smith machine. Now place the barbell at a height that you can reach when lying down and your arms are almost fully extended. Using a pronated grip that is wider than shoulder width, unlock the bar from the rack and hold it straight over you with your arms extended. This will be your starting position.
As you inhale, lower the bar under control by allowing the elbows to flex, lightly contacting the torso.
After a brief pause, bring the bar back to the starting position by extending the elbows, exhaling as you do so.
Repeat the movement for the prescribed amount of repetitions.
When the set is complete, lock the bar back in the rack.
exercises.name: Decline Smith Press
exercises.overview: The decline Smith press targets the lower portion of the pectoralis major while providing the stability and safety of a Smith machine. This exercise is excellent for developing lower chest definition and strength.
exercises.safetyInfo: Avoid this exercise if you have shoulder impingement or lower back issues. The decline position increases blood flow to the head, so move slowly when getting up.

commonMistakes: Using too high of a deficit before building adequate strength and mobility
Allowing the lower back to sag or hips to pike up
Bouncing at the bottom of the movement instead of controlled motion
exerciseTips: Keep your body in a straight line throughout the entire range of motion
Control the descent to maximize the stretch and muscle activation
Start with a small deficit and gradually increase the height as you adapt
Squeeze your chest muscles at the top of each repetition
instructions: Place your hands on two elevated surfaces like books, blocks, or push-up handles at shoulder-width apart.
Assume a standard push-up position with your body in a straight line from head to heels.
Lower your chest between the elevated surfaces, allowing for a deeper range of motion than standard push-ups.
Press through your palms to return to the starting position with arms fully extended.
Maintain tight core throughout the movement and repeat for desired repetitions.
exercises.name: Deficit Push Up
exercises.overview: Deficit push-ups involve performing push-ups with hands elevated on platforms, allowing for a greater range of motion and increased muscle stretch. This variation enhances chest development and shoulder mobility compared to standard push-ups.
exercises.safetyInfo: The increased range of motion places additional stress on the shoulder joint and chest muscles. Those with shoulder impingement or chest injuries should use caution and start with minimal deficit.

commonMistakes: Using arms instead of hips to generate power during the snatch
Allowing the dumbbells to drift away from the body during the lift
exerciseTips: Generate power from your hips during the snatch portion
Keep the dumbbells close to your body throughout the lifting phase
Use hex dumbbells to prevent rolling during the burpee portion
Coordinate your breathing with the explosive movements
instructions: Start with two dumbbells on the floor, perform a burpee by jumping back into plank position.
Complete a push-up with hands gripping the dumbbells, then jump feet back toward hands.
In one explosive movement, deadlift the dumbbells and swing them between your legs.
Use hip drive to propel the dumbbells up and overhead in a snatch-like motion.
Lock out the dumbbells overhead with arms fully extended.
Lower the dumbbells back to the floor with control and repeat.
exercises.name: Devil's Press
exercises.overview: The devil's press is an advanced CrossFit movement combining a burpee with a double dumbbell snatch. It's one of the most challenging full-body exercises, demanding explosive power, coordination, and exceptional conditioning.
exercises.safetyInfo: This is an extremely demanding exercise requiring excellent mobility, stability, and conditioning. Not suitable for beginners or those with shoulder, back, or cardiovascular issues. Master burpees and dumbbell snatches separately first.

commonMistakes: Allowing the elbows to flare out wide instead of staying close to the body
Sagging hips or piking up at the waist
Not descending low enough to achieve full range of motion
exerciseTips: Keep your elbows close to your sides to maximize tricep activation
Maintain a straight line from head to heels throughout the movement
Focus on controlled movement rather than speed
Squeeze your triceps hard at the top of each repetition
instructions: Start in a plank position with hands close together, forming a diamond shape with your thumbs and index fingers.
Position the diamond directly under your chest with your body in a straight line from head to heels.
Lower your chest toward the diamond formed by your hands, keeping your elbows close to your sides.
Descend until your chest nearly touches your hands or as low as your range of motion allows.
Push through your palms to return to the starting position, fully extending your arms.
Maintain a tight core and straight body line throughout the entire movement.
exercises.name: Diamond Push Up
exercises.overview: The diamond push-up is an advanced bodyweight exercise that primarily targets the triceps through a close-grip hand position. It's significantly more challenging than standard push-ups and effectively builds tricep strength and size.
exercises.safetyInfo: This variation places significant stress on the wrists and elbows. Those with wrist issues may need to modify by using push-up handles or performing the exercise on their fists.

commonMistakes: Allowing elbows to flare out wide which shifts emphasis to chest
Using momentum instead of controlled movement
Not achieving full range of motion at the bottom
exerciseTips: Keep your elbows close to your sides throughout the movement
Maintain an upright torso to emphasize triceps over chest
Control the negative portion of the movement slowly
Focus on full extension without locking out aggressively
instructions: Sit securely in a dip machine, select the weight and firmly grasp the handles.
Now keep your elbows in at your sides in order to place emphasis on the triceps. The elbows should be bent at a 90 degree angle.
As you contract the triceps, extend your arms downwards as you exhale. Tip: At the bottom of the movement, focus on keeping a little bend in your arms to keep tension on the triceps muscle.
Now slowly let your arms come back up to the starting position as you inhale.
Repeat for the recommended amount of repetitions.
exercises.name: Dip Machine
exercises.overview: The dip machine provides a controlled way to perform dips with adjustable resistance, making it ideal for building triceps strength safely. It's perfect for beginners or those unable to perform bodyweight dips.
exercises.safetyInfo: Machine dips are safer than free dips for beginners. Avoid if you have shoulder impingement or elbow pain. Start with lighter weights to master the movement pattern.

commonMistakes: Staying too upright which emphasizes triceps over chest
Not going deep enough to engage the chest fully
Using momentum to bounce out of the bottom position
exerciseTips: Lean your torso forward about 30 degrees throughout the movement
Allow your elbows to flare out slightly to target the chest
Lower until you feel a stretch in your chest muscles
Squeeze your chest muscles at the top of each rep
instructions: For this exercise you will need access to parallel bars. To get yourself into the starting position, hold your body at arms length (arms locked) above the bars.
While breathing in, lower yourself slowly with your torso leaning forward around 30 degrees or so and your elbows flared out slightly until you feel a slight stretch in the chest.
Once you feel the stretch, use your chest to bring your body back to the starting position as you breathe out. Tip: Remember to squeeze the chest at the top of the movement for a second.
Repeat the movement for the prescribed amount of repetitions.
exercises.name: Dips - Chest Version
exercises.overview: Chest dips with forward lean and flared elbows effectively target the pectorals, particularly the lower chest. This bodyweight exercise builds functional pushing strength and chest development.
exercises.safetyInfo: This exercise places significant stress on the shoulder joint. Avoid with shoulder impingement, AC joint issues, or if you lack shoulder flexibility. Progress gradually.

commonMistakes: Leaning forward too much which shifts emphasis to chest
Going too deep and stressing the shoulder joint unnecessarily
Allowing elbows to flare out wide
exerciseTips: Keep your torso upright throughout the entire movement
Maintain elbows close to your sides to maximize triceps engagement
Lower until your upper arms are parallel to the ground
Press up by extending your elbows fully but not aggressively
instructions: To get into the starting position, hold your body at arm's length with your arms nearly locked above the bars.

Now, inhale and slowly lower yourself downward. Your torso should remain upright and your elbows should stay close to your body. This helps to better focus on tricep involvement. Lower yourself until there is a 90 degree angle formed between the upper arm and forearm.
Then, exhale and push your torso back up using your triceps to bring your body back to the starting position.
Repeat the movement for the prescribed amount of repetitions.
exercises.name: Dips - Triceps Version
exercises.overview: Triceps dips with an upright torso position maximize triceps activation while minimizing chest involvement. This classic bodyweight exercise builds impressive triceps strength and size.
exercises.safetyInfo: Ensure adequate shoulder mobility before attempting. Avoid with shoulder impingement or elbow tendinitis. If you can't perform bodyweight dips, use assistance or machine variations.

commonMistakes: Not maintaining the low arcing motion
Failing to reverse the movement properly
Breaking the fluid motion into separate components
exerciseTips: Create a smooth arcing motion like an airplane diving and pulling up
Keep your hands firmly planted throughout the movement
Control both the forward and backward phases equally
Focus on keeping your body close to the ground during the transition
instructions: Start in downward dog position with hands shoulder-width apart and hips raised high.
Dive forward and down in an arc motion, lowering your body close to the ground.
Continue forward and press up into upward dog position with chest up and hips low.
Reverse the exact movement pattern back to the starting downward dog position.
Maintain the arcing motion throughout both directions of the exercise.
exercises.name: Dive Bomber Push Up
exercises.overview: Dive bomber push-ups create a flowing arc motion that challenges strength, flexibility, and coordination. Unlike Hindu push-ups, the movement reverses exactly, creating a more challenging return phase.
