commonMistakes: Rotating the torso during the rowing motion
Pulling the elbow too wide instead of back
Using momentum instead of controlled muscle contraction
exerciseTips: Pull your elbow back rather than out to the side
Squeeze your shoulder blade at the top of each rep
Keep your core tight to prevent rotation
Focus on feeling the lat muscle work
instructions: Place your left knee and hand on a bench for support, with your right foot on the floor.
Hold a dumbbell in your right hand with your arm hanging straight down.
Keep your back flat and parallel to the floor with your core engaged.
Pull the dumbbell up to your hip by driving your elbow back and squeezing your shoulder blade.
Lower the weight with control until your arm is fully extended.
Complete all reps on one side before switching to the other arm.
exercises.name: Dumbbell Single Arm Row On Bench
exercises.name_pt: Remada Unilateral com Halter no Banco
exercises.overview: The single arm dumbbell row is a fundamental pulling exercise that targets the lats and upper back muscles. The unilateral nature allows you to work each side independently and correct imbalances.
exercises.safetyInfo: This exercise is safe for most people when performed with proper form. Those with lower back issues should ensure the supporting leg provides adequate stability.

commonMistakes: Pressing the weight before fully extending the legs
Leaning away from the weighted side during the press
Squatting too shallow to generate proper leg drive
exerciseTips: Keep your core tight to prevent excessive spinal extension
Drive the movement from your legs rather than just your arms
Maintain an upright torso throughout the squat portion
Coordinate the squat and press into one fluid movement
instructions: Hold a dumbbell in your right hand at shoulder height with your palm facing forward.
Stand with feet shoulder-width apart and lower into a squat position.
Drive through your heels to stand up explosively while simultaneously pressing the dumbbell overhead.
Lower the dumbbell back to shoulder height as you descend into the next squat.
Complete all reps on one arm before switching to the other side.
exercises.name: Dumbbell Single Arm Thruster
exercises.overview: The dumbbell single arm thruster combines a squat with an overhead press, creating a full-body exercise that builds power and coordination. The unilateral loading challenges core stability and addresses imbalances.
exercises.safetyInfo: Start with lighter weight to master the coordination. Avoid if you have shoulder impingement or lower back issues. Ensure adequate shoulder mobility before attempting.

commonMistakes: Bouncing at the bottom of the movement instead of controlling the stretch
Using the non-working leg to assist or provide momentum
Not achieving full range of motion by limiting the stretch or contraction
exerciseTips: Keep your working leg straight throughout the entire movement
Focus on getting a full stretch at the bottom and maximum contraction at the top
Control the descent to maximize muscle engagement
Maintain balance by using your free hand for light support only
instructions: Hold a dumbbell in one hand and stand on the edge of a step or platform with the ball of one foot on the surface.
Allow your heel to drop below the platform level to feel a stretch in your calf muscle.
Hold onto a wall or rail with your free hand for balance while keeping your working leg straight.
Push through the ball of your foot to raise your heel as high as possible, contracting your calf muscle.
Hold the peak contraction for a brief pause, then slowly lower your heel back below the platform level.
Complete all repetitions on one leg before switching to the other leg.
exercises.name: Dumbbell Single Leg Calf Raise
exercises.overview: The dumbbell single leg calf raise is a unilateral exercise that targets each calf individually while improving balance and addressing muscle imbalances. The elevated position allows for greater range of motion than floor-based calf exercises.
exercises.safetyInfo: Those with Achilles tendon issues or calf strains should avoid this exercise or use very light weight. Ensure you have adequate balance support to prevent falls from the elevated surface.
variations: Dumbbell Seated Single Leg Calf Raise
Smith Machine Single Leg Calf Raise

commonMistakes: Placing the dumbbell too high on the torso instead of the hips
Pushing through the toes instead of driving through the heel
Hyperextending the lower back at the top of the movement
exerciseTips: Keep your extended leg straight and engaged throughout the movement
Drive through your heel rather than your toes
Focus on squeezing your glutes at the top of each rep
Maintain a neutral spine and avoid overarching your back
instructions: Sit with your upper back against a bench holding a dumbbell on your lap.
Plant one foot firmly on the ground and extend the other leg straight out.
Roll the dumbbell onto your hips and hold it in place with both hands.
Drive through your planted heel to lift your hips up toward the ceiling.
Squeeze your glutes at the top and hold for a brief moment.
Lower your hips back down with control and repeat before switching legs.
exercises.name: Dumbbell Single Leg Hip Thrust
exercises.overview: The single leg hip thrust isolates each glute individually while challenging stability and balance. This unilateral variation helps correct imbalances and builds functional hip extension strength for athletic performance.
exercises.safetyInfo: Start with bodyweight only before adding load. Those with lower back issues should avoid hyperextension at the top of the movement and focus on glute contraction.

commonMistakes: Placing too much weight on the back leg
Allowing the front knee to cave inward during the movement
Leaning the torso forward excessively
exerciseTips: Keep most of your weight on your front leg throughout
Maintain an upright torso and avoid leaning forward
Descend until your front thigh is parallel to the floor
Focus on pushing through your front heel to stand up
instructions: Stand in a split stance holding dumbbells at your sides with your stronger leg forward.
Position your feet so your front thigh will be parallel to the floor when you descend.
Lower your body by bending both knees until your front thigh is parallel to the ground.
Keep your torso upright and most of your weight on your front leg.
Push through your front heel to return to the starting position.
Complete all reps on one leg before switching to the other side.
exercises.name: Dumbbell Split Squat
exercises.overview: The dumbbell split squat is a unilateral lower body exercise that primarily targets the quadriceps while also engaging the glutes and hamstrings. This exercise helps build single-leg strength and improves balance.
exercises.safetyInfo: Avoid this exercise if you have knee injuries or balance issues. Start with bodyweight only before adding dumbbells to ensure proper form and stability.

commonMistakes: Knees caving inward during the descent or ascent
Rising up on toes instead of keeping weight on heels
Excessive forward lean that shifts weight to the toes
exerciseTips: Keep your chest up and core braced throughout the movement
Drive through your heels when standing up from the bottom position
Lower until your thighs are parallel to the floor or as deep as mobility allows
Maintain neutral spine alignment and avoid rounding your back
instructions: Stand up straight while holding a dumbbell on each hand (palms facing the side of your legs).

Position your legs using a shoulder width medium stance with the toes slightly pointed out. Keep your head up at all times as looking down will get you off balance and also maintain a straight back. This will be your starting position. Note: For the purposes of this discussion we will use the medium stance described above which targets overall development; however you can choose any of the three stances discussed in the foot stances section.

Begin to slowly lower your torso by bending the knees as you maintain a straight posture with the head up. Continue down until your thighs are parallel to the floor. Tip: If you performed the exercise correctly, the front of the knees should make an imaginary straight line with the toes that is perpendicular to the front. If your knees are past that imaginary line (if they are past your toes) then you are placing undue stress on the knee and the exercise has been performed incorrectly.
Begin to raise your torso as you exhale by pushing the floor with the heel of your foot mainly as you straighten the legs again and go back to the starting position.
Repeat for the recommended amount of repetitions.
exercises.name: Dumbbell Squat
exercises.overview: The dumbbell squat is a fundamental lower body compound movement that builds strength in the legs and glutes while improving functional movement patterns. It's an excellent alternative to barbell squats for beginners or those with shoulder mobility limitations.
exercises.safetyInfo: Avoid this exercise if you have knee or lower back injuries. Start with lighter weights to master the movement pattern before progressing load.

commonMistakes: Plopping down onto the bench and losing muscle tension
Using the bench to bounce or assist with the upward movement
Allowing knees to collapse inward when touching the bench
exerciseTips: Lightly touch the bench without fully sitting down or relaxing
Keep tension in your legs throughout the entire movement
Use the bench as a depth guide rather than a resting point
Focus on controlled descent and explosive ascent
instructions: Stand up straight with a flat bench behind you while holding a dumbbell on each hand (palms facing the side of your legs).

Position your legs using a shoulder width medium stance with the toes slightly pointed out. Keep your head up at all times as looking down will get you off balance and also maintain a straight back. This will be your starting position. Note: For the purposes of this discussion we will use the medium stance described above which targets overall development; however you can choose any of the three stances discussed in the foot stances section.

Begin to slowly lower your torso by bending the knees as you maintain a straight posture with the head up. Continue down until you slightly touch the bench behind you. Inhale as you perform this portion of the movement. Tip: If you performed the exercise correctly, the front of the knees should make an imaginary straight line with the toes that is perpendicular to the front. If your knees are past that imaginary line (if they are past your toes) then you are placing undue stress on the knee and the exercise has been performed incorrectly.
Begin to raise the bar as you exhale by pushing the floor with the heel of your foot mainly as you straighten the legs again and go back to the starting position.
Repeat for the recommended amount of repetitions.
exercises.name: Dumbbell Squat To A Bench
exercises.overview: The dumbbell squat to bench is a progression exercise that helps develop proper squat mechanics by providing a consistent depth reference. It builds confidence in newer lifters while still challenging the major leg muscles.
exercises.safetyInfo: Ensure the bench is stable and positioned at an appropriate height for your mobility. This variation is ideal for learning proper squat depth and building confidence.

commonMistakes: Relaxing the squeeze between dumbbells during the movement
Using dumbbells that are too heavy, compromising the squeeze
Allowing the dumbbells to drift apart during the exercise
exerciseTips: Squeeze the dumbbells together as hard as possible throughout the movement
Keep your core engaged and maintain a slight arch in your back
Focus on feeling the contraction in your inner chest
Use a controlled tempo on both the lowering and pressing phases
instructions: Lie on a bench holding two dumbbells with a neutral grip, pressing them together at chest level.
Keep the dumbbells pressed firmly against each other throughout the entire movement.
Lower the dumbbells together toward your chest while maintaining the squeeze.
Press the dumbbells back up to the starting position while continuing to squeeze them together.
Maintain constant pressure between the dumbbells throughout all repetitions.
exercises.name: Dumbbell Squeeze Press
exercises.overview: The Dumbbell Squeeze Press emphasizes the inner portion of the chest muscles through isometric contraction while performing a pressing movement. This exercise provides a unique stimulus for chest development and improved mind-muscle connection.
exercises.safetyInfo: This exercise requires significant grip strength and coordination. Start with lighter weights than you would use for regular dumbbell presses. Ensure the dumbbells have flat sides that can be pressed together effectively.

commonMistakes: Stepping down too quickly without control
Putting too much weight on the descending foot
Allowing the knee to cave inward
exerciseTips: Control the descent slowly for maximum benefit
Keep your knee aligned over your ankle
Maintain an upright posture throughout
Focus on the working leg doing most of the work
instructions: Stand on a sturdy box or platform holding dumbbells at your sides.
Position yourself so one foot is near the edge of the platform.
Slowly lower the outside foot toward the ground by bending the standing leg.
Lightly tap the ground with your toe, keeping most weight on the standing leg.
Push through the heel of the standing leg to return to the starting position.
Complete all reps on one leg before switching sides.
exercises.name: Dumbbell Step Down
exercises.overview: The dumbbell step down is an eccentric-focused exercise that builds unilateral leg strength and improves balance. It's particularly effective for developing control and stability in the quadriceps muscles.
exercises.safetyInfo: Start with a low platform and no weight to master the movement. Those with knee issues should avoid this exercise or use minimal weight under supervision.

commonMistakes: Pushing off the bottom foot instead of the top foot
Using momentum to swing the knee up
Allowing the knee to cave inward during the step up
exerciseTips: Drive through the heel of the foot on the box
Keep your torso upright throughout the movement
Focus on the working leg doing all the lifting
Control the descent to maximize muscle engagement
instructions: Hold a dumbbell in each hand at your sides and stand facing a sturdy box or bench.
Place your entire right foot on the box, ensuring your heel is not hanging off the edge.
Drive through your right heel to step up onto the box while simultaneously driving your left knee up toward your chest.
Pause briefly at the top with your right leg extended and left knee raised.
Lower your left foot back to the starting position with control, then step down with your right foot.
Complete all repetitions on one leg before switching sides.
exercises.name: Dumbbell Step Up With Knee Drive
exercises.name_pt: Subida no Banco com Elevação de Joelho com Halteres
exercises.overview: The Dumbbell Step Up With Knee Drive is a unilateral leg exercise that combines the step-up movement with an explosive knee drive. It targets the quadriceps and glutes while improving balance, coordination, and single-leg strength.
exercises.safetyInfo: Ensure the box or bench is stable and appropriate height (knee level or below). Start without weights to master the pattern. Avoid if you have knee problems or balance issues.
variations: Bodyweight Step Up with Knee Drive
Lateral Step Up with Knee Drive

commonMistakes: Pushing off with the trailing leg instead of using only the working leg
Using momentum or bouncing to assist with the step up movement
Allowing the knee to cave inward during the stepping motion
exerciseTips: Step onto the platform using only the working leg's power
Keep your torso upright and avoid leaning forward excessively
Control the descent by lowering slowly with the working leg
Choose a platform height that allows your thigh to be parallel when stepping up
instructions: Stand up straight while holding a dumbbell on each hand (palms facing the side of your legs).

Place the right foot on the elevated platform. Step on the platform by extending the hip and the knee of your right leg. Use the heel mainly to lift the rest of your body up and place the foot of the left leg on the platform as well. Breathe out as you execute the force required to come up.

Step down with the left leg by flexing the hip and knee of the right leg as you inhale. Return to the original standing position by placing the right foot of to next to the left foot on the initial position.
Repeat with the right leg for the recommended amount of repetitions and then perform with the left leg.
exercises.name: Dumbbell Step Ups
exercises.overview: Dumbbell step ups are a unilateral exercise that builds single-leg strength, balance, and coordination. This functional movement translates well to daily activities and athletic performance while addressing muscle imbalances.
exercises.safetyInfo: Ensure the platform or bench is stable and appropriate height. Start with bodyweight only before adding dumbbells to master the unilateral movement pattern.

commonMistakes: Rounding the back during the descent
Bending the knees too much, making it a squat
Not achieving adequate range of motion
exerciseTips: Focus on the hip hinge movement rather than knee bending
Keep the dumbbells close to your body throughout
Feel the stretch in your hamstrings on the way down
Squeeze your glutes to complete each rep
instructions: Stand upright holding a dumbbell in each hand with arms extended in front of your thighs.
Keep your knees slightly bent and your back straight throughout the movement.
Hinge at the hips and lower the dumbbells toward the floor, feeling a stretch in your hamstrings.
Lower until you feel a good stretch in your hamstrings or the weights reach mid-shin level.
Drive your hips forward to return to the starting position, squeezing your glutes at the top.
exercises.name: Dumbbell Stiff Leg Deadlift
exercises.overview: The dumbbell stiff leg deadlift is a hip-hinge exercise that primarily targets the hamstrings and glutes. It's excellent for developing posterior chain strength and improving hamstring flexibility.
exercises.safetyInfo: Those with lower back issues should use caution and lighter weight. Proper hip hinge technique is essential to avoid back strain.

commonMistakes: Allowing the elbows to flare out to the sides during the movement
Moving the upper arms or shoulders instead of isolating the triceps
Lowering the weight too quickly without controlling the eccentric phase
exerciseTips: Keep your upper arms stationary throughout the entire movement
Focus on moving only at the elbow joint while keeping elbows tucked
Lower the weight slowly to maximize muscle tension and control
Squeeze the triceps at the top of each repetition
instructions: Lie down on a flat bench holding two dumbbells directly above your shoulders. Your arms should be fully extended and form a 90 degree angle from your torso and the floor.
The palms of your hands should be facing forward, and your elbows should be tucked in. This will be your starting position.
Now, inhale and slowly lower the dumbbells until they are near your ears. Be sure to keep your upper arms stationary and your elbows tucked in.
Then, exhale and use your triceps to return the weight to the starting position.
exercises.name: Dumbbell Tricep Extension -Pronated Grip
exercises.name_pt: Extensão de Tríceps com Halteres - Pegada Pronada
exercises.overview: The dumbbell tricep extension with pronated grip is an effective isolation exercise for building tricep strength and size. The lying position allows for better stability and control compared to standing variations.
exercises.safetyInfo: Use a spotter when possible and start with lighter weights to avoid dropping dumbbells on your face or chest. Avoid this exercise if you have elbow pain or injuries.

commonMistakes: Allowing elbows to flare out to the sides instead of keeping them forward
Using the shoulders to assist instead of isolating the triceps
Lowering the weight too quickly without controlling the negative portion
exerciseTips: Keep your elbows stationary and pointing forward throughout the movement
Lower the weight slowly to feel a stretch in your triceps
Avoid flaring your elbows out to the sides
Keep your core engaged to maintain good posture
instructions: Sit on a bench with back support, holding one dumbbell with both hands behind your head.
Grip the dumbbell by placing your palms against the underside of the top weight plate.
Keep your upper arms vertical and elbows pointing forward throughout the movement.
Extend your forearms upward by straightening your elbows until your arms are fully extended.
Slowly lower the dumbbell back behind your head to the starting position.
exercises.name: Dumbbell Tricep Extension Overhead
exercises.name_pt: Extensão de Tríceps com Halteres Acima da Cabeça
exercises.overview: The overhead dumbbell tricep extension isolates all three heads of the triceps through a full range of motion. It's effective for building tricep mass and strength.
exercises.safetyInfo: Use a secure grip on the dumbbell to prevent dropping it behind your head. Those with shoulder or elbow issues should start with lighter weight.

commonMistakes: Lifting elbows too high above shoulder level
Using too wide of a grip or flaring elbows out excessively
Allowing the weights to drift away from the body during the lift
exerciseTips: Keep the dumbbells close to your body throughout the movement
Lead with your elbows rather than your hands
Avoid lifting your elbows higher than your shoulders
Maintain a neutral spine and avoid swaying or using momentum
instructions: Stand with feet shoulder-width apart, holding dumbbells at your sides with palms facing your body.
Keep your core engaged and shoulders back, with a slight bend in your knees.
Pull the dumbbells straight up along your body, leading with your elbows until they reach chest level.
Pause briefly at the top, then slowly lower the dumbbells back to the starting position.
exercises.name: Dumbbell Upright Row
exercises.overview: The dumbbell upright row targets the shoulders and upper back by pulling weights vertically along the torso. It's effective for building shoulder width and upper back strength.
exercises.safetyInfo: Avoid this exercise if you have shoulder impingement or rotator cuff issues. Stop immediately if you feel pinching or pain in the shoulder joint.

commonMistakes: Allowing dumbbells to drift forward or backward
Taking steps that are too short
Losing core stability and arching the back excessively
exerciseTips: Keep your core engaged to maintain overhead position
Take controlled steps to maintain balance
Keep dumbbells directly over your shoulders
Focus on proper lunge depth with each step
instructions: Hold dumbbells overhead with arms fully extended.
Step forward into lunge position with right leg.
Lower until front thigh is parallel and rear knee nearly touches ground.
Push through front heel and bring rear leg forward into next lunge.
Continue alternating legs while walking forward.
Maintain overhead position throughout entire set.
exercises.name: Dumbbell Walking Overhead Lunge
exercises.name_pt: Afundo Caminhando com Halteres Acima da Cabeça
exercises.overview: The dumbbell walking overhead lunge combines lower body strength training with overhead stability challenges. It builds functional strength while improving coordination, balance, and core stability.
exercises.safetyInfo: Requires good shoulder mobility and core stability. Start with lighter weights and ensure adequate space for walking. Avoid if you have shoulder impingement or lower back issues.

commonMistakes: Using too much weight and compromising range of motion
Moving the forearms instead of isolating the wrist movement
Performing the exercise too quickly without proper control
exerciseTips: Keep your forearms pressed firmly against your thighs throughout the movement
Allow the weight to roll to your fingertips for maximum range of motion
Move only at the wrist joint, keeping your forearms stationary
Use a slow, controlled tempo to maximize muscle activation
instructions: Sit on a bench holding a dumbbell in each hand with your forearms resting on your thighs.
Allow your hands to extend beyond your knees with palms facing upward.
Let the dumbbells roll down to your fingertips, allowing your wrists to extend fully.
Curl your wrists upward by flexing your wrist flexors, bringing the dumbbells back to starting position.
Repeat the movement in a controlled manner for the desired repetitions.
exercises.name: Dumbbell Wrist Curl
exercises.overview: Dumbbell wrist curls specifically target the wrist flexor muscles in the forearms. This isolation exercise is essential for grip strength and forearm development.
exercises.safetyInfo: Start with light weight to avoid overstraining the small wrist muscles. Those with wrist injuries should consult a healthcare provider before performing this exercise.

commonMistakes: Pulling on the neck instead of using core strength
Rushing through reps without proper rotation
Lifting hips off the ground during movement
exerciseTips: Focus on rotating your torso rather than just moving your elbow
Keep your lower back pressed against the floor
Exhale forcefully as you crunch up and across
Control the movement on the way down
instructions: Lie on the floor, crossing your right leg across your bent left knee. Clasp your hands behind your head, beginning with your shoulder blades on the ground. This will be your starting position.
Perform the motion by flexing the spine and rotating your torso to bring the left elbow to the right knee.
Return to the starting position and repeat the movement for the desired number of repetitions before switching sides.
exercises.name: Elbow to Knee
exercises.overview: A rotational core exercise that targets the rectus abdominis and obliques through cross-body movement patterns. Excellent for developing functional core strength.
exercises.safetyInfo: Avoid if you have neck injuries or lower back problems. Keep hands lightly behind head without pulling on neck.

commonMistakes: Taking too small a step backward
Allowing front knee to cave inward
Leaning forward excessively during descent
exerciseTips: Control the descent to maximize time under tension
Push through your front heel to return to starting position
Keep your torso upright throughout the movement
Ensure your front knee tracks over your toes
instructions: Position a bar onto a rack at shoulder height loaded to an appropriate weight. Place a short, raised platform behind you.
Rack the bar onto your upper back, keeping your back arched and tight. Step onto your raised platform with both feet. This will be your starting position.
Begin by stepping backwards with one leg. Descend by flexing your hips and knees until your knee touches the floor.
Pause, and extend through the hips and knees to rise up, returning all the way to the starting position before alternating.
exercises.name: Elevated Back Lunge
exercises.overview: An elevated barbell lunge variation that increases range of motion and muscle activation. The deficit position enhances strength and flexibility in the hip and knee joints.
exercises.safetyInfo: Use proper barbell racking technique and ensure platform is stable. Avoid if you have knee injuries or balance issues.

commonMistakes: Using excessive body swing to move weight
Pulling handle too high toward neck
Allowing shoulders to roll forward at start position
exerciseTips: Squeeze shoulder blades together at the end of each rep
Keep your torso stable and avoid using momentum
Pull the handle to your lower chest/upper abdomen
Maintain a slight arch in your lower back
instructions: Get a platform of some sort (it can be an aerobics or calf raise platform) that is around 4-6 inches in height.
Place it on the seat of the cable row machine.
Sit down on the machine and place your feet on the front platform or crossbar provided making sure that your knees are slightly bent and not locked.
Lean over as you keep the natural alignment of your back and grab the V-bar handles.

With your arms extended pull back until your torso is at a 90-degree angle from your legs. Your back should be slightly arched and your chest should be sticking out. You should be feeling a nice stretch on your lats as you hold the bar in front of you. This is the starting position of the exercise.

Keeping the torso stationary, pull the handles back towards your torso while keeping the arms close to it until you touch the abdominals. Breathe out as you perform that movement. At that point you should be squeezing your back muscles hard. Hold that contraction for a second and slowly go back to the original position while breathing in.
Repeat for the recommended amount of repetitions.
exercises.name: Elevated Cable Rows
exercises.overview: A cable rowing variation performed on an elevated platform to increase range of motion and lat stretch. Excellent for building back width and improving posture.
exercises.safetyInfo: Ensure platform is secure before starting. Use appropriate weight to maintain proper form throughout all repetitions.
variations: Bent Over Two-Dumbbell Row With Palms In

commonMistakes: Allowing hips to drop during the movement
Moving too quickly and losing balance
Pressing the weights together at the top instead of straight up
exerciseTips: Keep your hips elevated in a bridge position
Engage your core to maintain stability on the ball
Focus on squeezing your chest muscles during the press
Move slowly to maintain control and balance
instructions: Sit on an exercise ball holding dumbbells and slowly walk your feet forward as you roll down.
Position yourself so your upper back and shoulders are supported by the ball with your hips elevated.
Hold the dumbbells with arms extended above your chest, palms facing forward.
Lower the weights in a controlled arc until your elbows are slightly below chest level.
Press the dumbbells back up to the starting position by contracting your chest muscles.
Maintain a stable bridge position with your core engaged throughout.
exercises.name: Exercise Ball Chest Press
exercises.overview: The Exercise Ball Chest Press is a compound upper body exercise performed on an unstable surface that targets the chest muscles while engaging the core for stability. It adds a balance challenge to the traditional chest press movement.
exercises.safetyInfo: Ensure the exercise ball is properly inflated and weight-rated for your body weight plus additional load. Start with lighter weights due to the unstable surface. Have a spotter when first learning the exercise.

commonMistakes: Pulling on the neck when hands are behind the head
Rolling too far forward and losing contact with the ball
Using momentum instead of controlled muscle contraction
exerciseTips: Keep your lower back pressed against the ball throughout the movement
Focus on curling your shoulders toward your hips rather than just lifting up
Squeeze your abs at the top and hold for one second
Move slowly and controlled on both the up and down phases
instructions: Lie on an exercise ball with your lower back curvature pressed against the spherical surface of the ball. Your feet should be bent at the knee and pressed firmly against the floor. The upper torso should be hanging off the top of the ball. The arms should either be kept alongside the body or crossed on top of your chest as these positions avoid neck strains (as opposed to the hands behind the back of the head position).
Lower your torso into a stretch position keeping the neck stationary at all times. This will be your starting position.

With the hips stationary, flex the waist by contracting the abdominals and curl the shoulders and trunk upward until you feel a nice contraction on your abdominals. The arms should simply slide up the side of your legs if you have them at the side or just stay on top of your chest if you have them crossed. The lower back should always stay in contact with the ball. Exhale as you perform this movement and hold the contraction for a second.
As you inhale, go back to the starting position.
Repeat for the recommended amount of repetitions.
exercises.name: Exercise Ball Crunch
exercises.overview: The exercise ball crunch is an effective core exercise that uses an unstable surface to increase abdominal muscle activation while providing support for the lower back.
exercises.safetyInfo: Avoid placing hands behind the head to prevent neck strain. Ensure the ball is properly inflated and positioned on a non-slip surface.

commonMistakes: Allowing hips to drop during the curling motion
Rolling the ball with feet instead of actively contracting hamstrings
Moving too quickly and losing control of the ball
exerciseTips: Keep your hips elevated throughout the entire movement
Focus on pulling with your hamstrings rather than just rolling the ball
Maintain steady pressure on the ball with both feet
Control both the curling and extending phases of the movement
instructions: Lie on your back with your arms at your sides and place your heels on top of an exercise ball.
Lift your hips off the ground to create a straight line from your knees to your shoulders.
Keep your core engaged and maintain this bridge position throughout the exercise.
Pull your heels toward your glutes by bending your knees and rolling the ball toward you.
Squeeze your hamstrings at the fully contracted position, then slowly extend your legs back out.
Roll the ball back to the starting position while maintaining the bridge and repeat.
exercises.name: Exercise Ball Hamstring Curl
exercises.name_pt: Flexão de Posterior na Bola Suíça
exercises.overview: The exercise ball hamstring curl is an intermediate bodyweight exercise that targets the hamstrings while challenging core stability. The unstable surface of the ball increases muscle activation and improves functional strength and balance.
exercises.safetyInfo: This exercise requires good core stability and hamstring flexibility. Those with lower back issues should ensure proper hip bridge form before adding the curling motion. Start slowly to master the coordination required.
variations: Ball Hamstring Curl with Hip Thrust

commonMistakes: Bending the arms during the movement
Using momentum instead of controlled muscle contraction
Not achieving full pike position with hips high
exerciseTips: Keep your arms straight and shoulders directly over your wrists
Focus on using your abs to pull the ball in rather than your legs
Maintain a slow and controlled tempo throughout
Keep your head in neutral position looking at the floor
instructions: Start in a plank position with your shins resting on top of an exercise ball and hands on the floor.
Keep your arms straight and core engaged with your body in a straight line.
Roll the ball toward your chest by lifting your hips up and pulling your knees toward your chest.
Continue until you reach a pike position with your hips high in the air.
Slowly roll the ball back out to return to the starting plank position.
Maintain control throughout the entire movement.
exercises.name: Exercise Ball Pike
exercises.overview: The exercise ball pike is an advanced core exercise that combines stability training with dynamic abdominal strengthening. This movement challenges the entire core while improving balance and coordination.
exercises.safetyInfo: Those with wrist problems may experience discomfort in the plank position. Individuals with lower back issues should start with easier progressions and avoid if pain occurs.

commonMistakes: Allowing hips to sag or pike up during the movement
Rolling the ball too far under the body
Using primarily hip flexors instead of abdominal muscles
exerciseTips: Maintain a straight line from head to hips throughout the movement
Pull knees toward chest using your core, not your hip flexors
Keep shoulders directly over wrists in the plank position
Control the ball's movement rather than letting it roll freely
instructions: Place an exercise ball nearby and lay on the floor in front of it with your hands on the floor shoulder width apart in a push-up position.

Now place your lower shins on top of an exercise ball. Tip: At this point your legs should be fully extended with the shins on top of the ball and the upper body should be in a push-up type of position being supported by your two extended arms in front of you. This will be your starting position.

While keeping your back completely straight and the upper body stationary, pull your knees in towards your chest as you exhale, allowing the ball to roll forward under your ankles. Squeeze your abs and hold that position for a second.
Now slowly straighten your legs, rolling the ball back to the starting position as you inhale.
Repeat for the recommended amount of repetitions.
exercises.name: Exercise Ball Pull-In
exercises.overview: The exercise ball pull-in combines core strengthening with stability training, targeting the abs while challenging balance and coordination.
exercises.safetyInfo: Ensure proper wrist alignment to avoid strain. Stop immediately if you experience lower back pain or cannot maintain proper plank form.

commonMistakes: Allowing knees to cave inward during the squat
Losing contact between the back and the exercise ball
Placing feet too close to the wall, limiting range of motion
exerciseTips: Keep your weight evenly distributed across both feet
Maintain contact between your back and the ball throughout the movement
Keep your knees aligned over your toes, not allowing them to cave inward
Engage your core to maintain proper posture during the squat
instructions: Place an exercise ball between your lower back and a wall, leaning against it for support.
Walk your feet forward about 2 feet from the wall and position them shoulder-width apart.
Keep your back pressed against the ball and slowly squat down by bending your knees.
Lower until your thighs are parallel to the floor, keeping the ball in contact with the wall.
Push through your heels to return to the starting position, rolling the ball up the wall.
exercises.name: Exercise Ball Wall Squat
exercises.name_pt: Agachamento na Parede com Bola Suíça
exercises.overview: The exercise ball wall squat provides support and stability while learning proper squat mechanics. It's excellent for beginners and those working on squat form.
exercises.safetyInfo: Ensure the exercise ball is properly inflated and rated for your body weight. Those with knee issues should limit the depth of the squat as needed.

commonMistakes: Allowing the torso to rotate during the pressing movement
Pressing straight up instead of slightly inward
Using the legs to assist the press
exerciseTips: Keep the non-working leg bent and crossed over midline for stability
Press the kettlebell in a slight arc toward the centerline of your body
Keep your core tight to prevent rotation during the press
Lower until your tricep touches the floor, not your elbow
instructions: Lie on the floor and position a kettlebell for one arm to press. The kettlebell should be held by the handle. The leg on the same side that you are pressing should be bent, with the knee crossing over the midline of the body.
Press the kettlebell by extending the elbow and adducting the arm, pressing it above your body. Return to the starting position.
exercises.name: Extended Range One-Arm Kettlebell Floor Press
exercises.name_pt: Supino no Chão Unilateral com Kettlebell - Amplitude Estendida
exercises.overview: This unilateral pressing variation challenges core stability while building chest and arm strength through an extended range of motion.
exercises.safetyInfo: Start with lighter weight to master the stability component. The floor provides a safety stop, but control the descent to avoid impact.

commonMistakes: Allowing the elbow to drift away from the body
Using too much weight and compensating with momentum
Moving the upper arm instead of just rotating at the shoulder
exerciseTips: Keep your upper arm parallel to the floor and torso
Maintain the 90-degree elbow angle throughout
Move only at the shoulder joint, keeping elbow stationary
Control the weight on both the lifting and lowering phases
instructions: Lie sideways on a flat bench with one arm holding a dumbbell and the other hand on top of the bench folded so that you can rest your head on it.
Bend the elbows of the arm holding the dumbbell so that it creates a 90-degree angle between the upper arm and the forearm. Tip: Keep the arm parallel to your torso.

Now bend the elbow while keeping the upper arm stationary. In this manner, the forearm will be parallel to the floor and perpendicular to your torso (Tip: So the forearm will be directly in front of you). The upper arm will be stationary by your torso and should be parallel to the floor (aligned with your torso at all times). This will be your starting position.

As you breathe out, externally rotate your forearm so that the dumbbell is lifted up in a semicircle motion as you maintain the 90 degree angle bend between the upper arms and the forearm. You will continue this external rotation until the forearm is perpendicular to the floor and the torso pointing towards the ceiling. At this point you will hold the contraction for a second.
As you breathe in, slowly go back to the starting position.
Repeat for the recommended amount of repetitions and then switch to the other arm.
exercises.name: External Rotation
exercises.overview: External rotation strengthens the rotator cuff muscles, which are crucial for shoulder stability and injury prevention in overhead activities.
exercises.safetyInfo: Use very light weight as the rotator cuff muscles are small and injury-prone. Stop if you feel pain in the shoulder joint.

commonMistakes: Standing too close to the anchor point, reducing resistance
Allowing the elbow to move away from the body
Moving too quickly and losing control of the band
exerciseTips: Keep elbow firmly pressed against your side throughout the movement
Step far enough away to create tension in the starting position
Rotate only at the shoulder while keeping the upper arm still
Return slowly to control the eccentric portion
instructions: Choke the band around a post. The band should be at the same height as your elbow. Stand with your left side to the band a couple of feet away.
Grasp the end of the band with your right hand, and keep your elbow pressed firmly to your side. We recommend you hold a pad or foam roll in place with your elbow to keep it firmly in position.
With your upper arm in position, your elbow should be flexed to 90 degrees with your hand reaching across the front of your torso. This will be your starting position.
Execute the movement by rotating your arm in a backhand motion, keeping your elbow in place.
Continue as far as you are able, pause, and then return to the starting position.
exercises.name: External Rotation with Band
exercises.overview: Band external rotation provides variable resistance for rotator cuff strengthening and is excellent for rehabilitation and injury prevention protocols.
exercises.safetyInfo: Ensure the band is securely anchored at elbow height. Use appropriate resistance to avoid overstressing the small rotator cuff muscles.

commonMistakes: Setting the cable too high or too low relative to the elbow
Using the entire arm instead of just rotating at the shoulder
Choosing too heavy a weight and using compensatory movements
exerciseTips: Set the cable height exactly at elbow level for optimal angle
Keep your elbow pinned to your side using a towel or pad
Focus on the backhand rotation motion while keeping upper arm still
Use a slow, controlled tempo in both directions
instructions: Adjust the cable to the same height as your elbow. Stand with your left side to the band a couple of feet away.
Grasp the handle with your right hand, and keep your elbow pressed firmly to your side. We recommend you hold a pad or foam roll in place with your elbow to keep it firmly in position.
With your upper arm in position, your elbow should be flexed to 90 degrees with your hand reaching across the front of your torso. This will be your starting position.
Execute the movement by rotating your arm in a backhand motion, keeping your elbow in place.
exercises.name: External Rotation with Cable
exercises.overview: Cable external rotation provides consistent resistance throughout the range of motion, making it ideal for strengthening the rotator cuff and improving shoulder stability.
exercises.safetyInfo: Start with very light resistance as rotator cuff muscles fatigue quickly. Maintain proper posture to avoid compensating with other muscle groups.

commonMistakes: Letting the elbows drift forward during the curl
Using the back and shoulders to assist the movement
Gripping too close together causing wrist discomfort
exerciseTips: Keep your elbows pinned to your sides throughout the movement
Focus on the squeeze at the top of each rep
Control the eccentric (lowering) portion
Avoid swinging or using momentum
instructions: Stand with feet shoulder-width apart, holding an EZ curl bar with a close underhand grip.
Keep your elbows tucked close to your sides with the bar at arm's length.
Curl the bar up by contracting your biceps, keeping your upper arms stationary.
Squeeze your biceps at the top of the movement when the bar reaches chest level.
Lower the bar slowly back to the starting position with control.
exercises.name: EZ Curl Bar Close Grip Curl
exercises.name_pt: Rosca com Barra W - Pegada Fechada
exercises.overview: The EZ curl bar close grip curl is a bicep isolation exercise that emphasizes the short head of the biceps. The close grip and angled bar provide a unique stimulus for bicep development while being easier on the wrists than a straight bar.
exercises.safetyInfo: Those with wrist or elbow issues should use caution. The close grip may place additional stress on the wrists and forearms.

commonMistakes: Swinging the torso to generate momentum
Allowing elbows to drift forward during the curl
Dropping the weight too quickly on the descent
exerciseTips: Keep elbows pinned to your sides throughout the movement
Control the negative portion for 2-3 seconds
Squeeze biceps at the top for maximum contraction
Use the natural grip angle of the EZ bar to reduce wrist stress
instructions: Stand up straight while holding an EZ curl bar at the wide outer handle. The palms of your hands should be facing forward and slightly tilted inward due to the shape of the bar. Keep your elbows close to your torso. This will be your starting position.
Now, while keeping your upper arms stationary, exhale and curl the weights forward while contracting the biceps. Focus on only moving your forearms.
Continue to raise the weight until your biceps are fully contracted and the bar is at shoulder level. Hold the top contracted position for a moment and squeeze the biceps.
Then inhale and slowly lower the bar back to the starting position.
Repeat for the recommended amount of repetitions.
exercises.name: EZ-Bar Curl
exercises.overview: The EZ-Bar Curl is an isolation exercise that targets the biceps using a cambered bar that allows for a more natural wrist position. This variation reduces wrist stress while effectively building bicep mass and strength.
exercises.safetyInfo: The EZ bar's angled grip reduces wrist strain compared to straight bars. Start with lighter weight to master the movement pattern and avoid ego lifting that leads to poor form.

commonMistakes: Allowing upper arms to drift back toward the head
Lowering the bar too far past the forehead
Using momentum instead of controlled muscle contraction
exerciseTips: Keep upper arms perpendicular to the floor throughout
Lower the bar to just above your forehead, not to your chest
Focus on moving only at the elbow joint
Use a controlled tempo on both lifting and lowering phases
instructions: Using a close grip, lift the EZ bar and hold it with your elbows in as you lie on the bench. Your arms should be perpendicular to the floor. This will be your starting position.
Keeping the upper arms stationary, lower the bar by allowing the elbows to flex. Inhale as you perform this portion of the movement. Pause once the bar is directly above the forehead.
Lift the bar back to the starting position by extending the elbow and exhaling.
Repeat.
exercises.name: EZ-Bar Skullcrusher
exercises.overview: The EZ-Bar Skullcrusher is an isolation exercise that effectively targets all three heads of the triceps. The EZ bar's grip angle provides better wrist comfort while allowing for focused tricep development.
exercises.safetyInfo: Always use a spotter when possible and start with lighter weight. The EZ bar reduces wrist stress compared to straight bars. Avoid going too heavy as elbow joints are vulnerable in this position.

commonMistakes: Pulling too low toward the chest instead of face level
Using too much weight and compensating with poor form
Not separating the handles wide enough at the end position
exerciseTips: Pull the rope to eye level while separating your hands
Keep your upper arms parallel to the floor
Focus on squeezing your shoulder blades together
Lead the movement with your rear delts, not your arms
instructions: Facing a high pulley with a rope or dual handles attached, pull the weight directly towards your face, separating your hands as you do so. Keep your upper arms parallel to the ground.
exercises.name: Face Pull
exercises.overview: Face Pulls are a compound pulling exercise that primarily targets the rear deltoids while strengthening the mid-traps and rhomboids. This exercise is essential for shoulder health and correcting forward head posture.
exercises.safetyInfo: Start with lighter weight to master the movement pattern. This exercise is excellent for shoulder health and posture correction when performed correctly.
variations: Bent Over Two-Dumbbell Row With Palms In

commonMistakes: Not allowing the bar to roll down far enough in the fingers
Using wrist movement instead of isolated finger flexion
Rushing through the movement without proper control
exerciseTips: Allow the bar to roll down to your fingertips before curling back up
Use a full range of motion from fingers extended to fully flexed
Keep your wrists stable throughout the movement
Focus on controlled movement rather than heavy weight
instructions: Hold a barbell with both hands and your palms facing up; hands spaced about shoulder width.
Place your feet flat on the floor, at a distance that is slightly wider than shoulder width apart. This will be your starting position.
Lower the bar as far as possible by extending the fingers. Allowing the bar to roll down the hands, catch the bar with the final joint in the fingers.
Now curl bar up as high as possible by closing your hands while exhaling. Hold the contraction at the top.
exercises.name: Finger Curls
exercises.overview: Finger Curls are an isolation exercise specifically targeting the finger flexor muscles and grip strength. This exercise is beneficial for athletes requiring strong grip strength and those recovering from hand injuries.
exercises.safetyInfo: Start with very light weight as finger muscles are smaller and weaker than other muscle groups. Ensure secure grip to prevent dropping the barbell. This exercise may aggravate existing wrist or finger injuries.
variations: Seated One-Arm Dumbbell Palms-Down Wrist Curl

commonMistakes: Using too much weight causing elbows to bend excessively
Allowing shoulders to internally rotate and roll forward
Rushing through the eccentric phase without controlling the weight
exerciseTips: Keep a slight bend in your elbows throughout the entire movement
Focus on squeezing your chest muscles at the top of the movement
Control the negative portion slowly to maximize muscle tension
Maintain constant tension by not letting the weights rest at any point
instructions: Position a flat bench between two low pulleys so that when you are laying on it, your chest will be lined up with the cable pulleys.
Lay flat on the bench and keep your feet on the ground.
Have someone hand you the handles on each hand. You will grab each single handle attachment with a palms up grip.

Extend your arms by your side with a slight bend on your elbows. Tip: You will keep this bend constant through the whole movement. Your arms should be parallel to the floor. This is your starting position.

Now start lifting the arms in a semi-circle motion directly in front of you by pulling the cables together until both hands meet at the top of the movement. Squeeze your chest as you perform this motion and breathe out during this movement. Also, hold the contraction for a second at the top. Tip: When performed correctly, at the top position of this movement, your arms should be perpendicular to your torso and the floor touching above your chest.
Slowly come back to the starting position.
Repeat for the recommended amount of repetitions.
exercises.name: Flat Bench Cable Flyes
exercises.name_pt: Crucifixo na Polia no Banco Reto
exercises.overview: The flat bench cable fly is an isolation exercise that targets the chest muscles through a horizontal adduction movement pattern. It provides constant tension throughout the range of motion, making it excellent for muscle hypertrophy and definition.
exercises.safetyInfo: Avoid this exercise if you have shoulder impingement or recent chest/shoulder injuries. Start with lighter weight to master the movement pattern before progressing.

commonMistakes: Using momentum to swing the legs up instead of controlled movement
Allowing the lower back to arch excessively during the movement
Not fully contracting the abdominals at the top position
exerciseTips: Pull your knees toward your chest rather than just lifting your legs
Focus on contracting your abs throughout the movement
Keep your lower back pressed against the bench
Control both the lifting and lowering phases
instructions: Lie on an exercise mat or a flat bench with your legs off the end.

Place your hands either under your glutes with your palms down or by the sides holding on to the bench (or with palms down by the side on an exercise mat). Also extend your legs straight out. This will be your starting position.
Bend your knees and pull your upper thighs into your midsection as you breathe out. Continue this movement until your knees are near your chest. Hold the contracted position for a second.
As you breathe in, slowly return to the starting position.
Repeat for the recommended amount of repetitions.
exercises.name: Flat Bench Leg Pull-In
exercises.overview: The flat bench leg pull-in is a beginner-friendly core exercise that primarily targets the rectus abdominis. It combines knee flexion with spinal flexion to effectively strengthen the abdominal muscles.
exercises.safetyInfo: Avoid if you have lower back issues or hip flexor tightness. Keep movements controlled to prevent strain on the lumbar spine.

commonMistakes: Allowing the lower back to arch excessively off the bench
Using momentum to swing the legs up rather than controlled movement
Lowering the legs too quickly without maintaining tension
exerciseTips: Keep your legs as straight as possible with only a slight knee bend
Raise your legs until they form a 90-degree angle with the floor
Lower your legs slowly and stop just before your feet touch the ground
Keep your lower back pressed firmly against the bench
instructions: Lie with your back flat on a bench and your legs extended in front of you off the end.
Place your hands either under your glutes with your palms down or by the sides holding on to the bench. This will be your starting position.

As you keep your legs extended, straight as possible with your knees slightly bent but locked raise your legs until they make a 90-degree angle with the floor. Exhale as you perform this portion of the movement and hold the contraction at the top for a second.
Now, as you inhale, slowly lower your legs back down to the starting position.
exercises.name: Flat Bench Lying Leg Raise
exercises.name_pt: Elevação de Pernas Deitado no Banco Reto
exercises.overview: The flat bench lying leg raise is an isolation exercise that targets the lower portion of the rectus abdominis. It challenges core stability while building strength in the hip flexors.
exercises.safetyInfo: This exercise can place significant stress on the lower back. Avoid if you have lumbar spine issues or tight hip flexors.

commonMistakes: Flexing the wrists forward which can cause strain
Starting the curl before arms are fully extended
Using the shoulders to assist with the lifting motion
exerciseTips: Keep your wrists in a neutral or slightly extended position
Allow your arms to hang naturally at your sides before curling
Avoid swinging or using momentum during the movement
Squeeze the biceps hard at the top of each repetition
instructions: Hold the dumbbell towards the side farther from you so that you have more weight on the side closest to you. (This can be done for a good effect on all bicep dumbbell exercises). Now do a normal incline dumbbell curl, but keep your wrists as far back as possible so as to neutralize any stress that is placed on them.
Sit on an incline bench that is angled at 45-degrees while holding a dumbbell on each hand.

Let your arms hang down on your sides, with the elbows in, and turn the palms of your hands forward with the thumbs pointing away from the body. Tip: You will keep this hand position throughout the movement as there should not be any twisting of the hands as they come up. This will be your starting position.

Curl up the two dumbbells at the same time until your biceps are fully contracted and exhale. Tip: Do not swing the arms or use momentum. Keep a controlled motion at all times. Hold the contracted position for a second at the top.
As you inhale, slowly go back to the starting position.
Repeat for the recommended amount of repetitions.
exercises.name: Flexor Incline Dumbbell Curls
exercises.overview: The flexor incline dumbbell curl is a bicep isolation exercise performed on an inclined bench that emphasizes the long head of the biceps. The inclined position provides a greater stretch and range of motion compared to standard curls.
exercises.safetyInfo: Keep wrists in a neutral position to avoid strain. Start with lighter weights to ensure proper form and prevent bicep or forearm injury.

commonMistakes: Allowing the hips to flex and break the straight line from knees to head
Lowering too quickly without eccentric control
Relying too heavily on arm strength to return to starting position
exerciseTips: Keep your hips extended throughout the entire movement
Lower yourself slowly to control the eccentric phase
Use your arms minimally - only enough to prevent face planting
Focus on using your hamstrings to control the descent
instructions: You can use a partner for this exercise or brace your feet under something stable.
Begin on your knees with your upper legs and torso upright. If using a partner, they will firmly hold your feet to keep you in position. This will be your starting position.
Lower yourself by extending at the knee, taking care to NOT flex the hips as you go forward.

Place your hands in front of you as you reach the floor. This movement is very difficult and you may be unable to do it unaided. Use your arms to lightly push off the floor to aid your return to the starting position.
exercises.name: Floor Glute-Ham Raise
exercises.overview: The floor glute-ham raise is an advanced bodyweight exercise that intensely targets the hamstrings through knee flexion while maintaining hip extension. It's one of the most effective exercises for posterior chain development.
exercises.safetyInfo: This is an advanced exercise that can cause severe muscle soreness and potential injury if progressed too quickly. Start with assisted versions and gradually build strength.

commonMistakes: Using momentum instead of muscle contraction to lift legs
Bending knees during the kicking motion
Lifting legs too high and losing glute tension
exerciseTips: Squeeze glutes and hamstrings to initiate each leg lift
Keep legs straight throughout the entire movement
Maintain controlled tempo and avoid rushing the alternating pattern
Keep hips pressed against the bench edge for stability
instructions: On a flat bench lie facedown with the hips on the edge of the bench, the legs straight with toes high off the floor and with the arms on top of the bench holding on to the front edge.
Squeeze your glutes and hamstrings and straighten the legs until they are level with the hips. This will be your starting position.
Start the movement by lifting the left leg higher than the right leg.
Then lower the left leg as you lift the right leg.

Continue alternating in this manner (as though you are doing a flutter kick in water) until you have done the recommended amount of repetitions for each leg. Make sure that you keep a controlled movement at all times. Tip: You will breathe normally as you perform this movement.
exercises.name: Flutter Kicks
exercises.overview: Flutter kicks target the glutes and hamstrings through alternating hip extension movements, helping to strengthen the posterior chain and improve hip stability.
exercises.safetyInfo: This exercise is generally safe for most individuals. Those with lower back issues should start with smaller range of motion and progress gradually.

commonMistakes: Not exploring different grip positions to find the most comfortable
Pressing with flared elbows despite the neutral grip advantage
exerciseTips: Take advantage of the various grip widths available
Keep your shoulders pulled back and down on the bench
Use the neutral grip to reduce shoulder stress
Maintain steady breathing pattern throughout the set
instructions: Set up on the bench and select your preferred grip width on the football bar.
Grip the angled handles with a neutral hand position.
Unrack the bar and hold it over your chest with arms fully extended.
Lower the bar to your chest while maintaining control and proper elbow angle.
Press the weight back up to full arm extension.
Complete your set and re-rack the bar safely.
exercises.name: Football Bar Press
exercises.overview: The football bar press utilizes a specialty barbell designed with multiple angled, neutral grip positions. It allows for more natural hand positioning during pressing movements while reducing joint stress.
exercises.safetyInfo: The football bar's neutral grip positioning reduces stress on the shoulder joints and wrists. It's an excellent alternative for those experiencing discomfort with traditional bench pressing.

commonMistakes: Landing flat-footed with locked knees instead of absorbing impact
Pausing too long between jumps and losing the plyometric effect
Allowing knees to cave inward during landing or takeoff
exerciseTips: Land softly on the balls of your feet to absorb impact
Descend immediately into the next squat upon landing
Drive explosively through the balls of your feet during takeoff
Keep chest up and core engaged throughout the movement
instructions: Cross your arms over your chest.
With your head up and your back straight, position your feet at shoulder width.
Keeping your back straight and chest up, squat down as you inhale until your upper thighs are parallel, or lower, to the floor.
Now pressing mainly with the ball of your feet, jump straight up in the air as high as possible, using the thighs like springs. Exhale during this portion of the movement.
When you touch the floor again, immediately squat down and jump again.
Repeat for the recommended amount of repetitions.
exercises.name: Freehand Jump Squat
exercises.overview: Freehand jump squats develop explosive leg power and improve vertical jump ability through plyometric training that targets the entire lower body.
exercises.safetyInfo: This high-impact exercise may not be suitable for those with knee or ankle issues. Start with lower jump heights and progress gradually.

commonMistakes: Pulling up too high instead of just lifting shoulder blades
Using momentum instead of controlled abdominal contraction
Allowing lower back to arch off the floor
exerciseTips: Press your lower back flat against the floor before lifting
Cross your arms over your chest to maintain proper form
Lift only your shoulder blades off the ground
Keep your feet close to your body throughout
instructions: Lie with your back flat on the floor (or exercise mat) and your legs extended in front of you.
Now bend at the knees and place your outer thighs by the floor (or mat) as you make the soles of your feet touch each other.

Now try pushing both soles and bringing them up as near you as possible while you keep the outer thighs on the floor (or at least almost touching it). Tip: In this position your legs should create a diamond shape.
Now, cross your arms in front of you by touching the opposite shoulders. This will be your starting position.
As you exhale flatten your lower back to the floor while curling the torso upwards. Tip: This will be like performing the first 1/4 movement of a sit up. Hold at the top position for a second.
As you inhale, slowly lower back to the starting position.
Repeat for the recommended amount of repetitions.
exercises.name: Frog Sit-Ups
exercises.overview: Frog Sit-Ups target the rectus abdominis with legs in a butterfly position. This variation reduces hip flexor involvement while maintaining effective abdominal activation.
exercises.safetyInfo: This exercise may cause discomfort in those with lower back issues. Stop if you experience any back pain during the movement.

commonMistakes: Allowing elbows to drop which causes the bar to roll forward
Leaning forward excessively during the descent
Not reaching proper depth below parallel
exerciseTips: Keep your elbows high throughout the entire movement
Drive through your midfoot when ascending
Maintain an upright torso position
Descend until thighs are parallel to the floor
instructions: This exercise is best performed inside a squat rack for safety purposes. To begin, first set the bar on a rack that best matches your height. Once the correct height is chosen and the bar is loaded, bring your arms up under the bar while keeping the elbows high and the upper arm slightly above parallel to the floor. Rest the bar on top of the deltoids and cross your arms while grasping the bar for total control.
Lift the bar off the rack by first pushing with your legs and at the same time straightening your torso.

Step away from the rack and position your legs using a shoulder width medium stance with the toes slightly pointed out. Keep your head up at all times as looking down will get you off balance and also maintain a straight back. This will be your starting position. (Note: For the purposes of this discussion we will use the medium stance described above which targets overall development; however you can choose any of the three stances described in the foot positioning section).

Begin to slowly lower the bar by bending the knees as you maintain a straight posture with the head up. Continue down until the angle between the upper leg and the calves becomes slightly less than 90-degrees (which is the point in which the upper legs are below parallel to the floor). Inhale as you perform this portion of the movement. Tip: If you performed the exercise correctly, the front of the knees should make an imaginary straight line with the toes that is perpendicular to the front. If your knees are past that imaginary line (if they are past your toes) then you are placing undue stress on the knee and the exercise has been performed incorrectly.
Begin to raise the bar as you exhale by pushing the floor mainly with the middle of your foot as you straighten the legs again and go back to the starting position.
Repeat for the recommended amount of repetitions.
exercises.name: Front Barbell Squat
exercises.overview: The Front Barbell Squat emphasizes quadriceps development more than back squats while requiring greater core stability. It's excellent for building functional leg strength and improving posture.
exercises.safetyInfo: This advanced exercise requires good wrist and shoulder mobility. Use a squat rack with safety bars set just below your lowest squat position. Avoid if you have wrist or shoulder injuries.

commonMistakes: Plopping down onto the bench instead of controlled descent
Using the bench as a crutch to bounce back up
Allowing knees to cave inward during the movement
exerciseTips: Lightly touch the bench without sitting down completely
Keep your elbows elevated throughout the movement
Drive through your heels when standing up
Control the descent slowly to the bench
instructions: This exercise is best performed inside a squat rack for safety purposes. To begin, first set a flat bench behind you and set the bar on a rack that best matches your height. Once the correct height is chosen and the bar is loaded, bring your arms up under the bar while keeping the elbows high and the upper arm slightly above parallel to the floor. Rest the bar on top of the deltoids and cross your arms while grasping the bar for total control.
Lift the bar off the rack by first pushing with your legs and at the same time straightening your torso.

Step away from the rack and position your legs using a shoulder width medium stance with the toes slightly pointed out. Keep your head up at all times as looking down will get you off balance and also maintain a straight back. This will be your starting position. (Note: For the purposes of this discussion we will use the medium stance described above which targets overall development; however you can choose any of the three stances described in the foot positioning section).

Begin to slowly lower the bar by bending the knees as you maintain a straight posture with the head up. Continue down until you touch the bench with your glutes. Inhale as you perform this portion of the movement. Tip: If you performed the exercise correctly, the front of the knees should make an imaginary straight line with the toes that is perpendicular to the front. If your knees are past that imaginary line (if they are past your toes) then you are placing undue stress on the knee and the exercise has been performed incorrectly.
Begin to raise the bar as you exhale by pushing the floor mainly with the heel of your foot as you straighten the legs again and go back to the starting position.
Repeat for the recommended amount of repetitions.
exercises.name: Front Barbell Squat To A Bench
exercises.name_pt: Agachamento Frontal com Barra no Banco
exercises.overview: This variation teaches proper squat depth and provides confidence for beginners learning the front squat pattern. The bench serves as a depth guide and safety mechanism.
exercises.safetyInfo: Ensure the bench is stable and at appropriate height. This exercise is safer for beginners learning squat depth but still requires proper front rack mobility.

commonMistakes: Using momentum by swinging the torso
Lifting the arm too high above shoulder level
Using too much weight leading to poor form
exerciseTips: Keep a slight bend in your elbow throughout the movement
Lift your arm to shoulder height, not higher
Control the weight on both the lifting and lowering phases
Keep your core engaged to prevent swaying
instructions: Select the weight on a low pulley machine and grasp the single hand cable attachment that is attached to the low pulley with your left hand.

Face away from the pulley and put your arm straight down with the hand cable attachment in front of your thighs at arms' length with the palms of the hand facing your thighs. This will be your starting position.

While maintaining the torso stationary (no swinging), lift the left arm to the front with a slight bend on the elbow and the palms of the hand always faces down. Continue to go up until you arm is slightly above parallel to the floor. Exhale as you execute this portion of the movement and pause for a second at the top.
Now as you inhale lower the arm back down slowly to the starting position.
Once all of the recommended amount of repetitions have been performed for this arm, switch arms and perform the exercise with the right one.
exercises.name: Front Cable Raise
exercises.overview: The Front Cable Raise isolates the anterior deltoid muscle using constant tension from the cable. This exercise builds shoulder strength and improves front deltoid definition.
exercises.safetyInfo: Avoid this exercise if you have shoulder impingement. Stop if you feel any pinching sensation in the shoulder joint during the movement.

commonMistakes: Swinging the weight up with momentum
Raising arms too high above shoulder level
Using too heavy weight causing body swing
exerciseTips: Keep core engaged throughout movement
Control the weight on the way down
Stop at shoulder height to avoid impingement
Use a slight bend in elbows
instructions: Pick a couple of dumbbells and stand with a straight torso and the dumbbells on front of your thighs at arms length with the palms of the hand facing your thighs. This will be your starting position.

While maintaining the torso stationary (no swinging), lift the left dumbbell to the front with a slight bend on the elbow and the palms of the hands always facing down. Continue to go up until you arm is slightly above parallel to the floor. Exhale as you execute this portion of the movement and pause for a second at the top. Inhale after the second pause.
Now lower the dumbbell back down slowly to the starting position as you simultaneously lift the right dumbbell.
Continue alternating in this fashion until all of the recommended amount of repetitions have been performed for each arm.
exercises.name: Front Dumbbell Raise
exercises.overview: An isolation exercise targeting the front deltoids by raising dumbbells forward. Essential for building anterior shoulder strength and definition.
exercises.safetyInfo: Stop if you feel shoulder impingement. Avoid lifting above shoulder height to prevent subacromial compression.

commonMistakes: Lifting too high past shoulder level
Arching back off the bench
Using momentum instead of muscle control
exerciseTips: Keep your back pressed against the bench
Control the eccentric portion slowly
Maintain neutral wrist position
Breathe out during the lifting phase
instructions: Sit down on an incline bench with the incline set anywhere between 30 to 60 degrees while holding a dumbbell on each hand. Tip: You can change the angle to hit the muscle a little differently each time.
Extend your arms straight in front of you and have your palms facing down with the dumbbells raised about 1 inch above your thighs. This will be your starting position.

Slowly raise the dumbbells straight up until they are slightly above your shoulders, while keeping your elbows locked. Squeeze at the top for a second and make sure you breathe out during this portion of the movement. Tip: Keep your head resting down against the bench and your legs on the floor at all times.
Lower the arms back to the starting position as you inhale.
Repeat for the recommended amount of repetitions.
exercises.name: Front Incline Dumbbell Raise
exercises.overview: A variation of the front raise performed on an incline bench, providing better stability and isolation of the anterior deltoids. The angle changes the resistance curve for varied stimulus.
exercises.safetyInfo: The incline position reduces stress on the lower back compared to standing variations. Avoid if experiencing shoulder impingement symptoms.

commonMistakes: Allowing the weight to pull the torso into forward lean
Holding the weight too far away from the body
Taking uneven or rushed steps
exerciseTips: Keep the weight close to your chest throughout the entire carry
Maintain an upright posture and resist forward lean
Breathe normally while keeping your core engaged
Take even, controlled steps without rushing
instructions: Hold a dumbbell vertically against your chest with both hands cupping the top weight plate.
Keep your elbows close to your sides and maintain an upright torso position.
Engage your core and walk forward with controlled, even steps.
Maintain the weight close to your chest without allowing it to pull you forward.
Continue for the desired distance or time while maintaining perfect posture.
exercises.name: Front Loaded Carry
exercises.overview: The Front Loaded Carry challenges the core's anti-extension capabilities while promoting good posture. It's an excellent introduction to loaded carries and helps develop core endurance and stability.
exercises.safetyInfo: This exercise is beginner-friendly and generally safe for most individuals. The front-loaded position helps reinforce good posture naturally.

commonMistakes: Gripping plate only at the bottom
Using momentum to swing plate up
Raising plate too high above shoulders
exerciseTips: Grip plate at 3 and 9 o'clock positions
Keep slight bend in elbows throughout
Engage core to prevent back arch
Control the descent slowly
instructions: While standing straight, hold a barbell plate in both hands at the 3 and 9 o'clock positions. Your palms should be facing each other and your arms should be extended and locked with a slight bend at the elbows and the plate should be down near your waist in front of you as far as you can go. Tip: The arms will remain in this position throughout the exercise. This will be your starting position.

Slowly raise the plate as you exhale until it is a little above shoulder level. Hold the contraction for a second. Tip: make sure that you do not swing the weight or bend at the elbows. Your torso should remain stationary throughout the movement as well.
As you inhale, slowly lower the plate back down to the starting position.
Repeat for the recommended amount of repetitions.
exercises.name: Front Plate Raise
exercises.overview: A front deltoid isolation exercise using a weight plate instead of dumbbells. Provides a different grip challenge and can allow for heavier loading than dumbbells.
exercises.safetyInfo: Ensure secure grip on the plate to prevent dropping. Start with lighter weight to master the movement pattern before progressing.

commonMistakes: Bending elbows too much during movement
Moving too quickly through the range
Not maintaining control at the bottom position
exerciseTips: Keep slight bend in elbows throughout
Use controlled semicircular motion
Keep core engaged on the bench
Breathe in during the lowering phase
instructions: Lie on a flat bench while holding a barbell using a palms down grip that is about 15 inches apart.
Place the bar on your upper thighs, extend your arms and lock them while keeping a slight bend on the elbows. This will be your starting position.

Now raise the weight using a semicircular motion and keeping your arms straight as you inhale. Continue the same movement until the bar is on the other side above your head . (Tip: the bar will travel approximately 180-degrees). At this point your arms should be parallel to the floor with the palms of your hands facing the ceiling.
Now return the barbell to the starting position by reversing the motion as you exhale.
Repeat for the recommended amount of repetitions.
exercises.name: Front Raise And Pullover
exercises.overview: A compound movement combining elements of a front raise and pullover in one fluid motion. Targets chest, shoulders, and lats through a unique range of motion.
exercises.safetyInfo: Start with lighter weight to learn the movement pattern. The stretched position at the bottom can stress the shoulders, so progress gradually.
