commonMistakes: Dropping elbows during the descent, causing forward lean
Gripping the bar too tightly instead of using fingertip support
Allowing knees to cave inward during the squat
exerciseTips: Keep elbows high throughout the entire movement
Rest the bar on your clavicles and front deltoids, not your hands
Drive through your heels when ascending
Maintain an upright torso by engaging your core
instructions: To begin, first set the bar in a rack slightly below shoulder level. Rest the bar on top of the deltoids, pushing into the clavicles, and lightly touching the throat. Your hands should be in a clean grip, touching the bar only with your fingers to help keep it in position.

Lift the bar off the rack by first pushing with your legs and at the same time straightening your torso. Step away from the rack and position your legs using a shoulder width medium stance with the toes slightly pointed out. Keep your head and elbows up at all times. This will be your starting position.

Bend at the knees, sitting down between your legs. Continue down until your hamstrings are on your calves. Keep your knees aligned with your feet by consciously using your abductors to push your knees out as you squat.
Begin to raise the bar as you exhale by pushing the floor mainly with the heel or middle of your foot as you straighten the legs again and return to the starting position.
exercises.name: Front Squat (Clean Grip)
exercises.overview: The front squat with clean grip is a compound lower body exercise that emphasizes the quadriceps while building core strength and improving posture. It's essential for Olympic lifting development and creates better squat mechanics than back squats.
exercises.safetyInfo: The clean grip requires adequate wrist and shoulder mobility. Those with wrist injuries or limited thoracic spine mobility should consider alternative grip variations. Avoid if you have shoulder impingement or neck injuries.

commonMistakes: Allowing kettlebells to pull you forward during the squat
Incomplete clean causing kettlebells to rest on forearms instead of shoulders
Rising on toes instead of driving through full foot
exerciseTips: Clean both kettlebells simultaneously to the rack position
Keep kettlebells tight against your chest throughout the movement
Push knees out in line with toes during descent
Maintain vertical shin angle at the bottom position
instructions: Clean two kettlebells to your shoulders. Clean the kettlebells to your shoulders by extending through the legs and hips as you pull the kettlebells towards your shoulders. Rotate your wrists as you do so.

Looking straight ahead at all times, squat as low as you can and pause at the bottom. As you squat down, push your knees out. You should squat between your legs, keeping an upright torso, with your head and chest up.
Rise back up by driving through your heels and repeat.
exercises.name: Front Squats With Two Kettlebells
exercises.overview: This variation provides the benefits of front squats while improving unilateral stability and grip strength. The offset loading challenges core stability more than traditional barbell front squats.
exercises.safetyInfo: Ensure proper kettlebell clean technique before attempting. Start with lighter weights to master the rack position. Avoid if you have wrist or shoulder mobility restrictions.

commonMistakes: Using momentum or swinging the torso to lift heavier weights
Lifting arms too high above shoulder level
Allowing wrists to bend backward under load
exerciseTips: Maintain slight bend in elbows throughout the movement
Control the descent to maximize muscle tension
Stop at shoulder height to avoid shoulder impingement
Keep core engaged to prevent back arching
instructions: Pick a couple of dumbbells and stand with a straight torso and the dumbbells on front of your thighs at arms length with the palms of the hand facing your thighs. This will be your starting position.

While maintaining the torso stationary (no swinging), lift the dumbbells to the front with a slight bend on the elbow and the palms of the hands always facing down. Continue to go up until you arms are slightly above parallel to the floor. Exhale as you execute this portion of the movement and pause for a second at the top.
As you inhale, lower the dumbbells back down slowly to the starting position.
Repeat for the recommended amount of repetitions.
exercises.name: Front Two-Dumbbell Raise
exercises.overview: An isolation exercise targeting the front deltoid muscles, essential for developing shoulder strength and definition. This movement improves overhead pressing power and shoulder stability.
exercises.safetyInfo: Avoid if you have anterior shoulder impingement or rotator cuff injuries. Use lighter weights initially as the anterior deltoid is a small muscle group prone to overuse injuries.

commonMistakes: Pulling with arms instead of engaging the lats first
Leaning back excessively to use momentum
Failing to rotate hands during the movement
exerciseTips: Initiate the pull with your lats, not your arms
Rotate your hands during the movement for full range
Squeeze shoulder blades together at the bottom
Control the eccentric portion slowly
instructions: Either standing or seated on a high bench, grasp two stirrup cables that are attached to the high pulleys. Grab with the opposing hand so your arms are crisscrossed about you and your palms are facing forward.

Keeping your chest up and maintaining a slight arch in your lower back, pull the handles down as if you were doing a regular pulldown. The range of motion will be more of an arc. During the movement, rotate your hands so that in the bottom position your palms face each other rather than forward. Return slowly to the starting position and repeat.
exercises.name: Full Range-Of-Motion Lat Pulldown
exercises.overview: This advanced lat pulldown variation provides a greater range of motion than traditional pulldowns, maximizing lat development. The crossed cable setup creates a unique muscle recruitment pattern for enhanced back width.
exercises.safetyInfo: The crossed cable position requires good shoulder mobility. Those with shoulder instability or rotator cuff injuries should use caution. Ensure cables are securely attached before beginning.
variations: Bent Over Two-Dumbbell Row With Palms In

commonMistakes: Pulling chin to bar instead of chest to bar
Losing the lean-back position during the movement
Rushing the negative portion of the rep
exerciseTips: Lean back throughout the entire range of motion
Pull your chest to the bar, not your chin
Arch your spine and keep your head back
Focus on squeezing shoulder blades at the top
instructions: Grasp the pull-up bar with a shoulder width underhand grip.
Now hang with your arms fully extended and stick your chest out and lean back. Tip: You will be leaning back throughout the entire movement. This will be your starting position.

Start pulling yourself towards the bar with your spine arched throughout the movement and your head leaning back as far away from the bar as possible. Exhale as you perform this portion of the movement. Tip: At the upper end of the movement, your hips and legs will be at about a 45-degree angle to the floor.

Keep pulling until your collarbone passes the bar and your lower chest or sternum area touches it. Hold that contraction for a second. Tip: By the time you've completed this portion of the movement; your head will be parallel to the floor.
Slowly start going back to the starting position as you inhale.
Repeat for the recommended amount of repetitions.
exercises.name: Gironda Sternum Chins
exercises.overview: An advanced chin-up variation developed by Vince Gironda that emphasizes the lower lats and creates maximum back development. The leaning position and chest-to-bar contact provides superior lat activation.
exercises.safetyInfo: This advanced movement places significant stress on the shoulders and requires good mobility. Not recommended for beginners or those with shoulder impingement. Progress gradually from standard chin-ups.
variations: Bent Over Two-Dumbbell Row With Palms In

commonMistakes: Arching the lower back excessively
Using momentum to swing the leg up
Not maintaining the 90-degree knee angle
exerciseTips: Keep your core engaged throughout the movement
Squeeze your glute at the top position
Maintain a 90-degree angle at the knee
Control the lowering phase
instructions: Kneel on the floor or an exercise mat and bend at the waist with your arms extended in front of you (perpendicular to the torso) in order to get into a kneeling push-up position but with the arms spaced at shoulder width. Your head should be looking forward and the bend of the knees should create a 90-degree angle between the hamstrings and the calves. This will be your starting position.

As you exhale, lift up your right leg until the hamstrings are in line with the back while maintaining the 90-degree angle bend. Contract the glutes throughout this movement and hold the contraction at the top for a second. Tip: At the end of the movement the upper leg should be parallel to the floor while the calf should be perpendicular to it.
Go back to the initial position as you inhale and now repeat with the left leg.
Continue to alternate legs until all of the recommended repetitions have been performed.
exercises.name: Glute Kickback
exercises.overview: A bodyweight exercise that targets the glutes through hip extension in a quadruped position. Perfect for activating and strengthening the posterior chain.
exercises.safetyInfo: Avoid if you have knee or wrist pain. Keep movements controlled to prevent lower back strain.

commonMistakes: Holding the weight too far from the body
Allowing knees to cave inward
Not descending deep enough
exerciseTips: Hold the weight close to your chest
Push your knees out with your elbows at the bottom
Keep your chest up and core tight
Descend until your hamstrings touch your calves
instructions: Stand holding a light kettlebell by the horns close to your chest. This will be your starting position.
Squat down between your legs until your hamstrings are on your calves. Keep your chest and head up and your back straight.
At the bottom position, pause and use your elbows to push your knees out. Return to the starting position, and repeat for 10-20 repetitions.
exercises.name: Goblet Squat
exercises.overview: A front-loaded squat variation using a kettlebell or dumbbell that promotes proper squat mechanics. Excellent for teaching deep squat patterns and building lower body strength.
exercises.safetyInfo: Start with light weight to master the movement pattern. Avoid if you have knee or ankle mobility restrictions.

commonMistakes: Using momentum to complete the movement
Not fully engaging the core during the crunch
Going too fast and losing control
exerciseTips: Coordinate the pulling and crunching movements
Squeeze your abs at the top position
Control both the ascent and descent
Keep your core engaged throughout
instructions: Hang from a chin-up bar using an underhand grip (palms facing you) that is slightly wider than shoulder width.
Now bend your knees at a 90 degree angle so that the calves are parallel to the floor while the thighs remain perpendicular to it. This will be your starting position.

As you exhale, pull yourself up while crunching your knees up at the same time until your knees are at chest level. You will stop going up as soon as your nose is at the same level as the bar. Tip: When you get to this point you should also be finishing the crunch at the same time.
Slowly start to inhale as you return to the starting position.
Repeat for the recommended amount of repetitions.
exercises.name: Gorilla Chin/Crunch
exercises.overview: An advanced compound movement combining a chin-up with a hanging knee raise. Simultaneously targets the upper body pulling muscles and core.
exercises.safetyInfo: Requires adequate grip strength and core stability. Not suitable for beginners. Ensure you can perform regular chin-ups and hanging knee raises first.

commonMistakes: Allowing knees to cave inward during the movement
Pushing through toes instead of heels
Going too deep and losing back contact with pad
exerciseTips: Keep your back pressed against the pad throughout
Drive through your heels on the ascent
Control the descent for 2-3 seconds
Stop when thighs are parallel to the platform
instructions: Place the back of your torso against the back pad of the machine and hook your shoulders under the shoulder pads provided.
Position your legs in the platform using a shoulder width medium stance with the toes slightly pointed out. Tip: Keep your head up at all times and also maintain the back on the pad at all times.
Place your arms on the side handles of the machine and disengage the safety bars (which on most designs is done by moving the side handles from a facing front position to a diagonal position).

Now straighten your legs without locking the knees. This will be your starting position. (Note: For the purposes of this discussion we will use the medium stance described above which targets overall development; however you can choose any of the three stances described in the foot positioning section).

Begin to slowly lower the unit by bending the knees as you maintain a straight posture with the head up (back on the pad at all times). Continue down until the angle between the upper leg and the calves becomes slightly less than 90-degrees (which is the point in which the upper legs are below parallel to the floor). Inhale as you perform this portion of the movement. Tip: If you performed the exercise correctly, the front of the knees should make an imaginary straight line with the toes that is perpendicular to the front. If your knees are past that imaginary line (if they are past your toes) then you are placing undue stress on the knee and the exercise has been performed incorrectly.
Begin to raise the unit as you exhale by pushing the floor with mainly with the heel of your foot as you straighten the legs again and go back to the starting position.
Repeat for the recommended amount of repetitions.
exercises.name: Hack Squat
exercises.overview: The hack squat is a machine-based compound movement that targets the quadriceps while providing back support. It allows for heavy loading with reduced spinal stress compared to free weight squats.
exercises.safetyInfo: Ensure proper foot placement to avoid knee stress. Those with lower back issues should use caution and maintain pad contact throughout the movement.

commonMistakes: Swinging the weights using momentum
Allowing elbows to drift forward during the curl
Rotating wrists during the movement
exerciseTips: Keep elbows pinned to your sides
Maintain neutral wrist position throughout
Squeeze at the top for maximum contraction
Control the eccentric lowering phase
instructions: Stand up with your torso upright and a dumbbell on each hand being held at arms length. The elbows should be close to the torso.
The palms of the hands should be facing your torso. This will be your starting position.

Now, while holding your upper arm stationary, exhale and curl the weight forward while contracting the biceps. Continue to raise the weight until the biceps are fully contracted and the dumbbell is at shoulder level. Hold the contracted position for a brief moment as you squeeze the biceps. Tip: Focus on keeping the elbow stationary and only moving your forearm.
After the brief pause, inhale and slowly begin the lower the dumbbells back down to the starting position.
Repeat for the recommended amount of repetitions.
exercises.name: Hammer Curls
exercises.overview: Hammer curls target the biceps and brachialis using a neutral grip position. This variation reduces wrist stress while emphasizing the outer bicep and forearm development.
exercises.safetyInfo: Start with lighter weights to master the neutral grip position. Avoid excessive weight that forces compensation through the back or shoulders.

commonMistakes: Setting the incline too steep (over 45 degrees)
Allowing dumbbells to drift too wide during descent
Pressing straight up instead of slightly inward
exerciseTips: Set bench to 30-45 degree incline
Keep neutral grip throughout the movement
Lower weights to chest level with control
Press up and slightly together at the top
instructions: Lie back on an incline bench with a dumbbell on each hand on top of your thighs. The palms of your hand will be facing each other.
By using your thighs to help you get the dumbbells up, clean the dumbbells one arm at a time so that you can hold them at shoulder width.

Once at shoulder width, keep the palms of your hands with a neutral grip (palms facing each other). Keep your elbows flared out with the upper arms in line with the shoulders (perpendicular to the torso) and the elbows bent creating a 90-degree angle between the upper arm and the forearm. This will be your starting position.
Now bring down the weights slowly to your side as you breathe in. Keep full control of the dumbbells at all times.

As you breathe out, push the dumbbells up using your pectoral muscles. Lock your arms in the contracted position, hold for a second and then start coming down slowly. Tip: It should take at least twice as long to go down than to come up.
Repeat the movement for the prescribed amount of repetitions.
When you are done, place the dumbbells back in your thighs and then on the floor. This is the safest manner to dispose of the dumbbells.
exercises.name: Hammer Grip Incline DB Bench Press
exercises.name_pt: Supino Inclinado com Halteres - Pegada Neutra
exercises.overview: The hammer grip incline dumbbell bench press targets the upper chest using a neutral grip. This variation reduces wrist and shoulder stress while effectively building the clavicular portion of the pectoralis major.
exercises.safetyInfo: Use a spotter when working with heavy weights. The neutral grip reduces shoulder stress but still requires proper warm-up of the shoulder joint.

commonMistakes: Arching the back excessively
Lowering too fast and risking head injury
Not achieving full lockout at the top
exerciseTips: Keep your core tight and body in a straight line
Control the descent slowly to avoid head injury
Drive through your palms to press back up
Look at your hands, not down at the floor
instructions: With your back to the wall bend at the waist and place both hands on the floor at shoulder width.

Kick yourself up against the wall with your arms straight. Your body should be upside down with the arms and legs fully extended. Keep your whole body as straight as possible. Tip: If doing this for the first time, have a spotter help you. Also, make sure that you keep facing the wall with your head, rather than looking down.
Slowly lower yourself to the ground as you inhale until your head almost touches the floor. Tip: It is of utmost importance that you come down slow in order to avoid head injury.
Push yourself back up slowly as you exhale until your elbows are nearly locked.
Repeat for the recommended amount of repetitions.
exercises.name: Handstand Push-Ups
exercises.overview: Handstand push-ups are an advanced bodyweight exercise that develops exceptional shoulder and tricep strength while challenging core stability in an inverted position.
exercises.safetyInfo: Start with pike push-ups or assisted variations before attempting full handstand push-ups. Use proper head positioning and control descent speed to prevent neck injury.

commonMistakes: Pulling the bar away from the body instead of keeping it close
Failing to fully extend the hips during the explosive second pull
exerciseTips: Generate explosive power from your hips during the second pull
Keep the bar close to your body throughout the entire movement
Rotate your elbows around and under the bar quickly during the catch
Land in the front rack position with elbows high and forward
instructions: Start with the barbell at mid-thigh level, gripping it with hands slightly wider than shoulder-width.
Maintain a slight knee bend with shoulders over the bar and back in neutral position.
Explosively extend your hips and knees while simultaneously shrugging your shoulders upward.
As the bar reaches maximum height, quickly pull yourself under it and rotate your elbows forward.
Catch the bar on your front shoulders in a quarter squat position with elbows up.
Stand up fully to complete the clean, then lower the bar back to the hang position.
exercises.name: Hang Power Clean
exercises.overview: The hang power clean is an Olympic lifting derivative that starts from the hang position rather than the floor. It develops explosive hip power, coordination, and full-body strength while teaching the important skill of rapid force production.
exercises.safetyInfo: This is a highly technical Olympic lifting movement requiring extensive coaching and practice. Not appropriate for beginners or those with wrist, shoulder, or back injuries. Requires excellent mobility and coordination.

commonMistakes: Using momentum and swinging body
Lifting legs too high past 90 degrees
Gripping bar too tightly causing forearm fatigue
exerciseTips: Keep shoulders pulled down and back
Control the negative portion slowly
Avoid swinging by engaging core throughout
Focus on lifting with abs rather than hip flexors
instructions: Hang from a chin-up bar with both arms extended at arms length in top of you using either a wide grip or a medium grip. The legs should be straight down with the pelvis rolled slightly backwards. This will be your starting position.
Raise your legs until the torso makes a 90-degree angle with the legs. Exhale as you perform this movement and hold the contraction for a second or so.
Go back slowly to the starting position as you breathe in.
Repeat for the recommended amount of repetitions.
exercises.name: Hanging Leg Raise
exercises.name_pt: Elevação de Pernas na Barra Fixa
exercises.overview: A challenging bodyweight exercise that targets the core while hanging from a pull-up bar. Excellent for building functional core strength and grip endurance.
exercises.safetyInfo: Avoid if you have shoulder impingement or grip strength issues. Those with lower back problems should start with bent knee variations.

commonMistakes: Rushing the movement and using momentum
Failing to straighten legs at the top position
Not controlling the lowering phase
exerciseTips: Start with knees bent at 90 degrees
Straighten legs as you reach the top
Control descent to avoid swinging
Engage lats to stabilize shoulders
instructions: Hang from a chin-up bar with your legs and feet together using an overhand grip (palms facing away from you) that is slightly wider than shoulder width. Tip: You may use wrist wraps in order to facilitate holding on to the bar.

Now bend your knees at a 90 degree angle and bring the upper legs forward so that the calves are perpendicular to the floor while the thighs remain parallel to it. This will be your starting position.
Pull your legs up as you exhale until you almost touch your shins with the bar above you. Tip: Try to straighten your legs as much as possible while at the top.
Lower your legs as slowly as possible until you reach the starting position. Tip: Avoid swinging and using momentum at all times.
Repeat for the recommended amount of repetitions.
exercises.name: Hanging Pike
exercises.overview: An advanced hanging core exercise that combines knee tucks with leg straightening. Builds exceptional abdominal strength and control.
exercises.safetyInfo: More advanced than basic hanging leg raises. Requires significant grip strength and shoulder stability. Progress gradually from bent knee variations.

commonMistakes: Relying too heavily on arm support instead of leg strength
Using the support to bounce out of the bottom position
Neglecting proper foot positioning and knee tracking
exerciseTips: Use your hands for balance, not to reduce the load significantly
Focus on maintaining proper squat mechanics despite the support
Keep most of your weight on your legs, not your arms
Control the descent speed to maximize muscle activation
instructions: Set up a safety squat bar on your shoulders and position yourself between squat rack uprights or safety bars.
Grip the uprights or safety bars at shoulder height with both hands for support.
Position your feet shoulder-width apart with toes slightly turned out.
Descend into a squat while using your hands for light support and balance.
Squat to full depth, then drive through your heels to return to the starting position.
exercises.name: Hatfield Squat
exercises.overview: The Hatfield squat allows for heavier loading and deeper ranges of motion by providing upper body support. This enables greater quadriceps development while reducing stability demands and spinal loading.
exercises.safetyInfo: This exercise is generally safer than free squats due to the support provided. However, ensure the support structure is stable and can handle the forces involved.

commonMistakes: Allowing the dumbbell to drift away from the chest
Leaning forward excessively during the descent
Rising up on toes instead of driving through heels
exerciseTips: Keep the dumbbell close to your chest throughout the movement
Maintain an upright torso position to emphasize quad activation
Focus on keeping your knees tracking over your toes
Control the descent and pause briefly at the bottom
instructions: Place weight plates or a small platform under your heels and hold a dumbbell vertically against your chest with both hands.
Stand with feet shoulder-width apart, toes slightly turned out, keeping your torso upright.
Initiate the squat by pushing your hips back and bending your knees, keeping your chest up.
Descend until your thighs are parallel to the floor or as deep as mobility allows.
Drive through your heels to return to the starting position, squeezing your glutes at the top.
exercises.name: Heels Elevated Goblet Squat
exercises.overview: The heels elevated goblet squat uses heel elevation to increase quad activation while the goblet position promotes proper squat mechanics. This variation is excellent for building quad strength and improving squat depth.
exercises.safetyInfo: Avoid this exercise if you have ankle mobility restrictions or acute knee pain. The heel elevation increases ankle dorsiflexion demands and may cause discomfort for those with tight calves.

commonMistakes: Rolling the shoulders in a circular motion instead of straight up and down
Bending the arms and turning it into an upright row
Using momentum to swing the weight up
exerciseTips: Keep your arms straight throughout the movement
Focus on moving only at the shoulder joint
Squeeze your traps hard at the top of each rep
Avoid rolling your shoulders forward or backward
instructions: Stand inside a hex bar with feet hip-width apart.
Bend at the hips and knees to grasp the handles with a neutral grip.
Stand up straight with arms fully extended and shoulders in a neutral position.
Elevate your shoulders by shrugging them up toward your ears as high as possible.
Hold the contraction briefly at the top.
Lower your shoulders back to the starting position under control.
exercises.name: Hex Bar Shrug
exercises.overview: The hex bar shrug is an isolation exercise that targets the upper trapezius muscles using a neutral grip position. The hex bar allows for a more natural hand position and can accommodate heavier loads than dumbbell shrugs.
exercises.safetyInfo: This exercise is generally safe for most individuals. The neutral grip position may be more comfortable for those with wrist or elbow issues compared to traditional barbell shrugs.

commonMistakes: Allowing upper arms to drop during the curl
Using too much weight and compromising form
Not achieving full range of motion
exerciseTips: Keep upper arms parallel to floor throughout movement
Squeeze biceps hard at peak contraction
Control the weight on the negative portion
Maintain steady stance between cables
instructions: Stand between a couple of high pulleys and grab a handle in each arm. Position your upper arms in a way that they are parallel to the floor with the palms of your hands facing you. This will be your starting position.

Curl the handles towards you until they are next to your ears. Make sure that as you do so you flex your biceps and exhale. The upper arms should remain stationary and only the forearms should move. Hold for a second in the contracted position as you squeeze the biceps.
Slowly bring back the arms to the starting position.
Repeat for the recommended amount of repetitions.
exercises.name: High Cable Curls
exercises.overview: An isolation exercise targeting the biceps from a unique angle with arms elevated. Provides constant tension throughout the range of motion.
exercises.safetyInfo: Start with lighter weight to establish proper form. Avoid if you have elbow or shoulder issues. Ensure cables are at equal height.

commonMistakes: Pulling the bar away from the body instead of keeping it close
Using only arm strength instead of generating power from the hips
exerciseTips: Generate power from your legs and hips, not just your arms
Keep the barbell close to your body throughout the pulling motion
Lead with your elbows and keep them higher than your wrists
Use explosive hip extension to initiate the pull
instructions: Stand with feet hip-width apart, gripping the barbell with an overhand grip slightly wider than shoulders.
Begin with the barbell at mid-thigh level, maintaining a slight bend in knees and neutral spine.
Explosively extend your hips and knees while simultaneously shrugging your shoulders upward.
Pull the barbell vertically along your body, leading with your elbows high and wide.
Continue pulling until the bar reaches chest level, keeping it close to your body throughout.
Lower the barbell back to the starting position with control and repeat.
exercises.name: High Pull
exercises.overview: The high pull is an Olympic lifting derivative that develops explosive power and upper body pulling strength. It teaches the important skill of generating power from the hips while building strength in the traps, shoulders, and posterior chain.
exercises.safetyInfo: This exercise requires good shoulder mobility and stability. Those with shoulder impingement, rotator cuff injuries, or lower back problems should avoid or modify this movement. Start with lighter weights to master the technique.

commonMistakes: Breaking the flow into separate push-up and movement components
Not achieving full range of motion in either position
Moving too quickly without proper control
exerciseTips: Focus on creating one fluid motion rather than separate movements
Keep your hands planted throughout the entire exercise
Engage your core to control the flowing movement
Start slowly to master the movement pattern
instructions: Start in downward dog position with hands shoulder-width apart and hips raised high.
Dive forward and down, lowering your chest toward the ground between your hands.
Continue the fluid motion by pressing up and forward into upward dog position.
Reverse the movement by pressing back and up to return to downward dog.
Flow continuously between positions in a smooth, controlled manner.
exercises.name: Hindu Push Up
exercises.overview: Hindu push-ups are a flowing, dynamic movement that combines strength and mobility. This traditional exercise moves through multiple positions, building functional strength while improving flexibility and coordination.
exercises.safetyInfo: Those with wrist, shoulder, or lower back issues should use caution. The flowing nature can mask poor form, so master the movement slowly first.

commonMistakes: Allowing the knee to bend during extension
Leaning forward excessively to compensate
Moving too quickly without controlling the eccentric phase
exerciseTips: Keep your core engaged throughout the movement
Focus on squeezing your glutes at the end range
Maintain a straight knee during the entire motion
Control the return phase against band resistance
instructions: Secure one end of the band to the lower portion of a post and attach the other to one ankle.
Facing the attachment point of the band, hold on to the column to stabilize yourself.
Keeping your head and your chest up, move the resisted leg back as far as you can while keeping the knee straight.
Return the leg to the starting position.
exercises.name: Hip Extension with Bands
exercises.name_pt: Extensão de Quadril com Faixa Elástica
exercises.overview: A targeted glute strengthening exercise using resistance bands that improves hip extension strength and posterior chain activation. This exercise is excellent for correcting hip flexor tightness and enhancing athletic performance.
exercises.safetyInfo: Ensure the band is securely anchored to prevent snapping back. Avoid excessive range of motion that causes lower back arching.

commonMistakes: Leaning backward during knee raise
Not achieving full 90-degree hip flexion
Using momentum instead of controlled muscle contraction
exerciseTips: Drive your knee up to 90 degrees actively
Keep your standing leg straight and stable
Maintain upright posture throughout the movement
Control the lowering phase against resistance
instructions: Secure one end of the band to the lower portion of a post and attach the other to one ankle.
Face away from the attachment point of the band.
Keeping your head and your chest up, raise your knee up to 90 degrees and pause.
Return the leg to the starting position.
exercises.name: Hip Flexion with Band
exercises.name_pt: Flexão de Quadril com Faixa Elástica
exercises.overview: A hip flexor strengthening exercise that targets the muscles responsible for lifting the knee toward the chest. This movement is essential for running mechanics and functional mobility.
exercises.safetyInfo: Start with lighter resistance to avoid hip flexor strain. Stop if you experience any sharp pain in the hip joint or groin area.

commonMistakes: Hyperextending beyond neutral spine
Using momentum to swing up
Placing hands behind head with neck tension
exerciseTips: Maintain neutral spine throughout movement
Control the descent to maximize stretch
Squeeze glutes at top position
Keep head aligned with spine
instructions: Lie face down on a hyperextension bench, tucking your ankles securely under the footpads.
Adjust the upper pad if possible so your upper thighs lie flat across the wide pad, leaving enough room for you to bend at the waist without any restriction.

With your body straight, cross your arms in front of you (my preference) or behind your head. This will be your starting position. Tip: You can also hold a weight plate for extra resistance in front of you under your crossed arms.

Start bending forward slowly at the waist as far as you can while keeping your back flat. Inhale as you perform this movement. Keep moving forward until you feel a nice stretch on the hamstrings and you can no longer keep going without a rounding of the back. Tip: Never round the back as you perform this exercise. Also, some people can go farther than others. The key thing is that you go as far as your body allows you to without rounding the back.

Slowly raise your torso back to the initial position as you inhale. Tip: Avoid the temptation to arch your back past a straight line. Also, do not swing the torso at any time in order to protect the back from injury.
Repeat for the recommended amount of repetitions.
exercises.name: Hyperextensions (Back Extensions)
exercises.overview: Hyperextensions strengthen the erector spinae and posterior chain muscles while improving spinal mobility. This exercise is excellent for correcting forward posture and building lower back endurance.
exercises.safetyInfo: Avoid if you have acute lower back injury or disc problems. Never hyperextend past neutral spine as this can compress vertebrae and strain ligaments.

commonMistakes: Bouncing at bottom position
Partner not providing stable anchor
Moving too fast due to reduced range
exerciseTips: Ensure partner securely holds your legs
Move slowly through reduced range of motion
Focus on controlled eccentric phase
Stop before touching the floor
instructions: With someone holding down your legs, slide yourself down to the edge a flat bench until your hips hang off the end of the bench. Tip: Your entire upper body should be hanging down towards the floor. Also, you will be in the same position as if you were on a hyperextension bench but the range of motion will be shorter due to the height of the flat bench vs. that of the hyperextension bench.

With your body straight, cross your arms in front of you (my preference) or behind your head. This will be your starting position. Tip: You can also hold a weight plate for extra resistance in front of you under your crossed arms.

Start bending forward slowly at the waist as far as you can while keeping your back flat. Inhale as you perform this movement. Keep moving forward until you almost touch the floor or you feel a nice stretch on the hamstrings (whichever comes first). Tip: Never round the back as you perform this exercise.

Slowly raise your torso back to the initial position as you exhale. Tip: Avoid the temptation to arch your back past a straight line. Also, do not swing the torso at any time in order to protect the back from injury.
Repeat for the recommended amount of repetitions.
exercises.name: Hyperextensions With No Hyperextension Bench
exercises.overview: This variation allows you to perform spinal extension exercises without specialized equipment. The flat bench setup provides a modified range of motion while still targeting the erector spinae effectively.
exercises.safetyInfo: Requires a reliable partner to hold legs securely. The reduced range of motion makes this safer for beginners but still avoid rounding the spine.

commonMistakes: Lifting the chest off the bench and using momentum
Pulling the bar too high toward the neck instead of lower chest
Using primarily arm muscles instead of engaging the back
exerciseTips: Keep your chest pressed firmly against the bench throughout
Focus on pulling with your back muscles rather than your arms
Squeeze your shoulder blades together at the top
Maintain a neutral wrist position throughout the movement
instructions: Set an incline bench to approximately 45 degrees and lie face down with your chest supported.
Grasp a barbell with an overhand grip slightly wider than shoulder width.
Let the barbell hang straight down with arms fully extended.
Pull the barbell up toward your lower chest by retracting your shoulder blades.
Squeeze your back muscles at the top of the movement.
Lower the barbell back to the starting position with control.
exercises.name: Incline Barbell Row
exercises.name_pt: Remada Inclinada com Barra no Banco
exercises.overview: The incline barbell row is a chest-supported rowing exercise that targets the latissimus dorsi and middle back muscles. The incline position eliminates lower back involvement while allowing for focused back development.
exercises.safetyInfo: The chest-supported position reduces lower back stress, making this safer than bent-over rows for those with back issues. Ensure the bench is stable and properly angled before beginning.

commonMistakes: Allowing elbows to flare out excessively
Using shoulders to assist the movement
Lowering bar too far causing shoulder strain
exerciseTips: Keep upper arms stationary throughout movement
Lower bar behind head in controlled arc
Maintain elbow position close to head
Focus on triceps contraction at top
instructions: Hold a barbell with an overhand grip (palms down) that is a little closer together than shoulder width.
Lie back on an incline bench set at any angle between 45-75-degrees.
Bring the bar overhead with your arms extended and elbows in. The arms should be in line with the torso above the head. This will be your starting position.

Now lower the bar in a semicircular motion behind your head until your forearms touch your biceps. Inhale as you perform this movement. Tip: Keep your upper arms stationary and close to your head at all times. Only the forearms should move.
Return to the starting position as you breathe out and you contract the triceps. Hold the contraction for a second.
Repeat for the recommended amount of repetitions.
exercises.name: Incline Barbell Triceps Extension
exercises.name_pt: Extensão de Tríceps Inclinado com Barra
exercises.overview: This incline variation of triceps extensions provides a greater stretch on the triceps long head while reducing stress on the elbows compared to flat bench versions.
exercises.safetyInfo: Use a spotter when possible as the bar is positioned over your head. Start with lighter weight to assess shoulder mobility and comfort in this position.

commonMistakes: Using momentum to lift the weight
Not achieving full range of motion
Lifting elbows too high above back level
exerciseTips: Squeeze shoulder blades together at top
Keep chest pressed against bench
Control the weight on descent
Focus on rear delt and rhomboid activation
instructions: Grab a dumbbell in each hand and lie face down on an incline bench that is set to an incline that is approximately 30 degrees.
Let the arms hang to your sides fully extended as they point to the floor.
Turn the wrists until your hands have a pronated (palms down) grip.
Now flare the elbows out. This will be your starting position.

As you breathe out, start to pull the dumbbells up as if you are doing a reverse bench press. You will do this by bending at the elbows and bringing the upper arms up as you let the forearms hang. Continue this motion until the upper arms are at the same level as your back. Tip: The elbows will come out to the side and your upper arms and torso should make the letter "T" at the top of the movement. Hold the contraction at the top for a second.
Slowly go back down to the starting position as you breathe in.
Repeat for the recommended amount of repetitions.
exercises.name: Incline Bench Pull
exercises.overview: This chest-supported pulling exercise targets the often-neglected posterior deltoids and rhomboids. It's excellent for improving posture and balancing pressing movements.
exercises.safetyInfo: The chest-supported position makes this exercise very safe for the lower back. Ensure the bench angle allows comfortable arm movement without shoulder impingement.
variations: Bent Over Two-Dumbbell Row With Palms In

commonMistakes: Allowing shoulders to roll forward during the press
Using excessive weight that compromises form
Not controlling the eccentric phase
exerciseTips: Keep shoulder blades retracted throughout the entire movement
Press handles together in a slight arc rather than straight forward
Control the negative portion for 2-3 seconds
Maintain tension on cables at all times
instructions: Adjust the weight to an appropriate amount and be seated, grasping the handles. Your upper arms should be about 45 degrees to the body, with your head and chest up. The elbows should be bent to about 90 degrees. This will be your starting position.
Begin by extending through the elbow, pressing the handles together straight in front of you. Keep your shoulder blades retracted as you execute the movement.
After pausing at full extension, return to the starting position, keeping tension on the cables.
exercises.name: Incline Cable Chest Press
exercises.overview: The incline cable chest press targets the upper chest while providing constant tension throughout the range of motion. It's excellent for developing the clavicular head of the pectoralis major.
exercises.safetyInfo: Avoid if you have shoulder impingement or recent chest/shoulder injuries. Start with lighter weight to establish proper movement pattern.

commonMistakes: Bending elbows too much turning it into a press
Using momentum to bring weights together
Not maintaining proper shoulder position
exerciseTips: Maintain a slight bend in elbows throughout the movement
Focus on squeezing chest muscles at the top
Keep the arc of motion consistent
Control the stretch at the bottom position
instructions: To get yourself into the starting position, set the pulleys at the floor level (lowest level possible on the machine that is below your torso).
Place an incline bench (set at 45 degrees) in between the pulleys, select a weight on each one and grab a pulley on each hand.
With a handle on each hand, lie on the incline bench and bring your hands together at arms length in front of your face. This will be your starting position.

With a slight bend of your elbows (in order to prevent stress at the biceps tendon), lower your arms out at both sides in a wide arc until you feel a stretch on your chest. Breathe in as you perform this portion of the movement. Tip: Keep in mind that throughout the movement, the arms should remain stationary. The movement should only occur at the shoulder joint.

Return your arms back to the starting position as you squeeze your chest muscles and exhale. Hold the contracted position for a second. Tip: Make sure to use the same arc of motion used to lower the weights.
Repeat the movement for the prescribed amount of repetitions.
exercises.name: Incline Cable Flye
exercises.overview: The incline cable flye isolates the upper chest with constant tension, providing an excellent stretch and contraction for the clavicular pectoralis major.
exercises.safetyInfo: Stop if you feel shoulder pain or excessive stretch. This exercise puts significant stress on the shoulder joint in the stretched position.

commonMistakes: Rotating wrists during the movement
Lowering dumbbells too far past chest level
Using legs to assist the press
exerciseTips: Keep palms facing each other throughout the movement
Lower dumbbells under control to chest level
Press in a slight arc toward your face
Squeeze chest muscles at the top
instructions: Lie back on an incline bench with a dumbbell on each hand on top of your thighs. The palms of your hand will be facing each other.
By using your thighs to help you get the dumbbells up, clean the dumbbells one arm at a time so that you can hold them at shoulder width.

Once at shoulder width, keep the palms of your hands with a neutral grip (palms facing each other). Keep your elbows flared out with the upper arms in line with the shoulders (perpendicular to the torso) and the elbows bent creating a 90-degree angle between the upper arm and the forearm. This will be your starting position.
Now bring down the weights slowly to your side as you breathe in. Keep full control of the dumbbells at all times.

As you breathe out, push the dumbbells up using your pectoral muscles. Lock your arms in the contracted position, hold for a second and then start coming down slowly. Tip: It should take at least twice as long to go down than to come up.
Repeat the movement for the prescribed amount of repetitions.
When you are done, place the dumbbells back in your thighs and then on the floor. This is the safest manner to dispose of the dumbbells.
exercises.name: Incline Dumbbell Bench With Palms Facing In
exercises.name_pt: Supino Inclinado com Halteres - Palmas Voltadas para Dentro
exercises.overview: This variation of the incline press uses a neutral grip to target the upper chest while reducing stress on the shoulders and wrists.
exercises.safetyInfo: Use a spotter when working with heavy weights. The neutral grip reduces shoulder stress compared to pronated grip.

commonMistakes: Moving the upper arms during the curl
Using too much weight and swinging
Not fully extending arms at the bottom
exerciseTips: Keep upper arms stationary against the bench
Control the negative portion slowly
Squeeze biceps at the top of the movement
Don't swing or use momentum
instructions: Sit back on an incline bench with a dumbbell in each hand held at arms length. Keep your elbows close to your torso and rotate the palms of your hands until they are facing forward. This will be your starting position.

While holding the upper arm stationary, curl the weights forward while contracting the biceps as you breathe out. Only the forearms should move. Continue the movement until your biceps are fully contracted and the dumbbells are at shoulder level. Hold the contracted position for a second.
Slowly begin to bring the dumbbells back to starting position as your breathe in.
Repeat for the recommended amount of repetitions.
exercises.name: Incline Dumbbell Curl
exercises.overview: The incline dumbbell curl targets the biceps from a stretched position, emphasizing the long head and providing a greater range of motion than standing curls.
exercises.safetyInfo: The inclined position puts the biceps in a stretched position, increasing injury risk if proper form isn't maintained. Start with lighter weights.
variations: Seated One-Arm Dumbbell Palms-Down Wrist Curl

commonMistakes: Using too much weight causing elbow flexion
Lowering weights too far causing shoulder strain
Not maintaining consistent elbow angle
exerciseTips: Maintain slight bend in elbows throughout movement
Lower weights in wide arc until chest stretch
Squeeze chest muscles together at the top
Keep shoulder blades pulled back
instructions: Hold a dumbbell on each hand and lie on an incline bench that is set to an incline angle of no more than 30 degrees.
Extend your arms above you with a slight bend at the elbows.
Now rotate the wrists so that the palms of your hands are facing you. Tip: The pinky fingers should be next to each other. This will be your starting position.

As you breathe in, start to slowly lower the arms to the side while keeping the arms extended and while rotating the wrists until the palms of the hand are facing each other. Tip: At the end of the movement the arms will be by your side with the palms facing the ceiling.

As you exhale start to bring the dumbbells back up to the starting position by reversing the motion and rotating the hands so that the pinky fingers are next to each other again. Tip: Keep in mind that the movement will only happen at the shoulder joint and at the wrist. There is no motion that happens at the elbow joint.
Repeat for the recommended amount of repetitions.
exercises.name: Incline Dumbbell Flyes
exercises.name_pt: Crucifixo Inclinado com Halteres no Banco
exercises.overview: The incline dumbbell flye isolates the upper chest muscles, providing an excellent stretch and contraction for developing the clavicular pectoralis major.
exercises.safetyInfo: This exercise places significant stress on the shoulder joint in the bottom position. Use moderate weight and avoid excessive stretch.
variations: Incline Dumbbell Flyes - With A Twist

commonMistakes: Rotating wrists too quickly losing control
Changing elbow angle during the movement
Using excessive weight that prevents proper rotation
exerciseTips: Rotate wrists smoothly throughout the movement
Keep elbows slightly bent and fixed
Control both the lowering and rotation phases
Focus on chest contraction during the twist
instructions: Hold a dumbbell in each hand and lie on an incline bench that is set to an incline angle of no more than 30 degrees.
Extend your arms above you with a slight bend at the elbows.
Now rotate the wrists so that the palms of your hands are facing you. Tip: The pinky fingers should be next to each other. This will be your starting position.

As you breathe in, start to slowly lower the arms to the side while keeping the arms extended and while rotating the wrists until the palms of the hand are facing each other. Tip: At the end of the movement the arms will be by your side with the palms facing the ceiling.

As you exhale start to bring the dumbbells back up to the starting position by reversing the motion and rotating the hands so that the pinky fingers are next to each other again. Tip: Keep in mind that the movement will only happen at the shoulder joint and at the wrist. There is no motion that happens at the elbow joint.
Repeat for the recommended amount of repetitions.
exercises.name: Incline Dumbbell Flyes - With A Twist
exercises.name_pt: Crucifixo Inclinado com Halteres e Rotação
exercises.overview: This advanced variation of the incline flye adds wrist rotation to increase muscle activation and provide a unique stimulus to the upper chest.
exercises.safetyInfo: The wrist rotation adds complexity and potential for injury. Start with very light weights to master the coordination before progressing.

commonMistakes: Pressing dumbbells straight up instead of slightly inward
Using momentum to bounce weights off the chest
Allowing elbows to flare too wide past 45 degrees
exerciseTips: Keep your shoulder blades pulled back and down throughout the movement
Control the negative portion for 2-3 seconds
Press the dumbbells slightly inward at the top to maximize chest activation
Maintain a slight arch in your lower back
instructions: Lie back on an incline bench with a dumbbell in each hand atop your thighs. The palms of your hands will be facing each other.
Then, using your thighs to help push the dumbbells up, lift the dumbbells one at a time so that you can hold them at shoulder width.
Once you have the dumbbells raised to shoulder width, rotate your wrists forward so that the palms of your hands are facing away from you. This will be your starting position.
Be sure to keep full control of the dumbbells at all times. Then breathe out and push the dumbbells up with your chest.
Lock your arms at the top, hold for a second, and then start slowly lowering the weight. Tip Ideally, lowering the weights should take about twice as long as raising them.
Repeat the movement for the prescribed amount of repetitions.
When you are done, place the dumbbells back on your thighs and then on the floor. This is the safest manner to release the dumbbells.
exercises.name: Incline Dumbbell Press
exercises.overview: The incline dumbbell press targets the upper portion of the chest while allowing for a greater range of motion than barbell variations. It's excellent for building upper body strength and muscle mass.
exercises.safetyInfo: Avoid this exercise if you have shoulder impingement. Use a spotter for heavy weights and ensure proper bench angle (30-45 degrees) to prevent shoulder strain.

commonMistakes: Allowing upper arms to move forward during the curl
Using wrists to help lift the weight instead of keeping them neutral
Rushing through the negative portion of the movement
exerciseTips: Keep your upper arms pressed against the bench throughout the movement
Maintain a neutral wrist position with thumbs pointing up
Control the eccentric portion slowly
Avoid swinging or using momentum
instructions: Seat yourself on an incline bench with a dumbbell in each hand. You should pressed firmly against he back with your feet together. Allow the dumbbells to hang straight down at your side, holding them with a neutral grip. This will be your starting position.
Initiate the movement by flexing at the elbow, attempting to keep the upper arm stationary.
Continue to the top of the movement and pause, then slowly return to the start position.
exercises.name: Incline Hammer Curls
exercises.overview: Incline hammer curls target the biceps and brachialis from a stretched position, emphasizing the long head of the biceps. The neutral grip also engages the brachioradialis effectively.
exercises.safetyInfo: The incline position stretches the biceps significantly. Start with lighter weights than you'd use for standing curls to avoid strain.

commonMistakes: Allowing arms to drift inward during the curl
Using too much weight and compromising form
Not maintaining the stretched position at the bottom
exerciseTips: Keep your forearms in line with your side delts throughout the movement
Focus on squeezing the biceps at the top position
Control the weight on the way down
Maintain the wide arm position throughout the entire range of motion
instructions: Hold a dumbbell in each hand and lie back on an incline bench.
The dumbbells should be at arm's length hanging at your sides and your palms should be facing out. This will be your starting position.

Now as you exhale curl the weight outward and up while keeping your forearms in line with your side deltoids. Continue the curl until the dumbbells are at shoulder height and to the sides of your deltoids. Tip: The end of the movement should look similar to a double biceps pose.
After a second contraction at the top of the movement, start to inhale and slowly lower the weights back to the starting position using the same path used to bring them up.
Repeat for the recommended amount of repetitions.
exercises.name: Incline Inner Biceps Curl
exercises.overview: This unique bicep variation emphasizes the peak contraction of the biceps while targeting the muscle from an unusual angle. The wide arm position creates a distinctive training stimulus.
exercises.safetyInfo: This exercise places significant stress on the shoulder joint. Use lighter weights and ensure proper warm-up to avoid shoulder impingement.

commonMistakes: Allowing hips to sag or pike up
Placing hands too wide or too narrow
Not lowering chest fully to the elevated surface
exerciseTips: Keep your body in a straight line from head to heels
Place hands slightly wider than shoulder width
Lower your chest to touch the bench or platform
Engage your core throughout the movement
instructions: Stand facing bench or sturdy elevated platform. Place hands on edge of bench or platform, slightly wider than shoulder width.
Position forefoot back from bench or platform with arms and body straight. Arms should be perpendicular to body. Keeping body straight, lower chest to edge of box or platform by bending arms.
Push body up until arms are extended. Repeat.
exercises.name: Incline Push-Up
exercises.overview: The incline push-up is a beginner-friendly variation that reduces the amount of body weight you're pressing. It's perfect for building strength to progress to standard push-ups.
exercises.safetyInfo: Ensure the bench or platform is stable and secure. This variation is safer for beginners than standard push-ups as it reduces the load on the upper body.

commonMistakes: Allowing elbows to flare out wide
Placing hands too far apart
Not maintaining proper body alignment
exerciseTips: Keep hands close together, forming a diamond or triangle shape
Maintain elbows close to your sides throughout the movement
Keep your core engaged and body straight
Focus on feeling the movement in your triceps
instructions: Stand facing a Smith machine bar or sturdy elevated platform at an appropriate height.
Place your hands next to one another on the bar.
Position your feet back from the bar with arms and body straight. This will be your starting position.
Keeping your body straight, lower your chest to the bar by bending the arms.
Return to the starting position by extending the elbows, pressing yourself back up.
exercises.name: Incline Push-Up Close-Grip
exercises.overview: This variation emphasizes tricep development by using a narrow hand position. The incline reduces the load making it accessible while still providing an effective tricep workout.
exercises.safetyInfo: The close grip position may stress the wrists and elbows more than standard push-ups. Ensure proper wrist alignment and stop if you experience joint pain.

commonMistakes: Allowing hips to sag or pike up during the movement
Placing hands too wide or too narrow on the bar
Rushing through the movement without controlling the tempo
exerciseTips: Maintain a straight line from head to heels throughout the movement
Keep your core engaged to prevent sagging hips
Control the descent and focus on squeezing chest muscles at the top
Position hands directly under shoulders for optimal mechanics
instructions: Stand facing a Smith machine bar or sturdy elevated platform at an appropriate height.
Place your hands on the bar, with your hands about shoulder width apart.
Position your feet back from the bar with arms and body straight. This will be your starting position.
Keeping your body straight, lower your chest to the bar by bending the arms.
Return to the starting position by extending the elbows, pressing yourself back up.
exercises.name: Incline Push-Up Medium
exercises.overview: An incline push-up performed on a medium-height surface that reduces the load compared to floor push-ups. This variation is excellent for building upper body strength progressively and mastering proper push-up form.
exercises.safetyInfo: Ensure the elevated surface is stable and secure before beginning. Avoid if you have wrist pain or shoulder impingement.

commonMistakes: Placing too much stress on wrists by allowing them to bend backward
Using too low of an incline before mastering the movement pattern
Neglecting proper warm-up of wrists and forearms
exerciseTips: Start with a higher incline to accommodate the challenging grip position
Keep wrists straight and avoid excessive extension
Focus on slow, controlled movements due to increased wrist demands
Engage core muscles to maintain body alignment
instructions: Stand facing a Smith machine bar or sturdy elevated platform at an appropriate height.
Place your hands on the bar palms up, with your hands about shoulder width apart.
Position your feet back from the bar with arms and body straight. This will be your starting position.
Keeping your body straight, lower your chest to the bar by bending the arms.
Return to the starting position by extending the elbows, pressing yourself back up.
exercises.name: Incline Push-Up Reverse Grip
exercises.overview: A challenging push-up variation using a reverse grip that emphasizes different muscle recruitment patterns. This exercise requires good wrist flexibility and strength while still targeting the upper chest effectively.
exercises.safetyInfo: This variation places significant stress on the wrists and should be avoided by those with wrist injuries. Progress gradually and stop if you experience any wrist discomfort.

commonMistakes: Placing hands too wide, which can stress the shoulder joints
Allowing elbows to flare excessively outward
Partial range of motion by not lowering chest to the bar
exerciseTips: Position hands wider than shoulders but not excessively wide
Focus on squeezing chest muscles together at the top
Maintain proper body alignment from head to heels
Lower chest toward the bar rather than leading with your head
instructions: Stand facing a Smith machine bar or sturdy elevated platform at an appropriate height.
Place your hands on the bar, with your hands wider than shoulder width.
Position your feet back from the bar with arms and body straight. Your arms should be perpendicular to the body. This will be your starting position.
Keeping your body straight, lower your chest to the bar by bending the arms.
Return to the starting position by extending the elbows, pressing yourself back up.
exercises.name: Incline Push-Up Wide
exercises.overview: A wide-grip variation of the incline push-up that emphasizes chest muscle activation over triceps. This exercise provides an excellent introduction to wide-grip pressing movements while being more accessible than floor push-ups.
exercises.safetyInfo: Avoid placing hands excessively wide as this can increase shoulder injury risk. Those with shoulder impingement should use caution or avoid this variation.

commonMistakes: Allowing the elbow to drift away from the torso during movement
Using excessive resistance that compromises form
Moving through the motion too quickly without control
exerciseTips: Keep your elbow firmly pressed against your side throughout the movement
Move slowly and control both the pulling and return phases
Use a pad or towel under your elbow to maintain proper position
Start with light resistance and progress gradually
instructions: Choke the band around a post. The band should be at the same height as your elbow. Stand with your right side to the band a couple of feet away.
Grasp the end of the band with your right hand, and keep your elbow pressed firmly to your side. We recommend you hold a pad or foam roll in place with your elbow to keep it firmly in position.
With your upper arm in position, your elbow should be flexed to 90 degrees with your hand reaching away from your torso. This will be your starting position.
Execute the movement by rotating your arm in a forehand motion, keeping your elbow in place.
Continue as far as you are able, pause, and then return to the starting position.
exercises.name: Internal Rotation with Band
exercises.overview: A targeted exercise for strengthening the internal rotators of the shoulder using band resistance. This movement is essential for shoulder health, particularly for overhead athletes and those recovering from shoulder injuries.
exercises.safetyInfo: This exercise is commonly used in shoulder rehabilitation but should not cause pain. Consult a healthcare provider if you have existing shoulder injuries.

commonMistakes: Allowing hips to sag or pike up
Pulling with arms only without retracting shoulder blades
Using partial range of motion
exerciseTips: Keep your body in a straight line from head to heels
Pull your chest to the bar, not your stomach
Squeeze your shoulder blades together at the top
Control the lowering phase slowly
instructions: Position a bar in a rack to about waist height. You can also use a smith machine.

Take a wider than shoulder width grip on the bar and position yourself hanging underneath the bar. Your body should be straight with your heels on the ground with your arms fully extended. This will be your starting position.
Begin by flexing the elbow, pulling your chest towards the bar. Retract your shoulder blades as you perform the movement.
Pause at the top of the motion, and return yourself to the start position.
Repeat for the desired number of repetitions.
exercises.name: Inverted Row
exercises.overview: The inverted row is a horizontal pulling exercise that targets the middle back muscles while building functional upper body strength. It's an excellent progression toward pull-ups and helps balance pressing movements.
exercises.safetyInfo: Ensure the bar is securely positioned and rated for your body weight. Those with lower back issues should maintain proper core engagement throughout.
variations: Bent Over Two-Dumbbell Row With Palms In

commonMistakes: Allowing the straps to go slack at the bottom
Rowing to the stomach instead of chest level
Losing body alignment during the movement
exerciseTips: Maintain a straight body line throughout the movement
Pull your chest between your hands
Keep your core engaged to prevent sagging
Adjust difficulty by changing foot position
instructions: Hang a rope or suspension straps from a rack or other stable object. Grasp the ends and position yourself in a supine position hanging from the ropes. Your body should be straight with your heels on the ground with your arms fully extended. This will be your starting position.
Begin by flexing the elbow, pulling your chest to your hands. Retract your shoulder blades as you perform the movement.
Pause at the top of the motion, and return yourself to the start position.
Repeat for the desired number of repetitions.
exercises.name: Inverted Row with Straps
exercises.overview: The suspension strap inverted row provides variable resistance and requires additional stabilization compared to a fixed bar. The unstable nature of straps increases core activation and functional strength.
exercises.safetyInfo: Verify that suspension straps are properly anchored and rated for your body weight. Check straps for wear before each use.
variations: Bent Over Two-Dumbbell Row With Palms In

commonMistakes: Using too much weight initially
Allowing the torso to rotate or bend
Rushing through the movement phases
exerciseTips: Start with light weight to master the movement pattern
Keep your core braced throughout the entire exercise
Maintain controlled movement in all planes
Focus on shoulder stability before increasing load
instructions: Perform the exercise with proper form.
exercises.name: Iron Cross
exercises.overview: The Iron Cross is a complex, multi-planar exercise that challenges shoulder stability while engaging the entire kinetic chain. It develops functional strength and coordination across multiple muscle groups.
exercises.safetyInfo: This is an advanced movement requiring significant shoulder stability and mobility. Those with shoulder impingement or rotator cuff issues should avoid this exercise.

commonMistakes: Using excessive force that causes head movement
Holding breath during contractions
Tilting head out of neutral position
exerciseTips: Apply gentle, consistent pressure with your hands
Keep your head in neutral alignment
Contract neck muscles without allowing movement
Maintain steady breathing throughout
instructions: With your head and neck in a neutral position (normal position with head erect facing forward), place both of your hands on the front side of your head.
Now gently push forward as you contract the neck muscles but resisting any movement of your head. Start with slow tension and increase slowly. Keep breathing normally as you execute this contraction.
Hold for the recommended number of seconds.
Now release the tension slowly.
Rest for the recommended amount of time and repeat with your hands placed on the back side of your head.
exercises.name: Isometric Neck Exercise - Front And Back
exercises.name_pt: Exercício Isométrico de Pescoço - Frente e Trás
exercises.overview: Isometric neck exercises strengthen the cervical musculature without joint movement, making them ideal for rehabilitation and injury prevention. These exercises help improve neck stability and posture.
exercises.safetyInfo: Use gentle pressure only. Those with neck injuries, cervical disc problems, or recent whiplash should consult a healthcare provider before performing neck exercises.

commonMistakes: Applying too much force too quickly, causing neck strain
Allowing the head to move during the isometric hold
Holding breath during the contraction
exerciseTips: Apply gradual pressure to build tension slowly over 3-5 seconds
Keep your head in neutral alignment throughout the hold
Breathe normally and avoid holding your breath during contractions
Match resistance between your hand and neck muscles equally
instructions: With your head and neck in a neutral position (normal position with head erect facing forward), place your left hand on the left side of your head.

Now gently push towards the left as you contract the left neck muscles but resisting any movement of your head. Start with slow tension and increase slowly. Keep breathing normally as you execute this contraction.
Hold for the recommended number of seconds.
Now release the tension slowly.
Rest for the recommended amount of time and repeat with your right hand placed on the right side of your head.
exercises.name: Isometric Neck Exercise - Sides
exercises.overview: This isometric exercise strengthens the lateral neck muscles by creating resistance without movement. It helps improve neck stability and can reduce the risk of neck injuries.
exercises.safetyInfo: Avoid this exercise if you have cervical disc issues or recent neck injuries. Stop immediately if you experience sharp pain or dizziness.

commonMistakes: Not shifting weight far enough to one side, reducing the unilateral challenge
Allowing hips to sag or pike up during the movement
Moving too quickly without proper control
exerciseTips: Shift your weight completely to one side before lowering
Keep your core tight to prevent hip sagging
Control the movement on both the lowering and pushing phases
Maintain straight body alignment throughout the exercise
instructions: Assume a push-up position, supporting your weight on your hands and toes while keeping your body straight. Your hands should be just outside of shoulder width. This will be your starting position.
Begin by shifting your body weight as far to one side as possible, allowing the elbow on that side to flex as you lower your body.
Reverse the motion by extending the flexed arm, pushing yourself up and then dropping to the other side.
Repeat for the desired number of repetitions.
exercises.name: Isometric Wipers
exercises.overview: This dynamic push-up variation challenges unilateral strength by shifting body weight from side to side. It improves chest strength, core stability, and shoulder stabilization.
exercises.safetyInfo: This exercise places uneven stress on the shoulders and wrists. Avoid if you have wrist or shoulder injuries.

commonMistakes: Using momentum to swing arms and legs up instead of controlled muscle contraction
Bending the knees during the upward phase
Not achieving full range of motion by stopping short of the jackknife position
exerciseTips: Coordinate the lifting of arms and legs simultaneously
Focus on flexing your spine rather than just lifting limbs
Keep legs straight throughout the movement
Control the lowering phase to maximize muscle engagement
instructions: Lie flat on the floor (or exercise mat) on your back with your arms extended straight back behind your head and your legs extended also. This will be your starting position.

As you exhale, bend at the waist while simultaneously raising your legs and arms to meet in a jackknife position. Tip: The legs should be extended and lifted at approximately a 35-45 degree angle from the floor and the arms should be extended and parallel to your legs. The upper torso should be off the floor.
While inhaling, lower your arms and legs back to the starting position.
Repeat for the recommended amount of repetitions.
exercises.name: Jackknife Sit-Up
exercises.overview: This compound abdominal exercise simultaneously engages the upper and lower portions of the rectus abdominis. It provides an efficient full-range core workout by combining spinal flexion with hip flexion.
exercises.safetyInfo: Avoid this exercise if you have lower back problems. The simultaneous lifting can place significant stress on the lumbar spine.

commonMistakes: Not adequately contracting glutes and hamstrings before movement
Moving too quickly, defeating the purpose of the slow tempo
Allowing hip flexors to dominate the movement
exerciseTips: Squeeze your glutes and hamstrings hard before beginning the sit-up
Take 3-6 seconds to complete each upward phase
Maintain constant tension in posterior chain muscles throughout
Focus on spinal flexion rather than hip flexion
instructions: Position your body on the floor in the basic sit-up position; knees to a ninety degree angle with feet flat on the floor and arms either crossed over your chest or to the sides. This will be your starting position.

As you strongly tighten your glutes and hamstrings, fill your lungs with air and in a slow (three to six second count) ascent, slowly exhale. Tip: It is important to tighten the glutes and hamstrings as this will cause the hip flexors to be inactivated in a process called reciprocal inhibition, which basically means that opposite muscles to the contracted ones will relax.
As you inhale, slowly go back in a controlled manner to the starting position.
Repeat for the recommended amount of repetitions.
exercises.name: Janda Sit-Up
exercises.overview: This modified sit-up uses reciprocal inhibition to reduce hip flexor involvement by pre-activating the glutes and hamstrings. It provides more isolated abdominal training with reduced lower back stress.
exercises.safetyInfo: This exercise is generally safer than traditional sit-ups due to reduced hip flexor involvement. However, those with neck issues should support their head.

commonMistakes: Standing too far forward or backward on the bar, creating uneven loading
Allowing the bar to drift away from the centerline of the body
Leaning too far forward, placing excessive stress on the lower back
exerciseTips: Position yourself exactly in the center of the barbell
Keep your torso as upright as possible throughout the movement
Drive through your heels when standing up from the bottom position
Maintain equal grip strength with both hands
instructions: Place a barbell on the floor.
Stand in the middle of the bar length wise.
Bend down by bending at the knees and keeping your back straight and grasp the front of the bar with your right hand. Your palm should be in (neutral grip) facing the left side.

Grasp the rear of the bar with your left hand. The palm of your hand should be in neutral grip alignment (palms facing the right side). Tip: Ensure that your grip is even on the bar. Your torso should be positioned right in the middle of the bar and the distance between your torso and your right hand (which should be at the front) should be the same as the distance between your torso and your left hand (which should be to your back).
Now stand straight up with the weight. Tip: Your feet should be shoulder width apart and your toes slightly pointed out.

Squat down by bending at the knees and keeping your back straight until your upper thighs are parallel with the floor. Tip: Keep your back as vertical as possible with the floor and your head up. Also remember to not let your knees go past your toes. Inhale during this portion of the movement.

Now drive yourself back up to the starting position by pushing with the feet . Tip: Keep the bar hanging at arm's length and your elbows locked with a slight bend. The arms only serve as hooks. Avoid doing any lifting with them. Do the lifting with your thighs; not your arms.
exercises.name: Jefferson Squats
exercises.overview: This unique squat variation involves straddling a barbell and gripping it with opposing hand positions. It challenges unilateral strength, core stability, and provides a different stimulus for leg development.
exercises.safetyInfo: This exercise places unique rotational stress on the spine due to the mixed grip. Avoid if you have lower back injuries or disc problems.

commonMistakes: Starting with bar over shoulders instead of chest
Using too much weight before mastering technique
Rolling bar too far toward face
exerciseTips: Start with bar over upper chest, not shoulders
Keep elbows tucked throughout the movement
Allow controlled roll-back at mid-point
Focus on tricep contraction during press phase
instructions: Start the exercise the same way you would a close grip bench press. You will lie on a flat bench while holding a barbell at arms length (fully extended) with the elbows in. However, instead of having the arms perpendicular to the torso, make sure the bar is set in a direct line above the upper chest. This will be your starting position.

Now beginning from a fully extended position lower the bar down as if performing a lying triceps extension. Inhale as you perform this movement. When you reach the half way point, let the bar roll back about one inch by moving the upper arms towards your legs until they are perpendicular to the torso. Tip: Keep the bend at the elbows constant as you bring the upper arms forward.
As you exhale, press the bar back up by using the triceps to perform a close grip bench press.
Now go back to the starting position and start over.
Repeat for the recommended amount of repetitions.
exercises.name: JM Press
exercises.overview: The JM Press is a hybrid movement combining a lying tricep extension with a close-grip bench press, specifically designed to build lockout strength.
exercises.safetyInfo: Start with very light weight to learn the unique movement pattern. Use a spotter when learning this exercise as the bar path is unconventional.

commonMistakes: Landing with straight legs instead of absorbing impact with bent knees
Not squatting deep enough before the jumping phase
Allowing knees to cave inward during landing
exerciseTips: Focus on landing softly with bent knees to reduce impact stress
Keep your chest up and core engaged during both jumping and landing
