Drive your arms upward to help generate maximum jump height
Maintain proper squat depth before each explosive jump
instructions: Stand with feet shoulder-width apart and lower into a squat position with thighs parallel to the floor.
Explosively drive through your heels and jump as high as possible while extending your arms overhead.
Land softly on the balls of your feet with knees slightly bent to absorb the impact.
Immediately lower back into the squat position for the next repetition.
Maintain good squat form throughout both the jumping and landing phases.
exercises.name: Jump Squat
exercises.overview: Jump squats are a plyometric exercise that combines a traditional squat with an explosive jump. This movement develops lower body power, improves athletic performance, and provides cardiovascular benefits.
exercises.safetyInfo: The plyometric nature of this exercise places stress on joints during landing. Those with knee or ankle injuries should avoid or modify this movement. Ensure proper landing technique to prevent injury.

commonMistakes: Starting with kettlebell in wrong position
Rotating wrist too early in the movement
Arching back excessively during press
exerciseTips: Clean kettlebell to shoulder in rack position
Rotate wrist smoothly as you press overhead
Keep core tight to prevent back extension
Control the descent back to rack position
instructions: Clean a kettlebell to your shoulder. Clean the kettlebell to your shoulder by extending through the legs and hips as you raise the kettlebell towards your shoulder. The palm should be facing inward.
Looking straight ahead, press the kettlebell out and overhead, rotating your wrist so that your palm faces forward at the top of the motion.
Return the kettlebell to the starting position, with the palm facing in.
exercises.name: Kettlebell Arnold Press
exercises.overview: The kettlebell Arnold press combines shoulder pressing with rotation, providing comprehensive shoulder development and stability training.
exercises.safetyInfo: Master the basic kettlebell press before adding rotation. Use lighter weight initially to learn the movement pattern safely.

commonMistakes: Rounding the back during the movement
Using momentum to swing the weight up
Pulling the elbow too wide away from the body
exerciseTips: Keep your core tight throughout the movement
Drive your elbow back rather than out to the side
Squeeze your shoulder blade at the top of each rep
Maintain a neutral spine throughout the exercise
instructions: Stand with feet shoulder-width apart, holding a kettlebell in one hand with arm extended toward the floor.
Hinge at the hips and bend forward to about 45 degrees, keeping your back straight and core engaged.
Pull the kettlebell up toward your ribcage, driving your elbow back and squeezing your shoulder blade.
Lower the kettlebell with control back to the starting position.
Complete all reps on one side before switching to the other arm.
exercises.name: Kettlebell Bent Over Row
exercises.overview: The kettlebell bent over row is a unilateral pulling exercise that targets the lats and upper back muscles. It helps improve posture and builds a strong, stable back while challenging core stability.
exercises.safetyInfo: Avoid this exercise if you have lower back issues. Those with shoulder impingement should use caution and reduced range of motion.

commonMistakes: Using too heavy of a kettlebell before mastering the balance and technique
Allowing the kettlebell to tilt or lose the bottoms up position
Pressing too quickly without maintaining control and stability
exerciseTips: Squeeze the handle extremely tight to maintain stability and control
Keep your wrist straight and aligned with your forearm throughout
Focus on slow, controlled movements rather than speed or heavy weight
Engage your core and maintain perfect posture to assist with balance
instructions: Hold a kettlebell upside down by the handle with the bell pointing toward the ceiling at shoulder height.
Keep your grip tight and engage your core while maintaining perfect balance of the inverted kettlebell.
Press the kettlebell straight overhead while maintaining the bottoms up position throughout.
Focus on stability and control as you extend your arm to full lockout above your head.
Slowly lower the kettlebell back to shoulder height, maintaining the inverted position throughout.
exercises.name: Kettlebell Bottoms Up Press
exercises.overview: The kettlebell bottoms up press is an advanced shoulder exercise that challenges stability, grip strength, and core engagement while building shoulder strength. The inverted position forces maximum muscle activation and coordination.
exercises.safetyInfo: This is an advanced exercise requiring excellent shoulder stability and grip strength. Start with very light weights and ensure adequate space around you. Not recommended for those with wrist or shoulder injuries.

commonMistakes: Letting the kettlebell drift away from the body during the clean
Pressing with a loose core, causing back hyperextension
Catching the kettlebell with a loose wrist instead of proper rack position
exerciseTips: Generate power from your hips during the clean portion
Keep the kettlebell close to your body throughout the movement
Practice the rack position to avoid bruising your forearm
Maintain a tight core throughout both the clean and press phases
instructions: Place the kettlebell between your feet and hinge at the hips to grip the handle with one hand.
Explosively extend your hips and knees while pulling the kettlebell up close to your body.
As the kettlebell reaches chest level, rotate your elbow under the bell and catch it in the rack position against your forearm.
From the rack position, press the kettlebell overhead by extending your arm while keeping your core tight.
Lower the kettlebell back to the rack position with control.
Lower the kettlebell back down between your legs and repeat, or switch arms after completing the set.
exercises.name: Kettlebell Clean And Press
exercises.overview: The kettlebell clean and press is a dynamic full-body exercise that combines explosive hip drive with overhead pressing strength. This movement develops power, coordination, and functional strength while targeting multiple muscle groups.
exercises.safetyInfo: This is an advanced movement requiring proper kettlebell technique and shoulder mobility. Individuals with shoulder impingement or lower back issues should master the individual components before attempting the full movement.

commonMistakes: Swinging the kettlebell away from the body creating an arc
Using only arms instead of hip drive for power
Failing to rotate wrist causing bell to crash into forearm
exerciseTips: Drive through your heels explosively to generate power
Keep the kettlebell close to your body throughout the movement
Rotate your wrist as the bell reaches shoulder height
Land the bell softly in the rack position
instructions: Place kettlebell between your feet. To get in the starting position, push your butt back and look straight ahead.

Clean the kettlebell to your shoulder. Clean the kettlebell to your shoulders by extending through the legs and hips as you raise the kettlebell towards your shoulder. The wrist should rotate as you do so.
Lower the kettlebell, keeping the hamstrings loaded by keeping your back straight and your butt out.
exercises.name: Kettlebell Dead Clean
exercises.overview: A ballistic kettlebell movement that combines a deadlift with an explosive clean to the shoulder. Develops power, coordination, and full-body strength.
exercises.safetyInfo: Avoid if you have lower back injuries. Start with lighter weight to master the timing and coordination required for proper wrist rotation.

commonMistakes: Rounding the back excessively during the pass
Standing too narrow making the pass difficult
Moving too quickly and losing control of the kettlebell
exerciseTips: Maintain a wide stance for stability throughout
Keep your back flat and chest up during the movement
Pass the bell smoothly between hands without pausing
Engage your core to prevent lower back rounding
instructions: Place one kettlebell between your legs and take a wider than shoulder width stance. Bend over by pushing your butt out and keeping your back flat.
Pick up a kettlebell and pass it to your other hand between your legs. The receiving hand should reach from behind the legs. Go back and forth for several repetitions.
exercises.name: Kettlebell Figure 8
exercises.overview: A dynamic movement pattern that improves coordination, core stability, and hamstring flexibility while maintaining a hinged position.
exercises.safetyInfo: Keep movements controlled to prevent losing grip on the kettlebell. Those with lower back issues should avoid or use very light weight.
variations: Bent Over Two-Dumbbell Row With Palms In

commonMistakes: Allowing the kettlebells to shift or rotate during the press
Bouncing the elbows off the floor instead of pausing briefly
Pressing at an incorrect angle that stresses the shoulders
exerciseTips: Keep the kettlebell handles in a neutral position throughout the movement
Focus on driving through your chest rather than your shoulders
Maintain tension in your core to prevent excessive back arch
Use the floor contact as a natural stopping point to prevent overextension
instructions: Lie on the floor with knees bent and feet flat, holding kettlebells at chest level with handles vertical.
Position your upper arms on the floor at roughly 45 degrees from your torso with elbows bent.
Keep your shoulder blades pulled back and maintain a neutral spine with core engaged.
Press the kettlebells straight up until your arms are fully extended over your chest.
Lower the weights with control until your upper arms touch the floor, then immediately press back up.
exercises.name: Kettlebell Floor Press
exercises.overview: The kettlebell floor press is a horizontal pressing movement that targets the chest while limiting shoulder extension through floor contact. This exercise builds pressing strength and is excellent for those without access to a bench.
exercises.safetyInfo: The floor provides a natural range of motion limiter, making this exercise safer than traditional bench press. However, those with shoulder issues should ensure proper elbow positioning.

commonMistakes: Allowing the knees to cave inward during the squat
Rounding the back by letting the kettlebell pull you forward
exerciseTips: Keep the kettlebell close to your chest throughout the movement
Focus on sitting back into your hips rather than just bending your knees
Maintain an upright torso by keeping your chest proud
Drive through your heels when standing up
instructions: Hold a kettlebell by the horns at chest level with both hands, keeping your elbows close to your body.
Stand with feet slightly wider than shoulder-width apart and toes pointed slightly outward.
Initiate the squat by pushing your hips back and bending at the knees while keeping your chest up.
Lower your body until your thighs are parallel to the floor or as deep as your mobility allows.
Drive through your heels and push the floor away to return to the starting position.
Stand tall and repeat for the desired number of repetitions.
exercises.name: Kettlebell Goblet Squat
exercises.overview: The kettlebell goblet squat is a beginner-friendly squat variation that promotes proper squat mechanics while strengthening the legs and core. The front-loaded position helps maintain an upright torso and teaches proper movement patterns.
exercises.safetyInfo: This exercise is generally safe for most people and is often used as a teaching tool for proper squat mechanics. Those with knee or hip issues should start with bodyweight squats first.

commonMistakes: Moving the kettlebell too far away from the head during the circle
Using momentum instead of controlled movement
Arching the back excessively when the kettlebell is overhead
exerciseTips: Keep your core engaged to prevent arching your back
Maintain good posture with shoulders down and back
Move slowly and controlled, focusing on shoulder mobility
Keep the kettlebell as close to your head as comfortable
instructions: Hold a kettlebell upside down by the horns (sides of the handle) at chest level.
Stand with feet shoulder-width apart and engage your core for stability.
Slowly move the kettlebell in a circular motion around your head, starting to one side.
Keep the kettlebell close to your head as you move it behind and around to the other side.
Complete the circle and reverse direction, maintaining control throughout the movement.
exercises.name: Kettlebell Halo
exercises.overview: Kettlebell halos improve shoulder mobility and stability by moving a weight in a full circle around the head. This exercise enhances shoulder health and rotator cuff strength.
exercises.safetyInfo: Start with a light kettlebell to master the movement pattern. Those with shoulder impingement or neck issues should perform with caution or avoid this exercise.

commonMistakes: Starting the pull with arms instead of hip extension
Allowing the kettlebell to swing away from the body
Catching the bell with a rigid arm causing jarring impact
exerciseTips: Generate power from your hips, not your arms
Keep the kettlebell close to your body during the pull
Catch the bell with a soft elbow in rack position
Control the descent to maintain hamstring tension
instructions: Place kettlebell between your feet. To get in the starting position, push your butt back and look straight ahead.

Clean kettlebell to your shoulder. Clean the kettlebell to your shoulders by extending through the legs and hips as you raise the kettlebell towards your shoulder. The wrist should rotate as you do so.
Lower kettlebell to a hanging position between your legs while keeping the hamstrings loaded. Keep your head up at all times.
exercises.name: Kettlebell Hang Clean
exercises.overview: An explosive movement starting from a hanging position that develops power, timing, and coordination while targeting the posterior chain.
exercises.safetyInfo: Master the basic kettlebell swing before attempting cleans. Start light to develop proper timing and wrist rotation technique.

commonMistakes: Pulling with the arms first instead of generating power from the hips
Allowing the kettlebell to swing away from the body during the movement
Using too much weight and compromising the explosive nature of the exercise
exerciseTips: Generate power from your hips and legs, not just your arms and shoulders
Keep the kettlebell close to your body throughout the entire movement
Focus on explosive hip extension followed by the arm pull
Maintain an upright torso and avoid leaning backward during the pull
instructions: Stand with feet hip-width apart, holding a kettlebell with both hands in front of your thighs.
Initiate the movement by explosively extending your hips and knees while shrugging your shoulders.
As the kettlebell rises, pull it straight up close to your body, leading with your elbows high and wide.
Pull the kettlebell to chest level, then control its descent back to the starting position.
exercises.name: Kettlebell High Pull
exercises.overview: The kettlebell high pull is an explosive movement that develops power in the shoulders and upper back while requiring coordination from the entire body. It bridges the gap between deadlift variations and overhead movements.
exercises.safetyInfo: This is an advanced explosive movement that requires proper hip hinge mechanics and shoulder mobility. Those with shoulder impingement, lower back issues, or limited overhead mobility should avoid this exercise.

commonMistakes: Bending at the waist instead of hinging at the hips
Allowing the standing knee to lock completely
Rotating the hips or shoulders during the movement
exerciseTips: Focus on pushing your hips back rather than bending forward
Keep your standing leg slightly bent throughout the movement
Extend your free leg back for counterbalance
Maintain a straight line from head to extended heel
instructions: Hold a kettlebell by the handle in one hand. Stand on one leg, on the same side that you hold the kettlebell.
Keeping that knee slightly bent, perform a stiff legged deadlift by bending at the hip, extending your free leg behind you for balance.
Continue lowering the kettlebell until you are parallel to the ground, and then return to the upright position.
exercises.name: Kettlebell One-Legged Deadlift
exercises.overview: A unilateral deadlift variation that challenges balance while strengthening the hamstrings and glutes. Excellent for correcting imbalances and improving stability.
exercises.safetyInfo: Start with bodyweight only to develop balance and coordination. Those with ankle instability should use a support initially.

commonMistakes: Allowing the kettlebell to drift forward or backward
Not maintaining proper shoulder stability
Inadequate squat depth due to mobility restrictions
exerciseTips: Keep the kettlebell directly over your shoulder joint
Engage your core to maintain stability
Focus on ankle and hip mobility for proper depth
Start with lighter weight to master the movement pattern
instructions: Hold a kettlebell overhead with one arm, keeping your wrist straight and shoulder packed down.
Stand with feet slightly wider than shoulder-width, toes pointed slightly outward.
Initiate the squat by pushing your hips back and bending at the knees while keeping the kettlebell overhead.
Descend until your thighs are parallel to the floor or as deep as mobility allows.
Drive through your heels to return to the starting position, maintaining the overhead position throughout.
exercises.name: Kettlebell Overhead Squat
exercises.name_pt: Agachamento com Kettlebell Acima da Cabeça
exercises.overview: The kettlebell overhead squat is an advanced full-body exercise that combines lower body strength with overhead stability and mobility. It challenges coordination, balance, and strength while improving functional movement patterns.
exercises.safetyInfo: This is an advanced exercise requiring good shoulder mobility and stability. Those with shoulder or lower back issues should avoid or seek professional guidance.

commonMistakes: Rounding the lower back excessively
Using momentum instead of controlled passes
Standing too narrow making the pass awkward
exerciseTips: Maintain a wide stance for stability
Keep your back straight and core engaged
Pass the bell smoothly without momentum
Focus on controlled movement rather than speed
instructions: Place one kettlebell between your legs and take a comfortable stance. Bend over by pushing your butt out and keeping your back flat.
Pick up a kettlebell and pass it to your other hand between your legs, in the fashion of a "W". Go back and forth for several repetitions.
exercises.name: Kettlebell Pass Between The Legs
exercises.name_pt: Passagem de Kettlebell Entre as Pernas
exercises.overview: A coordination and core stability exercise that involves passing a kettlebell between your legs in a hinged position. Improves hand-eye coordination and core control.
exercises.safetyInfo: Use a light kettlebell initially to master the coordination. Avoid if you have lower back problems or limited hip mobility.

commonMistakes: Using only arm strength instead of core rotation
Swinging too fast and losing control
Allowing feet to shift during the rotation
exerciseTips: Generate power from your core rotation, not just arms
Keep your feet planted and rotate through your torso
Control the bell at the top of each swing
Maintain a wide stance for stability
instructions: With a wide stance, hold a kettlebell with both hands. Allow it to hang at waist level with your arms extended. This will be your starting position.
Initiate the movement by turning to one side, swinging the kettlebell to head height. Briefly pause at the top of the motion.
Allow the bell to drop as you rotate to the opposite side, again raising the kettlebell to head height.
Repeat for the desired amount of repetitions.
exercises.name: Kettlebell Pirate Ships
exercises.overview: A rotational movement that combines shoulder mobility with core strength. Develops coordination and functional rotation patterns.
exercises.safetyInfo: Start with a light kettlebell to master the coordination. Those with shoulder impingement should avoid overhead positions.

commonMistakes: Allowing the knee to cave inward
Leaning too far forward and losing balance
Not going deep enough in the squat position
exerciseTips: Keep the kettlebell close to your chest throughout the movement
Drive through your heel when ascending
Maintain an upright torso position
Extend the non-working leg straight out in front
instructions: Pick up a kettlebell with two hands and hold it by the horns. Hold one leg off of the floor and squat down on the other.
Squat down by flexing the knee and sitting back with the hips, holding the kettlebell up in front of you.
Hold the bottom position for a second and then reverse the motion, driving through the heel and keeping your head and chest up.
Lower yourself again and repeat.
exercises.name: Kettlebell Pistol Squat
exercises.overview: The kettlebell pistol squat is an advanced unilateral leg exercise that challenges balance, mobility, and strength while building functional single-leg power.
exercises.safetyInfo: This advanced exercise requires excellent ankle mobility, hip flexibility, and single-leg strength. Avoid if you have knee issues or poor balance.

commonMistakes: Allowing the kettlebell to drift away from the body
Leaning forward excessively during the descent
Not achieving adequate squat depth
exerciseTips: Keep the kettlebell close to your body in the racked position
Maintain an upright torso throughout the movement
Drive your knees out in line with your toes
Focus on keeping your weight evenly distributed across your feet
instructions: Clean a kettlebell to the racked position at shoulder height with elbow close to your ribs.
Stand with feet slightly wider than shoulder-width apart and toes pointed slightly outward.
Keep your chest up and core engaged while maintaining the racked position.
Lower into a squat by pushing your hips back and bending your knees until thighs are parallel to the floor.
Drive through your heels to return to the starting position while keeping the kettlebell stable.
Complete all reps on one side before switching to the other arm.
exercises.name: Kettlebell Racked Squat
exercises.overview: The kettlebell racked squat is a front-loaded squat variation that emphasizes the quadriceps while challenging core stability and upper body strength. The unilateral loading creates additional anti-lateral flexion demands.
exercises.safetyInfo: Individuals with wrist or shoulder mobility issues may find the racked position uncomfortable. Those with knee problems should limit depth and consult a healthcare provider.

commonMistakes: Taking too small of a step back, causing the front knee to track over the toes
Leaning forward excessively during the descent
Allowing the back foot to bear too much weight during the movement
exerciseTips: Keep your torso upright throughout the entire movement
Focus your weight on your front heel during the push-off
Step back far enough to create a 90-degree angle in both knees
Engage your core to maintain balance and stability
instructions: Hold a kettlebell by the handle at chest level with both hands, or hold one in each hand at your sides.
Stand with feet hip-width apart and core engaged.
Step one foot backward into a lunge position, lowering your back knee toward the floor.
Descend until your front thigh is parallel to the ground and your back knee nearly touches the floor.
Push through your front heel to return to the starting position and repeat on the other leg.
exercises.name: Kettlebell Reverse Lunge
exercises.overview: The Kettlebell Reverse Lunge is a unilateral lower body exercise that builds leg strength, improves balance, and enhances functional movement patterns. The reverse stepping motion is often easier on the knees than forward lunges.
exercises.safetyInfo: This exercise requires good balance and coordination. Start with bodyweight or lighter kettlebells before progressing. Those with knee injuries should consult a healthcare provider before performing lunging movements.

commonMistakes: Rounding the back instead of maintaining a neutral spine position
Bending the knees too much and turning it into a squat movement
Allowing the kettlebell to drift away from the body during the descent
exerciseTips: Keep the kettlebell close to your body throughout the entire range of motion
Focus on the hip hinge movement rather than squatting down
Maintain a neutral spine by keeping your chest up and shoulders back
Squeeze your glutes at the top of the movement to complete the hip extension
instructions: Stand with feet hip-width apart, holding a kettlebell with both hands in front of your thighs.
Initiate the movement by pushing your hips back while maintaining a slight bend in your knees.
Lower the kettlebell toward the floor by hinging at the hips, keeping your back straight and chest up.
Feel the stretch in your hamstrings, then drive your hips forward to return to the starting position.
exercises.name: Kettlebell Romanian Deadlift
exercises.overview: The kettlebell Romanian deadlift is a hip-hinge movement that primarily targets the hamstrings and glutes while improving posterior chain strength. It's excellent for developing proper deadlift mechanics and hamstring flexibility.
exercises.safetyInfo: Individuals with lower back issues or hamstring injuries should approach this exercise cautiously. Proper hip hinge mechanics are essential to prevent lower back strain.

commonMistakes: Pressing forward instead of straight overhead
Arching the back excessively
Not fully extending the arm at the top
exerciseTips: Keep your core engaged throughout the movement
Press the kettlebell straight up from shoulder level
Maintain a tall spine while seated
Control the descent slowly
instructions: Sit on the floor and spread your legs out comfortably.
Clean one kettlebell to your shoulder.
Press the kettlebell up and out until it is locked out overhead. Return to the starting position.
exercises.name: Kettlebell Seated Press
exercises.overview: The kettlebell seated press isolates the shoulders by removing leg assistance, building pure upper body pressing strength from a stable seated position.
exercises.safetyInfo: The seated position eliminates leg drive, placing more demand on the shoulders. Stop immediately if you feel shoulder impingement or pain.

commonMistakes: Rushing the alternating pattern
Not keeping the resting kettlebell stable at shoulder level
Using unequal ranges of motion between sides
exerciseTips: Clean both kettlebells to shoulder position first
Press one while the other remains at shoulder level
Maintain equal tempo on both sides
Keep your core tight throughout
instructions: Clean two kettlebells two your shoulders.
Press one kettlebell.
Lower the kettlebell and immediately press the other kettlebell. Make sure to do the same amount of reps on both sides.
exercises.name: Kettlebell Seesaw Press
exercises.overview: The kettlebell seesaw press develops unilateral shoulder strength and stability while challenging coordination through alternating pressing patterns.
exercises.safetyInfo: Ensure proper kettlebell clean technique before attempting. The alternating pattern requires good coordination and shoulder stability.

commonMistakes: Allowing the torso to rotate toward the working arm during the press
Pressing the kettlebell at an angle instead of straight up
Rushing through the movement without pausing at the bottom
exerciseTips: Keep your core engaged to prevent rotation during the press
Allow your elbow to fully rest on the floor between reps
Press the kettlebell in a straight line up and down
Keep your non-working arm extended out for stability
instructions: Lie on the floor with a kettlebell positioned next to your working shoulder.
Roll toward the kettlebell and grip the handle, then roll back to lying position with the kettlebell at chest level.
Keep your working arm's elbow at roughly 45 degrees from your torso with your tricep resting on the floor.
Press the kettlebell straight up until your arm is fully extended above your chest.
Lower the kettlebell back down until your elbow touches the floor, then repeat for all reps before switching sides.
exercises.name: Kettlebell Single Arm Floor Press
exercises.name_pt: Supino no Chão Unilateral com Kettlebell
exercises.overview: The Kettlebell Single Arm Floor Press develops unilateral pressing strength while challenging core stability. The floor provides a natural stopping point, making it excellent for building strength from a dead stop position.
exercises.safetyInfo: The floor limits the range of motion, making this safer for those with shoulder issues compared to bench pressing. However, getting into position with heavier kettlebells requires practice and proper technique to avoid injury.

commonMistakes: Pressing the weight forward instead of straight overhead
Arching the back excessively to complete the press
exerciseTips: Keep your core engaged throughout to prevent back hyperextension
Press the kettlebell in a straight line overhead
Maintain a tight grip on the kettlebell handle throughout
Focus on keeping your shoulder packed and stable
instructions: Clean a kettlebell to the rack position with the weight resting against your forearm and upper arm.
Stand with feet hip-width apart, core engaged, and the non-working arm at your side for balance.
Press the kettlebell straight up overhead by extending your arm while keeping your core tight.
Push until your arm is fully extended with the kettlebell directly above your shoulder.
Slowly lower the kettlebell back to the rack position with control.
Complete all repetitions on one side before switching to the other arm.
exercises.name: Kettlebell Single Arm Press
exercises.overview: The kettlebell single arm press is a unilateral overhead pressing exercise that builds shoulder strength while challenging core stability. The offset loading requires additional stabilization and helps identify strength imbalances.
exercises.safetyInfo: Those with shoulder impingement or lower back issues should use caution. Ensure proper shoulder mobility before attempting overhead pressing movements.

commonMistakes: Pulling the weight too high, causing shoulder impingement
Swinging the kettlebell away from the body
Using momentum instead of controlled muscle contraction
exerciseTips: Lead with your elbow rather than your hand during the pull
Keep the kettlebell close to your body throughout the movement
Stop when your elbow reaches shoulder height to avoid impingement
Engage your core to prevent compensatory movements
instructions: Stand with feet hip-width apart holding a kettlebell in one hand at arm's length in front of your thigh.
Keep your core engaged and maintain a neutral spine throughout the movement.
Pull the kettlebell straight up along your body by leading with your elbow.
Continue pulling until your elbow reaches just above shoulder height.
Slowly lower the kettlebell back to the starting position with control.
Complete all repetitions on one side before switching arms.
exercises.name: Kettlebell Single Arm Upright Row
exercises.overview: The Kettlebell Single Arm Upright Row is a unilateral pulling exercise that targets the shoulders and upper back muscles. It helps develop shoulder stability and strength while addressing muscle imbalances between sides.
exercises.safetyInfo: Avoid if you have shoulder impingement or rotator cuff injuries. Do not pull above shoulder height. Start with lighter weight to assess shoulder comfort and mobility.

commonMistakes: Allowing the hips to rotate or open up during the movement
Bending the knee too much instead of hinging at the hip
Losing balance by moving too quickly or not controlling the weight
exerciseTips: Keep your hips square and avoid rotating during the movement
Focus on hinging at the hip rather than bending at the waist
Maintain a straight line from your head through your lifted leg
Control the descent and use your hamstrings and glutes to return up
instructions: Stand on one leg while holding a kettlebell in the opposite hand, keeping a slight bend in your standing knee.
Begin to hinge at the hip by pushing your free leg back behind you while lowering the kettlebell toward the ground.
Keep your back straight and chest up as you lower the weight, feeling a stretch in your standing leg hamstring.
Continue until you feel a good stretch or the kettlebell approaches the floor, keeping your lifted leg in line with your torso.
Drive through your standing heel and squeeze your glutes to return to the upright starting position.
Complete all repetitions on one leg before switching to the other side.
exercises.name: Kettlebell Single Leg Deadlift
exercises.overview: The kettlebell single leg deadlift is an intermediate unilateral exercise that targets the hamstrings and glutes while challenging balance and stability. This movement helps identify and correct muscle imbalances between legs while improving functional strength.
exercises.safetyInfo: This exercise requires good balance and hamstring flexibility. Those with balance issues should start with bodyweight or use a light support for stability until proper form is mastered.

commonMistakes: Arching the back excessively to assist the press
Pressing the kettlebell forward instead of straight up
Using leg drive to assist the movement
exerciseTips: Keep your core engaged to prevent arching your back
Press the kettlebell in a straight line directly overhead
Maintain a neutral grip with wrist straight throughout
Keep your non-working arm relaxed at your side
instructions: Clean a kettlebell to the racked position at shoulder height with your elbow close to your ribs.
Stand with feet hip-width apart and maintain a tight core throughout the movement.
Press the kettlebell straight up by extending your arm until it's fully locked out overhead.
Keep your wrist straight and the kettlebell balanced over your shoulder.
Lower the kettlebell back to the racked position with control.
Complete all repetitions on one side before switching arms.
exercises.name: Kettlebell Strict Press
exercises.overview: The kettlebell strict press is a unilateral overhead pressing exercise that builds shoulder strength and stability. The offset loading challenges core stability while developing functional pressing power.
exercises.safetyInfo: Those with shoulder impingement or rotator cuff injuries should avoid overhead pressing. Start with lighter weight to master the racked position and pressing mechanics.

commonMistakes: Not setting back into the hips properly
Pulling with arms instead of using hip drive
Allowing knees to cave inward
exerciseTips: Start with hips back in a deep sumo stance
Pull the kettlebell to shoulder height
Lead with your elbows during the pull
Use hip drive to initiate the movement
instructions: Place a kettlebell on the ground between your feet. Position your feet in a wide stance, and grasp the kettlebell with two hands. Set your hips back as far as possible, with your knees bent. Keep your chest and head up. This will be your starting position.
Begin by extending the hips and knees, simultaneously pulling the kettlebell to your shoulders, raising your elbows as you do so. Reverse the motion to return to the starting position.
exercises.name: Kettlebell Sumo High Pull
exercises.overview: The kettlebell sumo high pull combines hip extension power with upper body pulling, developing explosive strength and posterior chain activation.
exercises.safetyInfo: Requires good hip mobility for the sumo stance. Avoid if you have lower back issues or shoulder impingement problems.

commonMistakes: Not squatting deep enough
Pressing too early before full hip extension
Allowing kettlebells to drift away from the body
exerciseTips: Clean both kettlebells to front rack position
Descend into a full squat
Drive through heels and use leg momentum to assist the press
Keep kettlebells close to your body
instructions: Clean two kettlebells to your shoulders. Clean the kettlebells to your shoulders by extending through the legs and hips as you pull the kettlebells towards your shoulders. Rotate your wrists as you do so. This will be your starting position.
Begin to squat by flexing your hips and knees, lowering your hips between your legs. Maintain an upright, straight back as you descend as low as you can.

At the bottom, reverse direction and squat by extending your knees and hips, driving through your heels. As you do so, press both kettlebells overhead by extending your arms straight up, using the momentum from the squat to help drive the weights upward.
As you begin the next repetition, return the weights to the shoulders.
exercises.name: Kettlebell Thruster
exercises.overview: The kettlebell thruster is a full-body explosive movement combining a front squat with an overhead press, building power and cardiovascular conditioning.
exercises.safetyInfo: This high-intensity movement combines squatting and pressing. Ensure proper mobility in ankles, hips, and shoulders before attempting.

commonMistakes: Moving too quickly through transitions
Not keeping the kettlebell arm locked out
Looking away from the kettlebell
exerciseTips: Keep your eyes on the kettlebell throughout the movement
Move slowly and control each transition
Use your free hand for support when needed
Maintain tension in the working arm
instructions: Lie on your back on the floor and press a kettlebell to the top position by extending the elbow. Bend the knee on the same side as the kettlebell.

Keeping the kettlebell locked out at all times, pivot to the opposite side and use your non- working arm to assist you in driving forward to the lunge position. Using your free hand, push yourself to a seated position, then progressing to one knee.
While looking up at the kettlebell, slowly stand up. Reverse the motion back to the starting position and repeat.
exercises.name: Kettlebell Turkish Get-Up (Lunge style)
exercises.overview: The Turkish get-up lunge style is a modified version of the classic TGU that develops shoulder stability, core strength, and movement coordination through a simplified transition pattern.
exercises.safetyInfo: This complex movement requires shoulder stability and mobility. Start with light weight and master the movement pattern before progressing.

commonMistakes: Looking away from the kettlebell during transitions
Rushing through the movement phases
Allowing the kettlebell to drift forward or backward overhead
exerciseTips: Keep your eyes on the kettlebell throughout the entire movement
Drive through your heel when transitioning to standing
Maintain a tight core to stabilize the overhead position
Move slowly and controlled through each transition phase
instructions: Lie on your back on the floor and press a kettlebell to the top position by extending the elbow. Bend the knee on the same side as the kettlebell.
Keeping the kettlebell locked out at all times, pivot to the opposite side and use your non- working arm to assist you in driving forward to the lunge position.

Using your free hand, push yourself to a seated position, then progressing to your feet. While looking up at the kettlebell, slowly stand up. Reverse the motion back to the starting position and repeat.
exercises.name: Kettlebell Turkish Get-Up (Squat style)
exercises.overview: The Turkish Get-Up is a full-body movement that develops overhead stability, core strength, and coordination while improving mobility through multiple planes of motion.
exercises.safetyInfo: Start with light weight to master the complex movement pattern. Avoid if you have shoulder impingement or lower back issues.

commonMistakes: Pulling the kettlebell too far from the body during the lift
Raising the elbows higher than shoulder level
Using momentum or swaying to assist the movement
exerciseTips: Keep the kettlebell close to your body throughout the movement
Lead with your elbows and keep them higher than your wrists
Maintain an upright torso and avoid leaning backward
Focus on the shoulder and trap muscles initiating the movement
instructions: Stand with feet shoulder-width apart, holding a kettlebell with both hands by the handle.
Let the kettlebell hang at arm's length in front of your body with arms straight.
Pull the kettlebell straight up along your torso, leading with your elbows until it reaches chest level.
Pause briefly at the top, then slowly lower the kettlebell back to the starting position.
exercises.name: Kettlebell Upright Row
exercises.overview: The kettlebell upright row targets the shoulders and upper traps using a single weight for a natural pulling motion. The kettlebell's design allows for comfortable hand positioning.
exercises.safetyInfo: Stop if you experience shoulder impingement or pinching sensations. The single kettlebell allows for a more natural grip width compared to barbells.

commonMistakes: Allowing the overhead arm to drift or bend
Rotating the torso instead of hinging at the hip
Moving too quickly through the range of motion
exerciseTips: Keep your eyes locked on the overhead kettlebell at all times
Push your hip toward the side of the overhead weight
Reach straight down toward your foot with control
Maintain a proud chest throughout the movement
instructions: Place a kettlebell in front of your lead foot and clean and press it overhead with your opposite arm. Clean the kettlebell to your shoulder by extending through the legs and hips as you pull the kettlebell towards your shoulders. Rotate your wrist as you do so, so that the palm faces forward. Press it overhead by extending the elbow.

Keeping the kettlebell locked out at all times, push your butt out in the direction of the locked out kettlebell. Turn your feet out at a forty-five degree angle from the arm with the locked out kettlebell. Bending at the hip to one side, sticking your butt out, slowly lean until you can touch the floor with your free hand. Keep your eyes on the kettlebell that you hold over your head at all times.
Pause for a second after reaching the ground and reverse the motion back to the starting position.
exercises.name: Kettlebell Windmill
exercises.overview: The Kettlebell Windmill develops core stability, hip mobility, and overhead strength while challenging coordination and body awareness.
exercises.safetyInfo: Requires good shoulder stability and hip mobility. Progress gradually and avoid if you have lower back or shoulder injuries.

commonMistakes: Poor false grip causing slippage during transition
Not generating enough momentum with the kip
Allowing elbows to flare out during the turnover phase
exerciseTips: Master the false grip before attempting the full movement
Generate momentum with your legs in a hollow-arch pattern
Pull the rings to your armpits as you transition over
Keep your elbows close to your body during the transition
instructions: Grip the rings using a false grip, with the base of your palms on top of the rings.
Begin with a movement swinging your legs backward slightly.

Counter that movement by swinging your legs forward and up, jerking your chin and chest back, pulling yourself up with both arms as you do so. As you reach the top position of the pull-up, pull the rings to your armpits as you roll your shoulders forward, allowing your elbows to move straight back behind you. This puts you into the proper position to continue into the dip portion of the movement.
Maintaining control and stability, extend through the elbow to complete the motion.
Use care when lowering yourself to the ground.
exercises.name: Kipping Muscle Up
exercises.overview: The Kipping Muscle Up is an advanced gymnastic movement that combines explosive pulling power with coordination to transition from below to above the rings.
exercises.safetyInfo: High risk of injury if attempted without proper progression. Master strict pull-ups and dips first. Use spotters when learning.

commonMistakes: Using momentum or swinging the legs
Only lifting feet instead of bringing knees toward chest
Allowing shoulders to shrug up toward ears
exerciseTips: Keep your back pressed against the pad throughout the movement
Focus on lifting your knees rather than just your feet
Control the descent to maximize time under tension
Engage your core before initiating the lift
instructions: Position your body on the vertical leg raise bench so that your forearms are resting on the pads next to the torso and holding on to the handles. Your arms will be bent at a 90 degree angle.
The torso should be straight with the lower back pressed against the pad of the machine and the legs extended pointing towards the floor. This will be your starting position.

Now as you breathe out, lift your legs up as you keep them extended. Continue this movement until your legs are roughly parallel to the floor and then hold the contraction for a second. Tip: Do not use any momentum or swinging as you perform this exercise.
Slowly go back to the starting position as you breathe in.
Repeat for the recommended amount of repetitions.
exercises.name: Knee/Hip Raise On Parallel Bars
exercises.overview: A bodyweight core exercise performed on parallel bars or captain's chair that targets the rectus abdominis through hip flexion. This exercise builds functional core strength and improves hip flexor mobility.
exercises.safetyInfo: Avoid this exercise if you have lower back issues or shoulder impingement. Ensure proper grip strength before attempting to prevent falls.

commonMistakes: Pulling with arms instead of crunching with abs
Rotating too early in the movement
Using excessive weight that compromises form
exerciseTips: Keep hands stationary by your ears throughout the movement
Initiate the twist from your core, not your arms
Pause briefly at the bottom of each rep
Control the return to starting position
instructions: Connect a rope attachment to a high pulley cable and position a mat on the floor in front of it.
Grab the rope with both hands and kneel approximately two feet back from the tower.
Position the rope behind your head with your hands by your ears.
Keep your hands in the same place, contract your abs and pull downward on the rope in a crunching movement until your elbows reach your knees.
Pause briefly at the bottom and rise up in a slow and controlled manner until you reach the starting position.
Repeat the same downward movement until you're halfway down, at which time you'll begin rotating one of your elbows to the opposite knee.
Again, pause briefly at the bottom and rise up in a slow and controlled manner until you reach the starting position.
Repeat the same movement as before, but alternate the other elbow to the opposite knee.
Continue this series of movements to failure.
exercises.name: Kneeling Cable Crunch With Alternating Oblique Twists
exercises.name_pt: Abdominal na Polia Ajoelhado com Rotação Oblíqua Alternada
exercises.overview: A compound core exercise that combines traditional cable crunches with oblique rotation. This movement targets both the rectus abdominis and obliques for complete core development.
exercises.safetyInfo: Start with lighter weight to master the movement pattern. Avoid if you have lower back issues or neck problems.

commonMistakes: Allowing elbows to flare out during the movement
Using shoulders instead of isolating triceps
Moving upper arms away from the head
exerciseTips: Keep upper arms stationary against the bench
Maintain elbow position throughout the movement
Press in a controlled semicircular arc
Contract triceps hard at full extension
instructions: Place a bench sideways in front of a high pulley machine.
Hold a straight bar attachment above your head with your hands about 6 inches apart with your palms facing down.
Face away from the machine and kneel.
Place your head and the back of your upper arms on the bench. Your elbows should be bent with the forearms pointing towards the high pulley. This will be your starting position.

While keeping your upper arms close to your head at all times with the elbows in, press the bar out in a semicircular motion until the elbows are locked and your arms are parallel to the floor. Contract the triceps hard and keep this position for a second. Exhale as you perform this movement.
Slowly return to the starting position as you breathe in.
Repeat for the recommended amount of repetitions.
exercises.name: Kneeling Cable Triceps Extension
exercises.name_pt: Extensão de Tríceps na Polia Ajoelhado
exercises.overview: An isolation exercise that targets all three heads of the triceps using cable resistance in a kneeling position. The bench support helps maintain proper form and isolate the triceps effectively.
exercises.safetyInfo: Ensure bench is stable and secure. Use appropriate weight to maintain control throughout the range of motion. Stop if you feel shoulder discomfort.

commonMistakes: Pulling primarily with arms instead of back muscles
Allowing shoulders to round forward at the start
Using momentum instead of controlled movement
exerciseTips: Initiate the pull by retracting your shoulder blades
Keep your elbows high and out to the sides
Focus on squeezing your shoulder blades together at the end
Maintain a strong kneeling position throughout
instructions: Select the appropriate weight using a pulley that is above your head. Attach a rope to the cable and kneel a couple of feet away, holding the rope out in front of you with both arms extended. This will be your starting position.
Initiate the movement by flexing the elbows and fully retracting your shoulders, pulling the rope toward your upper chest with your elbows out.
After pausing briefly, slowly return to the starting position.
exercises.name: Kneeling High Pulley Row
exercises.overview: A compound pulling exercise that targets the latissimus dorsi and supporting back muscles from a kneeling position. This variation provides excellent lat development while minimizing lower back stress.
exercises.safetyInfo: Ensure proper kneeling pad or mat to protect knees. Start with lighter weight to establish proper movement pattern before progressing.
variations: Bent Over Two-Dumbbell Row With Palms In

commonMistakes: Using too much weight and losing control
Not rotating the wrist during the movement
Allowing the torso to rotate or lean
exerciseTips: Rotate your wrist from palm forward to palm facing you
Squeeze your shoulder blade back at the bottom
Keep your core engaged to prevent rotation
Control the weight on the return
instructions: Attach a single handle to a high pulley and make your weight selection.
Kneel in front of the cable tower, taking the cable with one hand with your arm extended. This will be your starting position.

Starting with your palm facing forward, pull the weight down to your torso by flexing the elbow and retract the shoulder blade. As you do so, rotate the wrist so that at the completion of the movement, your palm is now facing you.
After a brief pause, return to the starting position.
exercises.name: Kneeling Single-Arm High Pulley Row
exercises.name_pt: Remada Unilateral na Polia Alta Ajoelhado
exercises.overview: A unilateral pulling exercise that combines lat development with rotational wrist movement. Excellent for addressing muscle imbalances and improving grip strength.
exercises.safetyInfo: Start with lighter weight to master the wrist rotation pattern. Avoid if you have wrist or shoulder impingement issues.
variations: Bent Over Two-Dumbbell Row With Palms In

commonMistakes: Allowing legs to drop during the pulling motion
Bending the knees to make the movement easier
Using momentum instead of controlled movement
exerciseTips: Keep your legs completely straight and parallel to the floor
Engage your core throughout the entire movement
Focus on pulling with your back muscles despite the added core challenge
Maintain the L-sit position during both the ascent and descent
instructions: Hang from a pull-up bar with an overhand grip, hands shoulder-width apart.
Lift your legs straight out in front of you to create an L-shape with your body.
Maintain the L-sit position while pulling your body up toward the bar.
Continue pulling until your chin clears the bar while keeping legs extended.
Lower yourself with control back to the starting L-sit hang position.
exercises.name: L-Sit Pull Up
exercises.overview: L-sit pull-ups combine the pulling strength of a pull-up with the core strength of an L-sit hold. This advanced exercise challenges the entire body and requires exceptional strength in both the lats and core muscles.
exercises.safetyInfo: This extremely advanced variation requires significant core and hip flexor strength. Only attempt after mastering regular pull-ups and L-sits separately. Stop if you experience lower back pain.

commonMistakes: Bending the arms during the rotation
Using only the upper body instead of full-body rotation
Moving too quickly and losing control
exerciseTips: Rotate from your hips and core, not just your arms
Keep your arms extended throughout the movement
Control the weight at both ends of the range
Maintain wide stance for stability
instructions: Position a bar into a landmine or securely anchor it in a corner. Load the bar to an appropriate weight.
Raise the bar from the floor, taking it to shoulder height with both hands with your arms extended in front of you. Adopt a wide stance. This will be your starting position.
Perform the movement by rotating the trunk and hips as you swing the weight all the way down to one side. Keep your arms extended throughout the exercise.
Reverse the motion to swing the weight all the way to the opposite side.
Continue alternating the movement until the set is complete.
exercises.name: Landmine 180's
exercises.overview: A dynamic rotational exercise that builds core strength, power, and stability. Excellent for athletes and anyone looking to improve rotational strength and coordination.
exercises.safetyInfo: Start with light weight to learn the movement pattern. Ensure adequate space around you. Avoid if you have lower back issues or shoulder impingement.

commonMistakes: Not squatting deep enough at the bottom
Pressing with arms only instead of using leg drive
Losing balance during the explosive phase
exerciseTips: Explode up from the squat position powerfully
Drive the weight straight up overhead
Keep your core tight throughout the movement
Land softly from the explosive extension
instructions: Position a bar into landmine or, lacking one, securely anchor it in a corner. Load the bar to an appropriate weight and position the handle attachment on the bar.
Raise the bar from the floor, taking the handles to your shoulders. This will be your starting position.
In an athletic stance, squat by flexing your hips and setting your hips back, keeping your arms flexed.

Reverse the motion by powerfully extending through the hips, knees, and ankles, while also extending the elbows to straighten the arms. This movement should be done explosively, coming out of the squat to full extension as powerfully as possible.
Return to the starting position.
exercises.name: Landmine Linear Jammer
exercises.overview: A powerful full-body exercise combining a squat with an explosive overhead press. Develops total-body power, coordination, and functional strength for athletic performance.
exercises.safetyInfo: This is an explosive full-body movement requiring good mobility and coordination. Start light and master the timing. Avoid if you have shoulder or knee issues.

commonMistakes: Pressing straight up instead of following the natural landmine arc
Allowing excessive back extension during the press
Using too much weight before mastering the movement pattern
exerciseTips: Press the weight up and slightly forward following the natural arc of the landmine
Engage your core to prevent excessive back extension
Keep your wrist straight and aligned throughout the movement
Control the weight on both the lifting and lowering phases
instructions: Secure one end of a barbell in a landmine attachment or corner, load the other end with weight plates.
Stand facing the weighted end and grip the barbell with one hand at chest level.
Position your feet in a staggered stance with the opposite foot forward for stability.
Press the barbell up and slightly forward in an arc motion until your arm is fully extended.
Pause briefly at the top, then lower the weight back to the starting position at chest level.
Complete all reps on one arm before switching to the other side.
exercises.name: Landmine Press
exercises.overview: The landmine press is a shoulder-dominant pressing exercise that follows a natural arc motion, making it joint-friendly while effectively building overhead strength. The angled press path reduces stress on the shoulder joint compared to traditional overhead presses.
exercises.safetyInfo: Start with lighter weight to learn the unique movement pattern. Those with shoulder impingement may find this more comfortable than traditional overhead pressing due to the angled press path.

commonMistakes: Allowing the back to round during the rowing motion
Using momentum to swing the weight up
Pulling the bar too high toward the neck instead of chest
exerciseTips: Keep your chest up and maintain a neutral spine throughout
Focus on pulling your elbows back rather than just lifting the weight
Squeeze your shoulder blades together at the top of the movement
Control the negative portion of each repetition
instructions: Set up a barbell in a landmine attachment or corner with weight plates on the free end.
Straddle the barbell with feet shoulder-width apart and hinge at the hips to lean forward.
Grasp the end of the barbell with both hands using an overhand or neutral grip.
Pull the barbell up toward your chest, leading with your elbows and squeezing your shoulder blades.
Lower the barbell back to the starting position with control.
exercises.name: Landmine Row
exercises.overview: The Landmine Row is a versatile rowing exercise that allows for heavy loading while providing a unique angle of pull. It develops lat strength and thickness while challenging core stability.
exercises.safetyInfo: This exercise is generally safe but requires proper hip hinge mechanics to protect the lower back. Those with lower back issues should be cautious with the bent-over position.

commonMistakes: Pressing straight up instead of following the landmine's natural arc
Allowing the torso to lean back excessively during the press
Using too much weight and compromising shoulder positioning
exerciseTips: Keep your core engaged throughout the entire movement
Follow the natural arc of the landmine rather than pressing straight up
Maintain a slight forward lean to optimize pressing angle
Control the eccentric portion to maximize muscle engagement
instructions: Set up a barbell in a landmine attachment or secure one end in a corner.
Stand facing away from the anchor point holding the free end of the barbell at shoulder height.
Position your feet shoulder-width apart with a slight forward lean.
Press the barbell up and forward following the natural arc of the landmine.
Pause briefly at the top with arms extended.
Lower the weight under control back to the starting position at shoulder level.
exercises.name: Landmine Shoulder Press
exercises.overview: The landmine shoulder press is a unilateral pressing movement that targets the shoulders through an angled plane of motion. This exercise provides a shoulder-friendly alternative to traditional overhead pressing while building functional pressing strength.
exercises.safetyInfo: Avoid this exercise if you have shoulder impingement or rotator cuff injuries. The angled pressing motion may be easier on the shoulders than vertical pressing for some individuals.

commonMistakes: Allowing the barbell to drift away from the chest
Knees caving inward during the descent or ascent
Rounding the back or leaning too far forward
exerciseTips: Keep the barbell pressed firmly against your chest throughout the movement
Maintain an upright torso by engaging your core
Drive your knees out in line with your toes
Focus on sitting back into your hips rather than just bending your knees
instructions: Secure one end of a barbell in a landmine attachment or corner and load the opposite end with weight.
Stand facing the weighted end and grasp it with both hands at chest level, holding it close to your body.
Position your feet shoulder-width apart with toes slightly turned out.
Initiate the squat by pushing your hips back and bending your knees while keeping the barbell pressed against your chest.
Lower until your thighs are parallel to the floor or as deep as mobility allows.
Drive through your heels to return to the starting position.
exercises.name: Landmine Squat
exercises.overview: The landmine squat is a beginner-friendly squat variation that uses an angled barbell to provide front-loading while maintaining an easier upright posture. It's excellent for learning proper squat mechanics while building leg strength.
exercises.safetyInfo: Those with lower back injuries should use caution and consider reducing the load. Ensure the landmine setup is secure before beginning. Stop if you experience knee or hip pain.

commonMistakes: Allowing feet to touch the ground or bench during the movement
Arching back excessively to compensate for lack of leg drive
Using too much weight without adjusting for reduced stability
exerciseTips: Keep feet consistently elevated throughout all repetitions
Focus on slower, more controlled movement without leg drive assistance
Maintain tight core to stabilize the body without foot contact
Emphasize proper bar path directly over chest
instructions: Lie on bench with feet elevated off the ground, pulled toward glutes to eliminate leg drive.
Grip barbell with hands slightly wider than shoulder-width and retract shoulder blades.
Unrack the bar and position it over chest with arms fully extended.
Lower bar with control to chest while maintaining feet position and core stability.
Press bar back to starting position using only upper body strength without leg assistance.
exercises.name: Larsen Press
exercises.overview: The Larsen press removes leg drive by keeping feet elevated, forcing the chest, shoulders, and triceps to work harder while improving core stability. This variation increases upper body isolation and addresses bench press weak points.
exercises.safetyInfo: Requires good core stability and shoulder health. Start with significantly less weight than regular bench press due to reduced stability and power output.

commonMistakes: Allowing the knee of the working leg to cave inward
Bending the straight leg instead of keeping it extended
Not sitting back enough into the hip of the working leg
exerciseTips: Keep the non-working leg completely straight throughout the movement
Maintain weight on the heel of your working leg
Keep your chest up and avoid rounding your back
Step wide enough to achieve proper depth without knee discomfort
instructions: Stand with feet hip-width apart and hands clasped in front of your chest.
Take a large step to the right, keeping your left leg straight.
Sit back into your right hip and lower down until your right thigh is parallel to the ground.
Keep your chest up, back straight, and weight on your right heel.
Push through your right heel to return to the starting position and repeat on the left side.
exercises.name: Lateral Lunge
exercises.overview: The lateral lunge is a side-stepping exercise that works the legs in the frontal plane of movement. It targets the quadriceps while improving hip mobility and strengthening the inner thigh muscles.
exercises.safetyInfo: This exercise requires good hip mobility and may challenge those with limited adductor flexibility. Those with groin or hip injuries should start with smaller steps and partial range of motion.

commonMistakes: Lifting arms too high above shoulder level
Using momentum or swinging the arms
Allowing shoulders to shrug up toward ears
exerciseTips: Keep a slight bend in your elbows throughout
Lead with your pinkies as you lift
Control the descent against band resistance
Stop when arms reach parallel to floor
instructions: To begin, stand on an exercise band so that tension begins at arm's length. Grasp the handles using a pronated (palms facing your thighs) grip that is slightly less than shoulder width. The handles should be resting on the sides of your thighs. Your arms should be extended with a slight bend at the elbows and your back should be straight. This will be your starting position.

Use your side shoulders to lift the handles to the sides as you exhale. Continue to lift the handles until they are slightly above parallel. Tip: As you lift the handles, slightly tilt the hand as if you were pouring water and keep your arms extended. Also, keep your torso stationary and pause for a second at the top of the movement.
Lower the handles back down slowly to the starting position. Inhale as you perform this portion of the movement.
Repeat for the recommended amount of repetitions.
exercises.name: Lateral Raise - With Bands
exercises.overview: Band lateral raises target the lateral deltoids while providing variable resistance throughout the range of motion. This exercise builds shoulder width and improves shoulder stability.
exercises.safetyInfo: Avoid if you have shoulder impingement or rotator cuff injuries. Start with lighter resistance and progress gradually to prevent shoulder strain.

commonMistakes: Setting the pad too high on the shins
Using momentum to swing the weight up
Allowing knees to go past 90 degrees at bottom
exerciseTips: Adjust pad to sit just above your ankles
Keep your back pressed against the seat
Pause briefly at the top of each rep
Control the weight on the way down
instructions: For this exercise you will need to use a leg extension machine. First choose your weight and sit on the machine with your legs under the pad (feet pointed forward) and the hands holding the side bars. This will be your starting position. Tip: You will need to adjust the pad so that it falls on top of your lower leg (just above your feet). Also, make sure that your legs form a 90-degree angle between the lower and upper leg. If the angle is less than 90-degrees then that means the knee is over the toes which in turn creates undue stress at the knee joint. If the machine is designed that way, either look for another machine or just make sure that when you start executing the exercise you stop going down once you hit the 90-degree angle.
Using your quadriceps, extend your legs to the maximum as you exhale. Ensure that the rest of the body remains stationary on the seat. Pause a second on the contracted position.
Slowly lower the weight back to the original position as you inhale, ensuring that you do not go past the 90-degree angle limit.
Repeat for the recommended amount of times.
exercises.name: Leg Extensions
exercises.overview: Leg extensions isolate the quadriceps muscles through knee extension movement. This machine exercise is effective for building quad strength and muscle definition.
exercises.safetyInfo: Avoid if you have knee pain or patellar issues. This exercise places significant stress on the knee joint, especially at the bottom position. Use appropriate weight.

commonMistakes: Using momentum to swing the leg up
Leaning forward excessively
Lifting the leg too high causing back arch
exerciseTips: Keep your torso upright and avoid leaning forward
Squeeze your glutes at the top of the movement
Control the descent to maximize muscle engagement
Keep the standing leg slightly bent for stability
instructions: While standing up straight with both feet next to each other at around shoulder width, grab a sturdy surface such as the sides of a squat rack or the top of a chair to brace yourself and keep balance.
With or without an ankle weight, lift one leg behind you as if performing a leg curl but standing up while keeping the other leg straight. Breathe out as you perform this movement.
Slowly bring the raised leg back to the floor as you breathe in.
Repeat for the recommended amount of repetitions.
Repeat the movement with the opposite leg.
exercises.name: Leg Lift
exercises.overview: A bodyweight isolation exercise that targets the glutes and hamstrings while improving hip stability and single-leg balance.
exercises.safetyInfo: Avoid this exercise if you have lower back issues. Stop if you feel pain in the lumbar spine or hip joint.

commonMistakes: Locking knees completely at the top
Allowing knees to cave inward
Placing feet too high or too low on the platform
exerciseTips: Keep your knees aligned with your toes
Press through your heels rather than toes
Maintain tension throughout the entire range of motion
Control the descent to avoid bouncing at the bottom
instructions: Using a leg press machine, sit down on the machine and place your legs on the platform directly in front of you at a medium (shoulder width) foot stance. (Note: For the purposes of this discussion we will use the medium stance described above which targets overall development; however you can choose any of the three stances described in the foot positioning section).

Lower the safety bars holding the weighted platform in place and press the platform all the way up until your legs are fully extended in front of you. Tip: Make sure that you do not lock your knees. Your torso and the legs should make a perfect 90-degree angle. This will be your starting position.
As you inhale, slowly lower the platform until your upper and lower legs make a 90-degree angle.
Pushing mainly with the heels of your feet and using the quadriceps go back to the starting position as you exhale.
Repeat for the recommended amount of repetitions and ensure to lock the safety pins properly once you are done. You do not want that platform falling on you fully loaded.
exercises.name: Leg Press
exercises.overview: A compound machine exercise that safely targets the quadriceps while allowing for heavy loads and reduced spinal stress compared to squats.
exercises.safetyInfo: Never fully lock out your knees as this can cause joint stress. Always engage safety stops and never let the platform drop uncontrolled.

commonMistakes: Using momentum to swing legs up
Lifting the lower back off the ground
Moving too quickly through the range of motion
exerciseTips: Focus on pulling your knees toward your chest
Keep your lower back pressed against the floor
Control both the lifting and lowering phases
Exhale as you pull your knees up
instructions: Lie on an exercise mat with your legs extended and your hands either palms facing down next to you or under your glutes. Tip: My preference is with the hands next to me. This will be your starting position.

Bend your knees and pull your upper thighs into your midsection as you breathe out. Continue the motion until your knees are around chest level. Contract your abs as you execute this movement and hold for a second at the top. Tip: As you perform the motion, the lower legs (calves) should always remain parallel to the floor.
Return to the starting position as you inhale.
Repeat for the recommended amount of repetitions.
exercises.name: Leg Pull-In
exercises.overview: A bodyweight abdominal exercise that targets the rectus abdominis through spinal flexion while lying supine.
exercises.safetyInfo: Avoid if you have lower back pain. Keep movements controlled to prevent strain on the hip flexors.

commonMistakes: Allowing the elbow to flare too wide
Pressing the weight at an angle
Losing stability due to poor leg positioning
exerciseTips: Keep the kettlebell directly above your elbow
Press straight up rather than toward your feet
Maintain tension in your core throughout
Use your free arm for stability
instructions: Lie on the floor with one kettlebell in place on your chest, holding it by the handle. Extend leg on working side over leg on non-working side.Your free arm can be extended out to your side for support.
Press the kettlebll into a locked out position.
Lower the weight until the elbow touches the ground, keeping the kettlebell above the elbow. Repeat for the desired number of repetitions.
exercises.name: Leg-Over Floor Press
exercises.name_pt: Supino no Chão com Perna Cruzada
exercises.overview: A unilateral floor press variation using kettlebells that challenges core stability while targeting the chest, shoulders, and triceps.
exercises.safetyInfo: Start with lighter weight to master the stability demands. Ensure the kettlebell handle is secure in your grip.

commonMistakes: Allowing shoulders to roll forward
Pressing too high toward the ceiling
Using partial range of motion
exerciseTips: Retract your shoulder blades before pressing
Keep your core engaged throughout the movement
Press in a straight line forward
Control the weight on both pressing and returning phases
instructions: Load an appropriate weight onto the pins and adjust the seat for your height. The handles should be near the bottom or middle of the pectorals at the beginning of the motion.
Your chest and head should be up and your shoulder blades retracted. This will be your starting position.
Press the handles forward by extending through the elbow.
After a brief pause at the top, return the weight just above the start position, keeping tension on the muscles by not returning the weight to the stops until the set is complete.
exercises.name: Leverage Chest Press
exercises.overview: A machine-based compound exercise that safely targets the chest muscles while providing stability and allowing for progressive overload.
exercises.safetyInfo: Adjust the seat height properly to align handles with mid-chest. Avoid locking elbows completely to reduce joint stress.

commonMistakes: Allowing knees to cave inward during the lift
Rounding the lower back under load
Not achieving full hip extension at the top
exerciseTips: Drive through your heels to initiate the lift
Keep your chest up and shoulders back throughout the movement
Squeeze your glutes at the top of the movement
Control the negative portion of the lift
instructions: Load the pins to an appropriate weight. Position yourself directly between the handles. Grasp the bottom handles with a comfortable grip, and then lower your hips as you take a breath. Look forward with your head and keep your chest up. This will be your starting position.
Return the weight to the starting position.
exercises.name: Leverage Deadlift
exercises.overview: The leverage deadlift is a machine-based variation that provides a stable platform for learning deadlift mechanics while emphasizing quadriceps development. It's excellent for beginners or those recovering from injury.
exercises.safetyInfo: Avoid if you have knee or lower back injuries. Start with lighter weight to master the movement pattern before progressing.

commonMistakes: Pressing straight up instead of following the natural arc
Allowing shoulders to roll forward during the press
Using momentum instead of controlled movement
exerciseTips: Retract your shoulder blades and keep them pinned
Press in a slight arc toward your lower chest
Control the eccentric phase for 2-3 seconds
Stop just short of full lockout to maintain tension
instructions: Load an appropriate weight onto the pins and adjust the seat for your height. The handles should be near the bottom of the pectorals at the beginning of the motion. Your chest and head should be up and your shoulder blades retracted. This will be your starting position.
Press the handles forward by extending through the elbow.
After a brief pause at the top, return the weight just above the start position, keeping tension on the muscles by not returning the weight to the stops until the set is complete.
exercises.name: Leverage Decline Chest Press
exercises.overview: The leverage decline chest press targets the lower portion of the pectoral muscles while providing machine stability. It's ideal for building chest mass and strength safely.
exercises.safetyInfo: Ensure proper seat adjustment to avoid shoulder impingement. Those with shoulder injuries should use lighter weight and focus on form.

commonMistakes: Leaning back excessively to assist the pull
Using arms primarily instead of back muscles
Allowing shoulders to round forward at the start position
exerciseTips: Pull your elbows back behind your torso
Squeeze your shoulder blades together at the peak contraction
Keep your torso upright throughout the movement
Focus on pulling with your back muscles, not your arms
instructions: Load an appropriate weight onto the pins and adjust the seat height so that you can just reach the handles above you. Adjust the knee pad to help keep you down. Grasp the handles with a pronated grip. This will be your starting position.
Pull the handles towards your torso, retracting your shoulder blades as you flex the elbow.
Pause at the bottom of the motion, and then slowly return the handles to the starting position.
For multiple repetitions, avoid completely returning the weight to the stops to keep tension on the muscles being worked.
exercises.name: Leverage High Row
exercises.overview: The leverage high row effectively targets the middle traps and rhomboids for improved posture and upper back strength. The machine provides stability for focused muscle activation.
exercises.safetyInfo: Adjust seat height properly to ensure natural pulling angle. Avoid jerky movements that could strain the lower back or shoulders.
variations: Bent Over Two-Dumbbell Row With Palms In

commonMistakes: Setting the seat too high or too low
Pressing straight up instead of following the incline angle
Bouncing the weight off the chest at the bottom
exerciseTips: Press up and slightly inward toward the center of your chest
Maintain a slight arch in your lower back
Keep your feet firmly planted on the floor
Pause briefly at the top without locking out completely
instructions: Load an appropriate weight onto the pins and adjust the seat for your height. The handles should be near the top of the pectorals at the beginning of the motion. Your chest and head should be up and your shoulder blades retracted. This will be your starting position.
Press the handles forward by extending through the elbow.
After a brief pause at the top, return the weight just above the start position, keeping tension on the muscles by not returning the weight to the stops until the set is complete.
exercises.name: Leverage Incline Chest Press
exercises.overview: The leverage incline chest press specifically targets the upper chest fibers while providing machine safety and stability. It's excellent for building upper body pressing strength.
exercises.safetyInfo: Proper seat adjustment is crucial to avoid shoulder strain. Start with lighter weight to establish proper movement pattern.

commonMistakes: Rotating the torso to assist the pulling motion
Pulling with the biceps instead of the lats
Not achieving full range of motion at the back
exerciseTips: Pull your elbow back past your torso
Initiate the movement by retracting your shoulder blade
Keep your torso stable and avoid rotation
Focus on squeezing your lat at the peak contraction
instructions: Load an appropriate weight onto the pins and adjust the seat height so that the handles are at chest level. Grasp the handles with either a neutral or pronated grip. This will be your starting position.
Pull the handles towards your torso, retracting your shoulder blades as you flex the elbow.
