Pause at the bottom of the motion, and then slowly return the handles to the starting position. For multiple repetitions, avoid completely returning the weight to the stops to keep tension on the muscles being worked.
exercises.name: Leverage Iso Row
exercises.overview: The leverage iso row allows for unilateral lat development while providing machine stability. It's effective for correcting strength imbalances and building back width.
exercises.safetyInfo: Ensure proper seat height to maintain natural pulling mechanics. Avoid using momentum which can strain the lower back.
variations: Bent Over Two-Dumbbell Row With Palms In

commonMistakes: Pressing behind the neck instead of in front
Using excessive lower back arch to assist the press
Lowering the handles too far below shoulder level
exerciseTips: Press straight up without arching your lower back excessively
Keep your core engaged throughout the movement
Lower the weight under control to just above shoulder level
Drive through your palms, not your fingertips
instructions: Load an appropriate weight onto the pins and adjust the seat for your height. The handles should be near the top of the shoulders at the beginning of the motion. Your chest and head should be up and handles held with a pronated grip. This will be your starting position.
Press the handles upward by extending through the elbow.
After a brief pause at the top, return the weight to just above the start position, keeping tension on the muscles by not returning the weight to the stops until the set is complete.
exercises.name: Leverage Shoulder Press
exercises.overview: The leverage shoulder press provides a safe and stable platform for building overhead pressing strength and shoulder development. It's ideal for beginners learning pressing mechanics.
exercises.safetyInfo: Avoid pressing behind the neck as this can cause shoulder impingement. Those with shoulder injuries should use reduced range of motion and lighter weight.

commonMistakes: Rolling shoulders backward or forward instead of straight up
Using arms to pull rather than isolating the traps
Rushing through the movement without pause at top
exerciseTips: Keep shoulders directly over hips throughout the movement
Drive through heels while maintaining straight arms
Hold peak contraction for 1-2 seconds at the top
Lower weight slowly under control
instructions: Load the pins to an appropriate weight. Position yourself directly between the handles.
Grasp the top handles with a comfortable grip, and then lower your hips as you take a breath. Look forward with your head and keep your chest up.

Drive through the floor with your heels, extending your hips and knees as you rise to a standing position. Keep your arms straight throughout the movement, finishing with your shoulders back. This will be your starting position.
Raise the weight by shrugging the shoulders towards your ears, moving straight up and down.
Pause at the top of the motion, and then return the weight to the starting position.
exercises.name: Leverage Shrug
exercises.overview: The leverage shrug is a machine-based isolation exercise that targets the upper trapezius muscles by elevating the shoulders against resistance. It provides stable support for heavy loading while minimizing lower back stress.
exercises.safetyInfo: Avoid excessive weight that causes shoulder rolling or compensatory movements. Those with shoulder impingement should use lighter loads.

commonMistakes: Allowing the elbows to flare too wide during the press
Pressing the bar forward instead of straight up
Failing to maintain core stability during the overhead position
exerciseTips: Keep your elbows slightly forward throughout the press
Drive your head through once the bar clears your face
Maintain a tight core throughout the entire movement
Use the neutral grip to engage your triceps more effectively
instructions: Clean the barbell to your chest using a neutral grip with hands closer than shoulder-width apart.
Position the bar against your upper chest with elbows slightly forward.
Take a deep breath and brace your core while maintaining an upright torso.
Press the bar straight up overhead by extending your arms and driving through your shoulders.
Lower the bar back to the starting position at your chest with control.
exercises.name: Log Press
exercises.overview: The Log Press is a strongman exercise that challenges overhead pressing strength with a neutral grip and thicker implement. It develops functional pressing power and shoulder stability through a different movement pattern than standard presses.
exercises.safetyInfo: This advanced exercise requires significant shoulder stability and mobility. Avoid if you have shoulder impingement or limited overhead mobility. Start with lighter weight to master the movement pattern.

commonMistakes: Allowing hips to sag or pike during movement
Moving hands too quickly without control
Using primarily arms instead of back muscles
exerciseTips: Maintain rigid body position throughout movement
Control hand-over-hand descent slowly
Keep core engaged to prevent sagging
Focus on pulling with lats rather than arms
instructions: Attach a climbing rope to a high beam or cross member. Below it, ensure that the smith machine bar is locked in place with the safeties and cannot move. Alternatively, a secure box could also be utilized.
Stand on the bar, using the rope to balance yourself. This will be your starting position.
Keeping your body straight, lean back and lower your body by slowly going hand over hand with the rope. Continue until you are perpendicular to the ground.
Keeping your body straight, reverse the motion, going hand over hand back to the starting position.
exercises.name: London Bridges
exercises.overview: London Bridges is an advanced bodyweight exercise using a rope to perform inverted rows at various angles. It builds significant lat strength, grip endurance, and core stability.
exercises.safetyInfo: Ensure rope and anchor point can support full body weight. Check equipment regularly for wear. Have spotter available for safety.
variations: Bent Over Two-Dumbbell Row With Palms In

commonMistakes: Using too much weight causing form breakdown
Allowing elbows to bend excessively during movement
exerciseTips: Maintain slight bend in elbows throughout
Squeeze chest muscles at the top
Control the negative portion
Keep core engaged for stability
instructions: To move into the starting position, place the pulleys at the low position, select the resistance to be used and grasp a handle in each hand.
Step forward, gaining tension in the pulleys. Your palms should be facing forward, hands below the waist, and your arms straight. This will be your starting position.
With a slight bend in your arms, draw your hands upward and toward the midline of your body. Your hands should come together in front of your chest, palms facing up.
Return your arms back to the starting position after a brief pause.
exercises.name: Low Cable Crossover
exercises.overview: A cable isolation exercise targeting the upper chest by bringing handles from low to high in an arc motion. Excellent for chest development and definition.
exercises.safetyInfo: Start with lighter weight to establish proper form. Avoid excessive shoulder extension at the bottom position.

commonMistakes: Allowing elbows to flare out or move during exercise
Using momentum instead of controlled movement
exerciseTips: Keep elbows stationary throughout movement
Focus on squeezing triceps at full extension
Control the weight on the return
Maintain neutral wrist position
instructions: Select the desired weight and lay down face up on the bench of a seated row machine that has a rope attached to it. Your head should be pointing towards the attachment.
Grab the outside of the rope ends with your palms facing each other (neutral grip).

Position your elbows so that they are bent at a 90 degree angle and your upper arms are perpendicular (90 degree angle) to your torso. Tip: Keep the elbows in and make sure that the upper arms point to the ceiling while your forearms point towards the pulley above your head. This will be your starting position.

As you breathe out, extend your lower arms until they are straight and vertical. The upper arms and elbows remain stationary throughout the movement. Only the forearms should move. Contract the triceps hard for a second.
As you breathe in slowly return to the starting position.
Repeat for the recommended amount of repetitions.
exercises.name: Low Cable Triceps Extension
exercises.name_pt: Extensão de Tríceps na Polia Baixa
exercises.overview: A lying cable triceps isolation exercise using rope attachment. Provides constant tension throughout the range of motion for triceps development.
exercises.safetyInfo: Use appropriate weight to maintain elbow stability. Avoid locking elbows aggressively at extension.

commonMistakes: Allowing torso to rock backward during pull
Pulling with arms instead of initiating with rear delts
exerciseTips: Keep elbows high and parallel to floor
Pull rope apart as you draw to neck
Maintain upright torso position
Focus on squeezing shoulder blades together
instructions: Sit on a low pulley row machine with a rope attachment.

Grab the ends of the rope using a palms-down grip and sit with your back straight and your knees slightly bent. Tip: Keep your back almost completely vertical and your arms fully extended in front of you. This will be your starting position.

While keeping your torso stationary, lift your elbows and start bending them as you pull the rope towards your neck while exhaling. Throughout the movement your upper arms should remain parallel to the floor. Tip: Continue this motion until your hands are almost next to your ears (the forearms will not be parallel to the floor at the end of the movement as they will be angled a bit upwards) and your elbows are out away from your sides.
After holding for a second or so at the contracted position, come back slowly to the starting position as you inhale. Tip: Again, during no part of the movement should the torso move.
Repeat for the recommended amount of repetitions.
exercises.name: Low Pulley Row To Neck
exercises.name_pt: Remada na Polia Baixa até o Pescoço
exercises.overview: A seated cable exercise targeting rear deltoids and upper back muscles. Essential for shoulder health and posture improvement.
exercises.safetyInfo: Maintain proper posture to avoid lower back strain. Use controlled movements to prevent shoulder impingement.
variations: Bent Over Two-Dumbbell Row With Palms In

commonMistakes: Leaning forward excessively during the lunge
Allowing the back knee to slam into the ground
Rushing the kettlebell pass and losing control
exerciseTips: Keep torso upright throughout the movement
Pass the kettlebell smoothly under your front thigh
Drive through your front heel to return to standing
Maintain control of the kettlebell during the pass
instructions: Stand with your torso upright holding a kettlebell in your right hand. This will be your starting position.
Step forward with your left foot and lower your upper body down by flexing the hip and the knee, keeping the torso upright. Lower your back knee until it nearly touches the ground.
As you lunge, pass the kettlebell under your front leg to your opposite hand.
Pressing through the heel of your foot, return to the starting position.
Repeat the movement for the recommended amount of repetitions, alternating legs.
exercises.name: Lunge Pass Through
exercises.overview: A dynamic lunge variation that adds coordination and core stability challenges by passing a kettlebell between hands under the front leg. Excellent for functional strength and athletic development.
exercises.safetyInfo: Start with a lighter kettlebell to master the coordination. Avoid this exercise if you have knee injuries or balance issues.

commonMistakes: Landing too hard on the back leg
Allowing the barbell to shift or bounce on shoulders
Not achieving full depth before exploding up
exerciseTips: Drive explosively through your front heel
Land softly to absorb impact
Keep the barbell stable on your shoulders
Maintain upright torso position throughout
instructions: Adjust a bar in a Smith machine to an appropriate height. Position yourself under the bar, racking it across the back of your shoulders. Unrack the bar, and then split your feet, moving one foot forward and one foot back. This will be your starting position.
Lower your back knee nearly to the ground, flexing the knees and lowering your hips as you do so.

At the bottom of the descent, immediately reverse direction. Explosively drive through the heel of your front foot with light pressure from your back foot. Jump up and reverse the position of your legs.
Repeat for the recommended amount of repetitions.
exercises.name: Lunge Sprint
exercises.overview: An explosive plyometric exercise that develops power and athleticism by combining lunges with jumping leg switches. Great for sports performance and lower body power development.
exercises.safetyInfo: This high-impact exercise requires good knee health and jumping mechanics. Start with bodyweight before adding load. Ensure proper Smith machine setup and safety stops.

commonMistakes: Allowing elbows to flare out from the body
Using momentum to swing the weight up
Not maintaining constant tension on the biceps
exerciseTips: Keep upper arms stationary throughout the movement
Squeeze biceps at the top of the curl
Control the negative portion of the lift
Maintain tension on the cable at the bottom
instructions: Grab a straight bar or E-Z bar attachment that is attached to the low pulley with both hands, using an underhand (palms facing up) shoulder-width grip.
Lie flat on your back on top of an exercise mat in front of the weight stack with your feet flat against the frame of the pulley machine and your legs straight.
With your arms extended and your elbows close to your body slightly bend your arms. This will be your starting position.
While keeping your upper arms stationary and the elbows close to your body, curl the bar up slowly toward your chest as you breathe out and you squeeze the biceps.
After a second squeeze at the top of the movement, slowly return to the starting position.
Repeat for the recommended amount of repetitions.
exercises.name: Lying Cable Curl
exercises.overview: A unique bicep isolation exercise performed lying down with a cable system. The horizontal position provides constant tension and eliminates momentum, making it highly effective for bicep development.
exercises.safetyInfo: Ensure proper positioning to avoid back strain. Use appropriate weight to maintain control throughout the range of motion.

commonMistakes: Allowing elbows to flare too wide
Using the legs to help lift the weight
Not achieving full range of motion at the top
exerciseTips: Keep elbows close to your body throughout the movement
Squeeze shoulder blades together at the top
Control the weight on both the lifting and lowering phases
Choose your target area - chest for upper back, stomach for lats
instructions: Place a cambered bar underneath an exercise bench.
Lie face down on the exercise bench and grab the bar using a palms down (pronated grip) that is wider than shoulder width. This will be your starting position.
As you exhale row the bar up as you keep the elbows close to your body to either your chest, in order to target the upper mid back, or to your stomach if targeting the lats is your goal.
After a second hold at the top, lower back down to the starting position slowly as you inhale.
exercises.name: Lying Cambered Barbell Row
exercises.overview: A chest-supported rowing variation using a cambered bar that eliminates lower back involvement. Excellent for targeting the middle back muscles with strict form and heavy loads.
exercises.safetyInfo: Ensure the cambered bar is properly secured under the bench. The prone position eliminates lower back stress but may be uncomfortable for those with breathing issues.
variations: Bent Over Two-Dumbbell Row With Palms In

commonMistakes: Moving the upper arms during the curl
Not achieving full range of motion to the chin
Using too much weight and compromising form
exerciseTips: Keep upper arms perpendicular to your torso
Curl in a smooth semicircular arc to your chin
Squeeze biceps at the fully contracted position
Maintain constant tension throughout the movement
instructions: Place a flat bench in front of a high pulley or lat pulldown machine.
Hold the straight bar attachment using an underhand grip (palms up) that is about shoulder width.
Lie on your back with your head over the end of the bench.
Now extend your arms straight above your shoulders. Your torso and your arms should make a 90-degree angle and the elbows should be in. This will be your starting position.

As you breathe out, curl the bar down in a semicircular motion until it touches your chin. Squeeze the biceps for a second at the top contracted position. Tip: As you execute this motion only the forearms should move. At no time should the upper arms be moving at all. They are to remain perpendicular throughout the movement.
Return to starting position slowly.
Repeat for the recommended amount of repetitions.
exercises.name: Lying Close-Grip Bar Curl On High Pulley
exercises.name_pt: Rosca Deitado na Polia Alta - Pegada Fechada
exercises.overview: An advanced bicep isolation exercise using a high pulley system while lying supine. The unique angle provides constant tension and targets the biceps from an overhead position for complete development.
exercises.safetyInfo: Position the bench securely and ensure adequate clearance for the movement. Start with lighter weight to master the unique angle and movement pattern.

commonMistakes: Allowing elbows to flare out during the movement
Using momentum to swing the weight up
Lowering the bar too quickly without control
exerciseTips: Keep elbows tucked in and stationary throughout the movement
Control the descent slowly to maximize muscle tension
Focus on squeezing triceps at the top of each rep
Maintain a slight arch in your back for stability
instructions: While holding a barbell or EZ Curl bar with a pronated grip (palms facing forward), lie on your back on a flat bench with your head close to the end of the bench. Tip: If you are holding a barbell grab it using a shoulder-width grip and if you are using an E-Z Bar grab it on the inner handles.

Extend your arms in front of you and slowly bring the bar back in a semi circular motion (while keeping the arms extended) to a position over your head. At the end of this step your arms should be overhead and parallel to the floor. This will be your starting position. Tip: Keep your elbows in at all times.
As you inhale, lower the bar by bending at the elbows and while keeping the upper arm stationary. Keep lowering the bar until your forearms are perpendicular to the floor.

As you exhale bring the bar back up to the starting position by pushing the bar up in a semi-circular motion until the lower arms are also parallel to the floor. Contract the triceps hard at the top of the movement for a second. Tip: Again, only the forearms should move. The upper arms should remain stationary at all times.
Repeat for the recommended amount of repetitions.
exercises.name: Lying Close-Grip Barbell Triceps Extension Behind The Head
exercises.name_pt: Extensão de Tríceps Deitado com Barra Atrás da Cabeça - Pegada Fechada
exercises.overview: This isolation exercise targets all three heads of the triceps by extending the forearms from an overhead position. The movement pattern emphasizes the long head of the triceps due to the overhead arm position.
exercises.safetyInfo: This exercise places significant stress on the elbow joints. Individuals with elbow or shoulder injuries should avoid this movement or use very light weight with proper supervision.

commonMistakes: Moving upper arms during the exercise
Touching the chin too forcefully with the bar
Using grip that's too wide for tricep emphasis
exerciseTips: Keep upper arms stationary throughout the movement
Lower the bar in a controlled arc to your chin
Maintain tight grip on the bar
Focus on triceps contraction at the top
instructions: While holding a barbell or EZ Curl bar with a pronated grip (palms facing forward), lie on your back on a flat bench with your head off the end of the bench. Tip: If you are holding a barbell grab it using a shoulder-width grip and if you are using an E-Z Bar grab it on the inner handles.
Extend your arms in front of you as you hold the barbell over your chest. The arms should be perpendicular to your torso (90-degree angle). This will be your starting position.
As you inhale, lower the bar in a semi-circular motion by bending at the elbows and while keeping the upper arm stationary and elbows in. Keep lowering the bar until it lightly touches your chin.

As you exhale bring the bar back up to the starting position by pushing the bar up in a semi-circular motion. Contract the triceps hard at the top of the movement for a second. Tip: Again, only the forearms should move. The upper arms should remain stationary at all times.
Repeat for the recommended amount of repetitions.
exercises.name: Lying Close-Grip Barbell Triceps Press To Chin
exercises.name_pt: Tríceps Deitado com Barra até o Queixo - Pegada Fechada
exercises.overview: This triceps isolation exercise combines elements of a close-grip press with the arc motion of an extension. The chin acts as a reference point for consistent range of motion.
exercises.safetyInfo: Be careful not to hit your chin with the bar. Use a controlled motion and consider having a spotter when using heavier weights.

commonMistakes: Allowing elbows to flare out excessively
Lowering dumbbells past the ears creating shoulder stress
Using dumbbells that are too heavy compromising form
exerciseTips: Keep elbows pointing forward and tucked in
Lower dumbbells slowly until they reach ear level
Squeeze triceps hard at the top of each rep
Maintain neutral wrist position throughout
instructions: Lie on a flat bench while holding two dumbbells directly in front of you. Your arms should be fully extended at a 90-degree angle from your torso and the floor. The palms should be facing in and the elbows should be tucked in. This is the starting position.
As you breathe in and you keep the upper arms stationary with the elbows in, slowly lower the weight until the dumbbells are near your ears.
At that point, while keeping the elbows in and the upper arms stationary, use the triceps to bring the weight back up to the starting position as you breathe out.
Repeat for the recommended amount of repetitions.
exercises.name: Lying Dumbbell Tricep Extension
exercises.overview: This isolation exercise effectively targets all three heads of the triceps using dumbbells for independent arm training. The lying position provides stability while allowing for a full range of motion.
exercises.safetyInfo: Control the weight carefully to avoid dropping dumbbells on your face or chest. Start with lighter weight to master the movement pattern before progressing.

commonMistakes: Using too much weight initially
Moving through the range too quickly
Not securing the plate properly on the head
exerciseTips: Start with very light weight to assess tolerance
Move slowly through the full range of motion
Keep the plate secure against your head throughout
Focus on controlled movement rather than speed
instructions: Lie face down with your whole body straight on a flat bench while holding a weight plate behind your head. Tip: You will need to position yourself so that your shoulders are slightly above the end of a flat bench in order for the upper chest, neck and face to be off the bench. This will be your starting position.
While keeping the plate secure on the back of your head slowly lower your head (as in saying "yes") as you breathe in.
Raise your head back up to the starting position in a semi-circular motion as you breathe out. Hold the contraction for a second.
Repeat for the recommended amount of repetitions.
exercises.name: Lying Face Down Plate Neck Resistance
exercises.name_pt: Resistência de Pescoço com Anilha de Bruços
exercises.overview: This exercise strengthens the neck extensor muscles, which are important for cervical spine stability and posture. It's commonly used in rehabilitation and contact sport preparation.
exercises.safetyInfo: Neck exercises carry inherent risk of cervical spine injury. Start with bodyweight only and progress very gradually. Discontinue if you experience any neck pain or dizziness.

commonMistakes: Progressing weight too quickly
Jerky or rapid movements
Allowing plate to slide during movement
exerciseTips: Begin with minimal weight to test tolerance
Perform movement in a smooth semi-circular arc
Keep weight plate centered on forehead
Focus on slow controlled repetitions
instructions: Lie face up with your whole body straight on a flat bench while holding a weight plate on top of your forehead. Tip: You will need to position yourself so that your shoulders are slightly above the end of a flat bench in order for the traps, neck and head to be off the bench. This will be your starting position.
While keeping the plate secure on your forehead slowly lower your head back in a semi-circular motion as you breathe in.
Raise your head back up to the starting position in a semi-circular motion as you breathe out. Hold the contraction for a second.
Repeat for the recommended amount of repetitions.
exercises.name: Lying Face Up Plate Neck Resistance
exercises.name_pt: Resistência de Pescoço com Anilha de Barriga para Cima
exercises.overview: This exercise targets the anterior neck muscles including the sternocleidomastoid, helping to balance neck strength and improve cervical stability. It's particularly beneficial for athletes in contact sports.
exercises.safetyInfo: Exercise extreme caution with neck flexion exercises as they can strain cervical vertebrae. Start without weight and add resistance very gradually. Stop immediately if experiencing neck pain or headaches.

commonMistakes: Allowing the elbows to flare out to the sides
Using momentum to swing the weight up
Not maintaining full range of motion at the bottom
exerciseTips: Keep your upper arms perpendicular to the floor throughout the movement
Squeeze the biceps at the top of each rep
Control the eccentric (lowering) phase slowly
Keep your wrists straight and strong
instructions: Lie face forward on a tall flat bench while holding a barbell with a supinated grip (palms facing up). Tip: If you are holding a barbell grab it using a shoulder-width grip and if you are using an E-Z Bar grab it on the inner handles. Your upper body should be positioned in a way that the upper chest is over the end of the bench and the barbell is hanging in front of you with the arms extended and perpendicular to the floor. This will be your starting position.
While keeping the elbows in and the upper arms stationary, curl the weight up in a semi-circular motion as you contract the biceps and exhale. Hold at the top of the movement for a second.
As you inhale, slowly go back to the starting position. Tip: Maintain full control of the weight at all times and avoid any swinging. Remember, only the forearms should move throughout the movement.
Repeat for the recommended amount of repetitions.
exercises.name: Lying High Bench Barbell Curl
exercises.name_pt: Rosca com Barra Deitado no Banco Alto
exercises.overview: A bicep isolation exercise performed lying face-down on a high bench, eliminating momentum and providing constant tension throughout the range of motion.
exercises.safetyInfo: Use a spotter for safety. Start with lighter weight to master the position before progressing. Avoid if you have shoulder impingement.

commonMistakes: Lifting your hips off the bench during the curl
Using too much weight and compromising form
Not achieving full range of motion
exerciseTips: Keep your hips pressed down against the bench
Curl your heels toward your glutes
Squeeze your hamstrings at the top of the movement
Control the weight on the way down
instructions: Adjust the machine lever to fit your height and lie face down on the leg curl machine with the pad of the lever on the back of your legs (just a few inches under the calves). Tip: Preferably use a leg curl machine that is angled as opposed to flat since an angled position is more favorable for hamstrings recruitment.

Keeping the torso flat on the bench, ensure your legs are fully stretched and grab the side handles of the machine. Position your toes straight (or you can also use any of the other two stances described on the foot positioning section). This will be your starting position.
As you exhale, curl your legs up as far as possible without lifting the upper legs from the pad. Once you hit the fully contracted position, hold it for a second.
As you inhale, bring the legs back to the initial position. Repeat for the recommended amount of repetitions.
exercises.name: Lying Leg Curls
exercises.overview: An isolation exercise targeting the hamstrings using a machine, providing excellent muscle activation and hypertrophy stimulus.
exercises.safetyInfo: Adjust the machine properly to fit your body size. Avoid if you have knee injuries or acute hamstring strains.

commonMistakes: Allowing knees to cave inward
Going too deep and rounding the lower back
Pressing only through the toes
exerciseTips: Keep your knees aligned with your toes
Press through your heels and balls of feet
Maintain tension in your core throughout
Stop when thighs reach parallel to the platform
instructions: Adjust the leg machine to a height that will allow you to get inside it with your knees bent and the thighs slightly below parallel.

Once you select the weight, position yourself inside the machine face up with the knees bent and thighs slightly below parallel to the platform. Make sure that the knees do not go past the toes. The angle created between the hamstrings and the calves should be one that is slightly less than 90 degrees (since your starting position requires you to start slightly below parallel). Your back and your head should be resting on the machine while your shoulders are pressed under the shoulder pads.
Place your hands by the handles and position your feet slightly pointing out at a shoulder width position. This will be your starting position.

While pressing with the balls of the feet as you breathe out, make your whole body erect as you squeeze the quads. Hold the contracted position for a second. Tip: Since you are starting below parallel, you can opt to use your hands to help you up by pressing on your thighs only on the first repetition.

Slowly squat down as you inhale but instead of going all the way down to the starting position, just stop once your thighs are parallel to the platform. The angle between your hamstrings and calves should be a 90-degree angle.
Repeat for the recommended amount of repetitions.
exercises.name: Lying Machine Squat
exercises.overview: A compound leg exercise performed on a specialized machine that mimics the squat pattern while providing back support and controlled movement path.
exercises.safetyInfo: Ensure proper machine adjustment before beginning. Keep your back flat against the pad and avoid locking knees aggressively.

commonMistakes: Raising the arm too high above shoulder level
Using momentum to swing the weight up
Allowing the torso to rotate during the movement
exerciseTips: Keep your arm perpendicular to your torso
Raise the dumbbell until your elbow reaches shoulder height
Maintain a slight bend in your elbow throughout
Control the lowering phase slowly
instructions: While holding a dumbbell in one hand, lay with your chest down on a flat bench. The other hand can be used to hold to the leg of the bench for stability.

Position the palm of the hand that is holding the dumbbell in a neutral manner (palms facing your torso) as you keep the arm extended with the elbow slightly bent. This will be your starting position.

Now raise the arm with the dumbbell to the side until your elbow is at shoulder height and your arm is roughly parallel to the floor as you exhale. Tip: Maintain your arm perpendicular to the torso while keeping your arm extended throughout the movement. Also, keep the contraction at the top for a second.
Slowly lower the dumbbell to the starting position as you inhale.
Repeat for the recommended amount of repetitions.
exercises.name: Lying One-Arm Lateral Raise
exercises.overview: An isolation exercise for the lateral deltoid performed lying on a bench, which eliminates momentum and provides constant tension throughout the range of motion.
exercises.safetyInfo: Start with light weight to master the movement pattern. Stop if you experience shoulder impingement pain.

commonMistakes: Using too much weight and compromising form
Allowing the chest to lift off the bench
Turning the movement into a rowing motion
exerciseTips: Keep your chest pressed firmly against the bench
Focus on squeezing your shoulder blades together at the top
Maintain a slight bend in your elbows throughout
Control the eccentric portion of the movement
instructions: While holding a dumbbell in each hand, lay with your chest down on a flat bench.
Position the palms of the hands in a neutral manner (palms facing your torso) as you keep the arms extended with the elbows slightly bent. This will be your starting position.

Now raise the arms to the side until your elbows are at shoulder height and your arms are roughly parallel to the floor as you exhale. Tip: Maintain your arms perpendicular to the torso while keeping them extended throughout the movement. Also, keep the contraction at the top for a second.
Slowly lower the dumbbells to the starting position as you inhale.
Repeat for the recommended amount of repetitions and then switch to the other arm.
exercises.name: Lying Rear Delt Raise
exercises.overview: An isolation exercise that targets the often-neglected posterior deltoids while lying prone on a bench. This movement helps improve shoulder stability and posture by strengthening the rear delts.
exercises.safetyInfo: Use light weights to start as the rear delts are smaller muscles prone to injury. Avoid this exercise if you have shoulder impingement issues.
variations: Bent Over Two-Dumbbell Row With Palms In

commonMistakes: Moving the upper arms during the curl
Using momentum to swing the weights up
Not fully extending the arms at the bottom position
exerciseTips: Keep your upper arms stationary throughout the movement
Rotate your wrists as you curl to maximize bicep activation
Lower the weight slowly to maximize time under tension
Squeeze hard at the top of each repetition
instructions: Lie down on a flat bench face up while holding a dumbbell in each arm on top of your thighs.
Bring the dumbbells to the sides with the arms extended and the palms of the hands facing your thighs (neutral grip).

While keeping the arms close to your torso and elbows in, slowly lower your arms (as you keep them extended with a slight bend at the elbows) as far down towards the floor as you can go. Once you cannot go down any further, lock your upper arms in that position and that will be your starting position.

As you breathe out, slowly begin to curl the weights up as you simultaneously rotate your wrists so that the palms of the hands face up. Continue curling the weight until your biceps are fully contracted and squeeze hard at the top position for a second. Tip: Only the forearms should move. Upper arms should remain stationary and elbows should stay in throughout the movement.
Return back to the starting position very slowly.
exercises.name: Lying Supine Dumbbell Curl
exercises.overview: A unique bicep exercise performed lying supine that provides constant tension on the biceps throughout the range of motion. The lying position eliminates the ability to use momentum, forcing the biceps to work harder.
exercises.safetyInfo: Start with lighter weights as this position can put stress on the shoulder joint. Those with shoulder problems should avoid this exercise or use very light weights.

commonMistakes: Lifting the torso off the pad during the pull
Using the biceps instead of back muscles to pull
Not achieving full range of motion at the bottom
exerciseTips: Keep your chest pressed firmly against the pad
Pull your elbows back rather than out to the sides
Squeeze your shoulder blades together at the top
Control the weight on the way down
instructions: Load up the T-bar Row Machine with the desired weight and adjust the leg height so that your upper chest is at the top of the pad. Tip: In some machines all you can do is stand on the appropriate step that allows you to be at a height that has the upper chest at the top of the pad.
Lay face down on the pad and grab the handles. You can either use a palms down, palms up, or palms in position depending on what part of your back you want to emphasize.
Lift the bar off the rack and extend your arms in front of you. This will be your starting position.

As you exhale slowly pull the weight up and squeeze your back at the top of the movement. Tip: Keep the upper arms as close to the torso as possible throughout the movement in order to better engage the back muscles. Also, do not lift your body off of the pad at any time and refrain from using the biceps to lift the weight.
After a second contraction at the top of the movement, as you inhale, slowly go back down to the starting position.
Repeat for the recommended amount of repetitions.
exercises.name: Lying T-Bar Row
exercises.overview: A machine-based rowing exercise that targets the middle back muscles while providing chest support to reduce lower back stress. This exercise is excellent for building thickness in the middle back and improving posture.
exercises.safetyInfo: The chest support reduces lower back stress, making this safer than free-weight T-bar rows. Ensure proper pad height to avoid shoulder impingement.
variations: Bent Over Two-Dumbbell Row With Palms In

commonMistakes: Allowing elbows to flare out to the sides
Lowering the bar too far behind the head
Using too much weight and compromising elbow position
exerciseTips: Keep your elbows tucked in and stationary
Lower the bar slowly to your forehead, not behind your head
Focus on moving only your forearms
Use a full range of motion for maximum muscle activation
instructions: Lie on a flat bench with either an e-z bar (my preference) or a straight bar placed on the floor behind your head and your feet on the floor.

Grab the bar behind you, using a medium overhand (pronated) grip, and raise the bar in front of you at arms length. Tip: The arms should be perpendicular to the torso and the floor. The elbows should be tucked in. This is the starting position.
As you breathe in, slowly lower the weight until the bar lightly touches your forehead while keeping the upper arms and elbows stationary.
At that point, use the triceps to bring the weight back up to the starting position as you breathe out.
Repeat for the recommended amount of repetitions.
exercises.name: Lying Triceps Press
exercises.overview: A classic isolation exercise that effectively targets all three heads of the triceps. Performed lying on a bench, this movement allows for heavy loading while maintaining proper form and muscle isolation.
exercises.safetyInfo: This exercise can stress the elbow joint, so use proper form and avoid excessive weight. Those with elbow problems should consider alternative tricep exercises.

commonMistakes: Extending too far beyond neutral spine alignment
Using momentum to swing up rather than controlled movement
Placing hands behind neck and pulling on the head
exerciseTips: Keep your head in a neutral position throughout the movement
Focus on using your lower back muscles, not momentum
Avoid hyperextending beyond a straight body line
Breathe out as you lift and in as you lower
instructions: Adjust the machine so the pad sits across your upper thighs and position yourself face down.
Cross your arms over your chest or place your hands behind your head.
Start with your torso hanging downward at approximately a 90-degree angle to your legs.
Raise your torso by contracting your lower back muscles until your body forms a straight line.
Slowly lower your torso back to the starting position with control.
exercises.name: Machine Back Extension
exercises.overview: The Machine Back Extension strengthens the erector spinae muscles that support spinal alignment and posture. This beginner-friendly exercise is essential for building a strong posterior chain and preventing lower back weakness.
exercises.safetyInfo: Avoid this exercise if you have acute lower back pain or disc issues. Never extend beyond neutral spine to prevent hyperextension injuries. Start with bodyweight only before adding resistance.

commonMistakes: Not using full range of motion
Pressing too high or too low on the chest
Allowing shoulders to roll forward during the press
exerciseTips: Keep your back pressed firmly against the pad
Push the handles away from you in a smooth arc
Squeeze your chest muscles at the peak contraction
Control the weight during the return phase
instructions: Sit down on the Chest Press Machine and select the weight.
Step on the lever provided by the machine since it will help you to bring the handles forward so that you can grab the handles and fully extend the arms.

Grab the handles with a palms-down grip and lift your elbows so that your upper arms are parallel to the floor to the sides of your torso. Tip: Your forearms will be pointing forward since you are grabbing the handles. Once you bring the handles forward and extend the arms you will be at the starting position.
Now bring the handles back towards you as you breathe in.
Push the handles away from you as you flex your pecs and you breathe out. Hold the contraction for a second before going back to the starting position.
Repeat for the recommended amount of reps.
When finished step on the lever again and slowly get the handles back to their original place.
exercises.name: Machine Bench Press
exercises.overview: A beginner-friendly machine exercise that targets the chest muscles while providing stability and safety. This compound movement helps build pressing strength and muscle mass in the chest, shoulders, and triceps.
exercises.safetyInfo: Machine bench press is safer than free weight bench press as there's no risk of dropping weights. Ideal for beginners learning the pressing movement pattern.

commonMistakes: Using momentum or swinging the weight up
Allowing elbows to flare out from the pads
Dropping the weight too quickly on the lowering phase
exerciseTips: Keep your upper arms pressed firmly against the pads throughout the movement
Control the eccentric portion by lowering slowly over 2-3 seconds
Squeeze your biceps hard at the top of each rep
Maintain a slight bend in your elbows at the bottom to keep tension
instructions: Adjust the seat to the appropriate height and make your weight selection. Place your upper arms against the pads and grasp the handles. This will be your starting position.
Perform the movement by flexing the elbow, pulling your lower arm towards your upper arm.
Pause at the top of the movement, and then slowly return the weight to the starting position.
Avoid returning the weight all the way to the stops until the set is complete to keep tension on the muscles being worked.
exercises.name: Machine Bicep Curl
exercises.overview: The machine bicep curl is an isolation exercise that safely targets the biceps using a fixed path of motion. It's excellent for beginners learning proper bicep training form and for experienced lifters wanting to isolate the biceps without stabilization demands.
exercises.safetyInfo: Avoid locking out completely at the bottom position as this can place excessive stress on the elbow joint. Those with elbow tendonitis should use lighter weights and focus on controlled movements.
variations: Seated One-Arm Dumbbell Palms-Down Wrist Curl

commonMistakes: Allowing shoulders to roll forward and losing back contact with the pad
Pressing the handles in a straight line instead of following the natural arc of motion
Using momentum or bouncing the weight at the bottom of the movement
exerciseTips: Keep your shoulder blades pulled back and down against the pad throughout the movement
Maintain a slight bend in your elbows at the top to protect your joints
Focus on squeezing your chest muscles as you press the weight forward
Control the negative portion of the rep to maximize muscle activation
instructions: Adjust the seat height so handles are at mid-chest level and sit with your back firmly against the pad.
Grasp the handles with an overhand grip, hands slightly wider than shoulder-width apart.
Press the handles forward in a smooth arc until your arms are fully extended but not locked out.
Slowly return the handles to the starting position with control, feeling a stretch in your chest.
exercises.name: Machine Chest Press
exercises.overview: The machine chest press is a seated pressing movement that targets the chest muscles while providing stability and support. It's an excellent beginner-friendly alternative to free weight pressing movements.
exercises.safetyInfo: Those with shoulder impingement or recent chest/shoulder injuries should consult a physician before performing this exercise. Start with lighter weights to ensure proper form and machine adjustment.

commonMistakes: Setting the seat height incorrectly, causing improper pressing angle
Using partial range of motion and not bringing the weight back far enough
Allowing the shoulders to roll forward during the press
exerciseTips: Keep your shoulder blades retracted and pressed against the back pad
Focus on squeezing your lower chest at the top of the movement
Maintain a controlled tempo throughout the entire range of motion
Keep your core engaged to maintain stability on the machine
instructions: Adjust the seat so the handles are positioned at lower chest level when seated.
Sit with your back firmly against the pad and grip the handles with a full grip.
Keep your feet flat on the floor and maintain a natural arch in your lower back.
Press the handles forward and slightly downward until your arms are fully extended.
Slowly return the weight to the starting position, feeling a stretch in your lower chest.
exercises.name: Machine Decline Chest Press
exercises.overview: The machine decline chest press targets the lower portion of the pectoral muscles while providing stability through its guided movement pattern. This exercise is excellent for developing lower chest definition and strength.
exercises.safetyInfo: This exercise is generally safe due to the machine's stability and guided motion path. Ensure proper seat adjustment to maintain optimal pressing angle and joint alignment.

commonMistakes: Allowing hips to flex during the movement
Descending too quickly without control
Using momentum to return to the starting position
exerciseTips: Keep your glutes contracted throughout the entire movement
Control the descent slowly to maximize eccentric strength
Focus on initiating the return with your hamstrings
Maintain a neutral spine position throughout
instructions: Adjust the machine so your knees are just behind the knee pad and your ankles are secured under the foot plate.
Start in an upright kneeling position with your torso vertical and hands crossed over your chest.
Slowly lower your torso forward by extending at the knee while keeping your hips extended.
Continue descending until your torso is parallel to the floor or as far as you can control.
Use your hamstrings to pull your torso back to the starting upright position.
Maintain a straight line from your knees to your head throughout the movement.
exercises.name: Machine Glute Ham Raise
exercises.overview: The Machine Glute Ham Raise is an advanced posterior chain exercise that primarily targets the hamstrings through both knee flexion and hip extension. It's considered one of the most effective exercises for developing hamstring strength and preventing injuries.
exercises.safetyInfo: This is an advanced exercise requiring significant hamstring strength. Begin with assisted variations or partner assistance. Avoid if you have knee problems or hamstring injuries. Progress gradually to prevent muscle strains.

commonMistakes: Using too much weight and compensating with hip movement
Not achieving full range of motion at either end
Allowing the weight stack to slam down between reps
exerciseTips: Keep your hips pressed firmly against the pad throughout the movement
Focus on squeezing your hamstrings at the top of the movement
Control the negative portion of the lift for maximum benefit
Keep your feet flexed to maintain proper ankle position
instructions: Adjust the machine so the pad sits comfortably against your lower calves just above your ankles.
Sit or lie on the machine with your knees aligned with the machine's pivot point.
Grip the handles for stability and keep your torso pressed against the back pad.
Curl your heels toward your glutes by contracting your hamstrings.
Hold the peak contraction briefly then slowly lower your legs back to the starting position.
Maintain tension throughout the entire range of motion.
exercises.name: Machine Hamstring Curl
exercises.overview: The machine hamstring curl is an isolation exercise that specifically targets the hamstring muscles. This exercise is excellent for building hamstring strength and size while providing a safe, controlled movement pattern.
exercises.safetyInfo: Generally safe for most individuals. Those with recent hamstring injuries should start with light weight and progress gradually. Avoid if experiencing knee pain during the movement.

commonMistakes: Using too much weight and compensating with upper body movement
Moving too quickly without controlling the resistance
Allowing the knees to cave inward during the return phase
exerciseTips: Keep your back pressed against the pad throughout the movement
Focus on initiating the movement from your glutes, not your thighs
Control both the opening and closing phases of the movement
Avoid using momentum or bouncing at the end ranges
instructions: Sit on the hip abduction machine with your back firmly against the pad and feet flat on the footrests.
Position the padded levers against the outside of your thighs, just above the knees.
Grasp the handles for stability and engage your core.
Push your legs outward against the resistance by contracting your glutes and outer thigh muscles.
Slowly return your legs to the starting position with control, maintaining tension throughout.
exercises.name: Machine Hip Abduction
exercises.overview: The Machine Hip Abduction targets the often-neglected gluteus medius and minimus muscles that are crucial for hip stability and proper movement mechanics. This exercise helps prevent knee valgus and improves functional movement patterns.
exercises.safetyInfo: This exercise is generally safe for beginners but should be avoided during acute hip or groin injuries. Start with light resistance to learn proper movement patterns and avoid overstretching the adductor muscles.

commonMistakes: Setting the seat too high or low, causing improper arm angle
Allowing the shoulders to roll forward during the press
Using partial range of motion and not bringing the weight back far enough
exerciseTips: Keep your shoulder blades retracted and pressed against the back pad
Focus on squeezing your upper chest at the top of the movement
Maintain control throughout the entire range of motion
Keep your wrists straight and aligned with your forearms
instructions: Adjust the seat height so the handles are at upper chest level and sit with your back firmly against the pad.
Grip the handles with a full grip, hands slightly wider than shoulder-width apart.
Keep your feet flat on the floor and maintain a natural arch in your lower back.
Press the handles forward and slightly upward until your arms are fully extended but not locked out.
Slowly return the weight to the starting position, feeling a stretch in your upper chest.
exercises.name: Machine Incline Chest Press
exercises.overview: The machine incline chest press targets the upper portion of the pectoral muscles while providing stability and safety through its guided movement pattern. It's an excellent exercise for beginners to build upper chest strength and size.
exercises.safetyInfo: This exercise is generally safe due to the machine's guided motion path. Individuals with shoulder injuries should start with lighter weights and ensure proper seat adjustment.

commonMistakes: Pulling the bar behind the neck
Leaning too far back or using momentum
Not achieving full range of motion at the top
exerciseTips: Pull with your back muscles, not just your arms
Keep your chest up and shoulders back throughout
Focus on bringing your elbows down rather than the bar
Maintain a slight backward lean
instructions: Sit at the lat pulldown machine and adjust the thigh pad to secure your legs.
Grasp the bar with a wide overhand grip, arms fully extended overhead.
Lean back slightly and pull the bar down to your upper chest, driving your elbows down and back.
Squeeze your shoulder blades together at the bottom of the movement.
Slowly return the bar to the starting position with control, feeling a stretch in your lats.
exercises.name: Machine Lat Pulldown
exercises.overview: The machine lat pulldown is a fundamental back exercise that targets the latissimus dorsi and supporting muscles. It's an excellent alternative to pull-ups and helps develop upper body pulling strength and back width.
exercises.safetyInfo: Avoid pulling the bar behind your neck as this can cause shoulder impingement. Those with shoulder issues should use a narrower grip.

commonMistakes: Placing feet too high or too low on the platform
Allowing knees to cave inward during the press
exerciseTips: Keep your core engaged and back pressed against the pad throughout
Drive through your heels rather than your toes when pressing
Control the descent to maximize muscle engagement
Maintain steady breathing throughout the movement
instructions: Sit in the leg press machine with your back flat against the backrest and feet on the platform shoulder-width apart.
Position your feet in the middle of the platform with toes pointing slightly outward.
Release the safety handles and slowly lower the weight by bending your knees toward your chest.
Lower until your knees reach approximately 90 degrees or as deep as your mobility allows.
Press the weight back up by driving through your heels and extending your knees.
Return to the starting position without locking your knees completely and repeat.
exercises.name: Machine Leg Press
exercises.overview: The machine leg press is a safe and effective compound exercise for building quadriceps and glute strength. It allows for heavy loading while providing stability and support, making it ideal for beginners and those returning from injury.
exercises.safetyInfo: Never fully lock out your knees at the top as this can cause injury. Those with lower back issues should be cautious with foot placement and range of motion.

commonMistakes: Using the shoulders and front delts instead of focusing on chest muscles
Allowing the elbows to bend and straighten during the movement
Setting the seat too high or too low, compromising proper arm position
exerciseTips: Keep your shoulders back and pressed against the pad
Maintain a consistent elbow bend throughout the entire range of motion
Focus on squeezing your chest muscles as you bring your arms together
Control the weight on both the positive and negative portions
instructions: Adjust the machine seat height so your upper arms are parallel to the floor when grasping the handles.
Sit with your back firmly against the pad and grasp the handles with a neutral grip.
Keep a slight bend in your elbows and maintain this position throughout the movement.
Bring your arms together in a wide arc until your hands meet in front of your chest.
Slowly return to the starting position, feeling a stretch in your chest muscles.
exercises.name: Machine Pec Fly
exercises.overview: The machine pec fly isolates the chest muscles through a controlled arc of motion. It's ideal for beginners learning chest isolation and for finishing chest workouts.
exercises.safetyInfo: Avoid overstretching at the bottom position if you have shoulder impingement. Start with lighter weight to establish proper form and range of motion.

commonMistakes: Positioning too high or low on the preacher pad
Using the pad to bounce or assist the movement
Completely straightening arms at the bottom causing elbow hyperextension
exerciseTips: Position your armpits at the top of the preacher pad
Keep your wrists straight and grip firm throughout
Focus on squeezing the biceps at the peak contraction
Lower the weight until your arms are almost straight but not locked
instructions: Sit down on the Preacher Curl Machine and select the weight.

Place the back of your upper arms (your triceps) on the preacher pad provided and grab the handles using an underhand grip (palms facing up). Tip: Make sure that when you place the arms on the pad you keep the elbows in. This will be your starting position.

Now lift the handles as you exhale and you contract the biceps. At the top of the position make sure that you hold the contraction for a second. Tip: Only the forearms should move. The upper arms should remain stationary and on the pad at all times.
Lower the handles slowly back to the starting position as you inhale.
Repeat for the recommended amount of repetitions.
exercises.name: Machine Preacher Curls
exercises.overview: Machine preacher curls isolate the biceps while the preacher pad prevents cheating and ensures strict form. This exercise effectively targets the biceps through their full range of motion with enhanced muscle stretch.
exercises.safetyInfo: The preacher position places the biceps in a vulnerable stretched position at the bottom. Never fully lock out the elbows and avoid heavy weights that could cause bicep tears.
variations: Seated One-Arm Dumbbell Palms-Down Wrist Curl

commonMistakes: Using partial range of motion
Bouncing at the bottom of the movement
Not pausing at the top to maximize contraction
exerciseTips: Focus on the full range of motion from stretch to peak contraction
Pause and squeeze at the top of each repetition
Control the descent to maximize the stretch reflex
Keep your knees aligned with your toes
instructions: Sit on the calf raise machine and position the balls of your feet on the foot plate.
Place the padded lever across your thighs just above your knees.
Release the weight stack and lower your heels as far as possible to stretch your calves.
Raise your heels as high as possible by contracting your calf muscles.
Pause briefly at the top before slowly lowering back to the stretched position.
exercises.name: Machine Seated Calf Raise
exercises.overview: The machine seated calf raise primarily targets the soleus muscle of the calf complex. The seated position emphasizes the soleus more than standing calf exercises and allows for safe, controlled loading.
exercises.safetyInfo: This is generally a very safe exercise. Those with Achilles tendon issues should avoid excessive stretching and use lighter weight.

commonMistakes: Pressing the handles straight up instead of following the natural arc
Allowing the lower back to arch excessively
Pressing with elbows flared too wide
exerciseTips: Keep your core tight and back pressed against the pad
Press the handles up and slightly forward following the natural arc
Control both the lifting and lowering phases equally
Keep your wrists straight and elbows under the handles
instructions: Sit down on the Shoulder Press Machine and select the weight.
Grab the handles to your sides as you keep the elbows bent and in line with your torso. This will be your starting position.
Now lift the handles as you exhale and you extend the arms fully. At the top of the position make sure that you hold the contraction for a second.
Lower the handles slowly back to the starting position as you inhale.
Repeat for the recommended amount of repetitions.
exercises.name: Machine Shoulder (Military) Press
exercises.overview: The machine shoulder press safely develops anterior and lateral deltoids while providing back support and a fixed movement path. It's ideal for building pressing strength without the balance requirements of free weights.
exercises.safetyInfo: Those with shoulder impingement should avoid overhead pressing or use a limited range of motion. Ensure proper warm-up of the shoulder joint before heavy pressing movements.

commonMistakes: Lowering the handles below shoulder level and putting stress on the shoulder joint
Arching the back excessively and losing contact with the back pad
Pressing the handles forward instead of straight overhead
exerciseTips: Keep your core engaged and maintain contact between your back and the pad
Press the weight straight up rather than forward or backward
Control the descent to shoulder level without going too low
Keep your wrists straight and aligned with your forearms
instructions: Adjust the seat height so the handles are at shoulder level when seated.
Sit with your back firmly against the pad and grasp the handles with an overhand grip.
Press the handles straight up overhead until your arms are fully extended but not locked out.
Lower the handles back to shoulder level with control, feeling a stretch in your shoulders.
exercises.name: Machine Shoulder Press
exercises.overview: The machine shoulder press is a seated overhead pressing movement that targets the deltoid muscles while providing back support and stability. It's an excellent beginner-friendly way to develop shoulder strength safely.
exercises.safetyInfo: Those with shoulder impingement or rotator cuff injuries should be cautious and may need to limit range of motion. Ensure proper seat adjustment before beginning the exercise.

commonMistakes: Using partial range of motion by not getting a full stretch
Bending the knees which shifts emphasis to the soleus
Moving too quickly and using momentum instead of muscle control
exerciseTips: Focus on achieving a full range of motion from deep stretch to peak contraction
Keep your legs straight throughout the movement to target the gastrocnemius
Control the lowering phase to maximize muscle engagement
Avoid bouncing at the bottom of the movement
instructions: Step onto the machine platform with the balls of your feet positioned on the foot plate and your shoulders under the pads.
Allow your heels to drop below the platform to achieve a full stretch in your calf muscles.
Keep your legs straight and core engaged while maintaining proper posture.
Push through the balls of your feet to raise your heels as high as possible, contracting your calf muscles.
Hold the peak contraction for a brief pause, then slowly lower your heels back to the stretched position.
Repeat for the desired number of repetitions while maintaining controlled movement.
exercises.name: Machine Standing Calf Raise
exercises.overview: The machine standing calf raise is a beginner-friendly isolation exercise that safely targets the gastrocnemius muscles. The machine provides stability and allows for progressive overload while maintaining proper form throughout the movement.
exercises.safetyInfo: Ensure the shoulder pads are properly adjusted to avoid neck strain. Those with Achilles tendon problems should use lighter weights and avoid excessive stretching at the bottom position.

commonMistakes: Allowing elbows to flare out from the pads during movement
Using momentum to swing the weight rather than controlled extension
Hyperextending elbows at the bottom of the movement
exerciseTips: Keep your upper arms pressed firmly against the pads
Extend your arms smoothly without locking out completely
Focus on feeling the stretch in your triceps at the starting position
Control the weight back to the starting position slowly
instructions: Adjust the seat to the appropriate height and make your weight selection. Place your upper arms against the pads and grasp the handles. This will be your starting position.
Perform the movement by extending the elbow, pulling your lower arm away from your upper arm.
Pause at the completion of the movement, and then slowly return the weight to the starting position.
Avoid returning the weight all the way to the stops until the set is complete to keep tension on the muscles being worked.
exercises.name: Machine Triceps Extension
exercises.overview: Machine triceps extension isolates all three heads of the triceps using a fixed path that eliminates stabilization needs. This exercise effectively builds triceps strength and size with reduced risk of form breakdown.
exercises.safetyInfo: Avoid fully locking out the elbows to prevent joint stress. Those with elbow issues should use lighter weights and ensure smooth, controlled movements throughout the range of motion.

commonMistakes: Allowing excessive torso rotation during the renegade rows
Rushing through the movement and losing proper form
exerciseTips: Keep your core tight during the plank and rowing portions
Use hex dumbbells to prevent rolling during the exercise
Control the renegade rows to avoid rotating your torso
Generate power from your legs during the clean and press
instructions: Start standing with dumbbells at your sides, then squat down and place the dumbbells on the floor.
Jump your feet back into a plank position, gripping the dumbbells as handles.
Perform a push-up, then immediately do a renegade row with each arm alternately.
Jump your feet back toward the dumbbells, returning to the squat position.
Clean the dumbbells to your shoulders, then press them overhead.
Lower the dumbbells back to your sides and repeat the sequence.
exercises.name: Man Maker
exercises.overview: The man maker is an advanced full-body exercise that combines elements of burpees, renegade rows, and overhead presses. It's designed to build total-body strength, power, and conditioning in one challenging movement pattern.
exercises.safetyInfo: This is a high-intensity, complex movement that requires good shoulder stability and core strength. Those with wrist, shoulder, or back issues should avoid this exercise or use very light weights.

commonMistakes: Using too much momentum and swinging the weight up
Allowing the shoulder to roll forward at the bottom position
Pulling the bar too high toward the chest instead of the hip
exerciseTips: Keep your chest up and maintain a neutral spine throughout the movement
Focus on pulling your elbow back rather than just lifting the weight
Squeeze your lat at the top of the movement before lowering
Allow your torso to rotate slightly at the top for maximum lat engagement
instructions: Position one end of a barbell in a corner or landmine attachment and load the opposite end with weight plates.
Stand perpendicular to the bar with your feet shoulder-width apart, staggering your stance with the outside leg forward.
Hinge at the hips and lean forward slightly, grasping the end of the barbell with your outside hand using a neutral grip.
Pull the bar up and back toward your hip, leading with your elbow and squeezing your shoulder blade.
Lower the weight under control back to the starting position with your arm fully extended.
exercises.name: Meadows Row
exercises.overview: The Meadows Row is a single-arm landmine rowing variation that targets the lats with a unique angle and range of motion. It allows for heavy loading while providing excellent lat development and core stability challenge.
exercises.safetyInfo: Avoid this exercise if you have lower back issues, as the bent-over position can place stress on the lumbar spine. Start with lighter weight to master the movement pattern.

commonMistakes: Elevating shoulders instead of retracting shoulder blades
Using momentum instead of controlled movement
Not maintaining proper bench angle
exerciseTips: Squeeze shoulder blades together without lifting shoulders up
Hold the contraction for a full second
Keep arms straight throughout the movement
Focus on pulling shoulder blades back, not up
instructions: Lie facedown on an incline bench while holding a dumbbell in each hand. Your arms should be fully extended hanging down and pointing towards the floor. The palms of your hands should be facing each other. This will be your starting position.

As you exhale, squeeze your shoulder blades together and hold the contraction for a full second. Tip: This movement is just like the reverse action of a hug, or trying to perform rear laterals as if you had no arms.
As you inhale go back to the starting position.
Repeat for the recommended amount of repetitions.
exercises.name: Middle Back Shrug
exercises.overview: An isolation exercise targeting the middle trapezius and rhomboids to improve posture and shoulder blade stability. Essential for counteracting forward head posture.
exercises.safetyInfo: Avoid excessive weight that compromises form. Keep neck in neutral position to prevent strain.
variations: Bent Over Two-Dumbbell Row With Palms In

commonMistakes: Always using the same grip configuration
Allowing one arm to dominate the movement
Swinging or using momentum
exerciseTips: Focus on pulling with your back muscles, not just arms
Keep the supinated arm's elbow close to your torso
Switch grip positions between sets
Control the descent for maximum benefit
instructions: Using a spacing that is just about 1 inch wider than shoulder width, grab a pull-up bar with the palms of one hand facing forward and the palms of the other hand facing towards you. This will be your starting position.

Now start to pull yourself up as you exhale. Tip: With the arm that has the palms facing up concentrate on using the back muscles in order to perform the movement. The elbow of that arm should remain close to the torso. With the other arm that has the palms facing forward, the elbows will be away but in line with the torso. You will concentrate on using the lats to pull your body up.
After a second contraction at the top, start to slowly come down as you inhale.
Repeat for the recommended amount of repetitions.

On the following set, switch grips; so if you had the right hand with the palms facing you and the left one with the palms facing forward, on the next set you will have the palms facing forward for the right hand and facing you for the left.
exercises.name: Mixed Grip Chin
exercises.overview: An advanced pull-up variation using different hand positions that challenges coordination while targeting the back muscles asymmetrically.
exercises.safetyInfo: Alternate grip positions regularly to prevent muscle imbalances. This exercise places different stresses on each arm, so progress gradually.

commonMistakes: Allowing knees to cave inward
Taking steps too wide causing band to lose tension
Leaning forward excessively during movement
exerciseTips: Maintain tension in the bands throughout the movement
Keep knees aligned over toes
Take controlled steps maintaining hip width
Engage core to maintain upright posture
instructions: Place a band around both ankles and another around both knees. There should be enough tension that they are tight when your feet are shoulder width apart.
To begin, take short steps forward alternating your left and right foot.
After several steps, do just the opposite and walk backward to where you started.
exercises.name: Monster Walk
exercises.overview: A functional exercise that activates the hip abductors and improves lateral stability. Excellent for injury prevention and glute activation.
exercises.safetyInfo: Use appropriate band resistance to maintain proper form. Avoid sudden movements that could cause band to snap.
variations: Side-Lying Hip Abduction With Mini Band

commonMistakes: Sticking with only one grip position without exploring options
Bouncing the bar off the chest instead of controlled touch-and-go
exerciseTips: Experiment with different grip positions between sets
Keep your core tight and feet firmly planted
Focus on squeezing the bar handles throughout the movement
Lower the bar with control to maximize muscle tension
instructions: Choose your desired grip position on the multi-grip bar handles.
Lie on the bench and grasp the bar with your selected grip width.
Unrack the bar and position it over your chest with control.
Lower the bar to your chest maintaining proper elbow positioning.
Press the bar back to full arm extension engaging chest and triceps.
Re-rack the bar after completing all repetitions.
exercises.name: Multi Grip Press
exercises.overview: The multi grip press uses a specialty barbell with various handle positions allowing for different grip widths and angles. It provides versatility in targeting different aspects of pressing strength.
exercises.safetyInfo: The multi-grip bar allows for customizable hand positioning to work around shoulder or wrist limitations. Always ensure proper bar control regardless of grip selection.

commonMistakes: Losing false grip during transition
Insufficient shoulder roll-forward in transition
Allowing rings to flare out during dip portion
exerciseTips: Establish false grip with palms on top of rings
Pull rings to armpits while rolling shoulders forward
Transition smoothly from pull to dip position
Maintain ring stability throughout movement
instructions: Grip the rings using a false grip, with the base of your palms on top of the rings. Initiate a pull up by pulling the elbows down to your side, flexing the elbows.

As you reach the top position of the pull-up, pull the rings to your armpits as you roll your shoulders forward, allowing your elbows to move straight back behind you. This puts you into the proper position to continue into the dip portion of the movement.
Maintaining control and stability, extend through the elbow to complete the motion.
Use care when lowering yourself to the ground.
exercises.name: Muscle Up
exercises.overview: An advanced gymnastic movement combining a pull-up with a dip transition on rings. Develops exceptional upper body strength, coordination, and ring control.
exercises.safetyInfo: Requires significant upper body strength and shoulder stability. Master pull-ups and dips separately before attempting. Use spotter or assistance bands when learning.

commonMistakes: Allowing back to round off the pad
Locking knees at top of movement
Using stance too wide for intended quad emphasis
exerciseTips: Keep back pressed against pad throughout movement
Descend until thighs are slightly below parallel
Drive through heels to return to start
Maintain 3 inches or less between feet
instructions: Place the back of your torso against the back pad of the machine and hook your shoulders under the shoulder pads provided.

Position your legs in the platform using a less than shoulder width narrow stance with the toes slightly pointed out. Your feet should be around 3 inches or less apart. Tip: Keep your head up at all times and also maintain the back on the pad at all times.
Place your arms on the side handles of the machine and disengage the safety bars (which on most designs is done by moving the side handles from a facing front position to a diagonal position).
Now straighten your legs without locking the knees. This will be your starting position.

Begin to slowly lower the unit by bending the knees as you maintain a straight posture with the head up (back on the pad at all times). Continue down until the angle between the upper leg and the calves becomes slightly less than 90-degrees (which is the point in which the upper legs are below parallel to the floor). Inhale as you perform this portion of the movement.
Begin to raise the unit as you exhale by pushing the floor with mainly with the heels of your feet as you straighten the legs again and go back to the starting position.
Repeat for the recommended amount of repetitions.
exercises.name: Narrow Stance Hack Squats
exercises.overview: A machine-based squat variation using a narrow stance to emphasize quadriceps development. Provides stable platform for heavy loading while minimizing lower back stress.
exercises.safetyInfo: Ensure proper foot placement to avoid knee stress. Keep safety bars engaged until fully set up. Avoid locking knees at top position.

commonMistakes: Allowing knees to cave inward
Lowering too far causing lower back rounding
Locking knees completely at top position
exerciseTips: Position feet 3 inches apart with toes slightly out
Lower until knees reach 90-degree angle
Drive through heels to press platform up
Keep head and back against pads throughout
instructions: Using a leg press machine, sit down on the machine and place your legs on the platform directly in front of you at a less-than-shoulder-width narrow stance with the toes slightly pointed out. Your feet should be around 3 inches or less apart. Tip: Keep your head up at all times and also maintain the back on the pad at all times.

Lower the safety bars holding the weighted platform in place and press the platform all the way up until your legs are fully extended in front of you. Tip: Make sure that you do not lock your knees. Your torso and the legs should make a perfect 90-degree angle. This will be your starting position.
As you inhale, slowly lower the platform until your upper and lower legs make a 90-degree angle.
Pushing mainly with the heels of your feet and using the quadriceps go back to the starting position as you exhale.
Repeat for the recommended amount of repetitions and ensure to lock the safety pins properly once you are done. You do not want that platform falling on you fully loaded.
exercises.name: Narrow Stance Leg Press
exercises.overview: A machine-based leg exercise using narrow foot placement to maximize quadriceps activation. Allows for heavy loading in a stable, controlled environment.
exercises.safetyInfo: Always engage safety locks before unloading. Never place hands on knees during movement. Ensure full range of motion without lower back rounding.

commonMistakes: Knees tracking forward past toes
Losing upright torso position
Insufficient depth due to narrow stance mobility limitations
exerciseTips: Place feet 3-6 inches apart with slight toe turn-out
Descend until thighs are below parallel
Drive through heels while maintaining upright torso
Keep knees tracking over toes throughout movement
instructions: This exercise is best performed inside a squat rack for safety purposes. To begin, first set the bar on a rack that best matches your height. Once the correct height is chosen and the bar is loaded, step under the bar and place the back of your shoulders (slightly below the neck) across it.
Hold on to the bar using both arms at each side and lift it off the rack by first pushing with your legs and at the same time straightening your torso.

Step away from the rack and position your legs using a less-than-shoulder-width narrow stance with the toes slightly pointed out. Feet should be around 3-6 inches apart. Keep your head up at all times (looking down will get you off balance) and maintain a straight back. This will be your starting position. (Note: For the purposes of this discussion we will use the medium stance described above which targets overall development; however you can choose any of the three stances discussed in the foot stances section).

Begin to slowly lower the bar by bending the knees as you maintain a straight posture with the head up.
