Continue down until the angle between the upper leg and the calves becomes slightly less than 90-degrees (which is the point in which the upper legs are below parallel to the floor). Inhale as you perform this portion of the movement. Tip: If you performed the exercise correctly, the front of the knees should make an imaginary straight line with the toes that is perpendicular to the front. If your knees are past that imaginary line (if they are past your toes) then you are placing undue stress on the knee and the exercise has been performed incorrectly.
Begin to raise the bar as you exhale by pushing the floor with the heel of your foot mainly as you straighten the legs again and go back to the starting position.
Repeat for the recommended amount of repetitions.
exercises.name: Narrow Stance Squats
exercises.overview: A barbell squat variation using a narrow foot stance to emphasize quadriceps development over glute activation. Challenges balance and mobility while targeting the front thigh muscles.
exercises.safetyInfo: Perform in squat rack with safety bars set appropriately. Requires good ankle and hip mobility. Start with lighter weight to assess comfort with narrow stance.

commonMistakes: Dropping too quickly during the eccentric phase
Using excessive hip flexion to cheat the movement
Not securing the ankles properly under the pads
exerciseTips: Keep your core tight throughout the entire movement
Control the descent slowly to maximize eccentric strengthening
Drive through your hamstrings to pull yourself back up
Maintain a straight line from knees to head
instructions: Using the leg pad of a lat pulldown machine or a preacher bench, position yourself so that your ankles are under the pads, knees on the seat, and you are facing away from the machine. You should be upright and maintaining good posture.
This will be your starting position. Lower yourself under control until your knees are almost completely straight.
Remaining in control, raise yourself back up to the starting position.
If you are unable to complete a rep, use a band, a partner, or push off of a box to aid in completing a repetition.
exercises.name: Natural Glute Ham Raise
exercises.overview: The Natural Glute Ham Raise is an advanced bodyweight exercise that builds exceptional hamstring and glute strength through both concentric and eccentric muscle actions. It's particularly effective for developing posterior chain power and injury prevention.
exercises.safetyInfo: This exercise places significant stress on the hamstrings and knees. Beginners should use assistance bands or push-off support. Avoid if you have knee injuries or acute hamstring strains.

commonMistakes: Lowering the bar too quickly without control
Pressing the bar toward the face instead of straight up
Using too heavy of a weight for this advanced variation
exerciseTips: Keep your shoulder blades retracted and pressed into the bench
Lower the bar slowly and under complete control
Press the bar in a straight line over your neck
Maintain tension in your chest throughout the movement
instructions: Lie back on a flat bench. Using a medium-width grip (a grip that creates a 90-degree angle in the middle of the movement between the forearms and the upper arms), lift the bar from the rack and hold it straight over your neck with your arms locked. This will be your starting position.
As you breathe in, come down slowly until you feel the bar on your neck.

After a second pause, bring the bar back to the starting position as you breathe out and push the bar using your chest muscles. Lock your arms and squeeze your chest in the contracted position, hold for a second and then start coming down slowly again. Tip: It should take at least twice as long to go down than to come up).
Repeat the movement for the prescribed amount of repetitions.
When you are done, place the bar back in the rack.
exercises.name: Neck Press
exercises.overview: The Neck Press is a high-risk bench press variation that targets the chest muscles with the bar lowered to the neck area. Due to significant safety concerns, this exercise is not recommended for general training.
exercises.safetyInfo: This is an extremely dangerous exercise that places the shoulder joint in a compromised position. High risk of shoulder impingement, rotator cuff tears, and neck injury. Not recommended for most trainees - standard bench press variations are safer and equally effective.

commonMistakes: Using momentum or swinging to complete reps
Not achieving full range of motion at the bottom or top
exerciseTips: Keep your shoulders down and back throughout the movement
Focus on pulling with your lats rather than just your arms
Maintain a slight hollow body position
Control the descent to maximize muscle engagement
instructions: Hang from parallel handles or a neutral grip pull-up bar with palms facing each other.
Start in a dead hang with arms fully extended and shoulders slightly engaged.
Pull your body up by driving your elbows down and back toward your ribs.
Continue pulling until your chin clears the bar or handles.
Lower yourself with control back to the starting dead hang position.
exercises.name: Neutral Grip Pull Up
exercises.overview: Neutral grip pull-ups use palms facing each other, which is often more comfortable for the shoulders and allows for greater bicep involvement. This variation is excellent for building upper body pulling strength with reduced stress on the wrists and shoulders.
exercises.safetyInfo: Those with shoulder impingement or elbow issues may find this variation more comfortable than traditional pull-ups. Avoid if you have acute shoulder or elbow injuries.

commonMistakes: Pulling on the neck with the supporting hand
Lifting straight up instead of rotating diagonally
Using momentum instead of controlled muscle contraction
exerciseTips: Focus on rotating your torso rather than just lifting
Keep your lower back pressed against the floor
Exhale forcefully as you crunch up and across
Control the movement on both the up and down phases
instructions: Lie flat on the floor with your lower back pressed to the ground. For this exercise, you will need to put one hand beside your head and the other to the side against the floor.
Make sure your feet are elevated and resting on a flat surface.
Now lift the shoulder in which your hand is touching your head.

Simply elevate your shoulder and body upward until you touch your knee. For example, if you have your right hand besides your head, then you want to elevate your body upwards until your right elbow touches your left knee. The same variation can be applied doing the inverse and using your left elbow to touch your right knee.
After your knee touches your elbow, lower your body until you have reached the starting position.
Remember to breathe in during the eccentric (lowering) part of the exercise and to breathe out during the concentric (upward) part of the exercise.
Continue alternating in this manner until all of the recommended repetitions for each side have been completed.
exercises.name: Oblique Crunches
exercises.overview: Oblique Crunches target the side abdominal muscles through rotational movement, helping develop core strength and definition. This exercise improves rotational stability and contributes to a well-rounded core training program.
exercises.safetyInfo: Keep one hand lightly behind the head for support without pulling on the neck. Avoid if you have lower back injuries or neck problems.

commonMistakes: Using the bottom arm to push off the floor
Craning the neck instead of moving from the core
Not maintaining side-lying position throughout the set
exerciseTips: Keep your bottom leg stable for support
Focus on lifting your elbow toward your hip
Squeeze your obliques at the top of the movement
Maintain slow and controlled tempo throughout
instructions: Start out by lying on your right side with your legs lying on top of each other. Make sure your knees are bent a little bit.
Place your left hand behind your head.
Once you are in this set position, begin by moving your left elbow up as you would perform a normal crunch except this time the main emphasis is on your obliques.
Crunch as high as you can, hold the contraction for a second and then slowly drop back down into the starting position.
Remember to breathe in during the eccentric (lowering) part of the exercise and to breathe out during the concentric (elevation) part of the exercise.
exercises.name: Oblique Crunches - On The Floor
exercises.overview: Side-lying Oblique Crunches isolate the lateral abdominal muscles from a floor position, providing targeted strengthening for the obliques. This variation allows for focused unilateral core training.
exercises.safetyInfo: Ensure your head and neck remain in neutral alignment. This exercise is generally safe for most individuals but should be avoided if you experience lower back or neck discomfort.
variations: Side-Lying Hip Abduction With Mini Band

commonMistakes: Allowing lateral flexion or leaning toward the weighted side
Taking steps that are too long or walking too quickly
Hiking the shoulder up on the weighted side
exerciseTips: Keep your shoulders level and resist the urge to lean toward the weighted side
Engage your core throughout the entire carry to maintain stability
Take controlled steps and maintain good posture
Keep your head in a neutral position looking straight ahead
instructions: Hold a dumbbell in one hand at your side with your arm hanging naturally.
Stand tall with your shoulders level and core engaged to resist lateral flexion.
Walk forward with controlled steps, maintaining an upright posture and level shoulders.
Focus on keeping your torso vertical and preventing lateral lean toward the weighted side.
Complete the desired distance or time before switching the weight to the opposite hand.
exercises.name: Offset Loaded Carry
exercises.overview: The Offset Loaded Carry challenges core stability by creating an asymmetrical load that the body must resist. It develops anti-lateral flexion strength and improves functional stability for daily activities.
exercises.safetyInfo: This exercise is generally safe but may aggravate existing lower back issues due to the asymmetrical loading. Start with lighter weights and shorter distances initially.

commonMistakes: Using momentum instead of controlled muscle contraction
Not achieving full range of motion at the bottom
Allowing the torso to swing during the movement
exerciseTips: Create a 30-degree torso lean with chest out
Pull until the bar touches your upper chest
Squeeze your back muscles at the top position
Control the descent for twice as long as the ascent
instructions: For this exercise, start out by placing a towel around a chin up bar.
Grab the chin-up bar with your palm facing you. One hand will be grabbing the chin-up bar and the other will be grabbing the towel.
Bring your torso back around 30 degrees or so while creating a curvature on your lower back and sticking your chest out. This is your starting position.v

Pull your torso up until the bar touches your upper chest by drawing the shoulders and the upper arms down and back. Exhale as you perform this portion of the movement. Tip: Concentrate on squeezing the back muscles once you reach the full contracted position. The upper torso should remain stationary as it moves through space and only the arms should move. The forearms should do no other work other than hold the bar.
After a second on the contracted position, start to inhale and slowly lower your torso back to the starting position when your arms are fully extended and the lats are fully stretched.
Repeat this motion for the prescribed amount of repetitions.
Switch arms and repeat the movement.
exercises.name: One Arm Chin-Up
exercises.overview: An advanced unilateral pulling exercise that challenges the back, biceps, and grip strength asymmetrically. This exercise builds incredible upper body strength and addresses muscle imbalances.
exercises.safetyInfo: This is an advanced exercise requiring significant upper body strength. Ensure proper progression and avoid if you have shoulder or elbow injuries.

commonMistakes: Allowing the torso to rotate excessively during the press
Using too heavy weight before mastering the balance
Not controlling the eccentric portion of the lift
exerciseTips: Use your thigh to help position the dumbbell initially
Rotate your wrist so palm faces away at starting position
Keep full control of the dumbbell throughout the movement
Use your non-lifting hand for balance if needed
instructions: Lie down on a flat bench with a dumbbell in one hand on top of your thigh.

By using your thigh to help you get the dumbbell up, clean the dumbbell up so that you can hold it in front of you at shoulder width. Use the hand you are not lifting with to help position the dumbbell over you properly.
Once at shoulder width, rotate your wrist forward so that the palm of your hand is facing away from you. This will be your starting position.

Bring down the weights slowly to your side as you breathe in. Keep full control of the dumbbell at all times. Tip: Use the hand that you are not lifting with to help keep the dumbbell balance as you may struggle a bit at first. Only use your non-lifting hand if it is needed. Otherwise, keep it resting to the side.

As you breathe out, push the dumbbells up using your pectoral muscles. Lock your arms in the contracted position, squeeze your chest, hold for a second and then start coming down slowly. Tip: It should take at least twice as long to go down than to come up.
Repeat the movement for the prescribed amount of repetitions of your training program.
Switch arms and repeat the movement.
exercises.name: One Arm Dumbbell Bench Press
exercises.overview: A unilateral chest exercise that challenges core stability while building pressing strength. This variation helps address muscle imbalances and improves unilateral strength.
exercises.safetyInfo: Start with lighter weights to master the balance and coordination. Ensure you can control the dumbbell throughout the full range of motion.

commonMistakes: Using momentum or swinging the weight up
Not achieving full extension at the bottom position
Allowing the wrist to bend backward under load
exerciseTips: Position your upper arm flat against the preacher bench
Lower the weight slowly until your bicep is fully stretched
Curl with control bringing the pinky higher than thumb
Squeeze the bicep hard at the top for one second
instructions: Grab a dumbbell with the right arm and place the upper arm on top of the preacher bench or the incline bench. The dumbbell should be held at shoulder length. This will be your starting position.
As you breathe in, slowly lower the dumbbell until your upper arm is extended and the biceps is fully stretched.

As you exhale, use the biceps to curl the weight up until your biceps is fully contracted and the dumbbell is at shoulder height. Again, remember that to ensure full contraction you need to bring that small finger higher than the thumb.
Squeeze the biceps hard for a second at the contracted position and repeat for the recommended amount of repetitions.
Switch arms and repeat the movement.
exercises.name: One Arm Dumbbell Preacher Curl
exercises.overview: An isolation exercise that targets the biceps with strict form using a preacher bench for support. This exercise eliminates momentum and ensures focused bicep development.
exercises.safetyInfo: Avoid locking out the elbow completely at the bottom to prevent hyperextension. Use controlled movements to prevent bicep tears.
variations: Seated One-Arm Dumbbell Palms-Down Wrist Curl

commonMistakes: Allowing the torso to rotate during the movement
Not lowering the weight until the elbow touches the ground
Using the non-working arm to assist the movement
exerciseTips: Keep your non-working hand pressed firmly against the floor for stability
Drive your feet into the ground to create tension throughout your body
Lower the weight until your elbow touches the floor completely
Press the weight in a straight line above your shoulder
instructions: Lie down on a flat surface with your back pressing against the floor or an exercise mat. Make sure your knees are bent.

Have a partner hand you the bar on one hand. When starting, your arm should be just about fully extended, similar to the starting position of a barbell bench press. However, this time your grip will be neutral (palms facing your torso).
Make sure the hand you are not using to lift the weight is placed by your side.
Begin the exercise by lowering the barbell until your elbow touches the ground. Make sure to breathe in as this is the eccentric (lowering part of the exercise).
Then start lifting the barbell back up to the original starting position. Remember to breathe out during the concentric (lifting part of the exercise).
Repeat until you have performed your recommended repetitions.
Switch arms and repeat the movement.
exercises.name: One Arm Floor Press
exercises.overview: The one arm floor press is a unilateral pressing exercise that builds single-arm strength while challenging core stability. It's excellent for addressing strength imbalances between arms and developing anti-rotation core strength.
exercises.safetyInfo: This exercise requires significant unilateral stability and may cause strain on the shoulder joint if performed with excessive weight. Ensure proper warm-up and start with lighter loads.

commonMistakes: Using momentum by swaying the torso
Pulling with the arm instead of engaging the lat
Not maintaining tension at the top of the movement
exerciseTips: Pull your elbow down and back toward your hip
Squeeze your shoulder blade down and back at the bottom
Control the weight on the way up to maintain tension
Keep your torso upright and avoid leaning
instructions: Select an appropriate weight and adjust the knee pad to help keep you down. Grasp the handle with a pronated grip. This will be your starting position.
Pull the handle down, squeezing your elbow to your side as you flex the elbow.
Pause at the bottom of the motion, and then slowly return the handle to the starting position.
For multiple repetitions, avoid completely returning the weight to keep tension on the muscles being worked.
exercises.name: One Arm Lat Pulldown
exercises.overview: The one arm lat pulldown allows for focused development of each side of the back independently. This exercise helps correct muscle imbalances and improves unilateral pulling strength.
exercises.safetyInfo: Avoid using excessive weight that causes you to lean or use momentum. The unilateral nature can stress the spine if form breaks down.
variations: Bent Over Two-Dumbbell Row With Palms In

commonMistakes: Allowing the upper arm to drift during the movement
Using momentum to lift the weight
Not achieving full range of motion at the bottom
exerciseTips: Keep your upper arm perpendicular to the floor throughout the movement
Use your free hand to stabilize your working arm
Lower the weight slowly to maximize muscle tension
Focus on squeezing the tricep at the top of the movement
instructions: Lie flat on a bench while holding a dumbbell at arms length. Your arm should be perpendicular to your body. The palm of your hand should be facing towards your feet as a pronated grip is required to perform this exercise.
Place your non lifting hand on your bicep for support.
Slowly begin to lower the dumbbell down as you breathe in.
Then, begin lifting the dumbbell upward as you contract the triceps. Remember to breathe out during the concentric (lifting part of the exercise).
Repeat until you have performed your set repetitions.
Switch arms and repeat the movement.
exercises.name: One Arm Pronated Dumbbell Triceps Extension
exercises.name_pt: Extensão de Tríceps Unilateral com Halter - Pegada Pronada
exercises.overview: This isolation exercise targets the triceps using a pronated grip, which can provide a different stimulus compared to neutral or supinated grips. It's effective for building unilateral arm strength.
exercises.safetyInfo: Start with light weight to master the movement pattern. The pronated grip can place additional stress on the wrist and elbow joints.

commonMistakes: Allowing the hips to rotate or sag during the movement
Using the non-working arm for assistance or balance
Insufficient range of motion due to lack of strength
exerciseTips: Keep your core extremely tight to prevent rotation and maintain body alignment
Start with feet wider apart and gradually bring them closer together as you progress
Focus on slow, controlled movements rather than speed
Practice with elevated surfaces before attempting full one-arm push-ups
instructions: Start in a standard push-up position, then shift your weight to one arm and place the non-working hand behind your back.
Spread your legs wider than shoulder-width apart for stability and angle your body slightly toward the working arm.
Lower your body slowly by bending the working arm until your chest nearly touches the floor.
Press through the working arm to return to the starting position while maintaining core tension.
Complete all repetitions on one side before switching arms.
exercises.name: One Arm Push Up
exercises.overview: The one-arm push-up is an advanced bodyweight exercise that requires exceptional upper body strength, core stability, and coordination. It develops unilateral pressing strength and challenges anti-rotation core muscles.
exercises.safetyInfo: This exercise places significant stress on the shoulder joint and requires exceptional upper body strength. Those with shoulder, wrist, or elbow injuries should avoid this movement. Build up gradually from assisted variations.

commonMistakes: Allowing the grip to rotate during the movement
Moving the upper arm instead of just the forearm
Rushing through the eccentric phase
exerciseTips: Maintain the supinated grip throughout the entire range of motion
Keep your upper arm stationary and perpendicular to the floor
Control the eccentric portion of the lift
Stabilize your working arm with your free hand
instructions: Lie flat on a bench while holding a dumbbell at arms length. Your arm should be perpendicular to your body. The palm of your hand should be facing towards your face as a supinated grip is required to perform this exercise.
Place your non lifting hand on your bicep for support.
Slowly begin to lower the dumbbell down as you breathe in.
Then, begin lifting the dumbbell upward as you contract the triceps. Remember to breathe out during the concentric (lifting part of the exercise).
Repeat until you have performed your set repetitions.
Switch arms and repeat the movement.
Switch arms again and repeat the movement.
exercises.name: One Arm Supinated Dumbbell Triceps Extension
exercises.name_pt: Extensão de Tríceps Unilateral com Halter - Pegada Supinada
exercises.overview: The supinated grip variation of the triceps extension provides a unique stimulus to the triceps muscles. This unilateral exercise helps identify and correct strength imbalances between arms.
exercises.safetyInfo: The supinated grip may feel awkward initially and can stress the wrist. Use lighter weight until comfortable with the grip position.

commonMistakes: Leaning too far forward losing upright posture
Placing too much weight on the rear foot
Allowing the knee to cave inward during the movement
exerciseTips: Keep your torso upright throughout the movement
Drive through your front heel on the ascent
Lower until your front thigh is parallel to the floor
Maintain balance by engaging your core
instructions: Start by standing about 2 to 3 feet in front of a flat bench with your back facing the bench. Have a barbell in front of you on the floor. Tip: Your feet should be shoulder width apart from each other.
Bend the knees and use a pronated grip with your hands being wider than shoulder width apart from each other to lift the barbell up until you can rest it on your chest.

Then lift the barbell over your head and rest it on the base of your neck. Move one foot back so that your toe is resting on the flat bench. Your other foot should be stationary in front of you. Keep your head up at all times as looking down will get you off balance and also maintain a straight back. Tip: Make sure your back is straight and chest is out while performing this exercise.
As you inhale, slowly lower your leg until your thigh is parallel to the floor. At this point, your knee should be over your toes. Your chest should be directly above the middle of your thigh.
Leading with the chest and hips and contracting the quadriceps, elevate your leg back to the starting position as you exhale.
Repeat for the recommended amount of repetitions.
Switch legs and repeat the movement.
exercises.name: One Leg Barbell Squat
exercises.overview: An advanced unilateral squat variation that challenges balance, stability, and single-leg strength. Excellent for correcting muscle imbalances and building functional leg strength.
exercises.safetyInfo: This advanced exercise requires excellent balance and mobility. Start with bodyweight only before adding load. Avoid if you have knee or ankle injuries.

commonMistakes: Rotating the torso instead of keeping it stable
Pulling the weight toward your shoulder instead of your hip
Using momentum instead of controlled movement
exerciseTips: Pull your elbow straight back toward your hip
Squeeze your shoulder blade toward your spine at the top
Keep your core braced to prevent rotation
Focus the movement in your back, not your arm
instructions: Choose a flat bench and place a dumbbell on each side of it.

Place the right leg on top of the end of the bench, bend your torso forward from the waist until your upper body is parallel to the floor, and place your right hand on the other end of the bench for support.

Use the left hand to pick up the dumbbell on the floor and hold the weight while keeping your lower back straight. The palm of the hand should be facing your torso. This will be your starting position.

Pull the resistance straight up to the side of your chest, keeping your upper arm close to your side and keeping the torso stationary. Breathe out as you perform this step. Tip: Concentrate on squeezing the back muscles once you reach the full contracted position. Also, make sure that the force is performed with the back muscles and not the arms. Finally, the upper torso should remain stationary and only the arms should move. The forearms should do no other work except for holding the dumbbell; therefore do not try to pull the dumbbell up using the forearms.
Lower the resistance straight down to the starting position. Breathe in as you perform this step.
Repeat the movement for the specified amount of repetitions.
Switch sides and repeat again with the other arm.
exercises.name: One-Arm Dumbbell Row
exercises.overview: A fundamental unilateral pulling exercise that builds back strength and addresses muscle imbalances. Allows for heavy loading while providing excellent stability and control.
exercises.safetyInfo: Maintain a neutral spine throughout the movement. Avoid if you have lower back issues without proper bracing.
variations: Bent Over Two-Dumbbell Row With Palms In

commonMistakes: Bending the elbow too much turning it into a press
Lowering the weight too far causing shoulder strain
Using too heavy weight compromising form
exerciseTips: Maintain a slight bend in your elbow throughout
Focus on the stretch at the bottom of the movement
Squeeze your chest muscle at the top
Use the same arc path up and down
instructions: Lie down on a flat bench with a dumbbell in one hand resting on top of your thigh. The palm of your hand with the dumbbell in it should be at a neutral grip.

By using your thighs to help you get the dumbbell up, clean the dumbbell so that you can hold it in front of you with your lifting arm being fully extended. Remember to maintain a neutral grip with this exercise. Your non lifting hand should be to the side holding the flat bench for better support. This will be your starting position.

Your arm with the weight should have a slight bend on your elbow in order to prevent stress at the biceps tendon. Begin by lowering your arm with the weight in it out in a wide arc until you feel a stretch on your chest. Breathe in as you perform this portion of the movement. Tip: Keep in mind that throughout the movement, your lifting arm should remain stationary; the movement should only occur at the shoulder joint.
Return your lifting arm back to the starting position as you squeeze your chest muscles and breathe out. Tip: Make sure to use the same arc of motion used to lower the weights.
Hold for a second at the contracted position and repeat the movement for the prescribed amount of repetitions.
Switch arms and repeat the exercise.
exercises.name: One-Arm Flat Bench Dumbbell Flye
exercises.name_pt: Crucifixo Unilateral com Halter no Banco Reto
exercises.overview: An isolation exercise targeting chest muscle development through a wide arc of motion. The unilateral version helps address strength imbalances between sides.
exercises.safetyInfo: Use lighter weight than pressing movements. Stop if you feel shoulder impingement. This exercise puts significant stress on the shoulder joint.

commonMistakes: Using the arm to pull instead of the obliques
Allowing the weight stack to rest between reps
Leaning forward or backward instead of purely lateral
exerciseTips: Contract your oblique to initiate the movement
Keep your arm position static throughout
Control the weight on the return
Maintain constant tension on the cable
instructions: Connect a standard handle to a tower. Move cable to highest pulley position.
Stand with side to cable. With one hand, reach up and grab handle with underhand grip.
Pull down cable until elbow touches your side and the handle is by your shoulder.
Position feet hip-width apart. Place free hand on hip to help gauge pivot point.
Keep arm in static position. Contract oblique to bring the weight down in a side crunch.
Once you reach maximum contraction, slowly release the weight to the starting position. The weight stack should never be unloaded in a resting position. The aim is constant tension during the set.
Repeat to failure.
Then, reposition and repeat the same series of movements on the opposite side.
exercises.name: One-Arm High-Pulley Cable Side Bends
exercises.name_pt: Flexão Lateral Unilateral na Polia Alta
exercises.overview: A targeted oblique strengthening exercise using cable resistance. Provides constant tension throughout the range of motion for effective core development.
exercises.safetyInfo: Start with light weight to master the movement pattern. Avoid if you have lower back issues or hernias.

commonMistakes: Allowing the dumbbell to tilt during movement
Using momentum to swing the weight up
Not maintaining contact with the incline bench
exerciseTips: Keep the dumbbell parallel to the floor
Raise your arm straight up toward the ceiling
Squeeze your shoulder at the top
Control the descent across your body
instructions: Lie down sideways on an incline bench press with a dumbbell in the hand. Make sure the shoulder is pressing against the incline bench and the arm is lying across your body with the palm around your navel.
Hold a dumbbell in your uppermost arm while keeping it extended in front of you parallel to the floor. This is your starting position.

While keeping the dumbbell parallel to the floor at all times, perform a lateral raise. Your arm should travel straight up until it is pointing at the ceiling. Tip: Exhale as you perform this movement. Hold the dumbbell in the position and feel the contraction in the shoulders for a second.
While inhaling lower the weight across your body back into the starting position.
Repeat the movement for the prescribed amount of repetitions.
Switch arms and repeat the movement.
exercises.name: One-Arm Incline Lateral Raise
exercises.overview: An isolation exercise for the lateral deltoid performed on an incline bench. The position provides stability while targeting shoulder width and definition.
exercises.safetyInfo: Use appropriate weight to maintain control throughout the range of motion. Stop if you experience shoulder impingement.

commonMistakes: Swinging the kettlebell away from the body
Using only arms instead of hip drive
Allowing the kettlebell to crash onto the forearm
exerciseTips: Drive through your heels explosively
Keep the kettlebell close to your body throughout
Rotate your wrist at the top to catch cleanly
Maintain a straight back throughout the movement
instructions: Place a kettlebell between your feet. As you bend down to grab the kettlebell, push your butt back and keep your eyes looking forward.
Clean the kettlebell to your shoulders by extending through the legs and hips as you raise the kettlebell towards your shoulder. The wrist should rotate as you do so.
Return the weight to the starting position.
exercises.name: One-Arm Kettlebell Clean
exercises.overview: The one-arm kettlebell clean is an explosive movement that develops posterior chain power and coordination. It builds functional strength for everyday activities and athletic performance.
exercises.safetyInfo: Avoid if you have lower back issues or shoulder impingement. Start with lighter weight to master the hip hinge pattern and wrist rotation.

commonMistakes: Pressing instead of jerking the weight
Poor timing between dip and drive
Landing too hard in the receiving position
exerciseTips: Use your legs to drive the weight overhead
Keep your core tight throughout the entire movement
Land softly in the squat position
Maintain control during the descent
instructions: Hold a kettlebell by the handle.
Clean the kettlebell to your shoulder by extending through the legs and hips as you pull the kettlebell towards your shoulder. Rotate your wrist as you do so, so that the palm faces forward.
Dip your body by bending the knees, keeping your torso upright.

Immediately reverse direction, driving through the heels, in essence jumping to create momentum. As you do so, press the kettlebell overhead to lockout by extending the arms, using your body's momentum to move the weight.
Receive the weight overhead by returning to a squat position underneath the weight.
Keeping the weight overhead, return to a standing position. Lower the weight to the floor to perform the next repetition.
exercises.name: One-Arm Kettlebell Clean and Jerk
exercises.name_pt: Clean and Jerk Unilateral com Kettlebell
exercises.overview: The one-arm kettlebell clean and jerk is a complex movement combining power development with overhead stability. It enhances total-body coordination and explosive strength.
exercises.safetyInfo: This is an advanced movement requiring good mobility and coordination. Avoid with shoulder or wrist injuries. Master the clean and jerk separately before combining.

commonMistakes: Bouncing the elbow off the floor
Allowing the shoulder to roll forward
Pressing at an angle instead of straight up
exerciseTips: Keep your shoulder blade pulled back and down
Pause briefly when your elbow touches the floor
Rotate your wrist as you press up
Maintain tension in your non-working side
instructions: Lie on the floor holding a kettlebell with one hand, with your upper arm supported by the floor. The palm should be facing in.
Press the kettlebell straight up toward the ceiling, rotating your wrist.
Lower the kettlebell back to the starting position and repeat.
exercises.name: One-Arm Kettlebell Floor Press
exercises.name_pt: Supino no Chão com Kettlebell - Um Braço
exercises.overview: The one-arm kettlebell floor press builds unilateral chest and tricep strength while improving shoulder stability. The floor provides a natural stopping point for safety.
exercises.safetyInfo: The floor limits range of motion, making this safer for those with shoulder issues. Ensure proper wrist position to avoid strain.

commonMistakes: Dipping too deep or too shallow
Pressing instead of using leg drive
Poor timing between dip and drive
exerciseTips: Dip straight down with knees tracking over toes
Drive explosively through your heels
Catch the weight with soft knees
Keep your torso upright throughout
instructions: Hold a kettlebell by the handle. Clean the kettlebell to your shoulder by extending through the legs and hips as you pull the kettlebell towards your shoulder. Rotate your wrist as you do so, so that the palm faces forward. This will be your starting position.
Dip your body by bending the knees, keeping your torso upright.

Immediately reverse direction, driving through the heels, in essence jumping to create momentum. As you do so, press the kettlebell overhead to lockout by extending the arms, using your body's momentum to move the weight. Receive the weight overhead by returning to a squat position underneath the weight. Keeping the weight overhead, return to a standing position.
Lower the weight to perform the next repetition.
exercises.name: One-Arm Kettlebell Jerk
exercises.overview: The one-arm kettlebell jerk develops explosive power and overhead stability using leg drive to move weight efficiently. It's excellent for athletic performance enhancement.
exercises.safetyInfo: Requires good shoulder mobility and timing. Start with lighter weights to master the technique. Avoid with knee or ankle issues.

commonMistakes: Leaning away from the weight
Using leg drive instead of strict pressing
Allowing the elbow to flare excessively
exerciseTips: Press the weight up and slightly out to the side
Keep your core braced throughout
Look up at the kettlebell during the press
Maintain lat tension for stability
instructions: Clean a kettlebell to your shoulder. Clean the kettlebell to your shoulder by extending through the legs and hips as you pull the kettlebell towards your shoulder. Rotate your wrist as you do so, so that the palm faces inward. This will be your starting position.
Look at the kettlebell and press it up and out until it is locked out overhead.
Lower the kettlebell back to your shoulder under control and repeat. Make sure to contract your lat, butt, and stomach forcefully for added stability and strength.
exercises.name: One-Arm Kettlebell Military Press To The Side
exercises.name_pt: Desenvolvimento Militar Unilateral Lateral com Kettlebell
exercises.overview: This variation of the military press emphasizes strict shoulder strength while challenging core stability. The side angle engages stabilizing muscles differently than traditional presses.
exercises.safetyInfo: Keep movements controlled to avoid shoulder impingement. Ensure adequate overhead mobility before attempting heavier weights.

commonMistakes: Bringing the elbow too close to the body
Using momentum instead of controlled strength
Neglecting core engagement
exerciseTips: Keep your elbow flared out to the side
Press up and out in an arc motion
Engage your lats and core for stability
Control the descent slowly
instructions: Clean a kettlebell to your shoulder. Clean the kettlebell to your shoulder by extending through the legs and hips as you pull the kettlebell towards your shoulder. Rotate your wrist as you do so, so that the palm faces forward. This will be your starting position.
Hold the kettlebell with the elbow out to the side, and press it up and out until it is locked out overhead.
Lower the kettlebell back to your shoulder under control and repeat. Make sure to contract your lat, butt, and stomach forcefully for added stability and strength.
exercises.name: One-Arm Kettlebell Para Press
exercises.overview: The para press targets the shoulders with the elbow positioned wide, emphasizing different muscle recruitment patterns. It builds functional pressing strength and shoulder stability.
exercises.safetyInfo: The elbow-out position can stress the shoulder joint. Ensure proper warm-up and avoid if you have shoulder impingement issues.

commonMistakes: Pressing the kettlebell away from the body instead of straight up
Insufficient dip depth limiting power generation
Allowing the elbow to flare out during the press
exerciseTips: Drive through your heels explosively during the dip phase
Keep the kettlebell close to your body throughout the movement
Punch straight up through the handle at lockout
Maintain tight core engagement throughout
instructions: Hold a kettlebell by the handle. Clean the kettlebell to your shoulder by extending through the legs and hips as you pull the kettlebell towards your shoulder. Rotate your wrist as you do so, so that the palm faces forward. This will be your starting position.
Dip your body by bending the knees, keeping your torso upright.

Immediately reverse direction, driving through the heels, in essence jumping to create momentum. As you do so, press the kettlebell overhead to lockout by extending the arms, using your body's momentum to move the weight. Lower the weight to perform the next repetition.
exercises.name: One-Arm Kettlebell Push Press
exercises.overview: The one-arm kettlebell push press combines lower body power with upper body strength to press a kettlebell overhead. It develops unilateral shoulder strength and full-body coordination.
exercises.safetyInfo: Avoid if you have shoulder impingement or wrist issues. Start with lighter weights to master the timing of the dip-and-drive movement.

commonMistakes: Rotating the torso during the pull instead of keeping it stable
Pulling the weight too high toward the chest
Using momentum instead of controlled muscle contraction
exerciseTips: Pull the kettlebell toward your hip, not your chest
Keep your back straight and core engaged throughout
Squeeze your shoulder blade at the top of the movement
Control the descent to maximize muscle engagement
instructions: Place a kettlebell in front of your feet. Bend your knees slightly and then push your butt out as much as possible as you bend over to get in the starting position. Grab the kettlebell and pull it to your stomach, retracting your shoulder blade and flexing the elbow. Keep your back straight. Lower and repeat.
exercises.name: One-Arm Kettlebell Row
exercises.overview: The one-arm kettlebell row targets the middle back muscles while challenging core stability. It's excellent for correcting muscle imbalances and building unilateral pulling strength.
exercises.safetyInfo: Maintain neutral spine throughout the movement. Avoid if you have lower back issues without proper form mastery.
variations: Bent Over Two-Dumbbell Row With Palms In

commonMistakes: Pulling with arms instead of driving with hips
Allowing the kettlebell to swing too far from the body
Failing to punch through at lockout causing the bell to crash
exerciseTips: Drive powerfully through your hips to generate momentum
Keep the kettlebell close to your body during the pull
Punch through at the top to receive the weight smoothly
Focus on hip hinge movement rather than squatting
instructions: Place a kettlebell between your feet. Bend your knees and push your butt back to get in the proper starting position.
Look straight ahead and swing the kettlebell back between your legs.

Immediately reverse the direction and drive through with your hips and knees, accelerating the kettlebell upward. As the kettlebell rises to your shoulder rotate your hand and punch straight up, using momentum to receive the weight locked out overhead.
exercises.name: One-Arm Kettlebell Snatch
exercises.overview: The one-arm kettlebell snatch is a full-body power movement that develops explosive hip extension and overhead stability. It's one of the most athletic kettlebell exercises.
exercises.safetyInfo: This is an advanced movement requiring excellent shoulder mobility and hip hinge mechanics. Master the kettlebell swing before attempting snatches.

commonMistakes: Insufficient dip depth limiting power transfer
Poor foot positioning in the split stance
Pressing the weight out instead of straight up
exerciseTips: Drive explosively through both legs during the dip phase
Split your legs quickly to get under the weight
Keep the kettlebell directly overhead in the receiving position
Stand up by bringing your back leg forward first
instructions: Hold a kettlebell by the handle. Clean the kettlebell to your shoulder by extending through the legs and hips as you pull the kettlebell towards your shoulder. Rotate your wrist as you do so, so that the palm faces forward. This will be your starting position.
Dip your body by bending the knees, keeping your torso upright.

Immediately reverse direction, driving through the heels, in essence jumping to create momentum. As you do so, press the kettlebell overhead to lockout by extending the arms, using your body's momentum to move the weight.
Receive the weight overhead by returning to a squat position underneath the weight, positioning one leg in front of you and one leg behind you.
Keeping the weight overhead, return to a standing position and bring your feet together. Lower the weight to perform the next repetition.
exercises.name: One-Arm Kettlebell Split Jerk
exercises.overview: The one-arm kettlebell split jerk combines explosive power with precise footwork to drive weight overhead. It develops unilateral pressing strength and athletic coordination.
exercises.safetyInfo: Requires good shoulder stability and leg strength. Practice the split position without weight first to ensure proper mechanics.

commonMistakes: Poor timing of the split leg movement
Insufficient hip drive limiting power generation
Landing with feet too narrow in the split position
exerciseTips: Generate power through explosive hip and knee extension
Split your legs to receive the weight in a stable position
Keep the kettlebell close during the upward pull
Ensure full lockout overhead before standing
instructions: Hold a kettlebell in one hand by the handle.
Squat towards the floor, and then reverse the motion, extending the hips, knees, and finally the ankles, to raise the kettlebell overhead.

After fully extending the body, descend into a lunge position to receive the weights overhead, one leg forward and one leg back. Ensure you drive through with your hips and lock the ketttlebells overhead in one uninterrupted motion.
Return to a standing position, holding the weight overhead, and bring the feet together. Lower the weight to return to the starting position.
exercises.name: One-Arm Kettlebell Split Snatch
exercises.overview: The one-arm kettlebell split snatch is an explosive movement that develops power, coordination, and unilateral strength. It's a technical lift requiring precise timing and positioning.
exercises.safetyInfo: This advanced movement requires excellent mobility, stability, and power. Master basic snatches and split positions separately before combining.

commonMistakes: Using arms to lift instead of hip drive
Allowing torso rotation due to unilateral loading
Squatting instead of hinging at the hips
exerciseTips: Drive the movement with your hips, not your arms
Keep your core tight to prevent rotation
Maintain neutral spine throughout the swing
Let the kettlebell fall naturally between your legs
instructions: Perform the exercise with proper form.
exercises.name: One-Arm Kettlebell Swings
exercises.overview: One-arm kettlebell swings develop explosive hip power while challenging core stability and grip strength. They're excellent for building unilateral posterior chain strength and power.
exercises.safetyInfo: Start with two-handed swings before progressing to single arm. Avoid if you have lower back issues without proper hip hinge mechanics.

commonMistakes: Rotating torso during the pull
Using momentum to jerk the weight up
Allowing the elbow to flare out wide
exerciseTips: Drive elbow back toward your hip
Keep chest up and core braced throughout
Squeeze shoulder blade at the top
Control the negative portion slowly
instructions: Position a bar into a landmine or in a corner to keep it from moving. Load an appropriate weight onto your end.
Stand next to the bar, and take a grip with one hand close to the collar. Using your hips and legs, rise to a standing position.
Assume a bent-knee stance with your hips back and your chest up. Your arm should be extended. This will be your starting position.
Pull the weight to your side by retracting the shoulder and flexing the elbow. Do not jerk the weight or cheat during the movement.
After a brief pause, return to the starting position.
exercises.name: One-Arm Long Bar Row
exercises.overview: A unilateral rowing exercise using a landmine setup that targets the middle back muscles. This movement helps address imbalances between sides and provides a unique angle of resistance.
exercises.safetyInfo: Avoid if you have lower back issues. Keep the core engaged to prevent spinal rotation and maintain proper hip hinge position throughout the movement.
variations: Bent Over Two-Dumbbell Row With Palms In

commonMistakes: Not fully extending before the slam
Slamming too close to the body
Neglecting the hip drive component
exerciseTips: Extend fully through hips before slamming
Focus on explosive hip extension
Keep core tight during the wind-up
Catch the ball with bent knees
instructions: Start in a standing position with a staggered, athletic stance. Hold a medicine ball in one hand, on the same side as your back leg. This will be your starting position.
Begin by winding the arm, raising the medicine ball above your head. As you do so, extend through the hips, knees, and ankles to load up for the slam.
At peak extension, flex the shoulders, spine, and hips to throw the ball hard into the ground directly in front of you.
Catch the ball on the bounce and continue for the desired number of repetitions.
exercises.name: One-Arm Medicine Ball Slam
exercises.overview: An explosive unilateral movement that develops core power and coordination. This exercise combines hip extension with rapid flexion for athletic power development.
exercises.safetyInfo: Use a slam ball that won't bounce unpredictably. Ensure adequate ceiling height and clear landing zone. Avoid if you have shoulder impingement issues.

commonMistakes: Trying to muscle the kettlebell up with arms
Poor timing on the flip transition
Landing the kettlebell too hard on the palm
exerciseTips: Drive through hips explosively
Let the kettlebell flip naturally
Keep wrist neutral during catch
Practice timing with lighter weights first
instructions: Place one kettlebell between your feet.
Grab the handle with one hand and raise the kettlebell rapidly, let it flip so that the ball of the kettlebell lands in the palm of your hand.
Throw the kettlebell out in front of you and catch the handle with one hand.
Take the kettlebell to the floor and repeat. Make sure to work both arms.
exercises.name: One-Arm Open Palm Kettlebell Clean
exercises.name_pt: Clean Unilateral com Kettlebell - Palma Aberta
exercises.overview: An advanced kettlebell exercise that develops explosive hip power and coordination. The open palm catch adds complexity and challenges grip strength differently than traditional cleans.
exercises.safetyInfo: Master the basic kettlebell clean first. Start with lighter weights to learn timing. Avoid if you have wrist injuries or limited shoulder mobility.

commonMistakes: Allowing the kettlebell to drift forward
Leaning toward the weighted side
Not achieving full depth in the squat
exerciseTips: Keep the kettlebell directly overhead
Drive through heels on ascent
Maintain rigid torso throughout
Look straight ahead, not up
instructions: Clean and press a kettlebell with one arm. Clean the kettlebell to your shoulder by extending through the legs and hips as you pull the kettlebell towards your shoulder. Rotate your wrist as you do so. Press the weight overhead by extending through the elbow.This will be your starting position.
Looking straight ahead and keeping a kettlebell locked out above you, flex the knees and hips and lower your torso between your legs, keeping your head and chest up.
Pause at the bottom position for a second before rising back to the top, driving through the heels of your feet.
exercises.name: One-Arm Overhead Kettlebell Squats
exercises.name_pt: Agachamento Unilateral com Kettlebell Acima da Cabeça
exercises.overview: An advanced single-limb loading squat variation that challenges stability, mobility, and unilateral strength. This exercise develops functional strength and addresses asymmetries.
exercises.safetyInfo: Requires excellent shoulder stability and mobility. Avoid with shoulder impingement or lower back issues. Master bilateral overhead squats first.

commonMistakes: Leaning toward the loaded side
Rounding the back during lift
Using excessive weight too soon
exerciseTips: Keep torso upright and centered
Drive through legs, not back
Maintain neutral spine throughout
Control the descent slowly
instructions: Stand to the side of a barbell next to its center. Bend your knees and lower your body until you are able to reach the barbell.

Grasp the bar as if you were grabbing a briefcase (palms facing you since the bar is sideways). You may need a wrist wrap if you are using a significant amount of weight. This is your starting position.
Use your legs to help lift the barbell up while exhaling. Your arms should extend fully as bring the barbell up until you are in a standing position.
Slowly bring the barbell back down while inhaling. Tip: Make sure to bend your knees while lowering the weight to avoid any injury from occurring.
Repeat for the recommended amount of repetitions.
Switch arms and repeat the movement.
exercises.name: One-Arm Side Deadlift
exercises.overview: A challenging unilateral deadlift variation that loads the body asymmetrically. This exercise develops anti-lateral flexion core strength and functional lifting patterns.
exercises.safetyInfo: High risk for spinal injury if performed incorrectly. Start with very light weight to learn proper form. Avoid if you have disc issues or poor core stability.

commonMistakes: Raising arm too high above shoulder
Using momentum or body swing
Allowing shoulder to roll forward
exerciseTips: Lead with your pinky finger
Stop at shoulder height
Control the lowering phase
Keep slight bend in elbow
instructions: Pick a dumbbell and place it in one of your hands. Your non lifting hand should be used to grab something steady such as an incline bench press. Lean towards your lifting arm and away from the hand that is gripping the incline bench as this will allow you to keep your balance.
Stand with a straight torso and have the dumbbell by your side at arm's length with the palm of the hand facing you. This will be your starting position.

While maintaining the torso stationary (no swinging), lift the dumbbell to your side with a slight bend on the elbow and your hand slightly tilted forward as if pouring water in a glass. Continue to go up until you arm is parallel to the floor. Exhale as you execute this movement and pause for a second at the top.
Lower the dumbbell back down slowly to the starting position as you inhale.
Repeat for the recommended amount of repetitions.
Switch arms and repeat the exercise.
exercises.name: One-Arm Side Laterals
exercises.overview: An isolation exercise targeting the lateral deltoid with added stability challenge from the supported unilateral position. The leaning position allows for greater range of motion.
exercises.safetyInfo: Avoid if you have shoulder impingement. Use lighter weights than bilateral version. Stop if you feel pinching in the shoulder joint.

commonMistakes: Using momentum instead of controlled muscle contraction
Allowing the torso to lean forward excessively
Moving the leg too high causing lower back arch
exerciseTips: Keep your core engaged throughout the movement
Focus on squeezing your glute at the top of the movement
Control the weight on both the lifting and lowering phases
Maintain a slight bend in your standing leg for stability
instructions: Hook a leather ankle cuff to a low cable pulley and then attach the cuff to your ankle.
Face the weight stack from a distance of about two feet, grasping the steel frame for support.

While keeping your knees and hips bent slightly and your abs tight, contract your glutes to slowly "kick" the working leg back in a semicircular arc as high as it will comfortably go as you breathe out. Tip: At full extension, squeeze your glutes for a second in order to achieve a peak contraction.
Now slowly bring your working leg forward, resisting the pull of the cable until you reach the starting position.
Repeat for the recommended amount of repetitions.
Switch legs and repeat the movement for the other side.
exercises.name: One-Legged Cable Kickback
exercises.name_pt: Coice de Glúteo Unilateral na Polia
exercises.overview: The one-legged cable kickback is an isolation exercise that targets the glutes through hip extension. It's effective for building glute strength and improving hip stability.
exercises.safetyInfo: Avoid excessive back extension at the top of the movement. Those with lower back issues should use lighter weight and focus on proper form.

commonMistakes: Releasing too early or too late causing awkward catch
Using arms instead of hips to generate power
Allowing the kettlebell to crash into the forearms
exerciseTips: Generate power from your hips and legs
Release the handle at the right moment for smooth rotation
Keep your core tight throughout the movement
Practice the timing with lighter weight first
instructions: Place one kettlebell between your feet. Clean the kettlebell by extending through the legs and hips as you raise the kettlebell towards your shoulders.
Release the kettlebell as it comes up, and let it flip so that the ball of the kettlebell lands in the palms of your hands.
Release the kettlebell out in front of you and catch the handle with both hands. Lower the kettlebell to the starting position and repeat.
exercises.name: Open Palm Kettlebell Clean
exercises.overview: The open palm kettlebell clean is an advanced variation that requires releasing and re-catching the kettlebell. It develops explosive power, coordination, and grip strength.
exercises.safetyInfo: This is an advanced movement requiring excellent timing and coordination. Start with lighter weights and ensure proper kettlebell clean technique before attempting. Risk of wrist or forearm injury if caught improperly.

commonMistakes: Using too much weight compromising sit-up form
Pressing the weight before initiating the sit-up
Allowing momentum to dominate the movement
exerciseTips: Press the weight up as you sit up in one fluid motion
Keep your core engaged throughout both movements
Control the descent without touching the ground completely
Choose a weight that allows proper form for both movements
instructions: Secure your feet and lay back on the floor. Your knees should be bent. Hold a weight with both hands to your chest. This will be your starting position.
Initiate the movement by flexing the hips and spine to raise your torso up from the ground.
As you move up, press the weight up so that it is above your head at the top of the movement.
Return the weight to your chest as you reverse the sit-up motion, ensuring not to go all the way down to the floor.
exercises.name: Otis-Up
exercises.overview: The Otis-up combines a sit-up with an overhead press, working the core and upper body simultaneously. It's an efficient compound movement for functional strength.
exercises.safetyInfo: Start with lighter weight to master the coordination. Those with neck or lower back issues should avoid or use minimal weight.

commonMistakes: Allowing the upper arms to drop during the movement
Using the shoulders and back to assist the curl
Rushing through the eccentric portion
exerciseTips: Keep your upper arms parallel to the floor throughout
Focus on squeezing your biceps at the peak contraction
Maintain equal tension on both sides
Keep your core engaged for stability
instructions: To begin, set a weight that is comfortable on each side of the pulley machine. Note: Make sure that the amount of weight selected is the same on each side.
Now adjust the height of the pulleys on each side and make sure that they are positioned at a height higher than that of your shoulders.

Stand in the middle of both sides and use an underhand grip (palms facing towards the ceiling) to grab each handle. Your arms should be fully extended and parallel to the floor with your feet positioned shoulder width apart from each other. Your body should be evenly aligned the handles. This is the starting position.
While exhaling, slowly squeeze the biceps on each side until your forearms and biceps touch.
While inhaling, move your forearms back to the starting position. Note: Your entire body is stationary during this exercise except for the forearms.
Repeat for the recommended amount of repetitions prescribed in your program.
exercises.name: Overhead Cable Curl
exercises.name_pt: Rosca na Polia Acima da Cabeça
exercises.overview: The overhead cable curl targets the biceps from a unique angle with arms elevated. This position emphasizes the peak contraction and provides constant tension.
exercises.safetyInfo: Ensure equal weight on both sides to prevent muscle imbalances. Start with lighter weight to establish proper form and shoulder stability.

commonMistakes: Allowing the weights to drift forward or backward instead of staying overhead
Arching the back excessively to compensate for shoulder instability
exerciseTips: Keep your shoulders actively engaged and pulled down away from your ears
Maintain a tight core to prevent excessive back arch
Take controlled steps and avoid bouncing the weights
Breathe steadily and don't hold your breath during the carry
instructions: Press dumbbells overhead with arms fully locked out and weights directly over your shoulders.
Engage your core and maintain a neutral spine with shoulders pulled down and back.
Begin walking forward with controlled steps, keeping the weights stable overhead.
Maintain proper posture and breathing while walking for the prescribed distance or time.
Keep your eyes forward and avoid looking up at the weights during the carry.
Lower the weights safely to your shoulders when completing the carry.
exercises.name: Overhead Carry
exercises.name_pt: Caminhada com Carga Acima da Cabeça
exercises.overview: The overhead carry challenges shoulder stability, core strength, and postural control while carrying weights overhead. It's excellent for building functional strength and improving shoulder health when performed with proper technique.
exercises.safetyInfo: This exercise requires good shoulder mobility and stability. Those with shoulder impingement, rotator cuff injuries, or neck problems should avoid overhead carries. Start with lighter weights and shorter distances.

commonMistakes: Allowing the cable to pull your torso toward the anchor point
Using too much weight and compensating with body movement
Pressing at an angle rather than straight ahead
exerciseTips: Maintain a rigid plank position throughout the movement
Press the handle straight out from your chest
Keep your hips square and avoid rotating
Engage your glutes to maintain stability
instructions: Connect a standard handle to a tower, and—if possible—position the cable to shoulder height. If not, a low pulley will suffice.
With your side to the cable, grab the handle with both hands and step away from the tower. You should be approximately arm's length away from the pulley, with the tension of the weight on the cable.
With your feet positioned hip-width apart and knees slightly bent, hold the cable to the middle of your chest. This will be your starting position.
Press the cable away from your chest, fully extending both arms. You core should be tight and engaged.
Hold the repetition for several seconds before returning to the starting position.
At the conclusion of the set, repeat facing the other direction.
exercises.name: Pallof Press
exercises.overview: An anti-rotation core exercise that challenges stability while pressing against lateral forces. Excellent for developing functional core strength and improving posture.
exercises.safetyInfo: Generally safe for most individuals. Start with lighter resistance to master the movement pattern before progressing weight.

commonMistakes: Rotating from the hips instead of the thoracic spine
Moving too quickly through the rotation
Using the arms to generate the rotational movement
exerciseTips: Control the rotation with your core, not your arms
Keep your hips facing forward during the twist
Maintain constant tension on the cable
Return to center position between each rep
instructions: Connect a standard handle to a tower, and position the cable to shoulder height.

With your side to the cable, grab the handle with one hand and step away from the tower. You should be approximately arm's length away from the pulley, with the tension of the weight on the cable. Align outstretched arm with cable.
With your feet positioned hip-width apart, pull the cable into your chest and grab the handle with your other hand. Both hands should be on the handle at this time.
Facing forward, press the cable away from your chest. You core should be tight and engaged.
Keeping your hips straight, twist your torso away from the pulley until you get a full quarter rotation.
Maintain your rigid stance and straight arms. Return to the neutral position in a slow and controlled manner. Your arms should be extended in front of you.
With the side tension still engaging your core, bring your hands to your chest and immediately press outward to a fully extended position. This constitutes one rep.
Repeat to failure.
Then, reposition and repeat the same series of movements on the opposite side.
exercises.name: Pallof Press With Rotation
exercises.overview: An advanced anti-rotation exercise that adds controlled rotation to challenge core stability in multiple planes. Develops functional strength for athletic movements.
exercises.safetyInfo: Requires good core stability and spinal mobility. Those with lower back issues should master the basic Pallof Press before adding rotation.

commonMistakes: Using momentum to lift the weight
Lifting the forearms off the bench
Using weight that's too heavy for proper form
exerciseTips: Keep your forearms flat against the bench throughout
Move only at the wrist joint
Control the eccentric lowering phase
Use a full range of motion from flexion to extension
instructions: Start out by placing two dumbbells on one side of a flat bench.
Kneel down on both of your knees so that your body is facing the flat bench.

Use your arms to grab both of the dumbbells with a pronated grip (palms facing down) and bring them up so that your forearms are resting against the flat bench. Your wrists should be hanging over the edge.
Start out by curling your wrist upwards and exhaling.
Slowly lower your wrists back down to the starting position while inhaling.
Your forearms should be stationary as your wrist is the only movement needed to perform this exercise.
Repeat for the recommended amount of repetitions.
exercises.name: Palms-Down Dumbbell Wrist Curl Over A Bench
exercises.name_pt: Rosca de Punho com Halteres no Banco - Palmas para Baixo
exercises.overview: An isolation exercise targeting the wrist extensor muscles to improve grip strength and forearm balance. Essential for preventing wrist injuries and improving grip endurance.
exercises.safetyInfo: Start with very light weight as wrist extensors are typically weak. Those with wrist injuries should avoid or modify this exercise.
variations: Seated One-Arm Dumbbell Palms-Down Wrist Curl

commonMistakes: Lifting forearms off the bench during the movement
Using momentum instead of controlled wrist extension
Gripping the bar too tightly which reduces range of motion
exerciseTips: Keep forearms pressed firmly against the bench throughout the movement
Control the lowering phase for 2-3 seconds to maximize muscle engagement
Focus on moving only at the wrist joint while keeping forearms stationary
Use a full range of motion from complete flexion to full extension
instructions: Start out by placing a barbell on one side of a flat bench.
Kneel down on both of your knees so that your body is facing the flat bench.
Use your arms to grab the barbell with a pronated grip (palms down) and bring them up so that your forearms are resting against the flat bench. Your wrists should be hanging over the edge.
Start out by curling your wrist upwards and exhaling.
Slowly lower your wrists back down to the starting position while inhaling.
Your forearms should be stationary as your wrist is the only movement needed to perform this exercise.
Repeat for the recommended amount of repetitions.
exercises.name: Palms-Down Wrist Curl Over A Bench
exercises.name_pt: Rosca de Punho no Banco - Palmas para Baixo
exercises.overview: This isolation exercise specifically targets the wrist extensors on the back of the forearms. It helps balance forearm strength and can prevent overuse injuries from gripping activities.
exercises.safetyInfo: Avoid this exercise if you have existing wrist pain or carpal tunnel syndrome. Start with light weight as wrist extensors are typically weaker than flexors.
variations: Seated One-Arm Dumbbell Palms-Down Wrist Curl

commonMistakes: Moving forearms during the curling motion
Not achieving full wrist extension at the bottom position
Using excessive weight that limits range of motion
exerciseTips: Maintain constant contact between forearms and bench surface
Allow wrists to extend fully at the bottom for maximum stretch
Curl wrists up as high as possible while squeezing forearms
Keep grip relaxed to allow full range of motion
instructions: Start out by placing a barbell on one side of a flat bench.
Kneel down on both of your knees so that your body is facing the flat bench.
Use your arms to grab the barbell with a supinated grip (palms up) and bring them up so that your forearms are resting against the flat bench. Your wrists should be hanging over the edge.
Start out by curling your wrist upwards and exhaling.
Slowly lower your wrists back down to the starting position while inhaling.
Your forearms should be stationary as your wrist is the only movement needed to perform this exercise.
Repeat for the recommended amount of repetitions.
exercises.name: Palms-Up Barbell Wrist Curl Over A Bench
exercises.name_pt: Rosca de Punho com Barra no Banco - Palmas para Cima
exercises.overview: This isolation exercise develops the wrist flexor muscles on the underside of the forearms. It's essential for grip strength and wrist stability in pulling movements.
exercises.safetyInfo: Start with light weight to assess wrist mobility and strength. Discontinue if you experience sharp wrist pain or numbness.
variations: Seated One-Arm Dumbbell Palms-Down Wrist Curl

commonMistakes: Rushing through the movement without proper control
Not maintaining forearm contact with the bench
Using dumbbells that are too heavy for proper form
exerciseTips: Work one arm at a time to identify and correct strength imbalances
Keep the non-working hand on the bench for stability
Focus on slow, controlled movements in both directions
Allow dumbbells to roll slightly into fingers at bottom for deeper stretch
instructions: Start out by placing two dumbbells on one side of a flat bench.
Kneel down on both of your knees so that your body is facing the flat bench.
Use your arms to grab both of the dumbbells with a supinated grip (palms up) and bring them up so that your forearms are resting against the flat bench. Your wrists should be hanging over the edge.
Start out by curling your wrist upwards and exhaling.
Slowly lower your wrists back down to the starting position while inhaling. Make sure to inhale during this part of the exercise.
Your forearms should be stationary as your wrist is the only movement needed to perform this exercise.
Repeat for the recommended amount of repetitions.
exercises.name: Palms-Up Dumbbell Wrist Curl Over A Bench
exercises.name_pt: Rosca de Punho com Halteres no Banco - Palmas para Cima
exercises.overview: This dumbbell variation allows for unilateral training of wrist flexors, helping identify and correct strength imbalances between arms while improving grip strength.
exercises.safetyInfo: The unilateral nature allows you to address imbalances but start conservatively. Stop if you experience tingling or sharp pain in the wrists.
variations: Seated One-Arm Dumbbell Palms-Down Wrist Curl

commonMistakes: Descending too far which places excessive stress on the shoulder joint
Allowing shoulders to roll forward and internally rotate
Using momentum to bounce out of the bottom position
exerciseTips: Lean slightly forward to emphasize chest or stay upright for tricep focus
Descend until upper arms are parallel to ground or slightly below
Press through palms while keeping shoulders down and back
Maintain tight core throughout the entire movement
instructions: Stand between a set of parallel bars. Place a hand on each bar, and then take a small jump to help you get into the starting position with your arms locked out.
Begin by flexing the elbow, lowering your body until your arms break 90 degrees. Avoid swinging, and maintain good posture throughout the descent.
Reverse the motion by extending the elbow, pushing yourself back up into the starting position.
Repeat for the desired number of repetitions.
exercises.name: Parallel Bar Dip
exercises.overview: This compound bodyweight exercise primarily targets the triceps while also engaging the chest and front deltoids. It's excellent for building upper body pressing strength and muscle mass.
exercises.safetyInfo: Avoid if you have shoulder impingement or previous shoulder injuries. The deep stretch position can stress the anterior shoulder capsule.

commonMistakes: Bouncing the bar off the chest instead of pausing
Losing tension and allowing the bar to sink during the pause
Changing grip or body position during the pause
exerciseTips: Keep the bar motionless during the pause - no sinking or bouncing
Maintain tension in your chest and lats during the pause
Focus on leg drive and maintaining your arch throughout the lift
Control the descent to set up a proper pause position
instructions: Lie on a bench with your eyes under the barbell, feet firmly planted on the floor.
Grip the bar with hands slightly wider than shoulder-width and create a slight arch in your back.
Unrack the weight and position it over your chest with arms fully extended.
Lower the bar slowly to your chest, touching lightly at the nipple line or slightly below.
Pause for 1-2 seconds with the bar motionless on your chest without bouncing.
Press the bar back to the starting position by driving through your chest and extending your arms fully.
exercises.name: Pause Bench Press
exercises.overview: The pause bench press eliminates the stretch reflex by requiring a dead stop on the chest, building raw pressing strength and improving competition-style bench pressing. It's particularly effective for developing strength off the chest.
exercises.safetyInfo: Always use a spotter when performing heavy bench press variations. The pause increases difficulty significantly, so reduce weight compared to your regular bench press. Ensure proper warm-up of shoulders and chest.

commonMistakes: Relaxing and losing tension during the bottom pause
Pausing for inconsistent amounts of time between repetitions
Allowing knees to cave inward during the pause position
exerciseTips: Maintain full-body tension throughout the entire pause duration
Keep chest up and knees tracking over toes during the pause
Focus on explosive drive out of the bottom position after the pause
Breathe at the top between repetitions rather than during the pause
instructions: Set up under barbell with feet shoulder-width apart and bar positioned on upper traps.
Unrack the bar and step back into proper squat stance with core engaged.
Descend slowly with control until reaching the bottom position of your squat.
Pause at the bottom for 2-3 seconds while maintaining tension and proper position.
Drive explosively upward from the paused position to return to starting stance.
exercises.name: Pause Squat
exercises.overview: Pause squats involve holding the bottom position for 2-3 seconds, eliminating bounce and building strength from compromised positions. This intermediate variation improves bottom position strength and control.
exercises.safetyInfo: The pause increases time under tension and can be more demanding on knee joints. Those with knee issues should start with shorter pauses and monitor comfort levels.

commonMistakes: Bouncing the weight off the floor instead of coming to a complete stop
Allowing the torso to rise up during the pulling motion
Using body momentum instead of strict pulling strength
exerciseTips: Start each rep from a complete dead stop on the floor
Pull explosively to maximize power development
Keep your torso parallel to the floor throughout the entire movement
Focus on pulling your elbows back rather than lifting with your hands
instructions: Load a barbell with plates and place it on the floor, stand with feet hip-width apart behind the bar.
Bend at the hips and knees to grip the bar with hands slightly wider than shoulder-width using an overhand grip.
Keep your back straight and torso parallel to the floor with the bar resting on the ground.
Explosively pull the bar from the floor to your lower chest/upper abdomen.
Pause briefly with the bar touching your torso and shoulder blades squeezed together.
Lower the bar back to the floor under control and let it come to a complete stop before the next rep.
exercises.name: Pendlay Row
exercises.overview: The Pendlay row is a strict barbell rowing variation that starts from a dead stop on the floor each rep. This eliminates momentum and develops explosive pulling strength while targeting the lats and supporting back muscles.
exercises.safetyInfo: Requires good hip hinge mobility and back strength to maintain proper position. Those with lower back issues should master the hip hinge pattern with lighter weights first.

commonMistakes: Pushing hips too high causing back extension
Using momentum instead of controlled movement
Allowing knees to cave inward
exerciseTips: Squeeze glutes at the top position
Keep core engaged throughout movement
Drive through heels not toes
Maintain neutral spine alignment
instructions: Lay on a ball so that your upper back is on the ball with your hips unsupported. Both feet should be flat on the floor, hip width apart or wider. This will be your starting position.
Begin by extending the hips using your glutes and hamstrings, raising your hips upward as you bridge.
Pause at the top of the motion and return to the starting position.
exercises.name: Physioball Hip Bridge
exercises.name_pt: Ponte de Quadril na Bola Suíça
exercises.overview: An unstable surface hip bridge that targets the glutes while challenging core stability and balance through the use of an exercise ball.
exercises.safetyInfo: Ensure the exercise ball is properly inflated and stable. Avoid overextending the lower back at the top of the movement.

commonMistakes: Allowing the hips to drop and turning it into a regular push-up
Using the legs to assist instead of relying on shoulder strength
Not maintaining the pike position throughout the movement
exerciseTips: Keep your legs as straight as possible to maintain proper pike position
Focus the movement on your shoulders rather than using your legs
Maintain steady breathing throughout the movement
Start with partial range of motion and gradually increase depth
instructions: Start in a downward dog position with hands shoulder-width apart and hips high in the air.
