Walk your feet closer to your hands to create a sharp angle at your hips with straight legs.
Keep your head in a neutral position looking down between your hands.
Lower the top of your head toward the floor by bending your arms at the elbows.
Descend until your head nearly touches the ground or as low as your range of motion allows.
Press through your palms to return to the starting pike position with arms fully extended.
exercises.name: Pike Push Up
exercises.overview: The pike push-up is a bodyweight exercise that targets the shoulders in a vertical pressing pattern similar to an overhead press. It serves as excellent preparation for handstand push-ups while building shoulder strength and stability.
exercises.safetyInfo: This exercise places the body in a semi-inverted position, so those with high blood pressure or neck issues should be cautious. Ensure adequate shoulder mobility before attempting full range of motion.

commonMistakes: Losing tightness by relaxing too much on the pins between reps
Setting pins at inconsistent heights that don't match squat depth
Using too much weight without accounting for lack of stretch reflex
exerciseTips: Set pins at the exact depth you want to train from
Generate maximum tension before initiating the concentric movement
Drive through whole foot while keeping knees aligned with toes
Maintain rigid core throughout the entire range of motion
instructions: Set safety pins in power rack at desired squat depth and place loaded barbell on pins.
Get into bottom squat position under the bar with feet positioned shoulder-width apart.
Position bar on upper traps and grip with hands evenly spaced outside shoulders.
Create tension throughout body, pause briefly, then explosively drive through heels to stand.
Control the eccentric lowering back to pins and reset for next repetition.
exercises.name: Pin Squat
exercises.overview: Pin squats eliminate the stretch reflex by starting from a dead stop at the bottom position, developing pure concentric strength. This advanced technique is excellent for building explosive power and overcoming sticking points.
exercises.safetyInfo: Advanced lifters only due to the unique loading pattern and lack of eccentric assistance. Proper pin height is crucial to avoid joint stress from awkward positions.

commonMistakes: Allowing the extended leg to touch the ground for assistance
Leaning too far forward and losing balance
Not achieving full depth due to lack of mobility or strength
exerciseTips: Keep your extended leg active and straight throughout the entire movement
Focus on sitting back into your heel rather than letting your knee drift forward
Use your arms actively for counterbalance
Start with assisted versions using a suspension trainer or holding onto something
instructions: Stand on one leg with the other leg extended straight out in front of you parallel to the ground.
Extend your arms forward for balance and keep your torso as upright as possible.
Begin lowering yourself by bending your working knee and pushing your hips back.
Descend as low as possible while keeping your extended leg straight and off the ground.
At the bottom position, your hamstring should touch your calf on the working leg.
Drive through your heel to return to the standing position while maintaining balance and leg position.
exercises.name: Pistol Squat
exercises.overview: The pistol squat is one of the most challenging bodyweight exercises, requiring exceptional single-leg strength, mobility, and balance. It's a full-range unilateral squat that builds functional strength and addresses imbalances.
exercises.safetyInfo: This is an advanced exercise requiring significant ankle mobility, hip flexibility, and single-leg strength. Progress gradually through assisted variations and ensure adequate warm-up of ankles and hips.

commonMistakes: Allowing hips to sag creating banana shape
Hiking hips too high reducing core engagement
Holding breath during the exercise
exerciseTips: Keep body in straight line from head to heels
Engage core by pulling belly button toward spine
Breathe steadily throughout hold
Focus on quality over duration
instructions: Get into a prone position on the floor, supporting your weight on your toes and your forearms. Your arms are bent and directly below the shoulder.
Keep your body straight at all times, and hold this position as long as possible. To increase difficulty, an arm or leg can be raised.
exercises.name: Plank
exercises.overview: A fundamental isometric core strengthening exercise that builds stability and endurance in the deep abdominal muscles while teaching proper spinal alignment.
exercises.safetyInfo: Stop if you experience lower back pain. Progress gradually by increasing hold time rather than forcing poor form for longer durations.

commonMistakes: Using palm instead of fingertips for grip
Allowing plates to slide during hold
Starting with plates too heavy for proper form
exerciseTips: Squeeze plates using fingertips and thumb
Keep wrist in neutral position
Focus on smooth sides facing outward
Hold until grip failure
instructions: Grab two wide-rimmed plates and put them together with the smooth sides facing outward
Use your fingers to grip the outside part of the plate and your thumb for the other side thus holding both plates together. This is the starting position.
Squeeze the plate with your fingers and thumb. Hold this position for as long as you can.
Repeat for the recommended amount of sets prescribed in your program.
Switch arms and repeat the movements.
exercises.name: Plate Pinch
exercises.overview: A grip strengthening exercise that specifically targets pinch grip strength using weight plates, improving thumb and finger strength for enhanced overall grip performance.
exercises.safetyInfo: Use appropriate weight plates to avoid dropping. Start with lighter weights and progress gradually to prevent overuse injuries to fingers and thumbs.
variations: Seated One-Arm Dumbbell Palms-Down Wrist Curl

commonMistakes: Using momentum instead of controlled movement
Allowing the feet to touch the ground during the exercise
Rotating only the arms rather than the entire torso
exerciseTips: Keep your core engaged throughout the entire movement
Move the plate in a controlled arc from side to side
Maintain a slight backward lean to engage your abs
Focus on rotating through your torso, not just moving your arms
instructions: Lie down on the floor or an exercise mat with your legs fully extended and your upper body upright. Grab the plate by its sides with both hands out in front of your abdominals with your arms slightly bent.

Slowly cross your legs near your ankles and lift them up off the ground. Your knees should also be bent slightly. Note: Move your upper body back slightly to help keep you balanced turning this exercise. This is the starting position.
Move the plate to the left side and touch the floor with it. Breathe out as you perform that movement.

Come back to the starting position as you breathe in and then repeat the movement but this time to the right side of the body. Tip: Use a slow controlled movement at all times. Jerking motions can injure the back.
Repeat for the recommended amount of repetitions.
exercises.name: Plate Twist
exercises.overview: The plate twist is a weighted core exercise that targets the obliques and rectus abdominis through rotational movement. It builds rotational strength and stability in the core.
exercises.safetyInfo: Avoid jerky or explosive movements which can strain the lower back. Those with lower back issues should keep feet on the ground or avoid this exercise.

commonMistakes: Allowing the hips to lift off the ground
Moving too quickly through the range of motion
Not maintaining tension during the eccentric phase
exerciseTips: Keep your hips pressed into the floor throughout the movement
Focus on pulling your heel toward your glutes
Control the eccentric portion as you extend your leg back
Maintain tension in the hamstring during the entire range of motion
instructions: For this movement a wooden floor or similar is needed. Lay on your back with your legs extended. Place a gym towel or a light weight underneath your heel. This will be your starting position.
Begin the movement by flexing the knee, keeping your other leg straight.
Continue bringing the heel closer to you, sliding it on the floor.
At full knee flexion, reverse the movement to return to the starting position.
exercises.name: Platform Hamstring Slides
exercises.overview: Platform hamstring slides are an isolation exercise that targets the hamstrings through knee flexion while lying supine. This exercise helps develop hamstring strength and can aid in injury prevention.
exercises.safetyInfo: This is a low-impact exercise suitable for most fitness levels. Ensure the sliding surface is appropriate to prevent excessive friction or sudden stops.

commonMistakes: Allowing knees to cave inward
Leaning forward excessively during the descent
Not achieving full depth with thighs parallel to floor
exerciseTips: Keep your knees tracking in line with your toes
Drive through your heels to return to standing
Maintain an upright torso throughout the movement
Squeeze your glutes at the top of the movement
instructions: Hold a dumbbell at the base with both hands and stand straight up. Move your legs so that they are wider than shoulder width apart from each other with your knees slightly bent.
Your toes should be facing out. Note: Your arms should be stationary while performing the exercise. This is the starting position.
Slowly bend the knees and lower your legs until your thighs are parallel to the floor. Make sure to inhale as this is the eccentric part of the exercise.
Press mainly with the heel of the foot to bring the body back to the starting position while exhaling.
Repeat for the recommended amount of repetitions.
exercises.name: Plie Dumbbell Squat
exercises.overview: The plie dumbbell squat uses a wide stance to emphasize the inner thighs and glutes while still targeting the quadriceps. This variation allows for greater adductor involvement than traditional squats.
exercises.safetyInfo: Ensure proper knee alignment to avoid stress on the knee joints. Those with hip mobility issues should start with a narrower stance and gradually widen as flexibility improves.

commonMistakes: Landing too heavily and losing control
Allowing the hips to sag during the movement
Not achieving full range of motion in the pushup
exerciseTips: Generate explosive power from your chest and arms
Land softly with control on each side
Keep your core tight throughout the movement
Maintain proper pushup form despite the dynamic nature
instructions: Place a kettlebell on the floor. Place yourself in a pushup position, on your toes with one hand on the ground and one hand holding the kettlebell, with your elbows extended. This will be your starting position.
Begin by lowering yourself as low as you can, keeping your back straight.

Quickly and forcefully reverse direction, pushing yourself up to the other side of the kettlebell, switching hands as you do so. Continue the movement by descending and repeating the movement back and forth.
exercises.name: Plyo Kettlebell Pushups
exercises.overview: Plyo kettlebell pushups combine plyometric training with unilateral challenges, developing explosive upper body power and coordination. This advanced movement builds functional pressing strength.
exercises.safetyInfo: This is an advanced exercise requiring significant upper body strength and coordination. Beginners should master standard pushups and plyometric pushups before attempting this variation.

commonMistakes: Letting the bar drift away from the body during the pull
Catching the bar on the fingertips instead of racking on shoulders
Not fully extending hips and knees during the second pull
exerciseTips: Drive through heels explosively during the second pull
Keep the bar close to your body throughout the movement
Catch the bar with elbows high and parallel to the floor
Use your hips to generate power, not just your arms
instructions: Stand with your feet slightly wider than shoulder width apart and toes pointing out slightly.
Squat down and grasp bar with a closed, pronated grip. Your hands should be slightly wider than shoulder width apart outside knees with elbows fully extended.
Place the bar about 1 inch in front of your shins and over the balls of your feet.
Your back should be flat or slightly arched, your chest held up and out and your shoulder blades should be retracted.
Keep your head in a neutral position (in line with vertebral column and not tilted or rotated) with your eyes focused straight ahead. Inhale during this phase.

Lift the bar from the floor by forcefully extending the hips and the knees as you exhale. Tip: The upper torso should maintain the same angle. Do not bend at the waist yet and do not let the hips rise before the shoulders (this would have the effect of pushing the glutes in the air and stretching the hamstrings.
Keep elbows fully extended with the head in a neutral position and the shoulders over the bar.
As the bar raises keep it as close to the shins as possible.
As the bar passes the knees, thrust your hips forward and slightly bend the knees to avoid locking them. Tip: At this point your thighs should be against the bar.
Keep the back flat or slightly arched, elbows fully extended and your head neutral. Tip: You will hold your breath until the next phase.
Inhale and then forcefully and quickly extend your hips and knees and stand on your toes.

Keep the bar as close to your body as possible. Tip: Your back should be flat with the elbows pointed out to the sides and your head in a neutral position. Also, keep your shoulders over the bar and arms straight as long as possible.
When your lower body joints are fully extended, shrug the shoulders upward rapidly without letting the elbows flex yet. Exhale during this portion of the movement.
As the shoulders reach their highest elevation flex your elbows to begin pulling your body under the bar.

Continue to pull the arms as high and as long as possible. Tip: Due to the explosive nature of this phase, your torso will be erect or with an arched back, your head will be tilted back slightly and your feet may lose contact with the floor.
After the lower body has fully extended and the bar reaches near maximal height, pull your body under the bar and rotate the arms around and under the bar.
Simultaneously, flex the hips and knees into a quarter squat position.
Once the arms are under the bar, inhale and then lift your elbows to position the upper arms parallel to the floor. Rack the bar across the front of your collar bones and front shoulder muscles.
Catch the bar with an erect and tight torso, a neutral head position and flat feet. Exhale during this movement.
Stand up by extending the hips and knees to a fully erect position.
Lower the bar by gradually reducing the muscular tension of the arms to allow a controlled descent of the bar to the thighs. Inhale during this movement.
Simultaneously flex the hips and knees to cushion the impact of the bar on the thighs.
Squat down with the elbows fully extended until the bar touches the floor.
Start over at Phase 1 and repeat for the recommended amount of repetitions.
exercises.name: Power Clean
exercises.overview: The power clean is an explosive Olympic weightlifting movement that develops total-body power and athletic performance. It trains the body to generate force rapidly while improving coordination and strength.
exercises.safetyInfo: This exercise requires proper coaching and gradual progression due to its technical complexity. Avoid if you have wrist, shoulder, or lower back injuries.

commonMistakes: Using too much weight and compromising form
Raising weights above shoulder level
Allowing torso to sway or lean during movement
exerciseTips: Keep slight bend in elbows throughout movement
Control the weight on both lifting and lowering phases
Focus on lifting from shoulder joint, not momentum
Maintain neutral spine and avoid swaying
instructions: Stand up with your torso upright and a dumbbell on each hand being held at arms length. The elbows should be close to the torso.
The palms of the hands should be facing your torso. Your feet should be about shoulder width apart. This will be your starting position.
Keeping your arms straight and the torso stationary, lift the weights out to your sides until they are about shoulder level height while exhaling.

Feel the contraction for a second and begin to lower the weights back down to the starting position while inhaling. Tip: Keep the palms facing down with the little finger slightly higher while lifting and lowering the weights as it will concentrate the stress on your shoulders mainly.
Repeat for the recommended amount of repetitions.
exercises.name: Power Partials
exercises.overview: Power partials are a variation of lateral raises focusing on the most challenging portion of the range of motion. They effectively target the lateral deltoids for shoulder width and strength.
exercises.safetyInfo: Start with lighter weights to master the movement pattern. Avoid if experiencing shoulder impingement or rotator cuff issues.

commonMistakes: Allowing the elbows to flare outward
Using momentum to swing the weight up
Fully extending arms at the bottom causing joint stress
exerciseTips: Keep your chest pressed against the pad throughout
Control the negative portion slowly
Stop just short of full arm extension to maintain tension
Squeeze your biceps hard at the top position
instructions: To perform this movement you will need a preacher bench and an E-Z bar. Grab the E-Z curl bar at the close inner handle (either have someone hand you the bar which is preferable or grab the bar from the front bar rest provided by most preacher benches). The palm of your hands should be facing forward and they should be slightly tilted inwards due to the shape of the bar.
With the upper arms positioned against the preacher bench pad and the chest against it, hold the E-Z Curl Bar at shoulder length. This will be your starting position.
As you breathe in, slowly lower the bar until your upper arm is extended and the biceps is fully stretched.
As you exhale, use the biceps to curl the weight up until your biceps is fully contracted and the bar is at shoulder height. Squeeze the biceps hard and hold this position for a second.
Repeat for the recommended amount of repetitions.
exercises.name: Preacher Curl
exercises.overview: The preacher curl isolates the biceps by eliminating momentum and stabilizing the upper arms against a pad. This creates constant tension throughout the range of motion for focused bicep development.
exercises.safetyInfo: Avoid locking out completely at the bottom to prevent elbow hyperextension. Use a controlled tempo to prevent bicep tears, especially with heavier weights.

commonMistakes: Rotating wrists during the movement
Using different tempos for each arm
Not fully controlling the eccentric portion
exerciseTips: Maintain neutral grip throughout the entire movement
Keep both arms moving in unison
Focus on squeezing at the top of each rep
Control the descent to maximize time under tension
instructions: Place the upper part of both arms on top of the preacher bench as you hold a dumbbell in each hand with the palms facing each other (neutral grip).
As you breathe in, slowly lower the dumbbells until your upper arm is extended and the biceps is fully stretched.
As you exhale, use the biceps to curl the weight up until your biceps is fully contracted and the dumbbells are at shoulder height.
Squeeze the biceps hard for a second at the contracted position and repeat for the recommended amount of repetitions.
exercises.name: Preacher Hammer Dumbbell Curl
exercises.overview: This variation of the preacher curl uses a neutral grip to target the biceps while emphasizing the brachialis and brachioradialis. The hammer grip allows for heavier loads and reduced joint stress.
exercises.safetyInfo: The neutral grip reduces stress on the wrists and elbows compared to supinated curls. Still avoid full lockout at the bottom to protect the elbow joint.
variations: Seated One-Arm Dumbbell Palms-Down Wrist Curl

commonMistakes: Performing the sit-up and press as separate movements
Arching the back excessively during the press
Using too much weight initially
exerciseTips: Coordinate the sit-up and press movements simultaneously
Engage your glutes to stabilize your pelvis
Press the barbell in a straight line overhead
Keep your core tight throughout both movements
instructions: To begin, lie down on a bench with a barbell resting on your chest. Position your legs so they are secure on the extension of the abdominal bench. This is the starting position.

While inhaling, tighten your abdominals and glutes. Simultaneously curl your torso as you do when performing a sit-up and press the barbell to an overhead position while exhaling. Tip: Use your arms to push the barbell out as you perform this exercise while still focusing on the abdominal muscles.
Lower your upper body back down to the starting position while bringing the barbell back down to your torso. Remember to breathe in while lowering the body.
Repeat for the recommended amount of repetitions.
exercises.name: Press Sit-Up
exercises.overview: The press sit-up combines core strengthening with upper body pressing in one compound movement. It challenges coordination while building functional strength and core stability under load.
exercises.safetyInfo: This is an advanced exercise requiring good shoulder mobility and core strength. Start with very light weight to master coordination. Avoid if you have shoulder impingement or lower back issues.

commonMistakes: Partner providing inconsistent resistance
Lifting hips off the ground during the curl
Moving too quickly through the range of motion
exerciseTips: Communicate constantly with your partner about resistance level
Keep your hips pressed down throughout the movement
Focus on controlled movement in both directions
Maintain steady breathing during the resistance
instructions: You will need a partner for this exercise. Lay face down with your legs straight. Your assistant will place their hand on your heel.

To begin, flex the knee to curl your leg up. Your partner should provide resistance, starting light and increasing the pressure as the movement is completed. Communicate with your partner to monitor appropriate resistance levels.
Pause at the top, returning the leg to the starting position as your partner provides resistance going the other direction.
exercises.name: Prone Manual Hamstring
exercises.name_pt: Flexão de Posterior Manual de Bruços
exercises.overview: This partner-assisted exercise provides variable resistance to strengthen the hamstrings through their full range of motion. It's useful for rehabilitation or when equipment isn't available.
exercises.safetyInfo: Requires clear communication between partners to avoid injury. The assisting partner should gradually increase resistance and be responsive to feedback about appropriate force levels.

commonMistakes: Pulling with arms instead of driving hips
Rounding back at bottom position
Standing too close to cable machine
exerciseTips: Drive hips forward explosively
Keep chest up throughout movement
Squeeze glutes hard at top position
Maintain slight knee bend throughout
instructions: Begin standing a few feet in front of a low pulley with a rope or handle attached. Face away from the machine, straddling the cable, with your feet set wide apart.

Begin the movement by reaching through your legs as far as possible, bending at the hips. Keep your knees slightly bent. Keeping your arms straight, extend through the hip to stand straight up. Avoid pulling upward through the shoulders; all of the motion should originate through the hips.
exercises.name: Pull Through
exercises.overview: The pull through is an excellent hip hinge exercise that teaches proper posterior chain activation while building glute and hamstring strength with constant cable tension.
exercises.safetyInfo: Avoid if you have lower back injuries. Start with light weight to master the hip hinge pattern before progressing.

commonMistakes: Using momentum and swinging the body to lift the legs
Allowing the knees to drop too quickly on the descent
Hanging passively without engaging the lats and shoulders for stability
exerciseTips: Keep your shoulders engaged and avoid hanging passively from the bar
Focus on using your abs to lift your knees rather than swinging your legs
Maintain control on both the lifting and lowering phases of the movement
Breathe out as you lift your knees and breathe in as you lower them
instructions: Hang from a pull-up bar with arms fully extended and hands shoulder-width apart.
Engage your core and maintain a stable hanging position with minimal swaying.
Lift your knees toward your chest by flexing your hips and contracting your abdominals.
Lower your legs back to the starting position with control, maintaining tension in your core.
exercises.name: Pull Up Bar Hanging Knee Raise
exercises.name_pt: Elevação de Joelhos na Barra Fixa
exercises.overview: The pull-up bar hanging knee raise is a challenging core exercise that targets the abdominals while requiring significant grip strength and shoulder stability. It's excellent for developing functional core strength and improving hanging ability.
exercises.safetyInfo: Requires adequate grip strength and shoulder stability. Those with shoulder impingement or recent abdominal surgery should avoid this exercise. Build up hanging endurance before attempting multiple repetitions.

commonMistakes: Allowing the legs to drop below parallel during the hold
Bending the knees to make the exercise easier
Swinging or using momentum to get into position
exerciseTips: Keep your legs completely straight and pressed together
Engage your lats to maintain a strong shoulder position
Focus on pushing your hands down into the bar
Breathe steadily throughout the hold, don't hold your breath
instructions: Hang from a pull-up bar with an overhand grip, hands shoulder-width apart.
Engage your core and press your shoulders down away from your ears.
Lift your legs up until they are parallel to the floor, forming an L-shape with your body.
Hold this position while keeping your legs straight and together.
Maintain the hold for the desired duration, then slowly lower your legs back down.
exercises.name: Pull Up Bar L-Sit
exercises.overview: The Pull Up Bar L-Sit is an advanced isometric exercise that develops tremendous core strength, hip flexor flexibility, and shoulder stability. It's a staple movement in gymnastics that builds functional strength and body control.
exercises.safetyInfo: This advanced exercise requires significant core and shoulder strength. Build up with hanging knee raises and tuck L-sits before attempting the full L-sit. Those with shoulder impingement should avoid this exercise.

commonMistakes: Using excessive swinging or kipping to complete the movement
Allowing the shoulders to relax and hang passively
Bending the knees excessively to make the movement easier
exerciseTips: Keep your shoulders active and engaged throughout the movement
Focus on using your abs rather than momentum to lift your legs
Maintain straight legs for maximum difficulty and core activation
Control the descent to prevent excessive swinging and maintain tension
instructions: Hang from a pull-up bar with an overhand grip, arms fully extended and shoulders engaged.
Start with legs straight and together, maintaining active shoulders to prevent swinging.
Initiate the movement by contracting your abs and raising your legs up toward the bar.
Continue lifting until your toes touch or come close to the pull-up bar above you.
Slowly lower your legs back to the starting position with control, avoiding excessive swing.
exercises.name: Pull Up Bar Toes To Bar
exercises.name_pt: Toes to Bar na Barra Fixa
exercises.overview: Toes to bar is an advanced hanging abdominal exercise that targets the entire core while requiring significant grip strength and shoulder stability. This movement builds exceptional core strength and coordination.
exercises.safetyInfo: This advanced exercise requires significant grip strength and core stability. Those with shoulder issues should ensure adequate strength before attempting. Beginners should start with hanging knee raises.

commonMistakes: Using momentum or swinging
Not achieving full range of motion
Allowing shoulders to internally rotate at bottom
exerciseTips: Pull elbows down and back
Engage core throughout movement
Control the descent slowly
Focus on chest to bar contact
instructions: Grab the pull-up bar with the palms facing forward using the prescribed grip. Note on grips: For a wide grip, your hands need to be spaced out at a distance wider than your shoulder width. For a medium grip, your hands need to be spaced out at a distance equal to your shoulder width and for a close grip at a distance smaller than your shoulder width.

As you have both arms extended in front of you holding the bar at the chosen grip width, bring your torso back around 30 degrees or so while creating a curvature on your lower back and sticking your chest out. This is your starting position.

Pull your torso up until the bar touches your upper chest by drawing the shoulders and the upper arms down and back. Exhale as you perform this portion of the movement. Tip: Concentrate on squeezing the back muscles once you reach the full contracted position. The upper torso should remain stationary as it moves through space and only the arms should move. The forearms should do no other work other than hold the bar.
After a second on the contracted position, start to inhale and slowly lower your torso back to the starting position when your arms are fully extended and the lats are fully stretched.
Repeat this motion for the prescribed amount of repetitions.
exercises.name: Pullups
exercises.overview: Pullups are a fundamental compound exercise that builds serious upper body pulling strength, primarily targeting the lats while engaging multiple secondary muscles.
exercises.safetyInfo: Progress gradually to avoid shoulder impingement. Use assistance bands or negative reps if unable to perform full pullups.

commonMistakes: Pressing the bar overhead without using leg drive from the dip
Failing to drop into the receiving position quickly enough
exerciseTips: Keep your elbows forward during the front rack position
Use leg drive to initiate the movement, not just arm pressing
Drop under the bar quickly as you press it overhead
Maintain a tight core throughout the entire movement
instructions: Start with the barbell racked across your front shoulders in a front rack position, elbows forward.
Dip down by slightly bending your knees and hips, keeping your torso upright.
Explosively drive up through your legs and extend your hips to create upward momentum.
As the bar travels upward, press it overhead while simultaneously dropping into a quarter squat.
Lock out your arms overhead and stabilize the weight in the receiving position.
Stand up fully to complete the lift, then lower the bar back to your shoulders.
exercises.name: Push Jerk
exercises.overview: The push jerk is an Olympic weightlifting movement that develops explosive power and overhead pressing strength. It teaches the coordination of lower body drive with upper body pressing while building functional strength and power.
exercises.safetyInfo: This is an advanced Olympic lifting movement requiring excellent shoulder mobility, stability, and technique. Not suitable for beginners or those with shoulder, wrist, or back injuries. Requires proper coaching and progressive learning.

commonMistakes: Allowing hips to sag during transition
Not fully extending into side plank
Rushing through the movement
exerciseTips: Maintain rigid plank position
Rotate smoothly into side plank
Keep hips elevated throughout
Alternate sides each rep
instructions: Get into pushup position on the toes with your hands just outside of shoulder width.
Perform a pushup by allowing the elbows to flex. As you descend, keep your body straight.
Do one pushup and as you come up, shift your weight on the left side of the body, twist to the side while bringing the right arm up towards the ceiling in a side plank.
Lower the arm back to the floor for another pushup and then twist to the other side.
Repeat the series, alternating each side, for 10 or more reps.
exercises.name: Push Up to Side Plank
exercises.overview: A dynamic combination exercise that builds chest, shoulder, and core strength while challenging stability and coordination through the rotational component.
exercises.safetyInfo: Requires good core stability and shoulder mobility. Progress from standard pushups and side planks before attempting this combination movement.

commonMistakes: Placing hands too wide causing shoulder strain
Not achieving full range of motion
Allowing lower back to arch excessively
exerciseTips: Position hands wider than shoulders
Lower chest to floor completely
Keep body in straight line
Push up explosively
instructions: With your hands wide apart, support your body on your toes and hands in a plank position. Your elbows should be extended and your body straight. Do not allow your hips to sag. This will be your starting position.
To begin, allow the elbows to flex, lowering your chest to the floor as you inhale.
Using your pectoral muscles, press your upper body back up to the starting position by extending the elbows. Exhale as you perform this step.
After pausing at the contracted position, repeat the movement for the prescribed amount of repetitions.
exercises.name: Push-Up Wide
exercises.overview: A pushup variation that emphasizes chest development by using a wider hand position, increasing pectoral activation while reducing triceps involvement.
exercises.safetyInfo: Wide hand position increases shoulder stress. Avoid if experiencing shoulder impingement. Ensure adequate warm-up and don't place hands excessively wide.

commonMistakes: Flaring elbows out wide instead of keeping them close to body
Allowing hips to sag or pike up
Not going deep enough in the bottom position
exerciseTips: Keep your hands close together forming a diamond or triangle shape
Maintain a straight body line from head to heels
Control the descent to maximize triceps engagement
Push through your palms explosively on the way up
instructions: Lie on the floor face down and place your hands closer than shoulder width for a close hand position. Make sure that you are holding your torso up at arms' length.
Lower yourself until your chest almost touches the floor as you inhale.
Using your triceps and some of your pectoral muscles, press your upper body back up to the starting position and squeeze your chest. Breathe out as you perform this step.
After a second pause at the contracted position, repeat the movement for the prescribed amount of repetitions.
exercises.name: Push-Ups - Close Triceps Position
exercises.overview: A push-up variation that emphasizes triceps development by using a narrow hand position. This exercise builds upper body pushing strength with greater triceps activation than standard push-ups.
exercises.safetyInfo: Avoid if you have wrist pain or shoulder impingement. The narrow hand position places more stress on the wrists and requires good shoulder stability.

commonMistakes: Using too high elevation before building adequate strength
Letting hips drop or rise out of alignment
Rushing through the movement without control
exerciseTips: Start with a low elevation and progress higher as you get stronger
Keep your core tight to prevent sagging hips
Control the tempo on both up and down phases
Maintain proper hand placement at shoulder width
instructions: Lie on the floor face down and place your hands about 36 inches apart from each other holding your torso up at arms length.
Place your toes on top of a flat bench. This will allow your body to be elevated. Note: The higher the elevation of the flat bench, the higher the resistance of the exercise is.
Lower yourself until your chest almost touches the floor as you inhale.
Using your pectoral muscles, press your upper body back up to the starting position and squeeze your chest. Breathe out as you perform this step.
After a second pause at the contracted position, repeat the movement for the prescribed amount of repetitions.
exercises.name: Push-Ups With Feet Elevated
exercises.overview: An advanced push-up variation that increases upper chest activation by elevating the feet above the hands. This exercise provides greater resistance and emphasizes the clavicular head of the pectoralis major.
exercises.safetyInfo: Higher elevations significantly increase difficulty and shoulder stress. Start conservatively and ensure you can perform 15+ regular push-ups before progressing to elevated versions.

commonMistakes: Moving too quickly and losing balance
Placing feet too far apart on the ball
Neglecting core engagement leading to compensatory movements
exerciseTips: Engage your core throughout the entire movement
Start with the ball closer to your knees before progressing to toes
Move slowly to maintain balance on the unstable surface
Keep your body in a straight line despite the ball movement
instructions: Lie on the floor face down and place your hands about 36 inches apart from each other holding your torso up at arms length.
Place your toes on top of an exercise ball. This will allow your body to be elevated.
Lower yourself until your chest almost touches the floor as you inhale.
Using your pectoral muscles, press your upper body back up to the starting position and squeeze your chest. Breathe out as you perform this step.
After a second pause at the contracted position, repeat the movement for the prescribed amount of repetitions.
exercises.name: Push-Ups With Feet On An Exercise Ball
exercises.name_pt: Flexão com Pés na Bola Suíça
exercises.overview: A challenging push-up variation that combines upper body strengthening with core stability training. The unstable surface forces increased activation of stabilizing muscles throughout the body.
exercises.safetyInfo: Requires good balance and core stability. Have a spotter nearby when first attempting. Ensure the exercise ball is properly inflated and rated for your body weight.

commonMistakes: Allowing hips to sag creating a banana shape
Not going through full range of motion
Pushing through fingertips instead of full palm contact
exerciseTips: Keep your hands slightly wider than shoulder-width apart
Maintain a straight line from head to heels throughout the movement
Lower until your chest nearly touches the ground
Push through your palms and exhale on the way up
instructions: Lie on the floor face down and place your hands about 36 inches apart while holding your torso up at arms length.
Next, lower yourself downward until your chest almost touches the floor as you inhale.
Now breathe out and press your upper body back up to the starting position while squeezing your chest.
After a brief pause at the top contracted position, you can begin to lower yourself downward again for as many repetitions as needed.
exercises.name: Pushups
exercises.overview: The fundamental bodyweight pushing exercise that builds chest, shoulder, and triceps strength. Push-ups are versatile, require no equipment, and can be modified for all fitness levels.
exercises.safetyInfo: Generally safe for most individuals. Modify by performing on knees if full push-ups are too challenging. Stop if you experience wrist or shoulder pain.

commonMistakes: Compromising form when switching between hand positions
Going too wide causing shoulder strain
Not adjusting elbow path based on hand position
exerciseTips: Use wide grip to emphasize chest activation
Use close grip to target triceps more heavily
Maintain proper body alignment regardless of hand position
Alternate between positions within the same workout for variety
instructions: Lie on the floor face down and body straight with your toes on the floor and the hands wider than shoulder width for a wide hand position and closer than shoulder width for a close hand position. Make sure you are holding your torso up at arms length.
Lower yourself until your chest almost touches the floor as you inhale.
Using your pectoral muscles, press your upper body back up to the starting position and squeeze your chest. Breathe out as you perform this step.
After a second pause at the contracted position, repeat the movement for the prescribed amount of repetitions.
exercises.name: Pushups (Close and Wide Hand Positions)
exercises.overview: A push-up variation that allows you to target different muscles by changing hand placement. Wide grip emphasizes the chest while close grip targets the triceps more heavily.
exercises.safetyInfo: Wide grip positions may stress shoulders more, especially if you have previous shoulder injuries. Close grip requires more wrist stability and triceps strength.

commonMistakes: Allowing the elbows to flare out away from the body
Leaning forward or backward to compensate for the load
exerciseTips: Keep your core braced throughout the entire walk
Maintain an upright chest and avoid leaning forward
Take smaller steps to maintain better balance and control
Breathe steadily and avoid holding your breath
instructions: Clean two kettlebells into the front rack position with bells resting on your forearms.
Keep your elbows tight to your ribs and maintain an upright torso.
Take slow, controlled steps forward while maintaining proper posture.
Walk for the prescribed distance or time while keeping core engaged.
Lower the kettlebells safely to complete the exercise.
exercises.name: Rack Walk
exercises.overview: The rack walk is a loaded carry exercise where kettlebells are held in the front rack position while walking. It develops core stability, postural strength, and challenges anti-extension patterns.
exercises.safetyInfo: Those with shoulder impingement or wrist issues should be cautious. Ensure proper rack position to avoid neck strain and maintain neutral spine alignment.

commonMistakes: Allowing elbows to drift forward or swing during the curl
Using momentum or swaying the body to assist the movement
Not maintaining tension in the band at the bottom position
exerciseTips: Keep your elbows stationary at your sides throughout the movement
Maintain constant tension by not allowing the band to go slack
Control the negative portion of the rep for maximum benefit
Keep your wrists straight and avoid swinging or using momentum
instructions: Stand on the center of the resistance band with both feet, holding the handles with palms facing forward.
Keep your elbows close to your sides and maintain a straight posture with shoulders back.
Curl the handles up by flexing your biceps, bringing your hands toward your shoulders.
Squeeze your biceps at the top of the movement, then slowly lower back to the starting position.
exercises.name: Resistance Band Bicep Curl
exercises.overview: Resistance band bicep curls provide variable resistance that increases through the range of motion. They're perfect for home workouts and rehabilitation purposes.
exercises.safetyInfo: Inspect resistance bands regularly for nicks or tears that could cause snapping. Start with lighter resistance to avoid overstretching.

commonMistakes: Standing too close to the anchor point, reducing tension
Allowing the band to snap back uncontrolled
Not maintaining proper posture during the exercise
exerciseTips: Maintain constant tension in the band throughout the movement
Keep your core engaged for stability
Focus on squeezing your chest muscles during the press
Control the return phase to maximize muscle engagement
instructions: Anchor the resistance band at chest height behind you or wrap it around a sturdy object.
Hold the handles with both hands and step forward to create tension in the band.
Position yourself with feet staggered and arms extended at chest level.
Press the handles forward by extending your arms, bringing your hands together in front of your chest.
Slowly return to the starting position, controlling the band's resistance.
exercises.name: Resistance Band Chest Press
exercises.overview: The resistance band chest press is a versatile upper body exercise that targets the chest, shoulders, and triceps. It provides variable resistance and is perfect for home workouts or travel.
exercises.safetyInfo: Check the band for wear or damage before use to prevent snapping. Start with lighter resistance and progress gradually.

commonMistakes: Allowing the band to snap back quickly without controlling the descent
Raising the arms too high above shoulder level causing shoulder impingement
Using the traps to initiate the movement instead of the deltoids
exerciseTips: Keep constant tension on the band throughout the entire range of motion
Focus on leading with your pinkies to better target the lateral deltoids
Maintain a slight forward lean to align with the deltoid fibers
Control the eccentric portion slowly to maximize muscle activation
instructions: Stand on the center of a resistance band with feet shoulder-width apart, holding the handles at your sides.
Keep your arms slightly bent and maintain good posture with shoulders back and core engaged.
Raise your arms out to the sides until they reach shoulder height, keeping the band under tension.
Pause at the top and squeeze your lateral delts before slowly returning to the starting position.
Control the band's resistance on the way down to maintain tension throughout the movement.
exercises.name: Resistance Band Lateral Raise
exercises.overview: The resistance band lateral raise effectively targets the lateral deltoids using variable resistance that increases throughout the range of motion. This exercise is perfect for building shoulder width and can be performed anywhere with minimal equipment.
exercises.safetyInfo: This exercise is generally safe for most individuals. Those with shoulder impingement should avoid raising arms above shoulder height and may need to modify the range of motion.

commonMistakes: Rounding the shoulders and upper back during the pull
Using momentum instead of controlled movement
Allowing the band to snap back without control
exerciseTips: Keep your chest up and shoulders down throughout the movement
Squeeze your shoulder blades together at the end of each rep
Control the return phase to maintain tension in the band
Focus on pulling with your back muscles, not just your arms
instructions: Sit on the floor with legs extended and wrap the resistance band around your feet or a sturdy anchor point.
Grasp the band handles with both hands, arms extended forward and shoulders relaxed.
Pull the band toward your lower ribs by driving your elbows back and squeezing your shoulder blades together.
Hold the contracted position briefly, then slowly return to the starting position with control.
exercises.name: Resistance Band Row
exercises.overview: The Resistance Band Row is an excellent beginner-friendly exercise for developing back strength and improving posture. It provides variable resistance and is highly portable for home or travel workouts.
exercises.safetyInfo: Inspect the resistance band for wear or damage before use to prevent snapping. Start with lighter resistance and progress gradually to avoid overstraining the shoulder joints.

commonMistakes: Allowing the band to snap back up without control
Not maintaining proper squat depth due to band tension
Allowing knees to cave inward during the movement
exerciseTips: Keep constant tension in the band throughout the entire range of motion
Focus on controlling both the descent and ascent
Drive your knees out in line with your toes
Keep your weight evenly distributed across your entire foot
instructions: Stand on the resistance band with feet slightly wider than shoulder-width apart.
Hold the handles or ends of the band at shoulder height with elbows bent.
Keep your chest up and core engaged with toes pointed slightly outward.
Lower into a squat by pushing your hips back and bending your knees until thighs are parallel to the floor.
Drive through your heels to return to the starting position against the band's resistance.
Maintain tension in the band throughout the entire movement.
exercises.name: Resistance Band Squat
exercises.overview: The resistance band squat is a beginner-friendly compound exercise that targets the quadriceps while providing variable resistance throughout the movement. This exercise is excellent for learning squat mechanics and building functional strength.
exercises.safetyInfo: Generally very safe for most populations. Ensure the band is securely under your feet to prevent snapping. Check bands regularly for wear and replace if damaged.

commonMistakes: Allowing elbows to flare out to the sides
Using momentum instead of controlled muscle contraction
Letting the band snap back without control
exerciseTips: Keep your elbows close to your head throughout the movement
Maintain tension in the band at all times
Focus on squeezing your triceps at the top of the movement
Control the band's resistance during both phases
instructions: Stand with feet hip-width apart and step on one end of the resistance band with both feet.
Grasp the other end of the band with both hands and raise your arms overhead with elbows pointing forward.
Keep your upper arms stationary and lower the band behind your head by bending at the elbows.
Extend your arms back to the starting position by contracting your triceps.
Return to the overhead position with control.
exercises.name: Resistance Band Tricep Extension
exercises.name_pt: Extensão de Tríceps com Faixa Elástica
exercises.overview: The Resistance Band Tricep Extension is an isolation exercise that targets all three heads of the triceps using variable resistance. It provides a joint-friendly alternative to weights while maintaining constant tension throughout the range of motion.
exercises.safetyInfo: Check band integrity before use to prevent snapping. Avoid if you have shoulder impingement or elbow pain. Start with lighter resistance to assess comfort.

commonMistakes: Relying too heavily on band assistance instead of using it as a training tool
Setting bands that provide too much assistance, negating training effect
Poor setup causing uneven band tension or unsafe bar position
exerciseTips: Focus on explosive movement out of the bottom position with band assistance
Maintain proper squat mechanics despite the assistance from bands
Use the band assistance to handle supramaximal loads at the top position
Control the descent even though bands provide assistance at the bottom
instructions: Set up bands from top of rack to barbell, providing assistance at the bottom and less at top.
Position under bar with feet shoulder-width apart and bar resting on upper traps.
Unrack the bar feeling band assistance and step back into squat position.
Descend into squat with band providing increasing assistance as you go deeper.
Drive up explosively as band assistance decreases, handling full weight at the top.
exercises.name: Reverse Band Squat
exercises.overview: Reverse band squats use bands attached from above to provide assistance at the bottom while allowing full weight at the top. This advanced method enables supramaximal loading and speed development in the lockout portion.
exercises.safetyInfo: Advanced technique requiring proper band setup and squat proficiency. The changing assistance can alter movement patterns, so ensure proper form is maintained throughout.

commonMistakes: Using momentum to swing the weight up
Allowing elbows to drift forward during the curl
Gripping the bar too tightly causing forearm fatigue
exerciseTips: Keep your wrists straight and neutral throughout the movement
Control the eccentric portion slowly
Keep your elbows stationary at your sides
Focus on squeezing at the top of the movement
instructions: Stand up with your torso upright while holding a barbell at shoulder width with the elbows close to the torso. The palm of your hands should be facing down (pronated grip). This will be your starting position.

While holding the upper arms stationary, curl the weights while contracting the biceps as you breathe out. Only the forearms should move. Continue the movement until your biceps are fully contracted and the bar is at shoulder level. Hold the contracted position for a second as you squeeze the muscle.
Slowly begin to bring the bar back to starting position as your breathe in.
Repeat for the recommended amount of repetitions.
exercises.name: Reverse Barbell Curl
exercises.overview: A bicep curl performed with an overhand grip that emphasizes the brachioradialis and wrist extensors while still working the biceps. Excellent for balanced arm development and grip strength.
exercises.safetyInfo: Start with lighter weight than regular curls as the pronated grip is mechanically disadvantageous. Avoid if experiencing wrist or elbow pain.

commonMistakes: Lowering the weight too quickly
Not achieving full range of motion at the bottom
Positioning arms too high or low on the preacher pad
exerciseTips: Position your armpits at the top edge of the preacher pad
Lower the weight slowly with complete control
Keep your wrists locked in neutral position
Squeeze hard at the top contraction
instructions: Grab an EZ-bar using a shoulder width and palms down (pronated) grip.
Now place the upper part of both arms on top of the preacher bench and have your arms extended. This will be your starting position.
As you exhale, use the biceps to curl the weight up until your biceps are fully contracted and the barbell is at shoulder height. Squeeze the biceps hard for a second at the contracted position.
As you breathe in, slowly lower the barbell until your upper arms are extended and the biceps is fully stretched.
Repeat for the recommended amount of repetitions.
exercises.name: Reverse Barbell Preacher Curls
exercises.overview: A preacher curl variation using an overhand grip that targets the brachioradialis and wrist extensors more than traditional curls. The preacher pad provides stability and prevents cheating.
exercises.safetyInfo: The preacher position can stress the bicep tendon at full extension. Avoid bouncing at the bottom and use controlled movements throughout the range of motion.

commonMistakes: Allowing the cable to go slack at the bottom
Using the body to generate momentum
Gripping the handle too tightly
exerciseTips: Keep constant tension on the cable throughout the movement
Maintain a slight forward lean to optimize cable angle
Focus on controlled tempo both up and down
Keep your shoulders pulled back and chest up
instructions: Stand up with your torso upright while holding a bar attachment that is attached to a low pulley using a pronated (palms down) and shoulder width grip. Make sure also that you keep the elbows close to the torso. This will be your starting position.

While holding the upper arms stationary, curl the weights while contracting the biceps as you breathe out. Only the forearms should move. Continue the movement until your biceps are fully contracted and the bar is at shoulder level. Hold the contracted position for a second as you squeeze the muscle.
Slowly begin to bring the bar back to starting position as your breathe in.
Repeat for the recommended amount of repetitions.
exercises.name: Reverse Cable Curl
exercises.overview: A cable variation of the reverse curl that provides constant tension throughout the range of motion. The pronated grip emphasizes forearm extensors and brachioradialis development.
exercises.safetyInfo: The constant tension from cables provides a different stress pattern than free weights. Start conservatively with weight selection as the pronated grip is weaker than supinated.

commonMistakes: Using momentum to swing legs up
Not actually flexing the spine - just lifting legs
Allowing lower back to arch off the floor
exerciseTips: Focus on curling your pelvis toward your ribcage
Keep the movement slow and controlled
Exhale forcefully as you crunch up
Think about bringing your knees to your chest, not just lifting your legs
instructions: Lie down on the floor with your legs fully extended and arms to the side of your torso with the palms on the floor. Your arms should be stationary for the entire exercise.
Move your legs up so that your thighs are perpendicular to the floor and feet are together and parallel to the floor. This is the starting position.
While inhaling, move your legs towards the torso as you roll your pelvis backwards and you raise your hips off the floor. At the end of this movement your knees will be touching your chest.
Hold the contraction for a second and move your legs back to the starting position while exhaling.
Repeat for the recommended amount of repetitions.
exercises.name: Reverse Crunch
exercises.overview: An abdominal exercise that targets the lower portion of the rectus abdominis by bringing the knees toward the chest. More effective than leg raises for true abdominal strengthening.
exercises.safetyInfo: Keep movements controlled to avoid straining the lower back. Those with lower back issues should consider modifications or alternative exercises.

commonMistakes: Using too much weight causing momentum
Allowing elbows to bend excessively during movement
Not maintaining chest contact with bench
exerciseTips: Squeeze shoulder blades together at the top
Maintain slight bend in elbows throughout movement
Control the weight on the way down
Keep chest pressed against bench for stability
instructions: To begin, lie down on an incline bench with the chest and stomach pressing against the incline. Have the dumbbells in each hand with the palms facing each other (neutral grip).

Extend the arms in front of you so that they are perpendicular to the angle of the bench. The legs should be stationary while applying pressure with the ball of your toes. This is the starting position.

Maintaining the slight bend of the elbows, move the weights out and away from each other (to the side) in an arc motion while exhaling. Tip: Try to squeeze your shoulder blades together to get the best results from this exercise.
The arms should be elevated until they are parallel to the floor.
Feel the contraction and slowly lower the weights back down to the starting position while inhaling.
Repeat for the recommended amount of repetitions.
exercises.name: Reverse Flyes
exercises.overview: Reverse flyes target the posterior deltoids and upper back muscles to improve posture and shoulder stability. This isolation exercise helps balance anterior shoulder development from pressing movements.
exercises.safetyInfo: Avoid this exercise if you have shoulder impingement or recent shoulder injury. Start with light weight to master the movement pattern.
variations: Bent Over Two-Dumbbell Row With Palms In

commonMistakes: Rushing the rotation component
Lifting arms too high above head level
Not maintaining stable torso position on bench
exerciseTips: Rotate wrists smoothly from pronated to neutral grip
Squeeze shoulder blades together at top position
Maintain 30-degree bench angle for optimal positioning
Control both the lifting and rotating phases
instructions: To begin, lie down on an incline bench set at a 30-degree angle with the chest and stomach pressing against the incline.

Have the dumbbells in each hand with the palms facing down to the floor. Your arms should be in front of you so that they are perpendicular to the angle of the bench. Tip: Your elbows should have a slight bend. The legs should be stationary while applying pressure with the ball of your toes (your heels should not be touching the floor). This is the starting position.
Maintaining the slight bend of the elbows, move the weights out and away from each other in an arc motion while exhaling.

As you lift the weight, your wrist should externally rotate by 90-degrees so that you go from a palms down (pronated) grip to a palms facing each other (neutral) grip. Tip: Try to squeeze your shoulder blades together to get the best results from this exercise.
The arms should be elevated until they are level with the head.
Feel the contraction and slowly lower the weights back down to the starting position while inhaling.
Repeat for the recommended amount of repetitions.
exercises.name: Reverse Flyes With External Rotation
exercises.overview: This advanced variation of reverse flyes adds external rotation to target the posterior deltoids and rotator cuff muscles simultaneously. It's excellent for improving shoulder stability and addressing internal rotation tightness.
exercises.safetyInfo: This exercise combines shoulder horizontal abduction with external rotation, which can stress the rotator cuff. Use lighter weights than standard reverse flyes and avoid if you have rotator cuff injuries.

commonMistakes: Allowing the torso to rise during the pull
Flaring elbows out wide instead of keeping them tucked
Using momentum instead of controlled movement
exerciseTips: Keep elbows close to your body throughout the movement
Maintain neutral spine with chest up
Pull the bar to your lower chest/upper abdomen
Squeeze shoulder blades together at the top
instructions: Stand erect while holding a barbell with a supinated grip (palms facing up).

Bend your knees slightly and bring your torso forward, by bending at the waist, while keeping the back straight until it is almost parallel to the floor. Tip: Make sure that you keep the head up. The barbell should hang directly in front of you as your arms hang perpendicular to the floor and your torso. This is your starting position.

While keeping the torso stationary, lift the barbell as you breathe out, keeping the elbows close to the body and not doing any force with the forearm other than holding the weights. On the top contracted position, squeeze the back muscles and hold for a second.
Slowly lower the weight again to the starting position as you inhale.
Repeat for the recommended amount of repetitions.
exercises.name: Reverse Grip Bent-Over Rows
exercises.overview: Reverse grip bent-over rows emphasize the middle back and rhomboids while also engaging the biceps more than overhand variations. This compound movement improves posture and upper body pulling strength.
exercises.safetyInfo: Maintain proper hip hinge position to protect the lower back. Avoid this exercise if you have lower back issues or inability to maintain neutral spine.
variations: Bent Over Two-Dumbbell Row With Palms In

commonMistakes: Allowing elbows to drift forward during movement
Using shoulders and lats to initiate the movement
Not fully extending arms at bottom position
exerciseTips: Keep elbows pinned to your sides throughout movement
Focus on squeezing triceps at bottom position
Control the weight on the way up
Maintain upright posture with core engaged
instructions: Start by setting a bar attachment (straight or e-z) on a high pulley machine.

Facing the bar attachment, grab it with the palms facing up (supinated grip) at shoulder width. Lower the bar by using your lats until your arms are fully extended by your sides. Tip: Elbows should be in by your sides and your feet should be shoulder width apart from each other. This is the starting position.
Slowly elevate the bar attachment up as you inhale so it is aligned with your chest. Only the forearms should move and the elbows/upper arms should be stationary by your side at all times.
Then begin to lower the cable bar back down to the original staring position while exhaling and contracting the triceps hard.
Repeat for the recommended amount of repetitions.
exercises.name: Reverse Grip Triceps Pushdown
exercises.overview: Reverse grip triceps pushdowns target the triceps with emphasis on the medial and lateral heads while reducing stress on the long head. This variation can be easier on the shoulders for some individuals.
exercises.safetyInfo: The reverse grip can place additional stress on the wrists and elbows. Start with lighter weight and ensure proper wrist alignment throughout the movement.

commonMistakes: Extending hips too far causing excessive lumbar extension
Using momentum to swing legs
Not controlling the eccentric portion of the movement
exerciseTips: Stop short of full hip extension to avoid overarching
Control the movement in both directions
Keep core engaged throughout the exercise
Focus on hamstring and glute contraction
instructions: Place your feet between the pads after loading an appropriate weight. Lay on the top pad, allowing your hips to hang off the back, while grasping the handles to hold your position.
To begin the movement, flex the hips, pulling the legs forward.
Reverse the motion by extending the hips, kicking the leg back. It is very important not to over-extend the hip on this movement, stopping short of your full range of motion.
Return by again flexing the hip, pulling the carriage forward as far as you can.
Repeat for the desired number of repetitions.
exercises.name: Reverse Hyperextension
exercises.overview: Reverse hyperextensions target the hamstrings and glutes while providing decompression for the spine. This exercise is excellent for posterior chain development and can be therapeutic when performed correctly.
exercises.safetyInfo: Avoid overextending the hips as this can stress the lumbar spine. This exercise is contraindicated for those with lower back injuries or hypermobility issues.

commonMistakes: Allowing the front knee to drift forward past the toes
Placing too much weight on the back leg
Taking too short of a step backward, limiting range of motion
exerciseTips: Keep your weight primarily on your front leg throughout the movement
Maintain an upright torso and avoid leaning forward
Control the descent and avoid bouncing at the bottom
Step back far enough to achieve proper knee angles
instructions: Stand upright with feet hip-width apart and hands on your hips or at your sides.
Step backward with one leg, landing on the ball of your back foot.
Lower your body until both knees are bent at approximately 90-degree angles.
Keep your front knee aligned over your ankle and your torso upright.
Push through your front heel to return to the starting position and repeat with the other leg.
exercises.name: Reverse Lunge
exercises.overview: The reverse lunge involves stepping backward into a lunge position rather than forward. This variation is often easier on the knees and helps develop unilateral leg strength, balance, and coordination.
exercises.safetyInfo: Generally safer than forward lunges as it reduces stress on the front knee. Those with balance issues should use support initially. Ensure adequate space behind you before stepping back.

commonMistakes: Setting seat height too low or too high
Using excessive weight causing compensation patterns
Not pausing at the contracted position
exerciseTips: Adjust seat height so handles align with shoulders
Keep slight bend in elbows throughout movement
Squeeze shoulder blades together at end range
Control the return to starting position
instructions: Adjust the handles so that they are fully to the rear. Make an appropriate weight selection and adjust the seat height so the handles are at shoulder level. Grasp the handles with your hands facing inwards. This will be your starting position.
In a semicircular motion, pull your hands out to your side and back, contracting your rear delts.
Keep your arms slightly bent throughout the movement, with all of the motion occurring at the shoulder joint.
Pause at the rear of the movement, and slowly return the weight to the starting position.
exercises.name: Reverse Machine Flyes
exercises.overview: Machine reverse flyes provide a stable platform to isolate the posterior deltoids and improve shoulder health. This exercise is ideal for beginners learning the movement pattern or advanced users seeking precise isolation.
exercises.safetyInfo: Machine provides stability and controlled range of motion, making it safer than free weight variations. Ensure proper seat adjustment to avoid shoulder impingement.

commonMistakes: Using momentum to swing the weight up
Allowing elbows to drift forward during the curl
Gripping too wide on the plate
exerciseTips: Grip the plate at 11 and 1 o'clock positions
Keep elbows stationary at your sides
Control the eccentric portion slowly
Focus on squeezing the forearms at the top
instructions: Start by standing straight with a weighted plate held by both hands and arms fully extended. Use a pronated grip (palms facing down) and make sure your fingers grab the rough side of the plate while your thumb grabs the smooth side. Note: For the best results, grab the weighted plate at an 11:00 and 1:00 o'clock position.
Your feet should be shoulder width apart from each other and the weighted plate should be near the groin area. This is the starting position.

Slowly lift the plate up while keeping the elbows in and the upper arms stationary until your biceps and forearms touch while exhaling. The plate should be evenly aligned with your torso at this point.
Feel the contraction for a second and begin to lower the weight back down to the starting position while inhaling
Repeat for the recommended amount of repetitions.
exercises.name: Reverse Plate Curls
exercises.overview: Reverse plate curls target the brachialis and forearms using a pronated grip, building arm strength and improving grip endurance.
exercises.safetyInfo: Start with a lighter weight than regular bicep curls as the reverse grip reduces mechanical advantage. Avoid excessive wrist extension under load.
variations: Seated One-Arm Dumbbell Palms-Down Wrist Curl

commonMistakes: Flaring elbows out like a regular bench press
Using too wide of a grip
exerciseTips: Keep elbows close to your torso throughout
Use a shoulder-width supinated grip
Lower the bar to mid-chest level
Focus on triceps contraction during the press
instructions: Lie back on a flat bench. Using a close, supinated grip (around shoulder width), lift the bar from the rack and hold it straight over you with your arms locked extended in front of you and perpendicular to the floor. This will be your starting position.

As you breathe in, come down slowly until you feel the bar on your middle chest. Tip: Make sure that as opposed to a regular bench press, you keep the elbows close to the torso at all times in order to maximize triceps involvement.

After a second pause, bring the bar back to the starting position as you breathe out and push the bar using your triceps muscles. Lock your arms in the contracted position, hold for a second and then start coming down slowly again. Tip: It should take at least twice as long to go down than to come up.
Repeat the movement for the prescribed amount of repetitions.
When you are done, place the bar back in the rack.
exercises.name: Reverse Triceps Bench Press
exercises.overview: This exercise emphasizes triceps development through a reverse grip bench press movement, requiring careful attention to elbow position and grip.
exercises.safetyInfo: Use significantly less weight than regular bench press due to compromised shoulder position. Consider having a spotter due to the awkward grip position.

commonMistakes: Allowing rings to flare out excessively
Not descending to proper depth
exerciseTips: Keep rings close to your body
Lower until arms break 90 degrees
Press through palms to extend arms
Engage core to minimize swinging
instructions: Grip a ring in each hand, and then take a small jump to help you get into the starting position with your arms locked out.
Begin by flexing the elbow, lowering your body until your arms break 90 degrees. Avoid swinging, and maintain good posture throughout the descent.
Reverse the motion by extending the elbow, pushing yourself back up into the starting position.
Repeat for the desired number of repetitions.
exercises.name: Ring Dips
exercises.overview: An advanced bodyweight exercise performed on gymnastic rings that challenges triceps, chest, and shoulders while requiring significant stability.
exercises.safetyInfo: Ensure rings are properly secured and at appropriate height. Progress gradually as rings provide significant instability challenge.

commonMistakes: Allowing excessive ring movement that breaks form
Not maintaining a straight body line
Progressing to rings before mastering standard push-ups
exerciseTips: Keep your core engaged to maintain stability on the unstable rings
Allow the rings to move naturally rather than fighting the instability
Start with rings higher to reduce difficulty
Focus on controlling the rings rather than letting them control you
instructions: Set gymnastic rings at an appropriate height and grab the rings with a neutral grip.
Walk your feet back and assume a plank position with arms extended and body straight.
Lower your chest toward the rings by bending your elbows, allowing the rings to move naturally.
Press through the rings to return to the starting plank position.
Maintain ring stability and body alignment throughout the movement.
exercises.name: Ring Push Up
exercises.overview: Ring push-ups use gymnastic rings or suspension trainers to create an unstable surface that challenges stability and strength. The instability recruits additional stabilizing muscles while building functional pushing strength.
exercises.safetyInfo: Ensure rings are properly secured before use. The instability increases stress on stabilizing muscles and joints. Start with higher ring positions to reduce difficulty.

commonMistakes: Using momentum instead of controlled muscle contraction
Not achieving full range of motion in toe raise phase
Allowing knees to bend during the heel raise portion
exerciseTips: Control the tempo throughout both phases of movement
Pause briefly at peak contraction for maximum activation
Keep your core tight to maintain balance with the barbell
Focus on full range of motion in both directions
instructions: This exercise is best performed inside a squat rack for safety purposes. To begin, first set the bar on a rack that best matches your height. Once the correct height is chosen and the bar is loaded, step under the bar and place it on the back of your shoulders (slightly below the neck).
Hold on to the bar using both arms at each side and lift it off the rack by first pushing with your legs and at the same time straightening your torso.

Step away from the rack and position your legs using a shoulder width medium stance with the toes slightly pointed out. Keep your head up at all times as looking down will get you off balance. Also maintain a straight back and keep the knees with a slight bend; never locked. This will be your starting position.

Raise your heels as you breathe out by extending your ankles as high as possible and flexing your calf. Ensure that the knee is kept stationary at all times. There should be no bending (other than the slight initial bend we created during positioning) at any time. Hold the contracted position by a second before you start to go back down.
Go back slowly to the starting position as you breathe in by lowering your heels as you bend the ankles until calves are stretched.
Now lift your toes by contracting the tibia muscles in the front of the calves as you breathe out.
Hold for a second and bring them back down as you breathe in.
Repeat for the recommended amount of repetitions.
exercises.name: Rocking Standing Calf Raise
exercises.overview: This compound calf exercise targets both the posterior and anterior lower leg muscles by combining heel raises with toe raises. The rocking motion provides comprehensive lower leg development in a single movement.
exercises.safetyInfo: Use a squat rack for safety when handling heavy loads. Avoid if you have Achilles tendon issues or ankle instability.
variations: Smith Machine Calf Raise Single Leg

commonMistakes: Pulling behind the neck too low causing shoulder impingement
Using excessive momentum to complete the alternating pattern
Not maintaining proper spinal alignment during transitions
exerciseTips: Maintain proper shoulder positioning throughout both variations
Control the transition between front and back movements
Keep your core engaged to prevent swinging
Focus on squeezing shoulder blades together at the top
instructions: Grab the pull-up bar with the palms facing forward using a wide grip.

As you have both arms extended in front of you holding the bar at the chosen grip width, bring your torso back around 30 degrees or so while creating a curvature on your lower back and sticking your chest out. This is your starting position.

Pull your torso up until the bar touches your upper chest by drawing the shoulders and the upper arms down and back. Exhale as you perform this portion of the movement. Tip: Concentrate on squeezing the back muscles once you reach the full contracted position. The upper torso should remain stationary as it moves through space and only the arms should move. The forearms should do no other work other than hold the bar.
After a second on the contracted position, start to inhale and slowly lower your torso back to the starting position when your arms are fully extended and the lats are fully stretched.

Now repeat the same movements as described above except this time your torso will remain straight as you go up and the bar will touch the back of the neck instead of the upper chest. Tip: Use the head to lean forward slightly as it will help you properly execute this portion of the exercise.
Once you have lowered yourself back down to the starting position, repeat the exercise for the prescribed amount of repetitions in your program.
exercises.name: Rocky Pull-Ups/Pulldowns
exercises.overview: This advanced pull-up variation alternates between front and behind-the-neck pulls to target the lats from multiple angles. It challenges coordination while providing comprehensive upper body pulling strength development.
exercises.safetyInfo: Behind-the-neck variations can cause shoulder impingement in individuals with limited shoulder mobility. Discontinue if you experience any shoulder discomfort.

commonMistakes: Squatting down instead of hinging at the hips
Allowing the bar to drift away from the body
Rounding the lower back during the descent
exerciseTips: Push your hips back while keeping the bar close to your legs
Maintain a neutral spine throughout the entire movement
Feel the stretch in your hamstrings at the bottom position
Drive your hips forward to return to the starting position
instructions: Put a barbell in front of you on the ground and grab it using a pronated (palms facing down) grip that a little wider than shoulder width. Tip: Depending on the weight used, you may need wrist wraps to perform the exercise and also a raised platform in order to allow for better range of motion.
Bend the knees slightly and keep the shins vertical, hips back and back straight. This will be your starting position.
Keeping your back and arms completely straight at all times, use your hips to lift the bar as you exhale. Tip: The movement should not be fast but steady and under control.

Once you are standing completely straight up, lower the bar by pushing the hips back, only slightly bending the knees, unlike when squatting. Tip: Take a deep breath at the start of the movement and keep your chest up. Hold your breath as you lower and exhale as you complete the movement.
Repeat for the recommended amount of repetitions.
exercises.name: Romanian Deadlift
