exercises.overview: The Romanian deadlift is a hip-hinge movement that primarily targets the hamstrings and glutes while strengthening the posterior chain. It's essential for developing proper deadlift mechanics and improving athletic performance.
exercises.safetyInfo: Avoid this exercise if you have lower back injuries or poor hamstring flexibility. Start with lighter weights to master the hip hinge movement pattern.

commonMistakes: Squatting down instead of hinging at the hips
Allowing the dumbbells to drift away from the legs
Rounding the back during the descent
exerciseTips: Initiate the movement by pushing your hips back, not bending your knees
Keep your shoulder blades pulled back and chest proud
Focus on feeling the stretch in your hamstrings during the descent
Squeeze your glutes hard at the top of each repetition
instructions: Hold a dumbbell in each hand with arms at your sides, feet hip-width apart with a slight knee bend.
Hinge at the hips by pushing your hips back while lowering the dumbbells toward the floor.
Keep your chest up and maintain a neutral spine as you descend until you feel a stretch in your hamstrings.
Drive your hips forward to return to the starting position, squeezing your glutes at the top.
Keep the dumbbells close to your legs throughout the entire movement.
exercises.name: Romanian Deadlift With Dumbbells
exercises.overview: The dumbbell Romanian deadlift is a hip-hinge movement that primarily targets the hamstrings and glutes. It's an excellent exercise for developing posterior chain strength and improving hip mobility.
exercises.safetyInfo: Start with light weights to master the hip hinge pattern. Avoid if you have lower back injuries or tight hamstrings. Stop the descent when you feel a good hamstring stretch rather than forcing range of motion.

commonMistakes: Relying solely on arm strength without using leg assistance
Gripping the rope too tightly causing premature forearm fatigue
Descending too quickly without control
exerciseTips: Use your legs to secure the rope and reduce arm fatigue
Climb with a hand-over-hand technique for efficiency
Engage your core to maintain stability during ascent
Control your descent to build eccentric strength
instructions: Grab the rope with both hands above your head. Pull down on the rope as you take a small jump.
Wrap the rope around one leg, using your feet to pinch the rope. Reach up as high as possible with your arms, gripping the rope tightly.
Release the rope from your feet as you pull yourself up with your arms, bringing your knees towards your chest.
Resecure your feet on the rope, and then stand up to take another high hold on the rope. Continue until you reach the top of the rope.
To lower yourself, loosen the grip of your feet on the rope as you slide down using a hand over hand motion.
exercises.name: Rope Climb
exercises.overview: Rope climbing is a functional compound exercise that develops upper body pulling strength, grip endurance, and coordination. It's an excellent functional movement that translates to real-world climbing and pulling activities.
exercises.safetyInfo: Ensure the rope is properly anchored and inspect for wear before use. Have adequate cushioning below and avoid if you have shoulder impingement or grip strength limitations.

commonMistakes: Using arms and shoulders to pull the weight instead of core flexion
Moving the hips forward instead of keeping them fixed
Using too much weight and losing proper form
exerciseTips: Focus on flexing your spine rather than pulling with your arms
Keep your hips stationary throughout the movement
Squeeze your abs at the bottom position for 1-2 seconds
Control the weight on the way up to maximize tension
instructions: Kneel 1-2 feet in front of a cable system with a rope attached.
After selecting an appropriate weight, grasp the rope with both hands reaching overhead. Your torso should be upright in the starting position.
To begin, flex at the spine, attempting to bring your rib cage to your legs as you pull the cable down.
Pause at the bottom of the motion, and then slowly return to the starting position.
These can be done with twists or to the side to hit the obliques.
exercises.name: Rope Crunch
exercises.overview: The rope crunch is an effective cable-based isolation exercise that targets the abdominal muscles through spinal flexion. It allows for progressive overload and consistent tension throughout the range of motion.
exercises.safetyInfo: Avoid this exercise if you have lower back issues or herniated discs. Keep movements controlled to prevent sudden spinal flexion under load.

commonMistakes: Bending the elbows during the movement
Using too much weight and compensating with other muscles
Standing too upright instead of leaning forward
exerciseTips: Keep your arms completely straight throughout the movement
Focus on pulling with your lats rather than your arms
Maintain a slight forward lean from the hips
Control the weight slowly on the eccentric portion
instructions: Attach a rope to a high pulley and make your weight selection. Stand a couple feet back from the pulley with your feet staggered and take the rope with both hands. Lean forward from the hip, keeping your back straight, with your arms extended up in front of you. This will be your starting position.
Keeping your arms straight, extend the shoulder to pull the rope down to your thighs.
Pause at the bottom of the motion, squeezing your lats.
Return to the starting position without allowing the weight to fully rest on the stack.
exercises.name: Rope Straight-Arm Pulldown
exercises.name_pt: Pulldown com Braços Estendidos na Corda
exercises.overview: The rope straight-arm pulldown is an isolation exercise that effectively targets the latissimus dorsi through shoulder extension while keeping the arms straight.
exercises.safetyInfo: Use moderate weight to maintain proper form. Avoid if you have shoulder impingement issues. Keep core engaged to protect the lower back.
variations: Bent Over Two-Dumbbell Row With Palms In

commonMistakes: Rounding the back excessively
Moving too quickly without control
Pulling on the neck or using neck muscles
exerciseTips: Keep chest up and spine neutral throughout movement
Focus on rotating from the core, not just moving arms
Control the movement rather than using momentum
Breathe out during each twist
instructions: Lie down on the floor placing your feet either under something that will not move or by having a partner hold them. Your legs should be bent at the knees.

Elevate your upper body so that it creates an imaginary V-shape with your thighs. Your arms should be fully extended in front of you perpendicular to your torso and with the hands clasped. This is the starting position.
Twist your torso to the right side until your arms are parallel with the floor while breathing out.
Hold the contraction for a second and move back to the starting position while breathing out. Now move to the opposite side performing the same techniques you applied to the right side.
Repeat for the recommended amount of repetitions.
exercises.name: Russian Twist
exercises.overview: Russian twists target the obliques and core through rotational movement. This exercise builds rotational strength and stability while improving core endurance.
exercises.safetyInfo: Avoid this exercise if you have lower back issues. Keep movements controlled to prevent strain on the spine during rotation.

commonMistakes: Pulling on the handles instead of using them for balance
Allowing the torso to fold forward excessively
exerciseTips: Keep your elbows down and hands light on the handles
Maintain an upright torso throughout the movement
Focus on sitting back into the squat pattern
Drive your knees out in line with your toes
instructions: Position the safety squat bar on your upper traps with the pad across your shoulders.
Grip the handles in front of your chest for stability and control.
Unrack the bar and step back into your squat stance.
Descend by pushing your hips back and bending at the knees while keeping chest up.
Drive through your heels to return to the starting position.
Re-rack the bar safely after completing your set.
exercises.name: Safety Bar Squat
exercises.overview: The safety bar squat uses a specialized barbell with a cambered design and handles that shifts the weight distribution forward. It allows for a more upright squat pattern and is easier on the shoulders.
exercises.safetyInfo: The safety squat bar is generally easier on the shoulders and wrists than a straight bar. However, it places greater emphasis on spinal erectors due to the forward weight distribution.

commonMistakes: Using arm muscles instead of scapular muscles
Not achieving full range of scapular movement
Moving too quickly without control
exerciseTips: Focus on pulling shoulder blades down and together
Keep arms straight throughout the movement
Hold the top position briefly
Control the descent back to starting position
instructions: Take a pronated grip on a pull-up bar.
From a hanging position, raise yourself a few inches without using your arms. Do this by depressing your shoulder girdle in a reverse shrugging motion.
Pause at the completion of the movement, and then slowly return to the starting position before performing more repetitions.
exercises.name: Scapular Pull-Up
exercises.overview: Scapular pull-ups isolate and strengthen the muscles that control shoulder blade movement. This exercise is essential for developing proper pulling mechanics and preparing for full pull-ups.
exercises.safetyInfo: This exercise is generally safe for most individuals and serves as excellent preparation for full pull-ups. Avoid if you have acute shoulder injuries.

commonMistakes: Lifting your chest off the bench to generate momentum
Using your legs to assist the pulling motion
Pulling the bar too high toward your neck instead of lower chest
exerciseTips: Keep your chest pressed firmly against the bench throughout the movement
Focus on squeezing your shoulder blades together at the top
Control the negative portion of the movement for maximum muscle engagement
Keep your core engaged to maintain stability on the bench
instructions: Set up a bench at an incline or lie prone on an elevated bench with your chest supported and feet on the ground.
Grasp a barbell with an overhand grip slightly wider than shoulder-width apart, arms hanging straight down.
Pull the barbell up toward your lower chest, leading with your elbows and squeezing your shoulder blades together.
Hold the contraction briefly at the top of the movement.
Lower the barbell slowly back to the starting position with full arm extension.
exercises.name: Seal Row
exercises.overview: The Seal Row is a chest-supported rowing variation that eliminates lower back involvement and allows for strict lat and rhomboid development. The prone position prevents cheating and ensures proper muscle targeting.
exercises.safetyInfo: This exercise is generally safe for most individuals as the chest support reduces lower back stress. Ensure proper bench setup to avoid sliding or instability during the movement.

commonMistakes: Using momentum to swing the legs back instead of controlled muscle contraction
Allowing the band to go slack at the bottom position
Leaning forward excessively which reduces hamstring activation
exerciseTips: Keep your core engaged and maintain upright posture throughout the movement
Control the eccentric portion by slowly extending your legs
Focus on squeezing your hamstrings at the peak contraction
Maintain tension in the band throughout the entire range of motion
instructions: Secure a band close to the ground and place a bench a couple feet away from it.
Seat yourself on the bench and secure the band behind your ankles, beginning with your legs straight. This will be your starting position.
Flex the knees, bringing your feet towards the bench. You may need to lean back slightly to keep your feet from striking the floor.
Pause at the completion of the movement, and then slowly return to the starting position.
exercises.name: Seated Band Hamstring Curl
exercises.name_pt: Mesa Flexora Sentada com Faixa Elástica
exercises.overview: The seated band hamstring curl is an isolation exercise that targets the hamstring muscles using resistance bands. It's an excellent option for home workouts and rehabilitation settings.
exercises.safetyInfo: Avoid this exercise if you have acute hamstring strains or knee injuries. Start with lighter resistance to prevent sudden muscle cramps.

commonMistakes: Lowering the bar too far behind the neck which stresses the shoulder joint
Arching the back excessively to compensate for poor shoulder mobility
Using too wide of a grip which reduces pressing efficiency
exerciseTips: Keep your core tight and maintain a neutral spine throughout the movement
Press the bar in a straight line directly overhead
Control the descent to collarbone level without bouncing
Engage your shoulder blades by pulling them down and back
instructions: Sit on a Military Press Bench with a bar behind your head and either have a spotter give you the bar (better on the rotator cuff this way) or pick it up yourself carefully with a pronated grip (palms facing forward). Tip: Your grip should be wider than shoulder width and it should create a 90-degree angle between the forearm and the upper arm as the barbell goes down.

Once you pick up the barbell with the correct grip length, lift the bar up over your head by locking your arms. Hold at about shoulder level and slightly in front of your head. This is your starting position.
Lower the bar down to the collarbone slowly as you inhale.
Lift the bar back up to the starting position as you exhale.
Repeat for the recommended amount of repetitions.
exercises.name: Seated Barbell Military Press
exercises.overview: The seated barbell military press is a compound upper body exercise that primarily targets the anterior and lateral deltoids while providing core stability challenges. The seated position reduces lower body involvement and focuses effort on the shoulders.
exercises.safetyInfo: This behind-the-neck variation can be problematic for those with shoulder impingement or limited shoulder mobility. Consider front military press as a safer alternative.

commonMistakes: Rotating too far and straining the spine
Using the arms to create momentum instead of core rotation
Hunching forward or losing neutral spine position
exerciseTips: Rotate from your core, not your shoulders or arms
Keep the movement controlled and avoid jerky motions
Maintain an upright posture throughout the exercise
Focus on feeling the contraction in your obliques
instructions: Start out by sitting at the end of a flat bench with a barbell placed on top of your thighs. Your feet should be shoulder width apart from each other.
Grip the bar with your palms facing down and make sure your hands are wider than shoulder width apart from each other. Begin to lift the barbell up over your head until your arms are fully extended.
Now lower the barbell behind your head until it is resting along the base of your neck. This is the starting position.

While keeping your feet and head stationary, move your waist from side to side so that your oblique muscles feel the contraction. Only move from side to side as far as your waist will allow you to go. Stretching or moving too far can cause an injury to occur. Tip: Use a slow and controlled motion.
Remember to breathe out while twisting your body to the side and in when moving back to the starting position.
Repeat for the recommended amount of repetitions.
exercises.name: Seated Barbell Twist
exercises.name_pt: Rotação de Tronco Sentado com Barra
exercises.overview: The seated barbell twist is an isolation exercise targeting the oblique muscles through rotational movement. It helps develop core strength and rotational power while seated.
exercises.safetyInfo: Avoid excessive range of motion which can strain the lumbar spine. Those with lower back issues should use lighter weight or avoid this exercise entirely.

commonMistakes: Swinging the upper arm instead of keeping it stationary
Using too much weight and compromising form
Not achieving full extension at the top of the movement
exerciseTips: Keep your upper arm parallel to the floor and stationary
Squeeze your tricep at the top of the movement
Control the weight on the way down to maximize muscle activation
Maintain a neutral wrist position throughout the exercise
instructions: Sit down at the end of a flat bench with a dumbbell in one arm using a neutral grip (palms of the hand facing you).
Bend your knees slightly and bring your torso forward, by bending at the waist, while keeping the back straight until it is almost parallel to the floor. Make sure that you keep the head up.

The upper arm with the dumbbell should be close to the torso and aligned with it (lifted up until it is parallel to the floor while the forearms are pointing towards the floor as the hands hold the weights). Tip: There should be a 90-degree angle between the forearms and the upper arm. This is your starting position.

Keeping the upper arm stationary, use the triceps to lift the weight as you exhale until the forearm is parallel to the floor and the whole arm is extended. Like many other arm exercises, only the forearm moves.
After a second contraction at the top, slowly lower the dumbbell back to the starting position as you inhale.
Repeat the movement for the prescribed amount of repetitions.
Switch arms and repeat the exercise.
exercises.name: Seated Bent-Over One-Arm Dumbbell Triceps Extension
exercises.name_pt: Tríceps Coice Unilateral Sentado Curvado com Halter
exercises.overview: The seated bent-over one-arm dumbbell triceps extension is an isolation exercise that effectively targets all three heads of the triceps. The bent-over position provides stability while allowing unilateral training.
exercises.safetyInfo: Use lighter weights to maintain proper form and prevent elbow strain. Avoid this exercise if you have elbow tendonitis or shoulder impingement.
variations: Bent Over Two-Dumbbell Row With Palms In

commonMistakes: Using too much weight and compensating with upper traps
Allowing the torso to rise up during the lifting phase
Bringing the arms behind the body instead of out to the sides
exerciseTips: Focus on squeezing your shoulder blades together at the top
Keep a slight bend in your elbows throughout the movement
Lift with your rear delts, not your traps
Maintain the bent-over position without rounding your back
instructions: Place a couple of dumbbells looking forward in front of a flat bench.
Sit on the end of the bench with your legs together and the dumbbells behind your calves.
Bend at the waist while keeping the back straight in order to pick up the dumbbells. The palms of your hands should be facing each other as you pick them. This will be your starting position.

Keeping your torso forward and stationary, and the arms slightly bent at the elbows, lift the dumbbells straight to the side until both arms are parallel to the floor. Exhale as you lift the weights. (Note: avoid swinging the torso or bringing the arms back as opposed to the side.)
After a one second contraction at the top, slowly lower the dumbbells back to the starting position.
Repeat for the recommended amount of repetitions.
exercises.name: Seated Bent-Over Rear Delt Raise
exercises.overview: The seated bent-over rear delt raise is an isolation exercise that targets the often-neglected posterior deltoids. It helps improve shoulder stability and posture by strengthening the rear shoulder muscles.
exercises.safetyInfo: Start with lighter weights as the rear deltoids are typically weaker. Maintain proper spinal position to avoid lower back strain.

commonMistakes: Moving the upper arms during the extension phase
Using momentum instead of controlled muscle contraction
Allowing one arm to work harder than the other
exerciseTips: Keep both upper arms parallel to the floor and stationary
Extend both arms simultaneously for balanced development
Focus on the tricep contraction at full extension
Maintain proper bent-over posture throughout the set
instructions: Sit down at the end of a flat bench with a dumbbell in both arms using a neutral grip (palms of the hand facing you).
Bend your knees slightly and bring your torso forward, by bending at the waist, while keeping the back straight until it is almost parallel to the floor. Make sure that you keep the head up.

The upper arms with the dumbbells should be close to the torso and aligned with it (lifted up until they are parallel to the floor while the forearms are pointing towards the floor as the hands hold the weights). Tip: There should be a 90-degree angle between the forearms and the upper arm. This is your starting position.

Keeping the upper arms stationary, use the triceps to lift the weight as you exhale until the forearms are parallel to the floor and the whole arm is extended. Like many other arm exercises, only the forearm moves.
After a second contraction at the top, slowly lower the dumbbells back to the starting position as you inhale.
Repeat the movement for the prescribed amount of repetitions.
exercises.name: Seated Bent-Over Two-Arm Dumbbell Triceps Extension
exercises.name_pt: Tríceps Coice Sentado Curvado com Halteres
exercises.overview: The seated bent-over two-arm dumbbell triceps extension is a bilateral isolation exercise that targets the triceps muscles. This variation allows for balanced development of both arms while providing core stability challenges.
exercises.safetyInfo: Use appropriate weight to maintain form with both arms simultaneously. Stop if you feel any elbow pain or cannot maintain proper positioning.
variations: Bent Over Two-Dumbbell Row With Palms In

commonMistakes: Using momentum by rocking the torso back and forth
Pulling the handle to the chest instead of the lower ribcage
Allowing shoulders to round forward at the start position
exerciseTips: Pull your shoulder blades together at the end of each rep
Keep your torso upright and avoid swaying back and forth
Focus on pulling with your back muscles, not your arms
Pause and squeeze at the fully contracted position
instructions: For this exercise you will need access to a low pulley row machine with a V-bar. Note: The V-bar will enable you to have a neutral grip where the palms of your hands face each other. To get into the starting position, first sit down on the machine and place your feet on the front platform or crossbar provided making sure that your knees are slightly bent and not locked.
Lean over as you keep the natural alignment of your back and grab the V-bar handles.

With your arms extended pull back until your torso is at a 90-degree angle from your legs. Your back should be slightly arched and your chest should be sticking out. You should be feeling a nice stretch on your lats as you hold the bar in front of you. This is the starting position of the exercise.

Keeping the torso stationary, pull the handles back towards your torso while keeping the arms close to it until you touch the abdominals. Breathe out as you perform that movement. At that point you should be squeezing your back muscles hard. Hold that contraction for a second and slowly go back to the original position while breathing in.
Repeat for the recommended amount of repetitions.
exercises.name: Seated Cable Rows
exercises.overview: A fundamental horizontal pulling exercise that strengthens the middle back, improves posture, and balances pressing movements.
exercises.safetyInfo: Keep knees slightly bent to protect the lower back. Avoid excessive backward lean which can strain the lumbar spine.
variations: Bent Over Two-Dumbbell Row With Palms In

commonMistakes: Pressing the weight straight up instead of in an arc
Arching the back excessively to generate momentum
Lowering the handles too far below shoulder level
exerciseTips: Press the handles up and slightly forward in an arc motion
Keep your core tight to maintain spinal stability
Control the weight on both the pressing and lowering phases
Stop just short of full elbow lockout at the top
instructions: Adjust the weight to an appropriate amount and be seated, grasping the handles. Your upper arms should be about 90 degrees to the body, with your head and chest up. The elbows should also be bent to about 90 degrees. This will be your starting position.
Begin by extending through the elbow, pressing the handles together above your head.
After pausing at the top, return the handles to the starting position. Ensure that you maintain tension on the cables.
You can also execute this movement with your back off the pad and alternate hands.
exercises.name: Seated Cable Shoulder Press
exercises.overview: A seated pressing movement that builds shoulder strength and stability while providing back support for those with lower back concerns.
exercises.safetyInfo: The seated position provides back support but maintain core engagement. Avoid pressing behind the neck which can impinge the shoulder joint.

commonMistakes: Bouncing at the bottom position instead of controlling the stretch
Using partial range of motion
Lifting the knees or shifting weight during the movement
exerciseTips: Focus on the full range of motion from deep stretch to peak contraction
Control the weight slowly on the lowering phase
Keep your knees directly over your toes throughout the movement
Pause briefly at the top of each repetition
instructions: Sit on the machine and place your toes on the lower portion of the platform provided with the heels extending off. Choose the toe positioning of your choice (forward, in, or out) as per the beginning of this chapter.

Place your lower thighs under the lever pad, which will need to be adjusted according to the height of your thighs. Now place your hands on top of the lever pad in order to prevent it from slipping forward.
Lift the lever slightly by pushing your heels up and release the safety bar. This will be your starting position.
Slowly lower your heels by bending at the ankles until the calves are fully stretched. Inhale as you perform this movement.
Raise the heels by extending the ankles as high as possible as you contract the calves and breathe out. Hold the top contraction for a second.
Repeat for the recommended amount of repetitions.
exercises.name: Seated Calf Raise
exercises.overview: An isolation exercise targeting the soleus muscle of the calves, particularly effective due to the knee-bent position which emphasizes the deeper calf muscle.
exercises.safetyInfo: Start with lighter weight to assess your range of motion. Avoid bouncing in the stretched position which can cause injury to the Achilles tendon.
variations: Smith Machine Calf Raise Single Leg

commonMistakes: Allowing the upper arms to move away from the thighs
Using momentum or swinging the weight up
Gripping too wide which reduces the close-grip benefit
exerciseTips: Brace your upper arms firmly against your inner thighs
Keep your wrists straight and grip secure throughout
Focus on squeezing the biceps at the top of each rep
Control the weight slowly on the lowering phase
instructions: Sit down on a flat bench with a barbell or E-Z Bar in front of you in between your legs. Your legs should be spread with the knees bent and the feet on the floor.

Use your arms to pick the barbell up and place the back of your upper arms on top of your inner thighs (around three and a half inches away from the front of the knee). A supinated grip closer than shoulder width is needed to perform this exercise. Tip: Your arm should be extended at arms length and the barbell should be above the floor. This will be your starting position.

While holding the upper arms stationary, curl the weights forward while contracting the biceps as you breathe out. Only the forearms should move. Continue the movement until your biceps are fully contracted and the dumbbells are at shoulder level. Hold the contracted position for a second as you squeeze the biceps.
Slowly begin to bring the barbell back to starting position as your breathe in. Tip: Avoid swinging motions at any time.
Repeat for the recommended amount of repetitions.
exercises.name: Seated Close-Grip Concentration Barbell Curl
exercises.name_pt: Rosca Concentrada Sentado com Barra - Pegada Fechada
exercises.overview: A focused bicep isolation exercise that uses the close grip and braced position to emphasize the peak contraction and inner portion of the biceps.
exercises.safetyInfo: The close grip puts additional stress on the wrists and forearms. Start with lighter weight and ensure proper wrist positioning to avoid strain.
variations: Seated One-Arm Dumbbell Palms-Down Wrist Curl

commonMistakes: Swinging the torso to generate momentum
Moving elbows away from the sides
Rushing through the eccentric portion
exerciseTips: Keep upper arms stationary throughout the movement
Squeeze biceps at the top for maximum contraction
Control the eccentric (lowering) portion slowly
Rotate wrists smoothly as dumbbells pass thighs
instructions: Sit on a flat bench with a dumbbell on each hand being held at arms length. The elbows should be close to the torso.
Rotate the palms of the hands so that they are facing your torso. This will be your starting position.

While holding the upper arm stationary, curl the weights and start twisting the wrists once the dumbbells pass your thighs so that the palms of your hands face forward at the end of the movement. Make sure that you contract the biceps as you breathe out and make sure that only the forearms move. Continue the movement until your biceps are fully contracted and the dumbbells are at shoulder level. Hold the contracted position for a second as you squeeze the biceps.
Slowly begin to bring the dumbbells back to the starting position as your breathe in and as you rotate the wrists back to a neutral grip.
Repeat for the recommended amount of repetitions.
exercises.name: Seated Dumbbell Curl
exercises.overview: A seated bicep curl that incorporates wrist rotation to target both heads of the biceps effectively. The seated position eliminates momentum and ensures strict form.
exercises.safetyInfo: Avoid using excessive weight that compromises form. Those with wrist issues should be cautious with the rotation component.

commonMistakes: Allowing elbows to drift forward
Using too much weight and losing the wide grip position
Not fully rotating the dumbbells during the movement
exerciseTips: Keep forearms aligned with outer deltoids throughout
Focus on the outward rotation as you curl
Maintain strict upper arm position
Squeeze at the peak contraction
instructions: Sit on the end of a flat bench with a dumbbell in each hand being held at arms length. The elbows should be close to the torso.
Rotate the palms of the hands so that they are facing inward in a neutral position. This will be your starting position.
While holding the upper arms stationary, curl the dumbbells out and up, turning the palms out as you lift and keeping your forearms in line with your outer deltoids. Tips:

Only the forearms should move. Continue the movement until your biceps are fully contracted and the dumbbells are at shoulder level. Hold the contracted position for a second as you squeeze the biceps.
Slowly begin to bring the dumbbells back to the starting position as your breathe in. Remember to rotate your arms as you lower the dumbbells so that you can switch back to a neutral grip.
Repeat for the recommended amount of repetitions.
exercises.name: Seated Dumbbell Inner Biceps Curl
exercises.name_pt: Rosca para Bíceps Interno Sentado com Halteres
exercises.overview: A bicep curl variation that emphasizes the inner head of the biceps through a wide, outward curling motion. The seated position provides stability for focused muscle activation.
exercises.safetyInfo: The wide grip position places additional stress on the shoulder joint. Start with lighter weights to assess comfort level.

commonMistakes: Using forearms to assist the movement
Choosing weight that's too heavy
Rushing through the eccentric portion
exerciseTips: Keep forearms firmly pressed against thighs
Move only at the wrist joint
Control both the lifting and lowering phases
Use lighter weight than flexor exercises
instructions: Start out by placing two dumbbells on the floor in front of a flat bench.
Sit down on the edge of the flat bench with your legs at about shoulder width apart. Make sure to keep your feet on the floor.

Use your arms to grab both of the dumbbells and bring them up so that your forearms are resting against your thighs with the palms of the hands facing down. Your wrists should be hanging over the edge of your thighs.
Start out by curling your wrist upwards and exhaling.

Slowly lower your wrists back down to the starting position while inhaling. Make sure to inhale during this part of the exercise. Tip: Your forearms should be stationary as your wrist is the only movement needed to perform this exercise.
Repeat for the recommended amount of repetitions.
When finished, simply lower the dumbbells to the floor.
exercises.name: Seated Dumbbell Palms-Down Wrist Curl
exercises.name_pt: Rosca de Punho Sentado com Halteres - Palmas para Baixo
exercises.overview: An isolation exercise targeting the wrist extensor muscles, which are often neglected but crucial for balanced forearm strength and wrist stability.
exercises.safetyInfo: Wrist extensors are weaker than flexors, so use significantly lighter weight. Stop if you experience wrist pain or discomfort.
variations: Seated One-Arm Dumbbell Palms-Down Wrist Curl

commonMistakes: Lifting forearms off thighs during the movement
Using momentum instead of controlled motion
Not achieving full range of motion
exerciseTips: Maintain forearm contact with thighs throughout
Isolate movement to wrist joint only
Lower weight slowly under control
Allow full range of motion at the wrist
instructions: Start out by placing two dumbbells on the floor in front of a flat bench.
Sit down on the edge of the flat bench with your legs at about shoulder width apart. Make sure to keep your feet on the floor.

Use your arms to grab both of the dumbbells and bring them up so that your forearms are resting against your thighs with the palms of the hands facing up. Your wrists should be hanging over the edge of your thighs.
Start out by curling your wrist upwards and exhaling.

Slowly lower your wrists back down to the starting position while inhaling. Make sure to inhale during this part of the exercise. Tip: Your forearms should be stationary as your wrist is the only movement needed to perform this exercise.
Repeat for the recommended amount of repetitions.
When finished, simply lower the dumbbells to the floor.
exercises.name: Seated Dumbbell Palms-Up Wrist Curl
exercises.name_pt: Rosca de Punho Sentado com Halteres - Palmas para Cima
exercises.overview: A targeted forearm exercise that strengthens the wrist flexor muscles, improving grip strength and forearm development while supporting wrist health.
exercises.safetyInfo: Start with light weight to assess wrist comfort. Avoid this exercise if you have acute wrist injuries or carpal tunnel syndrome symptoms.
variations: Seated One-Arm Dumbbell Palms-Down Wrist Curl

commonMistakes: Arching back excessively during the press
Pressing dumbbells forward instead of straight up
Lowering weights too far past shoulder level
exerciseTips: Press dumbbells straight up overhead
Keep core engaged and back against the pad
Lower weights under control to shoulder level
Drive through heels for additional stability
instructions: Grab a couple of dumbbells and sit on a military press bench or a utility bench that has a back support on it as you place the dumbbells upright on top of your thighs.
Clean the dumbbells up one at a time by using your thighs to bring the dumbbells up to shoulder height at each side.
Rotate the wrists so that the palms of your hands are facing forward. This is your starting position.
As you exhale, push the dumbbells up until they touch at the top.
After a second pause, slowly come down back to the starting position as you inhale.
Repeat for the recommended amount of repetitions.
exercises.name: Seated Dumbbell Press
exercises.overview: A fundamental shoulder strengthening exercise that builds overhead pressing strength and shoulder stability while the seated position provides back support.
exercises.safetyInfo: Use a bench with back support to protect the spine. Avoid overhead pressing if you have shoulder impingement or recent shoulder injuries.

commonMistakes: Using momentum to swing legs up
Not engaging the core throughout the movement
Allowing shoulders to round forward excessively
exerciseTips: Pull knees toward chest while bringing torso forward
Keep movements controlled and deliberate
Exhale forcefully as you pull knees in
Maintain grip on bench sides for stability
instructions: Sit on a bench with the legs stretched out in front of you slightly below parallel and your arms holding on to the sides of the bench. Your torso should be leaning backwards around a 45-degree angle from the bench. This will be your starting position.
Bring the knees in toward you as you move your torso closer to them at the same time. Breathe out as you perform this movement.
After a second pause, go back to the starting position as you inhale.
Repeat for the recommended amount of repetitions.
exercises.name: Seated Flat Bench Leg Pull-In
exercises.name_pt: Leg Pull-In Sentado no Banco Reto
exercises.overview: A compound abdominal exercise that combines knee raises with torso flexion to effectively target the entire rectus abdominis while providing core stability training.
exercises.safetyInfo: Ensure the bench is stable and won't tip. Those with lower back issues should avoid excessive spinal flexion and consider modifications.

commonMistakes: Using too much weight too soon
Moving too quickly through the range of motion
Allowing shoulders to round forward
exerciseTips: Keep movements slow and controlled throughout
Maintain proper posture with shoulders back
Focus on squeezing neck muscles at the top
Use full range of motion without forcing
instructions: Place a neck strap on the floor at the end of a flat bench. Once you have selected the weights, sit at the end of the flat bench with your feet wider than shoulder width apart from each other. Your toes should be pointed out.

Slowly move your torso forward until it is almost parallel with the floor. Using both hands, securely position the neck strap around your head. Tip: Make sure the weights are still lying on the floor to prevent any strain on the neck. Now grab the weight with both hands while elevating your torso back until it is almost perpendicular to the floor. Note: Your head and torso needs to be slightly tilted forward to perform this exercise.
Now place both hands on top of your knees. This is the starting position.
Slowly lower your neck down until your chin touches the upper part of your chest while breathing in.
While exhaling, bring your neck back to the starting position.
Repeat for the recommended amount of repetitions.
exercises.name: Seated Head Harness Neck Resistance
exercises.name_pt: Resistência de Pescoço Sentado com Cinta
exercises.overview: This isolation exercise specifically targets the neck extensors using a weighted harness to build strength and stability in the cervical spine.
exercises.safetyInfo: Avoid this exercise if you have any neck injuries or cervical spine issues. Start with very light weight and progress gradually as neck muscles are delicate.

commonMistakes: Using momentum to lift the weight
Not adjusting the machine properly for leg length
Lifting hips off the seat during the movement
exerciseTips: Keep your back pressed against the pad
Control the negative portion slowly
Squeeze hamstrings at the bottom position
Maintain tension throughout the full range
instructions: Adjust the machine lever to fit your height and sit on the machine with your back against the back support pad.

Place the back of lower leg on top of padded lever (just a few inches under the calves) and secure the lap pad against your thighs, just above the knees. Then grasp the side handles on the machine as you point your toes straight (or you can also use any of the other two stances) and ensure that the legs are fully straight right in front of you. This will be your starting position.
As you exhale, pull the machine lever as far as possible to the back of your thighs by flexing at the knees. Keep your torso stationary at all times. Hold the contracted position for a second.
Slowly return to the starting position as you breathe in.
Repeat for the recommended amount of repetitions.
exercises.name: Seated Leg Curl
exercises.overview: A machine-based isolation exercise that effectively targets the hamstring muscles while providing back support and stability.
exercises.safetyInfo: Ensure proper machine adjustment to avoid knee strain. Those with hamstring injuries should use lighter weight and focus on controlled movement.

commonMistakes: Using momentum instead of muscle control
Not maintaining tension in the core between reps
Leaning too far back and losing balance
exerciseTips: Keep core engaged throughout the movement
Control both the lifting and lowering phases
Focus on bringing knees to chest rather than chest to knees
Maintain steady breathing pattern
instructions: Sit on a bench with the legs stretched out in front of you slightly below parallel and your arms holding on to the sides of the bench. Your torso should be leaning backwards around a 45-degree angle from the bench. This will be your starting position.
Bring the knees in toward you as you move your torso closer to them at the same time. Breathe out as you perform this movement.
After a second pause, go back to the starting position as you inhale.
Repeat for the recommended amount of repetitions.
exercises.name: Seated Leg Tucks
exercises.overview: An effective bodyweight exercise that targets the abdominal muscles through knee-to-chest movement while seated on a bench.
exercises.safetyInfo: Maintain control throughout the movement to avoid straining the lower back. Those with lower back issues should limit range of motion.

commonMistakes: Rotating the torso during the pull
Using too much bicep and not enough back
Not maintaining proper posture throughout
exerciseTips: Initiate movement by squeezing shoulder blade back
Keep torso stable and avoid rotation
Pull elbow back rather than out to the side
Control the return to starting position
instructions: To get into the starting position, first sit down on the machine and place your feet on the front platform or crossbar provided making sure that your knees are slightly bent and not locked.
Lean over as you keep the natural alignment of your back and grab the single handle attachment with your left arm using a palms-down grip.

With your arm extended pull back until your torso is at a 90-degree angle from your legs. Your back should be slightly arched and your chest should be sticking out. You should be feeling a nice stretch on your lat as you hold the bar in front of you. The right arm can be kept by the waist. This is the starting position of the exercise.

Keeping the torso stationary, pull the handles back towards your torso while keeping the arms close to it as you rotate the wrist, so that by the time your hand is by your abdominals it is in a neutral position (palms facing the torso). Breathe out as you perform that movement. At that point you should be squeezing your back muscles hard.

Hold that contraction for a second and slowly go back to the original position while breathing in. Tip: Remember to rotate the wrist as you go back to the starting position so that the palms are facing down again.
Repeat for the recommended amount of repetitions and then perform the same movement with the right hand.
exercises.name: Seated One-arm Cable Pulley Rows
exercises.overview: A unilateral pulling exercise that targets the middle back muscles while allowing focus on muscle imbalances and core stability.
exercises.safetyInfo: Keep the core engaged to prevent lower back strain. Ensure proper setup to avoid shoulder impingement during the pulling motion.
variations: Bent Over Two-Dumbbell Row With Palms In

commonMistakes: Moving the forearm instead of just the wrist
Using too heavy weight and losing control
Not using full range of motion at the wrist
exerciseTips: Keep forearm stable against your thigh
Move only at the wrist joint
Control both the lifting and lowering phases
Maintain grip on dumbbell throughout
instructions: Sit on a flat bench with a dumbbell in your right hand.
Place your feet flat on the floor, at a distance that is slightly wider than shoulder width apart.

Lean forward and place your right forearm on top of your upper right thigh with your palm down. Tip: Make sure that the back of the wrist lies on top of your knees. This will be your starting position.
Lower the dumbbell as far as possible as you keep a tight grip on the dumbbell. Inhale as you perform this movement.
Now curl the dumbbell as high as possible as you contract the forearms and as you exhale. Keep the contraction for a second before you lower again. Tip: The only movement should happen at the wrist.
Perform for the recommended amount of repetitions, switch arms and repeat the movement.
exercises.name: Seated One-Arm Dumbbell Palms-Down Wrist Curl
exercises.name_pt: Rosca de Punho Unilateral Sentado com Halter - Palmas para Baixo
exercises.overview: An isolation exercise specifically targeting the wrist extensor muscles to improve grip strength and forearm balance.
exercises.safetyInfo: Start with light weight as wrist extensors are relatively weak muscles. Avoid this exercise if experiencing wrist pain or carpal tunnel symptoms.
variations: Seated One-Arm Dumbbell Palms-Down Wrist Curl

commonMistakes: Lifting the forearm off the thigh
Using momentum instead of muscle control
Not achieving full range of motion at the wrist
exerciseTips: Keep forearm pressed firmly against thigh
Focus on curling wrist up as high as possible
Lower weight slowly under control
Maintain constant tension on the muscle
instructions: Sit on a flat bench with a dumbbell in your right hand.
Place your feet flat on the floor, at a distance that is slightly wider than shoulder width apart.

Lean forward and place your right forearm on top of your upper right thigh with your palm up. Tip: Make sure that the front of the wrist lies on top of your knees. This will be your starting position.
Lower the dumbbell as far as possible as you keep a tight grip on the dumbbell. Inhale as you perform this movement.
Now curl the dumbbell as high as possible as you contract the forearms and as you exhale. Keep the contraction for a second before you lower again. Tip: The only movement should happen at the wrist.
Perform for the recommended amount of repetitions, switch arms and repeat the movement.
exercises.name: Seated One-Arm Dumbbell Palms-Up Wrist Curl
exercises.name_pt: Rosca de Punho Unilateral Sentado com Halter - Palmas para Cima
exercises.overview: A targeted isolation exercise for the wrist flexor muscles that helps develop grip strength and forearm muscle balance.
exercises.safetyInfo: Use appropriate weight to maintain control throughout the movement. Those with wrist injuries should avoid or modify this exercise.
variations: Seated One-Arm Dumbbell Palms-Down Wrist Curl

commonMistakes: Lifting the forearms off the thighs
Using momentum instead of controlled movement
Gripping the bar too tightly and restricting movement
exerciseTips: Keep forearms pressed firmly against thighs throughout
Use only wrist movement, no forearm lifting
Control the eccentric lowering phase slowly
Maintain a secure grip throughout the range of motion
instructions: Hold a barbell with both hands and your palms facing up; hands spaced about shoulder width.
Place your feet flat on the floor, at a distance that is slightly wider than shoulder width apart.
Lean forward and place your forearms on top of your upper thighs with your palms up. Tip: Make sure that the front of the wrists lay on top of your knees. This will be your starting position.
Lower the bar as far as possible while inhaling and keeping a tight grip.
Now curl bar up as high as possible while flexing the forearms and exhaling. Hold the contraction at the top for a second and Tip: Only the wrist should move.
exercises.name: Seated Palm-Up Barbell Wrist Curl
exercises.name_pt: Rosca de Punho Sentado com Barra - Palmas para Cima
exercises.overview: This isolation exercise specifically targets the wrist flexor muscles to improve grip strength and forearm development. It's essential for athletes requiring strong grip strength and those recovering from wrist injuries.
exercises.safetyInfo: Start with light weight to avoid wrist strain. Discontinue if you experience sharp pain or tingling in the hands or fingers.
variations: Seated One-Arm Dumbbell Palms-Down Wrist Curl

commonMistakes: Using too heavy weight leading to poor form
Allowing wrists to deviate sideways
Moving too quickly through the range of motion
exerciseTips: Keep wrists aligned and avoid lateral deviation
Focus on slow, controlled movements
Maintain forearm contact with thighs throughout
Use lighter weight than flexor curls
instructions: Hold a barbell with both hands and your palms facing down; hands spaced about shoulder width.
Place your feet flat on the floor, at a distance that is slightly wider than shoulder width apart.
Lean forward and place your forearms on top of your upper thighs with your palms down. Tip: Make sure that the back of the wrists lay on top of your knees. This will be your starting position.
Lower the bar as far as possible while inhaling and keeping a tight grip.
Now curl bar up as high as possible while flexing the forearms and exhaling. Hold the contraction at the top for a second and Tip: Only the wrist should move.
exercises.name: Seated Palms-Down Barbell Wrist Curl
exercises.name_pt: Rosca de Punho Sentado com Barra - Palmas para Baixo
exercises.overview: This exercise strengthens the often-neglected wrist extensor muscles, creating balance with the flexors and reducing risk of overuse injuries. It's particularly beneficial for preventing tennis elbow and improving wrist stability.
exercises.safetyInfo: Use significantly lighter weight than wrist flexor exercises as extensors are weaker. Stop if you experience pain in the wrist or forearm.
variations: Seated One-Arm Dumbbell Palms-Down Wrist Curl

commonMistakes: Raising arms above shoulder height
Using momentum or swinging the weights
Internally rotating shoulders during the movement
exerciseTips: Lead with your pinkies slightly higher than thumbs
Stop when arms reach parallel to floor
Keep a slight bend in elbows throughout
Control the descent to maximize muscle tension
instructions: Pick a couple of dumbbells and sit at the end of a flat bench with your feet firmly on the floor. Hold the dumbbells with your palms facing in and your arms straight down at your sides at arms' length. This will be your starting position.

While maintaining the torso stationary (no swinging), lift the dumbbells to your side with a slight bend on the elbow and the hands slightly tilted forward as if pouring water in a glass. Continue to go up until you arms are parallel to the floor. Exhale as you execute this movement and pause for a second at the top.
Lower the dumbbells back down slowly to the starting position as you inhale.
Repeat for the recommended amount of repetitions.
exercises.name: Seated Side Lateral Raise
exercises.overview: This isolation exercise effectively targets the lateral deltoid to build shoulder width and strength. The seated position eliminates lower body momentum, ensuring pure shoulder muscle activation.
exercises.safetyInfo: Avoid if you have shoulder impingement or rotator cuff injuries. Start with light weight and progress gradually to prevent shoulder irritation.

commonMistakes: Allowing elbows to flare outward
Lowering the weight too far and straining shoulders
Arching the back excessively
exerciseTips: Keep elbows close to your head throughout
Lower the weight slowly behind your head
Focus on moving only your forearms
Maintain tight core to support your spine
instructions: Sit down on a bench with back support and grasp a dumbbell with both hands and hold it overhead at arm's length. Tip: a better way is to have somebody hand it to you especially if it is very heavy. The resistance should be resting in the palms of your hands with your thumbs around it. The palm of the hand should be facing inward. This will be your starting position.

Keeping your upper arms close to your head (elbows in) and perpendicular to the floor, lower the resistance in a semi-circular motion behind your head until your forearms touch your biceps. Tip: The upper arms should remain stationary and only the forearms should move. Breathe in as you perform this step.
Go back to the starting position by using the triceps to raise the dumbbell. Breathe out as you perform this step.
Repeat for the recommended amount of repetitions.
exercises.name: Seated Triceps Press
exercises.overview: This compound triceps exercise effectively targets all three heads of the triceps while the seated position provides stability. It's excellent for building overall arm mass and pressing strength.
exercises.safetyInfo: Use a spotter for heavy weights and ensure secure grip. Avoid if you have shoulder mobility issues or recent shoulder injuries.

commonMistakes: Lifting forearms off thighs during movement
Using jerky motions instead of smooth control
Allowing cable tension to go slack at bottom
exerciseTips: Maintain constant tension on the cable
Keep forearms pressed against thighs
Focus on full range of motion at the wrist
Control both the lifting and lowering phases
instructions: Put a bench in front of a low pulley machine that has a barbell or EZ Curl attachment on it.
Move the bench far enough away so that when you bring the handle to the top of your thighs tension is created on the cable due to the weight stack being moved up.
Now hold the handle with both hands, palms up, using a shoulder-width grip.
Step back and sit on the bench with your feet about shoulder width apart, firmly on the floor.
Lean forward and place the forearms on your thighs with the back of your wrists over your knees. This will be your starting position.
Lower the bar as far as possible, while inhaling and keeping a tight grip.
Now curl the bar up as high as possible while contracting the forearms. Tip: Only the wrist should move; not the forearms.
After a second contraction at the top go back to the starting position as you inhale.
Repeat for the recommended amount of repetitions.
exercises.name: Seated Two-Arm Palms-Up Low-Pulley Wrist Curl
exercises.name_pt: Rosca de Punho Sentado na Polia Baixa - Palmas para Cima
exercises.overview: This cable variation of wrist curls provides constant tension throughout the range of motion, making it highly effective for forearm strength and endurance. The cable system allows for smooth, controlled movements.
exercises.safetyInfo: Start with light resistance to avoid overloading the small wrist muscles. Stop immediately if you experience sharp pain or numbness.
variations: Seated One-Arm Dumbbell Palms-Down Wrist Curl

commonMistakes: Leaning too far to the side creating spine stress
Pressing the weight forward instead of directly overhead
Using momentum instead of controlled movement
exerciseTips: Keep your core engaged throughout the movement to prevent excessive lateral flexion
Rotate the wrist smoothly from neutral to pronated position during the press
Control the lateral bend - lean only as far as comfortable
Press the weight in a straight line overhead rather than forward
instructions: Grab a dumbbell with each hand and stand up erect.

Clean (lift) the dumbbells to the chest/shoulder level and then rotate your wrists so that your palms are facing towards you as if you were getting ready to perform an Arnold Press. This will be your starting position.

Now start extending your left arm overhead as you rotate the wrist so that the palm of your hand faces forward as you go up. Your elbows should come out also as you lift the weight. Simultaneously, you will also be bending from your hip to your opposite side. Tip: If you perform the exercise correctly, is should look as if you are trying to reach for something overhead on the right hand side of your body, but with your left arm. Breathe out as you perform this movement.
Once you reach the top position breathe in. Then, with the weight fully extended overhead and you bent over to your right hand side, begin the movement to the left side.
Repeat for the recommended amount of repetitions.
exercises.name: See-Saw Press (Alternating Side Press)
exercises.overview: The See-Saw Press is a dynamic overhead pressing movement that combines shoulder pressing with lateral core stability. It challenges unilateral strength while improving coordination and functional movement patterns.
exercises.safetyInfo: Avoid this exercise if you have lower back issues or shoulder impingement. The lateral bending component can stress the lumbar spine if performed with excessive range of motion.

commonMistakes: Using too much arm and not enough back muscle activation
Allowing the torso to rotate during the pull
Starting the movement with elbow flexion instead of shoulder retraction
exerciseTips: Maintain a wide split stance for stability throughout the movement
Initiate the pull by retracting the shoulder blade first
Supinate the wrist as you pull to maximize bicep engagement
Keep your torso stable and avoid rotating
instructions: Attach a single handle to a low cable.
After selecting the correct weight, stand a couple feet back with a wide-split stance. Your arm should be extended and your shoulder forward. This will be your starting position.
Perform the movement by retracting the shoulder and flexing the elbow. As you pull, supinate the wrist, turning the palm upward as you go.
After a brief pause, return to the starting position.
exercises.name: Shotgun Row
exercises.overview: The Shotgun Row is a unilateral cable pulling exercise that targets the lats while challenging core stability. The staggered stance and supination component make it excellent for developing functional pulling strength.
exercises.safetyInfo: Use controlled movements and avoid jerky motions that could strain the shoulder or lower back. Start with lighter weight to master the coordination pattern.
variations: Bent Over Two-Dumbbell Row With Palms In

commonMistakes: Not standing firmly enough on the band causing inconsistent resistance
Pressing forward instead of directly overhead
Allowing the bands to snap back without control
exerciseTips: Stand firmly on the band to create consistent tension
Keep your core engaged and maintain neutral spine
Press straight up rather than forward
Control the descent to maximize muscle engagement
instructions: To begin, stand on an exercise band so that tension begins at arm's length. Grasp the handles and lift them so that the hands are at shoulder height at each side.
Rotate the wrists so that the palms of your hands are facing forward. Your elbows should be bent, with the upper arms and forearms in line to the torso. This is your starting position.
As you exhale, lift the handles up until your arms are fully extended overhead.
exercises.name: Shoulder Press - With Bands
exercises.overview: The Band Shoulder Press provides variable resistance training for the shoulders, offering a joint-friendly alternative to free weights while improving pressing strength and stability.
exercises.safetyInfo: Ensure the resistance band is in good condition without nicks or tears. Check that you're standing securely on the band to prevent it from snapping up.

commonMistakes: Releasing the back leg grip during the squatting motion
Allowing the working knee to cave inward during the descent
Using momentum instead of controlled strength to return to standing
exerciseTips: Keep your chest up and core engaged throughout the entire movement
Focus on sitting back into the squat rather than allowing your knee to drift forward
Maintain a firm grip on your back ankle to keep the non-working leg elevated
Practice single-leg balance and flexibility separately before combining movements
instructions: Stand on one leg and reach back to grab the ankle of your non-working leg behind you.
Hold your back foot while extending that leg behind and above you in a stretched position.
Slowly lower down into a single-leg squat while maintaining your grip on the elevated back leg.
Descend until your working thigh is parallel to the ground or as low as mobility allows.
Drive through your working heel to return to the starting position while maintaining balance and form.
exercises.name: Shrimp Squat
exercises.overview: The shrimp squat is an extremely advanced single-leg squat variation where the non-working leg is held elevated behind the body. It demands exceptional strength, balance, and flexibility while providing intense unilateral leg development.
exercises.safetyInfo: This exercise requires exceptional single-leg strength, balance, and flexibility. Those with knee injuries or severe balance issues should master easier single-leg variations first. Ensure adequate ankle and hip mobility.

commonMistakes: Allowing hips to sag or pike upward
Rolling forward or backward instead of staying lateral
Holding breath during the exercise
exerciseTips: Keep your body in a straight line from head to feet
Engage your core by drawing your navel toward your spine
Press your bottom elbow firmly into the ground
Breathe steadily throughout the hold
instructions: Perform the exercise with proper form.
exercises.name: Side Bridge
exercises.overview: The side bridge is an isometric core exercise that targets the obliques and improves lateral stability. It's essential for developing balanced core strength and preventing lower back injuries.
exercises.safetyInfo: Avoid if you have shoulder impingement or acute lower back pain. Progress gradually from knees to full side plank.

commonMistakes: Using momentum instead of muscle control
Pulling on your neck with the top hand
Incomplete range of motion
exerciseTips: Initiate movement by contracting your obliques
Keep your bottom arm stable for support
Focus on bringing knee and elbow together
Control the lowering phase
instructions: Perform the exercise with proper form.
exercises.name: Side Jackknife
exercises.overview: The side jackknife targets the obliques through lateral flexion while lying on your side. It's an effective exercise for developing core strength and definition in the waist area.
exercises.safetyInfo: Keep movements controlled to avoid neck strain. Those with hip flexor tightness should start with smaller ranges of motion.

commonMistakes: Raising arms above shoulder level
Using too much weight and compensating with body swing
Completely straightening arms instead of maintaining slight bend
exerciseTips: Lead with your pinkies slightly higher than thumbs
Stop at shoulder height to avoid impingement
Control the weight on the way down
Keep a slight forward lean to target the muscle better
instructions: Pick a couple of dumbbells and stand with a straight torso and the dumbbells by your side at arms length with the palms of the hand facing you. This will be your starting position.

While maintaining the torso in a stationary position (no swinging), lift the dumbbells to your side with a slight bend on the elbow and the hands slightly tilted forward as if pouring water in a glass. Continue to go up until you arms are parallel to the floor. Exhale as you execute this movement and pause for a second at the top.
Lower the dumbbells back down slowly to the starting position as you inhale.
Repeat for the recommended amount of repetitions.
exercises.name: Side Lateral Raise
exercises.overview: The side lateral raise isolates the lateral deltoid to build shoulder width and strength. It's a fundamental exercise for developing well-rounded shoulder development and upper body aesthetics.
exercises.safetyInfo: Avoid if you have shoulder impingement syndrome. Start with lighter weights to master the movement pattern before progressing.

commonMistakes: Using momentum to swing weights between positions
Allowing shoulders to roll forward during front raise
Raising weights too high above shoulder level
exerciseTips: Maintain constant tension throughout the movement
Keep the transition smooth between planes
Control both the lifting and lowering phases
Maintain proper posture throughout the entire sequence
instructions: In a standing position, hold a pair of dumbbells at your side. This will be your starting position.
Keeping your elbows slightly bent, raise the weights directly in front of you to shoulder height, avoiding any swinging or cheating.
At the top of the exercise move the weights out in front of you, keeping your arms extended.
Lower the weights with a controlled motion.
On the next repetition, raise the weights in front of you to shoulder height before moving the weights laterally to your sides.
Lower the weights to the starting position.
exercises.name: Side Laterals to Front Raise
exercises.overview: This combination exercise targets both the lateral and anterior deltoids in one fluid movement. It's an efficient way to build shoulder strength and endurance while improving coordination.
exercises.safetyInfo: Use lighter weights than single-plane raises due to the extended time under tension. Avoid if you have rotator cuff injuries or shoulder impingement.

commonMistakes: Using momentum to swing between sides
Not achieving full range of motion on each side
Gripping the bar too narrow limiting the lateral movement
exerciseTips: Pull toward one side while keeping the opposite arm extended
Focus on squeezing the lat on the working side
Control the eccentric portion of each rep
Alternate sides with each repetition for balanced development
instructions: Grab the pull-up bar with the palms facing forward using a wide grip.

As you have both arms extended in front of you holding the bar at a wide grip, bring your torso back around 30 degrees or so while creating a curvature on your lower back and sticking your chest out. This is your starting position.

Pull your torso up while leaning to the left hand side until the bar almost touches your upper chest by drawing the shoulders and the upper arms down and back. Exhale as you perform this portion of the movement. Tip: Concentrate on squeezing the back muscles once you reach the full contracted position. The upper torso should remain stationary as it moves through space (no swinging) and only the arms should move. The forearms should do no other work other than hold the bar.
After a second of contraction, inhale as you go back to the starting position.

Now, pull your torso up while leaning to the right hand side until the bar almost touches your upper chest by drawing the shoulders and the upper arms down and back. Exhale as you perform this portion of the movement. Tip: Concentrate on squeezing the back muscles once you reach the full contracted position. The upper torso should remain stationary as it moves through space and only the arms should move. The forearms should do no other work other than hold the bar.
After a second of contraction, inhale as you go back to the starting position.
Repeat steps 3-6 until you have performed the prescribed amount of repetitions for each side.
exercises.name: Side To Side Chins
exercises.name_pt: Barra Fixa de Lado a Lado
exercises.overview: Side To Side Chins are an advanced pull-up variation that emphasizes unilateral lat development and challenges core stability. This exercise builds functional pulling strength with an asymmetrical component.
exercises.safetyInfo: This is an advanced variation requiring significant upper body strength. Ensure you can perform regular chin-ups proficiently before attempting. May stress shoulder joints more than standard chin-ups.

commonMistakes: Bending the elbow too much, turning it into a press
Using the shoulder instead of the chest to move the weight
Not maintaining tension at the stretched position
exerciseTips: Maintain a slight bend in your elbow throughout the movement
Focus on squeezing your chest rather than moving the weight
Keep your shoulders down and back
Control the eccentric portion for maximum muscle activation
instructions: Set the cable handle to chest height and grab it with one hand.
Stand in a staggered stance with your opposite foot forward for stability.
Start with your working arm extended out to the side with a slight bend in your elbow.
Bring your arm across your body in a wide arc until your hand reaches the center of your chest.
Squeeze your chest muscle at the peak contraction.
Return to the starting position with control and repeat before switching arms.
exercises.name: Single Arm Cable Chest Fly
exercises.overview: The single arm cable chest fly is an isolation exercise that targets the chest muscles through a wide range of motion. The unilateral nature challenges core stability while the cable provides constant tension for optimal muscle activation.
exercises.safetyInfo: This exercise is generally safe for most people but those with anterior shoulder problems should start with light resistance. Avoid overstretching at the starting position.

commonMistakes: Using too much arm and not enough back muscle
Allowing the shoulder to roll forward
Using momentum to pull the weight down
exerciseTips: Focus on pulling with your lat, not your arm
Keep your shoulder blade down and back
Maintain a slight lean back position
Control the weight on the way up
instructions: Attach a single handle to a high cable pulley and select appropriate weight.
Sit on a bench or stand with feet shoulder-width apart facing the cable.
Reach up and grasp the handle with one hand, arm fully extended.
Pull the handle down toward your chest, focusing on using your lat muscle.
Slowly return to the starting position and repeat for desired reps.
Switch arms and repeat on the other side.
exercises.name: Single Arm Cable Pulldown
exercises.overview: The single arm cable pulldown is a unilateral exercise that targets the lats while allowing you to focus on one side at a time. It's excellent for correcting muscle imbalances and improving mind-muscle connection.
exercises.safetyInfo: Start with lighter weight to master the movement pattern. Those with shoulder injuries should avoid overhead reaching positions or consult a healthcare provider first.

commonMistakes: Rotating the torso excessively toward the working arm
Using momentum and swinging the body to move the weight
Pulling too high toward the shoulder instead of the lower ribs
exerciseTips: Keep your torso upright and avoid rotating toward the working side
Focus on pulling your elbow back rather than just moving your hand
Squeeze your shoulder blade toward your spine at the end
Control the weight on both the pulling and returning phases
instructions: Set a cable pulley to middle height and attach a single handle.
Sit on the bench or stand with feet staggered, grasping the handle with one hand.
Start with your arm extended and shoulder blade protracted.
Pull the handle toward your lower ribs while retracting your shoulder blade.
Squeeze your back muscles at the end of the movement.
Return to the starting position with control and repeat for all reps before switching arms.
exercises.name: Single Arm Cable Row
exercises.name_pt: Remada com Um Braço na Polia
exercises.overview: The single arm cable row is a unilateral pulling exercise that targets the latissimus dorsi and middle back muscles. Training one arm at a time allows for focused muscle development and helps correct imbalances.
exercises.safetyInfo: This exercise is generally safe for most individuals. The unilateral nature helps identify and correct strength imbalances between sides.

commonMistakes: Allowing the torso to rotate excessively
Using momentum instead of controlled movement
Lowering the dumbbell too far past chest level
exerciseTips: Keep your non-working hand on your core to maintain stability
Focus on preventing your torso from rotating during the movement
Control the descent to maximize time under tension
Squeeze your glutes to maintain a stable base of support
instructions: Lie on a flat bench holding a dumbbell in one hand with your arm extended above your chest.
Position your feet firmly on the floor and engage your core to prevent rotation.
Lower the dumbbell slowly to the side of your chest, keeping your elbow at a 45-degree angle.
Press the dumbbell back up to the starting position, focusing on squeezing your chest muscles.
Complete all repetitions on one side before switching to the other arm.
exercises.name: Single Arm Dumbbell Bench Press
exercises.name_pt: Supino Unilateral com Halter no Banco Reto
exercises.overview: The single arm dumbbell bench press is a unilateral chest exercise that challenges core stability while building chest strength. This variation forces your core to work harder to prevent rotation, making it an excellent functional movement.
exercises.safetyInfo: This exercise requires significant core stability and should be avoided by those with shoulder impingement or lower back issues. Start with lighter weights than your bilateral bench press.

commonMistakes: Allowing the torso to rotate during the movement
Pressing the weight at an angle instead of straight up
Arching the back excessively
exerciseTips: Engage your core to prevent rotation during the press
Keep your shoulder blade pulled back and down
Press the weight straight up, not angled toward your head
Control both the lifting and lowering phases
instructions: Lie on a bench with your feet planted firmly on the floor, holding a dumbbell in one hand.
Position the dumbbell at chest level with your palm facing forward.
Keep your non-working hand on your chest or extended to the side for balance.
Press the dumbbell straight up until your arm is fully extended above your chest.
Lower the weight with control until it reaches chest level.
Complete all reps on one side before switching to the other arm.
exercises.name: Single Arm Dumbbell Chest Press
exercises.overview: The single arm dumbbell chest press challenges unilateral pressing strength while demanding core stability to prevent rotation. It helps identify and correct strength imbalances between sides.
exercises.safetyInfo: This exercise requires significant core stability and shoulder control. Start with lighter weight than your regular dumbbell press to master the unilateral pattern safely.

commonMistakes: Using only arm strength instead of full body power
Allowing the dumbbell to swing away from the body
Failing to get the elbow under the weight quickly enough
exerciseTips: Generate power from your hips and legs
Keep the dumbbell close to your body throughout
Rotate your elbow under quickly during the catch phase
Land in a slight squat position when catching
instructions: Stand with feet shoulder-width apart, dumbbell on the floor between your feet.
Squat down and grasp the dumbbell with one hand using an overhand grip.
Explosively extend your hips and knees while pulling the dumbbell upward.
As the dumbbell reaches chest level, quickly rotate your elbow under the weight.
Catch the dumbbell at shoulder level in the front rack position.
Lower the dumbbell back to the starting position with control.
exercises.name: Single Arm Dumbbell Clean
exercises.overview: The single arm dumbbell clean is an explosive, full-body movement that develops power, coordination, and unilateral strength. It's a complex exercise that requires proper technique and should be learned progressively.
exercises.safetyInfo: This is an advanced explosive movement requiring good mobility and coordination. Those with wrist, shoulder, or lower back issues should avoid this exercise. Master the movement with light weight first.

commonMistakes: Pulling the weight too wide away from the body
Using only arm strength instead of full-body power
Allowing the dumbbell to swing away from the body
exerciseTips: Lead the movement with your elbow, not your hand
Keep the dumbbell close to your body throughout the pull
Generate power from your legs and transfer it through your core
Control the eccentric portion of the movement
instructions: Hold a dumbbell in your right hand and stand with feet shoulder-width apart.
Let the dumbbell hang at arm's length with your palm facing your body.
Explosively pull the dumbbell up by driving your elbow high and to the side.
Pull until the dumbbell reaches chest height and your elbow is higher than your hand.
Lower the dumbbell back to the starting position with control.
Complete all reps on one arm before switching to the other side.
exercises.name: Single Arm Dumbbell High Pull
exercises.overview: The single arm dumbbell high pull is an advanced power exercise that develops explosive upper body strength while challenging stability and coordination. It builds functional pulling power and shoulder stability.
exercises.safetyInfo: Requires good shoulder mobility and stability. Master the bilateral version before attempting single arm. Avoid if you have shoulder impingement or rotator cuff issues.

commonMistakes: Allowing the dumbbell to drift forward or to the side
Losing depth in the squat due to overhead challenge
Leaning toward the weighted side
exerciseTips: Keep the dumbbell directly over your shoulder
Engage your core to prevent lateral flexion
Focus on maintaining perfect squat mechanics
Start with lighter weight to master the movement pattern
instructions: Hold dumbbell overhead in one hand with arm fully extended.
Stand with feet slightly wider than shoulder-width apart.
Keep dumbbell directly over shoulder throughout movement.
Lower into squat by pushing hips back and bending knees.
Descend until thighs are parallel to floor while maintaining overhead position.
Drive through heels to return to starting position.
exercises.name: Single Arm Dumbbell Overhead Squat
exercises.name_pt: Agachamento com Halter Acima da Cabeça Unilateral
exercises.overview: The single arm dumbbell overhead squat is an advanced unilateral movement that challenges the entire body. It builds functional strength, improves shoulder stability, and develops anti-lateral flexion core strength while maintaining proper squat mechanics.
exercises.safetyInfo: This is an advanced movement requiring excellent shoulder mobility, core stability, and squat mechanics. Master bodyweight overhead squats and regular dumbbell squats before attempting. Avoid if you have shoulder impingement or mobility restrictions.

commonMistakes: Rounding the back instead of hinging at the hips
Allowing the dumbbell to drift away from the body
Bending the knees too much and turning it into a squat
exerciseTips: Initiate the movement by pushing your hips back
Keep the dumbbell close to your body throughout
Maintain a neutral spine and engaged core
Feel the stretch in your hamstrings at the bottom
instructions: Hold a dumbbell in one hand and stand with feet hip-width apart.
Keep a slight bend in your knees and engage your core for stability.
Hinge at the hips by pushing them back while lowering the dumbbell toward the floor.
Keep the dumbbell close to your body as you lower with control.
Feel the stretch in your hamstrings before driving your hips forward to return to start.
Complete all reps on one side before switching the dumbbell to the other hand.
exercises.name: Single Arm Dumbbell Romanian Deadlift
