instructions: Hold a dumbbell in each hand with a pronated grip. Your feet should be wide with your hips and knees extended. This will be your starting position.
Begin the movement by pulling both of the dumbbells to one side next to your hip, rotating your torso.
Keeping your arms straight and the dumbbells parallel to the ground, rotate your torso to swing the weights to your opposite side.
Continue alternating, rotating from one side to the other until the set is complete.
exercises.name: Spell Caster
exercises.overview: The spell caster is a dynamic rotational core exercise that challenges anti-rotation strength and rotational power. It improves core stability while training movement in the transverse plane.
exercises.safetyInfo: Start with lighter weights to avoid losing control during the rotational movement. Those with lower back issues should avoid this exercise or use very light weights.

commonMistakes: Allowing hips to pike up or sag during the movement
Rushing the movement without full knee-to-elbow contact
Losing plank position and allowing the back to round
exerciseTips: Keep your hips level and avoid rotation during movement
Drive your knee as close to your elbow as possible
Maintain strong plank position throughout the exercise
Control the movement rather than rushing through reps
instructions: Begin in a prone position on the floor. Support your weight on your hands and toes, with your feet together and your body straight. Your arms should be bent to 90 degrees. This will be your starting position.
Initiate the movement by raising one foot off of the ground. Externally rotate the leg and bring the knee toward your elbow, as far forward as possible.
Return this leg to the starting position and repeat on the opposite side.
exercises.name: Spider Crawl
exercises.overview: Spider crawl is a dynamic core and upper body exercise that combines plank stability with hip mobility. It challenges anti-extension core strength while improving hip flexor flexibility and shoulder stability.
exercises.safetyInfo: Maintain neutral spine throughout the movement. Those with wrist issues may need to modify by using push-up handles or performing on forearms.

commonMistakes: Allowing the torso to lift off the pad during the curl
Using momentum to swing the weight up
Fully extending arms at the bottom causing joint stress
exerciseTips: Keep your chest pressed firmly against the pad throughout the movement
Control the descent slowly to maximize muscle tension
Squeeze your biceps at the top of each rep
Maintain a slight bend in your elbows at the bottom position
instructions: Start out by setting the bar on the part of the preacher bench that you would normally sit on. Make sure to align the barbell properly so that it is balanced and will not fall off.

Move to the front side of the preacher bench (the part where the arms usually lay) and position yourself to lay at a 45 degree slant with your torso and stomach pressed against the front side of the preacher bench.
Make sure that your feet (especially the toes) are well positioned on the floor and place your upper arms on top of the pad located on the inside part of the preacher bench.
Use your arms to grab the barbell with a supinated grip (palms facing up) at about shoulder width apart or slightly closer from each other.
Slowly begin to lift the barbell upwards and exhale. Hold the contracted position for a second as you squeeze the biceps.
Slowly begin to bring the barbell back to the starting position as your breathe in. .
Repeat for the recommended amount of repetitions.
exercises.name: Spider Curl
exercises.overview: The spider curl is a bicep isolation exercise performed on the front side of a preacher bench in a prone position. This unique angle provides constant tension on the biceps throughout the range of motion and eliminates momentum.
exercises.safetyInfo: Avoid fully locking out the elbows at the bottom position to prevent hyperextension. Start with lighter weight to master the form as the prone position can be unstable for beginners.

commonMistakes: Not coordinating the leg movement with the push-up
Allowing the hips to rotate excessively
Rushing through the movement without control
exerciseTips: Coordinate the knee movement with the push-up descent
Keep your core tight to maintain stability
Aim to touch your knee to your elbow if flexibility allows
Maintain proper push-up form despite the added leg movement
instructions: Start in a standard push-up position with hands shoulder-width apart.
As you lower into the push-up, simultaneously bring one knee toward the elbow on the same side.
Press back up to the starting position while returning your leg to the original position.
Perform the next repetition bringing the opposite knee to its corresponding elbow.
Continue alternating legs with each push-up repetition.
exercises.name: Spiderman Push Up
exercises.overview: Spiderman push-ups add a dynamic leg movement to standard push-ups, bringing the knee toward the elbow during each rep. This variation increases core engagement and challenges stability while building upper body strength.
exercises.safetyInfo: The lateral leg movement increases stress on the core and hip flexors. Those with hip problems should modify the range of motion. Ensure adequate warm-up before attempting.

commonMistakes: Allowing front knee to cave inward during descent
Placing too much weight on rear foot instead of front leg
Taking too narrow of a stance limiting range of motion
exerciseTips: Keep front knee tracking over toes throughout the movement
Drive through front heel to engage glutes and quads
Maintain upright torso to prevent forward lean
Control descent for 2-3 seconds to maximize time under tension
instructions: Position yourself into a staggered stance with the rear foot elevated and front foot forward.
Hold a dumbbell in each hand, letting them hang at the sides. This will be your starting position.
Begin by descending, flexing your knee and hip to lower your body down. Maintain good posture througout the movement. Keep the front knee in line with the foot as you perform the exercise.
At the bottom of the movement, drive through the heel to extend the knee and hip to return to the starting position.
exercises.name: Split Squat with Dumbbells
exercises.overview: A unilateral lower body exercise that builds single-leg strength and stability while targeting the quadriceps and glutes. Excellent for correcting muscle imbalances between legs.
exercises.safetyInfo: Avoid if you have knee instability or ankle mobility issues. Start with bodyweight only before adding dumbbells to master the movement pattern.

commonMistakes: Hovering too high or inconsistent distance from chest
Allowing the bar to drift during the pause phase
Rushing through the pause instead of maintaining tension
exerciseTips: Maintain consistent hover distance above chest throughout the pause
Keep bar completely still during the pause phase without drifting
Focus on generating maximum tension during the pause before pressing
Control the eccentric portion to ensure proper pause position
instructions: Set up on bench with feet firmly planted and shoulder blades retracted against the pad.
Grip barbell with hands positioned slightly wider than shoulders and unrack with control.
Lower the bar slowly until it hovers 1-2 inches above chest without touching.
Pause in this position for 2-3 seconds while maintaining tension and control.
Press the bar back to full arm extension with explosive force from the paused position.
exercises.name: Spoto Press
exercises.overview: The Spoto press involves pausing the bar 1-2 inches above the chest rather than touching, building tremendous starting strength and control. Named after world record holder Eric Spoto, this variation develops explosive power from compromised positions.
exercises.safetyInfo: Requires excellent shoulder stability and control. The pause position can be challenging for those with shoulder impingement issues. Start with lighter weight to master the technique.

commonMistakes: Pressing the bar instead of driving it with leg power
Moving feet too slowly to receiving position
Catching with arms not fully extended overhead
exerciseTips: Drive explosively through heels during the dip and drive phase
Move feet quickly to receiving position as bar leaves shoulders
Catch the bar with arms fully locked overhead
Keep bar aligned over heels in the bottom squat position
instructions: Standing with the weight racked on the front of the shoulders, begin with the dip. With your feet directly under your hips, flex the knees without moving the hips backward. Go down only slightly, and reverse direction as powerfully as possible. Drive through the heels create as much speed and force as possible, and be sure to move your head out of the way as the bar leaves the shoulders.

At this moment as the feet leave the floor, the feet must be placed into the receiving position as quickly as possible. In the brief moment the feet are not actively driving against the platform, the athlete's effort to push the bar up will drive them down. The feet should move forcefully to just outside the hips, turned out as necessary. Receive the bar with your body in a full squat and the arms fully extended overhead.
Keeping the bar aligned over the front of the heels, your head and chest up, drive throught heels of the feet to move to a standing position. Carefully return the weight to floor.
exercises.name: Squat Jerk
exercises.overview: An advanced Olympic weightlifting movement that explosively drives a barbell from the shoulders to overhead while receiving it in a full squat. Develops total body power and coordination.
exercises.safetyInfo: Advanced Olympic lifting movement requiring extensive coaching and mobility. Risk of shoulder impingement or knee injury if performed incorrectly. Master front squat and overhead squat first.

commonMistakes: Rushing the lateral movement portion between squats
Taking too narrow stance reducing functional benefit
Losing balance during plate pushing motion
exerciseTips: Maintain wide stance throughout entire movement
Push plate smoothly with inside foot between reps
Keep torso upright during lateral stepping
Control the squat portion separate from the lateral movement
instructions: To begin, first set the bar on a rack to just below shoulder level. Position a weight plate on the ground a couple feet back from the rack. Once the bar is loaded, step under it and place the back of your shoulders across it.
Hold on to the bar with both hands and lift it off the rack by first pushing with your legs and at the same time straighten your torso.
Step away from the rack and adopt a wide stance with the toes slightly pointed out, with one foot on the weight plate. Keep your head up at all times. This will be your starting position.
Begin to slowly lower the bar by bending the knees and hips. Continue down until the angle between the upper leg and the calves becomes slightly less than 90-degrees.
Raise the bar as you exhale by pushing the floor with the heels of your feet as you extend the hips and knees.
At the top of the movement, side step, bringing your feet together on the opposite side of the plate.
Using your inside foot, push the weight plate, sliding it across the floor to where you were just standing.
Place your inside foot on the weight plate, adopting a wide stance for the next repetition.
exercises.name: Squat with Plate Movers
exercises.overview: A functional movement combining squats with lateral plate movement to build multi-directional strength and coordination. Develops lateral stability and functional athletic movement patterns.
exercises.safetyInfo: Ensure adequate space for lateral movement and plate sliding. Start with lighter weight to master coordination. Avoid on slippery surfaces that could cause falls.

commonMistakes: Allowing knees to cave inward under band tension
Not maintaining proper band positioning throughout the movement
Rising up on toes instead of driving through heels
exerciseTips: Keep your chest up and core engaged throughout the movement
Drive through your heels when standing up
Control the descent to maximize time under tension
Maintain equal tension on both sides of the band
instructions: To start out, make sure that the exercise band is at an even split between both the left and right side of the body. To do this, use your hands to grab both sides of the band and place both feet in the middle of the band. Your feet should be shoulder width apart from each other.

When holding the bands, they should be the same height on each side. You should be using a pronated grip (palms facing forward) and have the handles of the bands next to your face for this exercise. This is the starting position.
Slowly start to bend the knees and lower the legs so that your thighs are parallel to the floor while exhaling.
Use the heel of your feet to push your body up to the starting position as you exhale.
Repeat for the recommended amount of repetitions.
exercises.name: Squats - With Bands
exercises.name_pt: Agachamento com Faixa Elástica sobre a Barra
exercises.overview: Banded squats add variable resistance to the traditional squat pattern, increasing difficulty at the top of the movement. This variation helps improve squat strength and muscular endurance while providing accommodating resistance.
exercises.safetyInfo: Ensure the resistance band is securely positioned and won't snap during the movement. Avoid this exercise if you have knee or ankle injuries.

commonMistakes: Allowing the body to rotate due to the uneven hand position
Favoring one arm over the other
Placing hands too far apart initially
exerciseTips: Keep your core engaged to prevent rotation
Focus on pressing evenly with both arms despite the offset position
Start with small hand position differences
Switch hand positions between sets to ensure balanced development
instructions: Start in push-up position with one hand placed forward and one hand placed back, creating a staggered stance.
Lower your body toward the ground while maintaining the staggered hand position.
Press back up to the starting position, focusing on even force from both arms.
Complete the desired repetitions, then switch hand positions for the next set.
Maintain proper body alignment throughout the movement.
exercises.name: Staggered Push Up
exercises.overview: Staggered push-ups use an offset hand position to create uneven loading and challenge stability. This intermediate variation helps identify and correct strength imbalances while building functional pushing strength.
exercises.safetyInfo: The offset hand position increases stress on the wrists and shoulders. Those with wrist problems should use caution. Progress gradually from standard push-ups.

commonMistakes: Arching the back excessively during the press
Allowing the non-working arm to drift or drop
Using momentum to drive the weight up
exerciseTips: Engage your core to prevent back arching
Press dumbbells straight up rather than forward
Keep non-working arm stable at shoulder height
Control the descent with slow tempo
instructions: Stand with a dumbbell in each hand. Raise the dumbbells to your shoulders with your palms facing forward and your elbows pointed out. This will be your starting position.
Extend one arm to press the dumbbell straight up, keeping your off hand in place. Do not lean or jerk the weight during the movement.
After a brief pause, return the weight to the starting position.
Repeat for the opposite side, continuing to alternate between arms.
exercises.name: Standing Alternating Dumbbell Press
exercises.name_pt: Desenvolvimento Alternado em Pé com Halteres
exercises.overview: The standing alternating dumbbell press challenges shoulder stability and core strength while building unilateral pressing power. The alternating pattern helps identify and correct strength imbalances between sides.
exercises.safetyInfo: Use appropriate weight to maintain proper form throughout all repetitions. Avoid this exercise if you have shoulder impingement or lower back issues without proper progression.

commonMistakes: Bouncing at the bottom of the movement
Not achieving full range of motion at the top
Allowing knees to bend during the movement
exerciseTips: Rise up onto your toes as high as possible
Pause briefly at the top of each repetition
Control the descent to stretch the calves fully
Keep your torso upright throughout the movement
instructions: This exercise is best performed inside a squat rack for safety purposes. To begin, first set the bar on a rack that best matches your height. Once the correct height is chosen and the bar is loaded, step under the bar and place the bar on the back of your shoulders (slightly below the neck).
Hold on to the bar using both arms at each side and lift it off the rack by first pushing with your legs and at the same time straightening your torso.

Step away from the rack and position your legs using a shoulder width medium stance with the toes slightly pointed out. Keep your head up at all times as looking down will get you off balance and also maintain a straight back. The knees should be kept with a slight bend; never locked. This will be your starting position. Tip: For better range of motion you may also place the ball of your feet on a wooden block but be careful as this option requires more balance and a sturdy block.

Raise your heels as you breathe out by extending your ankles as high as possible and flexing your calf. Ensure that the knee is kept stationary at all times. There should be no bending at any time. Hold the contracted position by a second before you start to go back down.
Go back slowly to the starting position as you breathe in by lowering your heels as you bend the ankles until calves are stretched.
Repeat for the recommended amount of repetitions.
exercises.name: Standing Barbell Calf Raise
exercises.overview: The standing barbell calf raise is an effective isolation exercise for building calf muscle size and strength. The loaded position challenges balance while maximizing muscle tension in the gastrocnemius and soleus.
exercises.safetyInfo: Use a squat rack for safety when loading and unloading the barbell. Start with lighter weight to assess balance and coordination. Those with Achilles tendon issues should use caution.
variations: Smith Machine Calf Raise Single Leg

commonMistakes: Lowering the bar too far below the neck
Using excessive weight before adequate mobility is achieved
Jerking or using momentum to press the weight
exerciseTips: Keep your head in neutral position throughout the movement
Lower the bar only to ear level
Maintain a slight forward lean of the torso
Use a controlled tempo on both lifting and lowering phases
instructions: This exercise is best performed inside a squat rack for easier pick up of the bar. To begin, first set the bar on a rack that best matches your height. Once the correct height is chosen and the bar is loaded, step under the bar and place the back of your shoulders (slightly below the neck) across it.
Hold on to the bar using both arms at each side and lift it off the rack by first pushing with your legs and at the same time straightening your torso.

Step away from the rack and position your legs using a shoulder width medium stance with the toes slightly pointed out. Your back should be kept straight while performing this exercise. This will be your starting position.
Elevate the barbell overhead by fully extending your arms while breathing out.
Hold the contraction for a second and lower the barbell back down to the starting position by inhaling.
Repeat for the recommended amount of repetitions.
exercises.name: Standing Barbell Press Behind Neck
exercises.name_pt: Desenvolvimento por Trás da Nuca com Barra
exercises.overview: The behind-neck press targets the shoulders from a different angle than traditional presses, emphasizing the posterior deltoids. However, it places the shoulder in a compromised position and requires exceptional mobility and control.
exercises.safetyInfo: This exercise requires excellent shoulder mobility and should be avoided by those with shoulder impingement or neck issues. Always perform adequate warm-up and consider this an advanced movement requiring proper progression.

commonMistakes: Swinging the upper arm during the movement
Using momentum instead of controlled muscle contraction
Not achieving full extension at the elbow
exerciseTips: Keep your upper arm parallel to the floor throughout
Squeeze the triceps at the top of the movement
Maintain a neutral spine position
Focus on moving only the forearm
instructions: With a dumbbell in one hand and the palm facing your torso, bend your knees slightly and bring your torso forward, by bending at the waist, while keeping the back straight until it is almost parallel to the floor. Make sure that you keep the head up.

The upper arm should be close to the torso and parallel to the floor while the forearm is pointing towards the floor as the hand holds the weight. Tip: There should be a 90-degree angle between the forearm and the upper arm. This is your starting position.

Keeping the upper arms stationary, use the triceps to lift the weights as you exhale until the forearms are parallel to the floor and the whole arm is extended. Like many other arm exercises, only the forearm moves.
After a second contraction at the top, slowly lower the dumbbell back to the starting position as you inhale.
Repeat the movement for the prescribed amount of repetitions.
Switch arms and repeat the exercise.
exercises.name: Standing Bent-Over One-Arm Dumbbell Triceps Extension
exercises.name_pt: Tríceps Coice Unilateral em Pé Curvado com Halter
exercises.overview: This unilateral triceps exercise allows for focused muscle development while challenging core stability. The bent-over position provides a unique angle for triceps activation and helps identify strength imbalances between arms.
exercises.safetyInfo: Maintain proper spinal alignment to avoid lower back strain. Use appropriate weight that allows full range of motion without compromising form.

commonMistakes: Allowing upper arms to drop during extension
Using momentum to swing weights up
Rounding the back excessively
exerciseTips: Keep upper arms parallel to floor throughout movement
Squeeze triceps hard at the top position
Control the descent slowly for 2-3 seconds
Maintain neutral spine throughout the exercise
instructions: With a dumbbell in each hand and the palms facing your torso, bend your knees slightly and bring your torso forward, by bending at the waist, while keeping the back straight until it is almost parallel to the floor. Make sure that you keep the head up. The upper arms should be close to the torso and parallel to the floor while the forearms are pointing towards the floor as the hands hold the weights. Tip: There should be a 90-degree angle between the forearms and the upper arm. This is your starting position.

Keeping the upper arms stationary, use the triceps to lift the weights as you exhale until the forearms are parallel to the floor and the whole arms are extended. Like many other arm exercises, only the forearm moves.
After a second contraction at the top, slowly lower the dumbbells back to their starting position as you inhale.
Repeat the movement for the prescribed amount of repetitions.
exercises.name: Standing Bent-Over Two-Arm Dumbbell Triceps Extension
exercises.name_pt: Tríceps Coice em Pé Curvado com Halteres
exercises.overview: This isolation exercise targets the triceps while in a bent-over position, providing a unique angle of resistance that emphasizes the triceps' role in elbow extension.
exercises.safetyInfo: Avoid this exercise if you have lower back issues. Keep knees slightly bent and core engaged to protect the spine.
variations: Bent Over Two-Dumbbell Row With Palms In

commonMistakes: Swinging the body to generate momentum
Allowing elbows to drift forward
Not controlling the eccentric portion
exerciseTips: Keep elbows pinned to your sides throughout
Squeeze biceps hard at the top for 1-2 seconds
Control the weight down slowly
Maintain upright posture with core engaged
instructions: Stand up with your torso upright while holding a cable curl bar that is attached to a low pulley. Grab the cable bar at shoulder width and keep the elbows close to the torso. The palm of your hands should be facing up (supinated grip). This will be your starting position.

While holding the upper arms stationary, curl the weights while contracting the biceps as you breathe out. Only the forearms should move. Continue the movement until your biceps are fully contracted and the bar is at shoulder level. Hold the contracted position for a second as you squeeze the muscle.
Slowly begin to bring the curl bar back to starting position as your breathe in.
Repeat for the recommended amount of repetitions.
exercises.name: Standing Biceps Cable Curl
exercises.name_pt: Rosca Direta em Pé na Polia
exercises.overview: A fundamental biceps isolation exercise using cable resistance that provides constant tension throughout the entire range of motion, promoting muscle growth and strength.
exercises.safetyInfo: Use a weight that allows full control throughout the range of motion. Avoid sudden jerking motions that could strain the biceps or lower back.

commonMistakes: Going too low behind the head
Using too heavy weight
Pressing with internally rotated shoulders
exerciseTips: Use lighter weight than regular overhead press
Keep the movement controlled and deliberate
Stop at ear level when lowering behind head
Maintain tight core throughout movement
instructions: Place a loaded bar at shoulder level in a rack. With a pronated grip at shoulder width, begin with the bar racked across the front of your shoulders. This is your starting position.
Initiate the lift by extending the elbows to press the bar overhead. Avoid locking out the elbow as you move the weight behind your head.
Lower the bar down to the back of the head until your elbow forms a right angle.
Lift the bar back over your head by extending the elbows
Lower the bar down to the starting position.
Alternate in this manner until you complete the recommended amount of repetitions.
exercises.name: Standing Bradford Press
exercises.overview: A challenging shoulder exercise that combines front and back pressing motions, targeting the deltoids through an extended range of motion while building shoulder stability.
exercises.safetyInfo: This exercise requires good shoulder mobility. Avoid if you have shoulder impingement or limited overhead mobility. Never lower the bar below ear level behind the head.

commonMistakes: Standing too close to the cable machine
Allowing shoulders to round forward
Using leg drive to assist the movement
exerciseTips: Step forward to increase pre-stretch
Focus on squeezing chest muscles at peak contraction
Keep slight bend in elbows at full extension
Maintain stable stance throughout movement
instructions: Position dual pulleys to chest height and select an appropriate weight. Stand a foot or two in front of the cables, holding one in each hand. You can stagger your stance for better stability.
Position the upper arm at a 90 degree angle with the shoulder blades together. This will be your starting position.
Keeping the rest of the body stationary, extend through the elbows to press the handles forward, drawing them together in front of you.
Pause at the top of the motion, and return to the starting position.
exercises.name: Standing Cable Chest Press
exercises.overview: A functional chest exercise that allows for natural movement patterns while providing constant tension through cable resistance, effectively targeting the pectoral muscles.
exercises.safetyInfo: Ensure proper foot positioning to maintain balance. Start with lighter weight to establish proper movement pattern and avoid shoulder strain.

commonMistakes: Using arms to pull instead of core rotation
Moving too quickly through the range of motion
Not engaging the core at the starting position
exerciseTips: Initiate movement from your core, not arms
Keep arms relatively straight throughout
Pivot on back foot during the lift
Control the return to starting position
instructions: Connect a standard handle on a tower, and move the cable to the lowest pulley position.

With your side to the cable, grab the handle with one hand and step away from the tower. You should be approximately arm's length away from the pulley, with the tension of the weight on the cable. Your outstretched arm should be aligned with the cable.
With your feet positioned shoulder width apart, squat down and grab the handle with both hands. Your arms should still be fully extended.
In one motion, pull the handle up and across your body until your arms are in a fully-extended position above your head.
Keep your back straight and your arms close to your body as you pivot your back foot and straighten your legs to get a full range of motion.
Retract your arms and then your body. Return to the neutral position in a slow and controlled manner.
Repeat to failure.
Then, reposition and repeat the same series of movements on the opposite side.
exercises.name: Standing Cable Lift
exercises.overview: A dynamic functional exercise that trains the core through rotational movement patterns, improving power transfer and stability for athletic activities.
exercises.safetyInfo: Start with lighter resistance to master the movement pattern. Keep knees slightly bent and avoid twisting beyond comfortable range of motion.

commonMistakes: Bending the arms during the movement
Using too much weight and losing control
Rotating only at the shoulders instead of the torso
exerciseTips: Keep your arms straight throughout the entire movement
Rotate from your core, not just your arms
Step away from the cable to maintain constant tension
Control the return to starting position
instructions: Connect a standard handle to a tower, and move the cable to the highest pulley position.

With your side to the cable, grab the handle with one hand and step away from the tower. You should be approximately arm's length away from the pulley, with the tension of the weight on the cable. Your outstretched arm should be aligned with the cable.
With your feet positioned shoulder width apart, reach upward with your other hand and grab the handle with both hands. Your arms should still be fully extended.
In one motion, pull the handle down and across your body to your front knee while rotating your torso.
Keep your back and arms straight and core tight while you pivot your back foot and bend your knees to get a full range of motion.
Maintain your stance and straight arms. Return to the neutral position in a slow and controlled manner.
Repeat to failure.
Then, reposition and repeat the same series of movements on the opposite side.
exercises.name: Standing Cable Wood Chop
exercises.name_pt: Wood Chop em Pé na Polia
exercises.overview: The standing cable wood chop is a dynamic core exercise that trains rotational strength and stability. It mimics real-world movement patterns while building functional core strength.
exercises.safetyInfo: Avoid this exercise if you have lower back issues. Start with lighter weight to master the rotation pattern before progressing.

commonMistakes: Bouncing at the bottom instead of controlled movement
Not achieving full range of motion at the top
Locking out the knees completely
exerciseTips: Rise up on your toes as high as possible
Pause at the top for maximum contraction
Lower slowly to get a full stretch
Keep your knees slightly bent throughout
instructions: Adjust the padded lever of the calf raise machine to fit your height.

Place your shoulders under the pads provided and position your toes facing forward (or using any of the two other positions described at the beginning of the chapter). The balls of your feet should be secured on top of the calf block with the heels extending off it. Push the lever up by extending your hips and knees until your torso is standing erect. The knees should be kept with a slight bend; never locked. Toes should be facing forward, outwards or inwards as described at the beginning of the chapter. This will be your starting position.

Raise your heels as you breathe out by extending your ankles as high as possible and flexing your calf. Ensure that the knee is kept stationary at all times. There should be no bending at any time. Hold the contracted position by a second before you start to go back down.
Go back slowly to the starting position as you breathe in by lowering your heels as you bend the ankles until calves are stretched.
Repeat for the recommended amount of repetitions.
exercises.name: Standing Calf Raises
exercises.overview: Standing calf raises target the gastrocnemius and soleus muscles for lower leg strength and development. This exercise is essential for athletic performance and aesthetic calf development.
exercises.safetyInfo: Use proper machine setup and avoid excessive weight that could cause Achilles tendon strain. Those with calf or Achilles injuries should use caution.
variations: Smith Machine Calf Raise Single Leg

commonMistakes: Swinging the upper arm during the movement
Using momentum instead of controlled lifting
Not achieving full range of motion at the bottom
exerciseTips: Keep your upper arm completely stationary
Squeeze your bicep hard at the top
Lower the weight slowly and controlled
Focus on the mind-muscle connection
instructions: Taking a dumbbell in your working hand, lean forward. Allow your working arm to hang perpendicular to the ground with the elbow pointing out. This will be your starting position.
Flex the elbow to curl the weight, keeping the upper arm stationary. At the top of the repetition, flex the biceps and pause.
Lower the dumbbell back to the starting position.
Repeat the movement for the prescribed amount of repetitions.
exercises.name: Standing Concentration Curl
exercises.overview: The standing concentration curl isolates the biceps through a focused, single-arm movement. It's excellent for developing bicep peak and improving mind-muscle connection.
exercises.safetyInfo: Use appropriate weight to maintain strict form. Avoid this exercise if you have elbow tendinitis or bicep injuries.

commonMistakes: Using unstable platform or surface
Not elevating heels enough for full range
Rushing through the movement without control
exerciseTips: Use a stable platform for elevation
Hold dumbbells firmly at your sides
Rise up as high as possible on your toes
Control the descent for maximum muscle engagement
instructions: Stand with your torso upright holding two dumbbells in your hands by your sides. Place the ball of the foot on a sturdy and stable wooden board (that is around 2-3 inches tall) while your heels extend off and touch the floor. This will be your starting position.

With the toes pointing either straight (to hit all parts equally), inwards (for emphasis on the outer head) or outwards (for emphasis on the inner head), raise the heels off the floor as you exhale by contracting the calves. Hold the top contraction for a second.
As you inhale, go back to the starting position by slowly lowering the heels.
Repeat for the recommended amount of times.
exercises.name: Standing Dumbbell Calf Raise
exercises.overview: The standing dumbbell calf raise provides targeted calf development using free weights. It allows for greater range of motion when performed on an elevated surface.
exercises.safetyInfo: Ensure stable platform setup to prevent falls. Start with lighter weights to establish balance and control before progressing.
variations: Smith Machine Calf Raise Single Leg

commonMistakes: Arching the back excessively
Pressing the weights forward instead of straight up
Using leg drive when trying to isolate shoulders
exerciseTips: Keep your core tight throughout the movement
Press the weights straight up overhead
Maintain neutral spine position
Control the descent back to starting position
instructions: Standing with your feet shoulder width apart, take a dumbbell in each hand. Raise the dumbbells to head height, the elbows out and about 90 degrees. This will be your starting position.
Maintaining strict technique with no leg drive or leaning back, extend through the elbow to raise the weights together directly above your head.
Pause, and slowly return the weight to the starting position.
exercises.name: Standing Dumbbell Press
exercises.overview: The standing dumbbell press is a fundamental upper body exercise that builds shoulder strength and stability. It requires core engagement and promotes functional pressing strength.
exercises.safetyInfo: Avoid this exercise with shoulder impingement or rotator cuff injuries. Ensure proper warm-up before heavy overhead pressing.

commonMistakes: Allowing elbows to drift forward during the curl
Using too much weight and compromising form
Rushing through the eccentric portion
exerciseTips: Maintain overhand grip throughout the movement
Keep your elbows stationary at your sides
Focus on controlled lifting and lowering
Squeeze at the top of the movement
instructions: To begin, stand straight with a dumbbell in each hand using a pronated grip (palms facing down). Your arms should be fully extended while your feet are shoulder width apart from each other. This is the starting position.

While holding the upper arms stationary, curl the weights while contracting the biceps as you breathe out. Only the forearms should move. Continue the movement until your biceps are fully contracted and the dumbbells are at shoulder level. Hold the contracted position for a second as you squeeze the muscle.
Slowly begin to bring the dumbbells back to starting position as your breathe in.
Repeat for the recommended amount of repetitions.
exercises.name: Standing Dumbbell Reverse Curl
exercises.name_pt: Rosca Inversa em Pé com Halteres
exercises.overview: The standing dumbbell reverse curl targets the brachioradialis and builds forearm strength using an overhand grip. It's excellent for developing balanced arm strength and grip.
exercises.safetyInfo: Start with lighter weight than regular curls as this variation is more challenging. Avoid if experiencing wrist or elbow pain.
variations: Seated One-Arm Dumbbell Palms-Down Wrist Curl

commonMistakes: Using momentum to swing weights up
Allowing excessive back arch
Bending elbows significantly during movement
exerciseTips: Keep arms straight with slight elbow bend throughout
Control the weight with slow eccentric lowering
Engage core to prevent back arching
Lead with thumbs pointing up at top
instructions: Hold the dumbbells in front of your thighs, palms facing your thighs.
Keep your arms straight with a slight bend at the elbows but keep them locked. This will be your starting position.
Raise the dumbbells in a semicircular motion to arm's length overhead as you exhale.
Slowly return to the starting position using the same path as you inhale.
Repeat for the recommended amount of repetitions.
exercises.name: Standing Dumbbell Straight-Arm Front Delt Raise Above Head
exercises.name_pt: Elevação Frontal em Pé com Halteres Acima da Cabeça
exercises.overview: This isolation exercise targets the front deltoids through a full range of motion ending overhead. It builds shoulder strength and mobility while challenging stability.
exercises.safetyInfo: Avoid if you have shoulder impingement or overhead mobility issues. Start with lighter weights as the overhead position increases difficulty significantly.

commonMistakes: Allowing elbows to flare out wide
Moving upper arms during the exercise
Lowering weight too quickly
exerciseTips: Keep upper arms stationary and perpendicular to floor
Maintain elbows close to head throughout movement
Control descent to protect elbow joint
Squeeze triceps at top of movement
instructions: To begin, stand up with a dumbbell held by both hands. Your feet should be about shoulder width apart from each other. Slowly use both hands to grab the dumbbell and lift it over your head until both arms are fully extended.
The resistance should be resting in the palms of your hands with your thumbs around it. The palm of the hands should be facing up towards the ceiling. This will be your starting position.

Keeping your upper arms close to your head with elbows in and perpendicular to the floor, lower the resistance in a semicircular motion behind your head until your forearms touch your biceps. Tip: The upper arms should remain stationary and only the forearms should move. Breathe in as you perform this step.
Go back to the starting position by using the triceps to raise the dumbbell. Breathe out as you perform this step.
Repeat for the recommended amount of repetitions.
exercises.name: Standing Dumbbell Triceps Extension
exercises.name_pt: Extensão de Tríceps em Pé com Halteres
exercises.overview: This isolation exercise effectively targets all three heads of the triceps using overhead positioning. It's excellent for building triceps mass and strength.
exercises.safetyInfo: Use caution with heavy weights overhead. Avoid if you have elbow or shoulder injuries. Ensure secure grip to prevent dropping weight.

commonMistakes: Raising hands above shoulder level
Using too wide of a grip
Allowing dumbbells to drift away from body
exerciseTips: Lead with elbows higher than hands
Keep dumbbells close to body throughout
Pause briefly at top of movement
Control the negative portion slowly
instructions: Grasp a dumbbell in each hand with a pronated (palms forward) grip that is slightly less than shoulder width. The dumbbells should be resting on top of your thighs. Your arms should be extended with a slight bend at the elbows and your back should be straight. This will be your starting position.

Use your side shoulders to lift the dumbbells as you exhale. The dumbbells should be close to the body as you move it up and the elbows should drive the motion. Continue to lift them until they nearly touch your chin. Tip: Your elbows should drive the motion. As you lift the dumbbells, your elbows should always be higher than your forearms. Also, keep your torso stationary and pause for a second at the top of the movement.
Lower the dumbbells back down slowly to the starting position. Inhale as you perform this portion of the movement.
Repeat for the recommended amount of repetitions.
exercises.name: Standing Dumbbell Upright Row
exercises.name_pt: Remada Alta em Pé com Halteres
exercises.overview: This compound movement targets the upper traps and lateral deltoids while involving the biceps. It's effective for building upper body pulling strength and trap development.
exercises.safetyInfo: Avoid raising hands above shoulder height to prevent shoulder impingement. Stop if you experience shoulder pain. Not recommended for those with shoulder impingement syndrome.

commonMistakes: Using too much weight and compensating with back
Completely locking out elbows
Allowing bar to drift forward
exerciseTips: Maintain slight bend in elbows throughout
Keep core engaged to prevent back extension
Control both lifting and lowering phases
Use closer than shoulder-width grip
instructions: To begin, stand straight with a barbell in your hands. You should grip the bar with palms facing down and a closer than shoulder width grip apart from each other.
Your feet should be shoulder width apart from each other. Your elbows should be slightly bent. This is the starting position.
Lift the barbell up until it is directly over your head while exhaling. Make sure to keep your elbows slightly bent when performing each repetition.
Once you feel the contraction, begin to lower the barbell back down to the starting position as you inhale.
Repeat for the recommended amount of repetitions.
exercises.name: Standing Front Barbell Raise Over Head
exercises.name_pt: Elevação Frontal com Barra Acima da Cabeça
exercises.overview: This isolation exercise targets the front deltoids through a full overhead range of motion using a barbell. It challenges stability and builds functional shoulder strength.
exercises.safetyInfo: Start with light weight to assess shoulder mobility overhead. Avoid if you have shoulder impingement or limited overhead range of motion.

commonMistakes: Swinging the weights using momentum
Moving elbows away from the torso
Rushing the wrist rotation movement
exerciseTips: Keep elbows stationary against your sides
Rotate wrists smoothly from neutral to supinated
Control the negative portion slowly
Squeeze biceps at the top of each rep
instructions: Stand up with a dumbbell in each hand being held at arms length. The elbows should be close to the torso. Your legs should be at about shoulder's width apart from each other.
Rotate the palms of the hands so that they are facing inward in a neutral position. This will be your starting position.

While holding the upper arms stationary, curl the weights out while contracting the biceps as you breathe out. Your wrist should turn so that when the weights are fully elevated you have supinated grip (palms facing up).
Only the forearms should move. Continue the movement until your biceps are fully contracted and the dumbbells are at shoulder level. Tip: Keep the forearms aligned with your outer deltoids.
Hold the contracted position for a second as you squeeze the biceps.
Slowly begin to bring the dumbbells back to the starting position as your breathe in. Remember to rotate the wrists as you lower the weight in order to switch back to a neutral grip.
Repeat for the recommended amount of repetitions.
exercises.name: Standing Inner-Biceps Curl
exercises.name_pt: Rosca para Bíceps Interno em Pé
exercises.overview: This bicep curl variation emphasizes the inner portion of the biceps through wrist rotation and specific arm positioning. The movement pattern enhances both strength and muscle definition.
exercises.safetyInfo: Start with lighter weights to master the rotation pattern. Avoid this exercise if you have wrist or elbow injuries that are aggravated by rotation.

commonMistakes: Lifting the thigh off the support pad
Using momentum to swing the weight up
Not achieving full range of motion at the top
exerciseTips: Keep hips pressed against the pad throughout
Curl heel toward glute in smooth arc
Control the eccentric lowering phase
Point toes straight ahead
instructions: Adjust the machine lever to fit your height and lie with your torso bent at the waist facing forward around 30-45 degrees (since an angled position is more favorable for hamstrings recruitment) with the pad of the lever on the back of your right leg (just a few inches under the calves) and the front of the right leg on top of the machine pad.
Keeping the torso bent forward, ensure your leg is fully stretched and grab the side handles of the machine. Position your toes straight. This will be your starting position.
As you exhale, curl your right leg up as far as possible without lifting the upper leg from the pad. Once you hit the fully contracted position, hold it for a second.
As you inhale, bring the legs back to the initial position. Repeat for the recommended amount of repetitions.
Perform the same exercise now for the left leg.
exercises.name: Standing Leg Curl
exercises.overview: This isolation exercise specifically targets the hamstring muscles using a machine for support and resistance. It's excellent for building hamstring strength and addressing muscle imbalances.
exercises.safetyInfo: Adjust machine properly to fit your height. Those with hamstring injuries should start with very light resistance and progress gradually.

commonMistakes: Raising the arm too high above shoulder level
Using momentum instead of controlled movement
Allowing the shoulder to roll forward
exerciseTips: Keep your elbow slightly bent throughout the movement
Lead with your pinky finger at the top
Control the weight on the way down
Stop at shoulder height to avoid impingement
instructions: Start by standing to the right side of a low pulley row. Use your left hand to come across the body and grab a single handle attached to the low pulley with a pronated grip (palms facing down). Rest your arm in front of you. Your right hand should grab the machine for better support and balance.
Make sure that your back is erect and your feet are shoulder width apart from each other. This is the starting position.
Begin to use the left hand and come across your body out until it is elevated to shoulder height while exhaling.
Feel the contraction at the top for a second and begin to slowly lower the handle back down to the original starting position while inhaling.
Repeat for the recommended amount of repetitions.
Switch arms and repeat the exercise.
exercises.name: Standing Low-Pulley Deltoid Raise
exercises.name_pt: Elevação de Deltoide na Polia Baixa em Pé
exercises.overview: This cable exercise targets the lateral deltoid through a cross-body movement pattern, providing constant tension throughout the range of motion for improved shoulder development.
exercises.safetyInfo: Stop at shoulder height to prevent shoulder impingement. Those with shoulder injuries should consult a healthcare provider before performing.

commonMistakes: Allowing the upper arm to move during the exercise
Not getting full range of motion at the bottom
Using too much weight and compromising form
exerciseTips: Keep your upper arm vertical and stationary
Control the descent to stretch the triceps
Squeeze at the top for maximum contraction
Use your free hand to stabilize your elbow
instructions: Grab a single handle with your left arm next to the low pulley machine. Turn away from the machine keeping the handle to the side of your body with your arm fully extended. Now use both hands to elevate the single handle directly above the head with the palm facing forward. Keep your upper arm completely vertical (perpendicular to the floor) and put your right hand on your left elbow to help keep it steady. This is the starting position.

Keeping your upper arms close to your head (elbows in) and perpendicular to the floor, lower the resistance in a semicircular motion behind your head until your forearms touch your biceps. Tip: The upper arms should remain stationary and only the forearms should move. Breathe in as you perform this step.
Go back to the starting position by using the triceps to raise the single handle. Breathe out as you perform this step.
Repeat for the recommended amount of repetitions.
Switch arms and repeat the exercise.
exercises.name: Standing Low-Pulley One-Arm Triceps Extension
exercises.name_pt: Extensão de Tríceps Unilateral na Polia Baixa em Pé
exercises.overview: This overhead cable exercise effectively targets all three heads of the triceps with constant tension, particularly emphasizing the long head through the overhead position.
exercises.safetyInfo: Keep movements controlled to avoid strain on the elbow joint. Those with elbow issues should use lighter weight or avoid overhead positions.

commonMistakes: Arching the back excessively
Pressing the bar too far forward
Not engaging the core for stability
exerciseTips: Keep your core tight and glutes engaged
Press the bar in a straight line over your head
Drive through your heels for stability
Keep your wrists straight and elbows under the bar
instructions: Start by placing a barbell that is about chest high on a squat rack. Once you have selected the weights, grab the barbell using a pronated (palms facing forward) grip. Make sure to grip the bar wider than shoulder width apart from each other.
Slightly bend the knees and place the barbell on your collar bone. Lift the barbell up keeping it lying on your chest. Take a step back and position your feet shoulder width apart from each other.

Once you pick up the barbell with the correct grip length, lift the bar up over your head by locking your arms. Hold at about shoulder level and slightly in front of your head. This is your starting position.
Lower the bar down to the collarbone slowly as you inhale.
Lift the bar back up to the starting position as you exhale.
Repeat for the recommended amount of repetitions.
exercises.name: Standing Military Press
exercises.overview: The standing military press is a fundamental compound movement that builds overhead pressing strength and develops the anterior and lateral deltoids while challenging core stability.
exercises.safetyInfo: Maintain proper spinal alignment to avoid lower back injury. Those with shoulder impingement or lower back issues should use lighter weight or seated variations.

commonMistakes: Using the palm instead of fingers to grip
Holding breath during the exercise
Starting with plates that are too heavy
exerciseTips: Focus on squeezing with your fingertips
Keep your shoulders back and down
Breathe normally throughout the hold
Gradually increase hold time or weight
instructions: To begin, stand straight while holding a weight plate by the ridge at arm's length in each hand using a neutral grip (palms facing in). You feet should be shoulder width apart from each other. This will be your starting position.
Lower the plates until the fingers are nearly extended but can still hold weights. Inhale as you lower the plates.
Now raise the plates back to the starting position as you exhale by closing your hands.
Repeat for the recommended amount of repetitions prescribed in your program.
exercises.name: Standing Olympic Plate Hand Squeeze
exercises.name_pt: Pinça com Anilha Olímpica em Pé
exercises.overview: This grip strengthening exercise develops finger and forearm strength through isometric contraction, improving overall grip strength and hand endurance.
exercises.safetyInfo: Start with lighter plates to avoid finger or forearm strain. Those with arthritis or hand injuries should consult a healthcare provider.
variations: Seated One-Arm Dumbbell Palms-Down Wrist Curl

commonMistakes: Swinging the weight up using momentum
Moving the upper arm during the curl
Not controlling the eccentric portion of the lift
exerciseTips: Keep your upper arm stationary at your side
Control both the lifting and lowering phases
Squeeze your bicep at the top of the movement
Use your free hand on your hip for stability
instructions: Start out by grabbing single handle next to the low pulley machine. Make sure you are far enough from the machine so that your arm is supporting the weight.

Make sure that your upper arm is stationary, perpendicular to the floor with elbows in and palms facing forward. Your non lifting arm should be grabbing your waist. This will allow you to keep your balance.
Slowly begin to curl the single handle upwards while keeping the upper arm stationary until your forearm touches your bicep while exhaling. Tip: Only the forearm should move.
Hold the contraction position as you squeeze the bicep and then lower the single handle back down to the starting position as you inhale.
Repeat for the recommended amount of repetitions.
Switch arms while performing this exercise.
exercises.name: Standing One-Arm Cable Curl
exercises.name_pt: Rosca Unilateral em Pé na Polia
exercises.overview: This unilateral cable exercise isolates the biceps while providing constant tension throughout the range of motion, helping to address strength imbalances between arms.
exercises.safetyInfo: Use controlled movements to avoid bicep or elbow strain. Start with lighter weight to establish proper form before progressing.

commonMistakes: Using momentum to swing the weight up
Allowing the elbow to drift away from the bench
Releasing tension at the bottom of the movement
exerciseTips: Press chest firmly against incline bench for stability
Keep upper arm stationary throughout the movement
Control the negative portion slowly
Squeeze biceps at the top of each rep
instructions: Stand on the back side of an incline bench as if you were going to be a spotter for someone. Have a dumbbell in one hand and rest it across the incline bench with a supinated (palms up) grip.

Position your non lifting hand at the corner or side of the incline bench. The chest should be pressed against the top part of the incline and your feet should be pressed against the floor at a wide stance. This is the starting position.

While holding the upper arm stationary, curl the dumbbell upward while contracting the biceps as you breathe out. Only the forearms should move. Continue the movement until your biceps are fully contracted and the dumbbell is at shoulder level. Hold the contracted position for a second.
Slowly begin to bring the dumbbells back to starting position as your breathe in.
Repeat for the recommended amount of repetitions.
Switch arms while performing this exercise.
exercises.name: Standing One-Arm Dumbbell Curl Over Incline Bench
exercises.name_pt: Rosca Unilateral com Halter no Banco Inclinado
exercises.overview: This exercise provides strict bicep isolation by using the incline bench for support, eliminating momentum and forcing proper form. It's excellent for developing peak bicep contraction and addressing strength imbalances between arms.
exercises.safetyInfo: Avoid using excessive weight that compromises form. Those with shoulder impingement should be cautious with the overhead arm position.

commonMistakes: Allowing elbow to flare outward
Using too much weight and losing control
Arching the back excessively
exerciseTips: Keep elbow pointed straight up throughout the movement
Maintain neutral spine and engage core
Control the weight behind your head slowly
Focus on full tricep stretch at the bottom
instructions: To begin, stand up with a dumbbell held in one hand. Your feet should be about shoulder width apart from each other. Now fully extend the arm with the dumbbell over your head. Tip: The small finger of your hand should be facing the ceiling and the palm of your hand should be facing forward. The dumbbell should be above your head.
This will be your starting position.

Keeping your upper arm close to your head (elbows in) and perpendicular to the floor, lower the resistance in a semicircular motion behind your head until your forearm touch your bicep. Tip: The upper arm should remain stationary and only the forearm should move. Breathe in as you perform this step.
Go back to the starting position by using the triceps to raise the dumbbell. Breathe out as you perform this step.
Repeat for the recommended amount of repetitions.
Switch arms and repeat the exercise.
exercises.name: Standing One-Arm Dumbbell Triceps Extension
exercises.name_pt: Extensão de Tríceps Unilateral em Pé com Halter
exercises.overview: This unilateral overhead tricep exercise effectively targets all three heads of the triceps while challenging core stability. It's particularly effective for the long head due to the overhead arm position.
exercises.safetyInfo: Start with lighter weight to establish proper form. Those with shoulder mobility issues should avoid this exercise or use a seated variation.

commonMistakes: Allowing elbows to flare wide
Using momentum to press the weight up
Hyperextending the lower back under load
exerciseTips: Keep elbows close to your head throughout
Maintain tight core to prevent back arch
Lower the weight slowly for maximum muscle tension
Use full range of motion for complete tricep stretch
instructions: To begin, stand up holding a barbell or e-z bar using a pronated grip (palms facing forward) with your hands closer than shoulder width apart from each other. Your feet should be about shoulder width apart.
Now elevate the barbell above your head until your arms are fully extended. Keep your elbows in. This will be your starting position.

Keeping your upper arms close to your head and elbows in, perpendicular to the floor, lower the resistance in a semicircular motion behind your head until your forearms touch your biceps. Tip: The upper arms should remain stationary and only the forearms should move. Breathe in as you perform this step.
Go back to the starting position by using the triceps to raise the barbell. Breathe out as you perform this step.
Repeat for the recommended amount of repetitions.
exercises.name: Standing Overhead Barbell Triceps Extension
exercises.name_pt: Extensão de Tríceps em Pé com Barra Acima da Cabeça
exercises.overview: This compound tricep movement allows for heavier loading than single-arm variations while emphasizing the long head of the triceps. The standing position also engages core stabilizers.
exercises.safetyInfo: Use a spotter when possible and start with manageable weight. Avoid if you have shoulder impingement or lower back issues.

commonMistakes: Leaning away from the working arm
Pressing the weight out instead of straight up
Using the non-working arm to generate momentum
exerciseTips: Maintain neutral grip throughout the entire movement
Keep core engaged for stability
Press in a straight line overhead
Control the descent to maintain tension
instructions: Start by having a dumbbell in one hand with your arm fully extended to the side using a neutral grip. Use your other arm to hold on to an incline bench to keep your balance.

Your feet should be shoulder width apart from each other. Now slowly lift the dumbbell up until you create a 90 degree angle with your arm. Note: Your forearm should be perpendicular to the floor. Continue to maintain a neutral grip throughout the entire exercise.
Slowly lift the dumbbell up until your arm is fully extended. This the starting position.
While inhaling lower the weight down until your arm is at a 90 degree angle again.

Feel the contraction for a second and then lift the weight back up towards the starting position while exhaling. Remember to hold on to the incline bench and keep your feet positioned to keep balance during the exercise.
Repeat for the recommended amount of repetitions.
Switch arms and repeat the exercise.
exercises.name: Standing Palm-In One-Arm Dumbbell Press
exercises.name_pt: Desenvolvimento Unilateral em Pé com Halter - Pegada Neutra
exercises.overview: This unilateral shoulder press variation uses a neutral grip to reduce shoulder impingement risk while challenging core stability. It's excellent for addressing strength imbalances and improving functional pressing strength.
exercises.safetyInfo: The neutral grip reduces shoulder stress compared to pronated grip. Ensure proper warm-up and avoid if experiencing shoulder pain.

commonMistakes: Allowing one arm to lag behind the other
Pressing dumbbells together at the top
Using leg drive to assist the press
exerciseTips: Keep both dumbbells moving in unison
Maintain neutral spine throughout the movement
Press straight up without letting dumbbells drift forward
Squeeze shoulder blades slightly for stability
instructions: Start by having a dumbbell in each hand with your arm fully extended to the side using a neutral grip. Your feet should be shoulder width apart from each other. Now slowly lift the dumbbells up until you create a 90 degree angle with your arms. Note: Your forearms should be perpendicular to the floor. This the starting position.
Continue to maintain a neutral grip throughout the entire exercise. Slowly lift the dumbbells up until your arms are fully extended.
While inhaling lower the weights down until your arm is at a 90 degree angle again.
Repeat for the recommended amount of repetitions.
exercises.name: Standing Palms-In Dumbbell Press
exercises.name_pt: Desenvolvimento em Pé com Halteres - Pegada Neutra
exercises.overview: This bilateral shoulder press variation uses a neutral grip to provide a joint-friendly pressing movement while developing balanced shoulder strength. The standing position enhances core engagement and functional strength.
exercises.safetyInfo: The neutral grip position is shoulder-friendly for most individuals. Start with lighter weights to master the coordination between both arms.

commonMistakes: Using momentum from the arms or shoulders
Gripping the bar too tightly
Moving the entire forearm instead of just the wrist
exerciseTips: Keep wrists as the only moving joint
Maintain consistent grip width throughout
Use controlled tempo both up and down
Focus on full range of motion
instructions: Start by standing straight and holding a barbell behind your glutes at arm's length while using a pronated grip (palms will be facing back away from the glutes) and having your hands shoulder width apart from each other.
You should be looking straight forward while your feet are shoulder width apart from each other. This is the starting position.
While exhaling, slowly elevate the barbell up by curling your wrist in a semi-circular motion towards the ceiling. Note: Your wrist should be the only body part moving for this exercise.
Hold the contraction for a second and lower the barbell back down to the starting position while inhaling.
Repeat for the recommended amount of repetitions.
When finished, lower the barbell down to the squat rack or the floor by bending the knees. Tip: It is easiest to either pick it up from a squat rack or have a partner hand it to you.
exercises.name: Standing Palms-Up Barbell Behind The Back Wrist Curl
exercises.name_pt: Rosca de Punho por Trás do Corpo com Barra - Palmas para Cima
exercises.overview: This exercise specifically targets the wrist flexors from a unique behind-the-back position, providing a different angle of resistance. It's beneficial for grip strength and forearm development.
exercises.safetyInfo: Start with very light weight as the wrist flexors are relatively small muscles. Avoid this exercise if you have carpal tunnel syndrome or wrist injuries.
variations: Seated One-Arm Dumbbell Palms-Down Wrist Curl

commonMistakes: Using too much weight and compensating with arm pull
Not achieving full spinal flexion
Rushing through the movement without control
exerciseTips: Keep elbows close to your body throughout the movement
Focus on flexing your spine rather than just pulling with arms
Squeeze your abs at the bottom of the movement
Control the weight on the way up
instructions: Attach a rope to a high pulley and select an appropriate weight.
Stand with your back to the cable tower. Take the rope with both hands over your shoulders, holding it to your upper chest. This will be your starting position.
Perform the movement by flexing the spine, crunching the weight down as far as you can.
Hold the peak contraction for a moment before returning to the starting position.
exercises.name: Standing Rope Crunch
exercises.name_pt: Abdominal em Pé na Polia com Corda
exercises.overview: An effective cable exercise that targets the rectus abdominis through spinal flexion. Provides constant tension throughout the range of motion.
exercises.safetyInfo: Start with light weight to master the movement pattern. Avoid if you have lower back issues or herniated discs.

commonMistakes: Allowing elbows to flare out to the sides
Moving upper arms during the exercise
Using inconsistent resistance from partner
exerciseTips: Keep upper arms stationary and perpendicular to floor
Maintain elbow position close to your head
Control the resistance from your partner
Focus on full range of motion
instructions: To begin, stand up with both arms fully extended above the head holding one end of a towel with both hands. Your elbows should be in and the arms perpendicular to the floor with the palms facing each other while your feet should be shoulder width apart from each other. This is the starting position.

Now communicate with your partner so that he/she can grip the other side of the towel to apply resistance. Keeping your upper arms close to your head (elbows in) and perpendicular to the floor, lower the resistance in a semicircular motion behind your head until your forearms touch your biceps. Tip: The upper arms should remain stationary and only the forearms should move. Breathe in as you perform this step.
Go back to the starting position by using the triceps to raise the towel. Breathe out as you perform this step.
Repeat for the recommended amount of repetitions.
exercises.name: Standing Towel Triceps Extension
exercises.name_pt: Extensão de Tríceps em Pé com Toalha
exercises.overview: A partner-assisted triceps exercise using a towel for resistance. Provides variable resistance and promotes communication between training partners.
exercises.safetyInfo: Communicate clearly with your partner about resistance level. Stop if you feel any shoulder or elbow discomfort.

commonMistakes: Pushing off with the trailing leg
Leaning forward excessively
Not fully extending the hip at the top
exerciseTips: Drive through your heel when stepping up
Bring your knee as high as possible during the raise
Control the descent slowly
Keep your torso upright throughout
instructions: Stand facing a box or bench of an appropriate height with your feet together. This will be your starting position.

Begin the movement by stepping up, putting your left foot on the top of the bench. Extend through the hip and knee of your front leg to stand up on the box. As you stand on the box with your left leg, flex your right knee and hip, bringing your knee as high as you can.
Reverse this motion to step down off the box, and then repeat the sequence on the opposite leg.
exercises.name: Step-up with Knee Raise
exercises.name_pt: Subida no Banco com Elevação de Joelho
exercises.overview: Step-ups with knee raise are unilateral exercises that build glute strength, improve balance, and enhance functional movement patterns.
exercises.safetyInfo: Ensure the box height allows your thigh to be parallel to the ground when stepping up. Avoid if you have knee instability.

commonMistakes: Rounding the lower back during descent
Bending knees excessively
Going too low and losing spinal position
exerciseTips: Initiate movement by pushing hips back
Keep the barbell path over your feet
Maintain a neutral spine throughout
Stop when you feel a stretch in your hamstrings
instructions: This exercise is best performed inside a squat rack for safety purposes. To begin, first set the bar on a rack that best matches your height. Once the correct height is chosen and the bar is loaded, step under the bar and place the back of your shoulders (slightly below the neck) across it.
Hold on to the bar using both arms at each side and lift it off the rack by first pushing with your legs and at the same time straightening your torso.

Step away from the rack and position your legs using a shoulder width medium stance. Keep your head up at all times as looking down will get you off balance and also maintain a straight back. This will be your starting position.
Keeping your legs stationary, move your torso forward by bending at the hips while inhaling. Lower your torso until it is parallel with the floor.
Begin to raise the bar as you exhale by elevating your torso back to the starting position.
Repeat for the recommended amount of repetitions.
exercises.name: Stiff Leg Barbell Good Morning
exercises.name_pt: Good Morning com Pernas Estendidas com Barra
exercises.overview: The stiff leg barbell good morning strengthens the posterior chain, particularly the erector spinae, while improving hip hinge mechanics.
exercises.safetyInfo: Use light weight initially and progress gradually. Avoid if you have lower back injuries or limited hip mobility. Consider using a squat rack for safety.

commonMistakes: Allowing the bar to drift away from the body
Rounding the back instead of hinging at hips
Bending knees too much during the movement
exerciseTips: Keep the bar close to your legs throughout
Initiate by pushing hips back, not bending forward
Feel the stretch in your hamstrings at the bottom
Drive hips forward to return to standing
instructions: Grasp a bar using an overhand grip (palms facing down). You may need some wrist wraps if using a significant amount of weight.
Stand with your torso straight and your legs spaced using a shoulder width or narrower stance. The knees should be slightly bent. This is your starting position.

Keeping the knees stationary, lower the barbell to over the top of your feet by bending at the hips while keeping your back straight. Keep moving forward as if you were going to pick something from the floor until you feel a stretch on the hamstrings. Inhale as you perform this movement.
Start bringing your torso up straight again by extending your hips until you are back at the starting position. Exhale as you perform this movement.
Repeat for the recommended amount of repetitions.
exercises.name: Stiff-Legged Barbell Deadlift
exercises.overview: The stiff-legged barbell deadlift primarily targets the hamstrings and glutes while teaching proper hip hinge mechanics essential for athletic performance.
exercises.safetyInfo: Start with light weight to master the hip hinge pattern. Avoid if you have lower back issues or tight hamstrings without proper warm-up.

commonMistakes: Squatting down instead of hinging at hips
Holding dumbbells too far from the body
Not maintaining neutral spine alignment
exerciseTips: Keep dumbbells close to your legs during descent
Push your hips back to initiate the movement
Maintain a slight bend in your knees
Squeeze your glutes to return to starting position
instructions: Grasp a couple of dumbbells holding them by your side at arm's length.
Stand with your torso straight and your legs spaced using a shoulder width or narrower stance. The knees should be slightly bent. This is your starting position.

Keeping the knees stationary, lower the dumbbells to over the top of your feet by bending at the waist while keeping your back straight. Keep moving forward as if you were going to pick something from the floor until you feel a stretch on the hamstrings. Exhale as you perform this movement
Start bringing your torso up straight again by extending your hips and waist until you are back at the starting position. Inhale as you perform this movement.
Repeat for the recommended amount of repetitions.
exercises.name: Stiff-Legged Dumbbell Deadlift
exercises.overview: The stiff-legged dumbbell deadlift effectively targets the hamstrings and glutes while offering more freedom of movement than the barbell version.
exercises.safetyInfo: This exercise is more joint-friendly than the barbell version. Start with light weights and focus on form before progressing load.

commonMistakes: Using momentum to jerk the weight up
Not fully extending arms at the bottom
Allowing shoulders to roll forward at bottom position
exerciseTips: Retract shoulder blades before pulling the bar
Keep chest pressed against bench throughout movement
Pull bar to lower chest/upper abdomen area
Control the negative portion of the lift
instructions: Place a loaded barbell on the end of a bench. Standing on the bench behind the bar, take a medium, pronated grip. Stand with your hips back and chest up, maintaining a neutral spine. This will be your starting position.
Row the bar to your torso by retracting the shoulder blades and flexing the elbows. Use a controlled movement with no jerking.
After a brief pause, slowly return the bar to the starting position, ensuring to go all the way down.
exercises.name: Straight Bar Bench Mid Rows
exercises.overview: This chest-supported rowing variation eliminates lower back stress while effectively targeting the middle back muscles and improving posture.
exercises.safetyInfo: Ensure the bench can support your body weight plus the barbell load. Use a spotter if attempting heavy weights.
variations: Bent Over Two-Dumbbell Row With Palms In

commonMistakes: Lifting the weight too high above shoulder level
Using body momentum instead of shoulder muscles
Bending elbows during the movement
exerciseTips: Keep arms completely straight throughout movement
Lift bar only to shoulder height
Control the descent slowly
Engage core to maintain stability on bench
instructions: Place a bar on the ground behind the head of an incline bench.
Lay on the bench face down. With a pronated grip, pick the barbell up from the floor, keeping your arms straight. Allow the bar to hang straight down. This will be your starting position.
To begin, raise the barbell out in front of your head while keeping your arms extended.
Return to the starting position.
exercises.name: Straight Raises on Incline Bench
exercises.name_pt: Elevação com Braços Estendidos no Banco Inclinado
exercises.overview: This prone variation of front raises provides stability and isolates the anterior deltoids while minimizing compensatory movements from other muscle groups.
exercises.safetyInfo: Start with light weight to assess shoulder mobility and strength. Avoid if you have shoulder impingement or anterior shoulder pain.

commonMistakes: Bending elbows significantly during movement
Going too deep and straining shoulders
Using too heavy weight initially
exerciseTips: Keep arms straight but not locked throughout movement
Lower weight until you feel stretch in chest
Focus on arc-like movement pattern
Keep hips low and core engaged
instructions: Place a dumbbell standing up on a flat bench.

Ensuring that the dumbbell stays securely placed at the top of the bench, lie perpendicular to the bench (torso across it as in forming a cross) with only your shoulders lying on the surface. Hips should be below the bench and legs bent with feet firmly on the floor. The head will be off the bench as well.

Grasp the dumbbell with both hands and hold it straight over your chest at arms length. Both palms should be pressing against the underside one of the sides of the dumbbell. This will be your starting position.
Caution: Always ensure that the dumbbell used for this exercise is secure. Using a dumbbell with loose plates can result in the dumbbell falling apart and falling on your face.
While keeping your arms straight, lower the weight slowly in an arc behind your head while breathing in until you feel a stretch on the chest.
At that point, bring the dumbbell back to the starting position using the arc through which the weight was lowered and exhale as you perform this movement.
Hold the weight on the initial position for a second and repeat the motion for the prescribed number of repetitions.
exercises.name: Straight-Arm Dumbbell Pullover
exercises.name_pt: Pullover com Halter e Braços Estendidos
exercises.overview: This classic exercise targets the chest while providing a unique stretching component that can improve ribcage expansion and shoulder mobility.
exercises.safetyInfo: Ensure dumbbell plates are secure before starting. Use a spotter for heavy weights and stop if you experience shoulder pain or instability.

commonMistakes: Bending elbows too much turning it into a row
Using too much weight and losing form
Not maintaining forward torso lean
exerciseTips: Keep arms straight with slight elbow bend
Focus on pulling with lats rather than arms
Lean forward slightly at waist
Stop when hands reach thighs
instructions: You will start by grabbing the wide bar from the top pulley of a pulldown machine and using a wider than shoulder-width pronated (palms down) grip. Step backwards two feet or so.

Bend your torso forward at the waist by around 30-degrees with your arms fully extended in front of you and a slight bend at the elbows. If your arms are not fully extended then you need to step a bit more backwards until they are. Once your arms are fully extended and your torso is slightly bent at the waist, tighten the lats and then you are ready to begin.
While keeping the arms straight, pull the bar down by contracting the lats until your hands are next to the side of the thighs. Breathe out as you perform this step.
While keeping the arms straight, go back to the starting position while breathing in.
Repeat for the recommended amount of repetitions.
exercises.name: Straight-Arm Pulldown
