exercises.overview: This isolation exercise effectively targets the latissimus dorsi while teaching proper lat engagement, making it excellent for improving mind-muscle connection.
exercises.safetyInfo: Start with lighter weight to master the movement pattern. Avoid if you have lower back issues that prevent forward leaning position.

commonMistakes: Allowing the torso to lean away from or toward the weighted side
Hiking the shoulder up on the weighted side
Taking uneven or awkward steps to compensate for the weight
exerciseTips: Maintain level shoulders and avoid hiking the weighted shoulder up
Keep your core tight to prevent lateral bending
Walk with your normal gait pattern and stride length
Focus on keeping the weight close to your body throughout
instructions: Hold a dumbbell in one hand at your side as if carrying a suitcase.
Stand tall with your chest up, shoulders back, and core engaged.
Walk forward with normal stride length while maintaining perfect posture.
Keep the weight close to your body and resist any lateral lean or shoulder hiking.
Complete the desired distance before switching to the opposite hand.
exercises.name: Suitcase Carry
exercises.overview: The Suitcase Carry is a simple yet effective unilateral loading exercise that challenges core stability and grip strength. It mimics real-world carrying patterns while building functional strength and stability.
exercises.safetyInfo: This is a beginner-friendly exercise that's generally safe for most individuals. Use appropriate weight that allows you to maintain good posture throughout the carry.

commonMistakes: Allowing the hips to sag during the movement
Using momentum instead of controlled motion
Extending too far beyond your strength capacity
exerciseTips: Keep your core braced throughout the entire range of motion
Move slowly and under control to maximize tension
Maintain straight arms to focus stress on the core
Stop when you feel your lower back arch
instructions: Adjust the straps so the handles are at an appropriate height, below waist level.
Begin standing and grasping the handles. Lean into the straps, moving to an incline push-up position. This will be your starting position.
Keeping your arms straight, lean further into the suspension straps, bringing your body closer to the ground, allowing your shoulders to extend, raising your arms up and over your head.
Maintain a neutral spine and keep the rest of your body straight, your shoulders being the only joints allowed to move.
Pause during the peak contraction, and then return to the starting position.
exercises.name: Suspended Fallout
exercises.overview: An advanced core stability exercise using suspension straps that challenges anti-extension strength. Highly effective for building deep core stability and shoulder mobility simultaneously.
exercises.safetyInfo: High risk for lower back injury if performed with poor form. Start with shorter range of motion and progress gradually. Avoid if you have shoulder impingement.

commonMistakes: Allowing hips to sag or pike up
Not descending to full range of motion
Flaring elbows too wide causing shoulder stress
exerciseTips: Maintain a straight line from head to heels
Control the descent to maximize muscle tension
Keep your hands aligned under your shoulders
Adjust body angle to modify difficulty
instructions: Anchor your suspension straps securely to the top of a rack or other object.

Leaning into the straps, take a handle in each hand and move into a push-up plank position. You should be as close to parallel to the ground as you can manage with your arms fully extended, maintaining good posture.
Maintaining a straight, rigid torso, descend slowly by allowing the elbows to flex.
Continue until your elbows break 90 degrees, pausing before you extend to return to the starting position.
exercises.name: Suspended Push-Up
exercises.overview: A bodyweight pushing exercise using suspension straps that allows for variable resistance based on body angle. Excellent for building chest strength while improving core stability.
exercises.safetyInfo: Easier on wrists than floor push-ups due to neutral grip position. Ensure straps are securely anchored to prevent falls.

commonMistakes: Using momentum to swing legs up
Not maintaining plank position with upper body
Incomplete range of motion at the top
exerciseTips: Initiate movement by tilting your pelvis forward
Keep your upper body stable in plank position
Focus on bringing knees to chest rather than just lifting
Control the return to starting position
instructions: Secure a set of suspension straps with the handles hanging about a foot off of the ground. Move yourself into a pushup plank position facing away from the rack.
Place your feet into the handles. You should maintain a straight posture, not allowing the hips to sag. This will be your starting position.
Begin the movement by flexing the knees and hips, drawing the knees to your torso. As you do so, anteriorly tilt your pelvis, allowing your spine to flex.
At the top of the controlled motion, return to the starting position.
exercises.name: Suspended Reverse Crunch
exercises.overview: A suspension-based core exercise targeting lower abdominals through reverse flexion. Combines core strengthening with anti-extension stability training in a plank position.
exercises.safetyInfo: Requires good shoulder stability to maintain proper plank position. Start with bent knee variation before progressing to straight leg version.

commonMistakes: Allowing body to sag in the middle
Not pulling to full range of motion
Leading with the arms instead of retracting shoulder blades first
exerciseTips: Squeeze your shoulder blades together at the top
Keep your body in a straight line throughout
Pull your chest to your hands
Control the negative portion of the movement
instructions: Suspend your straps at around chest height. Take a handle in each hand and lean back. Keep your body erect and your head and chest up. Your arms should be fully extended. This will be your starting position.
Begin by flexing the elbow to initiate the movement. Protract your shoulder blades as you do so.
At the completion of the motion pause, and then return to the starting position.
exercises.name: Suspended Row
exercises.overview: A horizontal pulling exercise using suspension straps that targets the upper back and rear delts. Excellent for countering forward posture and building pulling strength.
exercises.safetyInfo: Low risk exercise suitable for most fitness levels. Adjust body angle to modify difficulty - more upright is easier, more horizontal is harder.
variations: Bent Over Two-Dumbbell Row With Palms In

commonMistakes: Putting too much weight on the rear foot
Leaning forward excessively during descent
Not descending to adequate depth
exerciseTips: Keep most of your weight on your front leg
Descend straight down rather than forward
Drive through your front heel to return to start
Maintain upright torso throughout the movement
instructions: Suspend your straps so the handles are 18-30 inches from the floor.
Facing away from the setup, place your rear foot into the handle behind you. Keep your head looking forward and your chest up, with your knee slightly bent. This will be your starting position.
Descend by flexing the knee and hips, lowering yourself to the ground. Keep your weight on the heel of your foot and maintain your posture throughout the exercise.
At the bottom of the movement, reverse the motion, extending through the hip and knee to return to the starting position.
exercises.name: Suspended Split Squat
exercises.overview: A unilateral leg exercise using suspension straps that challenges single-leg strength and stability. Highly effective for correcting muscle imbalances and building functional leg strength.
exercises.safetyInfo: May cause knee discomfort if rear foot is positioned too high. Start with lower suspension height and adjust as needed for comfort.

commonMistakes: Allowing the plates to separate during the movement
Pressing too quickly without maintaining the isometric contraction
Hunching shoulders forward instead of keeping them retracted
exerciseTips: Squeeze the plates together forcefully throughout the entire movement
Keep your shoulders down and back to maintain proper posture
Control the extension speed - take 2-3 seconds to press out
Maintain constant tension by never relaxing the squeeze
instructions: Begin in a standing position.

Press two lightweight plates together with your hands. Hold the plates together close to your chest to create an isometric contraction in your chest muscles. Your fingers should be pointed forward. This is your starting position.
Squeeze the plates between your palms and extend your arms directly out in front of you in a controlled motion.
Pause at the top of the motion, and then slowly return to the starting position.
exercises.name: Svend Press
exercises.overview: The Svend Press is an isometric chest exercise that combines a plate squeeze with a pressing motion. It's excellent for developing inner chest activation and mind-muscle connection.
exercises.safetyInfo: Use lightweight plates only (2.5-10 lbs typically) as the isometric contraction significantly increases difficulty. Avoid if you have wrist or shoulder impingement issues.

commonMistakes: Using the Swiss bar exactly like a regular barbell without adjusting technique
Neglecting to experiment with the various grip options available
exerciseTips: Keep your wrists straight and aligned with your forearms
Experiment with different grip widths to find optimal position
Maintain tight shoulder blade retraction throughout
Control the eccentric portion for maximum muscle development
instructions: Lie on the bench and grip the Swiss bar using the neutral grip handles.
Position your hands at your preferred width along the available grips.
Unrack the bar and position it over your chest with arms extended.
Lower the bar to your chest with control while keeping elbows at moderate angle.
Press the bar back to the starting position driving through your chest and triceps.
Re-rack the bar safely after completing your set.
exercises.name: Swiss Bar Press
exercises.overview: The Swiss bar press uses a specialty barbell with multiple neutral grip options, allowing for a more shoulder-friendly pressing angle. It reduces wrist and shoulder stress while still providing excellent chest development.
exercises.safetyInfo: The neutral grip position is generally easier on the wrists and shoulders compared to a pronated grip. Good option for those with shoulder or wrist discomfort during regular bench press.

commonMistakes: Rounding the back instead of maintaining neutral spine
Using momentum to jerk the weight up
Pulling too high toward the chest instead of the abdomen
exerciseTips: Keep your chest up and hips back throughout the movement
Pull the handle to your upper abdomen, not your chest
Squeeze your shoulder blades together at the top
Control the negative portion of the lift
instructions: Position a bar into a landmine or in a corner to keep it from moving. Load an appropriate weight onto your end.
Stand over the bar, and position a Double D row handle around the bar next to the collar. Using your hips and legs, rise to a standing position.
Assume a wide stance with your hips back and your chest up. Your arms should be extended. This will be your starting position.
Pull the weight to your upper abdomen by retracting the shoulder blades and flexing the elbows. Do not jerk the weight or cheat during the movement.
After a brief pause, return to the starting position.
exercises.name: T-Bar Row with Handle
exercises.overview: The T-Bar Row with Handle is a compound pulling exercise that targets the middle back muscles. It provides excellent lat and rhomboid development while allowing for heavy loading.
exercises.safetyInfo: Maintain proper hip hinge position to protect the lower back. Start with lighter weight to master the movement pattern before progressing.
variations: Bent Over Two-Dumbbell Row With Palms In

commonMistakes: Moving the upper arms instead of keeping them fixed
Dropping the weights too quickly on the eccentric
Pressing in a straight line instead of the semi-circular motion
exerciseTips: Keep your upper arms stationary throughout the movement
Move the dumbbells in a controlled semi-circular arc
Touch the weights to your upper chest lightly
Focus on the triceps contraction at the top
instructions: Lie down on a flat bench with a dumbbell in each hand on top of your thighs. The palms of your hand will be facing each other.

By using your thighs to help you get the dumbbells up, clean the dumbbells one arm at a time so that you can hold them in front of you at shoulder width. Note: when holding the dumbbells in front of you, make sure your arms are wider than shoulder width apart from each other using a pronated (palms forward) grip. Allow your elbows to point out. This is your starting position.

Keeping the upper arms stationary, slowly move the dumbbells in and down in a semi circular motion until they touch the upper chest while inhaling. Keep full control of the dumbbells at all times and do not move the upper arms nor rest the dumbbells on the chest.

As you breathe out, move the dumbbells up using your triceps and the same semi-circular motion but in reverse. Attempt to keep the dumbbells together as they move up. Lock your arms in the contracted position, hold for a second and then start coming down again slowly again. Tip: It should take at least twice as long to go down than to come up.
Repeat the movement for the prescribed amount of repetitions of your training program.
exercises.name: Tate Press
exercises.overview: The Tate Press is an advanced triceps isolation exercise that uses a unique semi-circular motion. It provides excellent triceps activation, particularly the long head.
exercises.safetyInfo: Use moderate weight as the unique angle puts stress on the elbow joint. Avoid if you have elbow problems or shoulder impingement.

commonMistakes: Speeding up as muscles fatigue
Not maintaining consistent timing
Sacrificing form for tempo
exerciseTips: Count the seconds to maintain consistent tempo
Focus on muscle tension throughout the entire range of motion
Keep your core tight during the slow movement
Maintain proper form even as muscles fatigue from the slow tempo
instructions: Start in a standard push-up position with hands shoulder-width apart.
Lower your body slowly over 3-4 seconds, maintaining perfect form.
Pause briefly at the bottom position with chest near the ground.
Press back up to the starting position over 2-3 seconds.
Maintain the controlled tempo for all repetitions.
exercises.name: Tempo Push Up
exercises.overview: Tempo push-ups use controlled timing to increase time under tension and improve form. This beginner-friendly variation builds strength and teaches proper movement patterns by slowing down each phase of the push-up.
exercises.safetyInfo: The slow tempo increases time under tension, which may cause faster fatigue. Start with shorter tempos and build up gradually. Excellent for beginners learning proper form.

commonMistakes: Inconsistent tempo timing between repetitions or sets
Losing control during the slow eccentric and dropping suddenly
Compromising squat form to accommodate the tempo requirements
exerciseTips: Count the tempo consistently on every repetition for training effect
Maintain constant tension throughout the extended eccentric phase
Focus on control rather than speed during the prescribed tempo portions
Keep proper squat mechanics despite the slower movement speed
instructions: Position barbell on upper traps with feet shoulder-width apart in standard squat setup.
Unrack the bar and step back, establishing proper squat stance and core tension.
Begin descent with a controlled 3-4 second eccentric lowering phase.
Reach bottom position maintaining tension, then pause briefly if prescribed.
Drive upward with normal or controlled speed depending on tempo prescription.
exercises.name: Tempo Squat
exercises.overview: Tempo squats use controlled timing on different phases of the movement, typically featuring slow eccentrics to increase time under tension. This intermediate technique builds control, strength, and muscle endurance.
exercises.safetyInfo: Extended time under tension increases metabolic stress and may cause more delayed onset muscle soreness. Reduce weight significantly from normal squat loads to accommodate tempo requirements.

commonMistakes: Using momentum to swing the legs apart
Leaning forward or allowing upper body movement
Not controlling the return phase of the movement
exerciseTips: Keep your upper body pressed against the back pad
Control both the opening and closing phases
Squeeze your glutes at the fully abducted position
Maintain steady breathing throughout the set
instructions: To begin, sit down on the abductor machine and select a weight you are comfortable with. When your legs are positioned properly, grip the handles on each side. Your entire upper body (from the waist up) should be stationary. This is the starting position.
Slowly press against the machine with your legs to move them away from each other while exhaling.

Feel the contraction for a second and begin to move your legs back to the starting position while breathing in. Note: Remember to keep your upper body stationary to prevent any injuries from occurring.
Repeat for the recommended amount of repetitions.
exercises.name: Thigh Abductor
exercises.overview: The Thigh Abductor machine targets the hip abductor muscles responsible for moving the legs away from the body's midline. It's essential for hip stability and athletic performance.
exercises.safetyInfo: Start with light weight to assess hip mobility. Avoid if you have hip impingement or recent hip injury.
variations: Side-Lying Hip Abduction With Mini Band

commonMistakes: Allowing the legs to snap back to starting position
Using upper body momentum to assist the movement
Not achieving full range of motion in the squeeze
exerciseTips: Keep your back pressed firmly against the pad
Squeeze your inner thighs together with control
Pause briefly at the fully contracted position
Resist the weight on the return to starting position
instructions: To begin, sit down on the adductor machine and select a weight you are comfortable with. When your legs are positioned properly on the leg pads of the machine, grip the handles on each side. Your entire upper body (from the waist up) should be stationary. This is the starting position.
Slowly press against the machine with your legs to move them towards each other while exhaling.

Feel the contraction for a second and begin to move your legs back to the starting position while breathing in. Note: Remember to keep your upper body stationary and avoid fast jerking motions in order to prevent any injuries from occurring.
Repeat for the recommended amount of repetitions.
exercises.name: Thigh Adductor
exercises.overview: The Thigh Adductor machine strengthens the inner thigh muscles responsible for bringing the legs toward the body's midline. It's important for hip stability and injury prevention.
exercises.safetyInfo: Use conservative weight initially as the adductors are often tight and prone to strain. Avoid if you have groin injuries or hip problems.

commonMistakes: Pressing the weights overhead without using leg drive momentum
Allowing knees to cave inward during the squat portion
exerciseTips: Use the momentum from your legs to help drive the weights overhead
Keep your core braced throughout the entire movement
Maintain an upright torso during the squat portion
Control the descent of both the squat and the weight lowering
instructions: Hold dumbbells at shoulder height with palms facing forward, feet shoulder-width apart.
Lower into a squat by pushing your hips back and bending your knees until thighs are parallel to the floor.
Keep your chest up and core engaged throughout the squat movement.
Drive through your heels to stand up explosively, using the momentum to press the dumbbells overhead.
Lock out your arms fully overhead, then lower the weights back to shoulder height.
Immediately begin the next repetition by lowering into the squat position.
exercises.name: Thruster
exercises.overview: The thruster combines a front squat with an overhead press in one continuous movement, making it an excellent full-body exercise. It builds functional strength, power, and cardiovascular endurance while targeting multiple muscle groups simultaneously.
exercises.safetyInfo: This exercise places significant stress on the knees, shoulders, and lower back. Those with shoulder impingement or knee problems should use lighter weights or avoid this exercise entirely.

commonMistakes: Allowing knees to cave inward during the lift
Rounding the back under heavy load
Not fully extending hips at the top of the movement
exerciseTips: Drive through your heels to initiate the lift
Keep chest up and shoulders back throughout
Maintain neutral spine position
Lower the weight under control to the ground
instructions: For this exercise load a trap bar, also known as a hex bar, to an appropriate weight resting on the ground. Stand in the center of the apparatus and grasp both handles.
Lower your hips, look forward with your head and keep your chest up.
Begin the movement by driving through the heels and extend your hips and knees. Avoid rounding your back at all times.
At the completion of the movement, lower the weight back to the ground under control.
exercises.name: Trap Bar Deadlift
exercises.overview: The trap bar deadlift is a compound movement that targets the quadriceps and posterior chain with a more upright torso position than conventional deadlifts. It's excellent for building functional strength and power.
exercises.safetyInfo: Master proper hip hinge mechanics before adding significant weight. Avoid with acute lower back injuries. Ensure proper warm-up before heavy lifting.

commonMistakes: Allowing shoulders to round forward during the carry
Taking steps that are too long or too rushed
Holding breath throughout the entire movement
exerciseTips: Keep your shoulders back and chest up throughout the walk
Maintain a strong grip on the handles without over-squeezing
Take controlled steps rather than rushing the movement
Keep your core engaged to maintain spinal stability
instructions: Load a trap bar with desired weight and step inside the frame.
Squat down and grip the handles with both hands, keeping your chest up and back straight.
Stand up by driving through your heels and fully extending your hips and knees.
Walk forward with controlled steps, maintaining an upright posture and tight grip.
Complete the desired distance or time, then carefully lower the weight back down.
exercises.name: Trap Bar Farmer's Walk
exercises.overview: The trap bar farmer's walk develops grip strength, core stability, and total-body strength through loaded carries. The trap bar design allows for heavier loading compared to traditional farmer's walk implements.
exercises.safetyInfo: This exercise is generally safe but requires proper lifting mechanics to pick up the weight. Ensure adequate space for walking and avoid carrying to complete grip failure.

commonMistakes: Swinging the weight using momentum
Moving the upper arm during the exercise
Not achieving full extension at the top
exerciseTips: Keep upper arms parallel to the floor throughout
Focus on squeezing triceps at full extension
Maintain bent-over position without moving torso
Control the weight on both lifting and lowering phases
instructions: Start with a dumbbell in each hand and your palms facing your torso. Keep your back straight with a slight bend in the knees and bend forward at the waist. Your torso should be almost parallel to the floor. Make sure to keep your head up. Your upper arms should be close to your torso and parallel to the floor. Your forearms should be pointed towards the floor as you hold the weights. There should be a 90-degree angle formed between your forearm and upper arm. This is your starting position.
Now, while keeping your upper arms stationary, exhale and use your triceps to lift the weights until the arm is fully extended. Focus on moving the forearm.
After a brief pause at the top contraction, inhale and slowly lower the dumbbells back down to the starting position.
Repeat the movement for the prescribed amount of repetitions.
exercises.name: Tricep Dumbbell Kickback
exercises.overview: The tricep dumbbell kickback is an isolation exercise that targets all three heads of the triceps through elbow extension. It's effective for building tricep strength and definition with controlled movement.
exercises.safetyInfo: Use appropriate weight to maintain proper form. Avoid if you have shoulder impingement or elbow issues. Support yourself properly in the bent-over position.

commonMistakes: Allowing elbows to flare out to the sides
Using momentum to swing the weight up
Arching the back excessively
exerciseTips: Keep elbows stationary and pointed forward
Lean slightly away from the machine for stability
Squeeze triceps at full extension
Control the negative portion slowly
instructions: Attach a rope to a low pulley. After selecting an appropriate weight, grasp the rope with both hands and face away from the cable.

Position your hands behind your head with your elbows point straight up. Your elbows should start out flexed, and you can stagger your stance and lean gently away from the machine to create greater stability. This will be your starting position.
To perform the movement, extend through the elbow while keeping the upper arm in position, raising your hands above your head.
Squeeze your triceps at the top of the movement, and slowly lower the weight back to the start position.
exercises.name: Triceps Overhead Extension with Rope
exercises.name_pt: Tríceps com Corda por Cima da Cabeça
exercises.overview: An isolation exercise that targets all three heads of the triceps with emphasis on the long head due to the overhead position. Provides excellent muscle stretch and contraction.
exercises.safetyInfo: Start with light weight to master the movement pattern. Avoid if you have shoulder impingement or elbow pain.

commonMistakes: Moving upper arms away from torso
Using too much weight and swinging body
Not fully extending arms at the bottom
exerciseTips: Keep upper arms pressed against your sides
Focus on squeezing triceps at the bottom
Maintain slight forward lean throughout
Use controlled tempo on both phases
instructions: Attach a straight or angled bar to a high pulley and grab with an overhand grip (palms facing down) at shoulder width.

Standing upright with the torso straight and a very small inclination forward, bring the upper arms close to your body and perpendicular to the floor. The forearms should be pointing up towards the pulley as they hold the bar. This is your starting position.

Using the triceps, bring the bar down until it touches the front of your thighs and the arms are fully extended perpendicular to the floor. The upper arms should always remain stationary next to your torso and only the forearms should move. Exhale as you perform this movement.
After a second hold at the contracted position, bring the bar slowly up to the starting point. Breathe in as you perform this step.
Repeat for the recommended amount of repetitions.
exercises.name: Triceps Pushdown
exercises.overview: A fundamental triceps isolation exercise using a straight bar attachment. Excellent for building tricep strength and definition with consistent resistance throughout the range of motion.
exercises.safetyInfo: Keep wrists straight and avoid locking elbows aggressively. Start with moderate weight to prevent elbow strain.

commonMistakes: Not separating rope ends at bottom of movement
Allowing elbows to drift forward
Using excessive weight causing form breakdown
exerciseTips: Pull rope ends apart at the bottom for maximum contraction
Keep elbows tucked close to your sides
Squeeze triceps hard at full extension
Control the return to starting position
instructions: Attach a rope attachment to a high pulley and grab with a neutral grip (palms facing each other).

Standing upright with the torso straight and a very small inclination forward, bring the upper arms close to your body and perpendicular to the floor. The forearms should be pointing up towards the pulley as they hold the rope with the palms facing each other. This is your starting position.

Using the triceps, bring the rope down as you bring each side of the rope to the side of your thighs. At the end of the movement the arms are fully extended and perpendicular to the floor. The upper arms should always remain stationary next to your torso and only the forearms should move. Exhale as you perform this movement.
After holding for a second, at the contracted position, bring the rope slowly up to the starting point. Breathe in as you perform this step.
Repeat for the recommended amount of repetitions.
exercises.name: Triceps Pushdown - Rope Attachment
exercises.overview: A triceps isolation exercise using a rope attachment that allows for greater range of motion and muscle contraction. The rope enables natural wrist positioning and enhanced tricep activation.
exercises.safetyInfo: Ensure proper rope attachment before starting. Maintain neutral wrists throughout the movement to prevent strain.

commonMistakes: Rotating wrists during the movement
Allowing upper arms to move forward
Bouncing the weight at the bottom position
exerciseTips: Maintain angled grip with thumbs higher than pinkies
Drive elbows down and back slightly
Focus on tricep contraction at full extension
Keep shoulders back and chest up
instructions: Attach a V-Bar to a high pulley and grab with an overhand grip (palms facing down) at shoulder width.

Standing upright with the torso straight and a very small inclination forward, bring the upper arms close to your body and perpendicular to the floor. The forearms should be pointing up towards the pulley as they hold the bar. The thumbs should be higher than the small finger. This is your starting position.

Using the triceps, bring the bar down until it touches the front of your thighs and the arms are fully extended perpendicular to the floor. The upper arms should always remain stationary next to your torso and only the forearms should move. Exhale as you perform this movement.
After a second hold at the contracted position, bring the V-Bar slowly up to the starting point. Breathe in as you perform this step.
Repeat for the recommended amount of repetitions.
exercises.name: Triceps Pushdown - V-Bar Attachment
exercises.name_pt: Tríceps na Polia com Barra V
exercises.overview: A triceps isolation exercise using a V-shaped bar that provides a comfortable angled grip. This variation can reduce wrist stress while effectively targeting the triceps muscles.
exercises.safetyInfo: The angled grip may feel uncomfortable initially - start light to allow adaptation. Stop if you experience wrist or elbow discomfort.

commonMistakes: Pulling on the neck or head during the crunch
Allowing lower back to arch off the floor
Using momentum instead of controlled muscle contraction
exerciseTips: Keep lower back pressed firmly against the floor throughout the movement
Exhale forcefully as you crunch up to maximize abdominal contraction
Control the descent to maintain tension on the abs
Focus on bringing ribs toward hips rather than just lifting shoulders
instructions: To begin, lie down on the floor or an exercise mat with your back pressed against the floor. Your arms should be lying across your sides with the palms facing down.

Your legs should be crossed by wrapping one ankle around the other. Slowly elevate your legs up in the air until your thighs are perpendicular to the floor with a slight bend at the knees. Note: Your knees and toes should be parallel to the floor as opposed to the thighs.
Move your arms from the floor and cross them so they are resting on your chest. This is the starting position.
While keeping your lower back pressed against the floor, slowly lift your torso. Remember to exhale while perform this part of the exercise.
Slowly begin to lower your torso back down to the starting position while inhaling.
Repeat for the recommended amount of repetitions.
exercises.name: Tuck Crunch
exercises.overview: The tuck crunch is a bodyweight abdominal exercise that combines the traditional crunch with elevated legs to increase difficulty and core engagement. It effectively targets the rectus abdominis while minimizing lower back stress.
exercises.safetyInfo: Avoid if you have lower back injuries. Keep movements slow and controlled to prevent neck strain from rapid jerking motions.

commonMistakes: Allowing the kettlebell to drift away from vertical alignment
Rushing through the movement instead of controlling each phase
Neglecting to keep the supporting shoulder stable and packed
exerciseTips: Keep your eyes on the kettlebell throughout the entire movement
Maintain a locked-out arm position with the weight directly overhead
Move slowly and deliberately through each phase of the movement
Keep your shoulder packed and stable throughout
instructions: Lie on your back holding a kettlebell in one hand with arm extended straight up, opposite knee bent with foot flat on floor.
Roll onto your elbow on the same side as the kettlebell, keeping the weight directly overhead.
Press up to your hand while keeping the kettlebell arm locked out overhead.
Lift your hips up and slide your straight leg under your body into a kneeling position.
Stand up from the kneeling position while maintaining the kettlebell overhead.
Reverse the entire sequence to return to the starting position.
exercises.name: Turkish Get Up
exercises.overview: The Turkish Get Up is a complex full-body movement that develops shoulder stability, core strength, and functional mobility. It challenges coordination, balance, and strength through multiple movement planes simultaneously.
exercises.safetyInfo: This is an advanced movement requiring significant shoulder stability and mobility. Avoid if you have shoulder impingement or instability. Practice the movement pattern without weight first.

commonMistakes: Extending arms completely at the bottom, causing elbow hyperextension
Using too much weight and swinging the dumbbells
Lifting shoulders off the preacher pad during the curl
exerciseTips: Keep chest pressed against the pad throughout the movement
Lower the weight slowly to fully stretch the biceps at the bottom
Stop just short of full arm extension to maintain tension
Squeeze biceps hard at the top of each rep
instructions: Grab a dumbbell with each arm and place the upper arms on top of the preacher bench or the incline bench. The dumbbell should be held at shoulder length. This will be your starting position.
As you breathe in, slowly lower the dumbbells until your upper arm is extended and the biceps is fully stretched.
As you exhale, use the biceps to curl the weights up until your biceps is fully contracted and the dumbbells are at shoulder height.
Squeeze the biceps hard for a second at the contracted position and repeat for the recommended amount of repetitions.
exercises.name: Two-Arm Dumbbell Preacher Curl
exercises.overview: The two-arm dumbbell preacher curl isolates the biceps by eliminating body swing and stabilizing the upper arms. This exercise provides excellent bicep isolation and promotes balanced arm development.
exercises.safetyInfo: Use controlled movements and avoid locking elbows at the bottom to prevent hyperextension injuries. Start with lighter weights as the preacher position increases bicep vulnerability.

commonMistakes: Using arms to pull rather than driving with hips and legs
Allowing kettlebells to swing too far away from the body
Failing to rotate wrists properly, causing kettlebells to crash on forearms
exerciseTips: Drive through your hips explosively to generate upward momentum
Rotate wrists smoothly as kettlebells reach shoulder height
Keep kettlebells close to your body throughout the movement
Land softly with kettlebells racked on shoulders
instructions: Place two kettlebells between your feet. To get in the starting position, push your butt back and look straight ahead.
Clean the kettlebells to your shoulders by extending through the legs and hips as you raise the kettlebells towards your shoulders. Rotate your wrists as you do so.
Lower the kettlebells back to the starting position and repeat.
exercises.name: Two-Arm Kettlebell Clean
exercises.overview: The two-arm kettlebell clean is an explosive full-body movement that develops power, coordination, and strength. It trains the posterior chain while improving athletic performance and functional movement patterns.
exercises.safetyInfo: Master single-arm kettlebell cleans before progressing to two-arm variations. Use appropriate weight to maintain proper form and prevent wrist or shoulder injury from improper rack position.

commonMistakes: Not dipping low enough before the explosive drive
Failing to get under the kettlebells after the initial push
Pressing out the kettlebells instead of using leg drive
exerciseTips: Dip quickly and drive up explosively through your legs
Get under the kettlebells immediately after the initial drive
Lock out arms completely before standing up fully
Keep core tight throughout the entire movement
instructions: Clean two kettlebells to your shoulders. Clean the kettlebells to your shoulders by extending through the legs and hips as you swing the kettlebells towards your shoulders. Rotate your wrists as you do so, so that the palms face forward. Squat down a few inches and reverse the motion rapidly driving both kettlebells overhead. Immediately after the initial push, squat down again and get under the kettlebells. Once the kettlebells are locked out, stand upright to complete the exercise.
exercises.name: Two-Arm Kettlebell Jerk
exercises.overview: The two-arm kettlebell jerk is an advanced overhead lifting technique that develops explosive power and coordination. It efficiently moves heavy weights overhead using leg drive rather than pure pressing strength.
exercises.safetyInfo: Ensure adequate overhead mobility before attempting. Practice the movement with lighter weights first as improper timing can lead to shoulder or wrist injuries.

commonMistakes: Arching the back excessively during the press
Allowing kettlebells to drift forward instead of staying over shoulders
Not engaging core and glutes for proper stability
exerciseTips: Keep core braced and glutes contracted for stability
Press straight up without arching your back
Lean slightly forward as kettlebells pass your head
Contract lats and maintain tight body position throughout
instructions: Clean two kettlebells to your shoulders. Clean the kettlebells to your shoulders by extending through the legs and hips as you swing the kettlebells towards your shoulders. Rotate your wrists as you do so, so that the palms face forward.

Press the kettlebells up and out. As the kettlebells pass your head, lean into the weights so that the kettlebells are racked behind your head. Make sure to contract your lats, butt, and stomach for added stability.
exercises.name: Two-Arm Kettlebell Military Press
exercises.overview: The two-arm kettlebell military press builds overhead pressing strength and core stability simultaneously. It develops functional upper body power while challenging balance and coordination.
exercises.safetyInfo: Ensure adequate shoulder and thoracic spine mobility before performing. Avoid if you have shoulder impingement or lower back issues without proper progression.

commonMistakes: Rounding the back during the rowing motion
Using momentum to swing the kettlebells up
Not retracting shoulder blades fully at the top
exerciseTips: Keep chest up and maintain natural back arch throughout
Pull kettlebells to your lower ribs/upper stomach area
Squeeze shoulder blades together at the top of each rep
Control the weight on both the pull and lowering phases
instructions: Place two kettlebells in front of your feet. Bend your knees slightly and then push your butt out as much as possible as you bend over to get in the starting position.
Grab both kettlebells and pull them to your stomach, retracting your shoulder blades and flexing the elbows. Keep your back straight. Lower and repeat.
exercises.name: Two-Arm Kettlebell Row
exercises.overview: The two-arm kettlebell row strengthens the posterior chain and improves posture by targeting the mid-back muscles. It develops pulling strength while teaching proper hip hinge mechanics.
exercises.safetyInfo: Maintain proper hip hinge position to protect your lower back. Start with lighter weights to master the movement pattern before progressing.
variations: Bent Over Two-Dumbbell Row With Palms In

commonMistakes: Using momentum by rocking the torso back and forth
Pulling the bar too low past the chest
Allowing the shoulders to roll forward at the top
exerciseTips: Keep your chest up and create a slight arch in your lower back
Pull your elbows down and back rather than just pulling with your arms
Squeeze your shoulder blades together at the bottom of the movement
Control the weight on the way up to maintain tension
instructions: Sit down on a pull-down machine with a wide bar attached to the top pulley. Adjust the knee pad of the machine to fit your height. These pads will prevent your body from being raised by the resistance attached to the bar.
Grab the pull-down bar with the palms facing your torso (a supinated grip). Make sure that the hands are placed closer than the shoulder width.

As you have both arms extended in front of you holding the bar at the chosen grip width, bring your torso back around 30 degrees or so while creating a curvature on your lower back and sticking your chest out. This is your starting position.

As you breathe out, pull the bar down until it touches your upper chest by drawing the shoulders and the upper arms down and back. Tip: Concentrate on squeezing the back muscles once you reach the fully contracted position and keep the elbows close to your body. The upper torso should remain stationary as your bring the bar to you and only the arms should move. The forearms should do no other work other than hold the bar.
After a second on the contracted position, while breathing in, slowly bring the bar back to the starting position when your arms are fully extended and the lats are fully stretched.
Repeat this motion for the prescribed amount of repetitions.
exercises.name: Underhand Cable Pulldowns
exercises.overview: This lat pulldown variation uses an underhand grip to emphasize the lower lats while providing additional bicep engagement. It's excellent for building a wider back and improving pull-up strength.
exercises.safetyInfo: Avoid if you have recent shoulder impingement or lower back injuries. The underhand grip places more stress on the biceps, so use appropriate weight to prevent bicep strain.
variations: Bent Over Two-Dumbbell Row With Palms In

commonMistakes: Leaning away from or toward the heavier weight
Using momentum instead of controlled movement patterns
exerciseTips: Start with a small weight difference between sides
Focus intensely on maintaining neutral spine alignment
Take shorter steps to maintain better control
Resist the urge to compensate by leaning or twisting
instructions: Hold kettlebells of different weights in the same carry position on each side.
Maintain strict upright posture despite the uneven loading.
Walk forward with controlled steps while fighting lateral lean.
Keep your core tight and shoulders level throughout the movement.
Complete the distance then switch the heavier weight to the opposite side.
exercises.name: Uneven Carry
exercises.overview: The uneven carry uses different weights on each side to create an imbalanced load that challenges lateral stability. It develops real-world strength for handling asymmetrical loads safely.
exercises.safetyInfo: The uneven loading creates significant lateral stress on the spine. Progress gradually and avoid excessive weight differences that compromise form.

commonMistakes: Pulling the bar too high above chest level
Using too wide of a grip which limits range of motion
Swinging the body to generate momentum
exerciseTips: Lead with your elbows and keep them higher than your wrists
Keep the bar close to your body throughout the movement
Stop when the bar reaches chest level to avoid impingement
Use a grip slightly narrower than shoulder width
instructions: Grasp a barbell with an overhand grip that is slightly less than shoulder width. The bar should be resting on the top of your thighs with your arms extended and a slight bend in your elbows. Your back should also be straight. This will be your starting position.

Now exhale and use the sides of your shoulders to lift the bar, raising your elbows up and to the side. Keep the bar close to your body as you raise it. Continue to lift the bar until it nearly touches your chin. Tip: Your elbows should drive the motion, and should always be higher than your forearms. Remember to keep your torso stationary and pause for a second at the top of the movement.
Lower the bar back down slowly to the starting position. Inhale as you perform this portion of the movement.
Repeat for the recommended amount of repetitions.
exercises.name: Upright Barbell Row
exercises.overview: A compound movement that primarily targets the side delts and upper traps. Effective for building shoulder width and upper body pulling strength.
exercises.safetyInfo: This exercise can cause shoulder impingement if performed incorrectly. Avoid pulling above chest level and discontinue if you experience shoulder pain.

commonMistakes: Allowing the cable to snap back quickly on the descent
Leaning back to assist the movement
Gripping too wide which reduces trap activation
exerciseTips: Maintain constant tension throughout the full range of motion
Keep your core engaged to prevent swaying
Focus on pulling your elbows up and out to the sides
Control the negative portion of the movement
instructions: Grasp a straight bar cable attachment that is attached to a low pulley with a pronated (palms facing your thighs) grip that is slightly less than shoulder width. The bar should be resting on top of your thighs. Your arms should be extended with a slight bend at the elbows and your back should be straight. This will be your starting position.

Use your side shoulders to lift the cable bar as you exhale. The bar should be close to the body as you move it up. Continue to lift it until it nearly touches your chin. Tip: Your elbows should drive the motion. As you lift the bar, your elbows should always be higher than your forearms. Also, keep your torso stationary and pause for a second at the top of the movement.
Lower the bar back down slowly to the starting position. Inhale as you perform this portion of the movement.
Repeat for the recommended amount of repetitions.
exercises.name: Upright Cable Row
exercises.overview: This cable variation of the upright row provides constant tension and emphasizes the upper traps more than the barbell version. Great for trap development and shoulder stability.
exercises.safetyInfo: The cable provides constant tension which can be easier on the joints than free weights. Still avoid pulling above chest level to prevent shoulder impingement.

commonMistakes: Not creating enough initial tension in the band
Allowing slack in the band during the movement
Standing too close to the band anchor point
exerciseTips: Step on the band to create appropriate tension at the start
Maintain steady resistance throughout the movement
Keep your shoulders back and chest up
Focus on squeezing your traps at the top
instructions: To begin, stand on an exercise band so that tension begins at arm's length. Grasp the handles using a pronated (palms facing your thighs) grip that is slightly less than shoulder width. The handles should be resting on top of your thighs. Your arms should be extended with a slight bend at the elbows and your back should be straight. This will be your starting position.

Use your side shoulders to lift the handles as you exhale. The handles should be close to the body as you move them up. Continue to lift the handles until they nearly touches your chin. Tip: Your elbows should drive the motion. As you lift the handles, your elbows should always be higher than your forearms. Also, keep your torso stationary and pause for a second at the top of the movement.
Lower the handles back down slowly to the starting position. Inhale as you perform this portion of the movement.
Repeat for the recommended amount of repetitions.
exercises.name: Upright Row - With Bands
exercises.overview: A portable alternative to barbell and cable upright rows using resistance bands. Perfect for home workouts and provides unique variable resistance that increases through the range of motion.
exercises.safetyInfo: Resistance bands provide variable resistance and are generally joint-friendly. Ensure the band is securely anchored and inspect for wear before use.

commonMistakes: Leaning too far back during the pull
Using momentum instead of controlled movement
Pulling the bar to the neck instead of chest level
exerciseTips: Pull your elbows down and back, not just down
Squeeze shoulder blades together at the bottom
Control the weight on the way up
Keep chest up throughout the movement
instructions: Sit down on a pull-down machine with a V-Bar attached to the top pulley.
Adjust the knee pad of the machine to fit your height. These pads will prevent your body from being raised by the resistance attached to the bar.

Grab the V-bar with the palms facing each other (a neutral grip). Stick your chest out and lean yourself back slightly (around 30-degrees) in order to better engage the lats. This will be your starting position.

Using your lats, pull the bar down as you squeeze your shoulder blades. Continue until your chest nearly touches the V-bar. Exhale as you execute this motion. Tip: Keep the torso stationary throughout the movement.
After a second hold on the contracted position, slowly bring the bar back to the starting position as you breathe in.
Repeat for the prescribed number of repetitions.
exercises.name: V-Bar Pulldown
exercises.overview: The V-Bar Pulldown is an effective lat-building exercise that allows for a neutral grip, reducing stress on the wrists and shoulders while targeting the upper back.
exercises.safetyInfo: Avoid if you have shoulder impingement. Don't lean back excessively as this can stress the lower back. Use appropriate weight to maintain form.
variations: Bent Over Two-Dumbbell Row With Palms In

commonMistakes: Not achieving full range of motion
Swinging or using momentum
Pulling chin over bar instead of chest to bar
exerciseTips: Start from a dead hang with arms fully extended
Pull your chest toward the bar
Control the descent to maximize muscle engagement
Keep core tight to prevent swaying
instructions: Start by placing the middle of the V-bar in the middle of the pull-up bar (assuming that the pull-up station you are using does not have neutral grip handles). The V-Bar handles will be facing down so that you can hang from the pull-up bar through the use of the handles.

Once you securely place the V-bar, take a hold of the bar from each side and hang from it. Stick your chest out and lean yourself back slightly in order to better engage the lats. This will be your starting position.

Using your lats, pull your torso up while leaning your head back slightly so that you do not hit yourself with the chin-up bar. Continue until your chest nearly touches the V-bar. Exhale as you execute this motion.
After a second hold on the contracted position, slowly lower your body back to the starting position as you breathe in.
Repeat for the prescribed number of repetitions.
exercises.name: V-Bar Pullup
exercises.overview: The V-Bar Pullup is a challenging bodyweight exercise that targets the lats with a neutral grip, making it joint-friendly while building impressive upper body strength.
exercises.safetyInfo: Ensure V-bar is securely attached to prevent falls. Build up gradually if new to pullups. Stop if experiencing shoulder or elbow pain.

commonMistakes: Fighting the natural arc of the landmine setup
Using too much weight before mastering the movement pattern
Allowing the core to relax during the pressing motion
exerciseTips: Follow the natural arc of the barbell rather than pressing straight up
Keep your core engaged throughout the movement
Maintain a stable base with your feet planted firmly
Allow your shoulders to move naturally with the angled pressing motion
instructions: Set up a barbell in a landmine attachment or corner with weight plates loaded on the free end.
Stand facing the barbell and grasp it with both hands at chest level.
Position your feet shoulder-width apart and maintain an upright torso.
Press the barbell up and away from your chest at an angle following the natural arc.
Lower the barbell back to the starting position with control.
exercises.name: Viking Press
exercises.overview: The Viking Press is an angled pressing exercise that follows the natural arc of shoulder movement. It provides a unique stimulus for shoulder development while being more joint-friendly than traditional overhead pressing.
exercises.safetyInfo: This advanced pressing variation is generally shoulder-friendly due to the natural arc of motion. However, those with shoulder issues should start light and progress gradually.

commonMistakes: Leaning away from the weight instead of maintaining upright posture
Allowing the weight to drift forward or backward during the carry
exerciseTips: Keep your shoulder blade pulled down and back on the working side
Resist side-bending by engaging your core muscles strongly
Maintain an upright posture and avoid leaning away from the weight
Keep the weight directly over your shoulder throughout the movement
instructions: Press a dumbbell overhead with one arm, locking out your elbow completely.
Position the weight directly over your shoulder with your palm facing forward.
Engage your core and maintain an upright posture with the free arm at your side.
Walk forward with controlled steps, keeping the overhead weight stable and balanced.
Resist the urge to lean away from the weight or bend sideways during the carry.
Complete the prescribed distance, then switch arms and repeat on the opposite side.
exercises.name: Waiter's Walk
exercises.overview: The waiter's walk is a unilateral overhead carry that challenges shoulder stability and core strength while fighting lateral flexion forces. It's named after waiters who carry trays overhead and builds functional strength and stability.
exercises.safetyInfo: Requires excellent shoulder stability and core strength. Those with shoulder injuries, neck problems, or severe core weakness should avoid this exercise. Start with lighter weights to develop proper stability patterns.

commonMistakes: Placing hands too close to or too far from the wall
Allowing the lower back to arch excessively during the movement
Using momentum instead of controlled strength for the pressing motion
exerciseTips: Keep your core tight and body in a straight line to prevent arching
Focus on pressing through your entire palm, not just fingertips
Control the descent slowly to build strength in the eccentric portion
Practice wall handstand holds before attempting the push-up movement
instructions: Place your hands on the floor about 6 inches from a wall and kick up into a handstand with your heels against the wall.
Position your hands slightly wider than shoulder-width apart with fingers spread for stability.
Lower your head toward the floor by bending your arms until your head lightly touches the ground.
Press through your palms to extend your arms and return to the starting handstand position.
Maintain tension throughout your core and legs while pressed against the wall.
exercises.name: Wall Handstand Push Up
exercises.name_pt: Flexão Parado de Mãos na Parede
exercises.overview: The wall handstand push-up is an advanced vertical pressing exercise performed in an inverted position against a wall for support. It primarily targets the shoulders while requiring exceptional upper body strength and core stability.
exercises.safetyInfo: This exercise requires significant shoulder strength and stability. Those with shoulder injuries, neck problems, or high blood pressure should avoid inverted exercises. Ensure adequate wrist mobility before attempting.

commonMistakes: Allowing knees to drift inward instead of staying aligned
Not maintaining proper 90-degree angles at hips and knees
Rising up on toes instead of keeping feet flat on the ground
exerciseTips: Keep your entire back pressed firmly against the wall throughout the hold
Maintain steady breathing and avoid holding your breath
Keep your feet firmly planted and weight distributed evenly
Focus on keeping your knees aligned over your ankles
instructions: Stand with your back against a wall and feet about two feet away from the wall.
Slowly slide your back down the wall while walking your feet out until your thighs are parallel to the ground.
Ensure your knees are at 90-degree angles and your back is flat against the wall.
Hold this position for the desired amount of time while breathing normally.
To finish, slowly slide back up the wall to the starting standing position.
exercises.name: Wall Sit
exercises.overview: The wall sit is an isometric exercise where you hold a squat position against a wall. It builds muscular endurance in the quadriceps and glutes while requiring no equipment and minimal space.
exercises.safetyInfo: This isometric exercise is generally safe but can cause significant muscle fatigue. Those with knee injuries or blood pressure issues should monitor their response and modify duration as needed.

commonMistakes: Hyperextending too far at the top
Using momentum instead of muscle control
Placing weight incorrectly causing neck strain
exerciseTips: Focus on squeezing glutes at the top
Move slowly and controlled throughout
Keep neck neutral, don't crane head back
Exhale as you lift, inhale as you lower
instructions: To begin, lie down on an exercise ball with your torso pressing against the ball and parallel to the floor. The ball of your feet should be pressed against the floor to help keep you balanced. Place a weighted plate under your chin or behind your neck. This is the starting position.
Slowly raise your torso up by bending at the waist and lower back. Remember to exhale during this movement.
Hold the contraction on your lower back for a second and lower your torso back down to the starting position while inhaling.
Repeat for the recommended amount of repetitions prescribed in your program.
exercises.name: Weighted Ball Hyperextension
exercises.overview: The Weighted Ball Hyperextension strengthens the posterior chain muscles, particularly the erector spinae, helping improve posture and reduce lower back pain risk.
exercises.safetyInfo: Start without weight to master the movement. Avoid if you have disc problems or acute back pain. Don't hyperextend beyond neutral spine.

commonMistakes: Rotating the torso instead of pure lateral flexion
Using momentum to swing up rather than controlled muscle contraction
Allowing the supporting arm to push off the torso
exerciseTips: Keep the weighted plate pressed firmly against your head throughout the movement
Focus on lateral flexion without twisting or rotating your torso
Squeeze your obliques at the top of the movement
Control the descent to maximize muscle engagement
instructions: To begin, lie down on an exercise ball with your left side of the torso (waist, hips and shoulder) pressed against the ball.

Your feet should be on the floor while your legs are crossed and hanging from the ball. Hold a weighted plate with your right hand directly to the right side of your head. Tip: Make sure the smooth side of the plate is resting against your head.
Place your left arm across your torso so that your palm is on your obliques. There should be a right angle between your left forearm and upper arm. This is the starting position.
Raise the side of your torso up by laterally flexing at the waist while exhaling.
Hold the contraction for a second and slowly lower yourself back down to the starting position while inhaling.
Repeat for the recommended amount of repetitions.
Switch sides and repeat the exercise.
exercises.name: Weighted Ball Side Bend
exercises.name_pt: Flexão Lateral com Bola e Peso
exercises.overview: The weighted ball side bend is an isolation exercise that targets the obliques using an exercise ball for instability and added resistance. This exercise enhances lateral core strength and stability.
exercises.safetyInfo: Avoid this exercise if you have lower back injuries or shoulder impingement. The unstable surface of the exercise ball requires good balance and core stability.

commonMistakes: Flaring elbows out wide which stresses the shoulders
Descending too deep beyond 90 degrees
Using legs to assist the movement
exerciseTips: Keep elbows close to your body throughout the movement
Lower until your upper arms are parallel to the floor
Press through your palms to engage triceps maximally
Maintain upright torso position
instructions: For this exercise you will need to place a bench behind your back and another one in front of you. With the benches perpendicular to your body, hold on to one bench on its edge with the hands close to your body, separated at shoulder width. Your arms should be fully extended.

The legs will be extended forward on top of the other bench. Your legs should be parallel to the floor while your torso is to be perpendicular to the floor. Have your partner place the dumbbell on your lap. Note: This exercise is best performed with a partner as placing the weight on your lap can be challenging and cause injury without assistance. This will be your starting position.

Slowly lower your body as you inhale by bending at the elbows until you lower yourself far enough to where there is an angle slightly smaller than 90 degrees between the upper arm and the forearm. Tip: Keep the elbows as close as possible throughout the movement. Forearms should always be pointing down.
Using your triceps to bring your torso up again, lift yourself back to the starting position while exhaling.
Repeat for the recommended amount of repetitions.
exercises.name: Weighted Bench Dip
exercises.overview: The weighted bench dip is a compound exercise that primarily targets the triceps while also engaging the chest and front deltoids. Adding weight increases the challenge beyond bodyweight dips.
exercises.safetyInfo: This exercise places significant stress on the shoulder joint. Avoid if you have shoulder impingement or rotator cuff injuries. Ensure proper warm-up and don't exceed comfortable range of motion.

commonMistakes: Pulling on the neck instead of using core muscles
Coming up too high and engaging hip flexors
Using momentum to swing the weight up
exerciseTips: Focus on flexing your spine rather than just lifting your shoulders
Keep your lower back pressed against the floor
Control both the lifting and lowering phases
Breathe out as you crunch up and in as you lower
instructions: Lie flat on your back with your feet flat on the ground or resting on a bench with your knees bent at a 90 degree angle.
Hold a weight to your chest, or you may hold it extended above your torso. This will be your starting position.
Now, exhale and slowly begin to roll your shoulders off the floor. Your shoulders should come up off the floor about 4 inches while your lower back remains on the floor.
At the top of the movement, flex your abdominals and hold for a brief pause.
Then inhale and slowly lower yourself back down to the starting position.
exercises.name: Weighted Crunches
exercises.overview: Weighted crunches add resistance to the basic crunch movement, increasing the challenge for the rectus abdominis. This exercise is effective for building abdominal strength and muscle mass.
exercises.safetyInfo: Avoid excessive neck strain by keeping your chin neutral. Those with lower back issues should be cautious and may need to modify the range of motion.

commonMistakes: Adding too much weight too quickly
Descending too deep and risking shoulder injury
Allowing the weight to swing and create momentum
exerciseTips: Start with small weight increments to avoid injury
Keep your shoulders down and back throughout the movement
Lean forward slightly to emphasize chest involvement
Control the descent to maximize muscle activation
instructions: Secure additional weight using a dip belt, weighted vest, or dumbbell held between your legs.
Grip the dip bars and support your body weight with arms fully extended.
Lean slightly forward and lower your body by bending your elbows until you feel a stretch in your chest.
Press through your palms to drive your body back up to the starting position.
Maintain control of the added weight throughout the entire range of motion.
exercises.name: Weighted Dip
exercises.overview: Weighted dips add external resistance to bodyweight dips, significantly increasing the challenge for the chest, triceps, and shoulders. This intermediate to advanced exercise is excellent for building upper body pushing strength and muscle mass.
exercises.safetyInfo: Only attempt weighted dips after mastering bodyweight dips with perfect form. Those with shoulder impingement or chest injuries should avoid this exercise. Use a spotter when possible.

commonMistakes: Using too heavy a weight that slows down the explosive movement
Landing with straight legs creating excessive joint stress
Allowing knees to cave inward during landing
exerciseTips: Land softly with bent knees to absorb impact
Keep your chest up and core engaged throughout
Jump as high as possible while maintaining control
Use a light weight that doesn't compromise explosive power
instructions: Position a lightly loaded barbell across the back of your shoulders. You could also use a weighted vest, sandbag, or other type of resistance for this exercise.
The weight should be light enough that it doesn't slow you down significantly. Your feet should be just outside of shoulder width with your head and chest up. This will be your starting position.

Using a countermovement, squat partially down and immediately reverse your direction to explode off of the ground, extending through your hips, knees, and ankles. Maintain good posture throughout the jump.
As you return to the ground, absorb the impact through your legs.
exercises.name: Weighted Jump Squat
exercises.overview: The weighted jump squat combines strength and power training by adding resistance to explosive jumping movements. This exercise develops lower body power and athletic performance.
exercises.safetyInfo: This high-impact exercise should be avoided by those with knee, ankle, or lower back injuries. Proper landing mechanics are crucial to prevent injury. Start with bodyweight before adding load.

commonMistakes: Using momentum or swinging to complete reps
Not achieving full range of motion at the bottom
Allowing shoulders to shrug up toward ears
exerciseTips: Pull your shoulder blades down and back before initiating the pull
Focus on pulling your elbows down toward your ribs
Control the descent to maximize muscle engagement
Keep your core tight to prevent swinging
instructions: Attach a weight to a dip belt and secure it around your waist. Grab the pull-up bar with the palms of your hands facing forward. For a medium grip, your hands should be spaced at shoulder width. Both arms should be extended in front of you holding the bar at the chosen grip.
You'll want to bring your torso back about 30 degrees while creating a curvature in your lower back and sticking your chest out. This will be your starting position.
Now, exhale and pull your torso up until your head is above your hands. Concentrate on squeezing yourshoulder blades back and down as you reach the top contracted position.
After a brief moment at the top contracted position, inhale and slowly lower your torso back to the starting position with your arms extended and your lats fully stretched.
exercises.name: Weighted Pull Ups
exercises.overview: Weighted pull-ups add external resistance to the classic pull-up movement, increasing the challenge for the latissimus dorsi and supporting muscles. This exercise builds serious upper body pulling strength.
exercises.safetyInfo: Ensure the weight is properly secured to prevent it from falling. Those with shoulder impingement or elbow issues should be cautious. Progress weight gradually to avoid overuse injuries.

commonMistakes: Bending at the hips instead of maintaining straight torso
Going too deep too quickly before building flexibility
Using the support arm to pull rather than just balance
exerciseTips: Keep your torso straight and lean back as a unit
Focus on pushing your knees forward while lowering
Rise up on your toes to maximize quad engagement
Control the movement with your quadriceps rather than using momentum
instructions: Standing upright, with feet at shoulder width and toes raised, use one hand to hold onto the beams of a squat rack and the opposite arm to hold a plate on top of your chest. This is your starting position.

As you use one arm to hold yourself, bend at the knees and slowly lower your torso toward the ground by bringing your pelvis and knees forward. Inhale as you go down and stop when your upper and lower legs almost create a 90-degree angle. Hold the stretch position for a second.
After your one second hold, use your thigh muscles to bring your torso back up to the starting position. Exhale as you move up.
Repeat for the recommended amount of times.
exercises.name: Weighted Sissy Squat
exercises.overview: The weighted sissy squat is an advanced quadriceps exercise that emphasizes knee extension while maintaining a straight torso line. This movement provides intense quadriceps isolation and development.
exercises.safetyInfo: This exercise places extreme stress on the knee joint and should be avoided by those with knee injuries or patellar tracking issues. Build up flexibility and strength gradually, starting with bodyweight only.

commonMistakes: Pulling on the bands with arms instead of using core
Using momentum to swing up rather than controlled movement
Not maintaining neutral wrist position with bands
exerciseTips: Keep the bands at collar bone level throughout the movement
Focus on pulling your ribcage toward your pelvis
Exhale forcefully at the top of the movement
Control the descent to maximize muscle tension
instructions: Start out by strapping the bands around the base of the decline bench. Place the handles towards the inside of the decline bench so that when lying down, you can reach for both of them.

Position your legs through the decline machine until they are secured. Now reach for the exercise bands with both hands. Use a pronated (palms forward) grip to grasp the handles. Position them near your collar bone and rotate your wrist to a neutral grip (palms facing the torso). Note: Your arms should remain stationary throughout the exercise. This is the starting position.

Move your torso upward until your upper body is perpendicular to the floor while exhaling. Hold the contraction for a second and lower your upper body back down to the starting position while inhaling.
Repeat for the recommended amount of repetitions.
exercises.name: Weighted Sit-Ups - With Bands
exercises.name_pt: Abdominal com Peso e Faixa Elástica
exercises.overview: A challenging core exercise that combines the benefits of decline sit-ups with variable resistance from bands. The resistance increases as you sit up, providing maximum tension at peak contraction.
exercises.safetyInfo: Avoid if you have lower back issues or neck problems. The decline position increases blood flow to the head, so stop if you feel dizzy or lightheaded.

commonMistakes: Allowing knees to cave inward during the movement
Leaning too far forward and losing balance
Not descending to proper depth below parallel
exerciseTips: Keep your torso upright throughout the movement
Drive through your heels on the way up
Maintain knee tracking over your toes
Descend until thighs are parallel to the floor
instructions: Start by positioning two flat benches shoulder width apart from each other. Stand on top of them and wrap the weighted belt around your waist with the amount of weight you feel comfortable with. Make sure your toes are facing out.
Once you are standing straight up with the weight hanging in between your legs, position your arms so that they are fully extended to the side of your body. This is the starting position.

Begin by bending the knees as you maintain a straight posture with the head up. Continue down until the angle between the upper leg and the calves becomes slightly less than 90-degrees (which is the point in which the upper legs are below parallel to the floor). Inhale as you perform this portion of the movement. Tip: If you performed the exercise correctly, the front of the knees should make an imaginary straight line with the toes that are perpendicular to the front. If your knees are past that imaginary line (if they are past your toes) then you are placing undue stress on the knee and the exercise has been performed incorrectly.

Begin to move the body back up by pushing the floor of the flat bench with the ball of your foot mainly as you straighten the legs again and go back to the starting position. Exhale as you perform this portion of the exercise.
Repeat for the recommended amount of repetitions.
exercises.name: Weighted Squat
exercises.overview: A unique squat variation performed on elevated platforms with a weight belt, allowing for deeper range of motion and reduced spinal loading compared to traditional back squats.
exercises.safetyInfo: Ensure benches are stable and properly spaced. Start with lighter weight to master balance. This exercise requires good proprioception and ankle mobility.

commonMistakes: Using too wide of a grip, which reduces range of motion and effectiveness
Swinging or using momentum to complete the repetition
Not achieving full range of motion at the bottom of each rep
exerciseTips: Focus on pulling your elbows down and back rather than just up
Initiate each rep by depressing your shoulder blades first
Maintain a slight backward lean to engage the lats maximally
Control the negative portion to build strength and muscle
instructions: Hang from a pull-up bar with hands wider than shoulder-width using an overhand grip.
Start from a full hang with arms completely extended and shoulders slightly stretched.
Initiate the pull by depressing your shoulder blades and engaging your lats.
Pull your body up until your chin clears the bar, focusing on pulling your elbows down and back.
Pause briefly at the top position with your chest near the bar.
Lower yourself slowly back to the starting position with full arm extension.
exercises.name: Wide Grip Pull Up
exercises.overview: The wide grip pull-up is a challenging bodyweight exercise that primarily targets the latissimus dorsi with emphasis on the outer portion. The wider grip increases lat activation while reducing bicep involvement compared to narrow grip variations.
exercises.safetyInfo: Ensure adequate shoulder mobility before attempting wide grip variations. Those with shoulder impingement should start with a narrower grip and gradually widen as mobility improves.

commonMistakes: Stance too wide causing hip impingement
Knees collapsing inward during ascent
Forward knee travel beyond toes
exerciseTips: Point your toes out 30-45 degrees
Push your knees out in line with your toes
Initiate the movement by sitting back with your hips
Keep your chest up and core braced
instructions: This exercise is best performed inside a squat rack for safety purposes. To begin, first set the bar on a rack that best matches your height. Once the correct height is chosen and the bar is loaded, step under the bar and place the back of your shoulders (slightly below the neck) across it.
Hold on to the bar using both arms at each side and lift it off the rack by first pushing with your legs and at the same time straightening your torso.

Step away from the rack and position your legs using a wider-than-shoulder-width stance with the toes slightly pointed out. Keep your head up at all times as looking down will get you off balance, and also maintain a straight back. This will be your starting position.

Begin to slowly lower the bar by bending the knees as you maintain a straight posture with the head up. Continue down until the angle between the upper leg and the calves becomes slightly less than 90-degrees (which is the point in which the upper legs are below parallel to the floor). Inhale as you perform this portion of the movement. Tip: If you performed the exercise correctly, the front of the knees should make an imaginary straight line with the toes that is perpendicular to the front. If your knees are past that imaginary line (if they are past your toes) then you are placing undue stress on the knee and the exercise has been performed incorrectly.
Begin to raise the bar as you exhale by pushing the floor with the heel of your foot as you straighten the legs again and go back to the starting position.
Repeat for the recommended amount of repetitions.
exercises.name: Wide Stance Barbell Squat
exercises.overview: A squat variation with wider stance that emphasizes the glutes and inner thighs more than conventional squats, while allowing for potentially heavier loads due to shortened range of motion.
exercises.safetyInfo: Requires good hip and ankle mobility. Use a squat rack with safety bars set just below your lowest squat position. May not be suitable for those with hip impingement issues.

commonMistakes: Grip too wide causing shoulder impingement
Bouncing the bar off the chest
Pressing the bar toward your face instead of straight up
exerciseTips: Retract your shoulder blades and keep them down
Lower the bar to your mid-chest area
Drive through your feet while pressing
Keep your wrists straight and over your elbows
instructions: Lie back on a flat bench with feet firm on the floor. Using a wide, pronated (palms forward) grip that is around 3 inches away from shoulder width (for each hand), lift the bar from the rack and hold it straight over you with your arms locked. The bar will be perpendicular to the torso and the floor. This will be your starting position.
As you breathe in, come down slowly until you feel the bar on your middle chest.

After a second pause, bring the bar back to the starting position as you breathe out and push the bar using your chest muscles. Lock your arms and squeeze your chest in the contracted position, hold for a second and then start coming down slowly again. Tip: It should take at least twice as long to go down than to come up.
Repeat the movement for the prescribed amount of repetitions.
exercises.name: Wide-Grip Barbell Bench Press
exercises.name_pt: Supino Reto com Barra - Pegada Aberta
exercises.overview: A bench press variation with wider hand placement that emphasizes the outer chest and reduces tricep involvement, commonly used in powerlifting for shorter range of motion.
exercises.safetyInfo: Wide grip increases shoulder stress and injury risk. Use a spotter or safety bars. Avoid if you have shoulder impingement or previous shoulder injuries.

commonMistakes: Lowering the bar too high on the chest
Using grip that's excessively wide
